PREFACE

As  I pullεd togethεr books, articles, chaptεrs, abstracts, unpublished papers, notes,
and electronic  sourcεs of information that spanned the  sεven years since the last
edition of Teachiηg ψ Priηciples, I was repeatedly rεmindεd of how compl℃X this
profession has bεcom℃! Dozens of respected periodicals and hundrεdsof t℃xtbooks
and  anthologies  currεntly offer  ample  evidencε that  language  teachers  must  be
technicians, well vεrsεd in thε pedagogical options available to meεt thespεcialized
neεds of the various  agεs, purposes, profici℃ncy levels, skills,  and  contexts of lan
guage lεarnεrs around the globe.

Such  was  not  always  the  case. The  first  40  years  of the  twentieth  century
saw little if any  dεvelopment of a field  of language pedagogy. But by the  middlε
of the century, language teachεrs witnεssed the “ birth” of a disciplined approach
to second language learning and teaching: Methodological framεworks were thε
subject of appliεd linguistic research on the nature of language learning and thε
successful acquisition of languages in classrooms.Yet thε nascent profession was
hard put to come up with viable answers to questions about how to teach intεr­
activ,ε  skills  in  thε classroom.  By  the  1970s,  sεcond language  acquisition  was
establishing  itsεlf as  a  disciplinε in  its own right, asserting its  place not merely
as  an offshoot of linguistics or psychology. The resulting resεarch of this adoles
cent  profiεssion was  beginning  to  provide  some  profound  obsεrvations about
interactivε language  pedagogy. As  thε field  gathered  momentum, journals, pro
fessional  organizations, university departments, and  research  studies grew with
amazing speed.

PURPOSE AND AUDIENCE

And so today, with reams of accumulatεd data to sift through in  ordεr to examine
our own craft, I hope that this third edition of Teachiηg byPriηciples will provide
the kind of synthesis of the state of our art that will  enli힐lten and challenge you. It
is a book for prospεctive and new teachers who need to learn how to walk into a
classroom  and  εffectiv1εly  accomplish  communicative  objectiv1εs.  It  primarily

x”

Preface

XIII

addr'εsses the needs of those in teacher education programs who have nevεr taught
bεforε, but it secondarily sεrves as a refoεsher coursε for thosε who have had som℃
expεrience in  the classroom. The book speaks both to those who arε in English as
a  Sεcond Language contexts (in English-speaking countries) and to  thosε who are
in  English  as  a  Foreign  Language  situations. And  thε book  is  dεsignεd to  bε read,
studied‘  and  enjoyεd by  thosε with  little  or no  prεvious work  in  linguistics, psy
chology, or second languagε acquisition.

Thε usε of thε term approach in the subtitle of thε book signals an important
characteristic  of current  language-t℃aching pedagogy. For a  sign표icant part  of thε
twεntieth century, tεachεr εducation programs wεrε εxpectεd to dεliv'εr a  handful
of prefabricated mεthods-r'εla ti、rely homogεneous sets of classroom practices that
sprang  from  onε  particular  theoretical  pεrspective.  In  today’s  “ postmethod”
approach, we havε graduated bεyond such a rεstrictivε concept of classroom prac
tice.  Our  current  and,  I  like  to  think,  more  enlighteηed foundations  of  languag1ε
Iεaching ar,ε built on numerous principlεs of languagε learning and teaching about
which we can be reasonably secure.  A principlεd approach to interactiv'ε language
pεdagogy is  onε that is  built on such foundation stones.

So, Teaching By Priηciples (TB꺼 is a book that helps teachers to build a  repεr­
toire of classroom techniques that arε firmly  εmbeddεd in well-established  princi
plεs  of  second  language  acquisition.  Most  of  thesε  principles  are  treated
com prεhεnsively in my companion volume‘ Prineψles of La η:guage Learηtηg aηd
Teachiηg (PLLT)  (H.D. Brown, 2007), now in  its  fifth  edition. Those who  usε TEP
in their teacher-training program would benefit from (a)  having first  rεad PLLT, or
(b) using PLLT as a companion text. Howev,εc TEP can be used εffectivεly without
its  companion, since  major principles on which current pedagogical  practicε are
basεd arε summarized herε in the early chapters.

PRINCIPAL FEATIJRES

Most of thε fεatures of thε first  and sεcond editions of TEP are r℃tained:

•  A practical focus grounded in fundamental  principlεs of second languagε

acquisition

•  Reader-friεndly prosε that talks  to teachers in plain, understandable lan
guage, with a  minimum of distracting retiεrεnces to thε dozens of poten
tially related research studies

•  A step-by-stεp approach to teaching languagε interactiv,εly that helps the

novice teacher to becomε confident in directing intεractive, student-centεred,
coopεrativ,ε classrooms

•  Sεparate treatmεnt of thε four skills of listεning, speaking, rεading,and

writing, but with special emphasis on the integration of skills

xiv

Preface

•  End-of-chapter topics for discussion, action, and research, many of which

model an interactive classroom by providing tasks for pairs or small groups

•  Suggestions for further reading at the end of each chapter, annotated to

facilitate  judicious choices of extra reading

IMPROVEMENTS IN THE THIRD EDffiON

A  number of improvements  have  been made  in  this Third  Edition, following  the
comments 없id suggestions of teachers, students, and reviewers who have used TBP
in its Second Edition. Here are the major changes:

•  Advance organizers at the beginning of chapters. Each chapter now

begins with a brief list of objectives of the chapteε which serve as
prer1εading organizers for students.

•  New chapters. Three chapters have been added. Chapter 9, Curriculum
Design, introduces Part III, offering readers the “big” picture into which
lessons, materials, and management issues must fit. Chapter 12,Technology
in the Classroom, is now a separate chapter that summarizes principles
and pedagogic때 options for using technology to promote interactive lan-
guage classrooms. Chapter 26, Teachers for Social Responsib피ty, the book’s
final chapter, underscores the importance of considering one’s  role as a
socially responsible agent for change in today’s world of global challenges.

•  New topics. In the span of seven years of research and practice in the

language-teaching profession, inevitably a number of new topics rise to the
top of the assortment of “ hot” issues. In this third edition of TBP, you will
find treatment of the f이lowing areas of concern that may not have
received as much attention in the last edition:

Postmethod condition
Lexical Approach
Multiple intelligences
Two new principles: Autonomy & Willingness to Communicate
Nonnative English-speaking teachers (nonNESTS)
Cultural issues
Practical steps to coursε design
Technological advances
Microskills and macroskills
Form-focused instruction
Corpus linguistics and the place of vocabulary teaching
Re야ganized principles of assessment
Completely reworked chaptεrs on assessment
Alternatives in assessment

Teacher development and reflective teaching

•  Updated references throughout. In sεven years, the field of language

pεdagogy has made some sign퍼cant advances that are reflected in ev1εηr
chapter of the book. Especially noticeable are new and updated sugges
tions for further rεading at the end of εach chapter.

ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

Teaching ψ Principles is  a product of several decades of instruction and research
in teaching English as a Sεcond/Foreign Language. During that time, it has been my
pleasure  and  challengε to  teach  and  to  lεarn from  thousands  of students  in  my
coursεs. I  am  grateful  for  all  thosε inquisitive  minds-now  scattεred around  the
world-whose insights are well represented here.

I  am  also  indebted  to  teachers  in  many  countries  of the world, especially  in
those countries where I have had the honor of lecturing 따id teaching: Bolivia, Brazil,
Canada, Chile, Costa Rica, Croatia, the Dominican Republic, Egypt, Guatemala, Hong
Kong,  Italy, Japan,  Korεa, Mexico, Panama, Peru, Portugal, Singapore, Spain, Taiwan,
Thailand,  Turkey,  Uruguay,  Yugoslavia, and of course the United States. I learn so
much from the exchanges of ideas and issues and stories from these contacts!

I of course wish to acknowledge the fεedback I received from my San Francisco
State  University  faculty  associates,  Professors  David  Olsher, Tom  Scovel,  and  May
Shih, and from my colleagues at  thε American Language Institute, Kathy Sherak and
Peg Sarosy.  The nurture and camaraderiε among these and other colleagues at SFSU
and the ALI  are a source of professional stimulation and of personal affirmation that
what wε are all collectively trying to do is  most certainly worth the effort.

Last but by no means least, I am eternally grateful for the support that my wife,
Mary,  unswervingly offers  as  she  tolerates  my  various  episodes  of crankiness  and
need for long hours of uninterrupted focus during these book-writing periods. This
support and nurture on the home front is a wondrous affirmation of my work!

H. Douglas Brown
San Francisco, California

OBJECTIVES  After  「eading this  chapte「, you  will  be  able to:

So  you’ve  decided  to  bε a  languagε tεacher! Welcome  to  a  profession  that  will
guarantee you more than your fair  share of challenges, growth, joy, and fulfillment.
Challenges await you at εV℃ηr turn in your professional path because th℃ discipline
of language  teaching  has  only  begun  to  solve  some  of the  pεrplexing questions
about how pεople learn foreign  languages successfully.  Opportunities for  growth
abound because, for as long as you continue to teach, you will never run out of new
questions, new possib피ties, new ways of looking at your students, and new ways of
looking at yourself.  Thε joy of teaching lies in witnessing your students' attainrnεnt
of broader  and  broader  vistas  of linguistic  proficiency  and  in  experiencing  the
communal  bond  that  you  have  been  instrumεntal in  creating  in  your  classroom.
And,  ultimatelκ few  professions  can  offer  thε fulfillment  of  knowing  that  your
seemingly  insign퍼cant work  really  can  m싫‘c a  diftiεrεnce in  a  world  in  nεεd of
communication that transcεnds national borders and intεr℃sts.

At  present,  all  those  lofty  ideals  notwithstanding,  you  may  be  a  little
apprehensive about what sort of a teacher you are going to bε:What will it be like
to be in front of a  classroom full  of expectant ears and eyes, hanging on my eveηr
word and action, rεady and waiting to pounce on me if I  make a false  move?  How
will I develop the composure and poise that I’veseεn modeled by“master” teachers?
Will  I  be  able  to  take  the  sea  of theoretical  information  about  second  language
acquisition  that  I  have  studied  and  by  some  miracle  transform  it  into  practical
classroom applications? How do I  plan a lesson? What do I  do if my lesson plan falls
apart? Where do I  bεgin?

Before you ask anymore questions, which might at this stage overwhelm you,
sit back for a moment and tell yourself that you can indeed bεcome a teacher who
will fully  meet the challenges ahead and who will grow in professional  expεrtise,
th erεby opening the doors of joy and fulfillmεnt.  This textbook is designed to hεlp
you take that developmεntal journey one stεp at a time.

The first  stεp in that journey is to come with me into a language classroom and
obseπε what happens.  As  the lesson unfolds‘ takε special note of εach choice that
the teacher makes: choices about how to begin the lesson, which activity W피 come
nεxt, how long to continue an activity, whom to call on, whεthεr to correct a student,
and  so  on.  Eveηthing a  teacher  says  and  does  in  thε classroom  is  thε result  of
conscious or subconscious choices among many alternatives.  Many of these choices
ar1ε-or should  be-the  result  of  a  car,εful  consideration  of  a  host  of  underlying
principles of second language learning and teaching.

A CIASSROOM OBSERVATION

Thε classroom wε arε about to enter is in a private language school in sεoul,Korεa.
Inside the classroom, a coursε in English as a Sεcond Language (ESL)* is taking place.
Thε 15  students in  thε coursε ar,ε young adults, most  of whom  ar1ε rεcent collεge
graduates and now arε working in businεSSεs in Sεoul.  This is an intermediatε lεV℃l
class; most of the students “graduatεd” into thε class after completing the beginner's
lev℃I.  The goal of thε course is for  studεnts to bε able to use English in their local
context (telεvision, movies, pop culture, Int℃met) and for international travel.  A fεw
might evεntually use English in job-relatεd duties.

Thε coursε focuses on intεgrativ℃ skills (combining the four skills of speaking,
listening, reading, and writing) ‘  표ie main textbook being used is Top 1Viυtι&. ·Englis/J
for Today's World,  Level 2 (Saslow &Ascher, 2006).  At this stage, two weeks into the
course, the  studεnts are still  not completεly confidεnt in their speaking ability but
thεy can engage in simple so[ ial conversations and  makε somε practical rεquests.
Thεir listening  ability  varies  but  thε coursε material  sεems to  be  appropriately
pitched  at  their  lev,εI.  They  ar,ε  quitε good  rεaders, having  had  English  in  their
university studies‘  Thεir writing is  fairly  accurate at the Sεntεncε levεl using basic
grammar, but rhεtorical factors  involvεd in composing an essay remain a  challenge.
The lesson wε are about to obsεπε covεrs Lesson 2 of Unit 2 of Top Notcb,  on
thε topic  of “ moviεs and  entertainmεnt.” Thε functional  focus  of thε lesson  is  to
discuss  prεfεrζnces, likes, and dislikes.  The formal  objεctiv1εs of the  lεsson are for
students to comprehend and producε 1νould rαt/Jer in mεaningful sentences and to
use a number of terms to categorize typεs of moviεs.

*  ESL  is  used in this book in two ways: (a) as  a generic acronym to refer to instruction of
English to speakers of other languages in any countπ under 없iy circumstance, and (b) to
rεfεr to English as a Second Language taught in countries (such as  the United States, the
United Kingdom, or India) where English is a major language of commerce and education, a
language that students often hear outside thε walls of their classroom.  Most instances of
rεference in this book to “ ESL'' are in the generic sense.  EFL (English as  a Foreign Language)
always refers specifically to En횡ish taught in  countries (such as Taiwan, Korea, or Brazil)
where En입ish is  not a major language of commercε and education.  See  Chaptεr 8 for
important pedagogical and curricular implications of each 다'Pe of English language
teaching.

CHAPTER  1  Getting Started

The  teacher,  Ms.  Lee,  a  native  of  Seoul,  has  about  five  years  of  teaching
experience, and  she  holds  a  certificate  in Teaching  English  to  Speakers  of Other
Languages (TESOL) from a local university in Seoul.  Her English is excellent, partly
the result of spending two years in Canada as a high school student while her father
was assigned work there for his electronics company.  She is confident and poised,
and shows a great deal of empathy for her students.  They seem to appreciate her
W값mth.

The lesson is reasonably well pl없med and executed, and characteristic of current
communicativ'ε  language-teaching  methodology.  However,  it  is  not  necessarily
“perfect” (are there ever any perfect lessons?), so what you are about to see may have
a few elements that you or others could take issue with.  Please remember this as you
read on and, if you wish, take note of aspects of the lesson that you  mi항1t question;
then compare these notes with the comments following the lesson description.

We  take  our  seats  in  the  rear  of  the  classroom  and  observe  the  following

sequence of activities.

1.  Ms. Lee (hereafter “T”) begins the SO-minute class hour on this Monday

evening with some small talk with the students (hereafter “ Ss”), commenting
on the weather, her own weekend’s activity hosting a family friend from
Canada, and a movie that several Ss saw (in English) over the weekend.
2.  As she engages them in small talk, she marks attendance in her class roster.
3.  She then asks the Ss to think of some movies they have seen recently, either

in English or subtitled.  She asks them not to n없ne 없1y movies that have bεen
dubbed (into Korean).  Ss volunteer names, somewhat hesitantly at first, but
come up with a list that the T puts on the board:

Harry  Potter
Pride and  Prejudice
March  of the Penguins
Mrs.  Henderson  Presents
Pink  Panther
Chicago
Ice Age
Good  Night and Good  Luck
War of the Worlds
Da Vinci  Code

4.  At this point the T stops and writes “Categories” on the board and then writes

the following movie types, or categories, on the board:

CHAPTER  1  Getting Started

5

Action
Comedy
Animated
Documentary
Drama
Mystery
Nature
Musical

5.  She then asks Ss  to 、rolunt℃er what each word means.  Onε by onε, Ss  slowly

venture “ dεfinitions ” for aηimated, comedy,  and documeηtaη with synonyms
such as “cartoon, Nemo, Disney” for the first, “funny, comic, makεs mε laugh" for
the second, and “ news, history” for the third.  They sεem hard-pressεd to definε
others.  Onε S says “Tom Cruisε” for action.  Ss  εventually fall  into silence.

6.  Sεεing that definitions may be too difficult for Ss  to creatε, th℃ T  takεs a

diffiεrεnt tack.  She provides her own definitions verbally, and as  somε Ss  nod
thεir hεads in apparent understanding, shε says, “ Okay, now, doεS ℃V℃ryone
understand thε meaning of each of thesε categories?” A few  morε heads nod,
and th℃ T moves on.

7.  Thε T  thεn says, “ Now, take out a  shεεt of paper and write down the names of
all the movies that ar'ε up on the board, and then with a partner, decide what
kind of movie each one is  and write the catεgory beside thε name of the
movie.” She quickly pairs up Ss, with onε group of threε.  Ss  write the movies
down, and proceed to engage in the pair work.  The T walks around listening
and checking on the pairs.

8.  Next, the T asks Ss  to report the movie categories.  Thεrε is  a  littlε

disagreemεnt among pairs in that some movies are  thought to bε in two or
more categories (Harη Potter,  for example, was thought to be action, drama,
and mysteηr).

9.  The T now says, “ I  want you to turn to page  18 in your books and listεn to a
dialoguε on my CD  play'εr  Just listen this first time.”  The following  dialoguε
is  thεn presented on the CD:

A:  What would you  rather see-a comedy or a  musical?
B:  It  doesn't matter to  me.
A:  Well,  what do you  think of Madonna?
B:  Actually,  not much.
A:  For  real?  She's  my  favorite  star.
B:  Not  mine.
A:  Well,  that's what makes the world  go  'round.

CHAPTER  1  Getting Started

10.  Next,T asks Ss  to listen again and repeat each line chorally in the pause
provided on the CD.  This procedure is repeated for a second time.

11.  The T then asks Ss  to turn to page  19 of their textbook, where examples are
given for the grammatical construction would rather in both statements and
questions:

Statements

I'd  rather  rent a  movie than  go  to the theater.
He’d  rather  not see a comedy tonight.

Questions

Would  you  rather see,  Star Wars  or  Frida?
Which  would they  rather see-a comedy or a drama?
Would  you  like  to  rent a movie?

12.  The T engages in some explanation of the structure, pointing out, for

example, that the phrase would rather is similar to saying “prefer.” She also
provides a rough Korean translation of the construction and gives a brief
explanation in Korean before reverting back to English.  Ss  remain attentive
but silent.

13.  N앉t, theT says, “ Now I want all of you to take your lists of the movies that

we discussed (the ones on the board) and make a grid like this."

Category

Would you  rather see it?
YES

Movie

Harry  Potter

Pride and Prejudice

March  of the  Penguins

Mrs.  Henderson  Presents

Pink  Panther

Chicago

Ice Age

Good  Night  &  Good  Luck

War of the Worlds

Da Vinci  Code

CHAPTER  1  Getting Started

7

14.  The T then directs Ss  to write in the categotγ or categories of each movie.

Next she says,“ Now, eveπone stand up and move around the room and talk
to as m따iy people as you can.  Choose two movies each time to compare, and
ask them, ‘Would you rather see _
, or ----?’ Then, write the name of
the person you talk to in the ‘yes’ box beside the movie thεy would rather
see.  Okay? Make sure your partner answers you in a complete sentence! So,
your partner must say ‘Yes, I’d  rather see ----’ or something like ‘'Actually, I
don’t  care.' Does everyone understand?” Ss  look a little confused, so T
translates the directions into Korean and then models in En밍ish as follows:

Student A:  Would you rather see Hatη Potter or Chicago?

Student B:  I’d  rather see Harry Poπ·er.

Student A then writes the name of Student B in the box by Haηy Potter.

Then Student B asks Student A a similar question and writes the answer
down.  Then you move on to a new partner.

But, listen carefully! If you don’t  have a preference, just answer., “It doesn’t
matter to me.” And in that case pick another pair of movies to compare until
your partner gives you a definite preference.

Okay, do you understand now?

15.  Ss  nod in agreement, and the T tells them to st따t their multiple interviews.

This exercise lasts for about 15  minutes as Ss  quite enthusiastically engage in
the task.

16.  When the T calls them back together, she tallies the number of students who
responded affirmatively to each movie and in an unscientific poll, announces
what appears to be their favorite  movie.  It’s a  tie between Haηy Potter and
War of the Wori떠s!

17.  With the time that’s  left (about 5 minutes) T asks Ss  to complete the exercise
on page 19 in which they write responses to six questions or statements,
such as “I’d  love to see a  movie toni양lt” and “Would you like to see a
comedy?”Their responses range from “I’d  rather not” and “ It doesn’t  matter to
me” to “'Actually, I’d  rather see an action movie.”

18.  As  time runs out and students gather papers together to exit the classroom,
the T tells Ss to complete their written exercise as homework, and to try to
see an English movie sometime before the next class (on Wednesday evening).

CHAPTER  1  Getting Started

ANALYZING THE LESSON

You’ve just obseπεd a relatively effective class hour in which the teacher competently
planned  a  lesson  around  a  textbook  lesson, managed  most  segments  of the  hour
without  major  problems,  and  carried  out  the  activities  with  some  warmth  and
enthusiasm.  Easy,  right?  Well,  maybe  not.  What  you  have  just  witnessed  is  the
product  of  a  teacher's  experience  and  intuition  grounded  in  reasonably  sound
theoretical  principles  of  learning  and  teaching.  For  eveπ tiny  moment  of  that
classroom hour, cεrtain choices were made, choices that can for  the most part be
justified by our collective knowledge of second language acquisition and teaching.
Think about those choices as you contemplate the numerous pedagogic따 questions
that arisε out of each numbered “statement” that follows.

1.  Why the small talk (versus just getting straight to the lesson)? ￦hat teaching
principlε justifies such an opening? How long should such chatter continue?
2.  Why did the T mark attendance while engaging in the small talk?  It apparently
didn’t  interfere with the small talk-how did theT manage to do two things
at once?

3.  The textbook began with the dialogue (see #9) that this T chose to insert

later.  Why do you suppose she didn’t  start with that dialogue? Was her choice
a better segue from the initial small t때k that began the class? What purpose
was served by asking Ss  to come up with names of movies themselves at the
outset? Why didn’t  the T just provide a list of her own? And if she simply
wants names of movies, why restrict the list to movies in En횡ish?What
purpose did that sεrve? She chose to write the names of movies on the
board-what purpose did that list serve?

4.  Here she initiated the names of the categories.  Should she have asked the Ss
to create that list? What you don’t  know is that the textbook referred to
“genres” of movies-why did the T not use that S없ne term?

5.  Why did the T ask Ss for definitions? Wouldn’t  it be more efficient for the T to
pro찌de them? What purpose was served by forcing them to struggle with
definitions? When Ss  had some difficulty with defining, they tended to
become more silent.  Why was that?

6.  At  this point it was apparent that T felt the task was over Ss’ heads-what led
her to that determination? Was it a good idea to switch to providing deftni
tions herself at that point? She then asked if everyone understood and seeing
some heads nodding affirmatively, she assumed they understood.  Is such a
ques다on appropriate in this situation? Are you sure the Ss  understood? ￦hat
alternatives might she have employed to caπy out that informal assessment?

Before  you  move  on,  notice,  that  each  question  implies  that  a  choice  was
exercised by the tεacher.  Among dozens of possibilities for teaching this lesson on
movies, categories, and the  Iνould rather construction, Ms.  Lee  has  chosen, either

[ HAPTER 1  Getting Started

9

consciously or subconsciously, a particular set of activitiεs, a particular order, and a
particular  tone  for  each.  A  rεlativεly  straightforward  lεsson  is  supportεd by  a
plethora  of principlεs of  Iεarning and  tεaching.  To  mπhεr complicate  matters,
somε of those principles arε disputable.  For examplε, the issuε of when to simply
give  information  to  Ss  ( #6) and when to  push for “ discovery learning” by the Ss  is
not always clearly dictated by the context.

7.  She now sεts in motion some pair work for Ss.  This ex℃rcise did not come
from thε tεxtbook; it was hεr own innovation, only distantly resεmbling one
in the textbook.  Why didn’t  shε just follow the book here? Werε hεr pair
work directions clεar? Some teachεr guidεlines suggεst modeling such pair
work-why didn’t  shε do so? What do you suppose she was listεning for as
she walked around thε classroom during this pair work?

8.  Why did the T havε Ss  rεport their rεsuits of such a  noncontrov,εrsial
℃Xεrcisε? What purposε did the reporting and processing serve?

9.  Thε T chose at this point to play the  opεning dialoguε for the lεsson.  Did the
background of thε first  10-15 minutεs of class provide enough contεxtand
intεrεst for  the Ss? What advantages and disadvantages do professionally
rεcorded audio sound bites offer in a classroom in this context? The dialoguε
isn’t  tεrribly exciting; is  that okay for the purposes of this lεsson?

10.  Choral drilling is a commonly used techniquε in language tεaching.  Was it
appropriate and useful h℃re? How do you think the T mentally justified its
use? Why  didn’t  thε drill continue for several more rεpetitions?

11.  This is one of thε moments in thε lεsson that the T turns Ss’ focus to form

that is, grammatical structure.  Doεs the textbook sεgment sufficiεntly εxplain
the structure?

12.  Is  the T’s  explanation justifiεd at this point? Or should Ss  just intuitively gεt a
“ feel ” for  thε would rather structure? And what do you think about providing
some explanation, as  the T did, in Korean? Why did she choose to do so thεn,
and was the languagε switch justifiεd? She seemed to be “lecturing” to Ss
hεre. Should shε havε asked explicitly for some kind of response from the Ss?
Or should th℃y hav,ε had some more choral or quasi-communicative practicε
at this point?

13.  Thε grid is an adaptation of a similar onε in  thε textbook, but the T added the
featur'ε of using it in face-to-face  intεrviεws.  Why did she choosε to have
another communicative activity here instead of following the textbook’s
suggestion of having Ss  listen to some movie reviews on the CD and write in
their recommεndations?

14.  The whole-class mingling activity seems simple enough, but Ss  had a little

difficulty figuring out thε process.  Wer1ε the T’s  dirεctions sufficient and clear,
once shε was able to follow up aftεr the looks of confusion? What could she
have done to makε this stage of the activity clearεr?

10

CHAPTER  1  Getting Staπed

15.  What is the objective of this activiη? It’s clear what Ss are being asked to do:

frame questions, respond to them, and record the responses.  They seemed
enthusiastic about the acti띠ty-why? Why was an activity with fairly routine
grammatical practice met with enthusiasm? Were those 15 minutes put to
good purpose?

16.  Did the informal tally serve the objectives of the activity or simply offer a

modicum of interest?

17.  It’s possible that this last activity was squeεzed into too short a time frame.

Was that okay? When a T runs out of time at the end of a lesson, should he or
she hurry through an activity like this? Or provide an 외ternative wrap-up?
What purpose did a writing activity (as opposed to the other threε S셈lls)
seπe here?

18.  Sometimes these last-second comments ar'ε lost in the shuffle of students get
ting ready to leave the classroom.  Was some purpo똥 nevertheless accom-
plished? If they are being asked to see an English movie as “homework,”
would it help to give them some more advice on what to do while seeing the
mo찌e?

A final  question: As you look back over the lesson you’ve  just observed, do you
think the initial objectives were accomplished? Is there anything you think you might
have done differently? Remember, you’re dropping in on a class that is ongoing, so it
may not bε possi비e to completely judge the effectiveness of this lesson without the
context of preceding and following lessons.

You’ve now skimmed throu맹 some of the many questions that one could ask
about why certain choices were made about how to teach this lesson.  Some of the
answers are relatively standard, with few disagreements.  Other answεrs would find
even the best of teachers arguing the merits and demerits of the teacher's choices.
But the answers to all these questions can be found, in one form or another, in the
huge stockpile of second language acquisition resεarch and collective experience of
language teachers around the world.  And many of those answers will appear in the
chapters ahead of you in this book.

As  you continue this journey, your job is  to make the connections between
research/theory/principles  on the  one  hand, and  classrooms/teaching/practice
on  the  other.  By  making  those  connections  as  you  learn  to  teach,  you  will
perhaps avoid some of the pitfalls of haphazard guesswork and instead engage in
teaching that is enlightened by research and theory, or put another way, teaching
by principles.

APPROACH, METHOD, AND TECHNIQUE

For  the  century  spanning  thε mid-1880s  to  the  mid-1980s,  the  languagε-tεaching
profession may bε aptly characterizεd by a series of methods that rosε and declinεd
in popularity.  It appεars that somε practitioners in this time period hopεd to define
the  ultimate  method,  one  that  would  be  generalizable  across  widεly varying
audiences, contexts, and  languages.  Historical  accounts  of the  profession  tend  to
describe a  succession of mεthods, each of which was  morε or less  discarded as  a
new method took its place (Larsen-Freeman, 2000; Richards &  Rodgεrs, 2001).  Wε
will turn to that “ methodical” history of languagε teaching in a moment, but first, wε
should try to undεrstand what we mean by method.

What  is  a method? About four decades ago  Edward Anthony (1963) gave  us  a
definition that has admirably withstood the test of time.  His  concept of “method”
was  the  second  of  three  hiεrarchical  elements,  namεly approach,  method,  and
technique.  An  approach, according  to Anthony, was a set of assumptions dealing
with the  naturε of language, learning, and teaching.  Method was described  as an
overall  plan  for  systεmatic  presentation  of  language  based  upon  a  select℃d
approach.  Techniques  were  the  spεcific  activitiεs manifested  in  the  classroom
that  were  consistent  with  a  method  and  therefor,ε  were  in  harmony  with  an
approach as  well.

To this day, for better or worse,Anthony’S  terms are still in common use among
language  teachers.  For  example, at  thε approach  levεl, a  teacher  may  affirm  thε
ultimate importance of learning in a  relaxed  statε of mental  awarenεss just abov,ε
the  threshold  of  consciousness.  The  method  that  follows  might  rεsemble, say,
Suggestopedia  (a  description  follows  in  this  chapter).  Tεchniques could  include
playing  baroque  music  while  reading  a  passage  in  the  foreign  language,  getting
students to sit in a yoga position while listεning to a list of words, or having learners
adopt a nεw name in the classroom and role-play that new person.

A  couple  of  decades  later,  Jack  Richards  and  Theodore  Rodgers  (1982)
proposed a reformulation of the concεpt of“method.” Anthony’S  approach, mεthod,
and  tεchnique were  renamed,  respectively,  approach,  design,  and  procedure,
with a superordinate term to describe this three-step process, now called “ method.”
A  method,  according  to  Richards  and  Rodgers,  was  “an  umbrella  tεrm for  the
specification and interrelation of theory and practice”(1982, p. 154).  An approach
defines assumptions, beliefs, and theories about the nature of language and language
learning.  Dεsigns specify the relationship of those theories to classroom materials
and activities.  Procεdures ar'ε the techniques 따id practices that are  derived from
one’s approach and design.

15

Through  their  reformulation,  Richards  and  Rodgers  (1982,  2001)  made  two

principal contributions to our undεrstanding of thε concept of method:

1.  Thεy spεcified the necessary elements of language-teaching designs that had
heretofore bεεn left somεwhat vague.  π1εir schematic represεntation of
method (sεc Figure 2.1) describεd six important features of designs:
objεctiv1εs, syllabus (criteria for selection and organization of linguistic and
subject-matter content), activities, learner roles, tεachεr roles, and the rolε of
instructional materials.  The latter three features have occupied a significant
proportion of our collεctiv1ε attεntion in the profession for the last decade or
so.  Already in this book you may hav,ε noted how, for example, learner roles
(styles, individual prεferences for group or individual learning, studεnt input
in determining curricular content, etc.) arε important considεrations in your
teaching.

2.  Richards and Rodgers nudged us into relinquishing thε notion that sεparate,
definablε, discrete methods are the essential building blocks of methodology.
By  helping us to think in terms of an approach that undεrgirds our language
dεsigns (curricula), which are realized by various procedurεs (techniquεs), we
could see that method, as the term was historically understood over thε last
centuη, is a concept that is  too restrictiv1ε, too pr'εprogrammed, and too
“ prepackaged." M따iy of the methods that form our historical milestones makε
the oversimplified assumption that what tεache rs “do” in the classroom can
be conventionalized into a set of procedures that훨t all contexts.  We  are now
all  too aware that such is clearly not thε casε.

Richards  and Rodgεrs’s rεformulation of the concept of method was soundly
conceived.  However,  their  attempt  to  give  new meaning  to  an  old  term  did  not
catch  on  in  the  pedagogical  literature.  What  they wanted  us  to  call “ method”  is
more comfortably ref<εrr'εd to, I think, as “methodology” in order to avoid confusion
with  what  we  W피 no  doubt  always  think  of as  thosε separate  εntities (like  thε
Audiolingual  Method  or  Sugg'εstopedia)  that  are  no  longer  at  the  center  of our
tεaching philosophy.

Another terminological problem lies in the use of thε term design; instead, we
mor1ε comfortably  rεfer  to  curriculum  or  syllabus  whεn we  rεfer  to  dεsign
fεatur,εs of a language pro!양am.

What are we left with in this lexicographic confusion? It’S interesting that thε
terminol앵Y of the pedagogical literatur1ε in the field appears to be mor'ε in line with
Anthony’s  original  terms,  but  with  some  important  additions  and  rεfinεments.
Following is  a  set of dεfinitions that as  closely as possible reflect what appears to
be a consensus on cumεnt usage αfarmer, 2001; Kumaravadivelu, 2006b; Richards &

Methodology:  Pedagogical  practices  in  general  (including  theoretical
underpinnings and related research).  Whatever considεrations are involved in “ how
to teach" ar,ε mεthodological.

Approach: Theoretically well-informed positions and beliefs about the nature
of  languagε, the  nature  of  languagε  lεaming, and  the  applicab피ty  of  both  to
pedagogical sεttings.

Method:  A  genεralized  set  of  classroom  specifications  for  accomplishing
linguistic  objectiv1εs.  Methods  tend  to  be  concerned  primarily  with  teacher  and
studεnt roles  and  behaviors  and  sεcondarily with  such  features  as  linguistic  and
subjεct-mattεr objectives, sequεncing, and matεrials.  They ar'ε sometimes-but not
always-thought of as being broadly applicable to a variety of audiences in a variεty
of contεxts.

Curriculum/syllabus: Specifications-or in Richards and Rodgers’S terminology,
“ designs” -for C따πing out a p따ticular l따iguagε program.  Features include a prim따y
concern with the specification of ling버stic 때d subjεct-matter objeαives, sequencing,
and materials to meet the needs of a designated group of learners in a defined context.
(The tεrm “syllabus” is  used more customarily in the United Kingdom to refer to what
is commonly called a “ curriculum” in thε U띠tεd Statεs.)

Technique (also commonly rεfεrrεd to by other terms•끼 Any of a wide variety
of exercisεs, activities, or tasks  usεd in  the language  classroom for realizing lεsson

objεctives.

CHANGING WINDS AND  SHπfINGSANDS

Aglancε through thε past centuη or so of language tεaching will give an interesting
picture  of how  varied  thε interpretations  have  been  of the  bεst way  to  teach  a
forεign language.  As  disciplinary schools  of thought-psychology, linguistics, and
εducation, for  examplε-have come and gone, so hav,ε languagε-teaching mεthods
waxεd and waned in popularity.  Teaching methods, as “ approaches in action,녕re of
coursε thε practical application of theoretical findings and positions.  In a field such
as  ours  that  is  relatively young, it  should  come  as  no  surprise  to  discover a  wide

*까iere is currently quite an intermingling of such terms as “technique,”“task,”
“ procedure’’,
across the profession.  Of these terms’ task has received the most concerted attention,
viewed by such scholars as Nunan (2004) and Ellis (2003) as incorporating specific
communicative and pedago잉cal principlεs.  Tasks, according to spεcialists in task-b싫εd
instruction, should be thought of as a speci따 kind of technique and, in fact, may aαu따1y
includε more than one tεchnique.  See Chapter 3 for a more thorou양1 expl없la디on.

CHAPTER  2  A  "Methodical" History of Language Teaching

variety of these applications over the last hundred years, some in total philosophical
opposition to others.

Albert Marckwardt (1972, p. 5) saw these “ changing winds and shifting sands"
as  a  cyclical  pattern  in  which  a  new method  emerged  about  eveη quarter  of a
century.  Each new method broke from the old but took with it some of the positive
aspects  of  the  previous  practices.  A  good  example  of  this  cyclic때 nature  of
methods is  found  in the “revolutionary” Audiolingual Method (ALM)  (a description
follows)  of  the  mid-twentieth  centurγ.  The  ALM  borrowed  tenets  from  its
predecessor  thε Direct  Method  by  almost  h때f a  centurγ while  brεaking away
entirely from thε Grammar Translation Method.  Within a short time, however, ALM
critics  were  advocating  more  attention  to  thinking,  to  cognition,  and  to  rule
learning, which to some smacked of a return to Grammar Translation!

What follows is a sketch of the changing winds and shifting sands of language

teaching over the years.

1HE GRAMMAR TRANSIATION MEIBOD

A historical sketch of the last hundred years of language teaching must be sεt in the
context of a prevailing, customary language-teaching “tradition.” For centuries, there
were few if any theoretical foundations  of language learning upon which to base
teaching methodology.  In the Western world,“foreign ” language learning in schools
was synonymous  with  the learning  of Latin  or Greek.  Latin, thought  to  promote
intellectualiη through “mental gymnastics,” was until relatively recently held to be
indispensable to an adequate higher education.  Latin was taught by means of what
has been called the Classical Method: focus  on grammatical rules, memorization
of vocabulary  and  of various  declensions  and  conjugations, translations  of texts,
written exercises.

As  other  languages  began  to  be  taught  in  educational  institutions  in  the
eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, the Classical Method was adopted as the chief
me없is  for  teaching  f야’eign  languages.  Little  thought  was  given  to  teaching
someone  how  to  speak  the  language;  after  all,  languages  were  not  being  taught
prim따ily  to  learn  oral/aural  communication, but  to  learn  for  the  sake  of being
“scholarly'’ or,  in  some  instances,  for  g찌ning a  reading  proficiency  in  a  foreign
l없iguage.  Since  there  was  little  if any  theoretical  research  on  second  language
acquisition in genεral or on the acq띠si다on of reading proficiency, foreign languages
were taught as any other skill was tau항lt.

In  the  nineteenth  cent따y the  Classical  Method  C따ne to  be  known  as  thε
Grammar  Translation  Method.  There  was  little  to  distinguish  Grammar
Translation  from  what had  gone  on in foreign  la맹uage classrooms  for  cεnturies
beyond a focus on grammatical rules as the basis for translating from the second to
the  native  language.  Remarkably,  the  Grammar  Translation  Method  withstood
attempts  at  야ie  turn  of  the  twentieth  centuηr to  “reform”  language-teaching
methodology (see Gouin’s Series Method 따id the Direct Method, which follow), and

CHAPTER  2  A  ιMeth。dical ” History of Language Teaching

19

to this day it is practiced in too m따iy εducational contexts.  Prator and Celce-Murcia
(1979, p. 3) listεd the major characteristics of Grammar Translation:

1.  Classes  are  taught  in  the  mother  tongue, with  little  activ,ε  use  of the  target

language.

2.  Much vocabulary is taught in the form of lists of isolatεd words.
3.  Long, elaborate explanations of the intricacies of grammar ar1ε given.
4.  Grammar providεs the rules for putting words together, and instruction often

focuses on the form and inflection of words.
5.  Reading of difficult classical tεxts is  begun early.
6.  Little attention is  paid to the content of texts, which are treatεd as εxercises

in grammatical analysis.

7.  Often the only drills are exercises in translating disconnεcted sentences from

the targ'℃t language into the mother tongue.
8.  Littlε or no attention is given to pronunciation.

It’s ironic that this method has until very recently been so stalwart among many
competing models.  It doεs virtually nothing to enhance a student’s communicative
ability  in  the  language.  It  is “remembered  with  distaste  by  thousands  of  school
learnεrs,  for  whom  foreign  language  learning  mεant a  tedious  experience  of
memorizing εndless lists of unusable grammar rulεs and vocabulary and attempting
to  producε perfect  translations  of stilted  or  literaπ prose” (Richards  &  Rodgers,
2001,p. 6).

On the other hand, one can understand why Grammar Translation  rεmains so
popular.  It requires few specializεd skills on the part of teachers.  Tests of grammar
rules and of translations are easy to construct and can be objectively scorεd.  Many
standardized  tests  of  foreign  languages  still  do  not  attempt  to  tap  into
communicative abilities, so  studεnts havε little  motivation  to  go  beyond grammar
analogies, translations, and rote exεrcises.  And it is sometimes successful in leading
a  student toward a  reading  knowledgε of a second language.  But, as  Richards and
Rodgers (2001, p. 7) pointed out,“ it has no advocates.  It is a mεthod for which there
is no theoπ.  There is no literature that offers a rationale or justification for it or that
attempts to relate it to issues in linguistics, psychology, or educational thεory.”As you
continue to εxamine language-teaching methodology in this book, I think you will
understand more fully the “ theorylessness” of the Grammar Translation Method.

GOUIN AND THE SERIES METHOD

The history of“ modern'’ foreign language teaching may be said to have begun in the
latε 1800s with Frarn;;ois Gouin, a French teacher of Latin with remarkable insights.
History  doεsn ’t  normally  credit  Gouin  as  a  founder  of  language-teaching
methodology  because,  at  the  time,  his  influencε was  ov,εrshadowed by  that  of
Charles  Berlitz, the popular German  foundεr of the  Direct  Mεthod.  Nevertheless,

20

[ HAPTER 2  A  "Methodical” History of Language Teaching

some  attεntion  to  Gouin’S  unusually  perceptive  observations  about  language
teaching  helps  us  to  set  thε  stagε  for  the  dεvelopment  of  language-teaching
methods for thε century following the publication of his book, The Art of Learηiηg
and Studying Languαges, in 1880.

Gouin  had  to  go  through  a  painful  set  of expεriences to  derive  his  insights.
Having  decided  in  midlife  to lεarn German, he took up  residency in Hamburg for
one year.  But rather than attempting to converse with the natives, he engaged in a
rathεr  bizam℃ sequence  of  attεmpts  to  “master”  the  language.  Upon  arrival  in
Hamburg, he felt  he should memorize a  German grammar book and a  tablε of the
248 irregular Gεrman vεrbs! He did this in a matter of only  10 days, and hurriεd to
“ the acadεmy” (th℃ univ1εrsity) to test his  new knowledge.  “ But alas! ” he wrote, “I
could not understand a sin!깎ε word, not a single word! ” (Gouin, 1880, p. 11).  Gouin
was undaunted.  Hε rεturned to the isolation of his room, this time to memorize thε
German roots and to rεm℃morize the gramm따 book and irrεgular verbs.  Again hε
emεrged with  εxpectations of success.  “But  alas  .. -”  the  result  was  thε same  as
before.  In  th℃ course of the year in Germany, Gouin mεmorizεd books, translatεd
Goεthe and Schiller, and even memorized 30,000 words in a G℃rman dictionary, all

But  thεrε was  a  happy ending.  After  returning home, Gouin  discovered that

So  Gouin set about devising a  tεaching method based on thεse insights.  And

(without
translation)  and  conceptually  (without  grammatical  rules  and

door.  I gεt to the door.  I stop at thε door.

I stretch out my arm.  I take hold of th℃ handle.  I turn the handle.  I

THE DIRECT MEffiOD

The  “naturalistic” -simulating  the  “natural”  way  in  which  childrεn  lεam first
languages-approaches of Gouin and a few of his contemporaries did not take hold
immediately.  A generation later, applied linguistics finally establishεd the credibility
of such approaches.  Thus it was that at the turn of thε centuη, thε Direct Method
bεcame quite widely known and practiced.

Thε basic  premise  of the  Direct Method was sinillar to  that of Gouin’S  Series
Method, namely, that sεcond language  learning  should bε more  like  first  language
learning-lots of oral  int℃raction, spontan℃ous use  of thε language, no  translation
bεtweεn first  and second languages, and little  or no analysis  of grammatical rules.
Richards and Rodgers (2001,p.12) summarized the principles of thε Dirεct Method:

1.  Classroom instruction was conducted exclusively in the target languagζ
2.  Only everyday vocabulaη and sentences were tau양lt.
3.  Oral communication skills were built up in a  carεfully traded progrεssion
organized around question-and-answεr exchanges betweεn teachers and
students in small, intensive classεs.

4.  Grammar was taught inductiv·εly.
5.  Nεw tεaching points were taught through modeling and practice.
6.  Concrete vocabulary was taught through demonstration, objects, and pictures;

abstract vocabulary was taught by association of idεas.
7.  Both speεch and listening comprehεnsion wεre taught.
8.  Cornεct pronunciation and grammar wεre emphasiz℃d.

22

CHAPTER 2  A  "Methodical” History of Language 1농·aching

πie Direct  Method  enjoyed  considerable  popularity  at  the  beginning  of the
twentieth century.  It was most widely accepted in private language schools where
students  were  hi양tly  motivated  and  where  native-speaking  teachers  could  be
employed.  One  of the  best  known  of its  popularizers  was  Charles  Berlitz  (who
never used the term Direct Method and chose instead to c삶l his method the Berlitz
Method).  To  this  day “Berlitz”  is  a  household  word; Berlitz  language  schools  are
thriving in every countπ of the world.

But  almost  any “method”  can  succeed  when  clients  are  willing  to  pay  high
prices for small classes, individual attention, and intensive study.  The Direct Method
did not take well in public education, where the constraints of budget, classroom
size, time, and teacher background made such a method difficult to use.  Moreover,
the Direct Method was  criticized for  its weak theoretical foundations.  Its success
may have been more a factor of the skill and personality of the teacher than of the
methodology itself.

By the end of the first  quarter of the twentieth centuη, the use of the Direct
Method  had  declined  both  in  Europe  and  in  the  United  States.  Most  language
curricula returned to the Grammar Translation Method or to a “reading approach”
that emphasized reading skills in foreign languages.  But it is interesting that by the
middle of the twentieth century, the Direct Method was revived and redirected into
what  was  probably  the  most  visible  of all  language-teaching “revolutions”  in  the
modern era, the Audiolingual  Method (see  below).  So  even  this  somewhat short
lived movement in language  teaching would reappear in the  chan핑ng winds and
shifting sands of histotγ.

THE AUDIOLINGUAL MEIBOD

In the first h따f of the twentieth centutγ, the Direct Method did not take hold in the
United States the way it did in Europe.  ￦hile one could easily find native-speaking
teachers of modern foreign languages in Europe, such was not 야ie case in the United
States.  Also, European high school and university students did not have to travel far
to find  opportunities to put the oral skills  of another language  to actual, praαic때
use.  Moreover,  U.S.  educational  institutions  had  become  firmly  convinced  that  a
reading approach to foreign languages was more useful than an oral approach, given
the  perceived  파iguistic  isolation  of  the  United  States  at  the  time.  The  hi힐피y
influential  Coleman  Report  (Coleman,  1929)  had  persuaded  foreign  language
teachers that it was impractical to teach oral skills and that reading should become
the focus.  Thus schools returned in the  1930s and 1940s to Grammar Translation,
“the handmaiden of rea띠ng” (Bowen, Madsen, & Hilferty, 1985).

Then World War II broke out, and suddettly the United States was thrust into a
worldwide conflict, heightening the need for Americans to become orally proficient
in  the  languages  of both their  allies  and  their enemies.  The  time  was  ripe  for  a
language-teaching revolution.  The U.S. military pro찌ded the impetus with funding

23

rεvived national  intεrεst in  foreign  languages  spurred  educational  institutions  to

The  Audiolingual  Method  (ALM)  was  firmly  grounded  in  linguistic  and

tεaching  mεthodologists  saw  a  direct  application  of  such  analysis  to  teaching

that  W℃rε  pεrf1℃ctly  married  with  the  mimicry  drills  and  pattern  practices  of
audiolingual methodology.

Th℃  charactεristics  of  the  ALM  may  be  summed  up  in  the  following  list

(adapted from Prator & Celeε-Murcia, 1979):

1.  Nεw material is  pr,εsεntεd in dialoguε form.
2.  Thεrε is  dεpεndence on mimicry, memorization of sεt phrases, and

overlεarning.

3.  Structur'εs are sequenced by mεans of contrastive analysis and taught one at a

4.  Structural patterns arε taught using repetitivε drills.
5.  There is  little or no grammatical explanation.  Grammar is  taught by inductive

6.  Vocabulary is  strictly limited and learned in context.
7.  There is  much use of tapes, language labs, and visual aids.
8.  Great importance is  attached to pronunciation.
9.  Vεry little usε of thε mother tongue by teachεrs is  permittεd.

10.  Successful rεsponses ar'ε immediately rεinforced.
11.  There is a great εffort to get students to produce error-free utterancεs‘
12.  There is  a tendency to manip띠ate language and disregard content.

For a  number of reasons, thε ALM  enjoy'εd many years of popularity, and even

ALM  was  firmly  rooted  in  respεctable  theoretical  perspectives  of  the  time.

revolution  in  linguistics  turnεd linguists  and  language  tεachers toward  thε “dεεp
structure” of language.  Increasing interest in generative  transformational grammar

The  profession  needed  some  spice  and  vervε, and  innovative  minds  in  thε

spirited 1970s were up to the challenge.

The  decade  of the  1970s  was  histori때1y significant  on  two  counts.  First,  perhaps

CHAPTER  2  A  “Methodical” History of Language Teaching

discip파1e  in  its  own  right.  As  more  scholars  speci외ized  their  efforts  in  second
l따1guagε acquisition studies, our knowledge of how people learn languages inside and

“ designer”  methods-to  borrow  a  teπn from  Nunan  (1989a,  p.  9끼-were soon
markεted  by  εntrepreneurs  as  thε  latest  and  greatεst  applications  of  the

audiolingual sleep, and for their stimulation of even more research as we sought to

thεy would be.  The scrutiny that the designer methods underwent has enabled us

1. Community Language Learning

By  thε decade of the  1970s, as  wε incrεasingly recognizεd thε importancε of
the affective domain, some innovativε methods took on a distinctly afuεctivε nature.
Community  Language  Learning  is  a  classic  example  of  an  afuεctively  based
method.

In  what  he  call℃d the  “ Counsεling-Leaming”  model  of  education,  Charles
Curran (1972) was inspired by Carl Rogers’S view of εducation (PLLT,  Chapter 4) in
which  learners  in  a  classroom  were  rεgardεd not as  a “class” but as  a “group” -a
group  in  need  of certain  thεrapy and  counseling.  The  social  dynamics  of such  a
group werε of primary importance.  In order for any learning to take  place, group
membεrs first  nεεdεd to intεract in an intεrpersonal relationship in which students
and  tεachεr joinεd together  to  facilitate  learning  in  a  contεxt of  valuing  εach
individual in  the group.  In such a  surrounding, each person lowered the dεfεnsεs
that  prεvent  open  intεrp℃rsonal  communication.  The  anxiεty  caus℃d by  the
εducational contεxt was  lεssened by  means  of  th℃ supportive  community.  The
teacher’s  presencε was not perceived as  a  threat, nor was it thε teacher’s  purpose
to impose limits and boundaries, but rather, as a true counselor, to cεnter his or her
attention  on  the  cliεnts (the  students)  and  their needs.  “ Defensiv1ε” learning was
made  unnecessary by  the  empathetic  relationship  between  teach℃r and  studεnts.
Curran’s  Counseling-Learning model of education thus capit싫ized on the primacy
of thε needs  of the  learners-clients-who gathered  together in  thε educational
community to be counsεled.

Curran’S  Counseling-Learning model of education was extended to languagε­

learning  contexts  in  the  form  of  Community  Language  Learning  (CLL).  Whilε
particular adaptations of CLL  wer1ε numerous, thε basic mεthodology was explicit.
Thε group  of  clients  (for  instancε, beginning  learners  of  English),  having  first
established in their native languag1ε (say,Japanese) an intεrpersonal relationship and
trust,  wer,ε seated  in  a  circlε with  thε counselor  (teachεr) on  the  outside  of the

26

CHAPTER  2  A  "Methodical" History of Language Teaching

circle.  When  one  of the  clients  wished  to  say  something  to  the  group  or to  an
individual, he  or she  said  it  in  the  native  language  Qapanese)  and  the  counselor
translated the utterance back to the learner in the second language (English).  까ie
learner  then  repeated  that  En웰sh sentence  as  accurately  as  possible.  Another
client  responded, in Japanese; the  utterance  was  translated  by the  counselor into
English;  the  client  repeated  it;  and  the  conversation  continued.  If possible  the
conversation  was  taped  for  later  listening,  and  at  the  end  of each  session,  the
learners  inductively  attempted  together  to  밍ean  information  about  the  new
language.  If desirable, the counselor might take a  more directive role and provide
some expl없iation of certain 파iguistic rules or items.

The  first  stage  of intense  struggle  and  confusion  mi양it  continue  for  many
sessions, but always  with the support of the counselor and  of the  fellow  clients.
Gradually the learner became able to speak a word or phrase directly in the foreign
language, without translation.  This was the first sign of the learner’s  moving away
from  complete  dependence  on  the  counselor.  As  the  learners  g없ned more  and
more familiarity  with the foreign  l없iguage, more  따id more  direct  communication
could take place, with the counselor providing less and less direct translation 때d
information.  After m따iy sessions, perhaps m없iy months or years later, the learner
achieved fluency  in the  spoken language.  The  learner had  at  that  point  become
independent.

CLL  reflected not only the principles of Carl  Rogers’s  view of education, but
also basic principles of the dynamics of counseling in which the counselor, through
careful attention to the client’s  needs, aids the client in moving from dependence
때d helplessness to independence and self assurance.

There were advantages and disadvantages to a method like CLL.  The affective
advantages  were  evident.  CLL  was  an  attempt  to  put  Rogers’s  philosophy  into
action and to overcome some of the threatening affective factors in second language
learning.  The threat of the all-knowing teacher, of making blunders in the foreign
l없iguage in front of classmates, of competing against peers-all threats that can lead
to  a  feeling  of  alienation  and  inadequacy-were  presumably  removed.  The
counselor allowed the learner to determine the type of conversation and to 때alyze
the fore핑n l때guage inductively.  In situations in which explan따ion or translation
seemed to be impossible, it was often the client-learner who stepped in and became
a counselor to 따d the motivation 없id capit따ize on intrinsic motivation.

There were some practic때 and theoretical problems with CLL.  The counselor-」
teacher  could  become  too  nondirective.  The  student  often  needed  direction,
especially  in  the first  stage, in  which  there  was  such  seemingly  endless  struggle
within the foreign language.  Supportive but assertive direction from the counselor

inductive strategy of learning.  It is well accepted that deductive learning is both a
viable and efficient strategy of learning and that ad띠ts p았ticularly can benefit from
deduction  as  well  as  induction.  While  some  intense  inductive  struggle  is  a
necessary  component  of second  language  learning,  the  initial  grueling  days  and

weεks of floundering  in  ignorance  in  CLL  could  be  alleviated  by  more  directed,

2. Suggestopedia

0thεr newmεthods of the  dεcadε wer'ε not quite as  strictly  aff;εctiv'ε as  CLL.
Su잃estopedia,  for  examplε, was  amεthod that  was  derived  from  Bulgarian

procεss great quantities of material if given thε right conditions for learning, among

Iρzanov, people are capable of learning much more than they give  themselves credit

refer  to  “Desuggestopedia”  to  capture  the  importancε  placεd  on  desuggesting
limitations on lεarning.  Learners all  too often feel  that learning a foreign language is
SO  OV1εrwhelmingly difficult that they can never be succεssful.

Drawing  on  insights  from  Soviet  psychological  rεsearch  on  εxtrasenso1γ
perception and from yoga, Lozanov  erεated amεthod for  learning that capitalized
on rεlaxed statεs of mind for maximum retention of matεrial.  Music was cεntral to

In applications of Suggestopedia to foreign language learning, Lozanov and his
followers  ε:xperimεnted with  the  presεntation of vocabulary,  readings, dialogues,
rolε plays, drama, and  a  variεty of other typical  classroom activities.  Some  of mε
classroom methodolo밍r was not particularly unique.  The primaπ diff;εrεnce lay in
a  significant proportion of activity carri℃d out in soft, comfortable seats in relaxed

yiεlding all authority to the teacher and sometimes assuming the roles (and namεs)
of  native  spεakers of  thε foreign  language.  Studεnts thus  became  “S맹gestible.'’
Lozanov  (1979, p. 272)  describεd thε concert session portion of a  Suggestopedia
language class:

28

CHAPTER  2  A  “Methodical” History of Language Teaching

At the beginning of the session, 때l conv,εrsation stops for a minut℃ or
two, and the teacher listens to thε music coming from a tape-rεcorder.
Hε waits  and  listens  to  several  passages  in  ordεr to  enter  into  thε
mood of the music and thεn begins to read or recite thε nεw text, his
voice modulatεd in harmony with the musical phrases.  The studεnts
