the mother tongue.  Betweεn the first and second part of the concert,
there  are  sev1εral  minutes  of  solemn  silεnce.  In  some  cases,  ev'εn
longer  pausεs can  be  given  to  pεrmit thε studεnts to  stir  a  little.
Bεforε the  beginning  of the  sεcond part  of thε concεrt, there  are
again  several  minutes  of silence and  somε phrases  of the  music  arε
heard  again  before  the  teacher  begins  to  read  the  text.  Now  the
students close their textbooks and listen to the teacher's reading.  At
thε end, the students silently leave the room.  They are not told to do
any homework on the lesson thεy have just had except for reading it
cursorily once before going to bed and again before getting up in thε
mornmg.

Suggestopedia  was  criticized  on  a  number of fronts.  Scovel  (1979)  showed
quite eloquently that Lozanov’s experimental data, in which he reported astounding
results with Suggestopedia, were highly questionable.  Moreover, the practicality of
using  Suggestopedia  is  an  issue  when  music  and  comfortable  chairs  are  not
available.  More  serious  is  the  issue  of  the  place  of  memorization  in  language
learning.  Scovel (1979, pp. 260-261) notεd that Lozanov’s “innumerablε rεfεrεnces
to ... memorization ... to the total exclusion of references to ‘understanding’ and/or
‘creative solutions of problems' convincεs this rεviewer at least that suggεstopedy
‘  . .  is  an attempt to  teach memorization techniques and is  not devoted to the far
more comprehensive enterprise of language acquisition." On the other hand, other
researchers,  including  Schiff!εr  (1992,  p.  xv),  have  suggested  a  more  modεrate
position  on  Suggestopedia,  hoping  “to  prevent  the  exaggerated  expectations  of
Suggestopedia that have been promoted in some publications.”

Like  some  other  designer  methods  (CLL  and  the  Silent  Way,  for  example),
Suggestopedia became a business enterprise of its own, and it made promises in thε
advertising world that were not  completely supported by research.  Despite  such
dubious claims, Suggestopedia gave the language-teaching profession some insi압1ts.
We  lεarned  to  believe  in  the  powεr of  the  human  brain.  We  learned  that
deliberately induced states  of relaxation  may  be beneficial in the  classroom.  And
numεrous teachers have  at  times  experimented with various  forms  of music  as  a
way to get students to sit back and relax.

3.  The Silent Way

Like  Suggestopedia, the  Silent Way  restεd on  more  cognitivε than  affective
argumεnts for  its  theoretical  sustenance.  While  Caleb  Gattegno, its  founder,  was
said to be interested in a “ humanistic” approach (Chamot &  Mc.Kiεon, 1984, p. 2) to

깐!APTER 2  A  “Methodical’  History of Language Teaching

29

education, much of the Silent Way was characterized by a problem-s이ving approach
to le따ning.  Richards and Rodgers (2001, p. 81) summarized the theory of learning
behind the Silent Way:

1.  Learning is facilitated if the learner discovers or creates rather than

remembers 따1d repeats what is  to be learned.

2.  Learning is facilitated by accompanying (mediating) physical objects.
3.  Learning is facilitated by problem solving involving the material to be learned.

“Discovery learning,” a popular educational trend of the 1960s, advocated less
learning “by being told” and more learning by discovering for oneself various facts
and principles.  In this way, students constructed  conceptu따 hierarchies  of their
own  that  were  a  product  of  the  time  they  invested.  Ausubel’S  “subsumption”
(PLLT,  Chapter  4)  was  enhanced  by  discovery  learning  since  the  cognitive
categories  were  created  meaningfully  with  less  chance  of  rote  learning  taking
place.  Inductive  processes  were  also  encouraged  more  in  discovery-learning
methods.

The  Silent Way  capitalized  on such discovery-learning  procedures.  Gattegno
(1972)  believed  that  learners  should  develop  independence,  autonomy,  and
responsib피ty.  At the same time, learners in a Silent Way classroom had to cooperate
with  each  other  in  the  process  of solving  language  problems.  The  teacher-a
stimulator but not a hand-holder-was silent much of the time, thus the name of the
method.  Teachers had to  resist their instinct to spell  eveπthing out in black and
white, to come to the aid of students at the slightest downfall; they had to “get out
of the way” while students worked out solutions.

In a  language classroom, the Silent Way  typically utilized as  materials  a  set of
Cuisenaire  rods-small  colored  rods  of varying  lengths-and  a  series  of colorful
wall  charts.  The  rods  were  used  to  introduce  vocabulary  ( c이ors,  numbers,
adjectives  [loη!g,  short,  and so  on]), verbs  (giνe,  take,  pick  up,  drop), and  하rntax
(tense, comparatives, pluralization, word ordeι and the like).  The teacher provided
sin윌e-word stimuli  or short  phrases  and  sentences  once  or  twice, and  then  the
students  refined  their  understanding  and  pronunciation  among  themselves  with
minimal  corrective  feedback  from
introduced
pronunciation models, grammatical p따'3.digms, and the like.

teacher.  The  charts

the

Like  Su잃.estopedia, the Silent Way has had its share of criticism.  In one sen않,
the Silent Way was too harsh a method, and the teacher too distant, to encourage a
communicative  atmosphere.  Students  often  need  more  guidance  and  overt
correction  than  the  Silent  Way  permitted.  There  are  a  number  of  aspects  of
language that can indeed be “told” to students to their benefit; they need not, as  in
αL as well, struggle for hours or days with a concept that could be easily clarified
by the teacher's direct guidance.  The rods and charts wear thin after a few lessons,
and other materials must be introduced, at which point the Silent Way classroom can
look like any other language classroom.

30

CHAf까R 2  A  ιMethodical" History of Language Teaching

And  yet,  the  underlying  principles  of the  Silent Way  are 때lid.  All  too  often
we’re tempted as teachers to provide everything for our students, neatly served up
on  a  silver  platter.  We  could  benefit  from  injeαing healthy  doses  of discovery
learning into our classroom activities and from providing less teacher talk than we
usually do to let the students work things out on their own.

4. Total Physical Response

James Asher (197η,the developer of Total Physical Response (fPR), actu따ly
began experimenting with TPR in the 1960s, but it was almost a decade before the
method was widely discussed in professional circles.  TodayTPR, with simplicity as
its most appealing facet, is a household word among language teachers.

You will recall from earlier in this chapter that more than a centwγ ago, Gouin
designed his Series Method on the premise that language associated with a series of
simple  actions  will  be  easily  retained  by  learners.  Much  later,  psychologists
developed  the “trace  theory” of learning in which it was claimed that  memotγ is
increased  if it  is  stim띠ated, or “ traced,"  through  association  with  motor  acti찌.ty.
Over  the  years,  language  teachers  have  intuitively  recognized  the  value  of
associating language with physical activity.  So while the idea of building a method
of language teaching on the principle of psychomotor associations was not new, it
was this veη idea that Asher capitalized upon in developing TPR.

TPR combined a  number of other insi앙its in its rationale.  Principles of child
language acquisition were important.  Asher (197η noted that cllildren, in lear따ig
their first language, appear to do a lot of listening before they speak, and that their
listening  is  accompanied  by  physical  responses  (reaching,  grabbing,  moving,
loo I섭ng, and so forth).  He  also  gave  some attention to  ri앙it-brain lear띠ng (PLLT,
Chapter 5).  According to Asher, motor activiη is a ri방it-brain function that should
precede  left-brain  language  processing.  Asher  was  also  convinced  that  language
classes were often the locus of too much anxiety, so he wished to devise a method
that  was  as  stress-free  as  possible,  where  learners  would  not  feel  overly  self
conscious and defensive.  The TPR classroom, then, was one in which students did
a great deal of listening 없id acting.  The teacher was veπ directive in orchestrating
a performance: “πie instructor is the 띠rector of a stage play in which the students
are the actors” (Asher, 1977, p. 43).

ηrpically, TPR heavily utilized the imperative mood, even into more advanced
proficiency levels.  Commands were an easy way to get learners to move about 없id
to loosen up: Opeη the  windoiι,  Close  the door,  Stand up,  Sit down,  Pick  tψ the
book, Gi:νe it to john, and so on.  No verbal response was neces잃η.  More complex
syntax  could  be  incorporated  into  the  imperative:  Draw  a  rectaηrgle  on  the
chalkboard, Walk quickly to the door and hit it.  Humor is e잃y to introduce: Walk
sloiν•ly to the window aηd jump, Put your toothbrush iη jηur book (Asher, 1977,
p. 55).  Interrogatives were also easily dealt with: Where  is the book? Who  is john?
(students pointed to the book or to John).  Eventually students, one by one, would
feel  comfortable  enough  to  venture  verbal  responses  to  questions,  then  to  잃k
questions themselves, and to continue the process.

CHAPTER  2  A  “Methodical” History of Language Teaching

31

Like  every  other  method  we  have  encountered, TPR  had  its  limitations.  It
seemed to be especially effective in the beginning levels of language proficien다, but
it  lost  its  distinctiveness  as  learners  advanced  in  their  competence.  In  a  TPR
classroom,  after  students  overcame  the  fear  of  speaking  out,  classroom
conversations and other activities proceeded as in almost any other communicative
language  classroom.  In TPR  reading and writing  activities, students are  limited to
spinning  off from  the  oral  work  in  the  classroom.  Its  appeal  to  the  dramatic  or
theatrical  nature of language learning was attractive.  (See  Smith,  1984, and Stern,
1983, for discussions of the use of drama in foreign language classrooms.) But soon
learners' needs for spontaneity and unrehearsed language must be met.

5. The Natural Approach

Stephen Krashen’s (1982,  199η theories of second language acquisition have
been widely discussed and hotly debated over the years (PLLT,  Chapter  10).  The
major methodological offshoot of Krashen’s views was manifested in the Natural
Approach,  developed  by  one  of Krashen’S  colleagues, Tracy Terrell  (Krashen  &
Terrell,  1983).  Acting  on  many  of  the  claims  that  Asher  made  for  a
comprehension-based  approach  such  as  TPR,  Krashen  and  Terrell  felt  that
learners  would  benefit  from  delaying  production  until  speech  “ emerges;’  that
learners should be as  relaxed as  possible in the classroom, and that a great deal of
communication and “ acquisition” should take place, as opposed to analysis.  In fact,
the Natural Approach advocated the use ofTPR activities at the beginning level of
language  learning  when  “comprehensible  input”  is  essential  for  triggering  the
acquisition of language.

There  are  a  number  of possible  long-range  goals  of language  instruction.  In
somε cases second languages are learned for oral communication; in other C싫es for
writtεn communication; and in st피 others there may be an academic emphasis on,
say,  listening  to  lectures,  spea임ng in  a  classroom  context, or writing  a  research
paper.  The  goal  of  the  Natural  Approach  was  to  build  the  basic  personal
communication  skills  necessary  for  everyday
language  situations-daily
conversations, shopping, listening to the radio, and the like.  The initial task of the
teacher  was  to  pro꺼de comprehensible  input,  that  is,  spoken  language  that  is
understandable  to  the learner or just a  little  beyond the learner's  level.  Learners
need not say anyt피ng during this “silent period” until they feel ready to do so.  The
teacher was the source of the learners' input and the creator of an interesting and
stimulating variety of classroom activities-commands, games, skits, 없id small-group
work.

In thε Natural Approach, learners presumably move through what Krashen and

Terrell defined as three stages:

a.  The preproduction stage is the development of listening comprehension s힘lls.
b.  The early production stage is usually marked with errors as the student

struggles with the language.  The teacher focuses on meaning here, not on
form, and therefore the teacher does not make a point of correcting cπors

32

CHAPTER 2  A  "Methodical" History of Language Teaching

during this stage (unless they are gross errors that block or hinder meaning
entirely).

c.  The last stage is one of ε:xtending production into longer stretches of discourse
involving more complex g따nes, role plays, open-ended dialogues, discussions,
and extended sm외I-group work.  Since the objective in this stage is to promote
fluency, teachers are asked to bε vεηr sparse in thεir correction of eπors.

The most controversial aspects of the Natural Approach were its advocacy of a
“ silent period”(delay of oral production) and its heavy emphasis on comprehensible
input.  The delay of oral production until speech “emerges” has shortcomings (see
Gibbons, 1985).  What about the student whose spεech never emerges? And with 따l
students on different timetables for this so-called emergence, how does thε teachεr
manage a classroom efficiently? Furthermore, the concept of comprehensible input
is  difficult to pin down, as Langi (1984, p. 18) noted:

How doεs one know which structures the learners are to be provided
with?  From  the  ex없nples  of “teachεr talk”  provided  in  the  book
(Krashen and Terrell, 1983), communication interactions  seεm to be
guided by the topic of conversation rather than by the structures of
the language.  The decision of which structures to usε appears to be
left  to  some mysterious  sort  of intuition, which  many  teachers  may
not possess.

On a more positiv'ε note, most teachers and researchεrs agree that we are prone
to  insist  that  learners  speak  right  away,  없id  so  we  can  take  from  the  Natural
Approach  the  good  advice  that  for  a  pεriod  of  time  while  students  grow
accustomed to the new languagε, their silence is beneficial.  Through TPR and other
forms  of input, students' language egos are not as easily threatened, and they ar,εn’t
forced  into  inlmediate  risk-taking  that  could  embarrass  them.  The  resulting  self
confidence ev1℃ntually can spur a student to venture to speak out.

Innovative  methods  such  as  these  five  methods  of the  1970s  expose  us  to
principles  없id practices  that  you  can  sift  through,  weigh, and  adapt  to  multiple
contεxts.  Your responsib피ty as a teacher is to choose the best of what others have
experimented with and adapt those insights to your situation.  Those insights and
intuitions C때 become a part of your principled approach to language teaching.

FUNCTIONAL SYLLABUSES

As the innovative methods of thε 1970s werε bεing touted by some and criticized
by many, some significant foundations  for  future  growth were  being  l없d in what
soon came to be known as the Notional-Functional Syllabus, or more commonly
the Functional Syllabus.  Beginning with the work of the Council of Europe αIan
Ek  &Alexandεr, 1975) and later followed by numerous interprεtations of “ notional”

CHAPTER  2  A "Methodical" History of Language 1능·aching  33

syllabuses (Wilkins, 1976), Notional-Functional Syllabuses (NFS) began to be used in
the United Kingdom in the 1970s.

The  distinguishing  characteristics  of the  NFS  were  its  attention  to  functions
(see PLLT,  Chapter 8) as  the  organizing elements of English  language  curriculum,
and  its  contrast  with  a  structural  syllabus  in  which  sequenced  grammatical
structures served as the orga피zers.  Reacting to methods that attended too strongly
to g없nmatical form, the NFS  focused strongly-and in some of its interpretations,
exclusively-on the pragmatic purposes to which we put language.  As such, it was
not  a  method  at  all.  It  was  close  to  what  we  can  call  an “ approach”  (see  next
chapter), but it  was  more  specifically  focused  on curricular structure than a  true
approach would be.

“ Notions;’  according  to Van  Ek  and Alexander  (1975),  are  both  general  and
specific.  General  notions  are  abstract  concepts  such  as  existence,  space,  time,
quantity,  and  quality‘  They  are  domains  in  which  we  use  language  to  express
thought and feeling.  Within the general notion of space and time, for example, are
the concepts of location, motion, dimension, speed, length of time, frequency, εtc.
“Specific  notions" correspond more closely to what we have become accustomed
to calling “contexts,” or “situations.” Personal identification, for example, is a specific
notion under which name, address, phone number, and other personal information
are subsumed.  Other specific notions include travel, health and welfare, education,
shopping, services, and free  time.

The  “ functional'’  part  of  the  NFS  corresponded  to  language  functions.
Curricula were organized around such functions as identifying, reporting, denying,
accepting,  declining,  asking  permission,  apologizing,  etc.  Van  Ek  and Alexander
listed some 70 different language functions.

The  NFS  quickly  provided  popular  underpinnings  for  the  development  of
communicative  textbooks  and  materials  in  English  language  courses.  The
functional  basis of language programs has continued to the present day.  In Brown
(1999), for example, the f이lowing functions are covered in the first several lessons
of an advanced beginner's textbook:

1.  Introducing self and other people
2.  Exchanging personal information
3.  Asking how to spell someone’s name
4.  Giving commands
5.  Apologizing and thanking
6.  Identifying and describing people
7.  Asking for information

A  typical  unit  in  this  textbook  includes  an  eclectic  blend  of  conversation
practice  with  a  classmate,  interactive  group  work,  role  plays,  grammar  and
pronunciation focus  exercises, information-gap  techniques, Internet activities, and
extra-class interactive practice.

34

[ HAPTER 2  A  “Methodical” History of Language Teaching

It is  imp야tant to emphasize, in this historical sketch of methodology, that the
NFS  did  not  nεcessarily develop  communicative  competencε in  learners.  First,  it
was not amεthod, which would spεci다 how you would tεach something; it was a
syllabus.  And while it was clearly a precursor to what W℃ now call Communicative
Languagε Tεaching (see  Chapter 3), as  a  syllabus  it  still  presented  language  as  an
inventory  of  units-functional  rather  than  grammatical  units-but  units
nonethelεss.  Communicative  competencε implies  a  set  of  strategies  for  getting
mεssages  sεnt  and  received  and  for  negotiating  meaning  as  an  interactive
participant in discourse, whether spokεn or written.  Therefore, the dangεr that the
NFS  could  simply  bε “ structural  lamb  serv,εd  up  as  notional-functional  mutton”
(Campbε11, 1978,p.18) was ever-present.  However, the NFS did indeed set the stage
for bigger and better things.  By  attending to the functional  purposes of language,
and  by  providing  contextual  (notional)  settings  for  the  rεalization  of  those
purposes, it provided a link between a dynasty of methods that were perishing and
a  nεw era of languagε tεaching that is  the subject of thε next chaptεr.

As  an  aid  to  your  rεcollection of the  characteristics  of some  of the  methods
reviewed  earlier, you  may  wish  to  refer  to Table  2.1  (pages  36-37), in  which  the
Audiolingual Method, the five “designεr” methods, and thε Communicativ1℃ Language
Teaching Approach ar1ε summarized according to eight different criteria.

*

*

영k

*·

On looking back over this meandering histotγ, you can no doubt see the cycles
of changing winds  and  shifting  sands  alluded  to  earlier.  In  some  ways  the  cycles
were, as Marckwardt (1972) proposed, each about a quarter of a century in lεngth,
or  roughly  a  generation.  In  this  remarkable  succession  of  changes,  we  learned
something  in  each  genεration.  We  did  not  allow  history  simply  to  dεposit nεw
dunes  exactly where  the old ones lay.  So  our cumulative  history has  taught  us  to
appreciate  thε valuε of “doing” language interactively, of the emotional (as well as
cognitive)  sidε  of  learning,  of  absorbing  language  automatically,  of  consciously
analyzing it when appropriate, and of pointing learners toward the real world whεre
thεy will use English communicatively.

In  thε next  chapter  we  look  at  how  we  reaped  those  benefits  to  form  a
rεlatively well-integrated,  unifiεd, communicative  approach  to  language  teaching
that is  no longer charactεrized simply by a series of rising and falling  methods.

TOPICS FOR DISCUSSION, ACTION, AND RESEARCH

[Note: (I) Individual work; (G) group or pair work; (C) whole-class discussion.]

1.  (I) Since this chapter refers to some basic principles and issues that are

normally cover'εd in a course in second language acquisition (and in books
likε PLLT and Mitchell & Myles, 2004), it is  quite important at this point for

35

you to review such material.  For examplε, varied theories of learning are

sεcond language acquisition is  highlightεd in some methods; conscious and
subconscious (or focal and peripheral) processing assumes various rolεs,
dεpending on the method in question.  If you encountεred concepts or issuεs
that you needεd to brush up on as you read this chapter, makε some time for
a thorough reviεw.

2.  (G) Given thε choicε of Richards and Rodgers’s or Anthony’s  earliεr model of

looking at the concepts of approach, method, design, procedure, and
tεchnique, which do students prefiεr? Direct small groups to discuss
pr,εfεrεnces.  If there is  disagr,εεmεnt, groups should try to comε to a
consensus.  Make sure groups dεal with Richards and Rodgers’s rationale for
the change.

3.  (G) Consider the Sεriεs Method, the Dir,εct Method, and the Audiolingual

Method.  Assign a different method to each of sεveral small groups.  The task
is  to list  thε theoretical foundations on which the method rested and sharε
findings with the whole class.

4.  (C) Richards and Rodgers (2001, p ‘  7)  said Grammar Translation “ is a method
for which there is  no thεory.” Is  this too harsh a judgment? Ask  studεnts if
they agree with thε theorylessness of Grammar Translation and why.

5.  (G) If timε permits. εach of the methods describεd in this chapter could be
assigned to a different small group of students.  The group’s  task (which
may require some εxtra-class research beyond what is  provided in this
chapter) is  to specify, for the mεthod assignεd to them, the following
descriptors (adapted from  Larsεn-Freeman, 2000) which differ from those in
Ta blε 2.1:

1.  Characteristics of the teaching/learning process
2.Thε nature of teachεr-student and student-student interaction
3.Thε ways in which students' f℃elings and emotions are handled
4.  The role of the native  languagε of students
5.  The languagε skills that are emphasized
6.  The way the tεacher responds to student εrrors

Each group can then report their findings to the rest of the class.  Students
often find it useful to see the information formatted as  a chart (on the chalk
board or computer generated) similar to Table 2.1.

6.  (G/C) Rεview the five “dεsigner” methods.  If class size permits, assign a

method to each of fivε different small groups, where each group will “dεfend”
their method against the others.  The group task is  to pr,εpare arguments in
favor of thεir m℃thod, questions to ask of othεr methods, and counter

7.  (C) πiree of the five “designer” methods (CLL, Silent Way, and Suggestopedia)
were propriet따y, with their own commercial publishing and educational
company.  Ask students to consider how that fact might have colored (a) the
objecti찌ty with which its backers promoted their method and (b) public
recept10n to it.

8.  (C) Chapter 1 described a classroom lesson in English as a second language.

Ask students to look back through that lesson now and, in light of the
various methodological positions that have occupied the last centuπ or so
of language teaching, to determine how the activities/techniques in the
lesson reflect some of the theoretical foundations on which certain methods
were constructed.  For example, when the teacher did a quick choral dr피
(#10), how would one support that technique with principles that lay
behind the ALM?

9.  (G/C) Ask students in small groups to review the cycles of “shifting sands”
since Gouin’s time.  How did each new method borrow from previous
practices? What did each reject in previous practices? Each group will then
share their conclusions with the rest of the class.  On the board, you might
reconstruct the historical progression in the form of a time line with
characteristics listed for each “era.'’ If time permits, try to determine what
the prevailing intellectual or political mood was when certain methods
were flowering.  For example, the ALM  was a product of a military training
program and flourished during an era when scientific solutions to all
problems were diligently sought.  Are there some logical connections here?

The  histoπ of language  teaching depicted in  the  pr,εvious chapter, characterized
by a  seriεs of “ methodical” milestones, had changed  its  course  by the  end of thε
1980s.  Thε  profession  had  lεarnεd  somε  profound  lessons  from  our  past
wanderings.  Wε had  learned  to  bε cautiously  εclεctic  in  making  enlightened
choices of tεaching practicεs that were solidly grounded in  the  bεst of what we
knew  about  second  language  learning  and  teaching.  We  had  amassed  enough
research  on learning  and  teaching  that we could indeed formulate  an  integrated
approach to language-tεaching practicεs.  And, perhaps ironically, the methods that
W℃rε such  strong  signposts  of our  century-old  journey were  no  longer  of great
consequence in marking our progrεss.  How did that happεn?

In  the  1970s  and  early  1980s,  thεre was  a  good  dεal of hoopla  about  the
“ designer”  methods  described  in  Chapter  2.  Even  though  they  weren’t  widεly
adopted as  standard methods, thεy were  nevertheless  symbolic  of a  profession at
least parti때ly caught up in a mad scramble to invent a new method when the veη
concept of “method” was eroding undεr our kεt.  By the early  1990s it was readily
apparent that we didn’t need a new method.  We nεεded, instead, to get on with the
business of uni따1g our approach to language teaching and of designing effective
tasks and techniques that wer'ε informεd by that approach.

Perhaps  the  spirit  of  those  times  was  best  captured  by  the  notion  of  a
postmethod  era  of language  teaching,  a  concεpt that  continues  to  be  used  in
pedagogical circles today (Kumaravadivelu, 2001, 2006b; Richards &  Rodgers, 2001).
Kumaravadivelu (1994), Clarke (1994), and Brown (1993), among others, expr1εssed
the  need  to put to  rest  the  limited  concept of method as  it  was  us℃d in  the last
century, and instead to focus on what Kumaravadivelu (2006b) calls a “pedagogy of
particularity,” by which he means being “ sensitive to a particular group of tεachεrs

40

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmeth。d Era:  T。ward Informed Approaches

41

tεaching a  particular group  of learners pursuing  a  particular  sεt of goals  within a
particular institutional  context  embεdded in  a  particular social  milieu”  (p.  538).A
soundly  coneεived  pedagogical  approach  undεrliεs  such  attention  to  the
particularitiεs of contexts.

And  so,  today  thosε  clearly  identifiablε  and  εntεrprising  methods  ar1ε  an
intεrεsting  if  not  insightful  contribution  to  our  professional  repεrtoire, but  few
practitionεrs look  to  any  one  of them, or their  pr,℃decessors, for  a  final  answer on
how to tεach a foreign  language.  Method, as a unif ed, finite sεt of design featurεs, is
nowgiv'εn only minor attεntion.  Instead, as noted in the previous chapter, the notion
of methodology nev'εrthεless continues to be viablε, as  it is in any other behavioral
science, as the systεmatic application of validated principlεs to practical contexts.

In all  this discussion of method, you do well to keep in mind thε commεnts of
the previous chapter, namεly, that some of the debate is simply a matter of sεmantics.
Bell (2003) astutely obsεrvεd that we have too many definitions attached to the same
word.  He  suggestεd that  methods  with  a  lowercase  m  can  mean  any  of a  widε
variεty of classroom practices, while Methods with an uppercase M  seεm to connote
a “ fixed set of classroom practicεs that servε as a pr,εscription” (p. 326).  On th℃ other
hand, Richards  and Rodgers  (2001), as  noted in  the pr,εvious chapter, use  the same
term as  an umbrella to comprise approach, design, and procedure.  What are we to
make  of  this  confusion?  Bεll  (2003)  is  joined  by  Larsen-Freeman  (2000),  among
othεrs‘  who  remain  comfortable  with  maintaining  thε notion  of methods  (with  a
small m) as long as we are clεar about the rεferent.  “Postmethod need not imply the
end  of  methods  but  rathεr an  understanding  of the  limitations  of the  notion  of
mεthod and a desire to transcend those limitations" (Bell, 2003, p. 334).

So  perhaps  thε profession  has  attained  a  modicum  of  maturity  where  wε
recognizε that  thε diversity  of languag1ε learnεrs in  multiplε worldwide  contεxts
dεmands an eclectic  blεnd of tasks, εach tailored for a  specified group of lεarnεrs
studying for particular purposes in geographic, social, and political contexts.  David
Nunan (1991b, p. 228) summed it  up  nicεly: “It has been realized  that  ther1ε nεver
was and probably nev'εr will  be a  method for  all, and thε focus in rεcent years has
been  on  the  dev,℃lopment of classroom  tasks  and  activities  which  arε consonant
with  what  we  know  about  second  language  acquisition,  and  which  ar1ε  also  in
keεping with the dynamics of the classroom itself.”

THE DYSFUNCTION OF THE THEORY-PRACTICE DICHOTOMY

The  now  discarded  concept  of mεthod (with  a  capital M)  as  a  discrete  set  of
unified techniques designed to mεεt a variety of contexts carriεd with it, in some
opinions (Clarke, 1994; Kumaravadiv'εlu, 2006b), an implicit assumption about the
rεlationship betwζen what we  havε customarily called “ theoπ” and “practice.” By
theorγ, pro tiεssional journals and books sometimes implied a  creator, or theorist,
who  carriεd out  research  and  proposed  the  rudiments  of an  organized  set  of

42

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmethod Era:  Toward Informed Approaches

hypotheses, and sometimes thεn further proposed a  mεthodological “application”
of  the  theory  (hence  the  perhaps  misguided  term, “ applied  linguistics”)‘  Thε
practice part of the formula was thought to bε the pm까ncε of classroom teachers
who  all  too  gladly  accepted  the  theorist’S  pronouncements, which  camε in  the
form  of a  method.  The  relationship  bεtwεen thε thεorist and  practitionεr was
(and in some cases, still is) similar to that of a producer of goods and a  consumer
or customer.

Mark  Clarke  (1994)  very  εloquently argued  against  such  a  relationship  in
analyzing  the  “dysfunction”  of  the  thεory-practice  relationship.  He  and  others
sincε thεn (Kumaravadivelu, 2006b; Larsεn-Freεman, 2000; Nunan, 2003; Richards &
Rodgers,  2001)  offer  strong  arguments  against  perpεtuating  this  “misl℃a ding
dualism"(Hεdgcock, 2002, p. 308).  Not only does such an understanding promotε
the  notion  of “ a  privilegεd class  of  thεorists  and  an  undεrprivileged  class  of
practitioners” (Kumaravadivelu, 2006, p. 166), but it  also  connotes a  separation of
researchεrs and  t℃achεrs and, at  worst, a  onε-way communication  linε from  thε
formεr to thε lattεr.

R℃cent work  in  the  language-teaching  profession  shows  a  marked  dεpartur,ε
from  the artificial  dichotomy of theory and  practicε (Bailεy, 2001; Johnson,  1999;
McKay, 2006; Murphy &  Byrd, 2001).  In this newer modε of viewing thε profession,
tεachers are researchers  and are  charged with  the  responsibility of reflεcting on
their  own  practice.  Calls  for  “action  research"  and  “classroom-based  resεarch”
reflect  a  new  and  healthier  attitude  toward  the  relationship  of  research  and
practice.  It  has  become  increasingly  inauthentic  for  univεrsity  professors  to
generate  ideas  from  the  protective walls  of an  ivoη tower without  ζxperiencing
them in person in thε classroom.  Likewise, morε and more teachers ar1℃ engaging
in the process of systεmatic observation, experim℃ntation, analysis, and rεporting of
their  own  experiences  in  classrooms  around  thε  world.  Morε detail  on  the
languag1ε tεacher as researchεr is  offered in Chapter 26 of this book.

As  you  continuε to  read on in  this  and following  chaptεrs, it  is  important to
view yourself as a capable observ,εr of your own-and othεrs’ -practice.  You need
not think of thεorists as people that are removed from the ar℃na of classroom rεality,
nor of tεach℃rs as anything lεss than εssential participants in a dialogue.  To  assumε
a gap bεtween theory and practice is  dysfunctional indeεd.

AN ENLIGH1’ENED,  ECLECTIC APPROACH

It  should  be  clear  from  thε foregoing  that  as  both  an  enlightεned and  eclectic
teachεr, you think in terms of a number of possible mεthodological options at your
disposal for tailoring classεs to particular contexts.  Your approach, or rationale for
language  lεarning  and  teaching,  therefore  takes  on  grεat  importance.  Your
approach includes a  number of basic principles of learning and teaching (such as

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmeth 。d Era:  Toward  Informed Approaches

43

thosε that  will  bε elaborated  on  in  the  next  chaptεr) on which  you  can  rεly for
dεsigning and  evaluating  classroom  lessons.  Your  approach  to  language-teaching
methodology is a theoretically wεll-informεd global understanding of thε process of
learning and tεaching.  It is  inspired by the interconnection of all  your rεading and
observing  and  discussing  and  tεaching,  and  that  interconnection  und℃rliεs
ev'℃πthing that you do in the classroom ‘

But your approach to language  pedagogy is  not just a  set of static  principlεs
“set in stone.” It is, in fact, a dynamic composite of ℃nergies within you that changε,
or should  changε, with your  experiencεs in your learning and teaching.  Thε way
you  understand  the  language-learning  process-what  makes  for  successful  and
unsuccessful learning-may bε rεlatively stablε across  months or years, but don’t
εvεr fεel too smug.  There is  far  too much that wε do not know collεctiv℃ly about
this procεss, and there are far too many new research findings pouring in, to allow
you  to  conf1dεntly assert  that  you  know  everything  you  already  nεed to  know
about languagε and language learning.

The  intεraction bεtwεen your  approach  and your classroom  practicε is  the
kεy to dynamic tεaching.  The best teachers always take a  fεw calculated risks in
the  classroom,  trying  nεw activitiεs  herε and  there.  The  inspiration  for  such
innovation comes from  thε approach  levε1, but the fε℃dback that  thεse teachers
gathεr from  actual  implεmentation then  informs  thεir ov'εrall  undεrstanding of
the tεaching-learning process.  Which, in turn, may givε risε to a  nεw insight and
mor'ε innovative possibilities, and the cycle continues.

If you  have  little  or  no  experiencε in  tεaching and  arε pεrhaps now  in  a
teachεr education  program,  you  may  fεεl  you  cannot  yεt dεscribe your  own
approach to language  learning and tεaching.  On the  othεr hand, you might just
surprise  yourself  at  the  intuitions  you  already  havε about  the  foundations  of
teaching.  Look  at  thε list  below  of potential  choices  you  have  in  designing  a
lεsson.  On the basis of what you know so far about second language acquisition
and  the  pedagogical  procεss, and  for  a  particular  context you’re  familiar  with,
think about:

a.  which sidε of a  continuum of possibilities you would generally lεan

toward,

b.  why you would lean that way, and most importantly,
c.  what contεxtual variablεs might influence a chang'ε away from your genεral

inclination‘

For  example,  thε first  item  bεlow off;εrs  a  choice  between  “ mεaning ” and
“ grammar” for  a focus.  While you might lεan toward meaning because you know
that  too  much  focus  on  form  could  detract  from  communicative  acquisition,
certain classroom objectives and tasks might dεmand a focus on grammar.  H℃re is
the list:

44

CHAPTER 3  The  Postmethod Era: Toward Informed Approaches

For a particular course and context you  are familiar with .  .  .

1.  Should the course focus  on  meaning or grammar?
2.  Will  my  students  learn  best by  using  plenty of analysis or  intuition?
3.  Would  it  be better for  my  students to  think directly in  the  L2  or to

use translation from  the L 1?

4.  Will  my  students  benefit more from  immediate rewards or from

long-term  rewards?

5.  As  a teacher should  I be tough  and demanding or gentle and

empathetic?

6.  Should  my  feedback to  students be given  frequently or
infrequently,  so  students will  develop autonomy?

7.  Should a communicative course give  more attention  to  accuracy or

fluency?

Were  you  able  to respond to these items?  If you could make  a choice within
each item, it indicates that you do indeed have some intuitions about teaching, and
perhaps the beginnings of an approach.  Your approach is  guided by a  number of
factors:  your  own  experience  as  a  learner  in  classrooms,  whatever  teaching
experience  you  may  already  have  had,  classroom  observations  you  have  made,
books you have read, and previous courses in the field.  But more importantly, if you
found that in almost every choice you wanted to add something like “but it depends
on .  .  ."  then you 따e on the way toward developing an enlightened approach to
language learning and teaching.  Our approaches to language teaching must always
be designed for specific contexts of teaching-what Kumaravadivelu (2006b) calls
a pedagogy of “p따ticularity," as  mentioned earlier.  Rarely can we say with absolute
certainty that some methodological set of techniquεs applies  to all  learners  in all
contexts for all purposes.

Your  approach  also  will  differ  on various  issues  from  that  of a  colleague  of
yours,  or even  a  supeπising teacher,  just  as  “experts” in  the  field  differ  in  their
interpretations of research  on learning and teaching.  There  are  three  reasons  for
variation at the approach level:

a.  an approach is by de tnition dynamic and therefore su비ect to alterations and

modification as a result of one’s observation and experience;

b.  research in second language acquisition and pedagogy almost always yields

findings that are not conclusive, but are subject to interpretation; and
c.  we are constantly making new discoveries about language learning and

teaching, as our profession외 stockpile of knowledge and experience b띠Ids.

45

COMMUNICATIVE LANGUAGE TEACHING

Is  thεre a  currently recognized approach that is  a  generally accepted norm in the
field?  Thε answer  depends  on whom  you  ask.  For  many  (Savignon,  2005, 없nong
othεrs), Communicative Language Teachlng (CLD is an accepted paradigm with
many  interpretations  and  manifestations.  For  othεrs (Kumaravadivelu,  2006a,  for
εxample), CLT  is  laden with issues of “au thεnticity, acceptability, and  adaptability”
(p. 62), and  instead wε are  e화iorted to embrace  task-based language teachlng
σBLT) as a more appropriatε model.  (See below for a discussion ofTBLT.)

The latter arguments  repr'εsent what appears to bε too strong a  rεjection of a
tradition that has been viable in many language-teaching circles for several decadεs.
In this chapter, in order to avoid a lot of nitpicking ovεr the nuances of differencε
between the two approaches, we’ll look at both CLT and TBLτ and then perhaps we
can allow the progression of I℃search in thε next few years to sort out the pros and
cons on  each  point  of view.  Sufficε it  to  say  that  no  model  will  bε sufficient  to
satisη all thε criteria  for  a  comprehensivε theory  of instructed  second  languagε
acquisition.  As  long as the language-teaching community recognizes shortcomings
and  seeks  to  remedy  them  in  local  contexts,  we  can  st퍼 use  such  modεls as
foundation stones for our pedagogy.

In the pr,εvious chaptεr you were introduced to a progrεssion of methods that
defined a cεntu1γ or more of languag'ε-tεaching history.  Ben℃ath those mεthods lay
some  important  theoretical  assumptions.  In  the  1940s  and  1950s, the  pro£εssion
was  to  some  extent  convinced  that  teachers  could  behaviorally  program  a
scientifically ordered set of linguistic structures into the minds of learnεrs through
conditioning.  In  the  1960s  W℃  were  quitε  worried  about  how  Chomsky’s
generative grammar was going to fit into our language classrooms and how to inject
the  cognitive  code  of  a  languag℃  into  the  procεss  of  absorption.  The
innovativ,εness  of  the  1970s  brou맹t  affectiv,℃ factors  to  the  forefront  of  some
εxperimental language-teaching  methods.  This  period  saw  a  focus  on  εmotional
and sociocultural factors operating within learnεrs.  The late  1970s and early 1980s
saw the beginnings of what we now r℃cognize as a communicativ,℃ approach as we
bεtter  and  better  understood  thε  functions  that  must  be  incorporated  into  a
classroom.  The  late  1980s  and  1990s  saw  thε dev1εlopment of approaches  that
highlighted  the  fundamentally  communicative  properties  of  language,  and
classrooms  wεr℃ increasingly  characterized  by  authεnticity, real-world  simulation,
and meaningful tasks.

Today we continue our profession띠 marchthrou항1 history.  Beyond grammatical
따id discourse elements in communication, we continuε to probe the nature of social,
cultural, and pragmatic features of language.  We are exploring pedagogical means for
“real-life”  communication  in  the  classroom.  We  are  trying  to  get  our  learners  to
dev1εlop  파iguistic  fluen다, and  not  just  thε  accuracy  that  oncε  consumεd our
predecεssors.  We  are  eq버pping our students with tools for generating unrehearsed
language performance “out thεre ” when they leav1ε the womb of our classrooms.  We

46

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmethod Era:  Toward  Informed Approaches

are coneεmεd with how to facilitate  lifelong l없1guage learning among our students,
and  not  just  with  the  immεdiatε classroom  task.  We  ar'ε  looking  at  learners  as
partners  in  a  coopεrativ,℃ Vεnture.  And  our  classroom  practicεs seek  to  draw  on
whatever intrinsically sparks learners to reach their fullest potential.

All  of thεse theoretical intεrεsts underlie what wε can best describe as CLT.  It
is difficult to offiεr a definition of CLT.  It is a unifiεd but broadly based, theoretically
well-informεd set of tenets about thε nature of languag'ε and of language learning
and  teaching.  From  the  εarliεr  sεminal works  in  CLT  (Br'εen  &  Candlin,  1980;
Savignon, 1983;Widdowson, 1978) up to more recent tεa ch er ℃ducation tεxtbooks
(Brown, 2007; Harmεc 2001; Jacobs &  Farrε11, 2003; Lεε & VanPatten, 2003; Nunan,
2003; Richard-Anlato, 2003; Savignon, 2005), we havε definitions enou맹 to send us
reεling.  For  the  sakε  of  simplicity  and  directness,  I  offer  the  following  sev'εn
interconnεctεd characteristics as a dεscription of CLT:

Characteristics of a  CLT Approach

1.  Overall goals.  CLT  suggests a focus  on  all of the components

(grammatical,  discourse,  functional,  soci 이 inguistic, and strategic)
of communicative competence.  Goals therefore  must  intertwine
the organizational  (grammatical,  discourse)  aspects of language
with  the pragmatic (functional,  soci이 inguistiι strategic)  aspects.

2.  Relationship of form  and function.  Language techniques are

designed  to engage learners  in  the  pragmatic,  authentic,
functional  use of language for  meaningf비 purposes.
Organizational  language forms  are  not the central  focus,  but
remain  as  important components of language that enable the
learner to  accomplish  those  purposes.

3.  Fluency and accuracy.  A focus  on  students'  "flow”  of

comprehension  and production  and a focus  on  the formal
accuracy of production  are seen  as  complementary principles
underlying communicative techniques.  At  times fluency  may  have
to  take on  more  importance than  accuracy  in  order to  keep
learners  meaningfully engaged  in  language  use.  At  other times
the students will  be encouraged to attend to  correctness.  Paπ of
the teacher's  responsibility  is  to offer appropriate corrective
feedback  on  learners'  errors.

4.  Focus on  real-world contexts.  Students  in  a  communicative class
비timately have to  use  the  language,  productively and receptively,
in  unrehearsed contexts outside the classroom.  Classroom tasks
must therefore equip students with  the skills  necessary for
communication  in  those contexts.

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmeth。d Era:  Toward Informed Approaches

47

5.  Autonomy and strategic involvement.  Students are given

opportunities to  focus  on their own  learning  process through
raising their awareness of their own  styles  of learning (strengths,
weaknesses,  preferences) and  through  the  development of
appropriate strategies for  production  and comprehension.  Such
awareness and  action  wi II  help  to  develop autonomous  learners
capable of continuing to  learn  the  language beyond  the  classroom
and  the  course.

6.  Teacher roles.  The  role  of the teacher  is  that of facilitator  and

guide,  not an  all-knowing font  of knowledge.  The  teacher  is  an
empathetic “coach” who values the students'  linguistic
development.  Students are encouraged to  construct meaning
through  genuine  linguistic  interaction  with  other students and
with  the teacher.

7.  Student roles.  Students  in  a CLT  class are  active  participants  in
their own  learning process.  Learner-centered,  cooperative,
collaborative  learning  is  emphasized,  but  not at the expense of
appropriate teacher-centered  activity.

Thεse seven  characteristics  underscore  some  major  dεpartures from  εarlier
methods and approaches.  In  some ways  those departures were a  gradual  product
of outgrowing  thε numerous methods that characterized a long stretch of history.
In  other  ways  those  departures  were  radical.  Structurally  (grammatically)
sequεnced curricula  were  a  mainstay  of  language  teaching  for  centuriεs.  CLT
suggests  that  grammatical  structure  might  better  be  subsumed  under  various
pragmatic  categories.  In  CLT  we  pay  considerably  less  attention  to  the  ovεrt
presentation and discussion of grammatical rules than we traditionally did.  Using a
great deal of authentic language is implied in CLT, as we attempt to build fluency.  It
is  important  to  notε, however,  that  fluency  should  never  bε encouraged  at  the
expεnse of clear, unambiguous, dirεct communication.  Much  mor,ε spontaneity is
presεnt  in  communicative  classrooms:  Students  are  encouraged  to  dεal  with
unrehεarsed situations  under  thε guidance,  but  not  control,  of  thε teachεr.  Thε
importance  of learners' developing  a  strategic  approach  to  acquisition  is  a  total
turnabout  from  earlier  methods  that nεvεr broached the  topic  of strategiεs-basεd
instruction.  And  finally,  the  teacher’s  facilitative  role  in  CLT  and  students'
collaborative role are the product of two decades or more of slowly recognizing the
importancε of learner initiative in the classroom.

Some  of the  characteristics  of CLT  make  it  difficult  for  a  nonnative-speaking
tεacher  who  might  not  be  veπ proficient  in  the  second  language  to  tεach
εffectively.  Dialogues,  drills,  rehearsed  exercises,  and  discussions  (in  thε  first
languag'ε)  of  grammatical  rules  are  much  simpler  for  somε nonnative-speaking
teachers  to  contend  with.  This  drawback  should  not  deter  one,  howe'℃r,  from
pursuing  communicative  goals  in  thε  classroom.  Technology  (such  as  vidεo,

48

CHAPTER  J  까1e Postmethod Era:  Toward Informed Approaches

television, audio  CDs, the Internet, the Web, and computer software) can aid  such
tεachers.  Moreover, in  the  last  dεcad℃ or so, wε have  sεen a  marked  increase  in
English teachers' proficiεncy lεvels around the world.  As  εducational and political
institutions  in  various  countries  bεcome more  sensitive  to  the  importance  of
teaching foreign languages for communicative purposes (not just for the purpose of
fulf버ing a requirement or of “passing a tεst”), wε may be better able, worldwide, to
accomplish th℃ goals of communicative languagε teaching.

CLT is not by any means a brand-new approach.  One of the most com pr'εhensive
lists  of CLT  features  camε a  quartεr of a  centwγ ago from  Finocchiaro  and Brumf1t
(1983, pp. 91-93) in a comparison of au띠olingual methodology with what they then
called the Communicative Approach.  Because of its practic따ity, their list is reprinted
in Table 3.1.  In subsequent chapters, as you grapple with designing specific classroom
techniques  and  planning  lessons,  you  will  be  given  chances  to  apply  your
understanding of CLT and, no doubt, to refinε that undεrstanding.

At  the  beginning  of this  section, it  was  noted  that  there  are  somε who  now
arguε that  CLT  may  not  be  as  sufficient  a  model  as  we  once  thought.  Why  the
caution? Doesn’t  all  the above make perfectly good sense?  Haven’t  CLT  principles
been applied repeatεdly, and successfully, in classrooms around the world? Indeed,
you can with some assurancε latch on to the CLT label and, likε a member of a club,
aver that you “ believe in CLT.,” and bε allowed to step inside the gates.  But as with
eveη issue in our field, ther℃ ar1ε cavεats (seε Kumaravadivelu, 2006, and Kramsch,
2006, for furthεr discussion):

1.  Beware of giving lip service to principlεs of CLT (and related principles like
coopεrative learning, interactive teaching, learner-centered classes, content-
centered education, whole language, etc.-s℃ε the next sections in this chapter)
but not truly groun띠ng your teaching tεchniqu℃s in such principles (Kramsch,
2006).  Few teachers would admit to a disbelief in principlεs of CLT; they would
be marked as her'εtics.  But if you bεlieve the term charactεrizes your teaching,
then m와t sur℃ you do indεed underst없id 따1d practicε your convictions.

2.  Avoid overdoing certain CLT fεatures: engaging in rεal-lifiε, authentic languagε

in the classroom to the exclusion of any potentially helpful controlled
exercisεs, grammatical pointers, and other analytical devices; or simulating the
real world but refraining from “interfεring ” in the ongoing flow of language.
Such an “ indirect” approach* (Celce-Murcia, Dornyei, &Thurrel, 1997) to CLT
only offers the  possibility of incidental learning without specific focus on
forms, rules, and principles of language organization‘  A more effective
application of CLT principlεs is  manifestεd in a “dirεct” approach that
carefully sequences and structures tasks for learners and offers optimal
intervention to aid learners in developing strategies for acquisition.

*Howatt (1984), Littlewood (1981), and Nunan (1988) referred to this as  the
“strong” approach to CLT, noting that most practitioners would follow a “weak”
version of CLT in which authenticity is coupled with structural and functional
practice and other procedures of intervention.

3.  Rεmembεr that thεrε ar,ε numerous int℃rprεtations of CLT.  Bεca usε it is a
catchall term, it is tempting to figurε that everyone agrees on its definition.
As  alrεady noted abovε, they don’t.  In fact, somε of those in thε profiεssion,

sible versions of CLT, it remains a term that can continue to capture current
language-teaching approaches.

Closely  alliεd to  CLT  are  a  numbεr of concepts  that  have, like  CLT,  bεcomε
bandwagon tεrms: task-based language tεaching (which is  for  somε a candidate for
replacing  the  notion  of  er:η, lεarn℃r-cεntered,  cooperative,  intεractivε,  whole
language  based,  and  content-based, to  name  a  few.  One  way  of looking  at  these
terms is that they arε simply expr,εssions for thε latest fads in languag'ε tεaching and
are  therefore relatively meaningless.  But  another viewpoint would  embrace  thεm
as  lεgitimate attεmpts to lab℃l curr'εnt concerns and rεcεnt dεvεlopments within a
CLT  framεwork, as  overlapping and confusing as  thosε concεrns sometimes are.  I
believ'ε thε lattεr is  the morε reasoned perspective.  Howev'εr, in order to takε that
perspεct ivε, somε explanation  is  in  order.  Henc℃, in  thε sections  that  follow,  a
number of the current CI:τI℃lated approachεs are summarized.

TASK-BASED LANGUAGE TEACHING

One of the most prominεnt perspectives within the CLT framework is Task-based
Language  Teaching  (TBLT).  While  somε researchεrs  (Kumaravadivelu,  2006a)
argue that TBLT  is  a significantly different approach, other proponents (Ellis, 2003)
would claim  that TBLT  is  at the very heart of CLT.  This  approach puts  thε usε of
tasks  at  thε cor1ε of  languag'ε Iεaching.  While  there  is  a  good  deal  of variation
among experts on how to  describe  or define  task,  Pet℃r Skehan’s  (1998a, p. 95)
concept of task still captures the essentials.  He  definεs task as an activity in which

•  meaning is primary;
•  there is  somε communication problem to solv1ε;
•  there is  some sort of relationship to comparable real-world activitiεs;
•  task completion has some priority; and
•  thε assessment of thε task is in terms of outcome.

Perhaps  more  simply  put, “a  task  is  an  activity  which  requires  lεarnεrs  to
use  languag1ε, with  emphasis  on  me따따1g,  to  att없n an  objεctive”  (Bygate,  Skεhan,
&  Sw:따n,  2001,  p.11).  In  some  cases,  task  and  techniquε  may  be  synonymous
(a problem-solving task/technique; a role-play task/technique, for examplε),  But in other
cases, a task may be comprised of several techniques (for εX따nple, a problem-solving
task that includεs, lεt’s say, grammatical explanation, teacher-initiatεd quεstions,and

CHAPTER  J  The  P〔>stmeth。d Era:  Toward Informed Approaches

51

a  specific  turn-ta성ng procedure).  Tasks  arε usually “bigger” in their ultimate ends
than  techniques.  No  small  effort  is  demanded  in  designing  effective  tasks,  as
Johnson (2003) and Nunan (2004) ably demonstrate.

Task-based  teaching  makes  an  impoπant distinction  between  target  tasks,
which students  must  accomplish beyond  thε classroom, and  pedago~혀.cal tasks,
which form  the  nucleus of the  classroom  activity.  Target  tasks  are  not  unlike  the
functions of languag1ε that are listed in Notional-Functional Syllabuses (see Chapter
2,  here, and Chapter 8  of PLLD; howεver, they are  much morε specific  and  more
explicitly  related  to  classroom  instruction.  If,  for  example,  “giving  personal
information” is a communicative function for language, then an appropriately stated
target task might be “ giving personal information in a job interview.” Notice that the
task  specifies  a  context.  Pedagogical  tasks  include  any  of a  series  of techniques
designed  ultimately  to  teach  students  to  perform  the  target  task;  the  climactic
pedagogical task actually involves students in some form of simulation of the target
task itself (say, through a role-play simulation in which certain roles are assigned to
pairs of learners).

Pedagogical  tasks  are  distinguished  by their specific  goals  that point beyond
the language classroom to the target task.  They may, however, include both formal
and functional  tεchniques.  A pedagogical  task  designed  to teach students to give
personal information in a job interview might, for example, involve

1.  doing exercises in comprehension of wh- questions with do-insertion (“When

do you work at Macy’s?”)

2.  doing drills in the use of frequency adverbs (“I usually work until five o’clock.")
3.  listεning to extracts of job interviews
4.  analyzing the grammar and discourse of the interviεws
5.  modeling an interview: teacher and one student
6.  role playing a simulated interview: students in pairs

While  you might  be  temptεd to  think that only the climactic  task  ( #6) fulfills  the
criterion of pointing beyond the classroom to the real world, 따l of the techniques
build toward enabling the students to perform the final  task.

A task-based  curriculum, then, specifies  what a  learner needs to do with the
En밍ish language in terms of target tasks and organizes a series of pedagogical tasks
intended  to  reach  those  go때s.  Be  careful  that  you  do  not  look  at  task-based
teaching as  a  hodgepodge of useful  little things that the learner should be able  to
do, all  thrown  together  haphazardly  into  the  classroom.  In  fact,  a  distinguishing
feature  of task-based curricula is  their insistence on pedago~킥.cal soundness in the
development  and  sequencing  of tasks.  The  teacher  and  curriculum  planner  are
called  upon  to  consider communicative  dimensions  such as  goal, input from  the
teacher, interaction, teacher and learner roles, 따id assessment.

Task-based instruction is not a new method.  Rather, it puts task at the center of
one’s  methodolo밍cal focus.  It views the learning process as a set of communicative

52

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmethod Era:  Toward Informed Appr'。aches

tasks that arε directly linked to the curricular goals they serve, the purposes of which
extend  beyond  the practice  of l없iguage for  its  own  sake.  Research  on task-based
learning (Ellis, 2003; Kumaravadivelu, 2006; Nunan, 2004; Richards & Rodgers, 2001;
Skehan, 2003) has attempted to ident파 types of tasks that enhance learning (such as
open-ended, structured, teacher-fronted, sm때l group, and pair work) to define  task
specific  learner  factors  (for  ex없nple, roles,  proficiency  levels,  and  stylεs), and  to
examine teacher roles and other variables that contribute to succεssful achievement
of objectives.

Task-based instruction is a perspective within a CLT framework that forces you
to carefully consider all the techniques that you use in the classroom in terms of a
number of important pedagogical purposes:

Characteristics of TBLT

•  Tasks  ultimately point learners  beyond the forms  of language alone

to  real-world  contexts.

•  Tasks  specifica”y contribute to communicative goals.

•  Their elements are caref비 ly designed and  not simply haphazardly

or  idiosyncratically thrown  together.

•  Their objectives are well  specified so that you  can at some later
point accurately determine the success of one task over another.
•  Tasks engage  learners,  at some  level,  in  genuine problem-s이ving

activity.

LEARNER-CENTERED INSTRUCTION

This term applies to curricula as well 잃 to specific techniques.  It C때 be contrasted
with teacher-centered instruction, and has received various recent interpretations.
Learner-centered instruction includεs

•  techniques that focus on or account for learners' needs, styles, and goals
•  techniques  that  give  some control to  the student 않roup work or strategy

training, for example)

•  curricula that include the consultation and input of students and that do not

presuppose objectives in advance

•  techniques that allow for student creativity and innovation
•  techniques that enhance a student’s sense of competence and self-worth

Because l없iguage teaching is  a  domain that so often presupposes classrooms
where students have very little language proficiency with which to negotiate with
the teacher, some teachers shy away from the notion of gi찌ng learners the “power”
associated  with  a  learner-centered  approach.  Such  restraint  is  not  necessaπ
because, even in beginning level classes, teachers can offer students certain choices.

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmethod Era:  Toward Informed Approaches

53

All of these efforts help to give students a sense of“ ownership” of their learning and
thereby add to  their intrinsic  motivation (see  Chapters  4  and 5  for  discussions  of
intrinsic motivation).

COOPERATIVE LEARNING

A curriculum or classroom that is cooperative-and therefore not competitive
usually  involves  the  above  learner-centered  characteristics.  As  students  work
together in pairs and groups, they share information and come to each other's aid‘
They  are  a “ team”  whose  players  must  work  together  in  order  to  achieve  go외S
successfully.  Research  has  shown  an  advantage  for  cooperative  learning  (as
opposed to individual learning) on such factors as “promoting intrinsic motivation,
.  .  heightening  self-esteem,  .  .  . creating  caring  and  altruistic  relationships,  and
lowering anxiety and prejudice” (0화ord, 1997, p. 445).  Some of the challenges of
cooperative  learning  are  accounting  for  varied  cultural  expectations,  individual
learning styles, and personality diffiεrences and an overreliance on the first language
(Crandall,  1999).  (The  effective  implementation  of cooperative  learning  through
group work in the language classroom is a topic that is covered in detail in Chapter
12 of this book.)

Cooperative  learning  is  sometimes  thought  to  be  synonymous  with
collaborative learning.  To  be  sure, in  a  cooperative  classroom  the  students and
teachers work together to pursue goals and objectives.  But cooperative learning “is
more structured, more prescriptive to teachers about classroom techniques, more
directive  to  studεnts about  how  to  work  together  in  groups  [than  collaborative
learning] ” (Oxford, 1997, p. 443).  In cooperative learning models, a group learning
activity is  dependent on the socially structured exchange of information  between
learners.  In collaborative learning, the learner engages “with more capable others
(teachers,  advanced  pεers, etc.), who  provide  assistance  and  guidance" (Oxford,
1997,  p.  444).  Collaborative  learning  models  have  been  developed  within  social
constructivist (see Chapter 1 of PLLT) schools of thought to promote communities
of learners that cut across the usual hierarchies of students and teachers.

INTERACTIVE LEARNING

At  the  heart of current theories  of communicative  competence  is  the  essentially
interactive nature of communication.  When you speak, for example, the extent to
which your intended message is received is a factor of both your production and the
listener’s reception.  Most meaning, in a sem없1tic sense, is a product of negotiation,
of  give  and
interlocutors  attempt  to  communicate.  Thus,  the
communicative p따pose of language compels us to create opportunities for genuine
interaction in the  classroom.  An interactive  course or technique will  provide  for
such negotiation.  Interactive classes will most likely be found

take,  as

54

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmethod Era:  Toward ’nformed Approaches

•  doing a significant amount of pair work and group work
•  receiving authentic language input in real-world contexts
•  producing language for genuine, meaningful communication
•  performing classroom tasks  that prepare them for  actual language use “out

there"

•  practicing oral communication through the give and take and spontaneiη of

actual conversations

•  writing to and for real audiences, not contrived ones

The  theoretical  foundations  of interactive  learning  lie  in  what  Michael  Long
(1985,  1996)  described  as  the  interaction  hypothesis  of  second  language
acquisition (sεe PLLT,  Chapter 10).  Going beyond Stephen Krashen’S (1985, 1997)
concept  of  comprehensible  input,  Long  and  others  have  pointed  out  the
importance  of  input  and  output  in  the  development  of language.  As  learners
interact with each other through oral and written discourse, their communicative
abilities are enhanced.

WHOLE LANGUAGE EDUCATION

A term that once swept through our profession and is still in common use is whole
langu;껴ge education.  Unfortunately, the term has been so widely and divergently
interpreted that it unfortunately lost the impact that it once had (see Rigg, 1991,for
an  εxcellent review  of whole  languagε education).  Initially  the  term  came  from
reading research and was used to emphasize:

a.  the “wholeness” of language as opposed to views that fragmented language
into its bits and pieces of phonemes, graphemes, morphemes, and words;

b.  the interaction and interconnections between oral language (listening and

speaking) and written language (reading and writing); and

c.  the importance, in literate societies, of the written code as natural and

developmental, just as the oral code is.

Now the term has come to encompass a great deal more.  ￦hole language is a

label that has been used to describe:

•  cooperative learning
•  P따ticipatory learning
•  student-centered learning
•  focus on the community of learners
•  focus on the social nature of language
•  use of authentic, natural language
•  meaning-centered language

[ HAPTER] 까1e Postmethod Era:‘  Toward Informed Approaches

55

•  holistic assessment tεchniques in tεsting
•  intεgration of thε “four skills"

With  all  thεse  int℃rprεtations, the  concept  of  whole  language  has  becomε
considerably  watεrεd  down.  Edεlsk'"Y  (1993,  pp.  550-551)  noted  that  whole
languagε is  not a  rεcipe, and it’S not an activity that you schedule into your lεsson;
“ it  is  an εducational way of life.  [It  helps pεop le to]  build mεaningful connεctions
bεtween everyday learning and school learning.”

It is appropriate, th℃n, that wε usε the term carefully so that it doεs not becomε
just anothεr buzzword for  tεachers and materials dεvelopers.  Two intεrconnected
concεpts arε brought together in whole languagε;

1.  Thε wholenεss of languagε implies that languagε is  not the sum of its many
dissεctible and discrete paπs.  First languagε acquisition research shows us
that children begin perceiving “wholεs” (sentεnces, emotions, intonation
patterns) well before “ parts.'’ Second languagε tεachers thε1℃forε do wε11 to
help their students attend to such wholes and not to yield to the temptation
to build languagε only from the bott。m up.  And since part of thε wholεnεSS
of language includes the intεrrelationship of the four skills  (listεning,
spεaking, reading, and writing), we must consciεntiously intεgrate two or
more of thesε sldlls in our classrooms

2.  Wholε languagε is a  perspεctive “ anchored in a vision of an equitablε,

democratic, div,εrse society” (Edelsky, 1993, p. 548).  Bεcause we use language
to construct mεaning and to construct rεality, tεaching a  languagε enables
lεarnεrs to understand a system of social practices that both constrain and
liberatε.  Part of our job as  teachεrs is  to empower our learners to  libεratε
th emsεlvεs from  whatεver social, p이itical, or econ<] mic forces constrain
them.

CONTENT-BASED INSTRUCTION

Content-based instruction (CBD, according to Brinton, Snow, and Wesche (1989,
p. vii),  is  “ thε integration  of content  learning  with  language  teaching  aims.  Mor℃
specifically, it refiεrs to the concurrent study of language and subject mattεc with thε
form and sequence of language presentation dictated by content material‘” Such an
approach contrasts sharply with many practices in which language skills are taught
virtually in isolation from substantive content.  Through CBI, language becomes the
medium to convey informational content of interεst and relevance  to  the  learner.
Language  takes  on  its  appropriate  role  as  a  vehicle  for  accomplishing  a  set  of
content goals.

A  surge  of interest  in  CBI  in  the  1990s  resulted  in  widespread  adoption  of
content-basεd curricula around the world, as  chroniclεd by Brinton (2003), Stoller

56

CHAPTER  3  The  Postmethod Era:  Toward Informed Approaches

(2004), Sehlεppegrell et al.  (2004), and others, even to the point that Brinton et al.’s
(1989) book was republished with an epilogue in 2003.  Content-based classrooms
have  the  potential  of  increasing  intrinsic  motivation  and  empowerment,  since
students are focused on subject matter that is important to their lives.  Students are
pointed  bεyond transient  extrinsic  factors,  like  grades  and  tests,  to  their  own
competence  and  autonomy  as  intelligent  individuals  capable  of  actually  doing
something with their new language.

The  challenges  of  CBI  range  from  a  demand  for  a  whole  new  genre  of
textbooks  없id  other  materials  to  the  training  of language  teachers  to  teach  the
concepts and  s따lls of various  disciplines, professions, and  occupations, 따id/or to
teach  in  teams  across  disciplines.  Allowing  the  subject  matter  to  control  the
selection  and  sequencing  of language  items  means  that  you  have  to  view  your
teaching from an entirely different perspective.  You are first and foremost teaching
geography or math or culture; secondarily you are teaching language.  So  you may
have  to  become  a  double  expert!  Some  team-teaching  models  of content-based
teaching alleviate this potenti때 drawback.  In some schools a subject-matter teacher
없id a language teacher link their courses and curricula so that each complements
the other.  Such an undertaking is not unlike what Brinton et al. (1989) describe as
an “a띠unct” model of content-based instruction.

Can  content-based  teaching  take  place  at  때l  levels  of  proficiency,  even
be횡nning levels? Whilε it is possible to argue, for εX따nple, that certain basic survival
skills are themselves content-based 없id that a  beginning level class could therefore
be  content-based, such  따1  argument  extends  the  content-based  notion  beyond its
normal  bounds.  Content-based  instruction  usually  pertains  to  academic  or
occupational  instruction  over  없1  extended  period  of  time  at  intermediate-to
advanced proficiency levels.  Talking about renting an apartment one daχ shopping
the  next,  getting  a  driver’s  license  the  next,  and  so  on  is  certainly  useful  따id
meaningful for  beginners, but would be  more appropriately called  task-based  than
content-based.

Several models of CBI  have now emerged.  Theme-based instruction may be

the most common offshoot of CBI; in this model language remains the primaη 없m
of a course, but special attention is given to meanin맹.tl, relevant themes as a point
of departure for instruction in l없iguage.  Sheltered content instruction is a form of
CBI  in which the teacher of a  school subject (say, science or histoη) modifies the
presentation  of material  to  help  L2  learners  process  the  content.  A  little  more
recently,  sustained-content  language  teaching  involves  a  6] cus on  a  “single
content area, or carrier topic ... [along with] a complementary focus on L2  learning
and teaching” (Murphy & Stoller, 2001, p. 3).  Here, the L2  classroom simulates the
structure  따id  demands  of  mainstream  courses  but  adds  explicit  instruction  in
language and academic skills.  All  three models are derived from the principle that
students' meanin뺑d involvement in relevant content W피 enhance acq띠sition.

A lexical εmphasis has somε obvious advantages.  Sometimεs in our penchant for

And cεrtainly a strategic languag'ε lεamεr can accomplish a gr1εat deal with words alonε.
It  rεmains somewhat  unclear’,  howεver, how  such  an  approach  diffi℃rs  from  othεr

Another  possible  qualifier  as  an  approach  lies  in  the  current  interest  in  the
application  of  thε concept  of  Multiple  Intelligences  (Ml)  to  L2  teaching.  As

Your  approach  to  language  teaching  is  obviously  the  keystone  to  all  your
teaching  method이ogy in  the  classroom.  By  now, you  may  bε able  to “ pro tiεSS ” at

predictably mirror those that have been espousεd here, especially since you are just
beginning  to  learn  your  teaching  craft.  That’s  quite  acceptable.  But  do  keep  in
mind the importance of the  dynamic  nature  of the theoretical stance of even  the
most experienced teachers.  We  have much to learn, collectively, in this profession.
And we w피 bεst instruct ourselves, and the profession at large, when we maintain
a  disciplined inquisitivεness about our teaching practices.  After all, that’s  how wε
got to this point after a centuη of questio따ig.

OBJECTIVES  After reading  this  chapter,  you  will  be able  to:

So far in this book you have obseπed a classroom in action, examined a centruγ of
language-teaching history, and taken a look at major constructs that define current
practices in language teaching.  In the foregoing chapters you may have already felt
a  little  bewildered  by  the  sheer  number  of methods  and  approaches  that  have
characterized our profession.  You may be as퍼ng questions like:

“'Am I a learner-centered, interactive, or task-based teacheζ or what?”

“π1e idea of CLT  appeals to me, but out of a number of possible approaches

within the tradition, how can I determine what my approach is?”

“Practicing a cautious, enlightened, eclectic approach is appealing, but isn’t

that way too broad a claim?”

“There are too many options here; how can I make informed choices about

what to do in the particular context of my classroom?”

In order to sort through those questions and find some plausible answers, it is
important for you to consider elements that are at the core of language pedagogy:
foundational principles that can form the building blocks for your own theoretical
rationale.  For  virtually  all  successful  teachers,  such  principles  comprise  their
approach to language teaching.

In Prineψles of Language Learning and Teaching,  (Brown, 2007), which I
refer to here as PLLT,  I noted that the last few  decades of research produced a
complex  storehouse  of  information  on  second  language  acquisition  and
teaching.  We  have  discovered  a  great  deal  about  how  to  best  teach  a  second
language  in  the  classroom.  And, while  many  mysteries  still  remain  about  why
and how learners successfully acquire second languages, it is appropriate for you
to focus  on what we do know: what wε have learned and what we can say with
some certainη about second language acquisition.  We can then clearly see that

62

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

63

a  grεat many  of a  tεachεr’S choices  arε grounded  in  established  principlεs of
languagε learning  and  teaching.  By  perceiving  and  internalizing  connεctions
between  practice  (choices  you  make  in  the  classroom)  and  thεory (principlεs
dεrived from  research), you are  morε likely to engage  in “ enlightened” teaching.
You will be bεtter ablε to see why you have chosen to use a  particular classroom
technique (or sεt of techniques), to carry it out with confidεnce, and to εvaluatε
its  utility  aftεr the fact.

You  may  be  thinking  that  such  a  principled  approach  to  language  Iεaching
sounds  only logical.  How could one proceed otherwise? Well, I  have  seen many a
novice  languagε  teacher  gobble  up  teaching  techniquεs  without  carefully
considering the criteria that underlie their successful application in thε classroom.
‘'Just  give  m℃ 101  recipes for  Monday morning teaching;’ say  somε, “ I  just want to
know what to do when I get into thε classroom.” Unfortunately, this sort of quick-fix
approach  to  teacher  education  will  not  give  you  that  all-important  ability  to
comprehend when to use a  technique, with whom it will work, how to adapt it for
your audiεnc℃, or how to judge its efnεctiveness.

We'll  now take  a  broad, sweeping look  at  12  overarching  principles of second
language learning that interact with sound practice and on which your teaching can
be  basεd.  Thεsε principles  form  the  core  of an  approach  to  language  teaching, as
discussed  in  thε previous  chaptεr.  There  is  no magic  about  the  numbεr 12.  lfyou
rεad Tom Scov,εl (2001) you’u find 5 principles, but if you rεad Bεrnard Spolsky (1989)
you’H discovεr 70!  I have chosen 12 for the sake of simplicity and inclusiveness.

Before proceeding with a  description of the  12  principles, a  special note is  in
order to  readers and instructors who have used the  pr,℃vious εditions of Teaching
낀l Rτηcψ!es.  I  continuε to enumerate 12  principlεs in my list but have made two
changes that  reflεct recεnt rεsearch and thinking.  The revisions are as follows:

•  Autonomy ( #6) is  a  nεw principle.
•  Willingness  to Communicate (WTC) (#8)  now rεplaces Self-confidencε and
Risk-taking  sincε the latter are, in recent research, well accounted for in the
concept of WTC.

One  further  note:  As  you  are  I℃ading, it  may  be  hεlpful  to  check  referenced
sεctions  of  PLLT  to  refresh  your  mεmory of  certain  terms  and  background
information.

COGNITIVE PRINCIPLES

We  will  call  the  first  set  of  principles “ cognitive”  because  they  relate  mainly  to
mental and  intεllεctual functions.  It should be  madε clear, howev1εr, that all  12  of
the  principlεs outlined  in  this  chapter spill  across  somεwhat arbitraπ cognitive,
affiεctive, and linguistic boundaries.
