CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

Principle 1: Automaticity

No  one  can  dispute  the  success  with  which  children  lεarn  foreign  languages,
especially whεn they arε living in  the cultural and linguistic milieu of the language
(see PLLT,  Chapter 3).  We  commonly  attributε childrεn’s success  to  thεir widεly
observed  tendency  to  acquir1ε  language  subconsciously,  that  is,  without  overtly
analyzing  the  forms  of  languagε  themselv1εs.  Through  an  inductive  process  of
exposure  to  language  input  and  opportunity  to  expεriment with  output,  they
appear to learn languages without “ thinking” about them.

This  childlike,  subconscious  processing  is  similar  to  what  Barry  M마.aughlin
(1990; McLaughlin,  Rossman,  &  McLeod,  1983)  called  automatic  procεssing with
periphεral attention  to  language  forms  (PLLT,  Chaptεr 10).  That  is,  in  order  to
manage the incredible complexity and quantity of language-the vast  numbers of
bits of information-both adults and children must sooner or later move away from
processing  language  unit  by  unit,  piece  by  piecε, focusing  dosεly on  each,  and
“graduate” to a form  of high-spεed, automatic  processing in which language forms
(words,  affixes,  word  order,  rules,  etc.)  are  only  on  the  peripheη of  attεntion.
Children  usually  make  this  transition  faster  than  adults,  who  tεnd to  lingεr in
analytical,  controllεd modεs, focusing  on  thε bits  and  pieces  of language  b℃for1℃
putting thosε bits and pieces into thε “ hard drive" of their mind.
The Principle of Automaticity hi힐liights the importance of

•  subconscious absorption of language through meaningful use;
•  efficient and rapid movemεnt away from a focus on the forms of language to

a focus on the purposes to which language is  put;

•  efficient and rapid movemεnt away from  a capacity-limited control of a few
bits  and  pieces  to  a  relatively  unlimited  automatic  mode  of  processing
language forms  (oftεn referred to as fluency); and

•  rεsistance to the temptation to analyze  languag1ε forms.

The Principle of Automaticity may be stated as follows:

Efficient  second  language  learning  involves  a  timely
movement of the control of a  few language forms into the
automatic,  fluent  processing  of  a  relatively  unlimited
number  of  language  forms.  Overanalyzing  language,
t펴nking  too  much  about  its  forms,  and  consciously
tinge뼈g on  rules  of  language  때l  tend  to  impede  d피S
graduation to 없itomaticity.

r

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

65

Notice  that  this  principle  does  not  say  that  focus  on  language  forms  is
necessarily harmful.  In fact adults, especiaUκ can benefit greatly from certain focal
processing  of rules,  definitions,  and  other formal  aspects  of language.  ￦hat the
principle  does  say  is  that  adults  can  take  a  lesson  from  children  by  speedily
overcoming our propensity to pay too much focal attention to the bits and pieces
of language and to move language forms quickly to the peripheπ by using language
in  authentic  contεxts for  meaningful  purposes.  In  so  doing, automaticity  is  built
more efficiently.

What does this principle, which ordinarily applies to adult instruction, mean to

you as a  teacher? Here are some possibilities:

1.  Because classroom learning normally begins with controlled, focal

processing, there is  no mandate to entirely avoid overt attεntion to language
systems (grammar, phonology, discoursε, etc.).  That attention, however,
should stop well short of blocking studεnts from achieving a  more
automatic, fluent grasp of the language.  Therefore, grammatical
explanations or exercises dealing with what is  sometimes called “ usage”
have a  place in the adult classroom (seε Principle  12), but you could
overwhelm your students with grammar.  If they become too heavily
centered on the formal aspects of language, such processes can block
pathways to fluency.

2.  Make sure that a large proportion of your lessons are focused on the “use” of
language for purposes that are as genuine as a classroom context will permit.
Students will gain more language compεtence in the long run 표 the
functional purposes of language are the focal point.

3.  Automaticity isn’t  gained overnight; therefore, you need to exercise patience

with students as you slowly help them to achieve fluency.

Principle 2:  Meaningful Lear피ng

Closεly related  to  the  Principle  of Automaticity are  cog띠tive theories  of learning
(PLLT,  Chapter 4), which convincingly argue the strength of meaningful as opposed
to rote learning (Ausubel, 1963).  Meaningful learning “subsumes” new information
into  existing  structures  and  memotγ systems,  and  the  resulting  associative  links
create  strongεr retention.  Rote  learning-taking  in  isolated  bits  and  pieces  of
information  that  are  not  connected with one’S  앉isting cognitive  structures-has
little  chance  of  creating  long-term  retention.  Children  are  good  meaningful
acquirers  of  language  (sεe  Principle  1)  because  they  associate  sounds,  words,
structures, and  discourse  elements  with  that  which  is  relevant  and  impoπant in
their daily quest for knowledge and survival.

66

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

The Principle of Meaningful Learning is quite simply stated:

The  process  of making  meaningful  associations  between
e화sting knowledge/experience and new material will lead
toward  better  long-term  retention  than  rote  learning  of
material in isolated pieces.

The  languag'ε classroom  has  not  always  bεen thε bεst place  for  meani앵ful
learning.  In  the  days  when  the Audiolingual  Method  (see  PLLT,  Chapter  4)  was
popular, rote learning occupied too much of the class hour as  students were drilled
and drillεd in an attempt to “overlεarn ” language forms.  The Principlε ofMεaningful
Learning  tells  us that some aural-oral  drilling is  appropriate; selεcted phonological
εlements like phonemes, rhythm, stress, and intonation, for  examplε, can indeed be
taught effectively through pattern repetition.  But  drilling ad  nausεam easily lends
itself to rote learning.

Somε classroom implications of the Principle of Meaningful  Learning  include

the following:

1.  Capitalize on thε power of meaningful learning by appealing to students’

intεrests, academic goals, and career goals.

2.  Whenever a new topic or concept is introduced, attempt to anchor it in

students’ existing knowledge and background so that it becomes associated
with something thεy airεady know.

3.  Avoid the pitfalls of rotε learning:

a.  too much grammar explanation
b.  too many abstract principles and thεories
c.  too much drilling and/or memorization
d.  activities whose purposes are not clear
e.  activities that do not contribute to accomplishing the goals of the

f.

lesson, unit, or coursε
techniques that are so mechanical or tricky that students focus on the
mechanics instead of on thε language or mεanings

Principle 3: The Anticipation of Reward

B. F.  Skinner and othεrs hav1ε clearly dεmonstrated thε strength of rewards in both
animal  and  human  behavior (see PLLT,  Chapter 4).  Virtually  eveηrthing we  do  is
inspirεd and driven  by a  sεnse of purpose  or goal,  and  according  to  Skinnεr, the
anticipation of reward is the most powerful factor in dirεcting one’s behavior.  The
principlε behind  Skinnεr’S opεrant conditioning paradigm, which could  be  callεd
thε R℃ward Principle, can be stated as follows:

•

CHAPπR 4

1능·aching by Principles

67

Hum없I beini품 are universally driven to act, or “behave,” by
the  anticipation  of  some  sort  of  reward-tangible  or
intanf앨hie, short-term  or  long-tenn-강iat will  ensue  as  a
result of the behavior.」

The implications for the classroom are obvious.  At one end of the spectrum,
you can perceive the importance of the immεdiate administration of such rewards
as praise for correct responsεs (“Very good, Maria! ”“Nice job!”), appropriate grades
or  scores  to  indicate  success,  or  other  public  recognition.  At  the  other  end,  it
behooves you to help students to see clearly why they are doing something and its
relevance to their long-tεrm goals in learning English.  On the other hand, a reward
driven, conditioning thεory of learning has some shortcomings that ultimately have
a  high  impact  on  classroom  instruction.  These  shortcomings  are  summarized
under Principle 4, but for the moment, keep in mind that conditioning by rewards
can (a) lead learners to become dependent on short-term rewards, (b) coax them
into a habit of looking to teachers and others for their only rewards, and therefore
(c) forestall the development of their own internally administered, intrinsic system
of rewards.

Considering  all  sides  of  the  Reward  Principle,  the  following  constructiv,ε

classroom implications may be drawn:

1.  Provide an optimal degree of immediate verbal praise and εncouragement
to students as a form of short-term reward (just enough to keep them
confident in their ability but not so much that your praise simply becomes
vεrbal gush).

2.  Encourag1ε students to reward each other with compliments and supportive

action.

3.  In classes with veπ low motivation, short-term reminders of progress may
help students to pεrceive their development.  Gold stars and stickers
(especially for young learners), issuing certain “privileges” for good work, and
progress charts and graphs may spark some interest.

4.  Display enthusiasm and excitement yourself in the classroom.  If you are dull,
lifeless, bored, and have low ene탱y, you can be almost sure that it will be
contag10us.

5.  Try to get learners to see the long-term rewards in learning En횡ish by

pointing out what they can do with En행sh where they live and around the
world, the prestige in being able to use En웰sh, the academic benefits of
knowing En맹sh, jobs that requirε English, and so on.

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

Principle 4: Intrinsic Motivation

This principle is elaborated upon in detail in the next chapter as an example of how
complex principles underlie a surprising number of our teaching practices.  Simply
stated, the Intrinsic Motivation Principle is:

πie most powerful rewards are those that are  intrinsiα페ly
motivated  within  안ie learner.  Because  the  behavior  stems
from  nee:화s, W없its, or desires within oneself  야ie behavior
itself is self-rewarding; therefor℃, noexterna퍼7 때ministered
reward is necessary.

If all  learners were intrinsically motivated to  perform all  classroom tasks, we
might not even need teachers!  But you can perform a great service to learners 따id
to the overall learning process by first considering carefully the intrinsic motives of
your students and then by designing classroom tasks that feed  into those intrinsic
drives.  Classroom  techniques have  a  much greater chance for  success if they are
self-rewarding  in  the  perception  of  the  learner.  The  learners  perform  the  task
because it is fun, interesting, useful, or challenging, and not because they anticipate
some cognitive or affective rewards from the teacher.

You  may  be  wondering  why  such  a  principle  is  listed  among  “cognitive”
principles.  The development of intrinsic motivation does indeed involve affective
processing,  as  most  of  these  first  five  principles  do,  and  so  the  argument  is
appropriate.  But reward갑irected behavior in all organisms is complex to the point
that  cognitive, physical, and  affective  processing are  all  involved.  In  the  specific
case  of  second  language  acquisition,  mental  functions  may  actually  occupy  a
greater proportion of the  whole  than  the  other two  domains, as  we shall  see  in
Chapter 5.

Principle 5: Strategic Investment

A few decades ago, the language-teaching  profession largely concerned itself with
the “delivery”  of language  to  the  student.  Teaching  methods, textbooks, or even
g없nmatical paradigms were cited as the primary factors in successful learning.  In
more  recent  years,  in  the  light  of many  studies  of successful  and  unsuccessful
learners, language teachers are focusing more intently on the role of the learner in
the process.  The “methods” that the learner employs to internalize and to perform
in the language are as  important as the teacher's methods-or more so.  I call this
the Principle of Strate앙C Investment:

•

CHAPTER  4  Teaching  by Principles

69

Successful mastery of 야ie second language will be due to a
large  extent  to  a  learn앙’S own  personal  “investm.ent'’  of
time, effort, and attention to 야ie second language in the form
of an individualized battery 。f Strate혈.es for compreheni하ng
and producing 야ie language.

This principle is laid out in full dεtail in Chapter 14, where practical classroom
applications are made.  For the time being, however, ponder two major pedagogical
implications of the principle: (a) the importance of recognizing and dealing with the
widε variety of styles and strategies that learners successfully bring to the learning
process and, therefor,ε, (b) the need for attention to each separate individual in thε
classroom.

As  research  on  successful  language  learners  has  dramatically  shown,  the
variation  among  learners  poses  a  thorny  pedagogical  dilemma.  Learning  styles
alone  signal  numerous  learner preferences that  a  teacher needs  to  attend to (see
PLLT,  Chapter  5).  For  example, visual  versus  auditory  preference  and  individual
versus  group  work  prefer,εnce are  highly  significant  factors  in  a  classroom.  In  a
related strain of research, we are finding  that learners also employ a  multipliciη of
strategies  for  sending  and  receiving  language  and  that one  learner's  strategies for
success may differ markedly from another’s.

A variety of techniques in your lessons will at least partially ensur,ε that you will
“ reach” a  maximum  number  of students.  So  you will  choosε a  mixture  of group
work and individual  work, of visual  and  auditotγ techniques, of easy  and difficult
exercises.  Beware, however, of variety at the expense of techniques that you know
are essential for the  learner!  If, for  example, you know that three-quarters of your
class  prefers  individual  work, that  should  not  dictate  the  proportion  of time  you
devote  to  activities  that  involve  silent work  at  their desks.  They  may  need  to  be
nudged, if not  pushed, into  mor'ε face-to-face  communicative  activities  than  their
preferences would indicate.

A teacher's greatest dilemma is  how to attend to each individual student in a
class while still reaching the class as a whole group.  In relatively large classes of 30
to  50  students,  individual  attention  becomes  increasin횡y difficult;  in  extra-large
classes* it is virtually impossible.  The Principle of Strategic Investment nevertheless
is a reminder to provide as much attention as you can to each individual student.

*Around the world, far too m따iy language class sizes ar,ε too large.  Numbers in the
range of 50-75 are not uncommon.  For years I’ve tried to persuade administrators to
lower those numbers and to understand that communicative acquisition of a language
is veπ difficult to achieve under such circumstances.  Nevertheless, the reality of
school budgets sometimes provides few alternatives.  See Chapter 15 for some
practical su잃estions for dεaling with large classes.

Principle 6:  Autonomy

One way of looking at the histoη7 of language teaching, described in Chapter 2, is to
consider  the  εxtent  to  which  mεthodological  trεnds  have  emphasized  the
respεctive  roles  of  the  teacher  and  the  learner.  Until  some  of  the  “ designer”
methods  appeared  in  the  1970s,  most  of  language-teaching  methodology  was
teacher-centεred.  Students  entεrεd a  classroom, sat  down dut띠illy at  thεir desks,
and  waitεd for  the  tεachεr to  tεII them what  to  do.  Those  directives  mi앙1t  have
been to translate  a passage, to memorize a rule, or to repεat a  dialogue.  Then, thε
profession began to value  the  concεpt of learner autonomy (Benson, 2001, 2003;
Schmenk,  2005;  Wenden,  2002),  which  Benson  (2001,  p.  290)  dεfined  as  “ the
capacity  to  control  onε’s  own  learning.”  Autonomy  is  now  almost  univ1εrs ally
manifestεd in thε classroom in the form of allowing learnεrs to do things like initiate
oral production, solve problems in small groups, and practice language with pεεrs.
Of utmost importance, language  curricula rεcognized the crucial objectiv'ε of
helping  learners  to  use  the  languagε  outside  of  the  classroom.  We  began  to
encourage  learners  to “take  charge” of their own learning and to  chart their own
“ pathways to success” (Brown,  1989, 2002b; Benson, 2003).  With  thε principle of
students'  taking  responsibility  for  their  own  learning,  yet  another  important
pedagogical foundation stone was set in place.

Briefly, the Principlε of Autonomy states:

Successful mastery of a  foreign language will depend to  a
흉·eat extent on l앓rners’  autonomous  ability both to  take
initiative in the classroom and to continue their journey to
succ엉S beyond the classroom 없id the teacher.

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Princip’es

71

Some have argued (Riley, 1988) that the Principle of Autonomy is a culturally
loaded, ethnocentric  construct-anything but universal  in  its  conceptualization.
Others  suggest  using  some  caution  in  making  assumptions  across  cultural
contεxts and to account for “ specific cultural backdrops and impacts” (Schmenk,
2005,  p.115)  in  promoting  autonomy  in  the  language  classroom.  Once  those
accommodations  have  been  appropriately  addressed, you  should  by  no  means
refrain  from  helping your  studεnts to  participate  actively  in  linguistic  exchange
and  to  continuε their  learning  beyond the  walls  of your classroom.  Consider a
number of classroom implications of this principle:

1.  Learners at the beginning stages of a language will of course be somewhat
dεpendent on the teacher, which is  natural and normal.  But teachεrs can
hεlp even beginners to develop a sense of autonomy through guided practice
and by allowing some creative innovation within limited forms.

2.  As  learners gain confidencε and begin to be able to experimεnt with

language, implement activities in the classroom that allow creativity but are
not completely beyond the capacity of students.

3.  Don’t  forget that pair and group work and other interactiv,ε activities in

your classroom provide opportunities for students to “do” language on their
own.

4.  In oral and written production in  the classroom, encourage creativity and
praise students for trying language that’s  a little beyond their present
capacity.

5.  Remember, you’re a facilitator and coach, so while your students are in your
“ care,'' provide feedback on their speech-just enough to be helpful, but not
so much that you stifle their creativity.

6.  Suggest opportunities for students to use their language (gaug,℃d for their

proficiency level) outside of class.  Examples include movies, TV, thε Internet,
books, magazines, practicing with each other, and-if feasible-using self
access centεrs available in some institutions.

SOCIOAFFECTIVE PRINCIPLES

The  Principles  of  Intrinsic  Motivation,  Strategic  Investment,  and Autonomy  are
clearly  not  purely  cognitive  in  nature.  They  share  some  attributes  with
socioaffective  principles,  the  focus  of this  section  of the  chapter.  But  the  next
three  principles  are  characterized  by  a  more  marked  degree  of  emotional
involvement, either within one’S own self or as a learner relates socially to others.
Here  we  look  at  feelings  about  sεlf,  about  communicating  with  others  in  a
community of learners, and  about  the  ties  between  language  and  one’s  culture,
worldview, and way of l표C

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

Principle 7:  Lan핑1age Ego

The Language Ego  Principle can be summarized in a well-recognized claim:

As human beings learn to use a  second language, they 떼so
develop a  new mode of thinking, feeling, 없td aαin앙-융
second identity.  The new “language ego,” intertwined
with the second language, can easily create within the
learner a  sense of fr쟁ility, a  defensiveness, and a  raising
of inhibitions.

The Languag1ε Ego  Principle might also be affectionately called the “warm and
fuz끽” principle: All  second  language  learners  need  to  be  treated  with  affective
tender loving care.  Remember when you were first learning a second language and
how you sometimes felt silly, if not humiliated, when the lack of words or structure
left you helpless in face-to-face communication? Otherwise highly intelligent adults
can be reduced to babbling infants in a second language.  Learners feel this fragility
because  the  strategic  arsenals  of  their  native-language-based  egos,  which  are
normally well developεd and resistant to attack, arε suddenly-in the perception of
야ie learner-obsolete.  Now they must fend for their emotional selves with a paltη
linguistic battεry that leaves them with a feeling of total defenselessness.

How can you bring some relief to this situation and provide affective support?

Here are some possibilities.

1.  Overtly display a supportive attitude to your students.  While some learners
may feel quite stupid in this new language, remember that they are capable
ad띠ts struggling with the acquisition of thε most complex set of skills that
없iy classroom has ever attempted to teach.  Your “warm 없id fuz강” pa tiεnee
and empathy nεed to be ope띠y and clearly communicated, for fragile
language egos hav1ε a way of misinterpreting intended input.

2.  On a more mechanical, lesson-planning level, your choice of techniques 따id

sequences of techniques needs to be cognitively challenging but not
overwhelming at an affective level.

3.  Considering learners' language ego states will probably help you to determine

•  who to call on
•  who to ask to volunteer information
•  when to correct a student’s speech error
•  how much to expl따n something
•  how structured and planned an acti찌다 should be
•  who to place in which small groups or pairs
•how “tough” you can be with a student

•

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

73

4.  If your students are learning En밍ish as a second language (in the cultural
milieu of an English-speaking country), they are likely to expεrience a
moderate identity crisis as they develop a “ second self.” Help such students to
understand that the confusion of developing that second self in the sεcond
culture is a normal and natural process (see PLLT,  Chapter 7).  Patience and
understanding on your part will also ease the process.

Principle 8: Willingness to Communicate

Closely  allied  to the Language  Ego  Principle  is  a construct that is  a  relatively  rεcent
nεwcomer to  second  l따iguage  acq버sition  rese따℃h: W「피ingness  to  Communicate
(WfC) (Macintyre, B때er, Clement, &  Conrod, 2001; Macintyre, Clement, D6rnyei, &
Noεls, 1998; Yashima, 2002), simply defined as“the intention to initiate communication,
given  a  choicε” αfaclntyre et al.,  2001, p. 369).  Observations  of language  learners'
uηW피ingness to communicate, or what we commonly label as “shyness사rave led us to
εmphasize classroom activity that εncourages learners to “come out of their shells” and
to εngage communicatively in thε classroom.

It  has  already  been  briefly  noted  that  WTC  combines  concepts  of  self
confidence and risk-taking,  as  they are  both interwoven in our human psyche.
Of further importance are two other related constructs: anxiety, that is, the extent
to which learners may “worπ” about themselves; and self-efficacy, a person’s belief
in  his  or  her  ability  to  accomplish  a  task  (See  PLLT,  Chapter  6,  for  further
description).  Linked to one’s self-confidence (and allaying anxieties) is the ability to
take  calculated  risks  in  attεmpting  to  usε  language-both  productively  and
receptively.  If learners recognize their own ego fragility and develop the firm belief
that,  yes,  they  can  indeed  do  it  (self efficacy), then  they  are  ready  to  take  those
necessaπ risks.  They are  ready to try out their newly acquired languag'ε, to use  it
for meaningful purposes, to ask questions, and to assert themselves.

This eighth principle may be summarizεd as follows:

Successful language learners generally believe in themselves
and in their capacity to  accomplish communicative  tasks,
없id are  therefore  willing  risk 없kers in  their  attempts  to
produce and to inti앙pret language that is a  bit beyond their
absolute  certainty.  Their  willingness  to  communicate
results in the generation of bo앙1 output (from the learner)
and input (to the learner).

The  concept  of WTC  strikes  at  the  heart  of educational  philosophy,  and  it
appears to be applicable across many cultures (Yashima, 2002).  Many instructional
contexts  do  not  encourage  risk-ta따ng; instead  they  encourage  correctness, right
answers, and withh이ding “guesses” until one is sure to be correct.  Most educational

74

[ HAPTER 4  Teaching by Principles

rεsearch shows  the  opposite  to  bε mor℃ conducivε to  long-tεrm I℃tεntion and
intrinsic motivation.  How can your classrooms reflect the Principlε ofWfC?

1.  Give ample vεrbal and nonverbal assurances to  studεnts, affirming your bεlief
in the studεnt ’s ability.  Energy that the learner would othεrwisε direct at
avoidance or at erecting emotional walls of defense is  thεr℃by rεleased to

2.  Sequεncε techniques from easier to morε difficult.  As  a teachεr you arε
callεd on to sustain self confidencε whεrε it air,εady exists and to build it
where it doesn’t.  Your activities in the classroom would ther,℃fore logically

3.  Create an atmosphere in the classroom that encouragεs studεnts to try out

language, to venturiε a response, and not to wait for  somεonε else to volunteer
language.

4.  Providε rεasonable challengεs in your techniques-makε them neither too

easy nor too hard.

5.  Help your studεnts to understand what calculatεd risk-taking is, lεst somε feel

that they must blurt out any old response.

6.  Respond to studεnts’ attempts to communicate with positiv1ε affirmation,

praising them for trying while at the same time warmly but firmly attending
to their language.

Principle 9: The Language-Culture Connection

Language and culture are intricatεly intεrtwinεd.  Any  time you  succεssfully lεarn a
language,  you  will  also  lεam something  of  thε culture  of  thζ speakers  of  that
language.  This  principlε focuses  on the complex intεrconn℃ction of language and
culture:

Whenever you teach a  language, you also teach a  complex
system of cultural customs,  values,  and ways  of thinking,
feeling, and acting.

Classroom applications include the following:

1.  Discuss cross-cultural differencεs with your students, emphasizing that no
culture is “ better” than another, but that cross-cultural understanding is an
important facet of learning a languagε.

2.  Includε among your techniques certain activities and matεrials that illustrate

thε connection bεtw℃en language and culture.

r

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

75

3.  Teach your students the cultural connotations, especially the sociolinguistic

aspects, of language.

4.  Screen your techniques for material that may be culturally offensive.
5.  Make explicit to your students what you may take for granted in your culture.

A second aspect of the language-culture connection is the extent to which your
students will themselves be affected by the process of acculturation, which will vaη
with  the  context  and  the  goals  of  learning.  In  many  second-language-learning
contexts, such  as  ESL  in  the  United  States, students  are  faced  with  the  full-blown
realities  of adapting  to  life  in  a  foreign  countπ, complete  with  various  emotions
accompanying  stages  of  acculturation  (see  Chapter  7  of  PLLT).  In  such  cases,
acculturation, social  distance, and psychological  adjustment  are factors  to  be dealt
with.  This aspect of the principle may be summed up in this way:

F.sp떠따ly in second (as opposed toforetgη) language-le양피ng
contexts,  the  suα:ess wi앙l  which  learners  adapt  to  a  new
cultural milieu will affect d떠r language  &웬:rlsition success,
andvicever앓, in some possibly sigi뼈.cant ways.

From thε perspectiv1ε of the classroom tεacher, this principle is similar to the
Principles  of  Language  Ego  and  Willingness  to  Communicate,  and  all  the
concomitant  classroom  implications  apply  here  as  well.  An  added  dimension,
however, lies in the intεraction between cultur1ε learning and language learning.  An
opportunity is  given  to teachers  to  enhance, if not speed up, both dεvelopmental
processes.  Once  stud℃nts become aware  that  some of their discouragement  may
stem from cultural sources, they can more squarely address their state of mind and
emotion and do something about it.

In the classroom, you can do the following:

1.  Help students to be aware of acculturation and its stages.
2.  Stress the importance of the second languagε as a powerful tool for

adjustment in the new culture.

3.  Be especially sensitive to any students who appear to be discouraged, then do

what you can to assist them.

LINGUISTIC PRINCIPLES

The  last  categoη of  principles  of  language  learning  and  teaching  centers  on
language itself and on how learners dεal with complex linguistic systems.

Principle 10: The Native Language Effect

It  almost  goes  without  saying  that  the  nativ,ε  language  of  eveη learner  is  an
extremely significant factor in the acquisition of a new language.  Most of the time,
we  think  of the  native  language  as  exercising  an  interfering  effect  on  thε target
languagε, and indeed the most salient, observable  εffect does appεar to be onε of
interference (see PLLT,  Chapter 4).  The majority of a learner's εrrors in producing
the  second  language,  especially  in  the  beginning  levels,  stem  from  the  learner’s
assumption that the target language operates like the native language.

But what we observe ma·χ like the tip of  an iceberg, bε only part of the rεality.
The facilitating effects of the native language are surely as powerful in the process,
or more so, even thou~강1 they are less observable.  When the native French speaker
who is  learning  English  says “I  am  here  since January,”  there is  one salient  native
language effect, a verb tense error stemming from French.  But the learner’s native
French  may  also  have  facilitated  the  production  of that  sentence’s  subject-viεrb­
com plεment word  order,  the  placement  of  the  locative  (here),  the  one-to-one
grammatical correspondence of the  othεr words in  the sentence, rules  governing
prepositional phrases, and the cognatε word (jaηuaη).

The  Principle  of the  Native  Language  Effect  stresses  the  importance  of that

native system in the linguistic attempts of the sεcond language learner:

The native langt뻐ge of learners exerts a strong influence on
the  acquisition  of the  target language  system.  While  that
native system will exercise both facilitating and interfering
effects on the production and comprehension of the  new
language,  the  interfering effects  are likely to be the  most
salient.

In your dealing with the Native Language Effect in the classroom, your feedback
will most often focus  on interference.  That’s perfectly sound pedagogy.  Learners’
errors stand out like the tips of icebergs, giving us salient signals of an underlying
system  at  work.  Errors  are,  in  fact,  windows  to  a  learner's  internalized
understanding of the second language, 없id therefore they give teachers something
observable  to  react  to.  Student  non-errors-the facilitating  effects-certainly do
not need to be treated.  Don’t  tπ to fix something that isn’t  broken.

Some classroom su잃.estions stemming from the Native Language Effect:

1.  Regard learners' eπors as imp야tant windows to their underlying system 따id
provide appropriate feedback on them (see Principle 11  and Chapter 17 for
more information on feedback).  Errors of native language interference may be
repaired by acqu없nting the learner with the native language cause of the error.

함

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

77

2.  Ideally, eveηr successful learner will hold on to the facilitating effects of the

native language and discard the interference.  Help your students to
understand that not everyπhing about their native language system w피 cause
error.

3.  Thinking directly in the target language usually helps to minimize interference
errors.  Tη to co짧 students into thinking in the second language instead of
resorting to translation as they comprehend and produce language.  An
occasional translation of a word or phrasε can actually be helpful, especially
for adults, but direct use of the second language will help to avoid the first
language “ crutch” syndrome.

Principle 11: Interlanguage

Just as  children develop  their native  language  in gradual, systematic  stages, adults,
too,  manifest  a  systematic  progression  of acquisition  of sounds  and  words  and
structures  and  discourse  features  (see  PLLT,  Chapter  8).  The  Interlanguage
Principle tells us:

Second language learners tend to go through a  systema성C
or q없si-systematic developmental procc용S as they
pr,앵ress to full competence in the target langt빼ge.
Successful interlanguage development is par썼와ly a  result
of utilizing feedback from others.

While  the  intεrlanguage of second language  learners varies  considεrably (see
PLLT,  Chapter  9,  on  variability)  between  systematic  and  unsystεmatic linguistic
forms and underlying rules, one important concept for the teacher to bear in mind
is  that  at  least  some  of a  learner's  language  may  indeed  be  systematic.  In  other
words, in the mind’S  eye of learners, a good deal of what they say or comprehend
may be  logically "correct” even  though, from  the  standpoint of a  native  speaker's
competence, its  use  is  incorrect.  A  learnεr who  says “ Does John  can  sing?” may
believe  it  to  be  a  correct  grammatical  utterance  because  of  an  internalized
systematic  rule  that  requires  a  pre-posed  do  auxiliary  for  English  question
formation.

Allowing  learners  to  pr앵ress through  such  systematic  stages  of acquisition
po않S  a  delicate  challenge  to  teachers.  The  collective  experience  of  language
teachers and a  respectable stockpile of second language  research (Doughty, 2003;
Ellis, 2005) indicatεs that classroom instruction makes a significant difference in the
speed and  success  with which  learners  proceed  through  interlanguage  stages  of
development.  This  highlights  the  import따ice of the  feedback  that  you  밍veto
learners in the classroom.  In many settings (especially in EFL  contexts where few

How,  then,  do  you  know  what  kind  of  feedback  to  offer  students?  Arε

internalization  of  a  language?  These  important  questions  are  to  some  extent
answered in Chaptεr 17.  For the moment, however, a number of general classroom
implications deservε your attention:

1.  Tη to distinguish betweεn a student’s systematic  interlanguag'℃ εrrors

(stemming from the nativ1ε language or target language) and other errors; the
former will probably havε a logical source that the student can be made

2.  Teachers need to exercisε somε tolerance for certain interlanguage forms  that

3.  Don’t  make a student feel stupid because of an interlanguage error; quietly

5.  Tηr to gεt students to sεlf correct selεcted errors; the ability to sεlfcorrect

7.  As you make judicious selection of which errors to treat (see Chapter  lη, do

Principle 12: Communicative Competence

Whilε communicativε competencε (CC)  has  comε to  capturε a  multiplicity  of
mεanings depεnding on who you ask, it is  n℃verthεlεSS a  USεful phrase.  Look back
at  Chapter  3  here  and  rεcall  the  dεscription  of  CLT,  and  you  will  seε  somε
combination of the following components of CC, which stem from Bachman ( 1990)
and thε sεminal Canalε and Swain (1980):

•  organizational compεt℃nce (grammatical and discourse)
•  pragmatic competence (functional and sociolinguistic)
•  stratεgic comp℃tεncε
•  psychomotor skills

Thε array of studies on CC  providεs what is  probably thε most sweeping and

compr1εhensive linguistic principle, if not thε most important:

Given  that  communicative  competence  is  the  goal  of  a
language classroom,  instruction needs  to point toward all
its  components:  organizational,  pragmatic,  strategic,  and
psychomotor.  Communicative  goals  are  best  achieved  by
giving due attention to language use and not just usage,  to
fluency  and  not  just  acctπacy, to  authentic  language  and
contexts, and to students’ eventual need to apply classroom
learning  to  previously  unrehearsed  contexts  in  the  real
world.

It  is  important  to  note  that  the  CC  principlε still  has  a  bit  of a  rεactionist
flavor:  rεacting  to  othεr paradigms  that  emphasizεd attention  to  grammatical
forms;  to  “ correct”  language  abovε  all;  to  artificial,  contrived  languagε  and
techniques  in  thε classroom;  and  to  a  finitε  rεpεrtoirε of  languagε forms  and
functions  that might not hav1ε lent themsεlv1εs to application in the world outside
thε classroom.  But since  most of our language-teaching  gεnεralizations are,  aftεr
all, at  least partially  conceivεd against  thε backdrop of prεvious practices, such a
statement  can  stand  as  a  reasonably  accuratε  description  of  our  currεnt
undεrstanding of CC.

To  attεmpt to  list  all  the  applications  of such  a  principlε to  thε language
classroom  would  be  an  exhaustive  εndeavor!  Many  such  applications  will
become  evident in  later chapters  of this  book.  But for  thε sake  of closurε and
simplicity,  consider  thε  following  six  classroom  teaching  “ rulεs”  that  might

εm℃rge:

80

CHAPTER  4  Teaching by Principles

1.  Remember that grammatical explanations or drills or exercises are only p았t

of a lesson or curriculum; give grammar some attention, but don’t  neglect the
other important components (e.g., functional, sociolinguistic, psychomotoι
and strategic) of CC.

2.  Some of the pragmatic (functional and sociolinguistic) aspects of language are
very subtle and therefore veπ difficult.  Make sure your lessons aim to teach
such subtlety.

3.  In your enthusiasm for teaching functional and sociolinguistic aspects of
language, don’t  forget that the psychomotor skills (pronunciation) are an
important component of both.  Intonation alone conveys a great deal of
pragmatic information.

4.  Make SW’e  that your students have opportunities to g따n some fluency in
En밍ish without having to be constantly wary of little mistakes.  They can
work on errors somε other time.

5.  Try to keep every technique that you use as authentic as possible: Use

language that students will actually encounter in the real world and provide
genuine, not rote, techniques for the actual convey:따ice of information of
interest.

6.  Some day your students will no longer be in your classroom.  Make sure you
are prepa다ng them to be independent learners and manip띠ators of language
“ out there."

까ie 12 principles that have just bεen reviewed (listed for your conv1εnience in
Table  4.1)  are  some  of the  major foundation  stones  for  teaching  practice.  While

insights on which your mεthodology can be based.  With these  12  principlεs, you
should  be  able  to  evaluate  a  course,  a  textbook,  a  group  of  students,  and  an
educational  context, and  to  determine  courses  of action  in  the  classroom.  You
should be able to assεSS thε strεngths 따id weaknesses of lessons you’ve observed
or lessons  you  plan  to  teach.  In  short, you  should  be  able  to  frame  your  own
때ljJroach  by  considering  the  extent  to  which  the  12  principles  inform  your
understanding of how languages are learned and taught.

I  hope  you  have  gained  from  this  discussion  a  realization  of the  value  of
undeq잊r떠ng your  teaching  (and  your  teacher  training  process)  with  sound
principles  that  help  you  to  understand  why you  choose  to  do  something  in  the
classroom: what kinds of questions to ask yourself before the fact about what you
are doing, how to monitor yourself while you are teaching, how to assess after the

next time around.

For every complicated problem there is an answer
that is short, simple, and wrong.

- H. L. Mencken

One of the morε complicated problems of second language  learning 따id teaching
has been to define and apply the construct of motivation in the classroom.  On the
one hand, it is an easy catchword that gives teachers a simple 없iswer to the mysteries
of  language  learning.  “Motivation  is  the  difference,"  I  have  heard  people  say,
“between  success  and  failure.  If they’re  motivated,  they’U  learn,  and  if  not,  they
won't.” That simplification  may  hold some of the  time.  Why  not all  the  time? Just
what is motivation? Can it be acq띠red, or is it just “there”? Can it be taught? Where
does  it  come  from?  Are  there  different  kinds  of motivation?  If you  don’t  address
questions  like  these  carefullχ you  run the  risk  of passing  off motivation  as  one of
H. L. Mencken’S short, simple answers to learner success when it is neither short nor
simple.  Ironically,  motivation  is  not  the  “wrong”  없iswer  to  explaining  learner
success, but  it  is  “ri힐lt” only  when  its  full  complexiη is  recognized  and  applied
appropriately in the language classroom.

In the previous chapter, 12 principles of language learning and teaching were
e었mined.  Underlying each of those 12 is a complex array of research and practice
that should remind  us that foundational  principles are  not simple  constructs that
can be adequately defined in a brief maxim.  One of the 12 principlεs was intrinsic
motivation.  In 야tis chapter we will take a  long, careful look at the complexity 없id
power of intrinsic motivation.

DEFINING MOTIVATION

CHAPTER  5

Intrinsic Motivation  in  the Classroom

85

How would you define motivation? Lεt me offer the following “ dictionary definition"
drawn  from  a  number of different  sourcεs: Motivation  is  the  extent  to  which you
make  choices  about (a)  goals  to  pursue  and  (b)  the  effort  you  will  devote  to  that
pursuit.

You  can  interpr,εt this definition in vaπing ways, dεpending on the theorγ of
human behavior you adopt.  Let’s  look at  thεoriεs of motivation  in  tεrms of three
different viewpoints.  One of these pεrspectives is  a  traditional view of motivation
that accounts for human behavior through a  behavioral paradigm that stresses the
importancε  of  rewards  and  reinforcemεnt.  Another  dustεr  of  pεrspεctives
contains  a  number  of  cognitive  psychological  theories  that  explain  motivation
through dεeper, less observable phenomena.  A third way of looking at motivation
involves a constructivist view that emphasizεs social context and personal choices.
These  three traditions  arε describεd bεlow.  (For further  perspectiv1εs on defining
motivation, especially constructivist viεws of motivation, see PLLτ Chapter 6.)

1. A  behavioral definition

A  behavioral  psychologist  like  Skinner  or  Watson  would  stress  the  role  of
rewards  (and  perhaps  punishments)  in  motivating  bεhavior.  In Skinner’s  operant
conditioning  model,  for  εxample, human  beings,  like  other  living  야ganisms, w피
pursue  a  goal  becausε they  perceive  a  rεward for  doing so.  This  reward  servεs to
reinforce  behavior:  to  cause  it  to  persist.  This  tradition  gav1ε  us  what  I  might
facetiously  refiεr to  as  the “ M&M  theory” of bεhavior, derived from  the now seldom
practiced administration of M&M  candiεs to childrεn for manifesting desired behavior.
A behaviorist would define  motivation as “ the  anticipation of rεinforcement.”
We  do well to heεd the credibility of such a definition.  There is  no question that a
tremendous proportion of what W℃ do is motivatεd by an anticipated reward.  From
eating to exercising to studying and εvεn to altruistic acts of ministεring to others,
there  is “something  in  it  for  me.” The  εmotional ovεrtones of the  mor1ε intangiblε
rewards  must  not  b℃ ignored.  M&Ms,  hugs, and  laughter are  all,  at  times, payoffs
worth striving for.

Reinforcement theory is  a  powεrful concept for  thε classroom.  Learnεrs, like
the  prov'εrbial  horse  running  after  thε carrot,  pursue  goals  in  ordεr to  rεceive
℃xternally administered  rεwards: praise,  g이d stars,  grades,  certificates,  diplomas,
scholarships, careers, financial indepεndence, and ultimately, happiness.

2.  Cognitive definitions

A  number  of  cognitive  psychological  viεwpoints  offer  quite  a  different
perspective  on  motivation.  While  rεwards are  very  much  a  part  of  the  whole
picture, the  difference  lies  in the  sourcεs of motivation and  in  thε power of self
rεward.  Three different theories illustratε this side of motivation.

[ HAPTER 5  ’ntrinsic Motivation  in  the [ /assroom

A.  Drive theoηr.  Those who sεε humandriνes as fundamental to human behavior
claim  that  motivation  stems  from  basic  innate  driv1εs.  David  Ausubεl  (1968)
εlaborated on six different drives:

•  exploration
•  manipulation
•  activity
•  stimulation
•knowledge
•  εgo enhancεment

All  of these drives act not so much as  r℃inforcers, as  in behavioristic theory, but as
innate predispositions, compelling us, as it wer,ε, to probe the unknown; to control
our εnvironment; to  bε physically  activ1ε; to  bε receptive  to  mental, emotional, or
physical stimulation; to yearn for  answers to  quεstions; and to build our own self
εsteem.  It takes  little  imagination  to  sεε how motivation  in  th℃ classroom  is  thε
fu1fi피mεnt of thes℃ undεdying drives.

B.  Hierarchy  of  needs  theory.  One  of  the  most  widely  cited  theories  of
motivation comes from Abraham Maslow (1970), who, in the spirit of drive theory,
elaborated  furthεr to  dεscribε a  systεm of needs  within  εach human  being  that
propel  us  to  highεr and  higher  attainmεnt.  Maslow’s  hierarchy  is  best  viewed
mεtaphorically  as  a  pyramid  of  needs  (see  Figur'ε  5.1),  progr1εssing  from  the
satisfaction  of purely  physical  needs  up  through  safety  and  communal  nεεds, to
needs  of esteem, and  finally  to “self actualization,”  a  state  of reaching  your  fullest
potεntial.

Of key importance here is that a person is not adequately εnergized to pursue
some of the  higher needs  until  the  low℃r foundations  of thε pyramid  have  b℃εn
satisfied.  Therefore, a  person who  is  hun~양y or cold, who  has  gottεn little  sleep,
etc.,  has  little  motivation  to  see  beyond  those  pressing  physical  discomforts  to

CHAPTER  5  ’ntrinsic Motivation  in  the Classroom

87

pursue  anything  higher.  Likewise,  nεεds for  safety  (comfort, routine,  protεction)
and for  a  feεling of belonging (in a group of classmatεs or friends)  must be mεt in
ordεr for a person to devote full energy to the higher needs of academic attainrn℃nt,
achiev℃rnεnt of recognition for successεs, and to the ultimate peak of“ being all that
vou can be.”

Maslow’s  thεory tells  us  that what might  be inappropriately view℃d as  rather
ordinary classroom routines may in fact  bε important pr1εcursors to motivation for
highεr attainment.  For  an  activity  in  the  classroom  to  be  considerεd motivating,
then, it neεd not bε outstandingly striking, innovative, or inspirational.  Evεn familiar
classroom procεdurεs (taking roll, checking homework, small talk at  thε bεginning
of class, εtc.), if they fulfill  lower-order neεds, can pave the way to meeting  highεr­
order needs.

C.  Self-control theoηr.  Certain cognitiv1ε psychologists (for instance, Hunt, 1971)
focus on the importance of p℃oplε deciding for themselves what to think or feel or
do.  Wε define  ourselves  by  making  our  own  decisions,  rathεr  than  by  simply
reacting  to  others.  Motivation  is  highest when one can  make  one’s  own choices,
whεther they are in short-term or long-term contexts.

In thε classroom, when learnεrs have opportunitiεs to make their own choicεs
about what to pursue and what not to pursuε, as in a cooperative lεarning context,
they are  fulfilling  this  need for  autonomy  When  learners get things  shoved down
thεir throats, motivation can wane, according to this branch of thεory, because thosε
learners have to yield to others’ wishεs and commands.

3.  A constructivist definition

A  constructivist  viεw of motivation  places  even  further  emphasis  on  social
context  as  wεII as  individual  personal  choices  (Williams  &  Burdεn, 1997, p. 120).
Each  person  is  motivated  differεntly,  and  will  thεreforε  act  on  his  or  hεr
℃nvironmεnt in ways that are unique.  But these uniquε acts are always carried out
within  a  cultural and  social  miliεu and cannot  be  completεly separated from  that
context.  In  some  ways  Maslow’S  (1970)  needs  theory,  summarizεd above, can be
sεen as  constructivist  in  that  ultimate  attainment  of goals  is  partly  due  to  factors
involving  community,  bεlonging, and  social  status.  Motivation, in  a  constructivist
view, is derived as  much from  our interactions with others as  it  is  from  onε ’s sεIf­

detεrmination.

Motivation is  something that can, 파ce self-esteεm, bε global, situational, or task
oriεnted.  Learning a foreign languag1ε requires some of all three levels of motivation.
For examplε, a learner may possess high “global” motivation but low “task끼notivation
to perform well in, say, the written mode of the language.  Motivation is also ηpically
examined in terms of thε intrinsic and εxtrinsic motives of the learnεr,which wε W피
now considεr.

R ’TRINSIC AND EXTRINSIC MOTIVATION

Before we look closely at intrinsic and 않trinsic motivation, let me offer a disclaimer
of sorts.  For several decades, research on motivation in the field of second language
acquisition  research  has been strongly influenced by the work of Robert Gardner
and his associates (Gardner, 1985; Gardner &  Lambert, 1972; Gardner &  Macintyre,
1991,  1993; Gardner  & Tremblaχ 1994).  In  this  succession  of research  studies, a
distinction  has  been made between integrative and instrumental orientations
(see PLLT, Chapter 6).  While the 1972 study claimed that an integrative orientation
(desire to learn a language stemming from a positive affect toward a community of
its speakers) was more stron밍y linked to success in learning a second language than
an instrumental  orientation  (desire  to  learn  a  language  in  order to  attain  certain
career, educational, or financial  goals), later studies showed that both orientations
could be associated with success.

Remember  two  import없it  points.  First,  the  research  by  Gardner  and  his
colleagues  centered  on a  dichotomy  of orieηtation,  not  motivation.  Orientation
means a context or purpose for learning; motivation refers to the intensity of one’s
impetus to learn.  An integrative orientation simply means the learner is pursuing a
second language for a social or cultural purpose or both, 없id within that purpose, a
learner could be driven by a high level of motivation or a low level.  Likewise, in an
instrumental  orientation,  learners  are  stud까ng a  language  in  order  to  further  a
career or academic goal.  The intensity or motivation of a learner to attain that goal
could be high or low.

and

extrinsic  motivation!  They

Second, integrative 없id instrumental orientations are not to be confused with
intrinsic
issues.  One
(integrative/instrumental orientation) is a dichotomy and refers only to the context
of learning.  The  other (intrinsic/extrinsic  motivation)  designates  a  continuum  of
possibilities  of  intensity  of  feeling  or  drive,  ranging  from  deeply  internal,  self
generated rewards to strong, externally administered rewards from beyond oneself.
Now, let’s move to speci다ring further what the intrinsic/integrative continuum

separate

are

implies.  Edward Deci (1975, p. 23) defined intrinsic motivation this way:

Intrinsically  motivated  activities  are  ones  for  which  there  is  no
apparent reward except the activity itself.  People seem to engage in
the  activities  for  their  own  sake  and  not  because  they  lead  to  때
extrinsic reward.  .  .  .  Intrinsically motivated behaviors are aimed at
bringing  about  certain  intern외ly rewarding  consequences, namely,
feelings of competence and self깅etermination.

Extrinsically  motivated  behaviors,  on  the  other  hand,  are  carried  out  in
anticipation  of  a  reward  from  outside  and  beyond  the  self.  껴rpic외 extrinsic
rewards  are  money,  prizes, grades,  and  even  certain  ηpes of positive  feedback.
Behaviors initiated solely to avoid punishment 따℃ also extrinsically motivated, even
though  numerous  intrinsic  benefits  can  띠timately accrue  to  those  who, instead,

viεw punishm℃nt avoidance as a challeng,ε that can build thεir sεnsε of competence
and self깅εtεrmination.

Which form of motivation is more powerful? A convincing stockpile of rεsεarch
on  motivation  strongly  favors  intrinsic  drives,  espεcially for  long-tεrm rεtεntion.
Jean  Piagεt (1972)  and  othεrs  pointεd out  that  human  beings  universally  viεw
incongrui다, uncertainty,  and  “ disequilibrium”  as  motivating.  In  other  words,  we
sεek out a  rεasonabl℃ challengε  Then wε initiatε bεhaviors intεndεd to  conquer
the  challenging  situation.  Incongruity  is  not  itsεIf  motivating,  but  optimal
incongruiη-or what Krashεn (1985) called “i + l ” -prεsents enough of a possib피ty
of being rεsolved that wε will go  aftεr that resolution.

Abraham Maslow (1970) claimed that intrinsic motivation is clearly superior to
εxtrinsic.  According  to  피S  hiεrarchy of  nεeds, wε are  ultimately  motivated  to
achieve “ selεactualization” once the basic physical, safety, and community needs are
mεt No matter what extrinsic rewards are present or absent, we will striv,ε for self
εstεem and fulfillmεnt.

Jerome Bruner (1962), praising th℃ “autonomy of sεlfr,ε:ward,낫laimed that onε of
the most eff;εctive ways to help both children and adults to think and lεam is to free
them from the control of rewards and punishments.  Onε of the principal wεaknesses
of εxtrinsically drivεn bεhavior is its addictive nature.  Once captivatεd, as it were, by
thε lure of an immediate prize or praisε, wε can becomε dεpendent on those tan밍blε
rewards, c、τn to the point that their withdrawal can extinguish the desire to learn

Now,  you  may  be  thinking, don't  extrinsic  rewards  play  a  role  in  a  learner’s
motivation? Wouldn’t  εxtrinsic rewards, coupled with intrinsic motivation, enhance
the  intrinsic?  Not  according  to  a  surprising  number  of  research  studiεs  Two
εxamplεs (Kohn, 1990) illustrate:

1.  Subjects were asked to solve an intrinsically fascinating  complεx puzzlε with
no stated reward.  Halfway through the procεss, the experimenter informed
thε subjects that thεre would be a  monetaη reward for solving the puzzle.
From that point onward, intrinsic motivation (as m℃asur'εd by speεd and
correct steps toward a S이ution) wan℃d.

2.  Teenagε girls wεre giv;εnthε task of teaching some g따nes to younger children.

One group of “teachεrs ” was simply given the tεaching task; the others were told
that they would rεceive a reward (a free ticket to the movies) for successfully
completing the task.  Res버ts: The first group did their task faster, with more
succεss, and reported greater pleasur℃ in doing so than the second group!

It is  intεresting that  thε rεsearch shows that one type of extrinsic reward can
indeed  have  an  εffect on  intrinsic  motivation:  thε positive  fεεdback that  learners
percεive as  a  boost  to  their  feelings  of  competεncε and  self깅εtermination‘  No
other ε:xternally administer'εd set of rewards  has a  lasting  effect.  So, for  example,
sincerely delivered positiv1ε feedback in a classroom, seεn by students as a validation
of their  own  pεrsonal autonomy,  critical  thinking  ability,  and  sεlffulfillment, can
increase or maintain intrinsic motivation.

Intrinsic Motivation  in  the Classroom

Intrinsic  motivation  is  of course  not  the  only  determiner  of success  for  a
language  learner.  Sometimes,  no  matter  how  much  you  want  to  accomplish
something  or how hard you tη, you may not succeed for  a host of other reasons.
But if the learners in your classroom are given an opportunity to “ do” language for
their own person때 reasons of achieving competence and autonomχ those learners
will  have  a  better chance  of success  than if they  become dependent on  external
rewards for their motivation.

INTRINSIC MOTIVATION IN EDUCATION

Educators  like  Maria  Montessori,  Rudolf  Steiner,  Paolo  Freire, A.  S.  Neill,  and  Carl
Rogers  have  all  provided  exemplaη models  of intrinsically  motivated  education.
Traditionally,  elementaη and  secondary  schools  are  fraught  with  extrinsically
motivated behavior.  The school curriculum is  dictated  by institutions (sometimes
politically  influenced)  and  can  be  far  removed  from  even  the  teacher's  choice.
없rents’  and society’s  values  and wishes  are  virtually forced  onto pupils, whether
they like it or not.  Tests and exams, many of which are standardized and given high
credence in the world “out there,” are  imposed on students with no  consultation
with  the  students  themselves.  The glorification  of content, product, correctness,
따id competitiveness has failed  to bring the learner into a collaborative process of
competence building.

The consequence of such extrinsic motivators is that schools 찌I too often teach
students to play the “g따ne” of pleasing teachers 없id authorities rather th없I dεvεloping
an  internalized  thirst  for  knowledge  따id expεrience.  The  administration  of grades
따id  praises  for  being  a  “good  child"  builds  a  dependency  on  immediatε M&M
gratification.  Competition  against  classmates  (who  might  otherwise  be  allies  or
paπners in learning) ensues.  If a communal bond is created, it runs the risk of being
motivated by the need to band together against teachers 따id authorities.  Over the
long  haul,  such  dependency  focuses  students  too  exclusively  on  the  material  or
monetaπ rewards of an education rather than ins떠피ig an appreciation for creati찌.ty
and  satisf싸ig  some  of  the  mor℃ basic  drives  for  knowledge  and  exploration.
illtimately, the product of this 하rstem is a student who has been taught to fear failure
above 따l and therefore to refrain from potentially rewar따ng risk-ta따ng or innovative
behavior.

A bleak picture? Too  harsh?  Of course, there  are  m없iy happy exceptions  to
such a depiction, but you don’t  have to look very far in 없iy corner of the world to
find major elements of the picture holding true.  The question is: Can something be
done to turn such a picture upside down? Or, more specifically to your quest, C때
your English classroom become a place where these e값rinsic elements are diverted
into a more positive direction? Or, better yet, can such elements be avoided entirely?
Table  5.1  depicts what can happen in an institution that takes eight 않trinsic
elements and, while  accep다ng 야ieir re외ity in virtually any society or educational
institution, turns those elements in an intrinsically oriented direction.  The notion

Such  activities  and  attitudes  on  your  part  appeal  to  the  deeper  causes  of
motivation.  They  get  at  needs  and  drives,  at  self control,  at  a  balancεd, rεalistic
perception of self, and even at the simple joy of learning for its own sakε!

CHAPTER  5

Intrinsic Motivation  in  the Classroom

Throughout the rest of this book, you will be reminded of the importance of
the  Intrinsic  Motivation  Principle  in  achieving  your  go쩌S as  a  teacher.  Think  of
yourself not so much as a teacher who must constantly “deliver” information to your
students,  but  more  as  a facilitator  of learning  whose  job  is  to  set  the  stage  for
learning, to start the wheels turning inside the heads of your students, to tum them
on to their own abilities, and to help channel those abilities in fruitful directions.

Zolran Domyei (2001) offers an insi항itful set of strategies for creating what he
calls “basic  motivational conditions” (p. 31) in the  classroom, based on a  survey of
Hungarian  for,εign language  teachers (Domyei &  Csizer,  1998).  All  eight strategies
focus on what the teacher can do to start the process of creating intrinsic motivation.

1.  Demonstrate 따id talk about your own enthusiasm for the course material, and

how it affects you person때ly.

2.  Take the studεnts’ learning ve디r seriously.
3.  Develop a person따 relationship with your students.
4.  Develop a collaborative relationship with the students' parents.
5.  Creatε a pleasant and supportive atmosphere in the classroom.
6.  Promote the development of group cohesiveness.
7.  Formulate group norms explicitly, and have them discussed and accepted by

the learners.

8.  Have the group norms consistently observed.

These ei앙1t guidε파1es are followed, in Domyei’s (2001) book, by 27 other strategies
for  generating  initial  motivation,  maintaining  and  protec디ng  motivation,  and
encouraging positive self-evaluation.

You  might wish to compare Domyei’s  35  strategies with my own six general
guidelines  for  infusing  your  En밍ish  language  classroom  with  some  intrinsically
motivating dynamics:

1.  Teachers are enablers, not rewarders.  Therefore, when you teach, focus less

on how to administer immediate or tan1앙ble rewards and more on how to get
students to tune in to their potential and to be challenged by self깅etermined
go따s.

2.  Learners need to develop autonomχ not dependence.  Therefore, be careful

not to let learners become dependent on your daily praise and other
feedback.  Rather, administer praise selectively and judiciously, helping
students to recognize their own self satisfaction in having done something
well.

3.  Help learners to take charge of their own learning through setting some

personal goals and ut피zing learning strategies.

4.  Leamer-centered, cooperative teaching is intrinsically motivating.  Therefore,

give students opportunities to make choices in activities, topics, discussions, etc.

Somεtitnεs a sitnple either/or choice (“Okay, class, for the next 10 minutes we

on a string if you can involv,ε them in various aspεcts of looking at their needs
and sεlf김iagnosing, of planning lessons and objεctiv'εs, of deciding in which
dirεction a lεsson might go, and of εvaluating their learning.

5.  Content-based activities and courses are intrinsically motivating.  Thεrefore,

you might strive to focus your students on interesting, relevant subject-matter
content that gets thεm morε linguistically involved with meanings and
purposes and less with verbs and prεpositions.

6.  Tεsts, with somε special attention from the tεachεr,can bε intrinsically

But  when all  thεsε factors  are  duly  considered, the  students’  long-tεrm goals,

During  the  first  six  or  seven  decades  of  the  twentieth  century,
language  teaching  as  a  disciplinary  field  seemed  to  operatε on  the
assumption  that  there  is-or should  be-one  standard, “orthodox”
method  for  all  contexts.  Some  claimed,  for  ex없nple,  that  the
Audiolingual  Method  was  “ the”  method  for  따l  language-teaching
contexts.

One of the principal contributions of the last few decades of the
twentieth  centlπy was  an  increasin항y complex sεt of descriptions of
the  m띠tipliciη of conte었S in which foreign  l따iguages were  learned
and tau쌀it.  As more and more research corroborated the concept that
all  learners  와id  때l  contexts  were  not  alike,  teaching  methodology
followed  with explorations  of how to  iden디한 an  array  of needs  and
how to meet them.

The three chapters of 없π II alert you to major v았iables in learning
and teaching contexts.  The most salient factor in a l따1guage cl싫sroom
is  age, treated in Chapter 6.  Indeed, some classes may include a  ran~뿜
of ages, from young ad띠ts to older adults. Chapter 7 treats the second
most  obvious  contextual variable, proficiency level, with descriptions
and applications for what are loosely labeled beginning, intermεdiate,
and advanced levels of l않rning.

Chapter 8  examines a number of variables that were often swept
under  the  rug  in  the  last  centlπy:  sociopolitical  없id  institutional
conte었s.  Issues  of  culture,  identity,  ethnicity,  and  the  situational
politics surrounding a language course are far-reaching  없id complex.
Differences between teaching  En행sh in an  En횡ish-sp없파ng countπ
and  a  non-암1앙ish-spe짧ing coun따, the  controversy  over  nonnative
English-speaking  teachers  (NNESTs),  and  the  idiosyncracies  of one’s
own  institution  can  따l  affect  the  final  outcome  of  learners  in  a
language proE양없n.

On occasion people who are quite unaware of the language-teaching field will walk
into  my  office  at  the  university  looking  for  reassurance.  They’ll  ask  mε, “Since
English is my native language, I won’t  have any problεm teaching it, w피 I?” Or they
might ask, on the eve of their departure for Japan (without the slightεst duε of who
their  future  students  will  bε), “ Can  you  recommend  a  good  textbook  for  my
studεnts?” 0thεr naive inquirers who have had a little  exposurε to the vastness and
complεxity of the field  still  might assert, “I  would like  to lεam how to  teach  ESL.
Can  you  rεcommend  a  good  workshop?”  Such  questions  are  prompted  by
advertisεments in local newspapers that promise l표elong employmεnt as an English
teacher (in exotic plac℃s) if only you’II  attend someone’s weekend seminar (or two)
and, of course, cough up a fairly hefty enrollment fee.

You  are  already  awar'ε  of  the  array  of  questions,  issues,  approaches,  and
techniques  that  must  be  included  in  any  training  as  a  language  tεacher-a
com plεxity that cannot bε covered εffectively in a weekεnd workshop.  Part of this
complexity is  brought on by thε multiplicity of contexts in which languages, and

pack a  suitcase full  of the most current teaching resources, you would st피 have to
face  the quεstion of gν'ho your learners are, αhere they are learning, and why they
are learning.

This  chapter  bεgins  to  deal  with  contextual  considerations  in  language

learner variable  of language  proficiency (beginning, intermediate, and advanced).
And Chapter 8 grapples with several complex variables introduced by sociopolitic싫
contexts  of  teaching  (countη,  societal  expectations,  cultural  factors,  political
constraints,  the  status  of English);  by  the  institution  one  is  teaching  in  (school,

TEACHING CHITDREN

Popular  tradition  would  havε  you  believe  that  children  arε  εffortlεss  second
language  learners  and  far  supεrior to  adults  in  their  ev1εntual success.  On  both
counts, some qualifications  arε in ordεr.

First,  childr,εn’s  widεspread success  in  acquiring  second  languages  belies  a
tremendous  subconscious  .φrt devotεd to  thε task.  As  you  have  discover1℃d in
othεr reading  (seε PLLT,  Chapters  2  and  3, for  examplε), childrεn exercise  a  good
deal  of both cognitive  and affective  effort  in  ordεr to  internalizε both  nativε and
sεcond languages.  Thε differencε bεtwεεn children  and  adults  (that  is,  persons
beyond  thε  age  of  puberty)  lies  primarily  in  the  contrast  betweεn thε child’s
spontanεous, peripheral attention to language forms and  thε adult’s  OV1εrt, focal
awareness  of and  attεntion to  thosε forms.  Therefor·ε, the  popular  notion  about
childrεn holds only if “effort” ref4εrs, rather narrowly, to focal  att℃ntion (sometimes
thought of as “ conscious” attention-see PLLT,  Chapter 10) to language forms.

Second, adults  ar1ε not necessarily less successful in their εfforts.  Studiεs have
shown  that  adults, in  fact,  can be  superior in  a  numbεr of aspects  of acquisition
(PLLT,  Chapter 3).  Thεy can lεarn and rεtain a  larger vocabulary.  They can utilize
various  dεductiv1ε and  abstract  procεSS℃S to  shortcut  the  learning  of grammatical
and  other linguistic  concepts.  And,  in  classroom  learning, their  supεrior intellεct
usually  hεlps thεm to  learn  faster  than  a  child.  So,  while  children’s  fluεncy and
naturalness  ar1℃ oftεn the  envy  of  adults  struggling  with  second  languages,  the
contεxt  of  classroom  instruction  may  introduce  some  difficulties  to  children
learning a second language.

Third, the popular claim fails  to differentiate very young  childr℃n (say, 4- to 6-
year-olds)  from  pubescent  children  (12  to  13)  and  the  whole  rangε of  agεs in
betweεn There  are  actually  many  instances  of  6-
to  12-year-old  childr1εn
manifesting significant difficulty in acquiring a  second language for a  multitudε of
reasons.  Ranking  hi압1 on that  list  of rεasons are  a  number of complex pεrsonal,
social, cultural, and political factors at play in elementary school education.

Teaching ESL to school-age children, therefore, is not mer1εly a matter of setting
them loosε on a  plethora of authentic language tasks in thε classroom.  In fact, for
some TESOL  professionals  (Camεron, 2003),  the  challenges  of tεaching children
warrants  a  sεparate acronym: TEYL  (teaching  En밍ish to young learners).  Teacher
reference  books  are  dεvoted solely  to  the  issues,  principlεs, and  mεthodology
surrounding the teaching of children (Linsε, 2005; Moon, 2000; Pinter, 2006;  Reilly
& Ward,  1997).  To  successfully teach children a  sεcond language  rεquires specific

102

CHAPTER  6  Teaching Across Age Levels

skills  and  intuitions  that  differ  from  those  appropriate  for  adult  teaching.  Five
categories may help give some practical approaches to teaching children.

1.  Intellectual development

An elementaη school teacher asked her students to take a piece of paper and
pencil and write something.  A boy raised his hand and said, “Teacher, I ain’t got no
penc江’ πie teacher, somewhat perturbed by his grammar, embarked on a barrage of
corrective patterns:“I don ’t  have a pencil.  You don ’t  have a pencil.  We don ’t have
pencils.”  Confused  and  bewildered,  the  child  responded, “Ain’t  nobody  got  no
pencils?”

Since  children (up to  the age  of about  11) are  st피 in an intellectual stage of
what  Piaget  (1972)  called  “concrete  operations,”  we  need  to  remember  their
limitations.  Rules, explanations, and other ev,εn sli힐itly abstract talk about language
must be approached with extreme caution.  Children are centered on the here 때d
now, on the functional purposes of language.  They have little appreciation for our
adult notions of‘'correctness,” and they certainly cannot grasp the metalanguage we
use to describe and explain linguistic concepts. Here are  some rules of thumb for
the classroom:

•  Don’t  expl따n grammar using  tεrms like “present progressive” or “relative

clause.”

•  Rules stated in abstract terms (“To make a statement into a question, you add

ado or does”) should be avoided.

•  Some grammatical concepts, especially at the upper levels of childhood, can
be called to learners’ attention by showing them certain patteηis (“Notice
the ing at the end of the word”) and examples (“πiis is  the way we say it
when it’s happening right now: ‘I’m  walking to the door”’).

•  Certain  more  difficult  concepts  or patterns  require  more  r적petitioη than
adults  need.  For  example,  repeating  certain  patterns  (without  boring
students) may be necessaη to get the brain and the ear to cooperate.  Unlike
the  boy  who  had  no  pencil,  children  must  understand  the  meaning  and
relevance of repetitions.

2.  Attention span

One of the salient differences between adults and children is  attention sp없1.
First, it is important to understand what attention span means.  Put children in front
of a 1V showing a favorite cartoon and they will stay riveted for the duration.  So,
you cannot make  a  sweeping claim  that  children have  short attention  sp따is!  But
short attention spans do come into play when children have to deal with material
that to them is boring, useless, or too difficult.  Since language lessons can at times
be difficult for children, your job is  to make them interesting, lively, and fun.  How
do you do that?

c”AfπER 6  Teaching Across Age Levels

103

•  Because  children  are  focused  on  the  here  and  noψ, activities  should  bε

designed to capture their immediate interest.

•  A lεsson needs a variety of activities to keep interest and attention alive.
•  A teacher needs  to  be animated,  lively, and enthusiastic  about the  subject
matter.  Considεr thε classroom a stage on which you are the lead actor; your
enεrgy will  be  infectious.  While  you  may  think  that  you’re  overdoing  it,
children need this exaggeration to keep spirits buoyed and minds alert.

•  Aseηse of humor will  go  a  long  way  in  kεeping children  laughing  and
learning.  Since children’s humor is quite different from adults', rεmember to
put yourself in their shoes.

•  Children have a lot of natural cu성osity.  Make sure you tap into that curiosity
whεnever possible,  and  you  will  thereby  help  to  maintain  attention  and
focus.

3.  Sensory input

Children need to have  all  five  senses stimulated.  Your activities should strive
to go well beyond the visual and audito1γ modes that we feel  ar℃ usually sufficiεnt
for a classroom.

•  Pepper your lεssons with pψ•sical ac다vity, such as  having students act out

things (role-play), play games, or do Total Physic때 Response activities.

•  Projects  and  other  bαndsθη activities  go  a  long  way  toward  helping
childrεn to internalize language.  Small「group science projects, for  examplε,
are excellent ways to get them to learn words and structures 따id to practice
mεaningful language.

•  Sensoηl aids  help  children  to  internalize  concepts.  Thε smell  of flowers,
the touch of plants and fruits, the taste of foods, liberal doses of audiovisual
aids  like  videos,  pictures,  tapes,  music-all  are  important  elements  in
childr℃n’s language teaching.

•  Remembεr that  your  own  nonverbal  laη!guage  is  important  because
children will indeed attend veη sensi디vely to your facial  features, gestures,
and body languagε.

A common myth is that children are relativelµnaffected by the inhibitions that
