•  Help your students to laugh with each other at various mistakes that they 따l

make.

•  Be  patiεnt and supportive to build self-esteem, yet at thε S없ne time be firm

in your expectations of students.

•  Elicit  as  much  oral  paπicipation 잃 possible  from  students, especially  the

quieter ones, to give them plenty of opportunities for trying things out.

5.  Authentic, meanin뺑tl language

Children are focused on what this new language can actually be usεd for here
and now.  They are less willing to put up with language that doesn’t hold immediate
rewards for them.  Your classes can 피 afford to have an overload of language that is
neither authentic nor meanin행IL

•  Children  are  good  at  sensing  language  that  is  not  authentic;  therefore,

“canned” or stilted language will likely be rεjected.

•  Language needs to be firmly context mηbedded.  Storγ lines, familiar situations
and  characters,  real-life  conversations,  meaningful  purposes  in  using
language-these  will  establish  a  context  within  which  language  can  be
received  따id  sent  따id  thereby  improve  attention  and  retention.  Context
reduced language  in abstract, isolated, unconnected sentences will  be  much
less rεadily tolerated by children’S  minds.

•  A whole language approach is essential.  If language is broken into too many
bits and pieces, students won’t see the relationship to the whole.  And stress
the interrelationships among thε various skills (listening, speaking, reading,
and writing), or they won’t  see important connections.

It  takes  a  very special person to  bε able  to  teach  children effectively.  Along
with all these guidelines, an elementaπ school teacher develops a certain intuition
with  increasing  months  and  years  of  experi℃nce.  If you  don't  yet  have  the
experience,  you  will  in  due  course  of  time.  Meanwhile,  you  must  begin
somewhere, and these rules of thumb will help.

TEACHING ADULTS

Although  m따iy of the “rules”  for  teaching  children  can  apply  in  some  ways  to
teaching adults, the latter age group poses some different, speci따 considerations for
the classroom teacher.  Adults have superior cognitive abilities that can render them
more successful in certain classroom endeavors.  Their need for sensoη input can
rely a little more on their imaginations (they can be told to “ima횡ne” sme피ng a rose
versus actually smelling one).  Their level of shyness can be equ때 to or greater than
that  of children,  but  adults  usually  have  acquired  a  self-confidence  not found  in
children.  And, because of adults' cognitive  abilities, they can at least  occasionally
deal with language that isn’t embedded in a “ here 없id now” context.

CHAPTER  6

1논·aching Across Age Levels

105

So, as you consider thε five variables that apply to children, keep in mind some

specific  su잃estions and caveats.

1.  Adults are more able to handle abstract rules and concepts.  But beware! As
you know, too much abstract generalization about usage and not enough real
life  language use can be deadly for adults, too.

2.  Adults have longer attention spans for  material that may not be intrinsically
interesting to them.  But again, thε rule of keeping your activities short and
sweet applies also to adult-age teaching.

3.  Sensory input need not always be as  varied with adults, but one of the sεcrets

of liv·εly adult classεs is  their appeal to multiple senses.

4.  Adults often bring a modicum of general self-confidence 않lobal self-esteem)
into a classroom.  With children you must compensate for their fragile egos;
such compensation may not be as critical with adults.  Yet we should never
underestimate the emotional factors  that may be attendant to adult second
language learning.

5.  Adults, with their more dev·εlopεd abstract thinking ability, are better able to
understand a context-reduced segment of language.  Authenticity and mean
ingfulness are of course still highly important, but in adult language teaching,
a teacher can take temporary digressions to dissect and examine isolated lin
guistic properties as long as students are  returned to the original context.

Some implications for general classroom management (see Chapter 13 for a full
treatment) can bε drawn from what we know about differences  betwεen children
and adults.  Some management “ do’S” and “ don’ts”;

1.  Do remembεr that even though adults cannot express complεx thinking in
the new languagε, they ar•ε nev·εrtheless intεlligent grown-ups with mature
cognition and fully  developed emotions.  Show respect for the deeper
thoughts and feelings that may be “ trapped” for the moment by a low
proficiency level.

2.  Don’t  treat adults in your class like childr•εn by

a.  calling them “kids,"
b.  using “ caretaker” talk (the way parents talk to children), or
c.  talking down to them ‘

3.  Do give your students as  many opportunities as possible to make choices

(cooperative learning) about what they will do in and out of the classroom.
That way, they can more effectively make an inv1εstment in their own learning
process.

4.  Don’t discipline adults in the same way you would children.  If discip파ie
problems occur (showing disrespect, laughing, disrupting class, etc.), first
assume that your students are adults who can be reasoned with like adults.

TEACHING TEENS

It is of course much too absolute to considεr that a child cεases to be a child at thε
age  of  pubeπy and  that  all  of  thε rulεs of adult  tεaching suddenly  apply!  It  is
thεrefor,ε appropriate  to  consider  briefly  thε sort  of variables  that  apply  in  the
teaching of “young adults,” “ teens,” and high school-age childr℃n whose ages  range
bεtweεn 12 and  18 or so.

The  “tεrrible  teens”  are  an  age  of  transition,  confusion,  self consciousness,
growth, and  changing bodies  and  minds.  What  a  challεnge for  the  teacher! Tεens
are  in  between  childhood  and  adulthood,  and  thεrεfore  a  very  special  sεt  of
considerations  applies  to  tεaching  them.  Perhaps  because  of  thε  enigma  of
teaching  teenagers,  littlε  is  spεcifically said  in  the  languagε-tεaching field  about
teaching at this lev,εI.  Nεvertheless, some thoughts are worth verbalizing, εvεn κ in
the form of simplε reminders.

1.  Intellectual capacity adds abstract opεrational thought around the age of 12.
Th erεfore, some sophisticated intellectual processing is increasingly possible.
Complex problems can be solved with logical thinking.  This mεans that
linguistic metalanguage can now, theoretically, havε some impact.  But the
success of any intellectual endeavor will bε a factor of the attention a  learnεr

being accepted, to sexual thoughts, to a wεekend party, to whatever, the
intellectual task at hand may suffer.

2.  Attention spans ar1ε lεngthening as a  I℃sult of intellectual maturation, but oncε

again, with many div1℃rsions present in a tεenager’s lκε, those potεntial
attention spans can εasily be shortened.

3.  Varieties of sensotγ input are  st퍼 important, but again, incriεasing capacitiεs
for abstraction lessen thε ess℃ntial nature of appealing to all five  sεnsεs.
4.  Factors surrounding ego, self imag℃, and sεIf-esteem are at thεir pinnacle.

Tεεns are ultrasensitive to how others perceiv1ε thεir changing physical and
emotional selves along with thεir mental capabiliti℃s.  One of the most
important concerns of the sεcondary school teacher is to kεep self-estεεm
high by

•  avoiding embarrassment of students at all  costs,
•  affirming each person’s  talεnts and strεngths,
•  allowing mistakes and other errors to be accepted,
•  de-emphasizing competition betweεn classmates, and
•  εncouraging small-group work wher,ε risks can be taken more easily

by a teen.

5.  Secondaπ school students are of course bεcoming increasingly adultlike in
their ability to make those occasional diversions from the “ her℃ and now”
nature of immediate communicative contexts to dwell on a grammar point or

5.  (C) Engage the class in a discussion about whether one should teach language

Hardly  a  teaching  day  goes  by  in  this  profession  without  someonε rεferring to
students’  proficiεncy  levels  with  the  terms  “ bεginning;’  “ intermediatε,”  or
“ advanced.” And  as  long  as  Earth  spins  on  its  axis,  I  suppose, teachers  will  diffiεr
among  themselv,εs on  just  what  those  terms  mean.  At  the  American  Languag,ε
Institute of San Francisco State Univ'εrsity,for εxamplε, what we call the “ beginning”
level consists of students who air,εady may know a couple of hundred English words
and are able to use a few common survival phrasεs.  In some circlεs these students
would  be  labeled  “fa lsε  bεginners”  as  distinguished  from  “ truε  beginners.”  The
“advanced” level, on  thε other hand, is  not as advanced as  some of the ESL  writing
courses offered for credit in the same university’S  Dεpartment of English.

So, a certain s℃nse of relativity must always be taken into account whεn thεsε
terms  ar'ε used.  What is  beginning for some may not be for  others.  Certainly  thε
language-teaching  profession  doεs  not  lay  unique  claim  to  such  subjectivity.
Consider, for  example, how “Intermediate Algebra”  might  be  variously  intεrpr,εted
according to the institution in which it is  offiεrεd.

DEFINING PROFIC표NCYLEVELS

Is  there a  st때dard set of guidelines by which these three mysterious tεrms may bε
uniformly  understood?  The  answer  is  a  qualified  yes,  in  the  form  of  the  ACTFL
P11때ciency Guidelines  (Amεrican Council  on  th℃ Teaching  of Foreign  Languages,
1999).  While textbooks and curricula do not by any mεans adhere to such guidεlinεs

110

r

CHAPTER  7  Teaching Across Proficiency Levels

111

univers외ly, the guidelines  nevertheless  offer us  a  practical  description of spea퍼ng,
listening, reading, and writing proficiency at numerous gradations.

The Guidelines, produced by the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign
Languages  (ACTFL),  are  a  rec<핑nized  proficiency  standard  in  many  language
teaching  circles.  Thε current  version  of the  Guidelines  is  historically  related  to
what for many yεars was referred to as “ FSI  levels” ofspea힘ng proficiency.  The FSI
(Foreign  Servicε Institute)  levels,  now  ref:iεrred to  as “ILR” (lnteragency  Language
Roundtable)  levels  in  more  formal  research  settings,  represent  points  on  an
increasing scale of sophistication as determined by the FSI Oral Interview.  The Oral
Interview  is  a  carefully  designed  set  of structured  tasks  that elicit  pronunciation;
fluency and integrative ability; sociolinguistic and cultural knowledge; grammar; and
vocabulaη.  The test-taker is  judged to possess proficiency that falls into one of the
following  11  different levels:

Level

Description

0
O+

1 +

2

2+

3

3+

4

4+

5

Unable to  function  in  the spoken  language.
Able  to  satisfy  immediate needs  using  rehearsed  utterances.
Able to  satisfy  minimum  courtesy  requirements and  maintain  very
simple face-to-face  conversations on familiar  topics.
Able to  initiate and  maintain  predictable face-to-face  conversa
tions  and  satisfy  limited  social  demands.
Able  to  satisfy  routine social  demands and  limited  work
requirements.
Able  to  satisfy  most work  requirements with  language  usage that  is
often,  but not always,  acceptable and effective.
Able  to  speak the  language with  sufficient structural  accuracy and
vocabulary  to  participate effectively  in  most formal  and  informal
conversations on  practical,  social,  and  professional  topics.
Often  able to  use the  language to  satisfy  professional  needs  in  a
wide  range of sophisticated  and  demanding tasks.
Able to  use  the  language fluently  and accurately on  all  levels
normally  pertinent to  professional  needs.
Speaking proficiency is  superior in  all  respects,  usually equivalent
to that of a well-educated,  highly  articulate native speaker.
Speaking  proficiency  is  functionally  equivalent to  that of a  highly
articulate,  well-educated  native speaker and  reflects the cultural
standards of the country where the  language  is  spoken.

The ACTFL Pri짜ci,αicy Guidelines were created to 얹pand on the FSl/ILR lev1εls
so that listening, rεacting, and writing would also be included.  The Guidelines have
one other important difference: They are  not connected with any  on℃ proficiency
test, as the FSl/ILR levels are.  Instead, they were created to guidε any test-maker in
the process of assessment.  Today, numerous tεst designers utilize the Guidelines as
a  standard  for  assessment.  While  they  were  expressly  not  designed  for  assessing
achievement in any one curriculum, the  Guideliηes can, when used with caution,
provide a number of useful checkpoints for curriculum development and revision.

You  will  notice  in  the  table  of  spea퍼ng guidelines  (Table  7 .1  on  pages
114-118) that the term “novice” replaces the term “beginning” due to the difficulty
of establishing  a  definitive  beginning  point  in  most  language  learners.  For  the
distinction  between what in ordinary conversation we might call “beginners” and
“ false  beginners;’ the Guidelines offer the terms “ novice-low” and “ novice-mid.” It is
also important to note that the various lεvels of the speaking guidelines reprinted
in Table 7.1  do ηot correspond to 때y one FSI  level.  In fact, in the introduction to
the Guidelines, it is suggested that their “superior” level is “ roughly equivalent to the
ILR  3 range.'’ Nevertheless, the Guidelines (presented here only for the one skill of
speaking) can offer an informative overall picture of a range of proficiency in terms
of assumed  competencies  at  different  levels.  For  other  skills,  you  can  visit  the
ACTFL Web site (http://www.actfl.org).

TEACHING BEGINNING LEVELS

Many  teachεrs 띤펀뺨 the be핑nning level of languagε instruction i_~쁘 thε most
challen핑ng.  Since  students at  this  level  hav1ε little  or no  prior  knowlεdge of the
target language, the teacher (따idaccomp따iying techniques and m~terials) becomes
a  central determiner in whether studεnts accomplish their goals.( This can also  be
the  most  tangibly  rεwarding level  for  a  teacher because  the  gro~πh of students'
proficiency is apparent in a matter of a few weeks.

At  the  beginning  or even false-beginning  level, your students  have  very little
language  “ behind”  them.  You  riiay  ihεrefore-be tempted  to  go  along  with  the
popular misconception that the target language cannot be taught directly, that you
will  have  to  resort  to  a  good  deal  of talking “about” the  second  language  in  the
students'  native  language.  Such  is  clearly  not  the  case/ as  beginning  language
courses have demonstrated for many decades.  Butyoiioo fiave to keep in mind that
your  students'  capacity  for  taking  in  and  rεtaining  new  words,  structures,  and
concepts is l뼈ted. (Foremost on your mind as a teacher should be thε presentatio n
in  simple  segments  that  don’t  overwhelm  yo따 student허 Remember,
of materi
they are just_J:l_ar~ly__,beginning!

The following  10 factors-and the words of advice  accomp따iying each-will

113

1. Students' cognitive learning processes

In those first  few days and even weeks of language learning, virtually 때l of the
studεnts’ processing with respect to the second language itself is 핀온~c외,con tr~말브
mode*  (see PLLT,  Chapter 10, for  a review of McLaughlin’s cognitive processes and
some  classroom  applications).  Therefore, you  can  expect  to 강ngage in  plεnty of
repetition  of a  limited  number  of words,  phrases,  and  sentences.  Don’t  become
frustrated if a considerable period of time goes by with little change in these learning
modes.
_
Even  in the first  few  days  of class, however, you  can coax your students into
( some peripheral processing by getting them to use practiced language__fur_~댄뼈앙y
rneaningful purposes. • For example, getting information from  a  classmate whom a
student  does  not  know w피 require  using _newly  learned language  (“What’S  your
name?”  “Where  do  you  live?”), but  with  a  focus  on  the  purposes  to  which  the
language is  put, not on the forms  of language.  The forms themselves, although st피
controlled  (limited  in  capacity),  neviεrtheless  move  into  a  peripheral  mode  as
students bεcome immεrsed in the task of seeking genuine information.

2. The role of the teaεher

Beginning  students  are . highly  dependent  on  the  teacher  for  models  of
language, and so a teacher-centered or teacher-fronted classroom is appropriate for
some  of your  classroom  time.  Students  are  able  to  initiate  few  questions  and
comments, so it is your responsibility to “keep the ball rolling.” St피, your beginning
level classes need not be devoid of a modicum of student-centered work.  Pair wQrk
and groue work (see Chapter 12) are effective techniques for taking students' focus
off you as  diε center of attention and for getting them into an interactive frame  of
mind even at thε most beginning lεvel.

It follows that the degree of contr이 of classroom time also leans strongly in the
direction  of the  teacher  at  the  beginning  levels.  In  a  second  language  context
where instruction is carried out in the target language, virtually all of your class time
will be teacher-controlled.  Since students have  no means, in the second language
a따way, of controlling  the  class  period, the onus is  on you to  plan topics, activity
types, timε-on-task, etc.  As  students gain  in  their proficiency, they will be able  to
initiate questions and comments of their own that may then occasionally shift the
locus  of control.  In  a  foreign  language  situation, where  your  students  speak  the
same native language (and you speak it as well), some negotiation might be possible
in the native language, allowing  for  a  sm따l amount of student control (see #3  on
page 118)‘

*A q띠ck review of PLLT may remind you that controlled processing is common in 따ty
new skill where few bits of information can be managed at once.  Focal attention is
giving notice to something in particular: a language form, an attempted message, a
person’s physical appearance, a person’s emotional state, etc.  Automatic processing
is the simultaneous management of a multitude of pieces of information.  And
peripheral attention refers to things that we give only incidental notice to.

High

Speakers  at  the Superior  level  are  able to  communicate in  the  language with accuracy
and  fluency  in  order to  participate fully and  effectively  in  conversations  on  a variety
of topics  in  formal  and  informal  settings from  both  concrete and abstract perspectives.
They  discuss  their  interests  and  special  fields  of competence,  explain  complex  matters
in  detail,  and  provide lengthy and  coherent narrations,  all  with  ease,  fluency,  and
accuracy.  They  explain  their opinions on  a number of topi [ S of importance to  them,
such  as  social  and  p이 itical  issues,  and  provide structured  argument to  support their
opinions.  They  are able to construct and  develop hypotheses  to explore alternative
possibilities.  When  appropriate,  they  use  extended  discourse  without unnaturally
lengthy  hesitation  to  make their point,  even  when  engaged  in  abstra [ t elaborations.
Such  discourse,  while coherent,  may still  be  influenced  by the  Superior speakers'  own
language  patterns,  rather than  those  of the  target  language.

Superior speakers  command a variety of interactive and  discourse strategies,  such  as
turn-taking and  separating main  ideas  from  supporting information through  the  use  of
syntactic  and  lexical  devices,  as  well  as  intonational features  such  as  pitch,  stress  and
tone.  They  demonstrate virtually no pattern  of error  in  the  use  of basic  structures.
However,  they  may  make sporadic e「rors, particularly  in  low-frequency structures  and
in  some  complex  high-frequency structures more common  to  formal  speech  and
writing.  Such  errors,  if they  do occur,  do not distract the  native  interlocutor or
interfere with communication.

Speakers  at the Advanced-High  level  perform  all Advanced-level  tasks  with  linguistic
ease,  confidence and  competence.  They  are  able to consistently explain  in  detail  and
nar「ate fully and  accurately  in  all  time frames.  In  addition,  Advanced-High  speakers
handle the  tasks  pertaining to the  Superior level  but cannot sustain  performance at
that  level  across  a variety  of topics.  They can  provide a stru [ tured argument to
support their opinions,  and  they may construct hypotheses,  but patterns  of error
appear.  They  can  discuss  some topics abstractly  especially those  relating to  their
particular interests  and  special  fields  of expertise,  but in general,  they  are  more
comfortable discussing a variety  of topics concretely.

Advanced-High  speakers  may demonstrate a well-developed  ability to  compensate for
an  imperfect grasp  of some forms  or for  limitations  in  vocabulary by the confident use
of communicative strategies,  such  as  paraphrasing,  circumlocution,  and  illustration.
They  use  precise vocabulary and  intonation to express  meaning and  often  show great
fluency  and  ease  of speech.  However,  when  called  on  to perform the  complex  tasks
associated  with  the Superior  level  over a variety of topics,  their language  will  at times
break down  or prove inadequate,  or they  may avoid the task  altogether,  for example,
by resorting to simplification through  the  use  of description or narration  in  place of
argument or hypothesis.

Advanced
Mid

Speakers  at  the Advanced-Mid  level  are able to  handle with ease  and  confidence a
large  number of communicative tasks.  They  participate actively  in  most informal  and
some formal  exchanges  on  a variety of concrete topics  relating to work,  school,
home,  and  leisure activities,  as  well  as  to events  of current,  publiι and personal
interest or individual  relevance.

Advanced-Mid  speakers demonstrate the ability to  narrate and  desc「ibe in  all  major
time frames  (past,  present,  and  future)  by providing a full  account,  with good  control
of aspect,  as  they  adapt flexibly to  the demands of the  conve「sation. Narration and

114

[ HAPTER 7  Teaching A [ ross Proficiency Levels

115

description  tend  to  be  combined  and  interwoven  to  relate  relevant  and  supporting
facts  in  connected,  paragraph-length  discourse.

Advanced-Mid  speakers  can  handle successfully  and  with  relative  ease  the
linguistic challenges  presented  by  a complication or unexpected  turn  of events that
occurs within the  context of a routine situation  or communicative task  with which
they  are  otherwise familiar.  [ ommunicative strategies  such  as  circumlocution or
rephrasing are  often  employed  for  this purpose.  The  speech  of Advanced-Mid
speakers  pe「forming Advanced-level  tasks  is  marked  by  substantial  flow‘  Their
vocabulary  is  fairly extensive although  primarily generic  in  nature,  except  in  the
case  of a partic 비 ar area  of specialization  or  interest.  Dominant language
discourse structures  tend  to  「ecede, although  discourse may  still  reflect the oral
paragraph  structure of their own  language  rather than  that  of the target  language.

Advanced-Mid speakers  contribute to  conversations  on  a variety  of familiar topics,
dealt with concretely,  with  mu[ h accura [ y, clarity  and  precision,  and  they convey
their  intended  message  without misrepresentation  or confusion.  They  are  readily
understood by native speakers  unaccustomed  to dealing with  non-natives.  When
called on  to perform  functions  or handle topics associated  with  the  Supe「ior level,
the  quality and/or quantity of their speech  will  generally de[
line. Advanced-Mid
speakers  are  often  able to state  an  opinion or cite  [ onditions; however,  they  lack
the  ability to consistently  provide a structured  argument  in  extended  discourse.
Advanced-Mid speakers  may  use  a number of delaying strategieκ resort  to
narration,  description, expianation  or anecdote,  or simply attempt to avoi [ i the
linguistic demands of Superior-level  tasks.

Advanced
Low

Speakers  at the Advanced-Low  level  are able to  handle a variety of communicative
tasks,  although  somewhat haltingly at  times.  They  participate actively  in  most
informal  and  a limited number of formal  conversations  on  activities  related  to
school,  home,  and  leisure activities  and,  to a lesser degree,  those  related  to events
of work,  current,  public,  and personal  interest or individual  relevance.

Advanced-Low speakers  demonstrate the ability to  narrate and  describe in  all
major time frames  (past,  present,  and  future)  in  paragraph  length  discourse,  but
control  of aspect may be  lacking at times.  They  can  handle appropriately the
linguistic challenges  presented  by a complication or unexpected  tu 「n of events  that
occurs within the context of a routine situation or communicative task  with  which
they  are otherwise familiar,  though  at  times  their discourse  may  be  minimal  for the
level  and  strained.  Communicative strategies  such  as  rephrasing and
circumlocution  may  be employed  in such  instances.  In  their narrations and
descriptions,  they combine and  link sentences  into connected discourse of
paragraph  length.  When  pressed  for a fuller account,  they  tend  to  grope and  rely
on  minimal  discourse.  Their utterances are typically not longer than  a single
paragraph.  Structure of the  dominant language  is  still  evident in  the  use  of false
cognates,  literal  translations,  or the oral  paragraph  structure of the  speaker’s own
language  rather than  that of the target  language.

While the  language of Advanced-Low speakers  may be marked  by  substantial,
albeit irregular flow,  it is  typically somewhat strained  and  tentative,  with  noticeable
self-correction  and  a certain  grammatical  roughness ‘  The  vocabulary of Advanced
Low speakers  is  primarily generic  in  nature.

116

CHAPTER  7  Teaching Across  Proficiency Levels

Advanced-Low  speakers  contribute to  the  conversation  with sufficient accu 「acy,
clarity,  and  precision  to  convey their  intended  message  without misrepresentation  or
confusion,  and  it can  be  understood by  native speakers  unaccustomed to dealing with
non-natives,  even  though  this  may  be achieved through  repetition  and  restatement.
When  attempting to perform  functions  or handle topics associated  with the Superior
level,  the  linguistic quality and  quantity of their speech  will  deteriorate significantly.

Intermediate
High

Intermediate-High  speakers  are able to converse  with  ease  and confidence when
dealing with  most routine tasks  and  social  situations of the  Intermediate  level.  They
are able to  handle successfully  many  uncomplicated tasks  and  social  situations
requiring an  exchange of basic  information  related  to  work,  school,  recreation,
partic 비 ar interests and  areas  of competence,  though  hesitation  and  errors  may be
evident.

Intermediate-High speakers  handle the tasks  peπaining to  the Advanced  level,  but they
are unable to sustain  performance at that  level  ove「 a variety of topics.  With some
consisten〔μ speakers at the  Intermediate High  level  narrate and describe in  major time
frames  using connected discourse of paragraph  len망h However, their performance of
these Advanced-level  tasks  will exhibit one or more features  of breakdown,  such  as  the
failure to maintain the  narration or description semantically or syntactically  in  the
app「opriate major time frame,  the disintegration  of connected  discourse,  the  misuse of
cohesive devices,  a reduction  in breadth and appropriateness of vocabulary,  the failure to
successfully circumlocute,  or a significant amount of hesitation.

Intermediate-High speakers  can  generally be  understood by native speakers
unaccustomed to dealing with  non  natives,  although the  dominant language  is  still
evident (e.g.,  use  of code-switching,  false  cognates,  literal  translations,  etc.),  and gaps
in  communication  may occur.

Intermediate
Mid

Speakers at the  Intermediate-Mid level  are able to handle successfully a variety of
uncomplicated communicative tasks  in straightforward social  situations.  Conversation
is  generally  limited to  those  predictable and concrete exchanges  necessa 「y for survival
in  the  target culture;  these  include personal  information covering self,  family,  home,
daily activities,  interests  and  personal  preferences,  as  well  as  physical  and social
needs,  such  as  food,  shopping,  travel  and  lodging.

Intermediate-Mid speakers  tend to function  reactively,  for example,  by responding to
direct questions  or requests  for  info「mation. Howeve「, they are  capable of asking a
variety of questions when  necessary  to obtain  simple information to satisfy basic
needs,  such  as  directions,  prices and  services.  When called on  to perform functions
or handle topics at the Advanced  level,  they provide some  information  but have
diffi [ ulty linking ideas,  manip비ating time and aspect,  and  using communicative
strategies,  such  as  circumlocution.

Intermediate-Mid speakers  are able to express  personal  meaning by creating with  the
language,  in  part by  combining and  recombining known elements and conversational
input to  make utterances of sentence  length  and  some strings  of sentences.  Their
speech  may  contain  pauses,  reformulations and self-corrections as  they search  for
adequate vocabulary and appropriate language forms to express themselves.  Because

Intermediate
Low

Novice High

CHAPTER  7  Teaching Across  Proficiency Levels

117

of inaccuracies  in  their vocabulary and/or pronunciation and/or grammar and/or
syntax,  misunderstandings can  occur,  but Intermediate  Mid speakers  are  generally
understood by sympathetic  interlocutors accustomed  to  dealing with  non-natives.

Speakers  at the  Intermediate-Low level  are  able to  handle successfully a  limited
number of uncomplicated communicative tasks  by creating with  the  language  in
straightforward  social  situations.  Conversation  is  restricted  to s。me 。f the concrete
exchanges  and  predictable topics  necessary  for  survival  in  the  target language
culture.  These  topics relate to  basic  personal  inf。rmation covering,  for example,
self and  family,  some  daily activities and  personal  preferences,  as  well  as  to  some
immediate needs,  such  as  ordering food and  making simple purchases.  At the
Intermediate-Low  level,  speakers  are  primarily reactive  and  struggle  to  answer
direct questions or requests  for  information,  but they are also able to  ask  a few
appropriate questions.

Intermediate-Low speakers express  personal  meaning by combining and
recombining  into sho 「t statements what they know and what they  hear from  their
interlocutors.  Their  utterances are often filled with  hesitancy and  inaccuracies as
they search  for appropriate linguistic forms  and  vocabulary while attempting to
give form  to the  message.  Their speech  is  characterized  by frequent pauses,
ineffective reformulations and  self-corrections.  Their pronunciation,  vocabulary
and  syntax are  st「ongly influenced by their first  language but,  in  spite of frequent
misunderstandings that require repetition  or rephrasing,  Intermediate-Low speakers

can  gεnerally be  ur녕e「sto。d by sympathetic  inter!。cut。rs, part‘cu!arly by th。se

accustomed to  dealing with  non-natives.

Speakers  at the  Novice-High  level  are  able to  handle a variety of tasks  pertaining
to  the  Intermediate level,  but are  unable to  sustain  performance at that  level.  They
are able to  manage  successfully a number of uncomplicated communicative tasks
in  straightforward  social  situations.  Conversation  is  restricted  to  a few of the
predictable topics  necessary for survival  in  the  target  language culture,  such  as
basic  personal  information,  basic  objects and  a limited number of activities,
preferences and  immediate needs.  Novice-High speakers  respond  to  simple,  di 「ect
questions or requests  for  information;  they  are able to  ask  only a very  few
formulaic  questions when  asked  to  do so.

Novice-High speakers  are  able to express  personal  meaning by  relying  heavily on
learned  phrases  or recombinations of these  and  what they  hear from their
interlocutor.  Their  utterances,  which consist mostly of short and  sometimes
incomplete sentences  in  the  present,  may  be  hesitant or inaccurate.  On the other
hand,  since these  utterances are frequently only expansions of learned  material
and  stock phrases,  they  may sometimes  appear surprisingly fluent and  accurate.
These  speakers'  first  language may strongly  influence their pronunciation,  as  well
as  their vocabulary and  syntax  when they attempt to  personalize their utterances.
Frequent misunderstandings may arise but,  with  repetition  or rephrasing,  Novice
High  speakers  can  generally be  understood  by sympathetic  interlocutors used  to
non-natives.  When called  on  to handle simply a variety of topics and  perform
functions peπaining to the Intermediate level,  a  Novice-High  speaker can
sometimes  respond  in  intelligible sentences,  but will  not be able to sustain
sentence  level  discourse.

118

CHAPTER  7  Teaching Across  Proficiency Levels

‘ovice Mid

Speakers at the  Novice-Mid  level  communicate  minimally and with  difficulty  by  using
a number of isolated  words and  memorized  phrases  limited  by  the  particular context
in  which  the  language has  been  learned.  When  responding to direct questions,  they
may  utter only two or three words at a time or an  occasional  stock  answer.  They
pause frequently  as they  search  for  simple vocabulary or attempt to  recycle their own
and their  interlocutor’s words.  Because of hesitations,  lack  of vocabulary,  inaccuracy,
or failure  to  respond  appropriately,  Novice-Mid  speakers  may  be  understood with
great difficulty  even  by  sympathetic  interlocutors accustomed to  dealing with  non
natives.  When  called on  to  handle topics  by  performing functions  associated with  the
Intermediate  level,  they frequently  resort to  repetition,  words from  their  native
language,  or silence.

'llovice  Low

Speakers at the  Novice-Low  level  have  no  real  functional  ability  and,  because of their
pronunciation,  they  may  be  unintelligible.  Given  adequate time and familiar  cues,
they  may  be able to exchange greetings,  give their  identity,  and  name a number of
familiar objects from  their immediate environment.  They are  unable to  perform
functions  or  handle topics  peπaining to  the  Intermediate  level,  and cannot therefore
participate  in  a true conversational  exchange.

3. Teacher talk

Your input in the class is crucial.  All  ears and eyes are indeed focused on yo·
u.
Your  o‘wn  English  needs  to  be  clear  articulated.  It is  appropriate  to  slow your
speech somewhat for easier student comprehension, but don’t  slow it so much that
it  loses  its  naturalness.  And  remember,  you  don’t  need  to  talk  any  lo때er  to
beginners  than  to  advanced  students  if your  articulation  is  clear.  Use  ~imple
vocabulary and structures that are at or just slightly beyond their level. 1

Is  it  appropriate  to  use  the  students'  nativ1ε  language?  As  noted  above,  in
second language situations, especially multilingual classes, your usε of a  studεnt’s
native  language  is  seldom  an  issue.  In  foreign  language  situations,  however,  it
becomes an option.  It is important not to let your classes go to e한원입n the use
of the  students'  native  language.  The  rule  of thumb  here  is  usually  to  restrict
classroom languag1ε to English unless some distinct advantage is gained by the use
of their native language, and then only for very brief stretches of time.  Examples
of such advantages include

•  negotiation of disciplinaπ and other m따iagεment factors,
•  brief descriptions of how to carπ out a technique,
•  brief explanations of grammar points,
•  quick pointers on meanings  of words that remain confusing after students

have had a try at defining something themselves, and

•  cultural notes and comments.

4. Authenticity of language

The language that you expose your students to should, according to principles
of CLT discussed in Chapters 3 and 4, be authentic language; this is just as important

CHAPTER  7  Teaching Across  Proficien [ y Levels

119

at  thε  beginning  levεls.  Simple  greetings  and  introductions,  for  examplε,  arε
authentic  and  yet  manageable.  Makε sur,ε utterancεs are  limited  to  short, simplε
phrasεs.  At  times  such  languagε may  appεar to  be  artificial  bεca usε of  all  the
rεpetition nεεded at  this  stage.  Don’t  despair; your  students  will  apprεciate thε
opportunity to practice their new language.

;. Fluency and accuracy

Fluency is a goal at this level but only within limitεd utterance lengths.  Fluency
doεs not have to apply only to long uttεrancεs.  Thε “ flow” of language is important
to  establish,  from  the  bεginning,  in  rεasonably  short  segments.  Attention  to
accuracy  should  cεnter on  the  particular  gr.immatical, phonological, or discourse
εlements that are bεing practiced.

In  tεaching speaking  skills, it  is  extrεmely important at  this  stagε that you bε
very sensitive to studεnts ’  nεεd to practicε freely and opεnly without fear of bεing
corrected  at  every  minor  1aw.  On  the  other  hand,  you  need  to  correct  somε
selεcted grammatical  and  phonological  errors  so  that  students  don’t  fall  into  thε
trap  of assuming  that “ no  news  is  good  nεws ” (no  correction  impliεs perfection).
Pronunciation  work  (on  phonemεs, phonemic  patt℃ms, intonation,  rhythm,  and
strεss) is  vεry important at  this  stage.  Neglεcting phonological  practice now may
be  at  the  εxpensε of later  ‘luency.  Your  job, of course,  is  to  creat℃ the  pεrfiεct
balance.  Chapter 17 will deal in mor,ε detail with this balance.

6. Student creativity

The  ultimate  goal  of learning  a  languag,ε is  to  bε ablε to  comprehend  and
produce it in uη rehearsed situations, which demand both rεceptive and productivε
cr,εativity.  But  at  the  beginning  level,  studεnts can  bε cr,εative  only  within  the
confines  of a  highly  controllεd rεpertoire of languagε.  Innovation will come  latεr
whεn studεnts get more language under their control.

7. Techniques (activities, procedures, tasks)

Short,  simpl℃  Iεchniques  must  bε used.  Some  mεchanical  tεchniquεs  arε
appropriate-choral  rεpetition  and  othεr  drilling,  for  example.  A  good  many
tεachεr-initiated questions dominate at this level, followed only after some timε by
an  increase  in  simpl℃ studεnt-initiated  questions.  Group  and  pair  activities  arε
excellent techniques as long as they are structured and clearly defined with specific
objεctives.  A  variety  of  techniques  is  important  becausε  of  limited  languag℃
capacity.

8. Listening and speaking goals

9. Reading and writing goals

A glance at the  Scopε and Sequence chart in Figure  7.1  demonstrates typical
goals for a  beginning level course: reading and writing topics are confined to brief
but  neverthεlεss real-life  written  matεrial.  Adv'εrtisements, forms,  and  recipes  are
grist for the beginner’S  reading miU, while written work may involve forms, lists, and
simple  notes  and  letters.  Thε most  important  contεxtual factor  that  you  should
bear in mind in teaching rεading and writing to  beginners is  their literacy level  in
their own native language, an issue that is  covered in Chaptεr 18.

10. Grammar

Whεther a  curriculum  or  textbook  is  billed  as  functional,  communicative,
structural, or whatev,εr, grammar and grammar sequencing is an issue.  As the charts
show, a typical bεginning level will deal at the outset ,with vεπ simple verb forms,
personal pronouns, definite  and  indefinitε articles, singular  and  plural  nouns, and
simple  sentences, in a  progr,εssion of grammatical topics from  simple to complex.
(See  Chapter  22  for  morε information  on grammar  sequencing  in  textbooks  and
curricula.)

Whether or not you  choose  to  overtly “explain” grammar in the  classroom  is
another  issuε (also  d℃alt with  in  Chapter  22).  If you  arε teaching  EFL  (in  a  non
English-speaking country) and your students all  spεak the same native languagε, you
may  profit  from  occasionally  using  their  native  language  to  explain  simplε
grammatical points.  In ESL situations, wher,ε you usually rely only on English in thε
classroom,  grammatical  explanations  of  any  complexity  would  at  this  lεvεl
overwhelm  the  studεnts.  Therefore,  an  inductive  approach  to  grammar  with
suitable examples and patterns will be more effective.

TEACHING INTERMEDIATE LEVELS

Now,  turn  your  attention  to  that  vague  curricular  tεrritory  that  we  call
intermediate, where students have pr,앵ressεd beyond novice stages to an ability to
sustain basic communicative tasks, to establish some minimal fluency, to deal with
a few unrehearsed situations, to self correct on occasion, to use a few compensatoη
strategies, and generally to “get along" in the language  beyond mere  survival.  The
pictur'ε  changes  somewhat.  Your  role  and  the  students’  capacities  change.
Consider the same  10 factors.

1. Students' cognitive learning processes

At the intermediate stage some automatic processing has taken hold.  Phrasεs,
sentences,  structures,  and  conversational  rules  hav1ε  been  practiced  and  are
increasing in number, forcing the mental processes to automatize.  I like to think of
automaticity  as  the  placing  of elements  of language  into  the “hard  driv1ε” of our
neurological  computers.  Our  immediately  controllεd  “ desktops”(limited  in
capacity)  are  too  small  to  contain  all  the  information  we  need.  One  of  your

CHAPTER  7  Teaching Across  Proficiency Levels

125

principal goals at t피S level is~。 get students to continue to autor때Z야o continue
to allow the bits and pieces of languagε that might clutter the mind to be relegated
to  automaticity.  There,  in  their  linguistic  hard  drives,  those  bits  and  pieces  are
beneath the surface, as  it wer,ε, yet readily available for  immediate (automatic) use
whenever neεded.

2. The role of the teacher

You  are  no  longer  the  only  initiator  of  language.  Studεnts  should  be
encouraged  to  ask  questions,  make  comments,  and  negotiate  certain  options  in
learning where appropriate.  Mordstudent-student interaction\fan now take place
in pairs, small groups, and whole-class activity.

Learner-centεred work is  now possible for  more  sustained lengths of time as
students are able to maintain topics of discussion and focus.  By its veπ nature, the
intεrmediate level is  richly diverse; that diversity can work to your advantage with
carefully  designed  cooperative  activities  that  capitalizε  on  differences  among
students.  Don’t  set  equal  expectations  for  all  studεnts, howev'εr, since  abilities,
especially speaking abilities, can vaη widely.

∼

3. Teacher talk

Most of your oral prodm;:tion can bε sustained at a natural pace, as long as your
articulation is clear.  Teacher talk should not occupy the major proportion of a class
hour; otherwise, you are probably not giving  studεnts enough opportunity’  to talk.
You should be using less of the native language of the learners at this level, but some
situations may still demand it.

4. Authenticity of language

At  this level students sometimes become overly concerned about grammatical
correctness  and  may  want  to  wander  into  esoteric  discussions  of  grammatical
details.  This penchant for analysis might get them too far afield from authεntic, real
language.  Make sure they stay on track.

5. Fluency and accuracy

The dichotomy between fluency and accuracy is a crucial concern here, more
so than at either of the other ends of the proficiency spectrum.  Some students are
likely to become overly concerned about accuracy, possibly berating themselves for
the  mistakes  they  make  and  demanding  constant  corrections  for  eveπ slip-up.
Others may slide into a self. satisfied rut in which they actually become quite fluent,
in  the  technical  sense  of the  term,  but  in  which  they  become  very  difficult  to
comprehend.  Be on thε lookout for both types of student and be prepared to oftiεr
individualizεd attention to each.

In general, fluency exercises (saying or writing a steady flow of language for a
short period of time without any selε or other-correction at all)  are a  must at this
level.  They help  to get students over the  hump of always  having to say or write
everything  absolutely  correctly.  You  want  them  in  due  course  of  time  to  go
through the “ breakthrough” stage of language learning, often thought of as a stage

126

CHAPTER  7  Teaching A[ ross Pr,。ficiency Levels

after  which  a  learner  looks  back  and  says,  “Wow!  I  just  carried  on  a  whole
conversation without  thinking  about  my  grammar! ” A big  part  of your task  with
most students is to maintain their flow with just enough attεntion to error to keεp
thεm growing.

6.  Student creativity

The fact that some of this nεw language is now under control giv1εs risε to more
opportunities for thε studεnt to be creative.  Interlanguage errors su난f급도-∼---

Does John can sing?

are  a  good indication of thε crεativ1ε application  of a  systεm within the  learnεr’s
mind.  Try  to  recognize  this  form  of  creativity  as  a  positive  sign  of  language
developmεnt and  of  thε internalization  of a  coherent  system.  Students  are  also
becoming  more  capable  of  applying  their  classroom  language  to  unrehearsed
situations.  In  EFL  settings  those  situations  may  be  more  difficult  to  find,  but
through the various forms  of mεdia and the written word, applications to the rεal
world,  heretofore  unrehearsed  in  the  classroom,  are  available  and  should  bε
encouraged.

7. Techniques

Because of the increasing languagε capacities of your students, techniques can
increase  in  complexity.  Common  intεractiv,ε techniques for  intermediates include
)℃없따학엔댄, surveys and polls, paired inteπiews, group problem solving, role pl때s,

storytεHing, and many others.

8. Listening and speaking goals

The  linguistic  complexity  of  communicative  listening-speaking  goals
incr'εases  stεadily.  Along  with  the  creation  of  novel  utterances,  students  can
participate  in  short  conversations,  ask  and  answεr quεstions, find  alternativε
ways  to  convey  meaning,  solicit  informatigg 」!9m others,  and  more.  The
functions themselves may nofl)터교화굽눈꾀fy more 야교꾀랴퍼ut the forms they
use are.  (For morε information on teaching listening and speaking, see Chapters
18 and  19.)

9. Reading and writing goals

Increasing  complεxity  in  tεrms  of  length,  grammar,  and  discourse  now
characterizes reading material as  studεnts read paragraphs and short, simple stories
and  begin  to  use  skimming  and  scanning  skills.  Writing  is  similarly  more
sophisticated.  (For more information on teaching reading and writing, see Chapters
20 and 21.)

TEACHING ADVANCED  LEVELS

As  studεnts move  up  the  developmεntal ladder, getting  closer and  closer  to  their
goals, dεvεloping fluency  along  with  a  greater  dεgr'εc of accuracy, able  to  handle
virtually  any  situation  in  which  targεt language  use  is  demandεd, thεy be comε
“ ad vaneεd” studεnts.  Toward  thε top  of this  ladder is  what the ACTFL  Guideliηes
dεscribε as  the “ superior” level, which is  not yεt εquivalent to an educat℃d nativε­
spεak℃r  lεvel,  but  implies  a  high  command  of  languagε  for  both  social  and
professional purposes.  Few if any ESL  classεs are  designεd for the supεrior lev,εl, so
in  order  to  be  more  in  kεeping with  reality,  we  will  simply  focus  on  what  the
Guideliηes describe as  thε “ advanced” lεvel.

1. Students' cognitive learning processes

As  competencε in  language  continues  to  build, students  can  realize  thε full
spectrum of procεssing,원sigr낀118 largεr ancj_l~!]~~겐맺햇t~-~Ut{)낀1atiCf!1Q잃융 and
gaining the confidεncε to put the formal structures of languagε on the periphεπ so
that focal  attεntion may be given to the interpretation and negotiation of meaning
and to thε conveying of thoughts and fεelings in interactive communication.  Somε

[ HAPTER 7  Teaching Across  Proficiency Levels

aspects  of  language,  of  course,  neεd  focal  attεntion  for  minor  cornεctions,
refinement, and othεr “tinkering”; otherwise, teachers would almost be unnεcεssary.
So  your task at this  lev,℃l is  to assist in  thε ongoing attεmpt to automatize languag,ε
and  in  thε delicate  intεrplay betwεεn focal  and  peripheral  attεntion to  sel℃cted
aspects of language.

2.  The role of the teacher

On the surface, your job may appεar easier with advancεd students; you can sit
back and  lεt their  questions  and  self generated  curiosity  take  over.  In  reality,  the
in dependεnce  that  students  have  acquired  must  be  cleverly  channeled  into
classroom  routines  that  benefit  most  of the  studεnts most  of the  time.  No  mεan
task!  Thε most  common  occurnεncε in  advancεd lεvεl teaching  is  that your  class
runs away with itsεIf and you ar1ε l℃ft with only a quartεr or half your plans fulfilled.
So, while you want to take advantage of the self starting personalities in your class,
ordεrly plans arε still important.  A directiv1ε rol℃ on your part can create ef:fiεctiv1ε
lεarning opportunities εven within a prεdominantly learner-centerεd classroom.

3. Teacher talk

Natural  language  at  natural  speed  is  a  must  at  this  lεvel.  Make  sure  your
students are challengεd by your choice of vocabulary, structures, idioms, and other
language  features.  But, after  all,  they are  still  learning  the  language, so  remember
that  they  have  not  yet  turned  into  native  speakεrs.  Thε amount  of teacher  talk
should be  commensuratε with the type  of activity.  Makε sure your students  havε
ample opportunities to produce language so that your role as a provider of feedback
takes prominence.  For some of your students, this is the last chance to bεnefit from
informed, systematic feedback on their performancε; from her,ε on out, thεy will be
“out there" wherε people, out of p이iteness or respect, rar1εly givε corrections.

Very  little,  if any,  reliance  on  the  students’  native  language  is  now  justified.
Disciplinε, εxplanations, and other more complεx language functions can be carriεd
out in En영ish.  Occasionally, a teacher of an advanced class will rεsort to a word or
two (a de mition,for εxample) in thε native language in order to help a studεntwho
is “ stuck.”

4. Authenticity of language

Ev1εrything  from  academic  prose  to  literatur℃ to  idiomatic  conversation
becomεs a  legitimate  resource for  the  classroom.  Virtually  no  authentic  language
material  should  be  summarily  disqualified  at  this  stage.  Certain  restrictions  may
come to bear, dεpending on how advanced your class is, of course.

5. Fluency and accuracy

At this level most, if not all, of your studεnts are “fluent ” in that they have passed
beyond  the  breakthrough  stage  and  are  no  longer thinking  about  eveη word  or
structure they are producing or comprehending.  A handful or two of problems still
need  attention.  If errors  are  relativ,εly rare, an  occasional  treatment  from  you  or
from peers may be quite helpful.

CHAPTER  7  Teaching Across Proficiency Levels

129

6. Student creativity

The joy of teaching at this level  is  in those moments of student performance
when you know that they are now able to apply classroom material to real contexts
beyond.  Make  sure  that  students  keep  their  eyes  fixed  on  those  goals.  Be  ever
waπ of classroom activity that simply ends right there in the classroom.

7.Techniques

Techniques  can  no‘W  tap  into  a  full  range  of sociolinguistic  and  pragmatic
competencies.  Typical  activities  include  group  debates  and  argumentation,  and
complex  role  plays.  Students  also  benefit  from  scanning  and  skimming  reading
material,  determining  and  questioning  author's  intent,  and  writing  essays  and
critiques.  Often  at  this  level  students  have  specifiι purposes  for  which  they  are
planning to use English.  Focus on those purposes as much as possible.

8. Listening and speaking go떼S

At  this  level  students can focus  more  carefully  on  all  the  sociolinguistic  and
pragmatic  nuances  of  language.  Thε teacher  needs  to  be  on  the  lookout  for
common areas  needing work and to  guide  students accordingly as  they fine-tune
their  production  and  comprehension  in  terms  of register, style, the  status  of the
interlocutor, the  specific  context  of a  convεrsational exchange, turn-taking, topi c
nomination  and  termination,  topic-다hanging, and  culturally  conditioned  language
constraints.

9. Reading and writing goals

Reading and writing skills similarly pr,앵ress closεr and closer to native-speaker
comp℃tence as students learn more about such things as critical reading, the role of
schemata in interpreting written texts, and how to write a document related to one’s
profession (laboratory reports, records of εxperimental research findings, etc.).

10. Grammar

The  concern  at  the  intermediate  level  for  basic  grammatical  patterns  now
graduates beyond some of the elements of Level 4 in Figure 7.1 to functional forms,
to  sociolinguistic  and  pragmatic  phenomena,  and  to  strategic  competence  (see
PLLτ Chapter  10).  Linguistic  metalanguage  may now seπe 힌nore useful  role  as
students  perceive  its  relevance  to  refining  their language.  Your  classes  need  not
become  saturated  with  language  about  language,  but  well-targeted  deductive
grammar has its place.

CHAPTER  8

SOCIOCULTURAL.  POLITICAL.

AND  INSTITUTIONAL CONTEXTS

OBJECTIVES  After  reading  this chapter,  you  will  be able to:

•  identi디 sociopolitical and

institutional variables that affect
how you would design and carη7
out a lesson or course

•  appreciate the nuances of culture,
identity, ethnicity, and situational
politics that are dεeply ingrained
in students' minds and emotions

•  evaluate the different factors

involved in teaching in a “ second”
language situation and a “foreign”
language situation

•  dev,εlop a position on the

advantages and disadvantages
of native and nonnative
En밍ish-spea힘ng teachers

•  understand how systems,

regulations, 따id customs within an
educational institution can affect
your approach to teaching

Age and proficiεncy are two major contextual variablεs that will affect every aspect
of your lesson  or curriculum.  Thεy may, in  fact,  be the  most  imp야tant variables.
But another cluster of factors also emerges for the languag1ε tεacher: sociocultural,
political, and institutional contexts, without consideration of which your classroom
lεssons may  miss  their  mark.  Thεsε domains  intertwine  in  such  a  way  that  it  is
sometimes  impossible  to  disentangle  them  and  examine  one without considering
the  other.  Culture  underlies  εvery  human  bεing ’s  ℃motion  and  cognition;
governments, politics, and p이icies are εqually powεrful influences on our behavior;
and  finally,  our  educational  institutions  are  products  of  culturε and  policy,  and
indeed are often microcosms of one’S  sociop이itical milieu.

While  this  chapter w피 not  attεmpt to  treat all  such  issues  in  detail, they are
neverthelεss important background variablεs to consider whεnever you step into a
language classroom.

CULTURAL CONTEXTS

Culture is a way of life.  It is the context within which we exist, think, feel, and relate
to others.  It is  the “glue” that binds a  gro띠P of people togethεr.  It’s  our collective
identity, our “blueprint” (Larson & Smalley, 1972, p. 39) that guides our bεhavior in a
community.  It  makes  us  sensitive  to  matters  of status, and  helps  us  know  what

133

others expect of us and what will happen if we do not live up to their expectations.
It’S  also  dynamic,  chani굉ng, situational,  and  at  the  same  time,  relatively  stable
(Matsumoto, 2000).

Culture  also  establishes  for  each  person a  context of cognitive  and  affective
behavior, a  template for personal and social existence.  We  tend to perceive reality
within the context of our own culture, a reality that we have “ created;’ and therefore
not necessarily a reality that is empirically defined.  Although the opportunities for
world  travel  in  the  last  several  decades  have  increased  markedly,  there  is  st피 a
tendency  for  us  to  believe  that  our  own  reality  is  the  “correct”  perception.
Misunderstandings  are  therefore  likely  to  occur  between  members  of  different
cultures.

It should be obvious, then, that culture is highly important in the learning of a
second  language.  A  language  is  a  part  of a  culture,  and  a  culture  is  a  part  of a
language.  The  two  are  intricately  interwoven  so  that  one  cannot  separate  them
without losing the significance of either language or culture.  The acquisition of a
second  language,  except  for  specialized, instrumental  acquisition  (as  may  be  the
case, say,  in acquiring a  reading  knowledge  of a  language  for  examining  scientific
texts), is also the acquisition of a second culture.  (For a more detailed treatment of
culture, consult PLLT,  Chapter 7.)

How  does  an understanding  of the  relationship  of languagε to  culture affect
your own teaching? The possibilities are almost  endless.  Wheth℃r you’re  teaching
ESL(in an “inner circle” countπ where English is the dominant language) or EFL (in
an outer or expanding circle country), cultural factors affect you.  In ESL  contexts,
your  students  are  usually  “guests”  from  other  countries  learning  both  a  new
language  and  a  new culture  simultaneously; in  EFL  contε:xts your  students  are  in
their  home  culture  attempting  to  learn  a  language  imbued  with  foreign  cultural
connotations.  Those cultural connotations will be discussed in the next section.

For the moment, however, consider the following guidelines on accounting for

cultural issues in your classroom:

1.  A student’s cultural identity is oftεn a deeply seated bundle of emotions.

Practice empathy as you relate to your students in cultural matters: behavior
patterns and expectations; expected relationship to authority, famil'κ and
peers; ambiguity, tolerance, and openness to new ideas and ways of thinking;
students’ attitudes toward their own and the L2  culture; their view of
individualism versus collectivism; linguistic conventions of politeness,
formality, and other sociopragmatic factors.

2.  Recognize the cultural connotations and nuances of En밍ish and of the first

language of your students.  Capitalize on them in your teaching.

3.  Use your classroom as an opportunity to educate your students about other
cultures and help them to see that no one culture is “better'’ than another.
Practice in words and 얀~d _your respect for your students’ deeply ingrained
emotions that stem from their cultural schemata.

4.  As  cultural differences emergε, help your students to appreciatε and celebrate
diversity.  Espεcially in an ESL  context whεre students in the samε class may
rεpr,℃sent many differ℃nt cultures, try to makε your classroom a modεl of
openness, tolerancε, and rεspεct.

SECOND AND FOREIGN LANGUAGE CONTEXTS

Insomε of our professional musing about tεaching and learning, wε intεrchange the
terms  second and  foreign  in  rεfεrring to  English  language  teaching.  (See  PLLT,
Chapter  7, for  a  discussion  of these  two  tεrms.) But  somε caution  is  warranted,
particularly  in  relation  to  a  curriculum  or  a  lesson,  because  (a)  the  difference
bεtweζn the two is  significant, and (b) this dichotomy has been overgeneralized in

foreign  languagε  departmεnt hallway,  out  on  thε  sidεwalks  and  in  thε  st or,εs?
Second  language  learning  contexts  are  thosε  in  which  the  classroom  targ'εt
language  is  rεadily available  out  there.  Teaching  English  in  the  Unitεd States  or
Australia clearly falls  into this (ESL)  catego디1.  Foreign languagε contexts are those
in  which  studεnts do  not  hav,ε ready-made  contεxts for  communication  beyond

opportunities, books, or an  occasional  tourist, but efforts  must be  madε to  create
such  opportunities.  Tεaching English  in Japan  or Morocco  or Thailand  is  almost
always a context of English as  a foreign  languagε (EFL).

The  seemingly  clear  dichotomy  betwεεn ESL  and  EFL,  however,  has  bεen
considerably muddied in recent years with the incr'εasing use of English worldwidε
for a  variety of purposes (Nayar,  1997).  First, ESL  contexts vary from  an American
or British context, wher,ε monolingual native speakers abound, to countries such as
India or Singapore, where  English  is  a widely us℃d second language for  education,
gov,εrnment, and  commercε, to  Scandinavian  countries,  whεrε English  has  no
official  status  but  is  commonly  spoken  by  virtually  every  educated  person.
Likewisε, in  countries  wher,ε a  language  might  bε quickly  judged  as  forεign (for
instanc℃, Spanish or Chinese in the United States, English in Japan), learnεrs may find
readily  available  potential  for  authentic  use  of  the  languag,ε in  such -~n_µ~s as
indigenous languagε comm uni tiεs and the media (Internεt,TV, film).

∼∼￦ml that fair warning, it is still useful to consider the pedagogical implications
for a continuum of contexts ranging from high-visibiliry, ready access to the target
languagε outside the language classroom to no access beyond the classroom door.
In a typical second language context, your students have a  tremendous advantage.
They  have  an  instant  “laboratory”  availablε  24  hours  a  day.  I  often  remind  my
studεnts studying ESL at the American Language Institutε that their classroom hours

Communicative language Iεaching in what we might broadly categorizε as  an
EFL  contεxt is clearly a greater challeng'ε for students and teachers,  Often, intrinsic

Can students learn English in an EFL setting? (or French in an “FFL'’ setting?) Thε

•  Provide  plenty  of extra-class  learning  opportunitiεs, such  as  assigning  an
English-spεaking movie,  having  them  listen  to  an  English-speaking  TV  or
radio  program,  getting  an  En밍ish-spεaking  convεrsation  partner,  doing
outside  reading  (news  magazines,  books),  writing  a  journal  or  diary,  in
English, on their learning process,

•  Form a language club and schedule regular activities,

ENGLISH AS  AN INTERNATIONAL LANGUAGE

Closely  I℃lated  to  thε  ESL/EFL  distinction  is  the  phenomenon  of  the  role  of
internationalizεd variεties of English  (seε PLLT,  Chaptεr 7).  As  English  takes  on
more and  morε of a  second language  role  in  a  country (such  as  in Singapore, for
example),  thεrε is  a  grεater likεlihood of  thε gro"πh of  a  nativized  variety  of
English  in  that  country.  A  good  dεal of  rεsearch has  bε℃n carriεd out  on  the
“ Indianization”  of  English  (Kachru,  1992),  with  implications  for  notions  of
acceptability and standardization in oth℃r countriεs like the Philippines, Singaporε,
or  Nigεria.  Two  basic  issues  for  English  teachers  have  emergεd (Kachru,  2005;
McKay, 2002):

1.  English is increasingly bεing used as a tool for interaction among nonnative

speakers.  Well over one-half of thε onε billion English sp℃akers of the world
lεarned English as a sεcond (or foreign) language.  Most English language
tεachers across the globe are nonnativ,ε English speakers, which mεans that
the norm is not monolingualism, but bilingualism.

2.  English is  not frequεntly learned as a tool for understanding and teaching U.S.
or British cultural values.  Inst℃ad, English has becomε a tool for international
communication in transportation, commercε, banking, tourism, technology,
diplomacy, and scientific research.

This multiplication of varieties of En밍ish posεS somε practical coneεrns for the
teacher.  One  of thosε concerns  is  the  issuε of grammaticalnεss and  correctness.
What standard do you accεpt in your classroom? The practical issue boils down to
the nεed for your opεn acceptance of thε prevailing variety of English in use in the
country where you ’rε tεaching, be it India, Nigeria, or the Philippines.  It is certainly
not nεcessaη to think of English as  a  languagε whose cultural identity can Iiε only
with countries like  the United  Statεs, the United Kingdom, or New Zεaland.  Your
students will no doubt be more  interestεd in  thε practical, nonstigmatized uses of
English  in  various  occupational  fields  in  their  own  country  than  in  imitating
American or British English.

If you’rε not teaching in a countπ whose people use a widely accepted variety
of English, you w피 still, no doubt, find  that your teaching must keep pace with the
nεw pragmatism.  Standards  of grammaticalness  and  of  pronunciation  may  well
need to bε viewed in tεrms of thε practice of natives who are educated, proficient
English speakers.  In Japan, for example, “Japanized” forms of English are becoming

may not be “ perfectly nativεlike” if you yourself are a nonnative En밍ish speaker (see
the  next  section  here  for  further  comments).  The  goals  that  you  set  for  your
students may  therefor,ε more wisely bε goals of clear, unambiguous pronunciation
of English phonology.

Even  if you  are  teaching  English  in what Kachru  (1992)  called  inner circle
countries (United States, United Kingdom, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand), you
are well advised to base your judgmεnts of the acceptability of students' production
on the ultimate practical uses to which they W피 put the language: survival, social,
occupational,  academic,  and  technical  uses.  In  a  city  like  San  Francisco,  for
example, we  hear many varieties  of English.  On one occasion, as  I  interviewed a
prospective  ESL  teacher,  I  conclud℃d from  her  excellent  but “Hong  Kong-ized”
variety  of  English  that  she  was  originally  from  Hong  Kong.  Upon  asking  her,  I
discovered she was a native San Franciscan!

NONNATIVE ENGLISH-SPEAKING TEACHERS

The  foregoing  discussion  unavoidably  leads  into  an  issue  that  has  been  and
continues  to  be  debated  in  language-teaching  circles:  How  imp야tant is  it  that  a
teacher  of a  language  be  a “native” speaker  of that  language? Take  a  look  at  the
employment ads for language schools in newspapers in Taipei, Seoul, Sao  없ulo, or
any  other city you  choose, and you will  find  that at least  9  out of 10 ads  ask  for
“ native  speakers  only.” The  assumption,  of  course,  is  that  a  native  speaker  w퍼
provide a “ correct” model of English.  And as George Braine pointed out (1999), in
such  a  job  market,  qualifications,  ability‘  and  experience  are  of  little  help  for
nonnative English-speakers.  What’s wrong with this picture?

For  many  decades  the  English  language-teaching  profession  assumed  that
native English-speaking teachers (NESTs), by virtue of their superior model of
oral  production,  comprised  the  ideal  English  language  teacher.  Then,  Medgyes
(1994), among others (Braine, 1999; Higgins, 2003; McArthur, 2001), showed in their
research  that  nonnative English-speaking teachers (NNESTs)  offer as  many if
not  more  inherent  advantages.  Not  only  are  multiple  varieties  of  En헝ish  now
considered  legitimate  and  acceptable, but  also  teachers  who  have  actually  gone
through  the  process  of learning  English  possess  distinct  advantages  over  native
speakers.

As  we  move  into  a  new  paradigm  in  which  the  concepts  of  native  and
nonnative speaker become less relevant, it is perhaps more appropriate to think in
terms of the proficiency level of a user of a language.  Spea힘ng is one of four skills
and  may  not  deserve  in  all  contexts  to  be  elevated  to  the  sole  criterion  for
proficiency.  So,  with  Murray  (2006),  Park  (2006),  Kachru  (2005),  K없nhi-Stein
(2004), McKay  (2002), and  others, we see that  the  profession  is  better served by
considering a  person’S  communicative proficiency across the four skills.  Teachers
of English, regardless of their own variety of En맹sh, can then be judged accordingly,
and in turn, their pedagogical training and experience can occupy focal attention.
What are the implications of the NEST versus NNEST  issue for your teaching?

Consider a few possibilities here:

138

[ HAPTER 8  Sociocultural,  Political,  and Institutional Contexts

1.  NESTs  are clεarly and unεquivocally not better teachers than NNESTs by

virtue of thεir native language background.  The most important qualification
for a teaching position is  traiηiηg and experience in teaching English.
2.  If you arε a NNEST searching for a teaching position and you have training
and experience, εntεr into interviεws and application procedures with
confidεnee, and capitalize on the adναntages you bring to such a  position
over a  NEST.

3.  Try to dissuade any employers/administrators you know from insisting on

native  spεakers as  their primary criterion for εmployment.  Try to show thesε
managers that there are a number of legitimate varietiεs of world Englishes,
and that the nativ1ε-spεaker model is  outmoded in today’S  world.

4.  If you are a NNEST and ar℃ now teaching English, and you feel  you haven’t

quite yet reached a point of “ satisfaction” in your own oral production ability,
you can quite εasily compensatε for any slight disadvantage you might have in
oral production through the following: using recorded mεdia to providε
models of speakers of English as a native language along with models of other
English varieties; openly admitting to your studεnts that your own
pronunciation may not correspond to that of a NS  (native speaker); consulting
with other highly proficient users of English if you are not sure about a
phonological or discourse matter; capitalizing on your ability in the skills of
listening, reading, and writing.

5.  If you are either a NEST  or a  NNEST and are now teaching English, considεr

doing the following: informing your students about varieties of English
among native speakers thεmselves (regional and social dialεcts); informing
your studεnts about worldwide varieties of English and not insisting on the
NS  modεl as  thε only possibility; being patient with students whose fragile
egos may bε disturbed by criticism that is  too harsh.

Language policiεs and social climatεs may dictatε the status accorded to native
and  second  languagεs, which  can, in  turn, positively  or  negatively  affect  attitud℃S
and eventual succεss in languagε learning.  Two commonly used terms characterize

A native language is  rεferrεd to as subtractive if it  is  considered to be detrimental

Most EFL pr앵rams are additiv'ε sincε thε native language is the accepted norm.
Moreover, as the foremost international language, English is usually valued highly as

INSTITIJTIONAL CONTEXTS

One  of  thε  most  salient,  if  not  relevant,  contexts  of  language  tεaching  is  the

Institutional  constraints  are  often  allied  to  the  sociop이itical  considerations
discussed  above.  Schools  and  universities  cannot  exist  in  a  social  (or  political)
vacuum.  Public  εl℃mentary and  secondary schools  ar1ε subjεct to  official  national
language p이icy issues.  In thε United States and othεr countriεs, the type of sεcond
languag,ε program  offered  in  schools  is  a  product of legislation  and  govεrnmεntal
rεd !aJ르 Studεnts’ purpos℃S in taking English at the higher education level may be

Elementary and Secondary Schools

Language policies and pr1앵rams in elεmen taη and secondaηr schools differ greatly
from  country  to  country.  Within  some  countries  likε thε United  States, English
Language Learner (ELL)  programs, designed for school-age children whosε nativ1ε
language is not English, vary not only by state but also by school districts, which may
number in the hundreds in larger states.  In EFL  countries, English  is  sometimes a
required  secondary  school  subject  and  almost  always  one  of  sev'εral  foreign
language options.  In certain countries (Sweden and N야way, for example) English
is  even rεquired in εlεmentary schools.

141

A  numbεr of models  are  currently practiced  in  the  Unitεd Stat℃s for  dealing
with  nonnative  English-spεaking students  in  el℃mentary and  secondary  schools.
Some  of  thεse  modεls  apply  to  othεr  countriεs  in  varying  adapted  forms.  A
summaπ of these modεls follows (adapted from Richard-Amato, 2003, pp. 354-357).

1.  Submersion.  The first way of treating nonnative  spεakers in classrooms is
really a lack of tr1εatmεnt: Pupils are simply “ submerg1εd” in regular contεnt­
arεa classes with no special  forεign language instruction.  T~ζ(lSSl!llJ.Ptiorris
that they will “ absorb” English as  thεy focus on the subj(:'.탄 111att~!:  Rεsearch
has shown that sometimes they don't succeedlneither English or thε contεnt
areas, espεcially in subtractive situations.  So, a fεw schools may provide a
pull-out program in which, for perhaps onε pεriod a day, students lεave their
rεgular classroom and attend special tutorials or an ESL  class.

2.  Immersion.  Her'ε,pupils attend 윤ge낀~lly d댄ignξd ~Ontεnt-area classes.  All

thε students in a class speak the same nativε languagε and arε at similar levεls
of proficiency in English.  The teacher is  not only cεrtified in  thε r℃gular
content areas but also has somε knowledge of the  studεnts ’  first  language and
culture.  Immersion programs are found morε commonly in  EFL  contexts than
in ESL  contexts.  In most immersion pr앵rams, pupils arε in an additiv1ε
bilingual contεxtand εnjoy the support of parents and the community in this
enriching experience.

3.  Sheltered En밍ish.  This is  a  specializεd form  ofimmεrsion program that has
bεcome popular in recεnt yεars.  It  diff4εrs from immersion in that students
come from varying native languag℃ backgrounds and the teacher is  trainεd in
both subjεct-matter content and ESL  mεthodology.  Also, students oftεn havε
a regular ESL  class as part of the curriculum.  At  Nεwcomer High School in
San  Francisco, for example, nεwly arriv1εd immigrants are  givεn onε yεar of
she Itεrεd instruction in which ESL-trainεd Iεachers combine contεnt and ESL
in every subject.

4.  Mainstreaming.  In some submεrsion programs, studεnts first  receive

instruction in ESL  before bεing placed into content areas.  Oncε tεachεrs and
tεsts conclude that students arε proficiεnt εnough to be plac℃d into ongoing
content classes, thεy arε mainstreamed into the regular curriculum.  Wε neεd
to  remεmber that this ESL  instruction should bε content-centεred so that
pupils will not be at a  disadvantag1ε once they arε placed in an ongoing class.

5.  Transitional bilingual programs.  In the United States, three different

forms of bilingual εducation-in which students rεceive instruction in some
combination of their first and sεcond languages-arε in common use.
Transitional pr앵rams teach subject-matter content in the native languag1ε,
combined with an ESL  component.  When teachers and tests determine that
thεy are ready, students arε transitioned into regular all-English classεs.  This
has thε advantagε of permitting studεnts to build early cognitive concepts in
their nativε language and then cross over latεr to the dominant language.  The

142

CHAPTER  8  Sociocultural,  Political,  and Institutional Contexts

major disadvantage is  that students are too oftεn mainstreamed bεforε th℃y
arε ready, b℃fore their acadεmic and linguistic skills have been sufficiently
built.

6.  Maintenance bilingual programs.  Here, students continuε throughout
their school years to learn at least a portion of their subject matter in thε
nativ'ε language.  This has thε advantage of stimulating thε continued
dεvelopmεnt of pupils’ nativ,ε languages and of building confidεncε and
expertisε in the content areas.  Disadvantagεs include discouraging thε
masteπ of En밍ish and the high cost of staffing maintεnance classes in
budgetary hard timεs.

7.  Enrichment bilingual programs.  A third form of bilingual εducation has
students taking sεlected subject-matter coursεs in a foreign language while
the bulk of thεir education is carried on in their native languag℃.  Studεnts in
such programs in thε United States are not doing so for survival purposεs, but
simply to “ εnrich ” thεms℃lv,εs by broadening thεir cultural and linguistic
horizons.

Post-Secondary and Adult Education

At  the  next  agζ  lεV℃l  one  finds  a  cluster  of  institutional  English  learning
opportunities  for  adults  who  may  or  may  not  anticipate  going  on  to  college  or
university degree  pr앵rams, but who  nevertheless  may  nεεd English  for  social  or
occupational  purposεs.  Typical  of  these  institutions  ar'ε  those  classified  as
language  schools  (usually  small  proprietary  schools  exclusively  focusεd  on
language instruction), adult education schoois(in thε United States, state- or city
sponsored  centεrs  for  educational,  vocational,  and  recreational  classεs),
community colleges  (two-year,  non-ba<tca!~,택얻-degree-granting institutions  of
highεr  εducation),  and  extended  learning  (also  known  as  continuing
education)  programs  affiliat℃d  with  four-year  collεges  or  universities.  The
following descriptions illustratε thε variεty of courses available in such institutions:

1.  SurvivaVsocial curricula run thε gamut from short courses that introducε
adults to conversational nεcessities to full-blown curricula design℃d to tεach
adults a  completε r따ige of languagε skills for survival in the contεxt of thε
second culture.  By definition, such programs would not progress beyond
intermediate skill levels.  Thεse coursεs ar'ε frequεntly offered in night-school
adult εducation programs and privatε languagε schools such as Bεrlitz
Schools.

2.  Literacy pr앵rams are designεd to teach students whose native language
reading/writing skills are eithεr nonexistent or very poor.  Learning to be
literate in English while learning aural-oral forms as well requires ener잉7 and
motivation on the part of students.  Teachers need spεcial training to tεach at
this challenging level.
