3.  VESL (Vocational ESL)  targets those who are learning trades (carpenters and
electricians, for example), arts (such as photography), and other occupations
not commonly includεd in university progr없ns.  VESL  coursεs may be offered
in technical schools or trade schools (institutions exclusively dedicated to
teaching various arts and trades, ranging from art, fashion design, 따id
architecture to carpentη, automotive mechanics, and masoruγ).

4.  Workplace ESL  pr,앵rams may be housed in any one of the above-named

types of institutions, or in the workplace itself.  These are courses specifically
devoted to teaching language needed for designated professional or
occupational purposes.  They differ from VESL programs in that a coursε w피
usually be narrowly focused on one context, and often offered in the
workplace itself.  For example, progr없ns around the world are offered for
hotel workers (or, even more specifically, housekeepers in hotels), groceπ
store workers, computer industry employees, and employees in multinational
corporations that use English for international communication.

Institutions of Higher Education

English language  teaching  pr1앵rams also  exist, of course, in four-year  colleg1εs and
universities and postgraduate universities.  The types of programs listed below may
also  be  found  in  two-year  community  colleges  and  language  schools,  refiεrred to
above.  Following  are  descriptions  of  three  broad  types  of  curricula  that  are
designed to fit  vaηring student goals:

1.  IEPs (Intensive English Programs) are pr1ε-academic progr따ns designed for

students-usually from non-English「speaking countries-who anticipate entering
a regul따 course of study in an English-speaking college or university.  Some such
programs are “full-time” 때d q띠te rigorous: Students attend classes for 찌 to 25
hours per week, usually for a quarter or a sεmester.  The focus 캔낀혀핀,such
programs from rather general l때gua양 S뻐웰 the advanced-bε맹ner-level to
advanced courses in'reading, writing, 얀때y skills,때d I℃$한ch.

2.  EAP(En웰sh for Academic Rπposes) is a term that is veπ broadly applied to
any course, module, or workshop in which students are taught to deal with
a cadεmically related language and subject matter.  EAP is common at the
advanced level of pre-academic programs as well as in colleges and universities,
especi와ly courses in wriψig at the academic level and in oral production 영i찌ng
presenta디ons, speeches, and p따디cipating in oral discourse in the classroom).
3.  ESP (En힐ish for Specific Purposes) pro~양ams are specifically devoted to pro
fessional fields of study.  A coursε in En행sh for Agriculture or in Business
Writing would fall  under the general rubric of ESP.  Usually ESP courses are
differentiated from VESL English in that ESP refers to disciplines in which
people can get university majors and degrees, while VESL refers to trades and
other certificate pr1앵rams.

CURRICULUM DEVELOPMENT: AN OVERVIEW

Here  is  one  of several  similar  stories  I  have  experiεncεd.  Gust  for  the  sake  of
prudence, I will not name specific places or people.)

A year or two ago I was approached by a university in CεntralAmerica
that wanted my university (San  Francisco  Statε University) to  oft1εr a
four-week  summer “short  course”  in  English.  My  primary  contact
informed me that th℃y would send 45  students betweεn the  agεs of
18  and  24, that  those  students wanted a  rigorous, serious  cours℃ in
academic  English,  and  that  thεy wεr℃  at  a  high  intermediate  to
advanced level of proficiency.  Wε werε told thεy wantedpr1εparation
in theTOEFL® ‘ as many intended to apply to Amεrican universities for
further  study.‘  They  wanted  to  reside  in  our  university  dormitoriεs
and  eat  th℃ir  meals  there.  Thεy also  wanted  several  weekday  and
weekend excursions  to various  sights  in  and  around  San  Francisco,
and  asked  for  an  activities  leader  to  coordinatε  this  part  of  the
pr앵ram.  This, thεn, was in addition to the acadεmic program, which
was to bε five  days a week, for four to five  hours a day.‘

For  reasons  that  are  difficult  to  explain, we  wεre not  ablε to  obtain
any  sort  of verification  of students’ proficiency  in  advance.  Wε had
no  test  scores,  gradεs, or  writing  samples.  A  veπ brief  pr,εliminaπ
questionnairε,  filled  out  by  about  15  students,  provid℃d minimal
dεmographic data  and  broadly  verified  their  intεrest  in  acadεmic

pro~양·ess.  Fin외ly, assessment of students, teacher, and pro~양am can fruitfully lead to

SITUATION ANALYSIS

The first-or perhaps 없nong the first-steps in course design is  따1 analysis of the
setting, the audience, and needs  of the students, otherwise known as  a  situation
analysis (Richards, 2001).  Every effective course is undergirded by a consideration
of the following factors:

1.  Educational setting.  Within what societal and cultural norms is the course
situated? What is the institutional framework into which the course must be
integrated? What are the broad instructional go때S of the pro~양없n? In general
what is the structure of the pro~앙am? What are the physical conditions (e.g.,
classrooms) and resources (labs, computers, AV, materials)? Who are the
learners, in very general terms? Basic questions here look at the larger
educational context within which a course is placed.

2.  Class characteristics.  How would you describe the class in terms of the
homogeneity of learners, the size of the class, and its relationship to others
that learners are taking?

to identiη at least two types of needs: 。이ective and subjeαive.

ob;ective needs are those that can be relativεly easily measurεd,quantifiεd,or

SPECIFYING GOAIS

The terms goal and o비ective are often interchanged in pedagogical literature, and
depending on whom you  consult, you might find  some confusion in defining  the
two  terms.  For  the  sake  of clarity and  brevity  here, I will  offer a  distinction that
seems to conform to the majority of uses of the two terms.  It is really quite a simple
distinction.  Goals  are  rather broadly  basεd aims  and purposes in an educational
context,  and  are  therefore  more  appropriately  associated  with  whole  programs,
courses, or perhaps sizable modules within a course.  According to Brown (1995,p.
71),  goals  are  “general  statements  concerning  desirable  and  attainable  pro~양am
purposes and  aims.” Objectives are  much more specific  than goals, both in their
conception  and  in  their  context.  Objectives  usually  refer  to  aims  and  purposes
within  the  narrow  context  of a  lesson  or  an  activity, within  a  lesson.  They  are
“spec퍼C statements  that  describe  partic비ar knowl  ge, behaviors, and/or skills”
(Brown, 1995, p. 73).  Obviously there can besom  gray  따℃a in between the two
concepts, but they are essentially distinguished by the size and scope of the context
m  question.

Most curriculum εxperts agree that once a situation analysis and needs analysis
have  confirmed  some  of  the  general  parameters  of a  course,  goals  need  to  be
carefully stated in order to be certain about what the course W피 accomplish 따id
what it w피 not.

156

CHAPTER  9  Curriculum  Design

For  the  Central  American  program,  to  guide  our  thinking,  we
developed the following  speaking goals  for  the lowest  of the three
levels  we  planned  (we  also  had  goals  for  listening,  reading,  and
writing):

By the end of the course, students will be able to:

1.  Participate in social conversations in En행sh
2.  Speak with few hesitations and with only minor (local) εrrors
3.  Successfully apply some form-focused instruction to their speech
4.  Self monitor their speech for potential errors
5.  없rticipate comfortably in pair, group, and whole-class discussions
6.  Give a simple oral presentation on a familiar topic

These  served  as  guidelines  for  determining  course  content  and
eventually lesson objectives.

CONCEPTUALIZING A COURSE SYLLABUS

The  n앉t two  steps  in many  cases  will  be  undertaken  simultaneously  or at  least
interactively:  As  you  put  together  what  most  institutions  call  a  syllabus  (a
sequential list  of objectives, topics, situations, skills,  and forms  to  be  tau1합it), it  is
often  helpful  to  carη out  a  review  of options  in  materials  (textbooks  and other
resources) that are already available.  It would be unrealistic to draw up a prototype
of your course with no consultation of the potential materials that might  suppoπ
the course.  You could end up having to redesign a great deal of your course if you
find that the right materials don’t  exist!

So,  how  much  time  and  effort  should  you  spend  on  designing  a  syllabus
before consulting the options for existing materials? This is a difficult question to
answer  because  it  depends  on  a  number  of  factors,  among  which  are  time,
expertise,  money,  and  one’s  own  need  to  offer  the  perfect  course.  Sometimes
excellent courses are launched simply on the basis of an available  textbook, and
sometimes  those  curricula  are  unsuccessful.  But  it’s  also  rare  to  design  the
perfect  curriculum  the  first  time  around, and  so  perhaps  through  the  revision
process  (the  bottom  box  in  Figure  9.1)  a  course  C때 be  redesigned  quite
successfully 때'ter a mediocre first run.

A  communicative  syllabus  (function-focused  as  opposed  to  form-focused)

should minimally consist of:

1.  Goals for the course (and possibly goals for modules within the course).
2.  Su잃:ested objectives for units and possibly for lessons.
3.  A sequential list of functions (purposes), following from the goals, that the
curriculum W피 fulfill.  Such a list is typically org따lized into weeks or days.

157

4.  A sequential list of topics and situations matched to the functions in #3.
5.  A sequential list of grammatical, lexical, and/or phonological forms  to be

taught, again matched to thε sequence of functions.

6.  A sequεntial list  of skills (listening, speaking, rεacting, writing) that are also

matched to thε above  sequencεs.

7.  Matched ref1εrences throughout to tεxtbook units‘ l℃ssons, and/or pages, and

additional resources (audio, visual, workbooks, etc.) to be used.

8.  Possible suggestions of assessmεnt alternativ,℃s, including critεria to be tεsted

and genres of assεssment (traditional tests, journals, portfolios, etc.).

In  addition,  depending  on  how  detailed  thε  syllabus  ultimatεly  gets,  it  is  not
unusual  to  see  syllabuses  that  include  outlines  of  lεsson plans  (εspεcially if  a
tεachεr’s manual is  included with a textbook) and/or suggεsted activities.  In such
lεsson plans, one will  oftεn find  suggestions for  extra-class (“ hom℃work ”) activity
to assign to students.

In the case of thε Cεntral American  program, with only about εight

“cultural.”  We  learne낀 that the prospective students  might only be
at a low-intermediate lεvεl of English; and it looked likε thε initial 45
students  would  not  matεrializε! W℃  had  neither  time  nor  money
available  for  course  dεvelopmεnt, εven though  wε clearly  had  thε
εxpertise.  With  a  number  of  uncertaintiεs  εntering  into  the
planning of the program, we dεcided to coneεptualize a course that
was  a  hybrid  of  a  publishεd  textbook  S℃ riεs  and  our  own  ALI
curriculum,  with  little  furthεr  planning.  This  dεcision  was
expεdiεnt, and saved us from spεnding a lot of time in developing a

SELECTING TEXTBOOKS, MATERIALS, AND RESOURCES

As  noted  abov1ε,  the  procεss  of  reviewing  potential  textbooks,  materials,  and
rεsources, beyond  those  that  you  might  design  yourself, is  onε that  ideally  takes
placε in  concert  with  conceptualizing  the  syllabus.  Of grεat importance  in  this
procεss is  ascertaining  that  your  goals-the  outgrov.πh of a  situation  and  needs
analysis-are in central focus at all times.  It is tempting to allow εxisting textbooks
to drive your goals, but doing so can obviously lead you 싫tray.

There  are  many  differ'εnt  ways  of  approaching  the  procεSS  of  rεviewing
tεxtbooks and making a final decision.  Richards (2001, p. 258), citing Cunningsworth
(1995), SU잃εsts the following criteria as a set of guidelinεs:

158

CHAPTER  9  Curriculum  Des땅n

1.  They should correspond to learners' needs.  They should match the aims and

objectives of the language pro!양am.

2.  They should reflect the uses (present or future) that learners will make of the
language.  Textbooks should be chosen that will help equip students to use
language effectively for their own purposes.

3.  They should take account of students' needs as  learners and should facilitate
their learning processes, without dogmatically imposing a rigid “method.”
4.  They should have a clear role as a support for lean파ig.  Like teachers, they

should mediate between the target language 따id the learner.

].  Brown  (1995,  p.  161)  lists  five  major  categories  to  consider  in  choosing  a
textbook: (a) author's and publisher’s  reputation; φ) fit  to the curriculum (mee따lg
needs, go와s, etc.); (c) physical characteristics (layout, org없lization, etc.); (d) lo횡stic외
factors  (price,  au;없liaηr  aids,  workbooks);  and  (e)  teachability  (especially  the
usefulness of a teacher's edition).

A further  consideration in  reviewing and choosing materials  is  the extent to
which  a  curriculum R떼l involve  teacher-made  materials  (additional  activities  따id
exercises, handouts, charts, review sheets, etc.).  Such personalizing of a curriculum
is of course highly recommended in that such material can be specifically gauged
for the particul따· audience, and it is a motivating factor for teacher 따id students.

ASSESSMENT

Assessment of the students' attainment of objectives of lessons and units, and of the
goals of the curriculum, may be offered in a wide 따ray of possible form따s.  Traditional
periodic  tests such as  quizzes, multiple-choice  tests, fill-in-the-blank  tests, 따id other
somewhat mechanical test types offer the possib피.ty of a practical, quick level check
of students' attainmεnt.  Midterm  and final  examinations  mi양it include, along with
some of the above  te띠niques, short essays, oral production, and more open-ended
responses.  Alternatives  in  such  assessment  techniques  are  available  in  joum따s,
portfolios, conferences, obseπa디ons,inten에ews, and self- and peer-e때luation.  Details
on 외l these possib피ties 따℃ described in Chapters 23, 24, and 25 of this book.

PROGRAM EVALUATION

No  curriculum  should  be  considered  complete  without  some  form  of pro!양따n
i  evaluation-which you w피 see toward the bottom of the chart in Figure 9 .1.  Two
않 manifestations of progr때1 evaluation are usually implied in a  course: evaluation of

the  teacher  and  of  the  proi양없n itself.  Of  course,  외l  three  of  these  factors
낼 students, teache때d progran댔 빠e뺑때ent.  If stu微 are succe빼, the
cause could be either the teacher or the syllabus, and not just the performance of
students.  Likewise, the success of a program may be attributed to a great extent to

CHAPTER  9  Curriculum  Design

159

a teacher's talent 따id ab피ty to adapt a syllabus to an audience.  And then again, a
poorly  motivated  group  of students  and  an  inadequate  program  could  make  the
world’s best teacher look bad.

So,  an  effective  program  evaluation  will  consider  all  three  factors  in
dεtermining  appropriate  revisions.  The  most  salient  aspεct  of  most  program
evaluations is the students' evaluation of both teacher and pro~양am elements, often
in the form of a checklist or questionnaire.  But hidden benεath the results of such
questionnaires  are  several  possible  root  causes  of success  or failure.  Perhaps  the
tεacher is  so  excellent  or so  poor  that  the  program  rises  or falls  on  that  alone.
Perhaps  the  textbook  and  materials  equally  drove  the  success  or  failure  of the
course.  It is  also  possible that students can give  hi앙1 ratings to teachers who are
pedagogically  mediocre  but  who  are  “띠ce” to  their  students;  likewise,  a  stern,  \,_.
impassioned  teacher  might  get  some  low  student  ratings  but  in  the  long  run
students will have learned a great deal from such a teacher.

How should one effectively account for all  the possible variablεs contributing
to the success of a program? First, all the “ players” need to be consulted.  This would
include  students,  teachers,  course  developers,  administrators,  and  if  relevant,
sponsors.  Second, consider the audience of the evaluation.  If the  course is  to  be
offered subsequently, then perhaps the primary audience is the administrator(s) and
teachers.  If it’s money for further offerings that concerns you, the sponsors are your
target.  If you are  concerned about which courses the students will move on into,
then the students take central focus.

A  third  way  to  view  program  evaluation  is  to  consider  various  aspects  of the
program, any or 때l of which mi방1t have contributed to the success or failw℃ of a course:

•  appropriateness of the course goals (in meeting needs and purposes)
•  adequacy of the syllabus to meet those goals
•  textbooks and materials used to support the curriculum
•  classroom methodology, activities, procedures
•  the teacher's training, background, and expertise
•  appropriate orientation of teachers and students before the course
•  the students' motivation and attitudes
•  the students' perceptions of the course
•  the students' actual performance as  measured by assessments
•  means for monitoring students' pr'앵ress through assessment
•  institutional support, including resources, classrooms, and environment
•  staff collaboration and development before and during the course

It should be readily apparent that pr,앵ram evaluation extends well beyond thε
administration of a simple student evaluation form!  If program evaluation is treated
as a serious and comprehensive process, it will take time, effort, and focus to achiev,ε
its ends.

I can now relate the rest of the stoπ of the Central American program, all in the

light of the last few sections of this chaptεr.

CHAPTER  9  Curriculum  Design

As  we approached the day of the students' arrival, we made sure  때l
the  physic때 arrangements  were  set:  securing  housing,  confirming

TOEFL®, issuing contracts to teachers, reserving classroom space, and
ascertaining
immigration  regulations  were  being  met.
Difficulties with this last item almost forced us to cancel the program

that

About two weeks before the arrival date, we were informed that there
would  be  only  25  students  in  thε pro~양am.  πtis had  a  significant
impact on our plan to offer three sections, 따id the outcome of that
was to  offer only two  sections, with another budgetary adjustment.
The  day  after  the  students  arrived,  we  had  placement  testing  따id
discovered that they were clearly of lowεr proficiency than we had
initially  expected,  but  even  more  significant  was  their  almost
unanimous  desire  not  to  undertake  a  strictly  acadenlic  course.
Instead, their wishes indicated a  course in conversation and culture,
with only a  U양lt treatment of reading and writing  sk피s, and with a
liberal  number of hours  each day  to  enjoy  the  beautiful  city of San
Francisco!

What  were  we  to  do  with  our  pl'εpared syllabus? An “emergency”
meeting 없nong the teachers and coordinator resulted in an amazing
collaborative  effort  to  revise  the  curriculum  on  the  spot  and  an
agreement to meet together daily for the first two weeks and to share
results  of the  previous  day  and  to  plan  activities  for  the  next.  We
wer℃ able to salvage some of the textbook lessons but the textbook
proved to be m따횡nally useful.  Some of our own Ali curriculum was
used to fill in certain gaps;  Most of the four-week course was a daily
process  of creative design  based on informal  monitoring of student
needs and their pr1앵ress.  We further arranged to have some cultural
connections between class activity and afternoon and weekend tours.
πie TOEFL® prep part of the curriculum was reduced to two optional
workshops.

At  the  end  of  the  course,  we  found  through  various  program
evaluative measures that the students loved the course, the teachers,
없id the setting.  They felt that their needs were more than fulfilled, as
they showed excellent improvement in their English skills.  They left
San Francisco with cherished memories and experiences they nlight
never agam encounter.

This  stotγ could  happen  to  you,  but  with  a  little  bit  of  luck  없id  better
predictions of needs, it W피 not.  What should have happened in order to prevent
thε numerous pi야alls encountered along the way?  Here were our conclusions:

1.  Carry out a more accuratε needs analysis.
2.  Begin the planning process much earlier in the academic year in order to

better reach the desired numbers of paπicipants, to establish a more realistic
budget, and to plan the course accordingly.

3.  With more accurate pinpointing of neεds and purposes of the course, the

syllabus, tεxtbooks, and other resources would have been appropriate, and we
would have avoided the “emergency” patching together of a course.

This  SUπey of course  design  is  intended  to  give  you  a  picture  of what  lies

designed to meet specific objectives: the lesson.

Having takεn a broad, sweeping look at the macro units of pedagogy, the curriculum
or course, we now takε the next logical  step in  a  consideration of the  procεSS of
teaching languagεs by zooming in on the organization of a course.  Most coursεs are
presented in a numbεr of units of varying lengths (usually from one to threε wεεks
in a normal 15-week term), whose focus is defined by goals that ultimately contributε
to OV1εrall goals of the course.  Such units are customarily characterized by a  series
of lessons:  the  building  blocks  of a  unit  and  course,  time김εfined in-class  sets  of
activitiεs designεd to accomplish one or more veη specific objεctives.

The term lesson is popularly considered to be a unified set of procedures that
cov,εr a  pεriod of classroom  timε, usually  ranging from  45  to  120 minutes.  These
classroom  time  units  ar,ε  administratively  significant  for  teachers  becausε  thεy
represent steps in a curriculum before which and aftεr which you havε a hiatus (of
a  day  or more)  in which to  evaluate  and prepare for  the  next  lesson.  Sometimεs
your whole life  seems to be caught up in a never-ending series of lesson plans.  But
those lessons, from the point of view of your own and students' tinle management,
are practical, tangible  units of effort that serve to provide a  rhythm to a  course of
study.

How do you  go  about planning a  lesson? This  chapter should  give  you  some

guidεlines.

FORMAT OF A  LESSON PLAN

While  variations  abound, seasoned teachers  gεnerally agree  on what  thε essential
elements  of a  lesson  plan  should  be.  For examples  of each  element, turn  to  the
sample lesson plan that begins on page  171  of this chapter.

1. Goal

As  you discover,εd in the previous chapter, a  well-designεd course w피 speci다
a number of goals.  Each lesson in the course will in some way addrεss onε ormor,ε

164

CHAPTER  10  Lesson  Planning

165

of those “ big” overall goals.  A first  step in a lesson, then, would be to acknowledge
the way in which your lesson is designed to contribute to such goals.  This goal may
be generalized, but it provides a pedagogical context for you.  Thus, in the sample
lεsson plan,“comprehending social conversations” generally identifies the curricular
goal being addressed.

2. Objectives

It’s  veπ imp야tant to state explicitly what you want students to gain from the

lεsson.  Explicit statements here help you to

a.  be sure that you indeed know what it is you want to accomplish,
b.  preseπc the unity of your lesson,
c.  predetermine whether or not you are trying to accomplish too much, and
d.  evaluate students' success at the end of the lesson.

Objεctives are most clearly captured in terms of stating what students will do
that  is,  what  they  will peψrm.  Tη to  avoid  vague, unverifiable  statements  like
th esε;

•  Students will learn about the passive voice.
•  Students will practice some listening exercises.
• Students will do the reading selection.
•  Students will discuss the homework assignment.

You  would be unable to confirm the  realization  of any of these  sorts of abstruse,
loosely stated objectives.  On the other hand, because they are  stated in  terms of
expected student performance, the objectives in the  samplε lesson plan can more
easily be verifiεd.  You can turn back to thε objectives after a lesson and determine,
to some extent a따way, how well students accomplished thεm.

Note, however, that  some  language  objectives  are  not  overtly  observable, and
therefore you may need to depart from strictly behavioral terms for such objectives.
Notably, comprehension objectives (listening, reading) are tricky because you cannot
actually observe either.  You're forced  to rely on performance that demonstrates or
confirms correct comprehension; in other words, you have to h따r the acquisition of
those objectives.

In stating objectives, you should be able to identi다 an overall p따pose that you
will attempt to accomplish by the end of the class period.  But there may be other
supportive  objectives  that  need  to  be  stated  as  well,  leading  us  to  distinguish
between two l따ds of objectives: Terminal objectives are final learning outcomes
that you will  be responsible for assessing.  Enabling objectives are interim steps
within  a  lesson  that  build  upon  each  other  and  ultimately  lead  to  a  terminal
objective.  Consider the following examples:

166

[ HAPTER 70  Lesson  Planning

Terminal lesson  o찌eαive:

•  Students  will  successfully  requ℃st  information  about  airplanε arrivals  and

dεpartures.

Enabling objectives:

items.  [vocabulary is  listεd]

•  Students will read and understand an airlinε schεdule.
•Studεnts will produce questions with  wheη, ν·here, and zν•hat time.
•Studεnts will produce appropriate polite forms of rεquesting.

You may bε able to identiη a numbεr of other enabling objεctives that will vaη
dεpending upon  what  students'  proficiεncy l℃Vεl is  and  what  they  havε already
learned in the course.  For another εxample, notice the difference between tεrminal
and enabling objectives in the sample lεsson plan.

3. Materials and equipment

It may sεεm a trivial mattεr to list materials needed, but good planning includes
knowing what you neεd to take with you or to arrange to hav1℃ in your classroom.
It is  easy, in the  usually  harried life  of a  teacher, to forget  to  bring  to class  a  tape
recordεr, a poster, somε handouts you left on your desk at home, or the workbooks
that students gave you thε night bεfore.

4. Procedures

At  this point, lessons clearly have tremendous variation.  But, as a  veπ gεneral
sεt of guidelinεs for  planning, you  might think in  terms of making  surε your plan
includes

a.  an opening statement or activity as a warm-up
b.  a set of activities and techniques in which you have considered appropriate
proportions of timε for whole-class work, small-group and pair work, t℃a ch er
talk, student talk,

c.  closure

5. Assessment

Next,  how  can  you  determine  whethεr  your  objectives  have  been
accomplished?  If your  lεsson has  no  assessment  component, you  can  easily  find
yourself simply making assumptions that are not informed by careful observation or
measurement.  Now, you must understand that every lesson does not need to end
with a little quiz or a formal test, nor does evaluation need to be a separate elεmεnt

CHAPTER  10

Less。n Planning

167

of  your  lesson.  Informal  assεssment  can  take  placε in  thε course  of “ rεgular”
classroom activity.  Some forms  of assessment may havε to wait a  day or two until
certain  abilities  have  had  a  chancε  to  build.  Whatever  manifestation  your
assessment  takεs,  make  surε,  after  students  havε  suffici℃nt  opportunitiεs  for
learning, that you havε appropriat℃ly considered how you will (a) assεss the success
of your students and possibly (b) make appropriate adjustments in your lesson plan
for  thε nεxt day.

6.  Extra-class work

What wε commonly call “ homεwork” (but students  don’t  necessarily do such
work only at  home), I likε to call extra-class work.  If it  is  warrant℃d, εxtra-class
work  nεeds to  b℃ plannεd car,εfully  and  communicated  clearly  to  thε studεnts.
Whεther you  ar,ε  tεaching in  an  EFL  or ESL  situation, you  can almost  always  find
applications  or  εxtεnsions of classroom  activity  that  will  help  studεnts do  somε
learning beyond thε class  hour.

GUIDELINES FOR LESSON PIANNING

1.  How to be횡n planning

In  most  normal  circumstances,  espεcially  for  a  teacher  without  much
εxperiencε, the first  stζp of lεsson planning will already have beεn pεrformεd for
you:  choosing  what  to  teach.  No  doubt  you  will  be-or have  already  bεεn­
givεn  a  tεxtbook  and  told  to  teach  from  it,  with  εither  a  suggestion  or  a
rεquiremεnt of how many chapters or units you should cover.  As  you look ovεr
thε cha ptεr you are to  covεr for a class hour, you might go through thε following

sεquεnce:

a.  Assuming that you ar,ε alrεady familiar with (i) the curriculum your students

are following (see “Adapting to an  Established Curriculum" on pagε 170 of this
section) and (ii) thε overall plan and “tone” of the textbook(s), look over thε
tεxtbook chapter.

b.  Based on (i) your view of the whole curriculum and (ii) your perception of

the languag'ε neεds of your students, determine what the topic and purposε of
the lesson will bε and write that down as the overall goal.

c.  Again considεring the curriculum and the students' nεεds, draft out pεrhaps

one to three explicitly stated terminal objectives for the lesson.

d.  For each exercise in the textbook, decide if you will use it as is, adapt it, or

skip it entirely-all basεd on the objectives you have drafted.
ε.  Draft out a skeletal outline of what your lesson will look like.
f.  Carεfully plan step-by-step procedures for carηing out all  techniquεs,

εspecially those that involve changεs and additions.  Statε thε purpose(s) of
each techniquε and/or activity as enabling objectives.

CHAPTER  10  Lesson  Planning

For t℃achers who  have  nev,εr taught  before, it  is  often very useful to  writε a
script of your lesson plan in which your εxact anticipated words are writtεn down
and followεd by exactly what you would expεct studεnts to say in return.  Scripting
out  a  lesson  plan  helps  you  to  be  morε spεcific in  your  planning  and  can  often
prevent classroom pitfalls where you gεt all  tangled up in εxplaining something or
students take you off on a  tangent.  Writing a  complεtε script for a whole hour of
tεaching  is  probably  too  laborious  and  unreasonable,  but  more  practical  and
instructive (for you) arε partial scripts that cover

a.  introductions to  activitiεs
b.  directions for a task
c.  statements of rules or gεneralizations
d.  anticipated interchang,εs that could εasily bog down or go astray
e.  oral testing techniques
f.  conclusions to activities and to the class  hour

2.  Variety, sequencing, pacing, and timing

As you are drafting step-by-stεp procedures, you need to look at how thε lesson

holds togεther as a whole.  Four considerations come into play hεre:

a.  Is  ther,ε sufficient νariety in techniques to keep the lesson lively and

intεresting? Most successful lessons give students a number of differ,εnt
activitiεs during the class hour, keeping minds alert and enthusiasm hi앙1.
b.  Are your techniques or activitiεs sequeηced logically?  Idε따lχ elements of a

lesson will build progressiv,εly toward accomplishing thε ultimatε goals.  Easier
aspεcts will usually bε placεd at the beginning of a lesson; tasks that require
knowledge gainεd from pr,εvious εxercises will be sεquenced appropriately.

c.  Is  the lesson as a whole paced adequately? Pacing can mean a number of

things.  First, it means that activities arε nεithεr too long nor too short.  You
could, for example, have so many short activities that just as students ar,ε
gεtting the “feel” for one activity, they g℃t bouncεd to the next.  Second, you
need to anticipate how well your various techniques “flow” together.  You
would not, for example, find a smooth flow in a class that had five  minutes
each of whole-class work, pair work, whole-class work, group work, pair work,
whole-class work, etc.  Nor would you normally plan two silent rεa ding
activities in a row.  Third, good pacing also is a factor of how well you provide
a transition from one activity to the next.  An example:

T:  Okay, you’ve just had a good chance to listen to  thε way a lecturer
signals various segments of a class lecture.  Now we’re going to use
this information to look at a reading passage about space exploration
and figure out .  .

d.  Is  the lesson appropriately timed,  considering the number of minutes in the
class hour? This is one of the most difficult aspects of lεsson planning to

r

CHAPTER  10  Lesson  Planning

169

control.  It’s not unusual for new teachεrs to plan a lεsson so tightly that they
actually complete their lesson plan early, but after just a little experience it is
more common that we don’t  complete our lessons within the planned time
allotment.  The latter is  not a cardinal sin, for most likely it means you have
given some tim℃ to students for gεnuine interaction and creative use of
language.  But timing is  an element that you should build into a lesson plan:
(i) If your planned lesson ends early, have some backup activity ready to
insεπ; (ii)  if your lesson isn’t comp let℃d as  planned, bε ready to gracεfully end
a class on time and, on the next day, pick up where you left off.

3. Gauging difficulty

Figuring  out  in  advance  how  easy  or  difficult  certain  techniques  will  be  is
usually  learned  by experience.  It takes  a  good  deal  of cognitive  empathy to  put
yourself in your studεnts’ shoes and anticipate their problem areas.  Some difficulty
is  caused  by  tasks  themselves;  therefore,  make  your  directions  crystal  clear  by
writing them out in advance (note the comments on “ scripting” lessons, above).  I
have  seen too many classεs where tεachers have not clearly planned exactly what
task directions  thεy will give.  、~riting them ahead of time allows you to be more
objective  in  determining  if everything  is  clear.  And  then, εither give  an 앉ample
yoursεg or solicit an example of a subtask within a technique.

Another source of difficulty, of course, is linguistic.  If you can follow the i  + 1
principle  of providing  material  that  is  just  a  little  above,  but  not  too  far  above,
students' ability, the linguistic difficulty should be optimal.  The main problem here
liεs in the heterogeneity of a classroom full  of learnεrs whose proficiency rang'ε is
very  broad.  Individual  attention, feedback,  and  small-group  work  can  sometimes
bring balance into the classroom.

4.  Individual differences

For the most part, a lesson plan will aim at the majority of studεnts in class who
compose  the “average” ability  range.  But  your  lesson  plan  should  also  take  into
account  the  variation  of ability  in  your  students,  especially  those  who  are  well
below or well above the classroom norm.  You can take sεveral steps to account for
individual differ,εnces:

a.  Design techniques that have easy and difficult aspects or items.
b.  Solicit responses to easier items from students who are below the norm and

to harder items from those above the norm.

c.  Tπ to design techniques that will involve all students actively.
d.  Use judicious selection to assign members of small groups so that each group
has either (” a heterogeneous range of ability or (갑) a homogeneous range (to
encourage equal participation).

e.  Use small-group and pair work time to circulate and give extra attention to
those below or above the norm (seε Chapter 14 on group work principles).

170

CHAPTER  10  Lesson  Planning

5. Student talk and teacher talk

Give careful consideration in your lesson plan to the balance between student
talk 따id teacher talk.  Our natural inclination as teachers is to talk too much! As you
plan  yo따 lesson, and  as  you  perhaps script out  some  aspects  of it, see  to it  that
students have a chance to talk, to produce language, and εven to initiate their own
topics and ideas.

6. Adapting to an established curriculum

In the previous chapter, you were introduced to the steps involved in designing
a curriculum.  Thε assumption then, and in this chapter, is  that your print따y task is
ηot to write a new curriculum or to revise an existing one, but to follow an established
curriculum 때d adapt to it in terms of your particular group of students, their neεds,
and their go외s, as well as your own philosophy of teaching.

As you plan lessons, your first concern is that εach class hour must contribute
to the goals that a curriculum is  designed to pursue.  But perhaps your institution
has  no  curriculum  spelled  out  in  a  documεnt; in  other words,  it  is  a “ textbook
driven"  curriculum  that,  in  practice,  simply  tells  you  to  tεach  everything  in  a
textbook.  Or you  may find  certain specifications for  the course you  are  about to
teach  somewhere  in  the  description  of  the  institution.  At  best,  you  would  be
presented with a document that clearly delineates the goals of thε curriculum 따id
offers  S앵gestions on how to  meet  those  goals  in  terms  of weekly  or even  daily
lesson objectives.

If you don’t  have such ov1εrail course go때s, it might be feasible to devise some
for yourself so that you can keep your course focused on attainable, practical ends.
To  do  so, consider the factors  outlined in the previous chapter that contribute to
curriculum planning:

• situation an때ysis (especially learner characteristics)
• needs analy잉S (especially learner factors and institutional factors)
• supporting materials and resources
• assessment requirements

By paying attention to the learner factors above, you will have  a good chance
of poin다ng your students  toward pragmatic, communicative goals  in which their
real-life  needs  for  English  will  be  met.  You  will  focus  on  the  learners  and  their
needs but temper those with the realities of your institution’s needs.  The latter will
add  some  administrative  practicality  to  your  goals.  After  따l,  every  educational
institution is limited in some way in its capacity to deliver the veη “ best.”

Your  course  go따s might  look  like  these  go삶s of an  advanced  pre-university

listening comprehension course:

a.  Students will understand the teacher’s instructions and demonstrate that

understanding.

b.  Students will understand the teacher's explanations and show that

comprehension.

j.  Students will  idεntiη kεy information as  signaled by grammatical structure.

7. Classroom lesson notes

A final  consideration  in  your  lεsson planning  procεss is  a  very  practical  one:

hav1ε pagεs and pagεs of notes and rεmindεrs and scripts, you w피 nev1εr freε yoursεlf
for  spontaneity.  Most  εxperiεncεd teachers  opεratε wε11 with about oηe page of a
lεsson outline and notεs.  Somε prεfεr to put lεsson notεs on a series of indεx cards
for easy handling.  By reducing your plans to such a physically manageablε minimum,
you will  reduc℃ thε chances of gεtting bogg,εd down in all the details that went into
the planning phase, y℃I you will have enough in writing to provide order and clarity
as you proceed.

What follows here is a lεsson plan* designed for an intεrmεdiatε lεV℃l pre-univεrsity

thε classroom, and reviewing and choosing from  a  wide variety  of materials.  The

to the comp。nents of a lesson: the techniques and activities that comprise a lesson,

TECHNIQUES REDEFINED

It is appropriate, before continuing, to m따@ SUf1ε that certain terms are well defined.
In Chaptεr 2, the term technique was  introducεd and dεfined, but it was  notεd in
passing that some oth℃r commonly used terms are considered by some to be virtually
synonymous.  These othεr terms include task, activity, procedure, practice, behavior,
exercise,  and even strate밍l  Since the  usε of m띠tiple terms  may  be confusing, you
W퍼 no doubt find it helpful to do some clariηing.  Bεar in mind, however, that experts
in the field  may have  sli형itly diffiεring points of view about the worlψig definitions
here.

179

180

CHAPTER  7 7  Techniques and Materials

1. Task

We  rεturn  to  the  tεrm task  again.  You  may  rεcall  from  Chaptεr 3,  in  a
discussion of task-based instruction, that task usually rεfεrs to aspεcializεd form of
techniquε or sεries of tεchniques closely allied with communicative curricula, and
as  such  must  minimally  have  communicative goals.  The  common thread  running
through half a dozεn dεfinitions of task is its focus on the authentic l1~tε of language
for mεaningful communicative p따poses beyond thε language classroom.

2. Activity

A vεry popular term in the litεranπe,an aαivity may refer to virtually anything
that  learnεrs dο in  thε classroom.  Mmε specific따ly, when wε rεfεr to  a  classroom
acti찌ty,w'ε usually refer to a reasonably unifrεd set of student bεhaviors, limited in timε,
pr,εceded by some direction  from  mε tεacher, with a  particular objective.  Activitiεs
include role plays, drills, g따nεs,pεer-editing, small-group information-gap exercises, and
much more.  Bεcause an activity impliεs some sort of activ'℃ performancε on thε part
oflεam,εrs, it is gεnerally not used to rεfer to cεrtain teacher bεha vi ors 파ce saying “good
morning,”  maintaining  eye  contact  with  students,  explaining  a  grammar  point,  or
writing a list of words on the challcboard.  Such teacher b℃haviors, howev1εr,canindεεd
bε rεfεπεd to 싫 techniques (see #5 below).

3. Procedure

Richards  and  Rodg,εrs (2001)  used  the  term  procedure  to  encompass “the
actual  moment-t〔>-momεnt techniques,  practices,  and  bεhaviors  that  operate  in
teaching a language according to a particular method” (p. 26).  Procedures, from this
definition,  include  tεchniques, but  thε  authors  appear  to  have  no  compelling
objection to viεwing the terms synonymously.  Thus, for Richards and Rodgers, this
appεars to bε a catchall tεrm.

4. Practice, behavior, exercise, strate잃7 •  - -

In  the  languag℃-teaching literature, thesε terms, and perhaps  some  othεrs, all
appear  to  refiεr,  in  varying  d℃gre℃S  of  intensity,  to  what  is  defined  bεlow as
technique.

5. Technique

Even  before Anthony  (1963)  discussεd and  definεd the  term,  the  languagε­
teaching literature generally accepted technique as  a  sup℃ror1따nate term to ref.εr
to various  activities  that  either teachers  or learners  pεrform in  the  classroom.  In
other words,  techniques  include  all  tasks  and  activities.  They  are  almost  always
planned and dεlibεrate.  They arε the product of a choice made by the teacher.  And
they  can,  for  your  purposes  as  a  languagε  teacher,  comfortably  refer  to  thε
pεdagogical units or components of a classroom sεssion.  You can think of a lesson
as  consisting  of a  number  of techniques,  somε tεacher-cεntered, some  learner
centered,  somε  production-orientεd,  somε  comprehεnsion-oriented,  some
clustering together to form a task, and some as a task in and of themsεlves.  We  now
turn to examinε thεse classroom components of focus or activity.

CATEGORIZING TECHNIQUES: A BIT OF ffiSTORY

CHAPTER  11  Techniques  and Materials

181

At  last count thεrε wer,℃ 28,732 t℃chniqu℃S for tεaching languagε in the classroom.
Okay, I’m  joking.  But a cursory glance at a fεw dozen tεxtbooks and other teacher
activitiεs books  rεvεals many, many possible  techniquεs! TESOL’S  New Ways  sεries
of teachεr referεncε books  is  an  excellent  examplε of a  plεthora of techniquεs
categoriz℃d into  such  arεas as  teaching  speaking,  listening,  reading,  and  writing.
Bailey (2005), for  εxamplε, suggests at least  100 techniquεs for  teaching speaking.
Llnsε  (2005),  Nunan  (2005),  and  Helgesen  and  Brown  (2007)  togεther  offer
hundreds  of  diffiεrent  techniques  for  teaching  young  childrεn  grammar  and
listening,  respεctiv'℃ly.  Other  teacher  refi℃rεnee books  from  virtually  ev1επ major
publisher collεctiv'εly describe thousands of Iεchniquεs for different ages, skills, and
proficiency levels (Clairε, 1988; Klippε1, 1984; Shoemakεr & Shoemakεr, 1991).

How can you best conceptu때ize this multitude of techniquεs? Over the years,

sεveral rubrics have beεn usεd to classify tεchniques.

1. From manipulation to communication

Techniques  can  bε thought  of as  existing  along  a  continuum  of possibilities
bεtweεn highly manipulative and veπ communicative.  At  the  extremε εnd of the
manipulative sidε, a  tσhnique is  totally controllεd by thε teachεr and r℃quires a
predicted  response  from  the  studεnt(s).  Choral  repetition  and  cued  substitution
drills are examples of oral techniques at this extreme.  0thεr examples are dictation
(listening/writing) and rεading aloud.

At  thε communicative  εxtremε, student  rεsponsεs  ar,℃  completely  open
εnded and  therefore  unpredictable.  Examples  includε storytεHing, brainstorming,
role  plays, certain games, etc.  Teachers  are  usually  put into  a  less  controlled  rol℃
here, as  students become freε to bε crεative with their rεsponses and interactions
with other  studεnts.  However, keεp in  mind  that  a  modicum  of tεacher control,
whether ovεrt or covert, should always be pr,εsent in thε classroom.

It is important to remember that the manipulation-communication scalε do℃S
not correspond to the beginning-through-advanced proficiency continuum! For too
many  years  the  languagε-teaching  profession  labored  under  the  incorrect
assumption  that  bεginnεrs  must  have  isolated,  mechanical  bits  and  piεces  of
languagε pro!양없nmed into them (typically through a mεmorized audiolingual drill)
and that only later could “rεal ” communication take place.  The whole CLT approach
accentuates a  diametric외ly opposed philosophy: that genuine communication can
take placε from the veπ first day of a language class.

Thε εxtεnt to  which  a  communicative  technique  can  sustain  itself  for  long
pεriods of time in the classroom will often bε a factor of the ov1εrail proficiency level
of your class.  But  even  at  the  beginning  level, students  can  engage  in  meaningful
communication  for  significant  stretches  of  time.  Communicative  techniques  for
bεginners  involve  appropriately  small  chunks  of  language  and  build  in  some
repetition of patterns for establishing fluεncy.  Ononε of the veπ first days of class,
for examplε, students can be taught to ask and respond to questions such as:

2. Mechanical, meaningful, and communicative drills

In  the  dεcades  of  the  1940s  through  the  1960s,  language  pedagogy  was
obsessed with the drill.  Great proportions of class  timε were often spεnt drilling:
repeating,  rεpeating,  repeating.  Today,  tha따fully,  wε have  developed  teaching
practices that make only minimal-or optimal-use of such drilling.

A  drill  may  be  defined  as  a  tεchnique that  focuses  on  a  minimal  numbεr
(usually  one  or two)  of language  forms  않rammatical or phonological  structures)
through  somε type  of rεpεtition.  Drills  are  commonly  donε chorally  (the  whole
class  rep℃ating in  unison)  or individually.  And  they  can  take  the  form  of simple
repεtition dr피s, substitution drills, and even the rather horrifying aberration known
as  thε moving slot substitution drill.  In  a  substitution dr피, the teacher provides  a
sentence, students rεpeat; teacher cuεs students to change one word or structure in
the sentence, students repeat.  For εxample:

T:  I went to the store yestεrday.  Ss:  I wεnt to the store yesterday.
Ss:  I wεnt to the bank yesterday.
T:  Bank.
Ss:  I went to the hospital yesterday.
T:  Hospital.

In a moving slot substitution drill, the slot moves, as in the following example:

T:  I went to the store yester1[ lay. Ss:  I went to the store yesterday.
Ss:  I wεnt to the bank yεstεrday.
T:  Bank.
Ss:  He went to the bank yesterday.
T:  He.
Ss:  Hε went to the bank in the
T:  In the morning.

T:  V이l go.

Ss:  He will go to the bank in the

morrung.

morrung.

By  this  time,  if studεnts haven’t  thrown  up  their  arms  in  the  frustration  of
having  to  retain  each  pr'εvious sεntencε alteration, they  may  have  accomplished
only the feat of overworking their short-term memories.  They certainly have gained
no communicative ability.

In referring to structural pattern drills, Paulston and Bruder (1976) used three
categories: mechanical, meaningful, and  communicative.  Mechanical drills  havε

only  one  correct  response  from  a  student, and  have  no  implied  connection with

And thε procεss may continuε on as thε teacher reinforces certain grammatical

communicativ,ε classrooms.

Now,  while  Paulston  and  Bruder  refiεrred to “communicativ,ε” drills  mor1ε than

possib피ty of an  open  response  and  negotiation  of mεaning, then  it  is  not  a  dr퍼.

Juan:  Oh ... εh ... I played soccer.

Techniques  and Materials

A final  word about drills:A communicative approach to language tεaching can
makε some use of dr피ing techniques, but only in moderation.  A fεw short, snappy
drills here and there, εspecially at the lower lεvεls of proficiency, can be quite useful
in  helping  students  to  establish  structural  patterns,  rhythm,  and  certain
pronunciation  elεments.  But  moderation  is  thε key,  especially  if your  drills  are
mechanical.  Thεre ’s  nothing  deadliεr than  a  class  hour  filled  with  audiolingual
parroting.

3. Controlled to free techniques

Perhaps  th℃ most useful  classification of techniques for  a  teacher to  use  as  a
continuum not unlike  the first  onε dεscribed on pages  181-182-a continuum of
possib피ties between highly  m없lipulative and  veπ communicativ,ε-but one  that
also specifically considers the εxtent to which  the tεacher maintains control over
thε learning activity.  Accordingly, it  is  important  to  understand what is  mεant by
coηtrot.  In the lists below are a few gεn℃ralizations.

Controlled

Free

Teacher-centered
Manipulativ,℃
Structured
Predicted student responses
Prepl따med objεctives
Set curriculum

Student-cεntered

Communicativ'ε

Clεarly, the real picture is not as  black-and-whitε as  these generalizations sεem
to  be.  For  ε:xample, many  controlled  techniques  are  manipulative,  as  dεscrib℃d
above.  But  controlled  techniqu℃S sometimes  have  communicative  elemεnts.  Th℃
form-focusεd communicative “ drill” just described, for εxample, is highly controlled
in  that  the  teacher  provides  set  quεstions and  εach studεnt has  a  short  time  in
which  to respond.  But  thεre is  an opportunity for  students to venture  out of the
mold if they wish; that’s  communicative.  So,  if you are  tempted to  draw a  clearly
defined line between controlled and foεε, rεsist that temptation.

A TAXONOMY OF TECHNIQUES

A comprehensive taxonomy of common techniques for language teaching, adapted
from Crookes and Chaudron (1991), is found in Table 11.1.  Notice that three broad
categories  are  used:  controlled,  semicontrolled,  and  foεc  Bearing  in  mind  the
somewhat slippery concept of control referred to above, you may be able to gain a
broad  picture  from  this  taxonomy  of  a  range  of  classroom  language-teaching
tεchniques.  In the chapters that follow, many of thesε techniques will be discussed
with ex없nples and analysis.

Table  11.1. Taxonomy of language-teaching techniques  (adapted from  Crookes &  Chaudron,
1991,  pp.  52-54)

[ ONTROLLED TE[ HNIQUES

1  Warm-up:  Mimeκ dance,  songs,  jokes,  play. This  activity gets  the  students stimulated,

relaxed,  motivated,  attentive,  or otherwise engaged  and  ready  fo「 the  lesson.  It does  not
necessarily  inv。 Ive use  of the target  language.

2.  Setting:  Focusing  in  on  lesson  topic. Teacher  directs attention  to the  topic by verbal  or
nonverbal  evocation  of the  context relevant to the  lesson  by questioning or miming or
pictu 「e presentation,  possibly  by  tape  recording of situations  and  people.

3.  Organizational: Structuring of lesson  or class  activities  includes  disciplinary action,

organization  of class  furniture and  seating,  general  procedures for class  interaction  and
performance,  structure  and  purpose of lesson,  etc.

4.  Content explanation: Grammatical,  phonological,  lexical  (vocabulary),  soci이 inguistic,

pragmatic,  or any other aspects  of language.

5.  Role-play  demonstration: Selected  students  or teacher  illustrate the procedure(s)  to  be
applied  in  the  lesson  segment to follow.  Includes brief illustration of language or other
content to  be  incorporated.

6.  Dialogue/Narrative presentation:  Reading or listening passage  presented  for passive

reception.  No implication of student production  or other  identification of specific target
forms or functions  (students  may  be asked  to “ understand”).

7.  Dialogue/Narrative recitation:  Reciting a previously  known  or prepared text,  either  in

unison or individually.
Reading aloud:  Reading directly from  a given  text.

8.
9‘  Checking: Teacher  either circulating or guiding the correction  of students'  work, providing

feedback  as  an  activity rather than  within another activity.

10.  Question-answer, display: Activity  involving prompting of student responses  by  means  of
display questions (i.e.,  teacher or questioner already knows the  response  or has  a very

limited set  of expectations for the  appropriate response).  Distinguished  fr。m referent‘al

questions  by the  likelihood of the questioner's  knowledge of the response  and  the
speaker's  awareness  of that fact.

11.  Drill: Typical  language a[ tivity involving fixed  patterns  of teacher  prompting and  student
responding,  usually with  repetition,  substitution,  and  other me[ hanical alterations.
Typically with  little meaning attached.

12.  Translation: Student or teacher  provision of L 1 or L2  translations of given text.
13.  Di[ tation: Student writing down orally presented text.
14.  [ opying: Student writing down text presented  visually.
15.  Identification: Student picking out and producing/labeling or otherwise identifying a

specific target form,  function,  definition, 0「 other  lesson-related  item.

16.  Recognition: Student identifying forms,  as  in  Identification (i.e.,  checking off items,

drawing symbols,  rearranging pictures),  but without a verbal  response.

17.  Review: Teacher-led  review of previous week/month/or other period as  a formal  summary

18.
19.

and  type of test of student recall  performance.
Testing:  Formal  testing  procedures to evaluate student progress.
Meaningful drill: Drill  activity involving responses  with  meaningf비 choices,  as  in
reference  to different information.  Distinguished from  Information exchange by the
regulated  sequence and  general  form  of responses.

(Continued)

185

SEMICONTROLLED TECHNIQUES

20.  Brainstorming: A form  of preparation for the lesson,  like Setting,  which  involves free,

undirected  contributions by  the students and teacher on a given  topic,  to generate multiple
associations without linking them;  no explicit analysis or interpretation  by  the teacher.
21.  Storytelling (especially when student-generated):  Not  necessarily  lesson-based,  a  lengthy
presentation  of story by teacher or student (may overlap with  Warm-up or Narrative
recitation).  May  be used  to  maintain  attention,  motivate,  or as  lengthy  practice.
22.  Question-answer,  referential: Activity  involving prompting of responses by  means of
referential  questions (i.e.,  the questioner does not know beforehand the response
information).  Distinguished from  Question-answer, display.

23.  Cued narrative/Dialogue: Student production  of narrative or dialogue following  cues from

miming,  cue cards,  pictures,  or other stimuli  related  to  narrative/dialogue (e.g.,
metalanguage requesting functional  acts).

24.  Information transfer: Application from  one mode (e.g.,  visual) to another (e.g.,  writing),

which  involves some transformation of the information  (e.g.,  student fills  out diagram  while
listening to description).  Distinguished from  Identification  in  that the student is  expected
to transform  and  reinterpret the  language or information.

25.  Information exchange: Task  involving two-way  communication as  in  information-gap

exercises, when one or both  parties (or a  larger group)  must share  information to achieve
some goal.  Distinguished from  Question-answer,  referential  in  that sharing of information
is  critical for  the task.

26.  Wrap-up:  Brief teacher- or student-produced summary of point and/or items that have been

practiced or learned.

27.  Narration/Exposition:  Presentation of a story or explanation derived from  prior stimuli.

Distinguished from  Cued narrative because of lack of immediate stimulus.

28.  Preparation: Student studι silent reading;  pair planning and  rehearsing,  preparing for  later

activity.  Usually a student-directed or -oriented project.

FREE  TECHNIQUES

29.  Role play:  Relatively free acting out of specified  roles and functions.  Distinguished from
Cued dialogues by the fact that cueing is  provided only minimally at the beginning,  and
not during the activity.

30.  Games: Various  kinds of language game activity  not  like other previously defined activities

(e.g.,  board and dice games  making words).

31 .  Report:  Report of student-prepared exposition on  books, experiences,  project work,

without immediate stimulus,  and elaborated on according to student interests. Akin  to
Composition in  writing  mode.

32.  Problem solving: Activity  involving specified  problem and  limitations of means to resolve

it;  requires cooperation on paπ of participants in  small  or large group.

33.  Drama:  Planned dramatic  rendition of play,  skit,  story,  etc.
34.  Simulation: Activity  involving complex interaction  between groups and  individuals based

on simulation of real-life actions and experiences.

35.  Interview: A student is  directed to get information from  another student or students.
36.  Discussion:  Debate or other form  of grouped discussion of specified topic,  with or without

specified sides/positions prearranged.

37.  Composition: As  in  Report (verbal),  written development of ideas, story,  or other exposition.
38.  A propos: Conversation or other socially oriented interaction/speech  by teacher,  students,

or even visitors,  on general  real-life topics. Typically authentic and genuine.

name ..  ?”“Are  you ... ?”

Step 3:Whεn evεryone has found his/her partnεc he/shε introduces

him/her to the group.

This  exercisε seems  to  fit  into  a  number  of possible  categories:  It  involves
question-answer,  referential activity; th•εre is  some information exchange as well;
and in some ways either problem  solving or games may fit  here.  The purpose in

tεchniquε spcε짧cally.  Rathεr, t섰c taxorn] my ism。rε of a help to you as

•  an aid to raising your awareness of thε wid℃ variety of available techniques
•  an  indicator  of how  tεchniquεs differ  according  to  a  continuum  ranging

from controlled to free

•  a  resource  for  your  own  personal  brainstorming  process  as  you  consider

types of techniquζS for your classroom

TEXTBOOKS

Techniques  consist  of  the  things  you  “do”  in  the  classroom,  but  only  a  few
techniques  do  not  in  some  manner  involv1℃ the  use  of materials  to  support  and
enhance  them.  What  would  language  classεs bε without  books, pictures, charts,
realia, and  technological  aids  (audiotapes, video, computers)? Yes, you  could  have
conv,εrsations,  role  plays,  discussions,  and  chalkboard  work,  but  much  of  the
richnεss of language  instruction is  derived from supporting materials.  Today such
materials abound for all levels and purposes.

What  kinds  of materials  are  available  to  you?  How  do  you  decide  what will
work and what won’t?  Is  it worthwhile  to  creatε your own matεrials? If so, what
sorts of things can be rel따ively easily made? We’11  look at these and related questions
herε as we considεr th esε all-important supporting elements in a lesson.

188

CHAPTER  11  Techniques  and Materials

The  most  obvious  and  most  common form  of material  support for  language
instruction comes through textbooks.  Most likely, as  a rεlatively new teachεr, your
first concern w피 not be to choosε a textbook, but rather to find cr1εativε use for the
textbook that has beεn handed to you by your supeπisor.  So, even though you may
have  idealistic  thoughts  about  other (and  better)  textbooks, your  challeng,ε is  to
make the veπ best use of the textbook that you have.  Sometimes new tεachers, in
thεir zeal  for  creating  wonderful,  marvεlous written  materials  for  their  students,
nεglect thε standard textbook prescribεd by the school curriculum and fail  to  seε
that this resourcε may actually be quite useful.  And you will no doubt find  that, as
a  n℃W tεacher, you  already  hav1ε enough  on your  hands  just  preparing  a  lesson,
carrying it out, monitoring its unfolding, and managing the dynamics of a classroom
full  of students.  You  don’t  need to add more stress to your life  by trying to create
brand-new materials.

about these two terms and then have a show of hands on those who feel they

Textbook Evaluation

The  abov'ε questions are issues  of textbook adaptation that you face  almost every
time  you  sit  down  to  plan  a  lesson.  You  see  to  it  that  the  way you  present  the
textbook  lesson  is  appropriately  geared  for  your  partic띠ar students-their level,
ability, and goals-and is just right for the number of minutes in your class.

If your teaching situation allows you to choose a textbook, you have an exciting
but complex task ahead of you.  In fact, the  number of questions that need to  be
asked  about  a  textbook  can  be  overwhelming.  (For  the  most  comprehensive
textbook evaluation checklist I have ever seen, see Skierso, 1991; this form occupies
more  than  eight  printed  pages!)  But  once  you  have  carried  out  a  thorough
investigation of textbooks using some kind of consistent evaluation procedure, you
will be rewarded by having chosen a textbook that best fits  all of your criteria.

Table  11.2  on  pagε 192  provides  an  abridged  evaluation  form  that  can be  a
practical  set  of  criteria  for  either  (a)  choosing  a  textbook  for  a  course  or  (b)
evaluating the textbook you are currently using.  Even though this list was origin때ly
created several dεcades ago, it  st퍼 stands as  an excellent set of evaluative factors.
As  you  read  through  this  evaluation  form,  think  of an  ESL  textbook  that you  are
reasonably familiar with and ask yourself how well that book meets the criteria.

learning?)

CHAPTER  11  Techniques and Materials

193

It needs to be made clear here how the word text is norm외ly used in the profession
and in this book, especially to distinguish texts from textbooks.  Texts are any of a
wide variety of types or ge따es of linguistic forms.  Texts can be spoken or written.
Among  written texts, the  range  of possib파ties extends from  labels  and forms  and
charts to essays and manuals and books.  Textbooks are one type of text, a book for
use in an educational curriculum.

Among  other written texts available for your u않 in the classroom, an almost
unlimited  supply  of  real-world  t앉tual material  is  available.  We  daily  encounter
signs, schedules, calendars, advertisements, menus, memos, notes, etc.  (see Chapter
20 for  a  long list of texts).  Aside  from  written texts, other visual aids can support
classroom lessons.  Consider some possibilities of both texts and other supporting
material:

a.  Teacher resource books.  Dozens of resource books are specifically

designed to provide ideas for teachers.  For example, books are available on
spea암ng (e.g., Bailey, 2005), listening (Helgesen &  Brown, 200η, reading
(Anderson, 1999; Nuttall, 1996), grammar (Nunan, 2005), activities for children
(Linse, 2005), and the list goes on.

b.  Other student textbooks.  Even a sm따I library of student textbooks other
than the one you are using W퍼 yield a book or two with some additional
material that you can employ as supplementaπ material.

c.  Realia.  Realia are probably the oldest form of classroom aid.  Tuer'ε is

nothing like an “ object” lesson.  Objects-food items, cosmetics, household
gadgets, tools, and other materials-always add some significant reality to the
classroom.  Their effectiveness in helping students to connect language to
reality cannot be underestimated.  Realia are especially useful and important
for teaching children, who benefit from tan밍ble objects that can stimulate
kinesthetic connections.

d  Self-made paper-based visual aids.  、1V1th the dominance of computer
media in our world today, you may think it’s  odd to think about some of
the more traditional forms of visual aids.  Posters, charts, and magazine
pictures represent “ old-fashioned” but effective teaching aids.  If you are
artistically inclined, you should consider trying your hand at creating
posters or charts for classroom use.  Otherwise, a  resource that many
teachers find helpful is an assemblage of a couple of hundred magazine
pictures that you can file  and cros웅index.  Start with a  pile of fairly  recent
magazines and pick out pictures (photos, diagrams, advertisements, etc.)
that show people or objects large enough to be easily seen by all students
in a classroom setting.  Mounting them on cardboard or laminating them
will protect your pictures from wrinkling.

rεsource cεnters in many institutions offer a diversity of materials that should
not be ignored.

When someone mentions technology in the language classroom, your first impulse is
to think computer te띠nology, mostly because computers have so pervaded our daily
home and workplace  conte갔s.  But technology covers everything from audiotapes
to video to, yes, computers.

Some like to think that technology first entered the language classroom in the
1950s  and  1960s in the form  of the language  laboratory.  Institutions hastened to
dedicate  rooms  to  the  installation  of multiple  tape갑eck-equipped booths  where
students gathered  to  listen  to  native  speakers  modeling  the  drills  of the  current
day’s lesson.  Often, users of language labs were able to record their own voices on
one track of a tape in 따1 attempt to match the native-speaker model; otherwise, they
simply had the benefit of a  listening lab.  The advent of the language lab  brought
promises of great breakthroughs in language  teaching: Technology would come to
the rescue of less than totally effective methods.  But when students were not being
transformed  into  communicatively  proficient  speakers  via  the  I따iguage lab,  we
discovered that there were some severe limitations to this new technological aid.

When  the  personal  computer  came  on  the  scene  in  the  1980s,  some
practitioners  in  the language-teaching  profession  had sinlilar  hopes for  salvation.
Once  again  educational  institutions  had  a  promising  new technology  that  could
offer 파iguistic input and output, feedback, 따id a  locus for  student collaboration,

196

CHAPTER  12  Technology in  the  [ /assroom

197

interactivity, and fun.  This  time, howev1εr, the promises wεre mor1ε guarded as  wε
werewaη of the complexities involved in incorporating this powεrful tool into our
classrooms.  The  lattεr part  of this  chaptεr will  specifically  focus  on  computer
assisted  language  learning (CALL);  but  first,  wε take  a  look  at  other  types  of
tεchnological aids that ar1ε commonly availablε to a languagε teachεr today.

NON-COMPUTER-BASED TECHNOLOGY

Bεlieve it  or not, there  is  technology  availablε that is  non-computεr-based! If you
went back a hundred years, you would find classrooms that had morε than a  piεce
of chalk and a  chalkboard as  tεaching aids.  In  the previous chapter, photographs,
posters,  charts,  and  rεalia  wεre  mεntionεd-all  timε-t℃sted,  readily  availablε,
potentially interesting matεrials that ar'ε still viablε in today’s classrooms.  But thesε
don’t  fall  into  the  catεgory  of  tεchnology-which  I’m  defining  hεrε  as  any
εquipmεnt that  requires  ℃lectricity to operate.  By  th℃ εarly part of thε twentieth
cεntury, as  ℃lectrical appliances  becamε commonplace, motion pictures (moviεs),
films  and  tlmstrips, slides, and phonograph records join℃d thε ranks of techrn] logy
in  thε  classroom.  Then,  in  the  middle  part  of  thε  cεntury,  thε  tape  rεcorder
rεvolutionized foreign  languagε learning in that, for the first  timε, ordinaη7 peoplε
(and languagε students) could record their own and  othεrs ’  voicεs and play  thεm
back.

Somε of that  technology  is  still  useful  in  today’s  ultra-fast-paced  paradε of
ubiquitous gimmicks and d℃vicεs and computer-based pr1앵rams.  Consider somε of
th esε pεdi℃ctly useful, instructive, and conv1εniεnt technologiεs:

1. Commercially produced audiotapes and CDs

It  may  be  a  해rprisε to  somε of thε more  youthful  genεration of tech-savvy
language students that audiotapes do in fact exist and arε still  used in some circles
today! Truε, cassettε tapεs have  been replaced by CDs  for virtually εvery textbook
and  program  today.  But  in  some  cases, audiotapes  are  a  financial  necessity  in  an
environment of budgεtary cutbacks that prev1εnt the purchase of thε hardware and
softwarε necessaηr for CDs and CD players‘  Furthεrm ore,“ old” matεrial may st피 be
effective.

The  mor,ε current  and  usually  mor1ε readily  available  audio  technology  is  of
course the CD (compact disc), which accomplishes ev1ε다πhing that a tape does, but
with greatεr accuracy, durability, and  storag'ε capacity.  Another fεatur,ε is  that the
user can pinpoint a “ spot” on a CD without thε old fast-forward and rewind buttons.
An  advantag℃ of both  audiotapes  and  CDs  is  that  thεy can  be  usεd for  self study
beyond the language classroom.  Many self instructional language pr앵rams (Berlitz,
Rosetta Stonε, etc.) are  availablε in this technology.  (Notε that CD-ROMs  rεquire a
computer to opεrate them, while CDs can be accessed simply through a CD player.)
Whatεver your  own  situation  is,  libraries  and  instructional  resource  centers
maybe ablε to providε a surprising variety of audiocassettes and a growing number

CHAPTER  12  Technology in  the  Classroom

of CDs with (a) listening exεrcises, (b) lectures, (c) stories, and (d) oth℃r au thεntic
samples of native-speaker texts.

2. Commercially produced videotapes and DVDs

In thε video catεgotγ, tape-based tεchnology is  also waning with thε advent of
DVDs  and the dεcreasing cost of digital video hardware.  DVDs  (digital vidεo disc)
offer the samε conveniencε and accuracy that CDs givε us, and so ar,ε of course the
tεchnology  of  choice.  Many  institutions  still  maintain  videotape  libraries,
supplemented  by an  inuεasingly large  collection of DVDs.  Among  some possible
catεgoriεs are  (a)  documentaries  on  special  topics,  (b)  moviεs, films,  and  news
media, and (c) pr앵rams dεsigned specifically to instruct students on certain aspects
of English.  An  option  that  some  language  teachers  have  found  useful  is  closed
captionεd video  that  offers  students  writtεn-languagε input  simultaneously  with
oral.

3. Self-made audiotapes and CDs

At  thε risk  of sounding  rεpetitious, I am listing  audiotapes  here  for  the same
reasons  specified  above.  However,  sincε  we  are  talking  about  self-made  audio
pr앵rams, we  should  note  the  ready  availability  and  affordab피ty today  of digital
recorders, usually no larger than a  cell phone, with amazing memo1γ on tiny mini
discs.  This  technology is  quickly rendering  the  tape  (cassette)  recorder obsolete,
and coupled with computer-based capacities to creatε your own recordings, oftiεrs
a  veη7 convenient  set  of  options.  Teachers  can  now  easily  create  their  own
supporting materials, with the possible drawback of amplifying devices suitable for
classroom  usεs.  Recordings  of conversations,  εspecially conv,εrsations of pεop le
known to one’s own students, can be stimulating.  Or just use your digital rεcorder
for radio or TV excerpts of news, speeches, talk shows, εtc., for listening tεchniques.
Includεd in  the  cat℃gory of  self made  audio  sεgments is  the  possibility  of
students creating their own recordings.  As  long as playback hardware is  available
in your classroom, students' creations may bε both instructive and fun.

4. Self-made videotapes and DVDs

Now  that  digital  video  cameras  are  also  accessible,  they  too  are  making
videotape recorders look like dinosaurs.  Potentially interesting person쩌ized video
segments can be created in two ways.  With a recordable DVD  unit you can record
television programs.  They need not be long or complex.  Sometimes a veπ simple
advertisement or a segment of the news makes an εxcellent audiovisual stimulus for
classroom work.  With a camera, you can try your hand at creating your own “film ”
(a story, a “candid camera” episode, a skit, etc.), perhaps with some of your students
as  principal actors.  Keep in mind, of course, the need for the hardware necεssaπ
to play back your creations to your students.

Students, too, can become the creators of video segments that are shown in the
classroom  (hardwar'ε  permitting).  I  have  sεen  some  delightful  little  skits  and

interviews that students have created, and they act as intrinsic motivators as well as
ex cellεnt practice in English.

5. Overhead projection

The  advent  of  computerizεd visual  presentations  through  such  software  as
PowerPoint  (see  the  next  section)  Iεnds to  dominatε our  profession.  Howεver,
many classrooms around the world  providε an ovεrhead transparency projector as
standard equipment (and, I must say, εqui pmεnt that will probably have less chancε
of failing).  Commercially  availablε transparencies are  available  that can enhance a
tεxtbook lesson.  Your  own  charts,  lists,  graphics,  and  other  visually  presented
material can bε easily reproduced (through most photocopying equipment or your
