can save papεr and can be reused in a subsequent term of teaching thε same course.

COMPUTER-ASSISTED LANGUAGE LEARNING (CALL)

In thε early  1980s the world was introduced to thε personal computer, and it soon
be camε a familiar  housεhold itεm.  With its  advent, dictionariεs bεgan to add nεw
terms that are  now commonplace: k양board, monitor, CPU,  mouse,  이1te, joystick,
modem,  and  the  list  εxpanded daily.  Now lexicographers  can’t  εven keep  up  as
othεr words,  phrases,  and  acronyms  becomε components  of  peoplε ’s  everyday
linguistic  corpus: iPods,  MP3 players, flash  drives,  WiFi,  along with alphabεt soup
it1εms like CD-ROM,  CD-RW, ISP,  HTI'P, JPEG,  PDF,  URL,  and USE.  These terms run
off the tips of our tonguεs and into usually understanding εars like pixels through
FireWires.  Undauntεd, our  silicon-driven  culture  clamors  for  more,  better,  and
faster!

The  recent  advancεs in  educational  applications  of  computer  hardwar1ε and
software  have  provided  a  rapidly  growing-and  sometimes  bewildering-sεt of
rεsources for languagε classrooms.  The practical applications of computer-assisted
language  learning  (CALL)  are  growing  at  such  a  rapid  pace  that  it  is  almost
impossible  for  a  classroom  teacher  to  keep  up  with  the  field.  But  don’t  lεt  the
multitude of options discourage you from at lεast considεring some CALL applications
in  your  own  teaching.  At  the  same  time,  don’t  let  the  allur1ε  of  computer-b싫ed
tεchnolo밍r fool you into thinking that computεrs w피 magically makε your students
happy and succεssful.

CALL,  CMC, TMLL,  or What?

First, a  word or two  about the popularly used acronym, CALL.  Somε practitionεrs
are questioning whether the tεrm is  st피 via blε (Bax, 2003; Kern, 2006;Warschauer,
1999).  Onε issue is whether or not computers assist our instruction any more than
books, pens, and libraries did in years past.  Another question asks if thε “ computer”
part of thε acronym eliminates the usε of other digital  technologiεs, such as DVDs

200

[ HAPTER 12

Te[ hnology in  the Classroom

and CDs, which can be utilizεd without a computer.  And finally, in an age in which
computer scienc℃ has  peπacted all  aspects of our liv'εs, including our classrooms,
why single out computers as  somε special  casε “whεn onlinε communication has
become  a  normal  part  of our daily  l표e? ” (Kern,  2006, p.  185).  Instεad, we  should,
according  to  Kern  and  others, “ refiεr broadly  to  information  and  communication
technologies rathεr than specifically to computers.” This, thεn, could lead us bεttεr
toward  what  Chap℃Ile  (2005)  has  suggested:  integrating  CALL  into  interactionist
thεories of second language learning and teaching.  After all, tεchnology is relatively
useless if thε science behind it is  not fully  amalgamated into language pedagogy.

So, is it st피 politically corrεct to retiεr to CALL?Thε alternatives are phrasεs likε
computer-mediated  communication (CMC),  which  is  bεcoming more  widely
accepted.  Even better, pεrhaps, is the concept of technology-mediated langu쟁e
learning (TMLL).  The  latter  captures  the  essence  of currεnt efforts  to  integrate
technology  into  our pedagogical  theories, and  allows  for  tεchnology beyond  just
com putεr science.  Having said all that, I W피 sidε with Chapelle (2005), who wisεly
refrainεd from  joining  thε alphabεt soup argument, and  simply accepted CALL  as
associated  with  “thε  broad  range  of  activiti℃S  associatεd with  technology  and
languag℃ learning”  (p. 743).  For  my part, becausε of thε popularity and  univ,εrs al
understanding  of the  term  CALL,  I  too  will  use  the  latter  acronym,  begging  the
indulgence of any rεadεrs who yearn for a new term!

Principles and Benefits of CALL

There  are  some  important  principlεs to  follow  in  using  εlectronic t℃chnology to
enhance  your  tεaching.  Following  ar'ε  some  guidεlines  that  capsulize  thosε
principles, adapt℃d from Egbert (2005, pp. 11-14) and Beatty (2003):

•  Usε technology to support the pedagogical goals of the class and curriculum.
That is,  don’t  design  instruction  to  fit  the  technology  (what  Egbert  calls  a
technocentric approach); rather, focus  on coursε goals and objectives and
t와@ advantagε  of  whatever  technology  is  availablε  to  enhance  those
purposεs.  T℃chnology is  a  tool and wε must rεmembεr that CALL  includes
the  word  assisted,  implying  that  technology  is  not  the “main  event”  in  a
classroom.

•  Evaluate  the  appropri,αteness  of  software  for  your  purpose  and  the
av:없!ability  of  sufficiεnt hardware.  It  is  useful  to  look  at  such  factors  as
ε:xpertisε  necessaη7 for  studεnts to  usε a  computer-based  resource,  thεir
interest  in  thε resource,  the  general  cognitive  difficulty  of the  task(s),  the
proficiency  level  required  to  do  the  task(s), and, of coursε, any  budgetary
costs involved in acquiring or using a resource.

•  Create a classroom εnvironment in which CALL is affiηηed by the students‘

Th℃y need to “buy in” to the concept of computer-enhanc℃d learning.

201

•  Makε the  technology  aceεssiblε to all Iεarners.  Varying  stylεs and abilities
among  lεarnεrs must bε considered so  that all  learners can benefit and the
risk  of “ losing”  somε learners  is  eliminated.  Wε are  wεll acquainted  with
“ tεchnophobic ”  learnεrs  and  must  not  assumε  that  everyone  finds
com putεrs and computεr programs easy to operate.

•  Usε Iεchnology 생강ctively  Egbert  (2005, p ‘  12) notes that “ξ야ctiνe mεans
that  studεnts lεam language bettεr or fast℃r using  thε technology than thεy
would  hav,ε  using  thε  tools  that  would  ordinarily  be  available.”  So,  for
εxamplε, thε individualization and feεdback possible with CALL may excεεd
thε teacher's ability otherwisε to reach all students.

•  Usε  tεchnology  efficieηψ Efficiency  is  rεlated  to  time.  Whεn CALL
enablεs a student (or teacher) to accomplish an objective in less timε, then
it  is  viable.  For εxample, listεning to  a  passage on a  CD  (as  opposεd to an
audiotape) is  mor,℃ εfficient whεn a  student wishes to  rεplay thε passag'ε a
numbεr of  timεs.  Dictionary  searchεs  can  be  fastεr,  and  with  language
corpus data availablε, a multiplicity of collocations can be instantly aceεssed.
Spεεch analyzεrs can provide instant visual feedback to students wishing to
improv'ε pronunciation.

•  Hav'ε a  bαckup plaη in  casε thε tεchnology fails.  Wε’ve all  witnεssed at
somε time  or  anothεr an  elεctronically based  presεntation go  awry  duε
either  to  hardware  or  s‘1깐ware problems.  I  have  personally  agonized  over
my own and my studεnts ’ moments of frustr‘  tionwhεnsomεthing fails in thε
fragilε  links  bεtween  preparation  and  delivery.  You  ar,ε  wise  to  have
something  ready  to  substitute  when  these  moments  occur-and they  will
occur!

With  thosε important guidelin℃S as a backdrop, we can enumeratε a numbεr of
bεnefits  of  CALL.  Consider  thε  following  possible  advantages  of  including  a
com putεr component in languag,ε instruction (adaptεd from Chapellε, 2005; Egbert,
2005; Miyagi, 2006; Nunan, 2006; Warschauer & Hεaly, 1998):

•  opportuniη for learnεrs to notice languagε forms
•  a  mεans for providing optimal modified input to lεarners
•  multimodal (visual, auditorγ, writtεn) practice
•  immediate, personalizεd feedback
•  individualization in a large class
•  selfpacing
•  private space to make rnistakεs
•  convεniεnt modε for  [distance]  teacher feedback
•  convεnient venue for  [written]  practice of the L2
•  collaborative projects
•  variεty in the resources available and learning  stylεs used

Uses of CALL in the Language Classroom

The possible uses  of computer technology in a  languagε classroom are  sεεmingly
endless, as a glancε through de Szεndeffy’s (2005) and Egbert’s (2005) highly usεful
rεsources will rεveal.  Every timε you turn around and read a new article or book or
attεnd a  conference  presentation,  a  novel  idea  is  presented.  So,  in  some  ways,
because  this  book-and  this  chapter  in  particular-is  designed  as  mεrεly  an
introduction to technology, I will only scratch the surfacε here.  In so doing, I hope
your own explorations w피 go far  beyond what is  sugg℃sted here, and that in  duε
course of time you will be the person presenting yet  anothεr eff.℃ctiv'ε way to  usε
CALL in a language course.

Here are some thoughts to start the wheels of your mind turning.  For furthεr
rεsources, consult  the  list  of Wεb-based information  as  well  as  thε list  of printεd
books suggestεd at the end of this chapter.

1. Collaborative projects

With as  many as  two  to four  studεnts to a  terminal, research  pr이ects can  be
carriεd out utilizing  data  availablε on  th℃ Intεrnεt.  Analysis  of data  can  be  donε
with data managεment or statistical processing software.  Charts, graphics, and text
can be generated for presentation of findings to the rest of the class.  Miyagi (2006)
reported a collaborativ,ε project on environmental awarenεss that involv1εd students
in Japan and in the Unitεd States working together.

2.  Peer-editing of compositions

The  exchange  of  information  on  discs,  Web-based  bulletin  boards,  or
networkεd computers offers students an efficient means of peer-editing of drafts of
compositions.  Many  instructors  effectively  use  e-mail  (se℃ below)  and  bull℃tin
boards  (see  below)  to  correspond  with  students, and  vicε vεrsa.  Instructors  can
easily manage commεnts on final drafts through this technology.

3. E-mail

The most obvious form  of using e-mail for  English  teaching is giving students
the  possibility  for  actual  communication  with  individuals  around  thε  world.
Discussion lists provide opportunities for reading and writing on topics of interest.
E-mail  pen  pals  have  become  popular.  Through  the  Web,  chat  programs  offer
students  the  novelty  of  asynchronous  (delay  in  time  between  sending  and
responding) discussion.  Teachers havε used ξ-mail communication for such things
as dialogue journals with students and collaboration with othεr teachers‘

4. Biogs

π1℃ usε of biogs (blogging) has become very popular, especi외ly among young
pεoplζ Biogs  are  easy-to-usε  simplε Wεb sitεs  whεrε one  can  quickly  write
thoughts, interact with  othεrs, get  feεdback, post photos, and  more.  A  numbεr of
biogs are free and can be easily set up in a minutε or two.  Biogs providε yet another
vεnue for  exchang,ε of ideas in  the L2.

5. Web-based bulletin board communication

With the increasing use of ￦eb-based discussion v℃nuεs, students are offer℃d a
mεans to  accomplish  objectivεs of a  coursε (such  as  problem  solving,  resolving
differεncεs in data, drafting compositions, εtc.).  By  setting up such bulletin board
(BB) discussion sites, teachers also giv,ε studεnts a mεans for using th℃ L2  directly in
wnting.

6. -’eb page design

A rapidly growing number of educational  institutions have offered courses to
students  in  Wεb page  design.  Many  computer  users  self teach  the  art  of Web
authoring, with the help of such books as  Lynch and Horton's (2002) book and/or
Web-basεd sites (listed at the end of this chapter).  In thε process, students not only
becomε acquaintεd with comput℃r technology in general but also utilize English in
doing research on a  topic, composing and designing, and collaborating with othεr
students.

An interεsting, motivating project for  students in a class is  to create their own
class Web  page,  complεtε with  stories,  interviews,  biographies,  accounts  of field
trips, restaurant reviεws, photos, vidεo clips, sound bitεs, and graphics.  Self made or
collaboratively dεsigned vidεos also offer students a chance to “ham it up” and make
thεir own vidεG cr,εations ‘

7. Videoconferencing

An  incr℃asingly popular  technology  is  thε carrying  out  of confer,εnces ov'εr
vidεo.  As  long as the hardware (a digital vidεo camera;wirεd orwirεlεss access) is
available, frεr software can be obtained from some Intεrnεt sites (Yahoo Messenger,
Festoon  with  Skype).  This  adds  th℃  all-important  nonverbal  dimεnsion  of
communication.

8. Reinforcement of classroom material

With rεady availability of a widε array of softwar,ε programs, coursε objectives
can be reinforced, and addεd material can bε made available.  Many textbooks now
come with an accompanying CD-ROM disc (accessible on a computεr),ifnot a DVD
(also accessible on many computers), filled with practice exεrcises, self-check tests,
and εxtra reading and visual  material.  Some  course programs  includε a  dedicatεd
Web  site  that  is  rεgularly updated  and/or  an  onlinε section  in  εach unit,  which
εncouragεs usε of Internet-related activity.  Thε process of learning to read a foreign

204

CHAPTER  12  Technology  in  the Classroom

language  can be enhanced throu양1 computer-adaptive programs that offer lexical
따id grammatical information at predicted points of difficulty.  And the list goes on!

9. Podcasting

Another  relatively  recent  technology,  podcasting  is  a  method  of distributing
multimedia  ties-such as  audio  pr'앵rams or music videos-over the  Internet, for
playback  on mobile  devices or personal computers.  Podcasts  can be  a  source of
authentic listening for students of En행sh, and, if students have a mobile devicε (an
iPod,for example), they can access such material at their convenience outsidε of the
classroom  environment.  Dervin  (2006)  offers  a  number of diffiεrent sourcεs and
uses of podcasting (including what he calls “podwitnessing” in which learners can
access matεrial on various cultures), as well as a set of references.

10. Games and simulations

Not to be overlooked are the m따iy engaging games and simulations, many of
them  involving  verbal  l따iguage, 야iat present  students  with  stimulating  problem
solving tasks in which they must use functional language to pursue the go외S of the
games.  Carefully planned uses of such g따nes in the classroom (e.g., for practicing
certain  verbs,  tenses,  questions,  locatives,  εtc.)  add  considerable  interest  to  a
classroom,  especially  if  the  curriculum  or  traditional  textbook  prescribes
mechanical drills that are seen as boring to the learner.

11. Computer-adaptive testing

Many current standardized tests are now computer-adaptive.  During the early
items,  right  and  wrong  없iswers are  electronically  analyzed  in  order  to  present
options for later items, from a bank of possible items, that will be neither too easy
nor too difficult and therefore W피 present an optimal challenge.  Test-takers need
to be aware of the technology and to develop appropriate strategies for responding.

12. Speech recognition software

A remarkable  degree  of accuracy is  now available  and affordable  in  softwar1ε
designed  to  process  human  speech  and  analyze  production.  Speech  recognition
programs  for  the  language  classroom  have  a  multitude  of potenti때 applications,
including simple  exercises  in pronunciation and electronic visual  feedback  αVF)
that  can  show  the  accuracy  of  a  learner's  control  of  phonemic  and  prosodic
elements (Igarashi, 2004).

13. Concordancing

With literally billions of ling띠stic corpus data now readily available, the process
of concordancing (searching for  words  in context and  collocations)  has  become
relatively simple.  Students wishing to research the possible contexts of words and
teachers  plaru파ig  a  g없nmar or  vocabulary  lesson  are  finding  such  sources
e띠ighte띠ng.

14. Multimedia presentations

Finally, both students and language  teachers have  used PowerPoint and other
media  presentation  software  to  enliven  a  presentation  with  graphics, charts, art,
photos, lists, and audio “sound bites.” Whether it’S a student giving a presεntation in
class or a researcher pr℃senting data at a  professional conference, these computer
based technologies have proven to be universally popular.

This  chapt℃r is  intended  to  be  simply  a  briεf primer  on  technology  in  the
language classroom.  With so many books, Web sites, resources, softwar'ε pr,앵rams,
and hardwar'ε options available, it is  truly impossible to give  adequate coverage to

chapter, I have offer'εd some ideas for you to begin that quεst.

TOPICS FOR DISCUSSION, ACTION, AND RESEARCH

[Note: (I) Individual work; (G) group or pair work; (C) whole-class discussion.]

1.  (I) How would you dεfine technology? Are  thεrε othεr non-computer-based

technological devices that you can think ot?

2.  (C) Is  there anyone in the class who would describe him- or herself as a

technophobe? If so, what are the root causes of this fear?  Perhaps others can
S앵gest ways to overcome or deal with such 때xieties.

3.  (C)Are thεre other principles and bεnεfits of CALL that the class can think of

and describe?

4.  (G) In groups, brainstorm some of the disadvantages of CALL  in thε language
classroom (which weren’t  specifically listed).  How would onε OV1εrcome or
respond to such disadvantages?

5.  (C) This chapter did not discuss thε p이itical and ethical consequences of the
widespread use of Internet-based communication around the world.  Issues of
censorship, freedom of speech, junk e-mails, privacy, and appropriate Web-
based verbal and nonverbal material are common.  What thoughts mi양it the
class have to share on any of these or other similar issues?

6.  (G/C) In small groups, class members can brainstorm other uses of CALL,

beyond the 14 that were listed and described in this chapter.  These could be
from their own experience learning or teaching a foreign l따iguage, or from
thεir rεading and discussion in other courses such as this onε.  As a whole
class, a composite list of S맹gestions might be put on the board and later
distributed to the class via e-mail, bullεtin board, or class Web site.

The quiet buzz of voices from the classroom echoes down thε hallway.  The 30-som℃­

stories, joking, gossiping, and talking  about the  latεst popular songs.  As  the  tεacher
walks  in,  the  studεnts  fall  silεnt,  face  forward,  and  opεn  their  tεxtbooks  in
anticipation  。f another  English  lesson, another day  of reciting,  rεpεating, copγing,

But today their usual teacher is absent, and a substitute teacher sits down at the

semicircles.  Surprisεd, thε students comply.  Then thε tεacher speaks:

[after some siteηce] I ’m  sorπ Please repeat.

[long silence, furtive glances to  classmates]  Best?

S:

WHAT IS  INTERACTION?

You  hav1ε been  introducεd to  some basic  issues  in  d℃signing courses and  lεssons,
and in using and adapting materials and resources, so your next movε is to st℃P into
the  classroom and  begin  thε process of stimulating  intεraction.  This  chaptεr will
oftiεr some pointers on how to do that.

The  class  just  described, whosε studεnts had  bεen accustomεd to  recitation
and  mechanical  output,  just  became-perhaps  for  the  first  timε-interactiv1ε.
Interaction  is  an  important  word  for  languagε  teachers.  In  thε  era  of
the  hεart  of
communicative  languagε  teaching,  interaction
communication;  it  is  what  communication  is  all  about.  We  send  messagεs, wε
recεiv,ε  them,  we  interpret  them  in  a  context,  wε negotiate  meanings,  and  we
collaborate to accomplish certain purposes.  And after several  decadεs of rεsεarch
on teaching and learning languages, we have  discoverεd that thε best way to learn
to interact is through interaction itseκ

in  fact,

is,

Interaction  is  the  collaborative  exchange  of  thou양its,  feelings,  or  idεas
bεtween two  or  more  pεople, resulting  in  a  reciprocal  effect  on  each  othεr.
Theories of communicativ1ε competence emphasize the importance of interaction

as human beings usε language in various contexts to “ negotiate” mεaning, or simply
statεd, to get an idεa out of one person’s  head and into th℃ head of another person
and  vie℃ versa.  From  the very  beginning  of language  study, classrooms  should bε
interactivε.  Wilga Rivers (1987, pp. 4-5) puts it this way:

Through  interacti。n, studεnts can  increase  their  language  stor'ε  as
they listen to or read authentic linguistic matεrial, or εvεn thε output
of  their  fellow  studεnts in  discussions,  skits,  joint  problεm-solving

in real-life exchanges .... Even at an elemεntary stagε, they learn in this
way to exploit the elasticity of language.

INTERACTIVE PRINCIPLES

Most of thε 12 principles listed and discussεd in  Chaptεr 4 form foundation  stonεs
for  structuring  a  theory  of interaction  in  thε language  classroom.  Considεr thε
following  selεcted relationships:

Automaticity:  Tmε human  interaction  is  bεst  accomplished  whεn focal
attεntion is  on  mεanings and  messages  and  not  on grammar  and  othεr linguistic
forms.  Learners are thus foεεd from keεping languagε in a controllεd mode and can
mor'ε easily proceed to automatic modes of processing.

Intrinsic  motivation:  As  studεnts  bεcomε engaged  with  εach other  in
speech  acts  of ful  tllment  and  sεlf actualization, their  deepεst drives  are  satisfiεd.
And as they more fully  apprεciatε thεir own compεtεnce to usε language, they can
develop a system of sεlfreward.

Strategic  investment:  Interaction  requires  the  use  of  stratεgic  language
competence both to  makε cεrtain decisions  on how to  say  or write  or interpret
languag℃, and  to  makε repairs  when  communication  pathways  are  blocked.  The
spontanεity of interactive  discourse  requires  judicious use  of numerous  strategies
for production and comprehension.

Willingness to communicate: Interaction requirεs an attitude on the part of
the  learner  that  says,  “ I  waηt to  rεach  out  to  others  and  communicate.” This
willingnεss to  communicatε further implies the risk of failing  to produce intεnded
mεaning, of failing to intεrpret intεndedmεaning (on th℃ paπ ofsomeonε εlsε), of
being laughed at, of being  shunnεd or rejected.  Thε rewards, of course, arε great
and worth the risks.

The language-culture connection:  The cultural loading of interactiv1ε spεεch
as  well  as  writing  requires  that  interlocutors  be  thoroughly  vεrsed in  the  cultural
nuances of language.

Interlanguage: Thε complexity  of intεraction entails  a  long  devεlopmental
procεss of acquisition.  Numerous εrrors of production and compr1εhension will be

Communicative  competence:  All  of  the  εlements  of  communicativ'ε  com
petence  않rammatical,  discourse,  sociolinguistic,  pragmatic,  and  strategic)  arε
involv1εd  in  human  interaction.  All  aspects  must  work  togεther  for  successful

ROLES OF THE INTERACTIVE TEACHER

An  intεractive  teachεr is  by  dεfinition  one  who  is  fully  awar·ε  of  thε  group
dynamics of a classroom.  As  Dornyei and Murphey (2003) εxplained, the success
of classroom learning is veη muchdepεndent on how students relatε to each other,
what  the  classroom  environment  is,  how  εffectively  students  cooperate  and
communicate with each  othεr, and  of course what  rolεs thε teachεr and  learners
play.  But it’s important to rεmεmber that effectiv·ε interaction within thε dynamics
of  a  classroom  is  a  gradual  incrεmental process.  According  to  Vygotsky  (1962,
1978),  εff1εctive  learning  in  students' “zones  of  proximal  development”  involves
“ [starting  out  with]  firm  leading  and  modeling  on  the  part  of the  teacher  and
[shifting]  as students internalizε morε and more of the processεs and tεachers lεarn
how to let go” (Dornyεi &Murphεy, 2003, p. 98).

Teachers  can  play  many  rolεs in  the  coursε of tεaching. Just  as  parεnts are
called upon to  be many things  to  thεir childrεn, teachεrs cannot bε satisfiεd with
only  one  role.  Rebεcca O화ord εt al.  (1998)  pointεd out  that  teacher  roles  are
often best described in the form  of metaphor: teacher as  manufacturer, teachεr as
doctor,  teachεr as  judge, tεacher as  gardεner, and others.  Following  you  will  find
another set of mεtaphors to  describe  a  spectrum of possibilities of teacher roles,
some of which are more conducive to creating an intεractive classroom than othεrs.

1. The teacher as controller

A role that is  sometimes expected in traditional educational institutions is that
of “master” controller, always in charge of every moment in the classroom.  Master
controllers  determine  what  thε students  do, when  they  should  speak, and  what
language  forms  they should use.  They can often  predict many  studεnt rεsponses
because  eveηrthing is  mapped  out  ahead  of time, with  no  leeway  for  divergent
paths.  In some respects, such control may sound admirable.  But for interaction to
takε place, the  teacher  must create  a  climate  in  which spontaneity can thrive, in

CHAPTER  13

Initiating Interaction  in  the  Classroom

215

which  unrehearsed  language  can  be  performed,  and  in  which  the  freedom  of
expression  given  over to  students  makes  it  impossible  to  predict  εverything that
they will say and do.

Nεvεrtheless, somε control  on  your  part  is  actually  an  important  element  of
succεssfully carrying out interactivε tεchniques.  In the planning phase espεcially, a
wise  controller will  carefully  project  how a  tεchnique W피 proceed, map  out  thε
initial  input  to  students,  sp℃cify directions  to  be  given, and  gaug,ε thε timing  of a
technique.  So, granted that allowing for  spontanεity of expression involv,εs yielding
certain elements of control to studεnts, nevertheless, even in the most cooperative of
interactive classrooms, the  teachεr must  maintain  some control  simply  to organize
the class hour.

2. The teacher as director

Somε interactivε classroom time can legitimately bε structurεd in such a way
that  thε teacher  is  likε a  conductor of an  orchestra  or a  director  of a  drama.  As
students engagε in εither rehearsed or spontaneous language performancε, it is your
job to keep  thε procεss flowing  smoothly and  efficiently.  The  ultimatε motiv,ε of
such direction, of coursε, must always be to enable studεnts eventually to εngagε in
the  real-life  drama  of improvisation  as  each  communicativ,ε event  brings  its  own
untqueness.

3. The teacher as manager

This  metaphor  captures  your  role  as  one  who  plans  lessons,  modules,  and
courses, and who structures the larger, longer segments of classroom time, but who
then  allows  each  individual  player  to  be  creative  within  thosε  parametεrs.
Managers  of succ℃ssful corporations, for  example, retain  control  of cεrtain larger
objectives  of  thε  company,  keεp  employees  pointed  toward  goals,  εngag,ε  in
ongoing evaluation and feedback, but give freedom to each p℃rson to work in his or
her  own  individual  areas  of expeπise.  A  language  class  should  not  be  markedly
differεnt.

4. The teacher as facilitator

A less directive role might be described as  facilitating  the process of learning,
of making learning  easiεr for  students: helping  thεm to  clear away  roadblocks, to
find shortcuts, to negotiate rou야 terrain ‘  The facilitating role rεquires that you stεp
away from the managerial or directive role and allow studεnts, with your guidance
and gentle prodding, to find their own pathways to success.  A facilitator capitalizes
on the principle of intrinsic motivation by allowing  students to discover language
through using it pragmatically, rather than by telling them about language.

5. The teacher as resource

H℃rε you take the least dirεctive role.  In fact, the implication of the resource
role  is  that  the  studεnt takεs the  initiative  to  come  to  you.  You  arε available  for
advice and counsel when the studεnt seeks it.  It is of course not practical to push
this mεtaphor to an extremε where you would simply walk into a classroom and say

something like,“Well, what do you want to learn today?” Some degree of control, of
pl없ming, of mana밍ng the classroom is  essential.  But  there are appropriate  times
when you can literally take a back seat and allow the students to proceed with their
own linguistic development.

In the  lessons  that you  deliver, you  should  be able  to  assume  때l five  of these
roles on this continuum of directive to nondirective teaching, depending on the
purpose and context of an activity.  The kεy to interactive teaching is to strive toward
the upper, nondirective  end of the continuum, gradually  enabling your students to
move  from  their  roles  of  total  dependence  (upon  you,  the  class  activities,  the
textbook, etc.) to relatively total independence.  The proficiency level of your class
w피 determinε to  some  extent which  roles  will  dominate.  But  even at the  lowest
levels,  some  genuine  interaction  can  take  place, and  your  role  must  be  one  that
releases your students to tη things for themselves.

We  turn now to a more empirical and practical consideration of interaction in

the communicative language  classroom.  In the remainder of this chapter you w피
get a sense of what you can do to initiate interaction in the classroom-that is, how
your input C때 stimulate student interaction.  (In Chapter 14, you ·떼l be given some
gui없nce on maintaining interaction through effective group work techniques.)

FOREIGN LANGUAGE INTERACTION ANALYSIS

One way to be횡n to look at your role as an initiator of interaction in the classroom
is to look at yoursεIf (and other teachers) in terms of a well-known taxonomy for
describing  classroom  interaction.  Almost  four  decades  ago, the work of Flanders
(1970)  and,  more  specific  to  foreign  language  teaching,  of Gertrude  Moskowitz
(1971, 1976) gave us some categories for observation of classes known as the FLINT
(Foreign Language Interaction Analysis) model (see Table  13.1).

How is  a model like  this helpful in developing  interactive language  teaching?
There are several practical uses.  First, it gives you a taxonomy for observing other
teachers.  Moskowitz  recommends using  a  chart or grid to note instances of each
category.  You  can  also  calculate  how much  classroom  time  is  devoted  to  each.
Then you can evaluate the wisdom of certain choices made by the teacher or look
at the overall distribution of time and ask yourself (or your teacher trainer) about
the appropriateness of such a distribution.

Second,  it  gives  you  a  framework  for  evaluating  and  improving  your  own
teaching.  For  example, how well  do  you  balance  teacher  talk  따id student  talk?
￦hile the FLINT model includes seven categories for teacher 때k 없id only two for
student  talk,  don’t  let  that  fool  you  into  believing  that  your  own  t때k should
dominate.  Depending on the objectives of the lesson, 야ie level of the students, and
other contextual factors, the proportions w피 vaη, but most of the time we teachers
tend to talk too much, without allowing enough time for students to respond to us
or to initiate talk.  A careful consideration of 외l seven of the teacher-talk categories

8a.  Student response,  choral:  Choral  response  by total  class  or part of class.

interaction.

This category  is  always combined with one of the categories from  1 to 9.

14.  Nonverbal:  Gestures  or  facial  expressions  by  the  teacher  or  the  student  that

of the  categories  of teacher or student behavior.

can  also  servε as  a  bluεp다nt for  your  tεaching behavior  in  the  classroom: Am  I

QUESTIONING STRATEGIES FOR INTERACTIVE LEARNING

Thε most important key to creating an interactive language classroom is the initiation
of interaction by thε teacher.  However nondirective your teaching style is, the onus
is  on  you  to  providε  the  stimuli  for  continued  intεraction.  Thεse  stimuli  ar'ε
important in thε initial  stagε of a classroom lesson as well as throughout the lesson.
Without  such  ongoing  teacher  guidance,  classroom  intεraction  may  indeed  be
communicative, but it can easily fall  pr1εy to tangential  chitchat and  other behavior
that is  off-course from the class objectiv,εs.

One  of the  best  ways  to  develop  your  role  as  an  initiator  and  sustainer  of
intεraction is  to develop a repertoire of questioning strategies.  In second language
classrooms, where learnεrs often do not have a great number of tools for initiating
and  maintaining  language,  your  questions  provide  nεcεssary stepping  stonεs to
communication.  Appropriate  questioning  in an  interactive  classroom  can fulfill  a
number of different functions  (adapted from  Christenbury &  Kelly,  1983; Kinsella,
1991).

1.  Teacher quεstions give  studεnts the impetus and opportunity to produce

language comfortably without having to risk initiating languagε themselves.
It’s very scary for students to have to initiate conversation or topics for
discussion.  Appropriately pitched quεstions can give more reticent students
an affective “grεen light" and a  structurεd opportunity to communicate in
thεir second language.

2.  Teacher questions can serve to initiatε a chain reaction of studεnt interaction

among themselves.  One question may be all that is  needed to staπ a
discussion; without the initial question, howev1εr, students will be reluctant to
initiatε the process.

3.  Teacher quεstions give the instructor immεdiate feedback about student com
prεhension.  After posing a question, a teacher can use the student rεsponse

CHAPTER  7 3

Initiating  lnteracti。n in  the  Classr。om

219

to diagnose linguistic or contεnt difficulties.  Grammatical or phonological
pro blεm areas, for εxamplε, may be exposed through the student’S  response
and give the tεacher some specific information about what to treat.

4.  Teacher quεstions provide students with opportunitiεs to find  out what they
think by hεaring what thεy say.  As  thεy ar1ε nudged into responding to ques
tions about, say, a reading or a film, they can discover what their own opin-
ions and rεactions are.  This sεlf갑iscovery can bε εspεcially useful for a
prεwriting activity.

Thεr℃ ar1ε  many  ways  to  classify  what  kinds  of questions  ar1ε  effectiv,ε  in  thε
classroom.  Pεrhaps thε simplεst way to concεptualize the possibilities is to think of
a  range  of  questions,  bεginning  with  display  questions  that  attεmpt  to  ℃licit
information already known by the teacher, all mε way to highly referential qu℃stions
that request information  not  known  by thε questionεr; sometimes  responsεs to  thε
latter involve judgmεnt about facts that ar,ε not clear or a  statement of valuεs.  Tabl℃
13.2  on  page  220  provides  seven  categories  of questions,  ran횡ng from  display  to
referential, with typical classroom question words associated with each categoπ.

All of these types of questions have their place in thε interactive classroom.  Even
those that are  mor1ε on thε display end of th℃ continuum ar,ε veπ us℃ful in eliciting
both content and  language  from  students.  Usually,  the  higher the  proficiency lεvεl
y。u tεach, them。rε y。u C없X venture into thε upper, re£εrential end of the εontinuum.
Onε intεresting study of high intermediate pr1ε-univ1℃rsity ESL  students (Brock, 1986)
found  that  teachers  who incorporated  mor1ε ref4εrential questions  into  their  classεs
stirnul. tεd student  responsεs that  wεrε longer  and  more  grammatically  complex.
Make  sure,  thεn,  that  you  challengε  your  students  sufficiεntly  but  without
OV1εrwhelming them.

Asking  a  lot  of quεstions in your classroom will  not by  any means guarantee
stimulation  of  interaction.  Cεrt a in  다pεs  of  questions  may  actually  discouragε
interactivε learning.  Bewarε of thε following  (adaptεd from  Kinsella, 1991):

•  Too  much  class  timε spεnt on display questions-students can easily grow
wεary of artificial contεxts that don’t  involv1ε genuine seeking of information.
•  A  question  that  insults  students’  intellig1εnce  by  being  so  obvious  that

students will think it’s  too silly to bother answering.

•  Vague  quεstions that  arε worded  in  abstract  or  ambiguous  language  (for

εxamplε, “ Do you pretty much understand more or less what to do?”).

•  Questions  stated  in  language  that  is  too  complex  or  too  wordy  for  aural

Table  13.2.  Categories of questions and  typical  classroom  question  words  (adapted from
Bloom,  1956;  Kinsella,  1991)

1.  Knowledge questions:  Eliciting factual  answers,  testing  recall  and  recognition  of

information.
Common  question  words:  define,  tell,  list,  identify,  describe,  select,  name,  point out,
label,  reproduce.  Who? What?  Where? When? Answer “yes ” or “no. ”

2.  Comprehension questions:  Interpreting,  extrapolating.

[ ommon question  words:  state in  your own words,  explain,  define,  locate,  select,
indicate,  summarize,  outline,  match‘

3.  Application questions: Applying  information  heard or  read  to  new  situations.

Common  question  words:  demonstrate how,  use the data  to  solve,  illustrate how,  show
how,  apply,  construct,  explain.  What i s_  used for?  What  would res비션 What would
happen?

4.  Inference questions:  Forming conclusions that are  not directly stated  in  instructional

materials.
Common question  words:  How?  Why?  What did_ mean by? What does_
believe? What conclusions can  you draw  from  ‘

‘  ?

·

5.  Analysis  questions:  Breaking down  into  parts,  relating parts to the whole.

[ ommon question  words:  distinguish,  diagram,  chart,  plan,  deduce,  arrange,  separate,
outline,  디assify,  c。ntrast, compare,  differentiate,  categorize‘  What is  the relationship
between? What is  the  function  of? What motive? What conclusions? What is  the main
idea?

6.  Synthesis questions: Combining elements  into  a  new  pattern.

Common  question  words:  compose,  combine,  estimate,  invent,  choose,  hypothesize,
build,  solve,  design,  develop.  What if?  How would you test? What  would you  have
done in  this  situation? What would happen if . .. ? How can  you  improve  ‘
else would you ... ?

.  ?How

‘

7.  Evaluation  questions:  Making a judgment of good  or bad,  right or wrong,  according to

some set of criteria,  and  stating why.
Common  question  words:  evaluate,  rate,  defend,  dispute,  decide  which,  select,  judge,
grade,  verify,  choose why‘  Which  is  best? Which  is  more important? Which  do  you
think is  more appropriate?

•  Too  many  rhetorical  questions  (that  you  intend  to  answer  yourself)  that
students think you want them to answer; they then get confused when you
supply the answer.

•  R없1domquεstions that don’t fall into a logical, well-planned sεquence, sending

studεnts’ thought pattεms into chaos.

ADVANTAGES OF GROUP WORK

What is group work? It is  a generic term cov1εring a multiplicity of techniques in
which two or more students are assignεd a task that involves collaboration and sεlε
initiated  language.  Note  that  what  we  commonly call  pair work  is  simply  group
work in groups of two.  It is also important to note that group work usually implies
“small"-group  work,  that  is,  students  in  groups  of  pεrhaps six  or  fεwεr.  Largε
groupings detiεat one of the major purposes for doing group work: giving  students
more opportunities to speak.

Group  work  is  solidly  groundεd in  resεarch principlεs (Dornyεi &  Murphεy,
2003; Long &  Porter, 1985; McDonough, 2004).  Consider thε 12 principles cited in
Chapter 4.  You  can think of other theor'εtical foundations  of successful language

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Gr。up Work

225

teaching and learning already discussed in this and other books on second language
learning and tεaching.  And consider thε importance of intεraction in the languagε
classroom discussεd in thε previous chapter.  An integration of these principles and
issues  yields  a  number  of advantages  of group  work  for  your  English  language
classroom.

1. Group work generates interactive language.

In so-called  tradi디on꾀 l따iguage classεs, teacher talk is  dominant.  Tεachers lecture,
explain grammar points, conduct drills, and at best lead whole-class discussions in which
each student mi양lt gεt a few seconds of a class period to talk.  Group work helps to solv;ε
the problem of classεs that  ar'ε too large  to offer m따iy opportuniti,εs to  spζ따ι By one
εstimate (Long & Port℃C 1985), if just half of your class 따nε wer℃ spεnt in group work, you
could increase individual practice time five-fold over who1ε-class traditional methodology.
Closely relatεd to the sheer quaηtity of output madε possible through group work
is  the variety and quality of interactiv;ε language.  With traditional mεthods, languag'ε
tends to be rεstricted to initiation only by thε teacher in an artificial setting where thε
whole  class  becomes  a “group  intεrlocutor.” Sm때l  groups  provide  opportunities  for
student initiation, for face-to-face  give and take, for practice in nεgotiation of mεaning,
for  extended conversational  exchangεs, and for  student adoption of rolεs that would
othεrwise be impossible.

2. Group work offers an embracing affective climate.

The  sεcond important  advantage  offered  by  group  work  is  thε security  of a
smaller group of students wher,ε each individual  is  not so  starkly on public  display,
vulnerable to what the student may pεrceiv1ε as  criticism and rεjεction.  In countless
observations  of  classes,  I  have  seen  the  magic  of  small  groups.  Quitε suddε띠y,
rεticent students bεcome vocal participants in the process.  Thε small group bεcomes
a  communiη of learners cooperating with each other in pursuit of common goals.

A  further  affective  bεnefit  of  small-group  work  is  an  increase  in  student
motivation.  With Maslow’s “sεcurity /safety” lεvel satisfiεd through the cohesiveness
of thε small group, lεarners ar,ε thus freed to pursue higher objεctives in their quεst
for success.

3. Group work promotes learner responsibility and autonomy.

Ev,εn in a relatively small class of 15 to 20 students, whole-class activiη oftεn 밍V℃S
students a screen to hide behind.  I remember a college French class I took in which
the teacher's single teaching technique was to call on students one by one to translatε
a sεntencε in our reading passagε of the day.  My way of playing that game was simply
to keep one sentence ahead of the teacher so that when my namε came up, I was ready.
I paid no attention to what was currently being translated, to the me없ling of the whole
passage, to commεnts by the teacher, or to fεllow classmates.  An 않treme case, to be
sur,ε!  But  even  in  lεss deadly  classroom  climates, students  can “relax”  too  much  in
whol,ε-class work. Group work places responsib피ty for action and pro1양ess upon each
of the mεmbers of the group somewhat equally.  It is d땀icult to “ hide” in a small group.

226

4. Group work is a  step toward individualizing instruction.

Each student in a classroom has needs and abilitiεs that are unique.  Usually the
most salient individual  diftiεrεncε that you  observ,ε is  a  rangε of proficiency levels
across  your class  and, even  more  specifically, differences  among  students  in  their
speaking,  listεning, writing, and  reading  abilities.  Small  groups  can  hεIp studεnts
with varying abilities to accomplish separate goals.  The tεacher can rεcognize and
capitalize  upon  other  individual  differences  (age,  cultural  heritage, fiεId of study,
cognitive  style,  to  name  a  few)  by  careful  sεlεction  of  small  groups  and  by
administεring different tasks to  differ'εnt groups.

EXCUSES  FOR AVOIDING GROUP WORK

Some  teachers  are  afraid  of group work.  They feel  they’11  losε control  or students
will  just  use  their  native  language,  and  so  they  shy  away  from  it.  Somε of these
apprehensions  arε  understandable;  group  work  does  not  mean  simply  putting
students into groups and having thεm do what you would otherwise do as  a whole
class.  But the limitations or drawbacks to group work arε all surmountable obstacles
when  group  work  is  used  appropriately-that  is,  for  objεctives that  clearly  lend
themselves to group work.  Let’s look at these limitations-or “ myths;’ pεrhaps-and
try to understand how to deal with them.

Excuse 1: The teacher is no longer in control of the class.

Now,  you  may  be  thinking, “Well,  I  don’t  mind  giving  control  ovεr to  the
students.” But, depending on the context of your teaching, control could be a  veη7
impoπant issue.  If you ar,ε

•  teaching in an institution wherε the administrator in charge requires that you

teach through a traditional, whole-class methodology,

•  teaching  in  a  culture  where “ good  teaching” is  defined  as  studεnts quietly
working in orderly fashion, speaking only when spoken to by the teach얀
•  tεaching veπ large classes (of 75  or mor'ε) where a plethora of small groups

becomes difficult to manage,

•  teaching  a  group  of unruly  students-possibly of secondary  school  age

where discipline is a major issue,

•  yourself a  nonnativ1ε speaker of English without the confidence to “ let your

students go” in small groups,

then control may  be an  issue.  There is  no doubt  that group  work  rεquires some
yielding  of  control  to  the  students.  In  numerous  cultures,  studεnts are  indeed
primed to be under the complete control and authority of the  teacher, and group
work therefore is a veηr strange activity to engage in.  In such contεxts the teacher
must be veηr clever to orchestrate successful small-group work.

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Group  Work

227

But  this  is  still  a  “drawback” rather  than  a  reason  to  avoid  group  work.  By
quietly  introducing  small  doses  of  group  work  into  your  otherwisε traditional
classroom,  you  may  bε  ablε  to  convincε  administrators  and  students  of  thε
advantagεs.  With careful attεntion to guidelines for implεmentation of group work,
administrative or managerial  dilεmmas should be avoidablε.  And if you arε unsure
of your own English  languagε abiliη, take heart in thε fact  that you are  still quite a
few steps ahead of your students.

As  wε noted  εarlier  in  Chapter  13,  if control  is  thought  of  as  predicting
εverything that is  going to transpire in a class hour, then you do not want “ control”
bεcause you  w피 be  thwarting  viπually all  possibility  of an  interactiv,ε  languagε
classroom.  Group  work  still  allows  you  to  play  the  roles  of  dirεctor, manager,
facilitator, and rεsource.  In those roles, thεre is  still an adequate degree of control;
the class will not necessarily run away with you.

Excuse 2:  Students will use their native language.

In  ESL  sεttings wher,ε a multiple number of l없iguages are often represεnted in
a  singlε classroom,  teachers  can  avoid  the  native  language  syndrome  by  placing
students in  heterog℃nζous languagε groups.  But  in  EFL  situations, whεre all  of thε
students hav1ε a  common native  language, it  is  indeεd possible, if not probable, that
students in small groups w피 covertly use their native language.  In fact, this is usually
the primaηr reason tεachers give  me for shying away from group work.  How can it
bε overcome?

Judicious following of guidelines for implementation (discussεd in the following
sεction) will  help.  If studεnts feel  that  the  task  is  too  hard  (or  too  easy),  or that
directions arε not clear, or that the task is  not inter,εsting, or that they are not sure of
the purpose of the task, thεn you may bε inviting students to take shortcuts via their
nativε language.  The most important factor, however, is sεtting thε climatε for group
work.  Herε ar'ε some suggestions:

•  Imprεss  upon  your  students  thε  importance  of practice  in  the  sεcond
language  for  eventual  success.  Make  sure-in whatεvεr way you  se℃ fit
that  they  clearly  understand  that  successful  learners  consistently  practice
using the target language  in face-to-face  contexts.

•  Appεal to various  motivational factors  affecting  thεm so that they can sεε
some  real  usεs for  English  in  their  own  lives.  Try  to  hone  in  on  their
intrinsic motivation to learn.

•  Dεmonstrate how  eηyoyable the  various  small-group  tasks  and  games  and
activities  are.  Careful  sεlection and administration of group activities  helps
to ensure such plεasurε.  Your own overt display of εnthusiasm w퍼 help to
set a  tonε.

•  Inform  thεm of the security offered by thε smaller groups.  Get the groups
to  think  of  themselves  as  teams,  the  members  of which  are  all  working
togεth er.  Rεmind thεm that,  in  thε  process,  they  can  tηr  out  languagε

228

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  πirough Group Work

without  feeling  that  the  whole  class  (and  the  teacher!)  is  watching  and
cnt1c1zmg.

•  For students who argue that the only reason they are in your class is to pass
an examination, remind them that research has shown that people do better
on tests if they dive into the language itself rather than just study test items.
If they  can  be  convinced  that  small  groups  help  to  build  their  intuitions
about language, they may also  understand that those intuitions will  be their
ally in a test situation.

ExαISe 3: Students' errors will be reinforced in small groups.

Teachers are usually concerned about the fact  that, especially in large classes,
students  will  simply  reinforce  each  other's  eπors and  the  teacher  won’t  get  a
chance  to  correct  them.  This  concern  can  really  be  laid  to  rest.  There  is  now
enou~ψ research on errors and error correction (Long & Porter, 1985; McDonou행,
2004) to tell us that (a) levels of accuracy maintained in unsupervised groups are as
hi앙1 as those in teacher-monitored whole-class work, and that (b) as  much as you
would like not to believe it, teachers' overt attempts to correct speech errors in the
classroom have a negligible effect on students' subsequent performance.  Errors are
a “necessaπ” manifestation of inter-language  development, and we do well not to
become  obsessed  with  their  constant  corr,εction.  Moreover, well-managed  group
work can encourage spont없1eous peer feedback on errors within the small group
itself.

Excuse 4: Teachers cannot monitor 때I groups at once.

Related to the issue of control is the sometimes misguided belief that a teacher
should  be  “in  on”  everyπhing  a  student  says  or  does  during  the  class  hour.
Interactive  learning  없1d  teaching  principles  counter  with  the  importance  of
meaningful, purposeful language and real communication, which in turn must allow
the  student  to  밍ve vent  to  creative  possibilities.  Yes,  the  effective  teacher  will
circulate among the groups, listen to students, and offer suggestions and criticisms.
But it is simply not necessary-for reasons cited in #3  above-to be a  party to all
linguistic intercourse in the classroom.

Excuse 5: Some learners prefer to work alone.

It is true that many students, especially adult-age students, prefer to work alone
because that is the way they have operated ever since they started going to school.
As  a  successful  manager  of  group  work,  you  need  to  be  sensitive  to  such
preferences, acknowledging that some if not m없1y of your students will fmd group
work frustrating because they may simply want you just to give them the answers
to  some  problem  and  then  move  on.  Help  your  students  to  see  that  language
learning is not a skill where you can simply bone up on rules and words in isolation.
Language  is  for  communicating  with  people  (whether  through  oral  or  written
modes), and the  more  they engage in such face-to-face  communication, the more
their overall communicative competence will improve.

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction Through  Croup Work

229

Related to the work style  issue are numerous other learning style variations
없nong students  that  are  magnified  in  small  groups.  Because  the  teacher  isn’t
present  within  the  group  at  all  times,  groups  are  often  left  to  derive  their  own
dynamic  inductively.  In  the  process,  individual  differences  become  more  salient
than they are in whole『class work.  Below are several possible scenarios:

•  A highly  left-brain-oriented student is  put off by the  otherwise more right

brain mεmbers of the group.

•  Quicker (impulsive) thinkers tend to blurt out their ideas, overwhelming the

slower (reflective) thinkεrs.

•  Impulsive learners get easily frustrated with the group process, which they

pεrceive as circuitous.

•  Competitive  members  of a  group  are  reluctant  to  share  information  with

others.

•  “Talkative” students dominatε the process.

While such problems can and do occur in group work, virtually every problem
that is  rooted  in learning style  differences  can be solved  by careful planning  and
management.  In fact, when the group  members  know their task  and  know their
roles  in  the group, learning style  differences  can be efficiently utilized  and highly
appreciated-much more so than in whole-class work.

IMPLEMENTING GROUP WORK IN YOUR CLASSROOM

As  you saw in the scene that opens this chapter, group work can go wrong if it is
not carefully planned, well executed, monitored throughout, and followed up on in
some way.  We’ll  now look at practical steps to take to carry out successful group
work in your classroom.

Evaluating Classroom Langt뼈ge

One of the first considerations in implementing group work is to 잃cert따n that your
students have an appropriate command of classroom language with which to carπ
out the group  task  that you have  in  mind.  Now,  some group work  is  linguistically
quite simple, and correctly so for lower proficiency levels.  But at somewhat hi양ier
levels, we teachers  love  to  direct our students  to  dis,α않 some  p았ticular point or
question, with no further explanation, assuming that our students know exactly what
we mean by the word.  When you say to your class,“Okay, everyone, get into pairs and
discuss  these  ideas  from  the  lecturette  you  just  heard,"  what  does  that  mean  to
students?

In order to make sure that a group task is accurately understood by students,
not only are clear directions (see below) important, but students must be able to

230

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Croup Work

carπ out the  discourse  necessaη to  accomplish  the  task.  This  means  that  prior
(eithεr  in  the  same  lesson  or  in  previous  lessons)  to  the  task,  studεnts  hav,ε
performed  the  various  bits  and  pieces  of  classroom  languagε  that  thε  task
presupposes.  Consider the following excerpt from Sarosy and Sherak (2006, p. 54)
that rεviews expressions for agreeing and disagreeing:

To agree with others:
I agree with_ ,
That’S  a good point, ...
I agree with ----’s point,.
S/He’s right,.
I think _

has the right idea, ...

To disagree with others:
I’m  afraid I don’t agree,.
I’m  sorry, but I have to disagree, .
I disagree with _
No, I don’t think that’s true,.
I see your point, but ‘  - -
Perhaps you’re right about _

, ...

, but I can’t agree with you on ...

There are other forms of classroom language that can make a difference in thε
success of a group task.  Consider thε following list (adapted from Sarosy &  Sherak,
2006):

Agreeing and disagreeing (“I see your point, but ... ")
Interrupting another student (“.  .Excuse me .. -”)
Asking for clarification (“Sorry, what did you mean by ----?”)
Asking for more information (“Could you εlaborate, plεase? ”)
Supporting your opinion (“Let me tell you why .. /’)
Coming to a consensus (“Would you 때l agree to ... ?”)
Going over exercises with a partner (“Oh, I wrote something different.”)
Gi꺼ng oral feedback to peers’ written work (“Have you thou행t about ... ?”)

The  important  point  here  is  ηot simply  to  assume  your  students  have  the
necessaηr competence to perform the discourse required of a  group task, without
m와ting sure those abilities are there.  Once the necessaπ classroom language is in
place, thε task can proceed to its aims mor'ε εfficiently.

Selecting Appropriate Group Techniques

So far in this chapter, as your attention has been focusεd on group work, differences
betweεn pair work and group work have not been emphasized.  There are, in fact,

[ HAPTER 14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Croup Work

231

some  important  distinctions.  Pair  work  is  more  appropriate  than group work for
tasks  that  arε  (a)  short,  (b)  linguistically  simple,  and  (c)  quite  controlled
in  tεrms of  thε structure  of  thε task.  Appropriate  pair  activities  (that  ariε  not
r℃commended for groups of morε than two) include:

1.  practicing dialogues with a  partnεr
2.  simple  quεstion-and-answer exercisεs
3.  pεrforming certain meaningful substitution “dr버S”
4.  quick (one minute or lεss) brainstorming activities
5.  checking written work with each othεr
6.  preparation for merging with a  largεr group
7.  any brief activity for which the logistics of assigning groups, moving furniture,

and getting  studεnts into thε groups is  too distracting

Pair work  ℃nables you  to  εngagε studεnts in  interactive (or quasi-interactive)
communication for a  short period of time with a minimum of logistical  problεms.
But don’t  misunderstand the  rolε of pair work.  It is  not to be used exclusively for
the above  typ℃S of activity; it is  also appropriate for  many group work tasks (listed
below).

The  first  step  in  promoting  succεssful  group  work,  thεn,  is  to  select  an
appropriate task.  In other words. choose something that  lεnds itself to the group
process.  Lectures, drills, dictations, certain listening tasks, silent rεading, and a host
of other activities are obviously not suitable for small-group work.

Typical  group  tasks  are  dεfined and  briεfly charactεrizεd below.  For further
exam pl℃S and  information, I  highly  recommend  that you  consult  a  few  of a  wide
variety of t℃acher resource books that offer a multitude of tasks for you to consider.
(Some arε listed at the εnd of this chapter.)

1. Games

A game could be any activity that formalizes a techniqu℃ into units that can be
scored in  some  way.  Several  of the  othεr group  tasks  outlined  below could thus
become  “games.”  Guessing  games  are  common  language  classroom  activities.
Twenty Questions, for  example, is  ℃asily adapted  to  a  small  group.  One  mεmber
secretly decides that he or she is some famous person; the rest of the group has to
find  out who, within  20 yes/no  questions, with each mεmber of thε group  ta힘ng
turns asking questions.  Thε person who is “ it” rotates around the group and points
ar'ε scored.

2. Role plays and simulations

Role  plays minimally  involv1ε (a)  giving a  role  to one or more  members of a
group and (b) assigning an objεctive or purpose that participants must accomplish.
In pairs, for ε:xample, student A is an employer; student B is a prospectiv1ε employeε;
the objective is for A to interviεw B.  In groups, similar dual roles could be assumεd
with  assignmεnts to  others  in  thε  group  to  watch  for  cεrtain  grammatical  or

232

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction Through  Group Work

discourse elements as the roles are acted out.  Or a group role play mi양it involve a
discussion of a  political issue, with each person assigned to represent a  particular
political point of view.

Simulations usually involve a more complex structure and often larger groups
(of 6  to  20) where the entire group is wor:따ng through an imaginary situation as a
social unit, the object of which is to solve some specific problem.  A common genre
of simulation games specifies that 따l members of the group are shipwrecked on a
“ desert  island.” Each  person has  been  assigned  an  occupation  (doctor, carpenter,
garbage  collector, etc.)  and  perhaps  some  other mitigating  characteristics  (has  a
physical disability, is  an ex-convict, is  a prostitute, etc.).  Only a  specified subset of
the group can survive on the remaining food supply, so the group must decide who
will live and who will die.

3. Drama

Drama is a more formalized form of role play or simulation, with a preplanned
story  line  and  script.  Sometimes  small  groups  may  prepare  their  own  short
dramatization of some event, writing the script and rehearsing the scene as a group.
This may be more commonly refeπed to 싫 a “skit.” Longer, more involved dramatic
performances have been shown to have positive effects on I없iguage learning, but
they are time consuming and rarely can form part of a typical school curriculum.

4. Projects

For  learnεrs of all  ages, but perhaps  espe띠lly for younger learners who  can
greatly  benefit  from  hands-on  approaches  to  language,  cert따n projects  can  be
rewarding indeed.  If you were to adopt an environmental awareness theme in your
class, for example, various small grou야 could each be doing different things: Group
A creates an environmental bulletin board for the rest of the school; group B develops
fact sheets; group C makes a three깅imensional display; group D puts out a newslεtter
for the rest of the school; group E develops a skit, and so on.  As learners get absorbed
in P따poseful projects, both receptive and productive language is used meaningfully.

5. Interviews

A pop띠ar activity for p없r work, but also appropriate for group work, interviews
are  useful  at  외l  levels  of proficiency.  At  the  lower levels, interviews  C없1 be  veπ
structured,  both  in  terms  of the  information  that  is  sou양it  and  the  grammatical
difficulty and variety.  The goal of an interview could at this level be limited to using
reques디ng functions,  learning  vocabulary for  expressing  person따 data,  producing
questions, etc.  Students might ask each other questions like

What’S your name?
Where do you live?
What counttγ (city) are you from?

and learn to give appropriate responses.  At the higher levels, interviews can probe
more complex facts, opinions, ideas, and feelings.

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction Through  Group Work

233

6.  Brainstorming

Brainstorming is a technique whose p따pose is to initiate some sort of thinking
process.  It gets students' “creative juices” flowing  without necessarily focusing on
specific  problems or decisions  or values.  Brainstorming  is  often  put  to  excellent
use in preparing studεnts to read a text, to discuss a complex issue, or to write on a
topic.  Brainstorming  involves  students  in  a  rapid-fire,  free-association  listing  of
concepts or ideas or facts or feelings  rεIevant to some topic or context.

Suppose you were about to read a  passagε on future  means of transportation.
You might ask small groups to brainstorm (a) different forms of transportation, past
and present, and  (b)  current obstacles  to  more  efficient  means  of transportation.
The groups’ task would be to make a composite list of everything they can think of
within  the  categoη, without evaluating  it.  In brainstorming, no discussion of the
relative merits of a thought takes place; eve11πhing and anything goes.  π1.is way, all
ideas  are  legitimate, and students  are  released  to  soar the  heights and plumb the
depths, as  it  wer,ε, with no obligation to dεfεnd a  concept.  In whatever follow-up
to brainstorming you plan, at that point evaluation and discussion can take place.

7. Information gap

These last  four types of techniques are  quite  commonly used in adult classes

around the world, up and down the proficiency continuum.

Information-gap activities include a tr,εmεndous variety of techniques in which
the objective is  to convey or to request information.  Thε two focal  characteristics
of information-gap techniques are (a) their primary attention to information and not
to language forms  and (b) the  necessity of communicative  interaction in order to
reach the objεctive.  The information that students must seek can range from very
simple to complεx.

Atthε beginning level, for  example, each  member of a  small  group  could be
given  the objective of finding  out from  the  others their  birthday, address, favorite
food, etc., and filling  in a little chart with the information.  In intermediate classes
you could ask groups to collectively pool information about different occupations:
necessary qualifications, how long it takes to prepare for an occupation, how much
the preparation costs, what typical job conditions are, what salary lεvels are, etc.  In
advanced  classes,  a  small-group  discussion  on  dεter뼈ning 없1  author's  message,
among many other possibilities, would be an information-gap technique.

8. Jigsaw activities

Jigsaw techniques are a sped때 form of information gap in whlch each member
of a group is given some specific information and the go외 is to pool 따l information
to achleve some objective.  Ima횡ne four members of a group each with a [fictitious]
application form, and on each form  different  information is  pro찌ded.  As  students
ask each other questions (without showing anyone their own application form), they
eventually complete 때l the information on the form.  Or you mi양it provide maps to
students in sm때l groups, each student receiving different sets of information (where
the bank is, whel'ε the park is, etc.).  πie goal for be횡nners might be simply to locate

234

CHAPTER  14

Sus떠ining Interaction Through  Croup Work

eveηt피ng correctly, and for  intermediatε learners to give directions on how to get
from  one  place  on  the  map  to  another,  requiring  a  collaborative  exchange  of
information in order to provide complete directions.

Oneveπ popular jigsaw technique that can be used in larger groups is known
as  a  “strip  stoπ.” The  teacher  takes  a  moderately  short  written  narrative  or
conversation and cuts each sentence of the text into a little strip, shuffles the strips,
and gives each student a strip.  The goal is for students to determine where each of
their sentences belongs in the whole context of the stoη, to stand in their position
once it is  detεrmined, and to read off the reconstructed storγ.  Students enjoy this
technique and almost always find it challenging.

9. Problem solving and decision making

Problem-solving group techniques focus  on the group’S  solution of a specified
problem.  They mi쌀it or might not involve  jigsaw characteristics, and the problem
i않elf mi양it  be  relatively  simple  (such  as  giving  directions  on  a  map), moderat태
complex (such as working out an itineraπ from train, plane, and bus schedules), or
q띠te complex (such as s이찌ng a mystery in a “crime storγ” or dealing with a political
or  moral  dilemma).  Once  again,  problem-solving  techniques  center  students'
attention  on me따파igful cognitive  challenges  and not so  much on grammatical  or
phonolo핑cal forms.

Decision-making techniques are simply one kind of problem solving where the
ultimate  go떼 is  for  students  to  make  a  decision.  Some  of  the  problem-solving
techniques  alludεd to  above  (say,  giving  directions  to  someone  and  solving  a
mystery)  don’t  involve  a  decision  about  what  to  do.  Other  problem-solving
techniques do involve such decisions.  For example, students presented with several
profiles of applicants for a  job may be asked to decide who they would hire.  The
“ desert island” simulation game referred to earlier involves a decision.  Or a debate
on environmental hazards might reveal several possible causes of air pollution, but
if decision making is the goal, then the group would have to decide now what they
would actually do to reduce to회ns in our 없I

10. Opinion exchange

An opinion is usually a belief or fee파ig that might not be founded on empirical
data or what others could plausibly take issue with.  Opinions are difficult for students
to  deal  with at  the  be횡nning levels  of proficien이, but by the  intermediate  level,
certain techniques can effectively include the  exch없ige of V따ious opinions.  Many
of  the  above  techniques  can  easily  incorporate  beliefs  and  feelings.  Sometimes
opinions are appropriate; sometimes they 따e not, especially when the objective of a
task is to deal more with “facts.”

Moral, ethical, religious, and political issues are usually “hot” items for classroom
debates,  a탱uments, and  discussions.  Students  can  get  involved  in  the  content
centered  nature  of  such  acti꺼ty  and  thus  pave  the  way  for  more  automatic,
peripheral processing of language itself.  Issues for discussion include

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Group Work

235

•  women’s rights
•  factors in choosing a marriage partner
•  cultural taboos
•  sexual orientation
•  political candidates and their stands
•  abortion
•  euthanasia
•  environmεntal crises (air, water, for·εsts, atmosphere, oceans)
•  war and peace

One warning: You play an important and sensitiv1ε role when you ask students
to discuss  their beliefs.  Some  beliefs are  deeply ingrained from childhood rεaring
or from  religious  training, among  other factors.  So,  it  is  easy  for  a  studεnt to  be
offended by what another student says.  In such exchanges, do eveηπhing you can
to assure everyone in your class that, while there may be disagreement on issues, 외l
opinions are to be valued, not scorned, and respected, not ridiculed.

Planning Group Work

Possibly  the  most  common  reason  for  the  breakdown  of  group  work  is  an
inadequate  introduction and  lead-in  to  the task  itself.  Too  often, teachεrs assume
that 야rposes ar'ε clear and directions are  understood, and then hav'ζ to  spεnd an
inordinate  amount  of  time  clarifying  and  redirecting  groups.  Once  you  have
selected  an  appropriate  type  of activity, your  planning  phase  should  include  the
following seven “rulεs” for implementing a group technique.

1.  Introduce the technique.  The introduction may simply be a  briεf

explanation.  For εxample, “ Now, in groups of four, you’re each going to gεt
diff;εrent transportation schedules (airport limo, airplane, train, and bus), and
your job is to figure out, as a group, which combination of transportation
services will takε the least amount of time.'’  The introduction almost always
should includε a statement of the ultimate p따pose so that students can apply
all other directions to that objective.

2.  Just펴r the use of small groups for the technique.  You may not neεd to
do this all the time with all your classεs, but if you think your studεnts have
any doubts about the significance of the upcoming task, then tell them
explicitly why the small group is  import따it for accomplishing the task.
Remind them that they will get an opportunity to practice ceπain language
forms or functions, and that if they are reluctant to speak up in front of the
whole class, now is their chance to do so in the security of a small group.
3.  Model the technique.  In simple techniques, especi따ly those that your

students have done before, modeling may not be necessaη.  But fora new
없id potentially complex task, it never hurts to be too explicit in making sure
studεnts know what they are supposed to do.  After students get into their

236

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Group Work

groups, you might, for example, show them (possibly on an overhead
projector) four transportation schedules (not the ones they will sεe in their
groups).  까1en select four students to simulate a discussion of meshing arrival
and departure times; your guidance of their discussion will help.

4.  Give explicit detailed instructions.  Now that students have seen the

purpose of the task and have had a chanc℃ to witness how their discussion
might proceed, give them spe삶ic instructions on what they are to do.
Include

•  a restatement of the purpose,
•  rules they are to follow (e.g., Don’t  show your schedule to anyone else in
your group.  Use “표” clauses as in “If I leave at 6:45 A.M., I will arrive at the
airport at 7:25.”),

•  a time frame (e.g., You  have  10 minutes to complete the task.),
•  assignment of roles (κ any) to  studεnts (e.g.,π1e 없rport limo  person for
each group is the “chair.” The airplane pεrson w피 present your findings to
the rεst of the class.  The train person is the timekeeper, etc.).

5.  Divide the class into groups.  This element is not as εasy as it sounds.  In
some cases you can simply number off (e.g., 1,2,3,4, ... ) and speci다r which
area of the room to occupy.  But to ensure participation or control you may
want to preassign groups in order to account for one or two of the following:

•  native language (especially in ESL classes with varied native languag'ε

backgrounds)
•  proficiency levels
•  age or gender differences
•  cultural or subcultural group
•  personality types
•  cognitive style preferences
•  cognitive/developmental stages (for children)
•  mterests
•  prior learning experience
•  target language goals

In classes of fewer th따1 30 people, preassigning groups is quite manageable if
you come to class with the preassignments, having thought through the vari
ables that you W따1t to control.  Just put the group names up on the chalk-
board 따1d tell people to get into their groups.

6.  Check for clarification.  Before students start moving into their groups,

check to make sure they all understand their assignment.  Do not do this by

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Group  w。rk

237

asking, “Does evεπonε understand? ”* Rathεr, tεst out certain elements of your
lεad-in by asking quεstions like, “ Keiko, please restate thε purpose of this

activiη.”

7.  Set the task in motion.  This part should now be a simple matter of saying
something like, “ Okay, gεt into your groups and get started right away on your
task‘” Somε facilitation  may be necessaπ to ensur,ε smooth logistics.

Monitoring the Task

Your job now becomεs onε of facilitator and rεsource.  To  carπ out your rolε, you
need to tread thε finε lin℃ betweεn inhibiting the group process and being a helpεr
or guide.  The  first  few  times  you  do  group  work, you  may  nεεd to  εstablish this
sensitive role, lεtting studεnts know you will be availablε for help and that you may
make a suggestion or two hεre and there to keep them on task, but that thεy arε to
carη out the task on their own.  There may actually be a fεw momεnts at thε outsεt
where  you  do  not  circulate  among  the  groups  so  that  they can  εstablish a  bit  of
momentum.  The rest of the time it is vεηr important to circulate so that, even if you
havε nothing  to  say  to  a  group, you  can  listen  to  students  and  get  a  sεnsε of the
groups’ pro gr℃ss and of individuals’ language production.

A fεw doη ’ts.’
•  Don't sit at your dεsk and grade papers.
•  Don’I  lεave the room and takε a break.
•  Don ’I  spεnd an  undue  amount  of time  with  onε group  at  thε expεnsε of

othεrs.

•  Don't correct studεnts ’ errors unlεss asked to do so.
•  Don't assumε a dominating or disruptive rolε while monitoring groups.

Debriefing

Almost all group work can bε brou힐1t to a beneficial close by somε sortofwholε-class
debriεfmg, once the group task is  completεd.  This debriefmg, or “processing,” as some
would refiεr to it, has two layers:

*Teachers arε oftεn tεmpted to assume that asking a blanket question like this
provides an informal assessmεnt of how well students comprehended something.
Usually, whether studεnts understood or not, a small minority of thεm will nod their
heads affirmatively while the rest of the class shows no response.  The few nodding
heads must not be takεn as a measure of comprehension by all.  It is  better, therefore,
never (or rarely) to say such things as “ Does everyone understand?” because it can lead
to a falsε sεnse of satisfaction on the part of thε teachεr.

238

CHAPTER  14  Sustaining Interaction  Through  Croup Work

1.  Reporting on task objectives.  If groups were assigned a rεportεr to

present something to the class, or if thε task implicitly lends itself to some
discussion of thε “ findings ” of the groups, th℃n make sure that you leav,ε
enough time for this to take placε.  As  repoπεrs or representatives of εach
group bring their findings, you may εntertain some brief discussion, but be
sure not to let that discussion steal time from other groups.  This whole-class
process gives each group a chancε to pεrc℃ivε differ,εnces and similarities in
their work.  Some group work involves different assignments to different
groups, and in these cases the reporting phasε is  interesting to all  and
provides motivation for furthεr group work.

2.  Establishing affective support.  A dεbriefing phase also sεrves the purpose
of exploring the group procεss itself and of bringing thε class back togεther
as  a whole community of learnεrs.  If you or some studεnts hav,ε questions
about how smoothly the task procεεded, how comfortablε pεoplε were with
a topic or task, or problems they encountered in reaching their objεctive, now
is  an ℃XCεllent time to encouragε somε whole-class fεedback.  This gives you
feedback for your next group work assignment.  Ultimatεly, even a V℃ry short
pεriod of whole-class discussion reminds students that eveπon℃ in thε room
is  a member of a team of learners and that the groups, εspecially if any
intεrgroup competition arosε, are but temporary artifacts of classroom
learning.

It’s  possible  that  this  chaptεr on  group  work  has  beεn so  εxplicit  in  its

work in your classroom.  If so, that neεd not bε the case! All  of the guidelines and

“ right” things.  In the meantime, just remember that conscientious attention to what

TOPICS FOR DISCUSSION, ACTION, AND  RESEARCH

[Note: (I) Individual work; (G) group or pair work; (C) whole-class discussion.]

Is teaching 따I art or a science? Are teachers born or madε? Is the learning-teaching
connection  poetic  or predictable? These  questions  are  commonly found  swirling
about in the minds of educators, not so much as “either-or” questions but rather as
“ both-없id” questions.  I think you C따1 easily agree that teaching is both an art and a
science, that some innate ability complements learned teaching skills, and that with
all  of our best-laid lesson  plans  there  st피 remains an  intan!김ble aura surrounding
acts of learning.  But how do the two traditions coexist in practice? How do art and
science  min헝C in the principles and approaches and techniques and plans of ESL
teachers?

In  the  pre찌ous chapters in  this section of Teacbiηg by Prineψles,  you have
considered  course  and  lesson  design, materials  따id technological  aids,  따id some
guidelines  for  creating  interaction  in  your  methodology.  The  next  step  in  a
succession of practic때ities for the language classroom is to grapple with what we
call  classroom  management,  which  encompasses  an  abundance  of  factors
ran!잊ng from how you physically 따range the classroom, to teacliing “styles," to one
of my  favorite  themes:  classroom  ene멍y.  By  understanding  what  some  of  the
variables  are  in  classroom  m따iagement, you  can  take  some  important  steps  to
sharpening  your  s때lls  as  a  l따iguage teacher.  And  then, as  you  improve  some  of
those identifiable, overtly observable s힘lls, you open the door to the intangible-to
앙t, to poetics, to the invisible sp따ks ofene멍Y that kindle the flames of learr피ig.

YOUR VOICE AND BODY LANGUAGE

Another fundamεntal classroom management coneεrn has to do with you and the
