Onε of the first  requirεments of good teaching is  good voice projection.  You

students in the room.  When you talk, project your voice so that the person sitting
farthest  away  from  you  can  hear you  clearly.  If you  are  dir1εcting comments to  a
student in the first  row sitting right in front of you, remember that in whole-class
work, all  the  rest  of the students  need  to  be  able  to  hear that  comment.  As  you
speak,  articulate  clearly;  remεmber, these  students  are  learning  English,  and  they
need every advantag1ε thεy can get.

Should  you  slow  down  your  normal  ratε  of  deliveη? For  beginning  lεvel
classεs, yes,  but  only  slightly  so, and  not  to  thε point  that  the  rate  of deliv1℃ry is
downright  silly.  Kεεp  as  natural  a  flow  to  your  language  as  possible.  Clεar
articulation is usually more of a kεy to comprehension than slowεd speech.

•  Make frequent eye contact with all  students in the class.
•  Do not “ bury yourself" in your notes and plans.

•  Move around the classroom, but not to distraction.
•  Follow  th℃  conv,εntional  rules  of  proxemics  (distance)  and  kinesthεtics

(touching) that apply for the cultur1ε(s) of your students.

•  Dress appropriately, considering  thε expectations of your students and thε

culture in which you are tεaching.

•  you digress and throw off the plan for thε day,

TEACHING UNDER ADVERSE CIRCUMSTANCES

Under  the  catego1γ of “adverse  circumstances”  are  a  number  of  managεmεnt

1.  Teacl피ng large classes

interaction and small enough to give students plenty of opportunity to participatε and

•  Proficiency and ab피tyvaπ widely among students.

Somε solutions  to  thεsε pro blεms ar'ε  availablε.  Considεr the  following  that

a.  Try to makε each student fεεl important (and not just a “ number”) by

g.  Don’t  collεct writtεn work from all of your students at the samε time;

h.  Sεt up small “ learning centers” in your class whεre students can do

i.  Organizε informal conv'εrsation groups and study groups.

2.  Teaching multiple proficiency levels in the same class

Thεre is  often a  wide rangε of proficiεncy l℃V℃ls among students in the same

of students who in your estimation should not all  be placed at the same level.  In

middle group well paced toward their goals.  Most of the time, the phenomenon of
widεly ranging competεncies in your class is a by-product of institutional placement
procedures  and  budgetary  limits,  so  there  is  little  you  can  do  to “kick  out”  thε
studεnts  at  either  extr'εme.  So,  how  do  you  dεal  with  this?  Hεre  are  somε
SU잃:estions to consid℃r:

[ HAPTER 7 5  Classroom Management

247

a.  Do not overgenεralizε your assessment of students' proficiency lev1℃ls by
blanket classifications into “the good studεnts'’ and “the bad students.” It is
a  common mistake among teachεrs (we all do it!) to talk about smart and
dumb students in our classes.  We  must be veπ sensitiv1ε to the issuε of
proficiency vεrsus ability.  In a set of skills as complex as language, it is
often difficult to determine whethεr a  studεnt ’S performance is a factor of
aptitudε, ability, a “ knack," or a factor of timε and ℃ffort.

b.  For most students, competεncies will vary among the fo따 skills, within

εach skill (e.g., in rεading, lexical  knowlεdge, meaning-seeking strategies,
speed, efficiεn다, εtc.), and by context.  As  much as possible, identify thε
specific skills and abiliti℃S of each studεnt in your class so that you can

and exεrcises and day-by깅ay monitoring of students, you may bε able to

those.

c.  Offer choicεs in individual (written and extra-class) techniques that vaη

according to needs and challenges.  In doing so, sensitiv1εly conv1εy to your
students that thεy all have challenges and goals to pursue 없id that if some
students sεem to be “ ahead” of othεrs, it  is  no doubt duε to pr,εvious
instruction, exposure, and motivation (see item [a]  above).

d.  Take advantage of whatever learning centers or tutorial laboratories that
may be available in your institution.  All  proficiency levels can benefit
from laboratories that providε computer softwarε for rεviεw and practice,
or trained tutors that can diagnosε nεeds and suggest avenues of further
work.  Students at higher lεvels and lower levels of proficienα can
thereby bε challenged to mεet thεir nεεds.

ε Obviously, thε Iεnor of your classroom tεacher talk (instructions,

εxplanations, lεctur1εs, etc.) w피 ne℃d to bε gauged toward the middle of
thε lεvels of proficiency in your class.  But group work tasks offer
opportunitiεs for you to solv1ε multiplε-proficiζncy issues.  Sometimes you
can place students of varying ranges in the same group, and at other times

3.  Using “En힐ish only” in the classroom?

In  language-teaching  circles  across  the  globe,  there  is  sometimes  an
und℃rcurrent of opinion that claims that  onε should nev,℃r use  native  languages  in
the classroom.  Such convictions may stem from a bygone εm when methods like the
Direct Method εndorsed “target language only” practices in the classroom, 따id from
a reaction to GrammarTranslation methodology that often saw little use at 와l of the
targ1εt language.  It is  now  clear from  resεarch and  ℃xperience that “암iglish only”
prohibitions go too far (Atkinson, 1987; Harmer, 2001; Ur, 1996).

On the othεr hand, especially in EFL contexts, a common problem is too much
usε of thε native language in the classroom.  Teachers who may not feεl supremεly

248

CHAPTER  15  Classroom Management

confident  in  their  En앙ish  proficiency  are  tempted  to  use  more  of  the  native
language than may be pedago~잊.Cally ad찌sable.  And students are naturally inclined
to  reveπ to various  forms  of code-switching  (alternating  between the native  and
target languages) in the classroom.

Is  there  a  middle  ground?  Your  cultural,  institutional,  없id  methodolo밍cal
contexts w피 usually lead to a  reasonable solution.  In EFL  classes, where students
와l share the same native language, a good deal of time is saved by using the native
language for certain definitions, grammatical explanations, directions for a  task, or
cultural  comments.  까ie  most  challenging  context  in  EFL  classes  is  in  pair  따ld
group work: Students will by nature revert to their native language  occ잃.ionally if
they C따1 “get away with it."

Some  institutions  pride  themselves  in  advertising “direct”  use  of the  target
language in their classrooms, and you will have  to discover for yourself how 따ld
when such proscriptions can be broken.  In ESL contexts, with multiple languages
often  represented  in  a  sin밍e classroom, the  issue  is  not as  acute, but even  here
clusters of students from the S따ne country may engage in native language chatter,
and it’s  up to you to determine how to deal with those possibilities.

Harmer (2001)  and  Gebhard  (2006)  offer  some  guide파ies for  dealing  with
issues of whether or not, and when, to use native languages in the classroom.  The
following is an amalgamation of the two sources:

a.  Set clear guidelines.  Students need to know when English use is essential,
when it is more or less “okay” to 않e the native language, and when it is
counterproductive to use the native language.

b.  Negotiate with students on why it is important for them to use En밍ish in
the classroom.  If they understand the importance of practicing English,
and the reasons for occasional uses of the native language, they will
experience the “ buy in” necessaπ for conforming to the negotiated
standard.

c.  Stimulate intrinsic motivation to use En밍ish in the classroom.  표 students
use En밍ish because they themselves see the value in it, they will be less
likely to use their native language.  They will view the use of En밍ish in
the classroom as an opportunity for practice and feedback from others.
d.  Choose appropriate tasks for students, so that they 따℃ capable, at their
level of English, of accomplis피ng the objectives of activities, and are
therefore less tempted to “cheat” by using their native language.
e.  Create in your classroom an En압ish “atmosphere." Especi때ly in EFL

situations, if students 따e surrounded by posters, magazines, computer
software, and other realia that stimulate the U않 of En밍ish, they will be
more likely to be in the “mood” to speak 잠핑lish in the classroom.

4.  Compromising with the “institution•’

Another adverse circumstance is one that most teachers have  to deal with at
some time in their car℃ers: teaching under institutional conditions that do not meet

CHAPTER  15  Classroom Management

249

their  ideal  standards  or philosophy  of education.  Sometimes  such  circumstancεs
focus on an individual in chargε, a director or principal.  And sometimεs they center
on  administrativ'℃  constraints  that  ar,ε  beyond  the  scope  and  powεr of  one
individual.  Som℃ examples:

•  classes  that  ar'ε  far  too  large  to  allow  for  thε  kind  of  results  that  the

administration expects (see above),

•  physical conditions in the classroom that ar℃ onerous,
•  administratively  imposεd constraints  on  νbαt you  have  to  tεach in  your

course (the curriculum, possibly in great detail),

•  administrativ'εly imposed  constraints  on  hotι you  should  teach  (a  specific

methodol앵Y that you disagree with is  rεquired),

•  courses that  satis다 an institutional  for,εign language  requirement, in which

students simply want a passing grade,

•  courses that are test-focusεd rather than language-focused.

5.  Disciplining

Many  volumes  of  rεsearch and  practical  advicε  hav,ε  beεn written  on  the
subject  of  classroom  discipline.  If  all  of  your  studεnts  wεre  hard-working,
intrinsically motivated, active, dedicated, intelligent  learnεrs-well, you would st피
have  what  we  could  label “discipline”  problems!  Without  making  this  section  a
whole primer on discipline, I will sin1ply offer some pointers her’t  and let yυu make
thε applications to specific instances.

a.  Lεarn to be comfortable with your position of authority.
b.  Gain the respect of your students by treating thεm all with equal fairness.
c.  Establish clearly and explicitly certain expectations regarding students'

behavior in class, or what Harmer (2001, p. 127) calls a “ code of conduct."
Harmer suggested that if the code is established through negotiation with
students, rathεr than as “ rules” that you as  the authority insist on, the
democratically established code “ has considerablε power” (p. 128).
d.  Include in the expεctations such issuεs as: con'℃ntions for turn-taking,

respect for oth℃rs, the importance of listening to othεr students,
attendance issues (tardiness and absence policy), and any extra-class
(“homework”) obligations.

ε.  Be firm but warm in dealing with variancεs to these expeαations.
f.  If a reminder, reprimand, or other form of verbal disciplinaηr action is

warranted, do your best to preserve the dignity of the student (in spite of
the fact  that you could be frustrated enou양1 to want to humiliatε the
student in front of classmates!) and thε tεrms of any negotiated “ rules.”
g.  Try, initially, to resolve disciplinaη7 mattεrs outside of class time (ask to see
a student after class and quietly but firmly make your obseπation and let
the student respond) so that valuable class minutes ar<εn’t spent focusing
on one student.

250

CHAPTER  15  Classroom Management

h.  In resolving  discip파iaπ problems, tη to find the source of the problem
rather than treating symptoms (for example, if a student isn’t  paying
attention in class, it could be because of a lack of sleep caused by wor피ng
a late night shift, in which case you could su잃est a different shift or a
different time bracket for the En웰sh class).

i.  If you cannot resolve a recurring discip파iaπ problem, then consult your

institution’s counselor or administrator.

6.  Dealing with cheating

Cheating is  a  speci때 discip파iary matter that W따·rants careful treatment.  For
the sake of definition, we W피 say cbeatiηg is a  surreptitious violation of standards
of individualized  responses to tests or other exercises.  The first  step to solving a
perceived problem of cheating is to ascertain a student’s own perception: Did he or
she honestly believe  they were  doing  sometlling wrong? There  is a  good deal  of
cultural variation in defining what is or isn’t  cheating, and for some, what you may
think is  cheating is  merely an intelligent utilization of resources close at hand.  In
other words, if the answer that is written on the test is correct, then the me따is used
to  come  up  with  the  correct  answer  are  just퍼ed.  Once  you  have  adequately
ascertained  a  student’s  perception, then  follow  the  disciplinary  suggestions  as  a
guide to a  solution.

Minimizing opportunities to cheat-that is, prevention-may prove to be more
fruitful than trying to tan양e with the mixture of emotions that ensue from dealing
with cheating after the fact.  Why do students cheat? Usually because of pressure to
“exceL” So  표 you can lower that pressure (see Chapters 21and22), you may reduce
the chance that someone will write notes on a fingernail or gl따ice across the aisle.
Remind students  that  you  and  the  test  are  there  to  help  them and  to 밍ve them
feedback, but if you don’t see their “real” selves, you won't be able to help them.  If
the classroom size permits, get students spread out as much as possible (this “elbow
room” also promotes some physical relaxation).  Then, consider an “A” and “B” form
of a  test  in  which  items  are  in  a  different  order for  every  other person, thereby
ma퍼ng it more difficult for someone to spot an answer.

All  these  and  even  further  adverse  circumstances  are  p따t of the  re따ity of
teaching and ultimately of classroom management because they all impinge in some
way  on what you  can  do  in  yo따 lessons.  Your  handling  of such  situations  will
almost  always  demand  some  sort  of compromise  on  your  part.  You  must,  as  a
professional “technician” in this field, be ready to bring professional diplomacy and
efficien이 to bear on the V따ying degrees of hardship.

1.  Roles

comfortablε with, say,  bεing an  authority figure, be consistent  in all  your  dea파1gs

Wherever you find  yourself teaching, the above forces  will come into play as

3.  Generating energy

What is classroom “energy”?  I like to usε this tεrm for a foreε that is unleashed
in a classroom, perceivablε only through a “sixth sεnse,~ if you will, that is acquired
in the ε·xperience of teaching itself  Energy is what you rεact to when you walk out
of a class period and say to yoursεIf, “Wow! That was a grεat class! ” or “What a gr1εat
group of students! ” Energy is the electricity of many minds caught up in a circuit of
thinking  and  talking  and  writing.  Enεrgy is  an  aura  of creativity  sparked  by  the
interaction of students.  Energy driv'εs students toward hi항ier attainmεnt.  Students
(and teachεrs) take  energy with thεm when they lεav·ε thε classroom and bring it
back the next day.

How  do  you  create  this  energy?  Not  necessarily  by  bεing  dramatic  or
flamboyant,  witty  or  wise.  Sometimes  enεrgy  is  unlεashed  through  a  quiet,
reserved,  but  focused  teachεr.  Sometimes  energy  forces  gather  in  th℃ corporate
intensity  of  students  focused  on  rathεr  mundane  tasks.  But  you  are  the  key.
Bεcause students initially look to you for lεadership and guidancε, you arε the one
to bεgin to get the creativε sparks flying.  And by whatεver means you accomplish
this,  you  do  so  through  solid  preparation,  confidence  in  your  ability  to  teach,  a
genuinely positiv1ε belief in your students' ability to learn, and a sense of joy in doing
what you do.  You  also  do so  by overtly manifesting  that prεparation, confid℃nce,
positive belief, and joy when you walk into th℃ classroom.

Perhaps the  art and the science of tεaching conv1εrg1ε on issues  of classroom

everything  that  those  individuals  will  do  or  say.  But  on  the  other  hand,  yo따
intuitions  are  informed  by the  collective  experiεnce and  best  practices  of those
who have gone befor,ε you in the form of guidεlines and parametεrs for successful
management  of  a  classroom  full  of  students.  By  combining  those  well-tested

classroom arena with confidence and creativity.

strategies  for  languag,ε  learning,  whether  the  learner  is  in  a  regular  language
classroom  or  working  on  a  sεIf-study  program.  Sometimes  these  stratεgies  are
subconsciously appliεd because certain learners seem to have a knack for language
learning that they are not consciously aware of.  But often, succεssful learnεrs have

STRATEGIC INVESTMENT

In  recent  yεars  language-teaching  mεthodology has  sεεn a  dramatic  increas℃ in
attεntion to what I like  to call  the sσate횡c investm.ent that learners can make in
their own  learning  process.  The  learning  of any  skill  involves  a  certain  degree  of
“investment” of one’s  time  and effort.  Eveπ complex set of skills-like learning to
play a musical instrument or tennis-is acquir℃d throu야 a combination of obs℃rving,
focusing,  practicing,  monitoring,  correcting, and  redirεcting.  And  so  onε dεvelops
strate밍es for perceiving others and for sin밍ing out rεlevant elements of language and
all  the  othεr  nεcεssaη bεhaviors  essential  for  ultimate  masteη. A  language  is
probably thε most complex set of skills one could ev1εr sεεk to acquire; therefor,ε, 없1
investmεnt is  nεcessaπ in  the  form  of developing  multiple  lay1εrs of strategies  for
getting that languag1ε into one’s brain.

In Chapter 4  thε Principle of Strategic Investment (Principle 5) was introduced.
In  this  chapter  we  probe  its  implications  for  your  tεaching methodology  in  the
classroom, spεcifically, how your language classroom tεchniques can encourag'ε,b버ld,
and  sustain  εffective  language-learning  strategies  in  yo따 students.  This  facet  of
language teaching has come to be known as strate얼es-based instruαion (SBI).

All  12 of the other principles outlined in Chapter 4 have a bearing on this issue.
If one’s language learning should sustain a modicum of automatic processing (Principle
1), what kind of strategies can students use to 싫sist in converting controlled processεs
into automatic onεs? If meaningful 1얹ming (Principle 2) is important, how can learners
maximize  me따ting  in  their  linguistic  input  and  output? ￦hat kind  of immediate,
extrinsic  rewards  (Principle  3)  and  long-range,  intrinsic  motives  (Principle  4)  are
necessaη to keep learners p아lted toward goals? As you run down th1ε list of principles
yourself,  it  bεcomes apparent  how learning  strat명ies are  germane  to  the  eventual
success of learners.  Strategies ar,ε, in essence, learners' techniques for capitali깅ng on
the principles of successful learning.

In an  era of communicative, interactive, learner-center,εd teaching, SBI  simply
cannot be ov,εrlooked.  All  too  oftεn, language teachers are so consumed with  thε
“delivery”  of language  to  their  students  that  they  neglect  to  spend  some  effort
preparing lεarnεrs to “receive” thε language.  And  studεnts, mostly unawar'ε of thε

CHAPTER  16  Strategies-Based Instruction

259

tricks of successful language learning, simply do what℃vεr the teacher tells them to
do, having no mεans to question the wisdom thεreof.  In an effort to fill class hours
with fascinating matεrial, tεachers might overlook thεir mission of enabling learners
to  ev,εntually become  independeηt of classrooms-that is, to bεcomε autonomous

lεarnεrs.

One of your principal goals as  an interactive language teacher is  to equip your
students with a  sensε of what  succεssful language  learners  do to  achieve  success
and  to  aid  them  in  develo1피ig their own unique, individual  pathways  to  success.
Bεca usε  by  definition  intεraction  is  unrehεarsed, mostly  unplanned  discourse,
students neεd to have thenεcessaη? strategic competencε to hold thεir own in thε
give and take of meaningful communication.

One  could  compare  language  learners  to  participants  in  an  elaborate  winε­
tasting  party.  Thε color and  sweetnεss of our linguistic  corkage  are enticing.  But
howarε studεnts to fully  appreciate this event without somε education on how to
partake of the libations spread befor℃ them? Tips on what to look for and what goes
with what and how to get the most out of something-these are necessary elements
of our mεthodology.  When studεnts are taught how to look at themselves and how
to  capitalize  on  their  talents  and  experiencεs, they learn  lεssons that  carπ them
wε11 bεyond any language classroom.  That’s what SBI  is  all about.

GOOD LANGUAGE LEARNERS

SBI  had its early roots in studies of “good” languag,ε learners.  Research in this ar,ζa
tended first  to  identi다 cεrtain successful  languagε learners and then to  extract
through  tests  of  psycholinguistic  factors,  interviews,  and  othεr data  analysis
re le、rant factors believed to contribute to their succεss.  Some genεralizations were
drawn by Joan Rubin and lriεne Thompson (1982) that will giv,ζ you a sense of the
flavor of this line of resεarch.  Good languagε lεarners

1.  find their own way, taking charge of their learning
2.  organize information about language
3.  are cr'℃ative, dev,εloping a “ feel” for the language by experimenting with its

grammar and words

4.  make thεir own opportunities for practice in using the language inside and

outside the classroom

5.  learn to live with uncertainty by not getting flustεrεd and by continuing to

talk or listen without understanding eveπ word

6.  use mnemonics and other memory strategies to recall what has been learnεd
7.  make errors work φr them and not against them
8.  use linguistic knowledge, including knowledge of their first language, in

learning a second language

9.  use contextual cues to help them in comprehension

10.  learn to make intelligent guesses
11.  learn chunks of l때guage as wholes and formalized routines to help them

perform “beyond their competence”

12.  learn certain tricks that help to keep conversations going

14.  learn different styles of speech and writing and learn to 때π their language

according to the form외ity of the situation

It  is  import따1t  to  remember  that  some  of the  above  characteristics  are  not
based on empirical findings, but rather on the  collective observations of teachers
and  learners  themselves.  Therefore,  do  not  assume  that  때l  successful  learners
exhibit 따l of these characteristics.  Nor is  this list of 14 an exhaustive one; in fact,
later in this chapter, you w피 find  a  much more detailed taxonomy (Oxford, 1990)
of successful learning strategies.

The  good  language  learner  studies  are  of obvious  interest  to  teachers.  The
more  y01표 classroom  activity  can  model  the  behavior  exhibited  by  successful
language learners, the better and more efficient your students will be, especially in
developing their own autonomy as learners.

S'IYLES OF SUCCESSFUL LANGUAGE LEARNING

One step in understanding SBI  is  to make a distinction between styles and strate횡es
(앞e PUT, Chapter 5).  Styles, whether related  to pers  뼈iη (s따.ch as  extroversior 1 ,

self-esteem,  따1회ety) or  to  c땅u디on (s따ch as  left/ri횡it-brain orientation, 따nbigui •

tolerance, field sensi디찌ty), characterize the consistent and enduring traits, tendencies,
or  preferences  that  may  이fferentiate you  from  another  person.  πiey are  “- .. an
individu외’s natural, habitual, and prefeπed ways of absorbing, processing, and re때피ng
new information 때d s뻐ls” (Kinsella, 1995,p.171).  You might, for 않따nple, tend to be
extroverted  or  ri양it-brain  oriented,  as  opposed  to  being  introverted  or  left-brain
oriented.  Or you  could  show a  preference  for  sol찌ng problems  in  a  파iear, lo횡C외
fashion  versus  an  integrated,  intuitive  approach.  These  sηles are  an  appropriate
characteriza디on of how you behave in generaJ,  even thoui갈1 you may for a multitude
of conscious  or subconscious  r싫ons operate  outside  of those  styles  in  a  쟁ec핸C
context.

Strat야셜es, on the other hand, are specific methods of approacl파ig a problem
or task, modes of operation for achieving a  particular end, or pl없med designs for
controlling and m따lip띠ating certain information.  Hsiao and Oxford (2002, p. 368)
emphasize  the role  of strategies  in enhandng students' learning.  Strategies  V았y
widely 쩌thin an  individu때, w피le styles  are  more constant and predi다able.  You
may almost simultaneously utilize a dozen strate핑es for figuring out what someone
뻐t  said  to  you, for  ex때1ple.  You  may  use  strate횡es of “playback”  (ima핑ne an
instant  taped  replay  of  the  conversation),  key  word  identification,  attention  to

CHAPTER  16  Strategies-Based Instruction

261

nonv1εrbal  cuε(s), attention  to  context,  dictionary  loo!ι-up, grammatical  analysis,
numerous direct  rεquεsts for  repεtition, rephrasing, word definition, or turning to
someonε εlse for  intεrpretation.  And the list could go on.

Succεssful second  language  learnεrs are  usually  people  who  know  how  to
manipulatε stylε (as wεII as strategy) lεvels in their day-to-day encountεrs with thε

ambiguity  (cognitive  traits)  rec<핑nizεs hεr dominant  traits  and  rεsolves to  force

toward  languagε she doεsn’t understand.  Why?  Because she has been informed of

In  other words, sty!εs are  not by any means  immutable  tendencies.  Learnεrs

commandmεnts” listed in Table  16. l  in this chapter may sufficiently  idεnti디 chief

AWARENESS AND ACTION

Implied  in  thε foregoing  discussion  is  thε  all-imp야tant  Principle  of Autonomy
(Principle  6, discussed in  Chapter 4).  In  ordεr for learners  to become  self-driven
independent learners beyond the classroom, they must be fully aware of their own

options.  Thε importance of awareness-raising in language  learning has caught the

Of course, the  awarenεss-action relationship  is  fundamental  to all  human learning
and especially to acquiring complex skills 파@ foreign languagεs.  When learners are
aware of their own capacities and limitations, they can efficiently adopt pathways
to success that capitalize on strengths and compεnsatε for weaknesses.

Figure  16. l  on  page  262  offers  my  visual  picturε of  the  awarenes~←action
connection.  A fully  functioning  learner is  aware, on a  number of lεvεls, of styles,

intellectual  capabilities,  cultural  schemata,  belief  야rstems,  and  more.  Such  selε
knowledge  thεn leads  to  action  in  the  form  of strategies:  direct,  metacog띠tive,
socioaffective, skill-specific  (listening, spea셈ng, reading, writing), and proficiency
based (varying from beginner to advanced) strategies, along with appropriate doses
of compensating for wεaknεsses and capitalizing on style advantages.

(a) if they are too dominant on one side, they may profit from allowing the

2.  Formal personality and cognitive style tests

If formal  personality or cognitivε sty Iε tests  ar'ε available  to you, you  might tπ

CHAPTER  16  Strategies-Based Instruction

4.  Encouraging “good Ian밍Iage learner” behavior

Yεt another  form  of instilling  sεlfawarenεss in  students  is  through  frequent
impromptu rεmindεrs of “rules” for good language lεarning and encouragement of
discussion or clarification.  Somεtimes the little comments you make her'ε and there
have  the  εn;εct of subtly  ur입ng students  to  take  charge  of their  own  dεstiny by
understanding their own styles of l℃arning and capitalizing on their abilities.

A set of successful styles for l때guag1ε learning might be appropriatεly capsulized
in the form  of 10 mlεs,or “commandments,” as  I have on occasion facεtiously c외lεd
thεm.  In Table  16.1, they are given in a teacher’s version and a learner’s version.  The
former is statεd in more technical terms; the latter usεs words and cliches dεsigned to
catch the attention of lεamεrs.  πie learners’ vεrsion, in the right-hand column with
appropriate  explanations,  mi힐lt  bε usεful for  a  classroom  bulletin  board, for  class
discussions, or for studεnt journal-writing topics‘

It is  extremely important to remember that thesε style continua do not always
fit all lεarnεrs.  These rulεs εncompass what most learnεrs n℃εd to point to most of
the  time  in  most  languag1ε-learning  contexts.  That  is,  most  learners  com℃ to  a
language  class  with  too  many  inhibitions,  not  enough  willingnεss to  take  risks,
rεlativ1εly low self-confidence in their ability to learn a  languagε, etc.  Your mission
to 90 percεnt of your studεnts is to pull them away from this potεntially internεring
side  of  the  continuum  and  to  get  them  to  grapplε with  these  “ pro blεms” and
overcome  them.  Ten  pεrcent (this is  just a  rough  guess-it could be  a  higher or
lower  proportion  dεpεnding  on  the  makeup  of  your  classroom  and  on  thε
particular  stylε tεndεncy in  question)  of your students could lean  thε othεr way‘
For them  your  job  is  to  put  th℃ brakes  on things  like  high  (and  haphazard)  risk
taking, εxcessive impulsivenεss, overinflated sεlf-conf1dεnce, an approach that is too
“ laid back,녀nd so forth.  Further commεnts on this issue follow later in this chapter.

Table  16.1. “ 1 0 commandments” for  good  language  learning

Teacher’s Version

1.  Lower  inhibitions.
2.  Encourage  risk  taking.
3.  Build self-confidence.
4.  Develop  intrinsic  motivation.
5.  Engage  in  cooperative  learning.
6.  Use right-brain  ~rocesses.
7.  Promote ambiguity tolerance.
8.  Practice intuition.
9.  Process error feedback.
10.  Set personal  goals.

Learner’s Version

Fear  not!
Dive  in.
Believe  in  yourself.
Seize the day.
Love  thy  neighbor.
Get the  BIG  picture.
Cope with  the chaos.
Go with  your  hunches.
Make  mistakes work  FOR  you.
Set your own goals.

HOW TO TEACH STRATEGIES IN THE CLASSROOM

Just  what  are  all  thesε tricks  of the  trade  that wε’re calling “strategiεs”?  Rebεcca
Oxford  (1990)  provides  the  most  compreh℃nsiv1℃ taxonomy of learning  stratεgies
currεntly available.  Thεsε strategiεs arε dividεd into what hav,ε come to be known
as dirεct or cognitivε stratεgiεs, which learners apply dirεctly to the languag,℃ itself,
and indirect or metacognitive  stratεgies, in which lεarners manage or control their
own learning process.  Direct strategies includ℃ a  number of diffi℃rεnt ways of

•  rεmembering m。rε εffiεctiv1εly,
•  using all  your cognitive processes,
•  compensating for missing knowl℃dge.

Indirεct strategiεs, according to Oxford’s  taxonomy, include

•  organizing and evaluating your learning,
•  managing your emotions,
•  learning with others.

A list of 50 specific strategiεs falling into thesε six g℃neral categories is contained in
O앞ord’s (1990) Strategy Inv,εntory for  Languagε Learning (SILL)  in Figur·ε 16.5 on
pages 272-275.  Turn to that inv1εntory now, bεfore reading on, in ord℃r to becomε
familiar with some spεcific strat℃gies for successful languagε learning.

Stratεgies, like stylεs,can b℃ taught, and because of their specificity, εvεn morε
εasily  than  styles.  Thεrε ar1ε  at  least  four  different  approaches  you  can  takε to
tεaching strategiεs in thε languag℃ classroom.

1.  Teach strategies through interactive techniques.

M따iy stratεgies are  related to, and actually become, thε outward manlliεstation
of  styles.  For  εxamplε,  a  risk-taking  stylε  would  result  in  seeking  practicε
opportunities,  making  conversation  ev,εn  when  it  isn’t  “ nεcessaπ,”  tηring  out
languag1ε  you ’rε  not  surε of,  asking  for  correction,  making  guesses  about  what
somεone said, εtc.

One way to familiarizε your students with this plethora of possible strategiεs is
to  promotε  thε  “ 10  commandments”  abov,ε  through  your  own  classroom
techniques.  Some  techniques  will  be  the  ones  you  would  utilize  anyway.  0thεr
techniques  will  perhaps  bε  spεcifically  gearεd  toward  building  stratεgic
competence.  Table  16.2  on  pagε 270  offers  some  su잃εstions  for  crεating  an
atmosphere  in  your  classroom  in  which  students  feel  comfortablε  and  ar,ε
εncouraged to dεvεlop thεir own strategiεs.

Reb℃cca Oxford  (1990)  offi℃rεd one  of  thε bεst tεacher resourcε books  to
appear  on  the  subject  of  SBI.  She  gave  ex따nples of  m따iy  diffiεrent  classroom
techniquεs  and  showed  which  stratεgies  they  encourage.  For  example,  an
information-gap listening technique was εxplained (pp. 109-110) in which students

270

CHAPTER  16  Strategies-Based Instruction

Table  16.2.  Building strategic techniques

1.  To  lower inhibitions:  play guessing games and communication games;  do role plays
and skits;  sing songs;  use  plenty of group work;  laugh  with  your students;  have them
share their fears  in  small  groups.

2.  To  encourage risk-taking:  praise students for  making sincere efforts to try  out

language;  use fluency  exercises where errors are  not corrected at that time;  give
outside-of-class assignments to speak or write or otherwise try  out the language.
3.  To  build students' self-confidence: tell  students explicitly (verbally and  nonverbally)
that you  do  indeed believe in  them;  have them  make lists of their strengths,  of what
they  know or have accomplished so far  in  the course.

4.  To  help them to develop intrinsic motivation:  remind them  about the rewards for

learning English;  describe (or  have students  look up)  jobs that require  English;  play
down  the final  examination  in  favor  of helping students to see rewards for  themselves
beyond the final  exam.

5.  To  promote cooperative learning: direct students to share their knowledge;  play down
competition among students; get your class to think of themselves as a team;  do a
considerable amount of small-group work.

6.  To  encourage them to use right-brain  processing:  use movies and tapes  in  디 ass; have
them read  passages  rapidly;  do skimming exercises;  do rapid “free  writes까 do oral
fluency  exercises where the object is  to  get students to talk (or write) a  lot without
being corrected.

7.  To  promote ambiguity tolerance: encourage students to ask you,  and each other,

questions when  they don’t understand something;  keep your theoretical  explanations
very simple and  brief;  deal  with  just a few  rules at a time;  occasionally resort to
translation  into a  native  language to clarify a word or meaning.

8.  To  help them use  their intuition: praise students for  good guesses;  do  not always give
explanations of errors-let a correction suffice;  correct only selected errors,  preferably
just those that interfere with  learning.

9.  To  get students to make their mistakes work FOR them: record  students’ oral

production  and get them  to  identify errors;  let students catch and correct each other’s
errors;  do not always give them  the correct form;  encourage students to  make lists  of
their common errors and to  work on them  on their own.

10.  To  get students to set their own goals: explicitly encourage or direct students to go
be~ond the classroom goals;  have them  make lists  of what the_y  will  accomplish on
their own  in  a particular week;  get students to  make specific time commitments at
home to study the language;  give “extra credit" work.

listen to a conversation on a tape and then, in groups, fill in an information grid (with
blank  spaces for  name, profession, address, age,  and  appearance) for  each  of four
people mentioned in the conversation.  Oxford explained that such a  task  involv;εs
direct  strate밍es  like  practicing  naturalistically,  guessing,  note  a피ng,  focusing
atten디on, 따id cooperating with co-learners.

2.  Use compensatory techniques.

A rεlated avenuε for SBI  is in thε spεcific idεntification of techniquεs that aim to
compεnsate for  certain  style  weaknεsses.  Some  thr'εe dεcades ago,  Alicε Omaggio
(1981)  publishεd a  little  book that  appliεs as  wε11 today  as  it  did  whεn it  was  first
publishεd.  Omaggio  classifiεd some 55  differ,εnt techniquεs according to  numεrous
cognitivε style “ pro blεms ” that  mi양1t  prevent  studεnts from  rεaching their  highεst
potential.  For  εxample, “excessive  I℃flεctiv,εness/caution ” is  a  problεm that  might
apply to certain  studεnts in your class:  Thεy are  unwilling  to  takε risks;  thεy pause
too  long  bεforε responding  orally;  they  want  to  get  eveη>thing ri양1t  beforε they
attempt  to  spe와i  or write.  Several  dozen  techniques  are  then “prescribed”  to  help
such  studεnts  overcome  their  problεm.  Hεrε  ar,ε  somε  typical  cognitiv1ε  style
“problεms”  and  a  few  tεchniquεs you  might  pr,εscribe  to  hεIp  overcome  εach
problem.

1.  Low tolerance of αmbiguity: brainstorming, rεtelling stories, role playing,

paraphrasing, finding synonyms, jigsaw techniques, skimming tasks
2.  Excessiνe impulsiνeηess: making infεrences, syntactic or sεmantic cluε
searches, scanning for specific information, inductiv,ε rulε gεnεralization

3.  Excessiνe r야ectiveness/caution: smal냉roup tεchniqu℃s,rolε playing,

brainstorming, fluency techniques

4.  Too  much right-braiη dominaηce: syntactic or semantic clue searchεs‘

scanning for specific information, proofrεading, categorizing and clustering
activitiεs, information-gap techniquεs

5.  Too  much l야-braiη dominance: integrative language techniquεs, fluεncy

techniques, retelling stories, skimming tasks

3.  Administer a  strategy inventory.

Earlier  in  this  chapter  wer,ε some  suggεstions for  using  a  selfchecklist  and
formal stylε tests in the classroom.  Following the samε format, you could introduce
a  strategy  invento1γ.  The  bεst and  most  comprehensive  of such  instruments  is
Rebεcca  Oxford’S  (1990)  Strategy  Inventory  for  Language  Lεarning  (SILL),
mentionεd in  the  introduction  to  this  section  of the  chapter.  Thε SILL  has  now
bεεn used  with  learnεrs in  a  number of different  countriεs including  the  United
Statεs, and  has  proven  to  be  exceptionally  enlightening  to  learners  as  they  are
εxposed, perhaps for the first time, to so many different strategic options.  The SILL
is  reprinted in Figure  16.5.

The SILL can bε used in class for developing awareness of strategiεs in thε samε

way suggested εarlier for the self-checklist on styles.  Or it could becomε an out-of
class  assignment  for  later  class  discussion.  Its  scoring  and  interpretation  can  be
tricky, however, so make  sur,ε that in either case students ar<ε fully aware of how to
score  it.  AND  the  SILL  can  do  double  duty  as  an  instrumεnt that  enlightens  you
about 50 diftiεrεnt ways that your learners could become a little mor1ε successful in
thεir language-learning endeavor.

(Overall  average)

What These Averages  Mean to You

The overall  average tells how often you  use  strategies  for learning English.  Each  part of
the SILL  represents  a group of learning strategies. The  averages  for each  pa야 of the  SILL
show which groups of strategies you  use the most for  learning English.

The  best use  of strategies  depends on  your age,  personality,  and purpose for  learning.
If you  have a very low average  on  one or more parts  of the SILL,  there  may be some
new strategies  in  these groups that you  might want to use.  Ask  your teacher about these.

4.  Make use of impromptu teacher-initiated advice.

Finally,  as  you  may  recall  from  thε discussion  of dεveloping stylε a warεness,
lεarnεrs can bεnefit greatly from your daily attεntion to thε many little tricks of the
tradε that  you  can  pass  on  to  them.  Think  back  to  your  own  languag,ε-learning
expεriences and  notε what it was that you now attributε your success (or failure!)
to, and pass thεsε insights on.  Did you use flash  cards? Did you practicε a lot?  Did
you sεε subtitled moviεs? Read books? Pin rules and words up on your wall? When
the  appropriatε moments occur in your class, seizε the opportunity to teach your
studεnts  how  to  lεam.  By  doing  so  you  will  increase  their  opportunitiεs  for
stratεgic investmεnt in their learning process.

“PACKAGED” MODEI..5  OF SBI

Many  of  your  opportunitiεs  for  strategy  training  in  thε  classroom  will  be
“ m℃thodological.”  That is, you will opt for onε of the four possible means suggεsted
above.  There  rεmain  three  more  formalized  models  of  incorporating  strategy
a warεnεss and practicε in language classrooms.  Thεsε are growing in popularity as
morε educational administrators appreciate the value of SBI  for ultimate success in
a  forεign languagε.

1.  Textbook-embedded instruction

An  increasing  numbεr of ESL  tεxtbooks are  oft4εring guidelines  and  exercises
for strategy awar,℃ness and practice within the stream of a chapter.  Brown’s (2000a)
New  Vistas  sεries  for  ESL  learnεrs offers  examples  of embedding  stratεgy work
within  the  exεrcisεs of  a  textbook.  Studεnts  ar'ε  encouragεd to  continuε thεir
learning  outsidε the  classroom, sometimes  individually,  som℃times with a  partnεE
One  unit  in  the  intermediate  level  suggests  practicing  communication  strategiεs,
identifying  positive  classroom  learning  stratεgies,  and  practicing  conversation
strategiεs in a discussion about cultural sterεotypes.  Thes℃ “ strategies for success”
ar,ε reprinted in  Figur,ε  16.6 on  pagε 277.  The  teacher's  rεsourc℃ manual  for  this
book  gives  detailed  directions  to  tεachεrs on  how  to  facilitate  these  extra-class
learning experiεnc℃s.

Ano thεr series  of  ESL  textbooks,  Lecture  Reaψ (Sarosy  &  Shεrak,  2006),
designed  to  tεach acad℃mic listening,  note  taking,  and  discussion,  also  includεs
strategic  suggestions  for  effectiv1εly comprehending  academic  lectures.  Students'
attention  is  explicitly  drawn  to  cues  for  listening  accurately  to  a  lecture,  for
example, by attεnding to languagε that signals sequences of points, thε “big picture,”
and signals for a new idea or topic.  Similarly, Chamot, 0 ’Malley, and Kupper (1992)
includε strategy training  modules in each unit.  Onε of thεir lessons  recommends
kεεping a daily log for one weεk and chεcking how many timεs a student uses any
of 14 different strategies.  A grid is  provided for  easy checking (sεe Figure  16.7 on
page 278).

Identifying behaviors for successful language learning

1.  W1tha Le값띠ng Partner, look at Lesson 1, Exercise 5 and add some more phrases
for understanding someone in a conversation.  You may think of expressions like:

Interactive language teachers must not underestimate the import없ice of getting
students strate밍C외ly invested in their language-learning process.  Perhaps the most
powerful  principles  of  learning  that  merge  here  are  Intrinsic  Motivation  따id
Autonomy.  One of the best ways of getting students intrinsically and autonomously
involved in their language learning is to offer them the opportunity to develop their
@νn strategies  for  success.  Having  thus  invested  their  time  and  effort  into  the
le따피ng of English, they can take responsib피q for much of their own learning.  This,
in nπn, generates more motivation as they become autonomous learners.

CHAPTER  1 7

OBJECTIVES  After  reading  this chapter,  you  will  be able  to:

•  appreciate the import없ice of
integrating skills for more
authentic따 and better
reinforcement

•  understand the characteristics of
several differ℃nt approaches that
illustrate the integration of s힘lls

•  analyze a lesson from the point

of view of its integration of skills

•  apply concepts of skills

integration to the next four
chapters, which deal with the
sep따ate s힘lls

For more than six decades now, research and practice in English language teaching has
identified the “four s때ls” -listening, speaking, rea띠ng, and writing-as of paramount
import없ice.  ESL  curricula  and  textbooks  around  the  world  tend  to  focus,  all  too
often, on just one of the four skills, sometimes to the exclusion of the others.  Books,
articles,  antholo~킥es, research  surveys,  and  conferences  ηpic외ly  indεx or 야ganize
their contents according to each of the four skills.

It is  perfectly appropriate to thus identify language performance.  The human
race has fashioned two forms of productive performance, oral and written, and two
forms  of  receptive  performance,  aural  (or  auditory)  and  reading.  There  are,  of
course, offshoots of each mode.  Lumped together under nonverbal communication
are  various  visually  perceived  messages  delivered  through  gestures,  facial
expressions,  proximity,  and  so  forth.  Graphic  art  (drawings,  paintings,  and
diagrams)  is  also  a  powerful  form  of communication.  But  attention  to  the  four
different  skills  does  indeed pay off as  learners  of a  second language  discover  the
differences and interrelationships among these four primary modes of performance.
Despite  our  histotγ  of  trea디ng the  four  skills  in  separate  segments  of  a
curriculum, there  is  a  recent  trend  toward  skill  integration.  π1at is,  rather  than
designing a curriculum to teach the many aspects of one skill, say, reading, curriculum
designers  are  ta따ng more  of  a  whole  language  approach  whereby  reading  is
treated as one of two or more interrelated skills.  A  course that deals  with reading
skills, then, will more often than not also  deal with related listening, spea퍼ng,and
writing s뼈s.  A  lesson in a so-called reading class, under this new p따adigm, mi양lt
include

•  a prereading discussion of the topic to activate schemata;
•  listening  to  a  teacher's  monologue  or  a  series  of informative  statements

about the topic of a passage to be read;

284

CHAPTER  17

Integrating the  "Four Skills"

285

•  a focus on a certain reading stratεgy,saκ scanning;
•  ιriting a response to or paraphrase of a reading passage.

This reading class, then, models for the students the real-life integration of language
skills, gets them to perceive the relationship among several skills, and provides the
teacher with a great deal of flexibility in creating interesting, motivating lessons.

WHY INTEGRATED SKILLS?

Some may 따gue that the integration of the four skills diminishes the importance of
the rules of listening, spea힘ng, reading, and writing that ar℃ unique to each separate
skill.  Such  an  a핑ument rarely  holds  up  under  careful  scrutiny  of integrated-skills
courses.  If anything, the added richnεss of the latter gives students greater motivation
that converts to better retention of principles of effective spe와dng, listening, reading,
and writing.  Rather than being forced to plod along through a course that limits itself
to one mode of pεrformance, students are given a chance to diversify their efforts in
more meaningful tasks.  Such int앵ration can, of course, st피 utilize a strong, principled
approach to thε separate, unique characteristics of each sk피.

So  you may be wondering why courses weren’t  always  integrated in the first

place.  There are sεveral reasons:

1.  In the pre-Communicative Language Teaching (Ctη days of language

teaching, the focus on the forms of language almost predisposed curriculum
designers to segment courses into the separate language skills.  It seemεd
logical to fashion a syllabus that dealt with, say, pronunciation of thε
phonemes of English, stress and intonation, oral structural patterns (carefully
sequenced according to presumed grammatical difficulty), and variations on
those patterns.  These language-based classes tended to be courses in “ baby
linguistics” where a preoccupation with rules and paradigms tau양it students a
lot about languag1ε but sometimes at the expense of teaching language itself.
2.  Administrative considerations still make it easier to pr앵ram separate courses

in reading and speaking, and so on, as a glance at current intensive and
university English courses reveals.  Such divisions can indeed be justified
when one considers the practicalities of coordinating three-hour-per-week
courses, hiring teachers for each, ordering textbooks, and pla따ig students
into the courses.  It should be noted, however, that a proficient teacher who
professes to follow principles of CLT would never conduct, say, a “reading”
class without εxtensive use of speaking, listening, and writing in the class.
3.  This leads to a third reason that not all classes are integrated.  There are cer
tain specific purposes for which students are studying English that may best
be labeled by one of the four skills, especially at the high intermediate to
advanced levels.  In an academic setting such as a university, specialized

286

CHAPTER  17

Integrating the  "Four Skills’

workshops, modules, tutorials, or courses may be constructed explicitly to
improve certain specialized skills.  Thus a module in listening comprehen
sion might include instruction on listening effectively to academic lectures,
to fellow students in the classroom, to audio pr앵rams wher'ε there are no
visual cues, to the consultative register used in the professor's office, and
even to fellow students in casual conversation.  Such a course might encom
pass phonological, morphological, syntactic, lexical, semantic, and discourse
elements.

Aside  from  these  caveats, the  intεgration of the four skills-or at lεast two  or
more skills-is the typical approach within a communicative, interactive framework.
As  Hinkel  (2006, p. 113) noted, “In  an age  of glob찌ization, pragmatic  objectives of
language  learning place  an increased value  on integrated and ψnamic multiskill
[my italics]  instructional models with a focus on meaningful communication and the
development  of  learners'  communicative  competence."  Most  of  the  interactive
techniques already described or referred to in this book involve  the integration of
skills.  The following observations support such techniques.

1.  Production and rεception are quite simply two sides of the same coin; one

cannot split the coin in two.

2.  Interaction means sending aηd receiving messages.
3.  Written and spoken language often φut not always!) bear a relationship to
each other; to ignore that relationship is to ignore the richness of language.
4.  For literate learners, the interrelationship of written and spoken language is
an intrinsically motivating reflection of language and culture and society.

5.  By attending primarily to what learners can do with language, 없id only

secondarily to the forms of language, we invite any or all of the four skills that
are relevant into the classroom arena.

6.  Often onε skill will reinforce another; we learn to speak, for example, in part
by modeling what we hear, and we learn to write by examining what we can
read.

7.  Proponents of the whole language approach (see Chapter 3) have shown us

that in the real world of l없iguage use, most of our nattπal performance
involves not only the integration of one or more skills, but connections
between language and the way we think and feel and act.

MODELS OF SKILLS INTEGRATION

How C따1 you maintain 없1 integrated-sk피S focus in your teaching? All  of the models
and approaches described in Chapter 3 are predicated on 야ie u똥 of at least two κ
not all four s따lls: learner-centered instruction; cooperative or collaborative learning,
interactive learning, whole l없iguage education, content-based instruction, and task
based instruction.  Even the added “candidates” for approaches, the Lexical Approach

CHAPTER  17

Integrating the  “Four Skills ”  287

and  Multiplε  Intelligences,  imply  several  skills  in  developing  communicativ,ε
com petεnee.

In  ordεr to  illustrate  a  numbεr of possible integrated  approaches  to  language
instruction, two of the  prεvious models  (content-basεd and task-based instruction)
will  be  briefly  analyzεd herε along  with  some  furthεr coneεpts that  highlight  thε
intεgration of listening, sp℃aking, reading, and writing.

Content-Based Instruction

Content-basεd (sometimes referred to as “ contεnt-cεntered”) instruction, dεscribεd
in  Chaptεr 3, integrates  thε learning  of some  specific  subjεct-mattεr content with
thε  learning  of  a  second  language.  Thε overall  structure  of  a  content-basεd
curriculum, in contrast to many  traditional language  curricula, is  dictatεd morε by
thε nature of thε subject matter than by language forms and sεquences.  The second
language, then, is  simply  the  mεdium to  convey informational  content of intεrεst
and  rεlevance  to  the  learner.  Examples  of  contεnt-based  curricula  includε
immersion  programs  for  elementary  school  children,  shelterεd English  programs
(mostly  found  at  εlementary and  sεcondary school  levels),  writing  across  th℃
curriculum  (wher℃ writing  skills  in  sεcondary schools  and  univ,εrsitiεs are  taught
within  subject-mattεr areas  like  biology,  history, art, etc.), and  English  for  Specific
Purposes (ESP)  (e.g., for εngineering, agriculture, or mεdicinε)

It  is  p℃rhaps  alrεady  clear  that  content-based  teaching  allows  learners  to
acquire knowledge and skills that transcεnd all the bits and pieces of language that
may  occupy  hours  and  days  of  analyzing  in  a  traditional  languagε classroom.
Research  on  sεcond language  acquisition  at  various  agεs indicates  the  ultimatε
strength  of  learning  that  is  pointεd  toward  practical  non-language  goals.  The
meaningful  learning  principle  applies  well  here.  Learners  are  focused  on  useful,
practical  objectives  as  the subject  mattεr is  pεrcεiv'εd to  bε relevant  to  l。ng-term
goals.  This also increasεS the intrinsic motivation that is so important to learning of
any kind.

Content-based  instruction  allows  for  the  complete  intεgration  of  language
skills.  As you plan a lesson around a particular subtopic of your subject-matter area,
your task bεcomes how bεst to present that topic or concept or principle.  In such
lessons  it  would  be  difficult  not  to  involve  all  four  skills  as  your  students  read,
discuss, solve problems, analyze data, and write opinions and reports.

Task-Based Language Teaching

Task-based language tεaching (fBLn was defined and discussed in Chapters 3 and 11.
As  you will recall, thεrε ar,ε a number of different  interprεtations in the literature on
what, εxactly, a task is.  ￦hat these various understandings all emphasize, howev,εc is
the centrality of the task itself in a language course and the importance of organizing

classroom.  At  its  heart,  then, TBLT  implies  several  integrated  skills  in  its  focus  on

language  in  the  rεal world.  Most  rεal-world situations  demand  simultanεous use  of

In task-basεd instruction, the priority is  not the forms  of languagε, but rather
the  functional  purposes  for  which  languagε must  bε used.  Whilε content-based
instruction focuses  on  subject-matter content, task-based  instruction focuses  on a

•  mεdia εxtracts

•  t℃lεphonε dir1εctoriεs
•  mεnus
•  labεls

And  the  list  could  continuζ  Evident  in  this  variety  of sourcε material  is  thε
nεcessity of attending to morε than just one of the four skills.  Coursε goals in TBLT

learners' pragmatic language compεtence.

So  we  hav1ε  in  task-based  teaching  a  well-integrated  approach  to  language

ourselv,εs from thinking only in tεrms of the separatε four skills.  Instεad, principlεs
of listening, speaking, reading, and writing become appropriately subsumεd under

Theme-Based Instruction

Another way of looking  at  the  integration  of skills  is  to  consider the  structure  of
many English language coursεs around the world.  Courses tend to focus on topics,

Theme-based  instruction  is  not  thε same  as  content-basεd.  In  order  to

strong  version  (content-based),  the  primaπ purpose  of  a  course  is  to  instruct

interest.  The  examples  of  content-based  instruction  mentioned  earliεr  in  this
chapter are good illustrations of the  strong vεrsion.  English for  Specific  Purpos℃S

(ESP)  at the  univεrsity level, for  examplε, gathεrs enginεεring majors  togethεr in a

A  wεak form  of  content-basεd tεaching actually  placεs an  εqual  valuε on

around subject-matter area, both students and teachers are fully aware that language

coursε or curriculum because their language skills n℃εd improvement, and they are

This  wεak vεrsion  is  actually  practical  and  eft4εctiv1ε  in  many  instructional
settings.  It  typically  manifi℃sts itself in what has  come to be called  theme-basεd or
topic-based  teaching.  Theme-based  instruction  providεs an  altεrnative  to  what
would  othεrwise bε traditional  languagε classes  by  structuring  a  course  around
themes  or  topics.  Themε-based  curricula  can  serve  the  multiplε  interests  of
students  in  a  classroom  and  can  offer  a  focus  on  content  while  still  adhεring to
institutional  needs  for  omεring  a  language  course  per  se.  So,  for  example,  an

programs, discuss issues, propose solutions, and carπ out writing assignments on a

Granted, there  is  a  fuz긴r  line  of distinction  betwεen theme-based  instruction
and “ traditional”  language  instruction.  You  could  easily  argue  that  many  εxis ting
reading  and  writing  courses,  for  example,  are  thεmε-based  in  that  thεy offer

I don’t  think it  is  important, or necessary, to  dichotomizε here.  ￦hat is  important
is  to viεw theme-based instruction as a context for the intεgration of skills.

Numerous  current  ESL  textbooks, especially at  the  intεrmediate to  advanced

Experiential Learning

Yet  another lεns through which wε could view the concept of integrated skills  is
thε  notion  of  experiential  languag'ε  lεarning.  Experiεntial  learning  includεs

language, that integrate skills, and that point toward authentic, real-world purposes.
Experiential learning offers a  dimεnsion that may not necessarily be implied in  thε
three  concepts  (content-based,  task-based,  theme-based)  already  discussed.  What
εxperiential  lεarning  highlights  for  us  is  giving  students  concrete  experiences

and εrror, by processing feedback, by building  hypotheses about language, and by

is, teachers do not simply tε11 students about how language works; instead, they giv1ε

com plεxities of a variety of concrete expεriences.

topic being studied rather than simply reading or talking about it.  Usually there is
some physical involvement in thε phenomenon as well.  Experiential learning is not
so  much a  novel  concept as  it  is  an emphasis on the  marriage  of two  substantiv,ε
principlεs  of  εffective  learning,  principlεs  εspoused  by  the  famous  Amεrican
εducator John Dewεy:

As  such  it  is  an  especially  useful  concept  for  tεaching children,  whose  abstract

Experiential lεarning tends to put an emphasis on the psychomotor aspects of

used in teaching native language reading skills, but more recently adapted to second

“ book.”A number of activities can then follow, including word study, spelling focus,

intrinsic  involvement  of students  in  creating  their  own  stories  rather  than  being
giv1εn  other  people’s  stories.  As  in  other  ε:xperiential  techniques,  students  ar,ε

The Episode Hypothesis

Well over a cεntury ago, Franc;ois Gouin, if you will recall from Chapter 2, designed
a  m℃thod of language  tεaching called  thε Seriεs Method.  One  of the  keys  to  the
success  of the  method  lay  in  the  presentation  of  languagε in  an  εasily followεd
sto1γline.  You may rememb℃r the sequence of sentences about opεning a door.  In
another lεsson, Gouin teaches a number of verbs, verb forms, and other vocabulary

Thε girl goes and seεks a  piεcε of wood.
Shε takes a  hatchet.
She draws near to thε block.
She places thε wood on this block.
Shε raises thε hatchet.
She brings down the hatchet.
The bladε strikes against the wood.
etc.

In εasily visualized steps, the studεnts arε lεd through the process of chopping and
gathεring wood, all  at a very εl℃m℃ntaπ levεl of the language.

In some ways, Gouin was utilizing a psychological devicε that, a hundred years
latεr,John Oil℃r cailed the episode hypothesis.  According to Oller (1983b,p. 12),
“ text (i.e‘, discourse in any form) will be easier to reproduce, understand, and rεcall,
to thε extent that it is structured episodically.” By this hε mεant that thε pres℃ntation
oflanguag,ε is εnhancεd if students receiv1ε interconnected sentences in an intεrest­
provol선ng εpisodε rather than in a disconnected seriεs of sεntences.

The εpisodε hypothesis goes well bεyond simpl℃ “mεaningful” lεarning.  Look

at this dialogue:

Jack:  Hi, Tony.  What do you usually do on weεkends?
Tony:  Oh, I usually study, but sometimεs I go to a movie.
Jack:  Uh-huh.  Well, I often go to moviεs, but I seldom study.
Tony:  Well, I don’t  study as much as Greg.  He always studies on the

weεkends.  He never goεs out.

You  can see that this  conv1εrsation, while easily  understood, clearly presented, 따1d
pεrhaps quitε relevant  to  stud℃nts lεarning English,  lacks  a  sensε of drama-of
“what’S going to happen next?” Most  of our communicativε textbooks  havε many
“Jack and Tony" types  of presεntation.  They may  illustrate  cεrtain grammatical or
discourse featur1εs, but they don’t grip thε learner with suspense.

how it diffiεrs from Jack and Tony’s.

This conversation uses a  familiar  setting and ordinary charactεrs to whet the
curiosity of the reader.  Because the outcome is not clear, learners are motivated to
continue reading and to become more involved in the content than in the language,
therefore increasing its episodic flavor.  Oller notes that the interaction of cognition
and  language  enables  learners  to  form  “ expectancies”  as  they  encounter  either
logically  or  episodically  linked  sentences.  Moreoveζ “ stories”  are  universal,  and
therefore students from many different cultures can understand their org없lizational
structure and identi다 with the characters.

You  may be wondering how the εpisode hypothesis contributes or relates  to

integrated-skills teaching.  Here are some possible ways:

•  Stories  or  episodes  challenge  the  teacher  and  textbook  writer  to  present
interesting,  natural  language,  whether  the  language  is  viewed  as  written
discourse or oral discourse.

•  Episodes can be presented in either written or spoken form, thus requiring

reading and/or writing skills on the students' part.

•  Episodes  can  provide  the  stimulus  for  spoken  or  written  questions  that

students respond to, in tum, by spea힘ng or writing.

•  Students can be encouraged to write their own episodes, or to complete 따1
episode  whose  resolution  or clinlax  is  not  presented  (such  as  the  above
conversation).

•  Those written episodes nlight then be dramatized in the classroom by the

students.

Thrεr people wεrε on a train in England.  As they approachεd what appεared to bε
Wεmberly Station, onε of thε travelers said,“ Is thisWembεrly? ” “ No,” replied a second

The importance of listεning in languagε learning can hardly be ov1εrestimated.
Through  recεption, we  internalizε linguistic  information without which wε could
not  producε language.  In  classrooms,  studεnts  always  do  more  listεning  than
speaking.  Listening  compεtencε is  universally “ largεr” than spεaking competence.
Is  it  any  wondεr, thεn, that  in  recent  yεars thε language-teaching  prof;εssion has
placed a concerted εmphasis on listεning comprehension?

Listεning comprζhension has  not always  drawn the attention of ℃ducat。rs to
the extεnt that it  now has.  Perhaps human bεings have a natural tendεncy to look
at speaking as  the major indεx oflanguagε proficiency.  Consider, for example, our
commonly  used  quεry, “Do  you  spεak Japanεse? ”  Of  course  wε don’t  mean  to
εxclude comprehension when we say that, but whεn we think of foreign language
learning,  we  first  think  of  speaking.  In  thε  de cadεs of  the  1950s  and  1960s,
language-teaching  mεthodology was  prεoccupied with  thε spokεn language, and
classrooms full  of students could bε heard pεrforming their oral  dr피s.  It was not
uncommon for students to practice phrases orally thεy didn’t  ev1εn undεrstand!

LISTENING COMPREHENSION IN PEDAGOGICAi‘  RESEARCH

Listεning  as  a  major  component  in  language  lεan피1g  따id  teaching  first  hit  the
spotlight in thε late 1970s with JamεsAshεr’S (197η work on Total Physical Rεsponse
(see Chapter 2).  In TPR the role of compr1εhension was given prominence as learnεrs

299

300

CHAPTER  18  Teaching Listening

were given great quantities of language  to listen to before they were encouraged to
respond orally.  Similarly, the NaturalApproach (ag따n see Chapter 2) recommended a
significant ‘패ent period” during which learners were allowed the sεcurity of listεning
without being forced to go through the an성℃ty of speaking before they were “ ready”
to do so.

Such  approachεs werε an  outgrowth  of  a  variety  of  research  studiεs  that
showed evidεnce of the importance of input in second language  acquisition (see
PLLT,  Chapter 10).  Stephen Krashen (1985), for example, borrowing insights from
first  language acquisition, stressed the significance of comprehensible input, or
the  aural  reception  of language  that  is  just  a  little  beyond  the  learner's  present
ability.  About the samε time, researchers were also stressing the crucial importance
of whatever  mental  processes were  brought  to  bear  on  the  learner’S  converting
input into  intake, or that which is  actually  stored  in  a  learner's  competence.  In
other words, you can be “ exposed” to great quantities  of input, but what counts is
the  linguistic  information  that  you  ultimately  glean  from  that  exposure  through
conscious  and  subconscious  attention,  through  cognitive  strategies  of retention,
through feedback, and through interaction.  As we shall see, the conversion of input
into  intake  is  absolutely  crucial  in  considεring the  role  of listening  in  language
learning.  As  you  consider  the  role  of  listεning  techniques  in  your  classes,  you
ultimately want to ask yourself what students have taken in from perhaps an array
of comprehension activity.

Subsequent pedago1깅cal research on listening  comprehension made  significant
refinements in the process of listening.  Studies looked at the effect of a  number of
different contextual characteristics 따id how they affect  the speed and efficiency of
processing aural language.  Rubin (1994) identified five such factors: text, interlocutor,
task,  listeneζ and  process  characteristics.  In  εach case,  important  elements  of the
listening  process  were  identified.  For  example,  the  listener  characteristics  of
proficiency,  memory,  attention, affect,  age, gender,  background  schemata, and  even
le았띠ng disabilities in the L1  외l a당ect thε process of listening (pp. 206-10).

In more recent research, attention is being given to ηpes of meaning involved
in  the  act  of  comprehending  language  (Flowerdew  &  Miller,  2005).  In  this
perspective,  phonological,  syntactic,  semantic,  and  pragmatic  knowledge  are
considered, along  with  nonverbal  elements  involved  in  most  real-world  (face-to
face)  listening.  Other studies  have  looked  at  the  extent  to  which first  language
listening ab피ty contributes to one’s performance of second language listening, with
the interesting su잃:estion by Vandergrift (2006) that if you're a good listener in your
L1, you stand a good chance of doing well in L2 listening tasks.  Further, the research
has  examined  the  role  of strategic  factors  and  of strategies-based  instruction  in
listening  comprehension  αlowerdew &  M피er,  2005;  Hinkel,  2006;  Mendelsohn,
1998;  Rost,  2005; V따ider흉ift,  2003,  2004).  Studies  tend  to  agree  that  liste따lg,
especi외ly  for  academic  따id professional  contexts,  is  a  highly  refined  skill  that
requires a learner's attention to a battery of strategies for extracting me따ling from
texts (Flowerdew, 1994).

AN INTERACTIVE MODEL OF LISTENING COMPREHENSION

Listening  is  not  a  one-way  street.  It  is  not  merely  the  process  of a  unidirectional
rεcεiving of audiblε symbols.  One fac℃t-the first  stεp-of listening comprehension
is  the  psychomotor  process  of  receiving  sound  waves  through  the  ear  and
transmitting  nεrv℃ impulses  to  the  brain.  But  that is  just  the  beginning of what is
clearly an interactive process as  the brain acts on thε impulses, bringing to bear a
number of differ,εnt cognitive and affective  m℃chanisms.

The  following  εight proc℃sses (adaptεd from  Clark  &  Clark,  1977; Richards,
1983) are all involvεd in comprεhension.  With the exception of thε initial and final
processes below, no sεquεnce is  impliεd hεrε; they all  occur if not simultaneously,
thεn in extremely rapid succεssion.  Nεurological timε must be viewed in tεrms of
microseconds.

1.  The hεarer processεs what wε’II call “ raw speech” and holds an “ image” of it in
short-term m℃morγ.  This imagε consists of the constituεnts (phrasεs, clauses,
cohesive markεrs, intonation, and str1εss patterns) of a  str,εam ofspεech.

2.  The hearer det℃rm inεs thε typε ofspεech ev'εnt being processed (for

exam plε, a  conv'εrsation, a  speech, a radio broadcast) and then appropriately
“colors” thε interpr1εtation of the percεived message.

3.  Thε hεarer iruεrs thε objectivεs of the spεaker through consideration of the
typε ofspεech event, the context, and the content.  So, for example, one
determinεs whether the speak℃r wishes to persuadε, to rεquεst, to exch와1ge
pleasantries, to affirm, to dεny, to inform, and so forth.  Thus the function of
the message is inferred.

4.  The hearer recalls background information (or g한~?ta; see Chapter 20 for
morε on this topic) relevant to the particular context and subject matter.  A
lifetime of expεriences and knowledge is used to perform cognitive
associations in ord℃r to bring a plausible intεrpretation to thε mεssage.

302

[ HAPTER 7 8  Teaching  Listening

5.  Thε hεarer assigns a  litεral meaning to th℃ Uttεranee.  This process involvεs a

set of sεmantic interpretations of the surface strings that the εar has
perceiv'εd.  In many instances, literal and intεndεd (see #6) meanings match.
So, for  examplε, if one of your students walks into your office  whilε you arε
madly grading papεrs and says she has a quεstion that shε would appreciate
your answer to, thεn says, “ Do you have thε tim℃?;’ the literal mεaning (Do you
possess enough time now to an5'\℃r mε?) is  appropriate.  However, this
procεss may take on a pεripheral role in cases wherε literal meanings are
irrelevant to the messag℃, as in metaphorical or “ idiomatic” language.  If, for
℃xamplε, a stranger sitting beside you on a bus has been silent for a pεriod of
time and thεn says, “ Do you have the time?,” your appropriate responsε is  not
yes or no but rather, “ It’s a  quartεr to nine” or whatever.  Second languagε
learners must, in such cases, learn to go “ beneath” thε surfacε of such
language in order to interpret corr,εctly.

6.  The hεar'εr assigns an intεnded meaning to the uttεranee.  Thε person on thε
bus intended to find out what time of day it was, ev,℃n though the litεral
mεaning didn’t  directly convey that message.  How often do
misunderstandings stεm from false  assumptions that are made on thε hεar,εr’s
paπ about thε intεndεd mεaning of the speakεr? A key to human
communication is  the ability to match perceived meaning with intended
mεaning.  This matchmaking, of course, can extend well beyond simplε
metaphorical and idiomatic language.  It can apply to short and long strεtches
of discourse, and its breakdown can bε caused by carelεSS spεech, inattention
of thε hearer, conceptual complexity, contextual miscu℃s,psych이ogical
barriεrs, and a host of other performancε variables.

7.  The hearer d℃termines whether information should be retained in short-tεrm
or long-term memory.  Short-term mεmory-a mattεr of a few seconds-is
appropriate in contexts that call for a quick oral response from the hεarer.
Long-tεrmmemotγ is  mor'ε common when, say, you are processing
information in a lecture.  끼1ere are, of coursε, many points in bεtwεεn.
8.  The hεarεr delεtes the form in which the message was originally receiv,εd.
Thε words, phrases, and sentenc℃S are quickly forgottεn- “pruned” -in 99
percent of spεech acts.  You  have no ne℃d to retain this sort of C<핑nitiV<ε
“ clutter.” Instead, the important information, if any (see #7 above), is  rεtainεd
conceptually.  (See also PLLT,  Chaptεr 4.)

It  should  be  clear  from  the  foregoing  that  listening  comprehεnsion is  an
interactiv'ε process.  After thε initial reception of sound, we human beings pεrform
at least sev1εn other major operations on that set of sound waves.  In conversational
settings, of course,  furthεr interaction  tak,εs place  immediately after  the  listening
stage  as  the  hεarer becomes  speaker  in  a  rεsponse of some  kind.  All  of  thεsε
processes are important for you to keεp in mind as you tεach.  They are all  relεvant
to a learner’S purpose for listening, to pεrformancε factors that may cause difficulty

'IYPES OF SPOKEN IANGUAGE

Much of our language-teaching energy is devoted to instruction in mastering En행sh
conversation.  However,  numerous  othεr  forms  of  spoken  languag'ε  are  also
import와it  to  incorporate  into  a  l와iguage course, especially  in  teaching  listening
comprehension.  As you plan lessons or curricula, the classification of types of oral
language  shown in Figure  18.1  should  enable you to  see  the  big  picture of what
teaching aural comprehension entails.

In monologues, when one speaker uses spoken language for any length of time,
as  in  speeches, lectures, readings, news  broadcasts, and  the  like,  the  hearer  must
process long stretches of speech without inteπuption-the stream of speech W피
go on whether or not the hearer comprehends.  Planned, as opposed to unpl따med,
monologues differ considerably in their discourse structures.  Planned monologues
(such as speeches and other prewritten material) usually manifest little redundancy
and  are  therefore  relatively  difficult  to  comprehend.  Unplanned  monologues
(impromptu lectures 따id long “stories” in conversations, for example) exhibit more
redundancy, which  makes  for  ease  in  comprehension, but  the  presence  of more
performance variables and other hesitations (see below) can either help or hinder
comprehension.

Dialogues  involv;ε  two  or  more  speakers  and  can  be  subdivided  into  those
exchanges that pf()mote..soc때l relationships (interpersonal) and those for which the
purpo앞 is  to  convey proposition외 or factual  information  (transaαional).  In each
case,p따ticipants tnay have a good de외 ofshar℃d knowledge (background information,
schemata); therefore, the  fam피았ity of the  interlocutors  w피 produce  conversations
with more 잃sump디ons, implications, and other mεanings hidden between the lines.
In conversations between or among P따디cipants who are unfamiliar with each other,
references  and  meanings  have  to  be  made  more  explicit  to  잃SW℃ εffective
comprehension.  When such refer℃nces 따e not explicit, misunderstandings can easily
follow.

Onε could also  have subdivided dialogues bεtween thosε in which the hearer
is a participant and those in which the hεarer is an “ eavesdroppεr.” In both cases, thε
above  conversational  descriptions  apply,  but  the  major-and  hi양tly significant
differencε is that in thε lattεr the h℃ar'εr is, as in monologues, unablε to int℃rrupt or
othεrwisε participatε vocally in the negotiation of m℃aning.

Remεmbεr that  in  all  cases  thesε categoriεs arε really  not  discrete,  mutually
εxclusive  domains;  rathεr,  εach dichotomy,  as  usual,  represεnts  a  continuum  of
possibilities.  For examplε, ev,εηday social conversations can easily contain elements
of transactional dialogues, and vieε vεrsa.  Similarly, “ familiar” participants may share
veη little common knowledg'ε on a particular topic.  If εach catego1γ, then, is viewed
as an end point, you can aim your tεaching at appropriate rangεs in bεtween.

WHAT MAKES LISTENING DIFFICULT?

As you contemplatε designing lεssons and tεchniquεs for teaching listεning s잉lls,or
that  have  listening  components  in  them,  a  numbεr of special  characteristics  of
spoken  language  nεεd to  be  takεn into  considεration.  Second  languag,ε learnεrs
need  to  pay special  attention  to  such  factors  becausε they  stron입y influεncε thε
processing of speech, and can even j)JQck compr'εhension if they are not attended to.
In other words, they can make  thε listεning process difficult.  The following  eight
characteristics of spoken language are adapted from sεvεral sources (Dunkel, 1991;
Flowerdew & Mille℃ 2005; Richards, 1983; Ur,  1984).

1.  Clustering

In written languag'ε we are  conditionεd to attend to th℃ sεntence as  thε basic
unit  of  organization.  In  spokεn language,  duε  to  mεmotγ limitations  and  our
predisposition  for “ chunking,”  or clustering, we  break  down  spεech into  smaller
groups of words.  Clauses  ar'ε common constituents, but phrases within clausεs ar,ε
ev1εn more εasily retainεd for comprehension.  In teaching listening comprehension,
therefore,  you  neεd to  hεlp students  to  pick  out  manageable  dustεrs of words;
somεtimes second  language  learners  will  try  to  retain  overly  long  constituents  (a
whole sentence or ev,εn several sentenc℃s), or thεy W피 err in the othεr direction in
trying to attεnd to eveη word in an utterance.

2.  Redundancy

Spokεn language, uruike most written language, has a good deal of rεdundancy.
Thε  next  time  you’rε  in  a  com℃rsation,  noticε  the  rephrasings,  rεpetitions,
elaborations, and little insεrtions of“I mεan” and “you know." Such redundancy hεlps
the  hearer  to  process  meaning  by  offering  more  timε and  extra  information.
Learners  can  train  thεmselv1εs to  profit  from  such  redundancy by first  becoming
aware  that  not  εvery  nεw sεntεncε  or  phrase  w피 nεcessarily  contain  nεw
information and by looking for  thε signals  of redundancy.  Consider the following
excerpt of a conv'εrsation.

th℃y can  learn  to  takε ad van tagε of  rεdundanciεs as  well  as  othεr markers  that
providε more procεssing time.

3.  Reduced forms

\Vhile  spokεn languagε does indeed contain a good dεal of rεdundancy, it also
has  many rεduced forms  and sentence fragments.  Rεduction can be phonological
(“Djeetyet?” for “Did you εat yet?”), morphological (contractions likε “I’U”), syntactic

classroom  learners  who  may  have  initially  beεn exposed  to  thε full  forms  of th℃
English language.

4.  Performance variables

In  spokεn language,  excεpt for  plannεd discoursε (spεεch es, lεctures, etc.),

In writtεn form  this looks like gibberish, but it’s  thε kind of language wε hεar
and process all the time.  Learners have to train themsεlv,εs to listεn for meaning in

Everyday  casual  spεεch  by  nativ℃  spεakers  also  commonly  contains

to hear soonεr or later.

5.  Colloquial language

Learners who have been εxposed to standard writtεn En입ish and/or “textbook”
languagε sometimes find  it surprising and difficult to deal with colloquial language.
Idioms, slang, reduced h] rms, and  shared  cultural  knowledge  are  all  manifested  at
