6.  Rate of delivery

Virtually ev,εry language learner initially thinks that native  speakεrs spεak too
fast! Actually, as Jack Richards (1983) points out, the  numbεr and length of pausεs

continue to flow!

7.  Stress, rhythm, and intonation

The  prosodic  features  of  the  English  language  ar,℃  very  important  for
comprehension.  Because English is a  stress-timed language, English  spεech can bε
ateπor for some learners as mouthfuls of syllables come sp피ing out between stress
points.  The sentence “The  PREsidεnt is  INTεrεsted in eLIMinating  the  ℃mBARgo,”
with  4  stressed  야rllabl℃S out of 18, theoretically  takes  about  the  S따ne amount  of

like sarcasm, endearment, insult, solicitation, praise, εtc.

8.  Interaction

Unless a  language learner's objεctiv'ε is exclusively to master some specialized
skill  like  monitoring radio  broadcasts or attεnding lectures, interaction will  play a
large role in listening comprehension.  Conversation is  especially subject to all  the

CHAPTER  18  Teaching  Listening

307

rules of interaction: negotiation, clarification; attending signals; tum-taking; and topic
nomination, maintenance, and termination (see Chapter 8  of PLLη. So, to learn to
listen is also to learn to respond and to continue a chain of listening and responding.
Classroom  techniquεs  that  include  listening  components  must  at  some  point
include instruction in the two-way nature of listεning.  Studεnts need to undεrstand
that  good  listεners (in  conversation)  ar'ε  good  responders.  They  know  how  to
negotiate meaning (to give feedback, to ask for clarification, to maintain a topic) so
that  the  process  of comprehending  can  be  complete  rather  than  be  aborted  by
insufficient interaction.

A fourth-century Chinese proverb says it morε eloquently:

Not to let a  word get in the way of its sentence,
Nor to let a sentence get in the way of its intention,
But to send your mind out to  meet the intention as a guest;
THAT is understanding.

MICROSKILIS AND MACROSKILIS OF USTENING

In his seminal article on teaching listening skills,Jack Richards (1983) provided
a  comprehεnsive  taxonomy  of  aural  skills,  which  he  called  microskills,
involved  in  convεrsational  discourse.  I  have  adapted  Richards’s  original
microskills  into  a  list  of micro- and  macroskills, the  latter  to  designate  skills
that are  technically  at  the  discourse  level.  The  former  pertain  to  skills  at  the
sentence level.  Such lists are  useful in helping you to  break down just what it
is that your learners nεed to actually pe따rm as they acquire effiεctive listening
strategies.  Through  a  checklist  of micro- and  macroskills, you  can get  a  good
idea  of  what  your  techniques  need  to  cover  in  the  domain  of  listening
comprehension.  As  you  plan  a  specific  technique  or listening  modulε, such  a
list  helps  you  to  focus  on  clearly  conceptualized  objectives.  And  in  your
evaluation of listening, these micro- and macroskills can become testing criteria.
Table  18.1  on pagε 308 is just such a checklist, adapted from Richards and other
sources.

It is important to note that these 17 skills apply to conversational discourse.
Less  interactive  forms  of  discourse,  such  as  listening  to  monologues  like
academic  lectures,  include  further,  more  specific  micro- and  macroskills
(Blackwell &  Naber, 2006; Flowerdew, 1994; Sarosy &  Sherak, 2006).  Students in
an  academic  setting  nεed to  be able  to  perform such  things  as  ident퍼ring the
structure  of  a  lecture,  weeding  out  what  may  be  irrelevant  or  tangential,
detecting  thε possible  biases  of the  speakεζ critically  evaluating  thε speaker's
assertions, and developing means (through note taking, for example) of retaining
the content of a  lecture.

308

CHAPTER  18  Teaching Listening

Table  18.1. Micro- and  macroskills of listening comprehension (adapted from  Richards,  1983)

Microskills

1.  Retain  chunks of language of different  lengths  in  short-term  memory.
2.  Discriminate among the distinctive sounds of English.
3.  Recognize  English  stress  patterns,  words  in  stressed  and  unstressed  positions,

rhythmic  structure,  intonational  contours,  and their role  in  signaling  information.

4.  Recognize  reduced forms  of words.
5.  Distinguish  word  boundaries,  recognize a core of words,  and  interpret word order

patterns and their significance.

6.  Process speech  at different  rates  of delivery.
7.  Process speech  containing pauses,  errors,  corrections,  and other performance

variables.

8.  Recognize grammatical  word classes (nouns,  verbs,  etc.),  systems  (e.g.,  tense,

agreement,  pluralization),  patterns,  rules,  and elliptical forms.

9.  Detect sentence constituents and distinguish  between  major and  minor

constituents.

10.  Recognize that a  particular meaning may  be expressed  in  different grammatical

forms.

Macroskills

11.  Recognize cohesive devices  in  spoken  discourse.
12.  Recc핑nize the communicative functions  of utterances, according to  situations,

participants, goals.

13.  Infer situations,  paπicipants, goals  using  real-world  knowledge.
14.  From  events,  ideas,  etc.,  described,  predict outcomes,  infer  links  and  connections
between events, deduce causes and effects,  and detect such  relations  as  main
idea,  supporting idea,  new  information, given  information, generalization,  and
exemplification.

15.  Distinguish  between  literal  and implied meanings.
16.  Use facial,  kinesic,  body  language,  and other nonverbal  clues to decipher

meanings.

17.  Develop and use a  battery of listening strategies,  such as detecting key  words,
guessing the meaning of words from  context,  appealing for  help,  and signaling
comprehension or lack thereof.

1YPES OF CIASSROOM USTENING PERFORMANCE

With literally hundreds of possible techniques available for teaching listening S셈Us,

performance-that is, what your students do in a  listening technique.  Sometimes
these  types  of  pεrformance are  embedded  in  a  broader  technique  or  task,  따id
sometimes they are themselves the sum total of the activity of a technique.

1.  Reactive

2.  Intensive

Tεchniques whosε only purpose is  to focus  on components (phonεmεs, words,

3.  Responsive

A significant proportion of classroom listening activity consists of short stretches

In longer stretches of discourse such as monologues of a couple of minutes

PRINCIPLES FOR TEACIDNG LISTENING SKIUS

Several decades of rεsearch and practice in teaching listening comprehension have
yielded  some  practical  principles  for  designing  classroom  aural  comprehension
techniques.  These principles should help you to create your own techniques and
activities.  Some  of them,  especially  the  second  and  third,  actually  apply  to  any
technique; the others are more germane to listening.

1.  Include a  focus on listening in an integrated-skills course.

Assuming that your curriculum is dedicated to the integration of 때l four skills,
remember  that  each  of the  separate  s힘lls  deserves  sped외 focus  in  appropriate
doses.  It is easy to adopt a p피losophy of just letting students “ experience” language

CHAPTER  18  Teaching  Listening

311

without carεful attention to componεnt sk피s.  Because aural comprehension itsεIf
cannot be oveπly “ obsεπed” (see#4 bεlow), teachers somεtimes incorrεctly assume
that the input provided in the classroom will always be com℃rtεd into intake.  The
creation  of  effectivε  listening  tεchniquεs  requires  studiεd  attention  to  all  the
principlεs of listening air'εady summarizεd in this chapter.

2.  Use techniques that are intrinsically motivating.

Appeal to listenεrs ’ pεrsonal intεrests and goals.  Sincε background information
(schemata) is an important factor in listening, take into full account the experiencεs,
goals, and abilities of your students as  you dεsign lεssons.  Also, remember that the
cultural  background(s) of your students  can be both facilitating  and  internεring in
the process of listening.  Then, once a Iεchnique is  launchεd, try to construct it  in
such a way that students arε caught up in the activity and feel sεIf-propelled toward
its final  objective.

3.  Utilize authentic language and contexts.

Authentic language and real-world tasks enable students to see the relevance of
classroom activity to thεir long-tεrm communicative goals.  If you introduce natural
texts  (for a  list  of real-world  texts, see  page  288)  rathεr than  concocted, artificial
material, students will more readily div,℃ into the activity.

4.  Carefully consider the form of listeners’ responses.

Com pr,εhεnsion  itself  is  not  εxternally  obsεπable.  Wε cannot  peer  into  a
learnεr’s brain through a little window and εmpirically observe what is stored ther,ε
aftεr someone else has said something.  We  can only iηfer that certain things have
bεen comprehεndεd through  students'  overt  (verbal  or  nonverbal)  responses  to
speech.  It is  thεrεfore important for  teachers to design  tεchniques in  such a  way
that  students'  responsεs indicate  whether  or  not  thεir comprehension  has  bεen
correct.  Lund  (1990)  offer'εd  ninε diffiεrεnt  ways  that  we  can  chεck listeners’
comprehension:

•  doing-thε listεnεr responds physically to a command
•  choosing-the listεner selects from alternatives such as pictures, objects, and

tεxts

•  transferring-the listener draws a  picturε of what is  hεard
•  answering-the listener answers questions about thε message
•  condensing-the listener outlinεs or takεs notεs on a  lecture
•  extending-the listener provides an ending to a  stoπ heard
•  duplicating-the listener translates thε messagε into the native language or

repeats it verbatim

•  modeling-the listεnεr orders a meal, for example, after listεning to a model

ordεr

•  conversing-the listener εngag'εs in a conversation that indicatεs appropriate

processing of information

312

CHAPTER  18  Teaching  Listening

5.  Encourage the development of listening strategies.

Most  for,εign language students are simply not aware of how to listen.  One of
your  jobs  is  to  equip  them  with  listening  strategies  that  εxtend  beyond  the
classroom.  Draw thεir attεntion to thε valuε of such strategiεs as

•  looking for kεy words
•  looking for nonverbal cues to meaning
•  predicting a speaker’s  purpose by the context of thε spoken discourse
•  associating  information  with  onε ’s εxisting cognitive  structure  (activating

background information)

•  guessing at meanings
•  seeking clarification
•  listening for the general gist
•  various tεst-taking strategies for listening cornpreh℃nsion

As  you “ teach lεarnεrs how to learn” by hεlping them to dεvelop their overall
strategic competence (see Chapter 16), strategies for effective listening can becornε
a highly significant part of thεir chances for successful learning.

6.  Include both bottom-up and top-down listening techniques.

Spεεch-processing theory distinguishes between two types of processing in both
listening and rεacting cornprehεnsion.  Bottom-up processing proceeds from sounds
to words to grammatical  relationships  to lexical  me없tings, etc., to a  final “message.'’
Top-down  processing  is  evoked  from  “a  bank  of  prior  knowledge  and  global
expectations”  (Moriεy, 1991a, p. 87) and  other background  information  (schemata)
that the listener brings to thε text.  Bottom-up techniques typic따ly focus on sounds,
words, intonation, grammatical structures, 따id other components of spoken languagε.
Top-Oown  techniques  are  more  concerned  with  the  activation  of schemata,  with
deriving mea띠ng, with global understanding, and with the interpretation of a text.  It
is  import따1t for learners to operatε from both directions since both can offer keys to
dεterrnining the meaning of spoken discourse.  But  in a  communicative, interactive
context, you  don’t  W따1t  to  dwell  too  hεavily on the  bottom-up, for  to  do  so  may
hamper  thε development  of  a  learner's  따I-important  automaticity  in  processing
speεch.

LISTENING TECHNIQUES FROM BEGINNING TO ADVANCED

Techniques  for  teaching  list℃ning w피 vaηr considerably  across  the  proficiency
continuum.  Chapter  7  has  already  dealt  with  general  characteristics.  Listening
techniques are no exception to the genεral rule.  Table  18.2 provides thrεc lists of
techniques  for  εach of  three  proficiency  levels.  Each  list  is  broken  down  into
bottom-up, top깅own, and interactive typεs of activity.

313

The  importancε of listεning comprehεnsion in  language  learning  should  by
now be quite apparent.  As we move on to look at spea성ng skills, always r℃mεmbεr
thε evεr-pr'εsεnt relationship  among  all  four  skills  and  the  nεcessity in  authεntic,

Table  18.2. Techniques for  teaching  listening comprehension  (adapted  from  Peterson,  1991,
pp.  114  121)

FOR  BEGINNING  LEVEL  LISTENERS

Bottom-Up  Exercises

1.  Goal:  Dis [ riminate Between  Intonation  Contours  in  Sentences

Listen  to  a sequence of sentence patterns with  either rising  or falling  intonation.  Place a
check  in  column  1  (rising)  or column 2  (falling),  depending on  the  pattern  you  hear.

2.  Goal:  Discriminate Between  Phonemes

Listen  to  pairs  of words.  Some pairs differ  in  their final  consonant,  and  some pairs are the

same.  Circle the word  “ same” or “ different,'’  depending on  what you  hear.

3 ‘  Goal:  Selective  Listening for Morphological Endings

Listen  to  a series of sentences.  Circle “ yes”  if  the verb  has  an  -ed ending,  and circle “ no”  if

it  does  not.

Listen  to  a series of sentences.  On your  answer sheet,  cir[ le the one (of three)  verb forms

contained  in  the sentence that you  hear.

4.  Goal:  Select Details from  the Text  (Word Recognition)

Match  a  word  that you  hear with  its  picture.
Listen  to  a weather  「eport. Look  at a  list  of words and circle the words that you  hear.
Listen  to a senten[ e that contains clock time.  Circle the clock time that you  hear,  among

three choices (5:30,  5:45,  6:15) ‘

Listen  to  an  advertisement,  select the price of an  item,  and  write  the amount on  a  pri[ e tag.
Listen  to a series of recorded  telephone  messages from  an  answering machine.  Fill  in  a

chart with  the following  information  from  each caller:  name,  number,  time,  and  message.

5.  Goal:  Listen  for  Normal Sentence Word Order

Listen  to a  sho「t dialogue and fill  in  the missing words that  have  been  deleted  in  a  partial

transcript.

Top-Down  Exercises

6.  Goal:  Discriminate Between  Emotional Reactions

Listen  to  a sequence of utterances.  Place a check  in  the column that  desc 「ibes the

emotional  reaction  that  you  hear:  interested,  happy,  surprised,  or unhappy.

7 ‘  Goal:  Get the  Gist of a Sentence

Listen  to  a sentence des[ ribing a picture and select the correct picture.

8.  Goal:  Recognize the Topi[

Listen  to  a dialogue and decide where the conversation occurred.  Circle the correct

location  among three multiple-choice items.

Listen  to  a conversation  and  look at the pictured greeting cards.  Decide which  of the

greeting cards was  sent.  Write the greeting  under the appropriate card.

Listen  to a conversation  and decide what the  people are talking about.  Choose the picture

that shows the topic.

(Continued)

314

CHAPTER  18  Teaching Listening

Interactive Exercises

9.  Goal:  Build a Semantic Network of Word Associations

Listen  to a word and  associate all  the  related  words that come to  mind.

10.  Goal:  Recognize a Familiar Word and Relate  It  to a Category

Listen  to words from  a shopping  list and  match each  word to the store that sells  it.

11.  Goal:  Follow Directions

Listen  to  a description of a route  and  trace  it on  a map.

FOR  INTERMEDIATE  LEVEL  LISTENERS

Bottom-Up Exercises
12.  Goal:  Recognize Fast Speech  Forms

Listen  to a series  of sentences that contain  unstressed  function  words.  Circle your choice

among three words on the  answer sheet-for example:  띠p,''’  “a,” “of.”

13.  Goal:  Find the Stressed Syllable

Listen  to words of two (or three)  syllables.  Mark them  for word  stress  and predict the

pronunciation of the unstressed  syllable.

14.  Goal:  Recognize Words  with Reduced Syllables

Read  a list of polysyllabic words and  predict which  syllabic vowel will be dropped.  Listen

to the words read  in  fast  speech  and  confirm your prediction.
15.  Goal:  Recognize Words as  They Are Linked in  the Speech Stream

Listen  to a series  of short sentences with consonant/vowel  linking between  words.  Mark the

linkages on your answer sheet.

16.  Goal:  Recognize Pertinent Details in  the Speech Stream

Listen  to a shoπ dialogue between  a boss  and a secretary regarding changes  in the daily

schedule.  Use an  appointment calendar.  Cross  out appointments that are being changed
and  write in  new ones.

Listen  to announcements of airline arrivals and departures. With a model  of an  airline

information board  in front of yoι fill  in  the flight numbers,  destinations,  gate numbers,
and  departure times.

Listen to a series  of short dialogues after reading questions that apply to the dialogues.

While listening,  find the answers to questions about prices,  places,  names,  and  numbers.
Example:  “Where are the  shoppersγ "How much  is  whole wheat bread?”

Listen  to a short telephone conversation  between  a customer and a service station  manager.
Fill  in a chart which  lists  the car repairs  that must be done.  Check the part of the car that
needs  repair,  the reason,  and the approximate cost.

Top-Down  Exercises
17.  Goal:  Analyze Discourse Structure to Suggest Effective  Listening Strategies

Listen  to six  radio commercials with attention to the use  of music,  repetition of key words,

and  number of speakers. Talk about the effect these  techniques  have on  the  listeners.

18.  Goal:  Listen  to  Identify the Speaker or the Topic

Listen  to a series  of radio commercials. On your answer sheet,  choose among four types of

sponsors or products and  identify the picture that goes  with the commercial.

19.  Goal:  Listen  to  Evaluate Themes and Motives

Listen to a series  of radio commercials.  On your answer sheet are four possible  motives that
the companies use  to appeal  to their customers.  Circle all the motives that you feel  each
commercial  promotes: escape from  realiη, family security,  snob appeal,  sex  appeal.

CHAPTER  18  Teaching  Listening

315

20.  Goal:  Find Main Ideas  and Supporting Details

Listen  to a short conversation  between two friends.  On  your answer sheet are scenes  from
television  programs.  Find  and  write the  name of the program  and the channel.  Decide
which speaker watched which program.

21.  Goal:  Make Inf강rences

Listen  to  a series  of sentences,  which  may  be  either statements or questions. After each
sentence,  answer  inferential questions such  as  “Where  might the speaker be?” “How
might the speaker  be  feeling?” “ What might the speaker be  referring to?”

Listen  to a series  of sentences.  After each  sentence,  suggest a possible context for the

sentence (place,  situation,  time,  participants).

Interactive Exercises
22.  Goal:  Discriminate Between  Registers  of Speech  and Tones  of Voice

Listen  to  a series  of sentences.  On your answer sheet,  mark whether the sentence  is  polite

or  imp이 ite.

23.  Goal:  Recc핑nize Missing Grammar Markers  in Colloquial Speech

Listen  to  a series of short questions  in which  the auxiliary verb and subject have been

deleted.  Use grammatical  knowledge to fill  in  the  missing words:  I“Have you]  got some
extra?”

Listen  to  a series  of questions with  reduced  verb auxiliary and  subject and  identify the

missing verb  (does  it/is  it)  by checking the form  of the  main verb.  Examples:  “’Zit come
with anything else?",'“ Zit arriving on  time?”

24.  Goal:  Use  Knowledge of Reduced Forms  to  Clarify the Meaning of an  Utterance

Listen  to a short sentence  containing a reduced  form.  Decide what the sentence  means.  On
your answe「 sheet,  choose the one (of three) alternatives that  is  the  best paraphrase of the
sentence you  heard.  Example: You  hear ”You  can't be  happy with that." You  read:  (a)  “Why
can't you  be  happy?” (b)  “That will make you  happy."  (c)  “l don’t think you  are  happy.”

25.  Goal:  Use Context to  Build Listening Expectations

Read  a short want-ad  describing job qualifications from  the employment section of a

newspaper.  Brainstorm  additional  qualifications that would be  important for that type of
job.

26.  Goal:  Listen  to  Confirm Your  Expectations

Listen  to short radio  advertisements  for jobs that are available.  Check the job [ lualifications

against your expectations.

27.  Goal:  Use Context to  Build Expectations.  Use  Bottom-Up Processing to Recognize Missing

Words.  Compare Your  Predictions  to What You  Actually Heard
Read  some telephone  messages  with  missing words.  Decide what kinds of information are
missing so  you  know what to  listen for.  Listen  to the  information and  fill  in the blanks.
Finally,  discuss  with the class  what strategies you  used  for your predictions.

28.  Goal:  Use  Incomplete Sensory Data  and Cultural Background Information to Construct a

More Complete Understanding of a 7논•xt
Listen  to  one side of a telephone conversation.  Decide what the topic of the conversation

might be and create a title for  it.

Listen  to the beginning of a conversation  between  two people and answer questions about
the number of paπicipants, their ages,  gender,  and social  roles.  Guess  the time of day,
location, temperature,  season,  and  topic.  Choose among some statements to guess  what
might come  next.

Bottom-Up Exercises
29.  Goal:  Use  Features  。f Sentence Stress  and Volume to  떠entify Important Information  for Note

Taking
Listen  to a number of sentences  and  extract the  content words,  which are read  with greater

stress.  Write the content words  as  notes.

30.  Goal:  Become Aware of Sentence-Level Features  in  Lecture Text

Listen  to a segment of a lecture while reading a transcript of the  material.  Notice the

incomplete sentences,  pauses,  and  verbal  fillers.
31.  Goal:  Become Aware of 。rganizational Cues  in Lecture Text

Look at a lecture transcript and circle all  the cue words  used  to enumerate the main  points.

Then  listen to the  lecture segment and  note the organizational  cues.

32.  Goal:  Become Aware of Lexical and Suprasegmental Markers for Definitions

Read  a list of lexical cues that signal  a definition; listen  to signals of the speaker's  intent,

such  as  rhetorical  questions;  listen to special  intonation patterns and  pause patterns  used
with appositives.

Listen  to short lecture segments  that contain  new terms and  their definitions in context.  Use

knowledge of lexical  and  intonational cues  to  identify the definition of the word.

33.  Goal:  Identify Specific Points of Information

Read  a skeleton  outline of a lecture in  which  the main categories  are  given  but the specific

blanks.

Top-Down Exercises
34.  Goal:  Use the Introduction to the Lecture to Predict Its  Focus  and Direction

Listen  to the  introductory section of a lecture. Then  read  a number of topics on your answer

sheet and  choose the topic that best expresses  what the lecture will discuss.

35.  G。al: Use  the  Lecture Transcript to  Predict the Content of the Next Section

Read  a section  of a lecture transcript.  Stop  reading at a juncture point and predict what will

come next. Then  read  on to confirm your prediction.

36.  Go,하; Find the Main Idea  of a Lecture Segment

Listen  to a section  of a lecture that describes a statistical trend. While you  listen,  look at

lecture.

Interactive Exercises
3 7.  Goal:  Use Incoming Details to Determine the Accuracy of Predictions About Content

Listen  to the  introductory sentences to predict some of the main  ideas you  expect to hear  in
the  lecture. Then  listen to the lecture.  Note whether or not the  instructor talks about the
points you  predicted.  If she/he does,  note a detail about the point.

Section
Listen  to a section  of a lecture and take  notes on the  important details. Then  relate the

details to form  an  understanding of the main point of that section. Choose from a list of
possible controlling ideas.

39.  Goal:  Make Inferences  by Identifying Ideas on the Sentence Level That Lead to Evaluative

Statements

Listen  to  a lecture segment for  its  gist. Then  listen  to  a statement from  which  words  have

ASSESSING LISTENING IN THE CIASSROOM

Every classroom lesson  involvεs somε form of assessment, whεther it’S in the form
of informal, unplanned, and intuitivε teacher processing and feedback, or in formal,
pr'εpared, scored  tεsts.  In  ordεr to  appropriatεly call  some  attεntion to  this  very
important  rolε  that  teachers  must  assume,  I  offer-in  this  and  the  next  thr,εc
cha ptεrs on th℃ four skills-a few principles and practical guidelines for assessing
thosε skills  in  thε classroom.  For  a  much  more  comprehensivε treatm℃nt of the
assεssmεm of the four sk피s, as well as  background rεsearch and thεory, l  rdiεr you
to  my  companion  tε:xtbook,  Language  Assessment:  Principles  and  Classroom
Practices (Brown, 2004).  For a  dεtailεd look at the assessment of listening, Buck’s
(2001) tεxtbook is  also vεry useful.

Undet걱standing the Terms “Assessment”  and “Test"

Before specifically considering the topic of assessing listεning in particular, a word
is in order about two commonly used terms.  It’s  tempting at times to simply think
that assessment and test are  synonymous, appearing in free  variation depending
on the whim of the spεaker or writer.  A glance at some teacher reference books of
10 or more yεars ago could bear out such an assumption.  However, in recent years,
thankfully, thε prof1εssion seems to hav'ε come to an appropriate consεnsus that the
two tεrms ar'ε, in fact, not synonymous.

Tests ar1ε a subset of assessment.  Assεssment is an ongoing pedagogical procεSS
that includes a number of evaluative acts on the part of the teacher.  Whεna student
responds to a  question, offers a  comment, or tries out a  new word or structure, thε
teacher  subconsciously  makes  an  evaluation  of  the  student’s  performance.  A
studεnt’s written  work,  from  notes  or  short  answers  to  essays,  is  judged  by  the
teacher.  In  rεading  and  listening  activities,  students'  responses  are  implicitly
evaluated.  All  that  is  assessment.  Technically  it  is  ret4εrnεd  to  싫 informal
assessment-because it is usually unplanned and spontaneous and without specific

318

CHAPTER  18  Teaching Listening

scoring  or  gra따ig  formats,  as  opposed  to  formal  assessment,  which  is  mo1℃
deliberate  and  usu따ly  has  conventionalized  feedback.  Tests  fall  into  the  latter
catego1γ.  They are planned sets of tasks or exercises, with designated time h없nes,
often announced in advance, prepared for (and sometimes feared)  by students, and
characteristically offering specific scoring or grading formats.

In considering classroom assessment, then, be prepared to entertain a range
of possible  pedagogical procedures.  In the comments that follow, for  the most
part  more  formal  aspects  of assessment  are  implied.  The  informal  processes
have  already  beεn subsumed  into  the  various  guidelines  and  examples  of this
chapter.

One of the first  observations that needs to be made in considering assessment
is  that listening  is  unobservable.  You  cannot directly  see or measure or otherwise
observe  either the process or the product of aural  comprehension.  Oh, yes, I C때
hear you saying that if you ask someone to close the window, and they close it, you
have  obseπed aural  comprehension.  Or that if someone  nods and says, “uh-huh,"
while you’re talking, you have  e까dence of comprehension.  Well, what you have in
these cases is indeed ev따ence of comprehension, but you have not aαu외lyobseπed
receptors  sending  messages  to  the  brain  or  the  brain’S  processing  of sound  따ld
converting it to me따피ng.  So, when it comes to 싫sessing listening, we’re pretty well
stuck with reliance on our best i깐-erence in determining comprehension.  How you
do that, and remain  as  accurate  in your assessment as  possible, is  the challenge of
assessing listening.

Assessing Iγpes of Listening and Micro- 없id Macroskills

In  this  chapter,  wε have  already  looked  at  types  of listening,  from  intensive
listening  on  up  to  extensive  and  interactive.  We  have  also  considered  the
micro- and  macroskills  of listening,  from  processing  tiny  bits  and  pieces  of
language  to  strategic,  interactive,  and  complex  skills  of  extendεd discourse.
These two related taxonomies are indispensable to valid, reliable assessmεnt of
students' listening  comprehension ability.  The  more  closely you  can  pinpoint
exactly  what  you  want  to  assess,  the  more  reliably  will  you  draw  your
conclusions.

What  assessment  methods  (tasks,  item  formats)  are  commonly  used  at  the
various levels? Consider the following list of S따nple tasks (not an exhaustive list, by
any means), and for further information consult Brown (2004).

1.  Intensive listening tasks

•  distinguishing phonemic p없rs (grass·장lass;leat씬'ive)
•  distinguishing morpholo횡cal pairs (miss-misse,찌
•  distinguishing stress patterns (/ can go; I can’tgo.)
•  paraphrase r℃cognition (I come from Taiwan; I’m  Taiwanese.)
•  repetition (S  repeats a word)

[ HAPTER 18  Teaching  Listening

319

2.  Responsive listening tasks

•  question (W'hat time is  it?-multiplε choice [MC]  responsε)
•  question (W'hat time is it?-opεn-ended response)
•  simple discourse sεquences (Hello.  Nice  weather.  Tough  test.)

3.  Sεlεctivε liste띠ng tasks

•  listening clozε (Ss fill  in blanks)
•  verbal information transfer (Ss  givε MC verbal rεsponse)
•  picture-cued information transfer (Ss  choose a  picture)
•  chart completion (Ss  fill  in a grid)
•  sentence repetition (Ss  repeat stimulus sentεnce)

4.  Extensive listening tasks

•  dictation (Ss listen  [usually 3 times]  and write a  paragraph)
•  dialogue (Ss  hear dialogue-MC comprehension questions)
•  dialogue (Ss  hear dialogue-open「ended response)
•  lectur'ε (Ss  take notes, summarizε, list main points, etc.)
•  interpretive tasks (Ss  hear a poem-interpret meaning)
•  stories, narratives (Ss  retell a story)

The fifth catεgory of listening, interactive tasks, is deliberately omitted from this
list since such interaction involves speaking and will be covered in the nεxtchapt℃[
、~ith this  brief outline, I hope you can gain  a  bit of a  picturε of some assessment
possibilities in listening comprehension.

TOPICS FOR DISCUSSION, RESEARCH, AND ACT’ION

[Note: (I) Individual work; (G) group or pair work; (C) whole-class discussion.]

1.  (G) Direct pairs to review the difference between input and intake (refeπing, if
necessaη, to PLLT, Chapter 10) and to illustrate with classroom examples how
input gets converted into intake.  What hints or idεas could one recommεnd for
hεlping students to maximize the conversion of input to intake?

2.  (C) Ask  the class for specific language examples of each of the eight

procεsses of listening refiεrr℃d to on pages 301-302.  How are factors  2
through 8  interactive by dεfinition?

3.  (G) Ask pairs to look at the chart in Figure  18.1  and make sure they

understand each type of oral language.  Pairs will then dεvise an illustration of
each and compare their illustrations with those of some other pairs.

4.  (C) Pick an English languag,ε news program and audiotape a two- or three
minute segment.  In class, have students listen to the tape and ident따 the
“dusters” of words that form thou행t groups.  까ien 따rect them to
brainstorm hints they could give to ESL students to help them to listen to
such dusters rather than to each separate word.

5.  (G) Instruct p따rs to specifically identi다 the redundant words/phrases in the
conversation between Matt and Jeff (pag'ε 305), and to brainstorm how they
would teach students (a) to use such redundancies for comprehension and
(b) to overlook them when comprehension is already sufficient.

6.  (C) Tape-record a  casual conversation between two native speakers of
English.  In class, play the tape and ask students to pick out as many
“performance variables” as  they can.  How do these perform따ice variables
differ from those of a learner of English? Can students be taught to

variables?

7.  (G) Direct sm때l groups to look again at the taxonomy of listening microskills
(fable 18.1) and to make sure they understand each item by offering an
example.  Groups will then look at the six types of classroom listening
performance (pages 309-310), shar'ε examples of each, and discuss their

8.  (G/C)Tell p없rs to considεr the listening strategiεs referred to on page 312

and to make sure they understand each strategy.  Then, have them sketch out
some techniques that they could use to teach such strategies to students.
They can then shar,ε their tecllniques with the rest of the class.

9.  (G)As a whole class, review the six principles for teaching effective listening
techniques on pages 310-312.  Then, assign to pairs one or two of the 41
techniques outlined in Table  18.2, and have them systematically evaluate the
techniques they have been given.  Their evaluation should be based on the
six principles.

10.  (I) One type of listening technique (combined with writing) not

considered in this chapter is dictation (only mentioned in the final section).
How useful is dictation? What are the pros and cons of using dictation in a
classroom?

11.  (G) In small groups, each assigned to one of the four categories listed in
the section on assessment, brainstorm some other possible tasks (test
methods) that could be employed in each category.  Groups will report
their findings on,  the chalkboard, and discuss any difficulties they had in

From  a  communicative, pragmatic  view  of the  language  classroom, listening  and
spea셈ng skills are closely intertwined.  Mor'ε often than not, ESL curricula that treat
oral  communication  skills  will  simply  be  labeled  as  “Liste띠ng/Spea힘ng” courses.
The  interaction  between  these  two  modes  of  performance  applies  especially
strongly  to  conversation, the  most  popular discourse  categoπ in  the  profession.
And, in the classroom, even relatively unidirectional types of spoken language input
(speeches, lectures, etc.) are  often followed  or preceded by various  forms  of oral
production on the part of students.

Some  of thε components  of teaching  spoken  language  were  covered  in  the
previous chapter as we looked closely at teaching listening comprehension: types of
spoken language, idiosyncrasies of spoken language that make listening difficult, 따1d
listening microskills  that are  a factor  of the  oral code.  This  chapter will build  on
those considerations as we investigate the teaching of oral communication skills.

ORAL COMMUNICATION SKILI.S IN PEDAGOGICAL RESEARCH

A review of some of the current issues in teaching oral communication will help to
pro찌de some perspective to the more practic때 considerations that follow  in this
chapter.

1.  Conversational discourse

When someone asks you “Do you speak En행sh?," they usu때ly mean: Can you
carπ on  a  coηversation reasonably  competently? The  benchmark  of successful
l없1guage acquisition is almost always the demonstration of an ability to accomplish
pragmatic goals through interactive discourse with other speakers of the language.
And yet, as Richards (1990, p. 6η noted,“the conversation class is something of an

322

CHAPTER  19

1농aching Speaking

323

enigma  in  language  teaching.”  The  goals  and  the  techniques  for  teaching
conversation are extremely diverse, depending on the student, teacher, and overall
context  of the  class.  Historically, “conversation”  classes  have  ranged  from  quasi
communicative drilling to free, open, and sometimes agendaless discussions among
students.

Recent  pedagogical  research  on  teaching  conversation  has  provided  some
parameters for  developing  objectives and techniques αlfcCarthy & 0 ’Keefe, 2004).
We  have  learned  to  differentiate  between  transactional  and  interactional
conversation (see Chapter 18).  We have discovered techniques for teaching students
conversation  rules  for  topic  nomination,  maintaining  a  conversation,  tum-taking,
inteπuption, and  termination.  Our  pedagogic외 storehouse  has  equipped  us  with
ways  to  teach  sociolinguistic  appropriateness,  styles  of  speech,  nonverbal
communication, and conversational routines (such as “Well, I’ve g아ta go now.”“Great
weather today, huh?'’“Haven’t I met you somewhere before?”).  Within all these foci,
the  phonological, lexical, and  syntactic  properties of language  can  be  attended  to
either directly or indirectly.

2.  Teaching pronunciation

There  has  been  some  controver윈r  over  the  role  of pronunciation work in  a
communicative,  interactive  course  of study  (Levis,  2005;  Setter  &  Jenkins,  2005;
Tarone,  2005).  Because  the  overwhelming  majority  of adult  learners  will  never
acquire an aceεnt-free command of a foreign  languagε, should a language  pr1앵ram
that  emphasizes  whole  language,  meaningful  contexts,  and  automaticity  of
production focus on these tiny phon이ogical details of language? The answer is yes,
but in a different way from what was perceived to be essential a couple of decades
ago.  This topic will be taken up later in thε chapter.

3.  Accuracy and fluency

An issue that pervades all of language performancε is  the distinction between
accuracy and fluency (Baileχ 2003).  In spoken language the question we face  as
teachers  is:  How  shall  we  prioritize  the  two  clearly  important  speaker  goals  of
accurate (clear, articulate, grammatically and phonologically correct) languagε and
fluent (flowing, natural) language?

In  the  mid  to  late  1970s, egged  on  by  a  somewhat  short-lived  antigrammar
approach, some teachers turned away from accuracy issues in favor of providing a
plethora of “natural” language  activity  in their  classrooms.  The  a명ument was, of
course,  that  adult  second  language  acquisition  should  simulate  the  child’S  first
language-learning processes.  Our classrooms must not become linguistics courses
but rather the locus of meaningful language involvement, or so the argument went.
Unfortunately, such classrooms so strongly emphasized the importance of fluency
with  a  concomitant  playing  down  of  the  bits  and  pieces  of  g없nmar and
phonology-that  many  students  managed  to  produce  fairly  fluent  but  barely
comprehensible language.  Something was lacking.

324

CHAPπR 19  Teaching Speaking

It’s  now  very  clear  that  fluency  and  accuracy  are  both  important  goals  to
pursue in CLT  and/or TBLT.  While fluency may in many communicative language
courses be an initial goal in language teaching, accuracy is achieved to some extent
by allowing students to focus on the elements of phonology, grammar, and discourse
in  their spoken  output.  If you  were  learning  to  play  tennis  instead  of a  second
language, this same philosophy would initially get you out on the tεnnis court to feel
what it’S  like to hold a racket, to hit the ball, to sεπe, etc., and then have you focus
more  cognitively  on  certain fundamentals.  Fluency  is  probably  best  achieved  by
allowing the “stream” of speech to “flow”; then, as  some of this speech spills over
beyond  comprehensib피ty,  the  “riverbanks”  of  instruction  on  some  details  of
phonology, grammar, or discourse  can channel  the  speech on  a  more  purposeful
course.

The  fluency /accuracy  issue  often  boils  down  to  the  extent  to  which  our
techniques should be message oriented (or, as some call it, teaching language use)
as  opposed  to  language  oriented  (also  known  as  teaching  language  usage).
Current approaches to language teaching lean strongly toward message orientation
with language usage offering a supporting role.

4.  Affective factors

One of the major obstacles learners have to overcome in learning to speak is
the 없lXiety generated over the risks of blurting things out that are wrong, stupid, or
incomprehensible.  Because of the language ego (see PLLT,  Chaptεrs 3 and 6) that
informs others that “you are what you speak,” learners are reluctant to be judged by
hearers.  Language  learners  can  put  a  new  twist  on  Mark Twain’s  quip  that “It’S
better to keep your mouth closed and have  others think you are  ignorant than to
open it and remove all doubt.” Our job as teachers is  to provide the kind of warm,
embracing  climate  that encourages students  to speak, however halting  or broken
their attempts may be.

5.  The interaction effect

The greatest difficulty that learners encounter in attempts to speak is  not the
m버tiplicity of sounds, words, phrases, and  discourse  forms  that  characterize  any
language, but rather the interactive nature of most communication.  Conversations
are collaborative as particip없its engage in a process of negotiation of meaning.  So,
for the learner, the matter of what to say-a tremendous task, to be sure-is often
eclipsed by conventions of how to say things, when to speak, and other discourse
constraints.  For example, 따nong the many possible grammatical sentences that a
learner could  produce in  response  to a  comment, how does  that  learner make a
choice?

댐외ne1l따one (2005) I맹orted on a number of studies that claim a crucial role for
communication strate횡es in l얹n따igto p와디cipate in conversational discourse.  A few
such strategies are 띠scussed later in this chapter.  Da찌d Nw때n (1991b,p. 4η notεd
yet  another  complication  in  interactive  discourse:  what  he  calls  the  interlocutor
effect, or the 이fftet피.tyofa spea퍼ng task as gauged by the s힘lls of one’S  interloαit or.

In  other words, one  learner’s  performance  is  always  colored  by that  of thε person
(interlocutor) he or she is talking with ‘

mor1ε com pl℃X questions, εspεcially sineε statistically, most intεractions among 암1glish

7.  The growth of spoken corpora

깐1ε intε피gib피ty issuε is  now being  informεd by what  McCaπhy and  O’Keεfe

Thεsε data ar,ε spurring the language-teaching pronεssion-espεcially textbook

8.  Genres of spoken language

Finally, rεsearch on spokεn languagε has recently attεndεd to aspεcification of

research  more  accuratεly  dεscribes  thε constraints  of  such  genres  on  spoken

techniques  in your interactive  classroom, make sure  yo따 students  can deal  with

WHAT MAKES SPEAKING DIFFICULT?

Again,  Chapter  18  out피ied  some  idiosyncrasies  of  spoken  language  that  make
listening skills  somewhat difficult  to acquire.  These  S없ne characteristics must be
taken into account in the productive generation of speech, but with a slight twist in
that the learner is now the producer.  Bear in mind that the following characteristics
of spoken  language  can  make  oral  perform따ice easy  as  well  as,  in  some  cases,
difficult.

1.  Clustering

Fluent speech is phrasal, not word by word.  Learners can organize their output

both cognitively and physi때ly (in breath groups) through such clustering.

2.  Redundancy

πie speaker has an opportunity to make mear파ig clearer through the redundancy

oflar핑uage.  Learners can capitalize on this featur℃ of spoken l없iguage.

3.  Reduced forms

Contractions, elisions, reduced vowels, etc., 때l formspeci떼 problems in teaching
spoken  English  (see  the  section  “Teaching  Pronunciation”  later  in  this  chapter).
Students who don’t  learn colloquial contractions can sometimes develop  a  stilted,
bookish quality of spe따ting that in tum stigmatizes them.

4.  Performance variables

One of the advantages of spoken l없iguage is that the process of thinking as you
speak allows you to manifest a certain number of performance hesitations, pauses,
backtracking, and  corrections.  Learners  can actually  be  taui각it  how to  pause  and
hesitate.  For example, in English our “d따뼈ng time” is  not silent; we insert cert따n
“fillers” such as  uκ um, well, jηu knou, I  mean, like,  etc.  One of the most salient
differences between native and nonnative speakers of a l따iguage is in their hesitation
phenomena.

5.  Colloquial language

Make sure yo따 students are reasonably well acqu따nted with the words, idioms,
and  phrases  of colloquial  I없iguage and  that  they get practice  in  producing  these
forms.

6.  Rate of delivery

Another salient characteristic of fluency is  rate of delivery.  One of your tasks
in teaching  spokεn En밍ish is  to  help  learnεrs achieve  an acceptable speed along

7.  Stress, rhythm, and intonation

깐lis is  the most important characteristic  of En빼sh pronunciation, as  will be
explained  below.  The  str,εss-timed rhythm  of  spoken  En횡ish and  its  intonation

8.  Interaction

As  noted  in  the  prεvious section, learning  to  produce waves  of language  in a
vacuum-without interlocutors-would rob speaking skill of its rich℃st component:
the creativity of conversational negotiation.

In  the  previous  chapter,  17  nli다· and  macroskills  for  listening  comprehension

One implication of such a list is the importance of focusing on both the forms
of language  and the functions  of language.  In  teaching oral  communication, we
don't limit students' attention to the whole picturε, even though that whole picture
is  important.  We  also  help  students  to  see  the  pieces-right down  to  the  small
parts-of languagε that  make  up the  whole.  Just  as  you  would  instruct a  novice
artist in composition, the effect of color hues, shading, and brush strokε techniques,
so language students need to be shown the details of how to convey and negotiate
the ever-elusive meanings of language.

1YPESOF α..ASSROOM SPEAKING PERFORMANCE

In  Chapter  18, six  types  of listening  performancε were  listed.  With  the  obvious
connection bεtween listening 없id speaking, six similar categories apply to the kinds

1.  Imitative

A very linlited portion of classroom speaking time  may legitimately be spent

328

CHAPTER  79  Teaching Speaking

Table  19.1. Micro  and  macroskills of oral  communication

Microskills

1.  Produce chunks of language of different  lengths.
2.  Orally produce differences  among the  English  phonemes and allophonic variants.
3.  Produce  English  stress  pa야erns, words  in  stressed and  unstressed  positions,

rhythmic  structure,  and  intonational  contours.
4.  Produce reduced forms  of words  and phrases.
5.  Use an  adequate number of lexical  units  (words)  in  order to  accomplish

pragmatic  purposes.

6.  Produce fluent  speech at different  rates  of deliverγ
7.  Monitor your own oral  production  and  use various  strategic devices-pauses,
fille「s, self-corrections,  backtracking-to enhance the clarity of the message.

8.  Use grammatical  word  classes (nouns,  verbs,  etc.),  systems (e.g.,  tense,

agreement,  pluralization),  word order,  patterns,  rules,  and elliptical forms.
9.  Produce speech  in  natural  constituents-in appropriate phrases,  pause groups,

breath  groups,  and sentences.

10.  Express  a  particular meaning  in  different grammatical  forms.

Macroskills

11.  Use cohesive devices  in  spoken  discourse.
12.  Accomplish  appropriately communicative functions  according to situations,

participants,  and goals.

13.  Use appropriate registers,  implicature,  pragmatic conventions,  and other

sociolinguistic features  in  face-to-face conversations.

14.  Convey  links  and connections between  events and communicate such  relations as
main  idea,  supporting idea,  new  information,  given  information,  generalization,
and exemplification.

15.  Use facial  features,  kinesics,  body  language,  and other nonverbal  cues along with

verbal  language to convey  meanings.

16.  Develop and  use a  battery of speaking strategies,  such  as  emphasizing key words,

rephrasing,  providing a context for  interpreting the  meaning of words,  appealing
for  help,  and accurately assessing how well  your  interlocutor is  understanding you.

New teachers in thε field always want the answer to this quεstion:  Is drilling a
legitimate part of the communicative languag℃ classroom? The answεr is a  qualified
yes. Drills  offi℃r students an opportunity to listen and to orally repeat certain strings
of  language  that  may  pose  some  linguistic  difficulty-either  phonolo횡cal  or
grammatical.  Drills  are  to  language  teaching  what  the  pitching  machine  is  to
baseball.  They offiεr limited practice through repetition.  πiey 따low one to focus on
one εlemεnt of languagε in a controlled activity.  πiey C따1 help to establish certain

psychomotor patterns (to “loosen thε tongue”) and to associatε sεlεcted grammatical

1.  Focus on both fluency and accuracy, depending on your objective.

In  our current zeal for interactive  languag1ε tεaching, we can εasily slip  into a
pattern of providing zesty content-based, interactive activities that don’t  capitalize
on  grammatical  pointers  or  pronunciation  tips.  We  nεed to  bear  in  mind  a
spectrum of learner nεeds, from language-based focus on accuracy to messag1ε-based
focus on interaction, mεaning, and fluency.  When you do a jigsaw group technique,
play  a  gamε, or discuss solutions to the environmental crisis, make  surε that your
tasks have a linguistic (language-based) objective, and seize the opportunity to help
students  to  perceive  and  use  the  building  blocks  of language.  At  the  S따nε timε,
don’t  borε your students to death with lifeless, rεpetitious drills.  As  noted abov,ε,
make any drilling you do as meaningful as possible.

2.  Provide intrinsically motivating techniques.

Tπ at all  tim℃S to appeal to students’ ultimate goals and intεrests, to thεir need
for knowledge, for status, for achieving competεnce and autonomy, and for “b며1g all
that  they can be.” Ev1εn in  those  techniques that don’t  send students into  ℃cstasy,
hεIp them  to  seε how the  activity w피 benefit  them.  Often students don’t  know
why we ask them to do certain things; it usually pays to tell them.

3.  Encourage the use of authentic language in meat파i뺑U conte웠s.

This  themε has  been  played  time  and  again  in  this  book,  but  one  more
reminder  shouldn’t  buπ!  It  is  not  easy  to  keep  coming  up  with  meaningful
interaction.  We  all  succumb  to  the  temptation  to  do,  say,  disconnected  little
grammar exercises whεre we go around the room calling on students one by one to
pick the  right answer.  It  takes  ener잃r and creativity to devise  authentic  contexts
and meaningful interaction, but with the hεIp of a  storehouse of tεacher resource
material (see the recommended books and articles at the end of this chapter) it can
be done.  Even drills (see pages  342-344) can be structured to provide a  sense of
authenticity.

4.  Pro여de appropriate feedback and correction.

In most EFL situations, students are totally dependent on the teacher for useful
linguistic  feedback.  In  ESL  situations,  they  may  get  such  feedback  “out  there"
beyond the classroom, but even then you 따e in a position to be of great benεfit.  It
is  important  that you  take  advantage  of your knowledge  of English  to  inject  the
kinds of corrective feedback that are appropriate for 삼1e moment.

5.  Capitalize on the natural link between speaking and listening.

Many  interactive techniques that involve speaking w피 also of course include
listening.  Don’t  lose out on opportunities to integrate these two skills.  As you 앙e
perhaps focusing on spea셈ng goals, listening goals may na따ally coincide, and the
two S때S can reinforce each other.  Skills in producing language 없℃ often initiated
through comprehension.

6.  Give students opportunities to initiate oral communication.

A  good  dεal  of  typical  classroom  interaction  is  characterized  by  t℃acher

TEACHING CONVERSATION

Res℃arch on teaching conversational skills  (McCarthy &  0’Kεεfe, 2004;Tarone, 2005)
historically dεscribes two major approaches for teaching conversation.  The first is an
indirect approach in which learnε:rs are more or less sεt loose to εngag1ε in interaction.
The  sεcond is  a  direct  approach  that  “involves  planning  a  conversa디on pl'앵ram
around  the specific  microskills, strategies, and  processes  that  are  involv;εd in  fluent
conversation”(Richards, 1990, pp. 76-77).  깐1ε indirεct approach implies that one does
not  actually  teach  conversation,  but  rather  that  students  acq따℃ conversational
compεtεnce, pεripherally,  by  εnga횡ng  in  meaningful  tasks.  A  dirεct  approach
explicitly calls students' attention to conversational rules, conventions, and strate횡es.
While both approachεs can be found in  languag℃-teaching institutions around
the world, recent developments in such models as task-based  languagε instruction
have  taken  the  learner  well  beyond  simply  using  language.  Nunan  (2004),  Ellis

[ HAPTER 19  Teaching Speaking

333

(2003), and Willis ( 1996), in their descriptions of task-based instruction, for examplε,
includεd focus  on form, including analysis and practicε, as an integral part of eveπ
task.  Likεwisε, Skehan  (1998a,  p.  131)  rεcommendεd that  communicative  tasks
“ maximize  thε chances of a  focus  on form  through  attεntional m뼈pulation.” It  is
clear,  upon  scanning  currεnt  English  language  textbooks,  that  thε  preva피ng
approach to  tεaching conversation includes  thε learnεr’s inductivε involvεment in
meaningful tasks as wε11 as consciousnεss-raising εlεmεnts of focus on form.

Richards (1990, pp. 79-80) offtεrεd thε following list of featur,εs ofconvεrsation

that can rεcεivε spεcific focus in classroom instruction:

•  how to usε conversation for  both transactional and interactional p따posεs
•  how to produce both short and long turns in com℃rsation

holding a turn, and rεlinquishing a  turn

dev'εlop and maintain talk on these topics

•  how to usε both a casual stylε of speaking and a neutral or morε formal style
•  how to use conversation in diftiεrεnt social settings and for diftiεrent kinds
of social εncounters, such as on the telephonε and in informal and formal
social gatherings

•  strategies for  rεpairing troublε spots in conversation, including

communication br1εakdown and comprehεnsion problems

•  how to maintain fluεncy in conversation through avoiding εxcessive

pausing, br,εakdowns, and errors of grammar or pronunciation

•  how to produce talk in a conversational mode, using a conversational

rεgistεr and syntax

•  how to  us℃ conv,εrsational fillers and small talk
•  how to use con'℃rsational routinεs

It  isn’t  possible  in  thε  contεxt  of  one  introductory  chaptεr on  tεaching
speaking  to  call  attεntion to  all  the  possiblε tεchniques and  tasks  available  for
teaching these fεatures of conversation.  I recommεnd that you turn to such books
as Bailey (2005), Golεbiowska (1990),and Klippel (1984) for an exploratory journey
into some of the possibilities.  What follows here (including Figures 19.1, 19.2, and
19.3) are some samplε tasks that illustrate teaching various aspects of conversation,
as  well  as  an  oral grammar practice  tεchnique (Figure  19.4).  (Note: For  more on
oral grammar instruction, see Chaptεr 22‘)

Level
Time
Aim

Intermediate or above
30-35  minutes
For  students to consider ways  in  which  they can  learn  English
outside the classroom

Preparation

Make  photocopies of the task sheet for  your class.

Procedure

Task Sheet

1.  Arouse student interest in  the planning task.
2.  Set  up the  initial  pair work and give the students five  to ten
minutes to  discuss,  add to,  or modify the list of s앵gestions.
3.  When the initial  discussion  is  over,  you  should facilitate the

suggestions.  Make OHTs or posters available to help the groups
present their ideas.

Here  is  a  list of techniques that people use to  help them  learn
English  outside the classroom:

-memorizing a ”st of words

-reading a g떠mmar book
--doing grammar exercises
-reading a  book or magazine  in  English
-recopying things from  their class notebook
--correcting mistakes  made in  written  work
-preparing the next unit of the coursebook
Work with  a partner and add any others of your own. Tell  each
other which ones in  the  list you  find  helpful,  if any,  then  tell  the
class about the  new ones you  have added.

Arrange yourselves  in  groups and take a time  period from  this  list:
-thiπy minutes  per day for  six  days a week

-one hour per day for “ve days a week

-two hours per day for four days a week
In  your group,  plan a  program  to show how you  could  make use of
the time to do extra work on your English.  Use the ideas from  the
earlier list,  as well  as any others you  can think of.  Choose one
person to  present your plan to the rest of the class.

Remarks

If students agree to  experiment with a study  plan,  some time should
be allowed  in  class for  them to discuss  how they are getting on.

JENE  REGRETTE  RIEN

level
Time
Aim
Preparation
Procedure

Intermediate and above
15-20 minutes
To  give  students  practice  in  hypothetical  would
None
1.  Put the following  list  on  the  board  or on  an  OHT:

-Yc。ur school
-Your job or occupation
-Your friends
-Your habits,  e.g.,  smoking,  exercising,  eating,  etc.

Your  skills,  e.g.,  languages,  carpentry,  etc.

2.  Ask  the students to  write  a personal  entry for  each  heading,  i.e.,
the  name  of their school,  job,  etc. They  should then  decide
which  of these they would  or would  not change  if  they  were to
live  their  lives  again ‘

3.  Once they  have done this,  encourage them  to  sha 「e their

thoughts  in  small  groups of three or four.

4.  Ask  the students to  take  turns telling others  in  the  group what
they  would change if  they  had  their  life  again. The  others  can
ask  questions or  comment.

5.  Wind  up  the activity  by  seeing  if there are any  areas that  most of

the  [ lass would  want to  change.

The  title of the activity  comes from  an  Edith  Piaf song.  An  English
ve 「sion is  called  “ No  Regrets. ”  It  would  make a  lively  and
stimulating start to this  activity.

Remarks

E.  Individual practice: Oral dialogue journals

For  extra-class  practice, aside  from  rεcommεnding that  your  students  seek  out
opportunities  for  authentic  use  of English,  several  teacher  trainers  (Celeε-Murcia &
Goodwin,  1991; McDonald,  1989)  recommend  using  oral  dialoguε journals.  Written
dialogue journals (where the student records thoughts, ideas, and/or reactions, and the
teacher reads and rεsponds with written comments) hav℃ been in use for some time.
Why not usε thε conv'εnience of a  voice (digital or tape) rεcordεr for audio journals?
With  large  classes,  such  a  technique  is  too  time-consuming  for  the  teacher,  but  for
individual  students, tutees, or vεry small  classεs, it  offers  students  a  way  to  exprεSS

CHAPTER  19  Teaching Speaking

339

themselves  (without  risking ridicule  from  peers) orally, to  convey real  concerns and
thoughts, to practice speaking, and to get feedback from the teacher on both form and
content.

F.  Other interactive techniques

Of  course,  many  other  tasks  따id techniques  can  be  applied  to  the  teaching  of
conversation.  They  ar'ε  almost  impossible  to  categorize,  but  here  are  a  few  possible
types, gleaned simply from the table of contents of Friederike Klippel's (1984) practical
little reso따ce book:

•  interviews
•  guεssing g없nes
•  jigsaw tasks
•  ranking exercises
•  discussions

•  values clarification
•  problem-sol찌ng activities
•  role plays
•  simulations

TEACHING PRONUNCIATION

Views  on  teaching  pronunciation  changed  dramatically  over  the  last  h외f of  the
twentieth cεntutγ (see Tarone, 2005, for a 윈rnopsis).  In the heyday of audiolingualism
and its  various  bεhavioristic method이0밍cal variants, the pronunciation component
of  a  course  or  prograni  was  a  mainstay.  In  the  1970s,  as  the  language-teaching
profession  began  to  experience  a  revolution  of  sorts  (see  Chapter  2),  explicit
pedago!혀cal focus  on  anyt비ng that smacked of 피iguistic nuts and bolts was under
siege  by  proponents  of the  various  nondirective  “let-it-just-happen”  approaches  to
language  teaching.  Pronunciation  instruction  became  somewhat  incidental  to  a
course of study.‘  By the mid-1980s, with greater attention to g없nmatical structures as
important elements in discourse, to a balance between fluency and accuracy, and to
the  explicit  specification  of pedago횡cal tasks  that  a  learner  should  accomplish, it
became  clear  that  pronunciation  was  a  key  to  gaining  full  communicative
competence.

Current approaches to pronunciation contrast starkly with the early approaches.
Rather  than  attempting  only to  build  a  learner's  articulatory  competence from  the
bottom up, and simply as  the mastery of a  list of phonemes and 와lophones, a  top
down approach is now taken in which the most relevant featw℃S of pronunciation
stress, rhythm, and intonation-are given 피양1 priority.  Instead of teaching only the
role  of articulation  within  words, or at  best, phrases, we teach  its  role  in  a  whole
stream of discourse.  Over two decades ago, Rita ￦ong (1987, p. 21) reminded us that
“contemporaπ views  [of language]  hold that the sounds of language are less crucial
for  understanding than the  way they are  야뿔피zed.  πie rhythm and  intonation  of
En밍ish are  two  major org따lizing sσuctures that native  speakers rely on to process
speech .... Because  of their major  roles  in communication, rhythm  and  intonation
merit greatεr priority in the teaching progr없n than attention to individual sounds.”

340

CHAPTER  19  Teaching Speaking

Wong’s  comments  reflect  an  approach  that  puts  때l  aspects  of  English
pronunciation into the perspective of a communicative, interactive, whole l따iguage
view  of human  speech.  Once  again,  history  taught  us  the  lesson  of maintaining
balance.

Many learners of f야eign languages feel that their ultimate goal in pronunciation
should be accent-free speech that is  indistinguishable from that of a  native speaker.
Such a  goal  is  not only  unattainable (see PLLT,  Chapter 3) for  virtu외ly eveη adult
learner, but in a multilingual, multicultural world, accents are quite acceptable.  With
the rapid spread of English as an international language, native accents have become
almost  irrelevant  to  cross-cultural  communication.  Moreover,  as  the  world
community  comes  to  appreciate  and  value  people’s  heritage,  onε’s accent  is  just
another symbol of that heritage.

Our  goal  as  teachers  of  English  pronunciation  should  thεrefore  be  more
realistically  focused  on  clear,  compr1εhεnsible  pronunciation.  At  the  beginning
levels,  we  W없it  learnεrs to  surpass  that  threshold  beneath which  pronunciation
detracts from  thεir ability to communicate.  At  the advanced  lev1εls, pronunciation
goals can focus on elements that enhance communication: intonation features that
go beyond basic patterns, voice quality, phonetic distinctions between rεgisters, and
other  refinements  that  are  far  more  important  in  the  overall  stream  of  clear
communication than rolling the English /r/ or getting a vowel to perfectly imitate a
“native speaker.”

What  are  the factors  within  learnεrs that  affect  pronunciation, and  how can
you deal with each of them? Below is a list (adapted from Kenworthy, 1987,pp.4-8)
of variables that you should consider.

1.  Native language.  Clearly, the native language is the most influential factor
affecting a learner's pronunciation (see PLLT, Chapter 9).  If you are familiar
with thε sound system of a learner's native language, you will bε better able
to diagnose studεnt difficulties.  Many L1 - L2  carryovers can be overcome
through a focused awareness 없id effort on the learner's part.

2.  Age.  Generally spea해ng, children under the age of pubeπy stand an

excellent chance of“sounding like a  native" if they have continued exposure
in authentic contexts.  Beyond the age of puberty, while adults will almost
surely maintain a “foreign accent;’ there is no particular advantage attributed
to age (see PLLT, Chapter 3).  A 5ιyear-old can be as successful as an  1용year­
old if all other factors are equal.  Remind your students, especially if your
students are older, that “the younger, the better” is a myth.

3.  Exposure.  It is difficult to define exposure.  One can actually live in a

foreign country for some time but not take advantage of being “ with the
people.” Research seems to support the notion that the quality and intensity
of exposure are more important th따1 mere length of time.  If class time spent
focusing on pronunciation demands the full attention and interest of your
students, then they stand a good chance of reaching their goals.

--

CHAPTER  19

1능aching Speaking

341

4.  Innate phonetic ability.  Often referred to as having an “ear” for language,
some pεople manifiεst a phonetic coding ability that others do not.  In many
cases, if a person has had exposure to a foreign language as a child, this
“knack” is present whether the early language is remembered or not.  Others
are simply more attuned to phonetic discriminations.  Some people would
have you bεlieve that you either have such a knack, or you don't.  Strategies[
based instruction (see Chapter 16), however, has proven that some elements
of learning are a matter of an awareness of your own limitations combined
with a conscious focus on doing something to compensate for those
limitations.  Th℃refore, if pronunciation sεems to be naturally difficult for
some students, they should not despair; with some effort and concentration,
they can improve their competencε,

5.  Identity and language ego.  Yet another influencε is one’s attitude toward
speakers of the target language and the extent to which the language ego
identifies with those speakεrs.  Learners nεed to be reminded of the
importancε of positive attitudes toward the people who speak the language
(if such a target is  idεntifiable), but more important, students need to become
aware of-and not afraid of-the second identiη that may bε ℃merging
within them.

6.  Motivation and concern for good pronunciation.  Some learners are not
particularly concerned about their pronunciation, while others are.  The
extent to which learners' intrinsic motivation propels them toward improve
ment will be perhaps the strongest influencε of all six of the factors in this
list.  If that motivation and concεrn are high, then the necessaπ effort will be
expended in pursuit of goals.  You can help learners to perceive or develop
that motivation by showing, among othεr things, how cla다ty of speech is sig
nificant in shaping their self image and, ultimately, in reaching some of their
higher goals.

All  six of the  above  factors  sugg℃st that any learner who really wants to  can
learn  to  pronounce  English  clearly  and  comprehensibly.  You  can  assist  in  the
process  by  gearing  your  planned  and  unplanned  instruction  toward  these  six
factors.

On the  next few pages (including Figures  19.6 and  19.η, you will find  three
techniques  for  teaching  different  aspects  of En웰sh pronunciation.  Take  note  of
how  those  techniques  may  capitalize  on  the  positive  benefits  of the  six  factors
above, and the extent to which they reflect a discourse-based view of pronunciation
teaching.  A significant factor for you in the success of such techniques liεs in your
ability to instill  in your students the  motivation  to put forth  the effort needed to
dεvelop clear, comprehensible pronunciation.

A.  Intonation-Listening for pitch changes

Figure  19.6.  (adapted from  Wong,  1987,  p.  61)

Record the f이 lowing conversation and play it for the students ‘  Establish the par
ticipants,  the  setting,  and  the  event  by  asking  the  students  to  guess  who  and  what
they are.

She:  No.  、
He:  Why?  、

He:  Ready?  *"'
She:  Problems. ‘·
Problems?  *"'
She:  Yes. ‘·

He:  What?  、
She:  Babysitter.  、

He:

After  the  students  have  figured  out what  is  going on,  you  can  play  the  conver
sation  again. This  time  put the transcription  of the conversation  on  the board or on
an  overhead  projector  and  ask  the  students  to  try  to  determine  for  each  utterance
whether the speaker’5 voice ends with  a  rising  or falling  pitch.  Draw arrows  next to
each  utterance  and  play  the  conversation  once  more.  To  isolate  pitch  from  the
words,  you  can  use  a  kazoo,  which  can  be  purchased  at  a  toy  store  (see  Gilbert,
1978).  By  humming  into  it,  you  can  demonstrate  rising  and  falling  pitch  to  the
amusement and  illumination  of your students.

Ask  the  students  to  explain  what each  utterance  means. Then  point  out that  a
change in  pitch can indicate a change in  meaning (e.g., “ Ready?” with a  rising pitch
means "Are  you  ready?”  but “ Ready”  with  a  falling  pitch  means “| am  ready”).

Additional  practice dialogues are provided  here.  Make up  more for  your partic

ular  students.  Follow  the procedure described for  the first  conversation.

C.  Me없따1행il minimal p삶rs

Traditional  minimal-pair  drills,  used  for  decades  in  language  teaching,  go

something like this:

T:  Okay, class, on the board, picture number 1 is a “pen,” and picture

number 2 is a “pin.” Listen: Pen  ψoiηts to number 1], pin  ψoiηts to
number 2)  [several repetitions].  Now, I’m going to say either
number 1 or number 2.  You  tell me which.  Ready?  [pause]  Pin.

Ss:  Number 2.
T:  Good.  Ready.  Pin.
Ss:  Number 2.
T:  Okay.  [pause]  Pen.
Ss:  Number 1.

CLT  and  TBLT  principles  prod  us  to  be  a  little  more  meaningful.  In  the

following examples you can see that a little contextualization goes a long way:

1.  T:  This pen leaks.

S:  Then don’t write with it.
T:  This pan leaks.
S:  Then don’t  cook with it.

2.  T:  Where can I buy cold cream?

S:  At the daiη.
T:  Where can I buy cold cream?
S:  At  the drugstore.

3.  T:  The sun is  hot on my head!

S:  Then get a cap.
T:  Oh, no, I missed the bus.  I’m going to be late!
S:  Then get a cab.

These  are  good examples of drilling  techniques that have  been modified to bring
context, interest, and a modicum of authenticity to what would otherwise be a very
mechanical task.

FOCUS ON FORM AND ERROR TREATMENT

One of the most frequently posed questions by teachers who are new to the trade is
the following: When and how should I  coπect the speech errors of learners in my
classroom? This  happens  also  to  be  one  of the  most  enigmatic  questions  in  야ie
language-teaching profession.  I offer some guide파ies here, but at the same time urge
you to read the last part of Chapter 9 of PLLT, where issues surrounding form-focused
instruction are described in more detail.

The Role of Feedback

CHAPTER  19

Teaching Speaking

345

One of the keys, but not the only key, to successful second l때guage learning lies in
the feedback that a learner receives from  othεrs.  Chapter 9  of PLLT dεscribed Vigil
and  Oiler's  (1976)  model  of  how  affective  and  cognitiv1ε  feedback  affects  the
message-sending procεss.  Figure 19.8 depicts, metaphorically at least, what happens
in Vigil and Oiler's model in the case of learners’ orally produced utterances.

The “ green light” of the affective fεedback mode allows the sender to continue
attempting  to  get  a  message  across;  a  “ red  light"  causes  the  sender  to  abort  the
attεmpt.  (The  metaphorical  nature  of  such  a  chart  is  evident  in  the  fact  that
affective feedback does not precede cognitive feedback, as this chart may lead you
to believe; both modes can take place simultaneously.) The traffic signal of cognitive
feedback  is  the  point  at  which  error  tfεatment  enters.  A  green  light  here
symbolizes  noncorrective  feedback  that  says, “ I  understand  your  mεssage.'’ A  red
light  symbolizes  corrective  feedback  that  takes  on  a  myriad  of  possible  forms
(outlined  below)  and  causes  the  learner  to  make  some  kind  of  alteration  in
production.  To  push  the  metaphor further,  a  yellow  light  could  represent  those
various shades of color that are interpreted by the learner as falling  somεwhere in
between a complete green light and a red light, causing the learnεr to adjust, to 따ter,
to recycle back, to tπ again in some way.  Note that fossilization  may be the result
of too many green lights when there should have been some yellow or red li양its.

The most useful implication ofVigil 따id Oller’s model for determining how you
will administer error treatment is that cognitive feedback must be optimal in order
to be effective.  Too much negative cognitive feedback-a barrage of interruptions,
corrections, and overt attention to malformations-often leads learners to shut off
their attempts at communication.  They perceive that so much is wrong with their
production that there is little hope of getting anyt피ng right.  On the other hand, too
much positive cognitive feedback-willingness of the teacher-hearer to let errors go

346

CH재TER  19  Teaching Speaking

uncorrected,  to  indicatε  understanding  when  understanding  may  not  hav'ε
occurred-serves to  reinforce  thε errors  of the speaker-learner.  Thε result is  the
persistencε, and pεrhaps the eventual fossilization (or stabilization), of such errors.
The  task  of thε teacher  is  to  discern  the  optimal  tension  between  positive  and
nεgative cognitive feedback: providing enough green lights to encourage continued
communication, but  not  so  many  that  crucial  errors  go  unnoticed; and  providing
enough red lights to call attention to those crucial errors, but not so many that the
learner is  discouraged from  attεmpting to speak at all.

We do well to recall at this point the application of Skinnεr’S opεrant conditioning
model  of  learning  (see  PL다 Chapter  4).  The  affective  and  cognitive  modes  of
feedback  are  reinforcers  to  speakers’  responses.  As  speakers  percεiv'ε  “positive”
reinforcement (the green lights  of Figurε 19.8), they will be led to intεrnalize cεrtain
speech patterns.  Corrective fεεdback can st피 bε “positiv1ε” in the Skinnεrian sεnsε, as
we  shall  see  below.  Becausε ignoring  erroneous  behavior  also  has  the  eff농ct of a
positive  reinforcεr, tεachers must  be vεη car,εful to  discern  the possible  reinforcing
consequencεs  of  nεutral  feedback.  What  we  must  avoid  at  all  costs  is  the
administration of puη떠νe reinforcement-correction that is viewed by learners as an
affective red light-dεvaluing, dehumanizing, or insulting them.

When and How to Treat Errors

Against  this  thεoretical backdrop  we  can  εvaluat℃ some  possibilities  of whεn and
how to treat errors in the languagε classroom.  Michael Long (1977,p. 288) suggested
that the question of νheη to treat an error (that is, which errors to provide some sort
of feedback on) has no simple answer:

Having noticεd an error, thε first (and, I would argue, crucial) decision
the tεacher makes is whether or not to treat it at all.  In order to make
the  decision  the  teachεr  may  have  recourse  to  factors  with
immεdiate, temporaπ bεaring, such as the importance of the error to
thε current pedagogical focus of the lesson, the tεacher's perception
of the  chance  of ℃liciting correct  performancε from  the  student  if
negative  feεdback  is  given,  and  so  on.  Consideration  of  thesε
εphemeral factors  may  bε prεempted, however,  by  thε  teacher's
beliefs (conscious or unconscious) as to what a language is and how
a  new  onε is  learned.  These  beliefs  may  have  been  formεd yεars
before the lesson in question.

Ina veπ practical article on εrror treatment, James Hendrickson (1980) advised
teachers  to  tπ to  discern  the  difference  between  global  and  local  errors  (to  be
dεscribed latεr in this  chapter).  Once a  learner of En맹sh was describing a  qu따nt
old hotel in Europe and said, “There is a French widow in e¥εη7 bedroom.” The local

CHAPTER  19

1농aching Speaking

347

error  is  clearly-and  humorously-recogr피zed.  Hendrickson  recommended  that
local errors usually need not be corrected since the message is clear 따id correction
might  inteπupt a learner in the flow of productive communication.  Global  eπors
need to be treated in some way  since  the message  may  otherwise remain garbled.
“The  different  city  is  another  one  in  thε another  two” is  a  sentence  that  would
certainly need treatment because it is  incomprehensible as  is.  Many utterances are
not  clearly global  or local, and it  is  difficult  to  discern the  necessity for  coπective
feedback.  A learner once wrote, “ The grammar is  the basement of eveη language.”
While  this  witη little  proclamation  may  indeed  sound  more  like  Chomsky  than
Chomsky  does,  it  behooves  the  teacher  to  ascertain  just  what  the  learner  meant
her•ε (no  doubt “ basis”  rather  than “ basement”), and  to  provide  some  feedback  to
clarify the difference between the two.  The bottom line is that we simply must not
stifle  our  students'  attempts  at  production  by  smothering  them  with  corrective
feedback.

The  matter of how to  treat  errors  is  complex.  Research  on error correction
methods  is  not  at  all  conclusive  on  the  most  effective  method  or  technique.  It
seems quite clear that students in the classroom generally want and expect errors
to be corrected.  However, some methods recommend no direct treatment of error
at  all  (Krashen  &  Terrell,  1983).  After  따1,  in  natural,  untutor,εd  environments,
nonnative  speakers  generally  get  corrected  by  native  speakers  on  only  a  small
percentage of errors that they make.  Balancing these perspectives, I think wε can
safely  conclude that a  sensitive  and perceptive language  teacher should make the
language  classroom a  happy optimum between some of the  overp이iteness of the
real world and the expectations that learners bring with them to the classroom.

Error treatment options can be classified in a number of possible ways, but one
useful taxonomy was recommended by Kathleen Bailey (1985,p. l l 1).  Seven “ basic
options” are complemented by eight “possible features" within each option.

Basic Options

Possible Features

1.  To  treat or to ignore
2.  To  tr•εat inlmediately or to delay
3.  To  transfer treatment (to, say,

other lεarners) or not

4.  To  transfer to another individual,
a subgroup, or the whole class
5.  To return, or not, to the original
error maker after treatment
6.  To  permit other learners to

irut1ate  treatment

7.  To  test for the efficacy of the

treatment

1.  Fact of error indicated
2.  Location indicated
3.  Opportun따 for new

attempt given
4.  Model pro찌ded
5.  Error type indicated
6.  Remedy indicated
7.  Improvement indicated
8.  Praise indicated
