CHAPTER  19  Teaching Speaking

All  of the  basic  options and features  within each option are  viable  modes  of
error treatment in the classroom.  It is importaηt to  understaηd that not all error
treatment is  eη·or correctioη.  Among  Bailey’s  eight features, it  is  possible  to see
ηone of them  as  a  correction, in  that  none  (with  the  possible  exception  of #6)
specify the wrong form and supply the correct form.  Error treatment encompasses
a wide range of options, one of which-at the extreme end of a continuum-may
be considered to be a correction.  The research (Williams, 2005) shows that the best
way to help a learner to repair malformεd utterances is, first, to assist the learner in
ηoticiηg an  incorrect form  (through  rεcasts, prompts, and  other attention-g'εtting
devices), and second, for  the learner to initiate  repair (with as  little  prompting as
possible from the teacher).

The teacher needs to develop the intuition, through experience and established
theoretical foundations, for  ascert없ning which option or combination of options is
appropriate  at  given  moments.  Principles  of  optimal  affective  and  cognitive
feedback,  of  reinforcement  theory,  and  of  communicative  language  teaching  all
combine to form those intuitions.

One step toward developing such intuitions may be taken by considering the
model in Figure 19.9, which illustrates what I would claim are the split-second series
of decisions that a teacher makes when a student has uttered some deviant form of
English  in  the  classroom.  In  those  few  nanoseconds,  information  is  accessed,
processed, and evaluated, with a decision forthcoming on what the teacher is going
to “ do” about the deviant form.  Imagine that you 앙e the teacher and let me walk
you through the flowchart.

Some  sort of deviant  utterance  is  made  by a  student.  Instantly,  you  run  this

speech event through a number of nearly simultaneous screens:

1.  You identi다 the type of deviation (lexical, phonological, etc.).
2.  In addition, you often, but not always, identiη its source, which will be useful

in determining how you might treat the deviation.

3.  Next, the complexity of the deviation may determine not only whether to
treat or ignore but how to treat, if that is your decision.  In some cases a
deviation may require so much explanation, or so much interruption of the
task at hand, that it isn’t  worth treating it.

4.  Your most crucial and possibly the veπ first decision among these 10 factors
is  to quickly decide whether the utterance is interpretable (local) or not
명lobal).  Iρcal errors can some디mes be ignored for the sake of maintaining a
flow of communication.  Global errors by definition often call for some sort
of treatment, even if only in the form of a clarification request.

5.  Then, from your knowledge of this student, you make a guess at whether it is
a performance slip (mistake) or competence error (see PLLT,  Chapter 2); this
is not always easy to do, but you may be surprised to know that a teacher's
intuition on this factor will often be correct.  Mistakes rarely call for treat
ment, while errors more frequently demand some sort of teacher rεsponse.

The  above  information  is  quickly  stored  as  you  perhaps  simultaneously  run

through the next five  possible considerations.

6.  From your knowledge about this learner, you make a series of instant

judgments about the learner's language ego fragility, anxiety level, confidence,
and willingness to accept correction.  If, for example, the learner rarely speaks
in class or shows high an회ety and low confidence when attempting to speak,
you may decide to ignore the deviant utterance.

7.  Your knowledge of the learner's 파iguistic stage of development will help you

decide how to treat the deviation.

8.  Your own pedagogical focus at the moment (Is this a form-focused task to

begin with? Does this lesson focus on the form that was deviant? What are the
overall objectives of the lesson or task?) will help you to decide whether or
not to treat.

9.  Also consider the communicative context of the deviation (Was  thε studεnt in

the middle of a productive flow of language? How easily could you
interrupt?).

10.  Somewhere in this rapid-fire processing, your own style as a teacher comes

into play:Are you generally an interventionist? laissez-faire? If, for example, you
tend as a rule to make veη few error treatments, a treatment now on a minor
deviation would be out of character and misinterpreted by the student.

You  are now ready to decide whether to treat or ignore the deviation!  If you
decide to do nothing, you simply move on.  But if you decide to do somεthing in the
way of treatment, you have a number of treatment options, as discussed earlier.  You
have  to decide when to  treat, who will treat, and how to treat, and each of those
decisions offers a  range of possibilities, as  indicated in the chart.  Notice that you,
the  teacher,  do  not  always  have  to  be  the  person  who  provides  the  treatment.
Manner of treatment varies according to the input to the student, the directness of
the treatment, the student’s output, and your follow-up.

After one very quick deviant utterance by a student, you have made an amazing
number of observations and evaluations that go into the process of error treatment.
New teachers will  find  such a  prospect  daunting, perhaps, but with  experience,
m없iy of these considerations will become automatic.

ASSESSING SPEAKING IN THE CIASSROOM

Assessing  spea힘ng skills  in the classroom  has one clear advantage  over assessing
listening:  Speech  is  observable,  recordable,  and  measurable.  However,  once  the
criterion of your assessment moves beyond the phonolo밍cal level, this advantage
quickly disappears as accEψtable responses are more difficult to speci다 reliably.

valid, reliable  assεssmεnt procedure.

Evaluating and Scoring Speaking Tasks

The  evaluation  of oral  production  pεrformance can  get  quitε complicated.  First,
you nεed to be clear in specifying  thε level of language you  ar,ε targeting.  One or
more of at l℃ast six possiblε critεria may be your target:

•  pronunciation
•  fluency
•  vocabulary
•  grammar
•  discourse features ( cohεsion, soci이inguistic appropriateness, etc.)
•  task (accomplishing the objεctiv1ε of the task)

Some scales add “ comprehension” to account for thε extent to which a student
has compr'εhεnded directions or elicitation.  This categorγ can be subsumed in thε
last two criteria above.

Within each of these  categoriεs you can judge a student’s response(s) to be at
one  of several  possible  lev'εls  of pεrformance.  ηpi callχ we  think  of beginning,
intermediate, and advancεd as  potential lev1εls.  But as  we saw in Chaptεr 7, those
categories are quite  slippεη.  Moreover, threε lev'εls may not bε sufficient for your
classroom purposes, and you may wish to score perform없ice on fiv,ε or six  levεls,
ranging  from “novice” or “ true  bεginner” to “superior” or “ comp letεly acceptable.”
What℃Vεr those categories are, it is important to describe thεm as clearly as possible
in order to  make reliable  evaluations.  For more on the issue  of specifying scoring
criteria, I suggest you consult my Language Assessment book (Brown, 2004) and/or
Underhill's (1987) manual of oral testing techniques.

(pagε 330) and, in pairs, to identi디 as many of the sevεn factors of

Thε written word surrounds us  daily.  It  εnlightens and confuses us, it  amuses and
dεpresses us,  it  hεals and  sickεns us.  At  every  turn,  we  who  are  members  of a
literate society are depεndεnt on 20-somε-odd letters and a handful of othεr written
symbols f。r significant, εvcn life-and깅eath, matters in our lives.  How do we teach
second  language  learnεrs to  master  this  written  code? What  do  wε tεach them?
What ar,ε the issues?

As  you  rεad this  chaptεc keep  in  mind  that  once  again,  interactiv1ε, integrated
approaches  to  language  tεaching emphasizε the  interriεlationship of skills.  Reading
ab피ty w버 bε developed  bεst in  association  with  writing,  listεning, and  speaking
activitiεs.  Even in thosε coursεs that may bε labεled “rεading;’ your goals w피 bε best
a chiεvεd by  capitalizing  on  the  connection  betweεn reading  and  othεr modes  of
performance,  especially  thε  reading-writing  relationship.  So,  W℃  focus  here  on
reading as a componεnt of general second language proficiency, but ultimately reading
must  be  considered  only  in  the  perspective  of  the  whole  picture  of  intεractiv1ε

languag,ε tεaching.

RESEARCH ON READING A SECOND LANGUAGE

By  the  1970s, research  on  reading  onε’s first  language  had  beεn flourishing  for  a
couple of decadεs as  solutions wεre being sought to why some children couldn’t
read.  But research on reading in a second language was almost nonεxistent.  Then,
with  Kennεth  Goodman’s  (1970)  sεminal  article,  “ Reading:  A  Psycholinguistic
Guessing Game,” and other subsequεnt work, second language specialists began to
tackle the unique issues and questions facing sεcond language reading pedagogy.  A
glancε through  what  is  now  thrεε decades  of rεsearch reveals  some  significant

357

358

CHAPTER  20  Teaching  Reading

findings  that will affect you and your approach to tεaching reading skills.  Some of
the highlights arε reviewed here.

1.  Bottom-up and top-down processing

Led  by Goodman’S  (1970) work, the  distinction  betweεn bottom-up  and top

down processing bεcame a cornerstone of reading mαhodology for yεars to comζ
In bottom-up processing, r℃aders must first  recognize a  multiplicity of linguistic
signals  Getters,  morphεmes, syllables, words, phrases, grammatical  cuεs, discoursε
markers) and usε th℃ir linguistic  data-processing mechanisms to impose somε sort
of  order  on  thεsε  signals.  These  data갑riven  operations  obviously  require  a
sophisticated knowledgε of the language itself.  From among all thε p℃rceived data,
the reader selects the signals that make some sense, that cohεre, that “ mean.”

Virtually  따l  reading  involves  a  risk-a guessing  game,  in  Goodman’S  words
be ca usε readers must, through a puzzle-solving process, infer meanings, decidε what
to retain and not to rεtain, and move on.  This is  where a  complementaη method of
processing  writtεn  text  is  imperative:  top-down,  or  conceptually  driven,
processing in which we draw on 01표 own intelligence and expεriεnce to understand
a  text.  Christinε Nuttall  (1996, pp.  16-1 꺼 compares  bottom-up processes with the
image of a scientist with a  magnifying glass or microscopε ℃xamining all  the minute
details of some phenomεnon, w피le top-down processing is  파ce taking an εagl℃’s-eye
view of a landscape below.  Such a pictur℃ rεminds us that field-indεpεndεnt andfiεld­
dependent cognitive styles (sεe PLLT, Chapter 5) are analogous to bottom-up and top
down processing, respectiv,εly.

A half-century ago, perhaps, reading spεcialists might hav'ε argued that the bεst
way  to  teach  rεading  is  through  bottom-up  methodology:  tεach  symbols,
grapheme-phonεme correspondences, syllables, and lexical  recognition first,  then
comprehension would be derived from the sum of thε parts.  More recent research
on  tεaching reading  has  shown  that  a  combination  of top-down  and  bottom-up
processing, or what  has  come  to  be  called  interactive  reading, is  almost  always  a
primaη ingredi℃nt in successful teaching methodology becausε both processes ar,℃
imp야tant.  “ In practice, a  reader continually shifts from  onε focus  to  anothεr,now
adopting  a  top-down  approach  to  predict  probable  meaning,  thεn moving  to  thε
bottom-up approach to check whether that is  rεally what the writer says" (Nuttall,
1996, p. 17).

2.  Schema theory and background knowledge

How do readers construct meaning? How do thεy dεcide what to hold on to, and
having made that decision, how do they infer a wtiter’s  messag'ε? Thεse arε the sorts
of questions  addressed  by  what  has  come  to  b℃ known  as  schema  theory,  thε
hallmark  of which is  that a  text does  not by itself caπy meaning  (Anderson, 2004;
Eskεy,  2005;  Grabe,  2004).  πie  rεadεr brings  information,  knowledge,  emotion,
experience, and  culture-that is,  schemata  (plural)-to  the  ptinted  word.  Schema
thε01γ is not a new construct.  Three decades ago, Mark Clarkε and S없idra Silberstein
(1977, pp. 136-13η captured the essence of schema theory:

359

Resεarch  has  shown  that  rεacting  is  only  incid℃ntally  visual.  More

bεtween linguistic  knowledg,ε and knowledge of thε world.

A good  εxamplε of thε rolε of schεmata in  reading  is  found  in  thε following

an℃cdotε;

A  15-year-old boy got up the nεrve one day to try out for  the school

The  widεsprεad acceptancε of schema  theotγ by reading  rεsεarchers has  not
gonε unchallengεd.  Nassaji  (2002)  provided  an  alternative  vi℃W of  th℃  role  of

“ construction-integration”  modε1, thε learner  is  seen  to  play a  more  activε rolε in
constructing meaning, while reading, than is proposed by schema theory.

Onε of thε quεstions that has beεn askεd about teaching reading has been thε

(Anderson,  1999, 2004; Eskey,  2005; Grabe, 2004), and  citε research  in support of

Instruction should of coursε provide an optimal n디X ofεach,but NεilAndεrson(l999,

strategies  of sεlfplanning, monitoring, and evaluating  one’S own reading  procεSSεs.
William  Grabe  (2004)  str'℃ssed  the  coordinated  use  of  multiple  sσatεgies  while

reading, postr'εading, and follow-up strat℃gies for reading, will bε discussed later in this

On the  othεr hand, there is  a  place for extensive rεading of longεr tεxts with

5.  Fluency and reading rate

In recent yεars thεre has bεen a rεsurgεncε of interest in thε rolε of vocabulary

It’s  readily  apparεnt  from  just  a  cursory  survεy of  rεsεarch  on  second

As  ESL  materials  and  mεthods continue  to  apply  both  bottom-up  and  top

Aside from thε eight major issues touched on above, a multitude of othεr topics

are grist for currεnt researchers’ mills:

•  the rolε of cognition in rεading
•  the role of automaticity in word recognition
•  reading as sociocultural practicε
•  effective tεchniques for activating schemata
•  relationships of reading to writing

And  the list  goes  on.  At  this  stag℃ in  your  professional  career when you  are
learning to teach, rather than immersing you in oc℃ans of resεarch data, it is perhaps

GENRES OF WRITIEN IANGUAGE

In  thε previous  two  chapters we looked  at  types  of spoken language  so  that you
could ident파 the kinds of language your listening and spεaking tεchniques should
includζ  Here, we do the samε for typεs, or genres, of reading and writing.

In  our highly literate  society, there are literally  hundrεds of diffiεrent types of
written tεxts, a  much larger variety than found  in spokεn texts.  Each of thε typεs
listed below represents, or is  an example of, a genre of written language.  Each has
certain rules or convεntions for its manifestation, and we are thus able immediately
to  identi디7  a  gεnre and  to  know  what  to  look  for  within  the  tεxt.  Consider  thε
following  nonexhaustiv1ε list:

•  nonfiction: reports, editorials, εssays, articles, rεfεrεnce (dictionaries, etc.)
•  fiction: novels, short stories, jokes, drama, poetry
•  lettεrs: personal, business
•  grεeting cards
•  diaries, journals
•  memos (e.g., interofficε mεmos)
•  messages (e.g., phone messages)
•  announcemεnts
•  newspaper “journalεse ”
•  academic writing: short-answεr tεst responses, reports, papers, thesεs, books
•  forms, applications
•  questionnaires
•  directions
•  labels
•  signs
•  recipes
•  bills (and other financial statements)

CHAPTER  20  Tea [ hing Reading

363

•  maps
•  manuals
•  menus
•  schedules (e.g., transportation tables)
•  advertisements: commercial, personal
•  invitations
•  dirεctories (e.g., telephone, yellow pages)
•  comic strips, cartoons

And  I’m  surε you  could  name  a  few  more!  It’s  interesting that  every literate
adult knows  thε distinctive features  of each of these genres ‘  You  can immediately
distinguish a menu from a map, an interoffice memo from a telephone message, and
a  bill  from  an  invitation-well, okay,  some  bills  are  invitations  to  pay!  When  you
encounter onε of the above, you usually know what your p따posε is  in reading  it,
and therefor,ε you know what to select and what not to select for  short- and long
term memory-in other words, you bring various schemata to bear on the message
that you  have  chosen to  retain.  What would happen if you  didn’t  know some of
these  differences?  That  is  what  your  students  may  encounter  when  they  read
English, so part of your job as a teacher is  to enlighten your students on features of
these  genres  and  to  help  them  to  develop  strategies  for  extracting  necessaη
meaning from each.

CHARACTERISTICS OF WRITIEN LANGUAGE

There are quite a  number of salient and relevant differences between spoken and
written language.  Students already literate in their native languages will of course
be familiar with the broad, basic characteristics of written language; however, some
characteristics of English writing, especially certain rhetorical conv1εntions, may be
so  different  from  their  native  language  that  reading  efforts  are  blocked.  The
characteristics listed below will also be of some help for you in

a.  diagnosing certain reading difficulties arising from the idiosyncrasiεs of

written language,

b.  pointing your tεchniquεs toward specific objectives, and
c.  reminding students of some of the advantages of written language over

spoken.

1.  Permanence

Spokεn language  is  fleeting.  Once you  speak a  sentence, it  vanishes  (unless
there  is  a  tape  recorder  around).  The  hearer,  therefore,  is  called  upon  to  make
immediate percεptions and immediate storage.  Written language is permanent (or
as  permanent as  paper and computer disks  are!), and therefore the  reader has an
opportunity to return again and again, if necessary, to a word or phrasε or sentence,
or even a whole text.

364

CHAPTER  20  Tea [ hing Reading

2.  Processing time

A  corollary  to  the  above  is  the  processing  time  that  thε rεader gains.  Most
reading  contεxts allow  readers  to  rεad at  thεir own rate.  They aren’t  forced  into
following  the  rate  of delivery,  as  in  spokεn language.  A good  deal  of emphasis  is
placed on  rεading speεd in  our fast-paced,  timε-conscious socieη, which  is  good
nεws and  bad  nεws.  The  good  nεws is  that  rεadεrs can indeed  capitalizε on  thε
natur'ε of the printεd word and dεvεlop very rapid  rεading rates.  The bad nεws is
that  many  pεoplε who  are “ slow”  readers  are  made  to  fεel  inferior.  In  practice,
εxcept for  the  timε factor  itself, fast  rεaders do  not necessarily have  an  advantagε
ovεr slow rεadεrs.

3.  Distance

The written word allows  messag℃S to bε sεnt across two dimensions: physical
distance  and  temporal  distance.  Thε pedagogical  significance  of this  centers  on
interpretation.  The task  of thε readεr is  to  interprεt languagε that was writtεn in
some  other place  at  some  other time  with  only the written  words  thεmsεlves as
contεxtual clues.  Rεaders can’t  confront an author and say, “ Now, what εxactly did
you mean by that?” Nor can they transport themsεlves back through a time machine
and “ sεε” the  surrounding  context, as  wε can  in  face-to-facε conversations.  This
sometimes  decontextualized  natur'ε  of  writing  is  onε of  thε things  that  makes
reading difficult.

4.  Orthography

In spoken language, we have phonemes that correspond to writing's graphεm℃s.
But  we  also  have  stress, rhythm,  juncture, intonation, pauses, volume, voice  quality
S℃ttings, and nonverbal cues, all  of which  ℃nhancε thε mεssage.  In writing we have
graphemεs-that’s it! Yes,  somεtimes punctuation, pictmεs, or charts lend a  helping
hand.  And, yes,  a  writεr can  dεscribe the  afor'εmentioned phonological cues, as  in,
“With loud, rasping gnmts, punctuated by roars of pain, he slowly dragged himsεIf out
of the  line  of εnemy fire.” But  th℃se written  symbols  stand alone  as  the  one  set  of
signals  that  thε  reader  must  perceive.  Because  of the  frequεnt ambiguity  that  is
present in a good deal of writing, readers must do their best to inf야 to interpret, and
to “ read bεtwεεn the linεs.”

English orthography itself, in spite of its reputation for being “irrεgular.,” is highly
predictable  from
its  spoken  counterpart,  espεcially  when  one  considers
m야phological information  as  well.  For  literate  learnεrs  of  English,  our  spelling
systεm prεsents 01괴y minor difficulties, even for those whose native languages have
quite different systems.  Actually, most of the irregula디ty in English manifests itsεlfin
high-frequency words (of, to,  have, do,  doηe, was, etc.), and oncε those words are in
place, the rεst of the system can usually be master,εd without special instruction.

5.  Complexity

You might be tempted to say that writing is mor'ε complex than speech, but in
reality,  that  would  bε difficult  to  demonstrate.  Writing  and  speεch  repr,εsent

CHAPTER 20  Teaching  Reading

365

different  modes  of complexity, and the most salient difference  is  in the  nature  of
clauses.  Spoken  language  tends  to  have  shorter  clauses  connected  by  more
coordinate conjunctions, while writing has longer clauses and more subordination.
Thε shorter  clausεs are  often  a  factor  of the  redundancy  we  build  into  speech
(repeating subjεcts and verbs for clarity).  Look at the following pair:

1.  Bεcause of the frequent ambiguity that therefore is present in a good deal of
writing, rεaders must do their bεst to infer, to interpret, and to “ read between
the lines."

2.  Ther'ε’s frεquεnt ambiguity in a lot of writing.  And so, readers have to infer a
lot.  They also have to interpret what they read.  And sometimes they have to
“rεad between the lines."

Thε cognitive  compl℃xity of version  1, thε written version, is  no greater than
version 2, the spoken version.  But structurally, four clauses were used in version 2
to replace the one long clause in version  1.

Rεaders-especially sεcond language  readers  who  may  be quite  adept  in  the
spoken  language-have  to  retool  their  cognitive  perceptors  in  order  to  extract
meaning  from  the  written  code.  The  linguistic  differences  betwεεn speech  and
writing are another major contributing cause to difficulty.

6.  Vocabulary

It is  true that written English typically utilizεs a greater variety of lexical  itεms
than  spoken  conversational  English.  In  our  ev'επday give  and  takε with  family,
friends,  and  colleagues,  vocabulary  is  limitεd.  Because  writing  allows  the  writer
more  processing  time,  bεca usε of a  desir'ε  to  be  precise  in  writing,  없id  simply
because of the formal conventions of writing (see #7 below), lower-frequency words
often  appear.  Such  words  can  present  stumbling  blocks  to  learners.  However,
because the meaning of a good many unknown words can be predicted from their
context, and because sometimes the overall  mεaning of a sentence or paragraph is
nevertheless  still  clear, learners should refrain from  the frequent  use  of a  bilingual
dictionaη.

7.  Form떼ity

Writing  is  quite  frequently  more  formal  than  speech.  What do  we mean  by
that?  Formality  refers  to  prescribed  forms  that  certain  written  messages  must
adhere to.  The reason that you can both rεcognize a menu and decide what to eat
fairly quickly is that menus conform to certain conventions.  Things ar,ε categorized
(appetizers, salads, entrees, desserts, etc.)  in  logical  order and  subcategorized (all
seafood  dishεs ar,ε listεd together); exotic or creative names for dishes are  usually
defined;  pricεs  are  given  for  each  item;  and  the  menu  isn’t  so  long  that  it
overwhelms you.  We  have rhetorical, or organizational, formality in essay writing
that demands a  writεr’s conformity to conventions like  paragraph topics; we have
logical order for, say, comparing and contrasting something; we have openings and

366

CH.재TER 20  Teaching  Reading

closings,  and  a  pr1εfεr℃nc℃ for  nonr1εdundancy and  subordination  of clauses,  εtc.
Until a readεr is familiar with the formal fεatures of a writtεn text,somε difficulty in
interprεtation may εnsue.

MICRO- AND MACROSKILLS  FOR READING COMPREHENSION

Table  20.1  on  page  367,  an  adaptation  of the  modεIs of micro- and  macroskills
offered in the previous two chapters, is a brεakdown of what students of ESL  nεed
to do to become efficiεnt rεadεrs.

STRATEGIES FOR READING COMPREHENSION

For most sεcond language learners who are already litεrate in a  pr1εvious language,
reading  comprehεnsion is  primarily  a  matter  of developing  appropriatε, efficient
comprehension  strategies.  Somε strategies  are  relatεd to  bottom-up  procedures,
and others enhance the top-down processes.  Following ar,ε 10 such strategies, εach
of which can be practically applied to your classroom techniques.

1.  Identify the purpose in reading.

How many times have you been told to rεadsomεthing without knowing why
you’vε beεn asked  to  read  it? You  do  only  a  mεdiocr1ε job  of rεtaining what  you
“rεad” and  pεrhaps are  rather  slow  in  the  process.  Efficient  rεading consists  of
clearly identifying thε purposε in reading somεthing.  By doing so, you know what
you’re  looking for  and  can weed out potential distracting information.  Whεnεver
you  arε teaching  a  reading  technique, make  sure  students  know their purpose  in
reading something.

2.  Use graphemic rules and patterns to aid in bottom-up decoding.

At  the  beginning  levels  of lεarning English,  one  of the  difficultiεs students
encounter in learning to read is  making the correspondencεs between spokεn and
writtεn  English.  In  many  cases,  learners  havε  bεcome  acquainted  with  oral
language and havε some difficulty learning English spelling convεntions.  They may
need  hints  and  εxplanations  about  certain  English  orthographic  rules  and
peculiaritiεs.  While  you  can  often  assume  that  one-to-one  grapheme-phonemε
corr'εspondenc℃S  will  be  acquired  with  ease,  other  rεlationships  might  prove
difficult.  Considεr how you might provide hints and pointers on such patterns as
these:

•  “ short” vowel sound in VC  patterns (bat,  him, leg,  wish,  etc.)
•  “ long” vowel sound in VCe  (final silεnt e) patterns (l,αte, time,  bite,  etc.)
•  “long” vowel sound in VV  patterns (seat,  coat,  etc.)
•  distinguishing “hard” candgfrom “soft" c andg (cat vs.city, game vs.gem, etc.)

Table 20.1.  Micro- and  macroskills for  listening comprehension

Microskills

1.  Discriminate among the distinctive graphemes and orthographic  patterns of English.
2.  Retain  chunks of language of different  lengths  in  short-term  memory.
3.  Process writing at  an  efficient rate of speed to suit the purpose.
4.  Recognize a core of words,  and  interpret wo「d order patterns and their significance.
5.  Recognize grammatical  word  classes (nouns,  verbs,  etc.),  systems  (e.g.,  tense,

agreement,  pluralization),  patternκ rules,  and elliptical  forms.

Macroskills

7.  Recognize cohesive devices  in  written  discourse and their role  in  signaling the

relationship  between and among clauses.

8.  Recc핑nize the rhetorical  forms  of written  discourse and their significance for

interpretation.

9.  Recognize the communicative functions  of written  texts,  according to  form  and

purpose.

10.  Infer  context that is  not explicit by  using  background  knowledge.
11.  Infer  links  and connections between events,  ideas,  etc.;  deduce causes and effects;
and detect such  relations as  main  idea,  supporting idea,  new  information,  given
information,  generalization,  and exemplification.

12.  Distinguish  between  literal  and  implied  meanings.
13.  Detect culturally specific  references and  interpret them  in  a context of the appropriate

cultural  schemata.

14.  Develop and  use a  battery of reading strategies such as scanning and skimming,

detecting discourse  markers,  guessing the  meaning of words from  context,  and
activating schemata for  the  interpretation  of texts.

These and a multitude of other phonics approaches to reading can prove usεful for
learners  at  the  bεginning level  and  especially  useful  for  teaching  children  and
nonliterate adults.

3.  Use efficient silent reading techniques for improving fluency.

If you  are  teaching  beginning  level  students, this  particular strategy W피 not

4.  Skim the text for main ideas.

Perhaps the two most valuable reading strategies for learners (as wεII as  nativε
speakers) ar'ε skimming and scanning.  Skimming consists of quickly running one’s
eyes across a whole text (such as an essay, article, or chapter) for its gist.  Skimming
giv1εs readεrs thε advantage of being ablε to predict the purposε of the passagε, the

This giv,εs thεm a head start as thεy εmbark on mor'ε focused reading.  You can train
students  to  skim  passagεs by giving  them, say,  30  seconds  to  look  through  a  fεw
pages of material, dosε thεir books, and  thεn tell you what they learned.

5.  Scan the text for specific information.

Thε sεcond in the most valuablε catεgory is scanning, or quickly sεarching for
some particular piεcε or piεces of information in a text.  Scanning exercises may ask
students to look for  namεs or dates, to find a  definition of a  kεy concept, or to list a
certain number of supporting details.  The purpose of scanning is  to extract specific
information without reading through the wholε text.  For acadεmic English, scanning
is  absolutεly  ℃ssential.  In  vocational  or  general  English,  scanning  is  important  in
dealing with genrεs lik℃ schedules, manuals, h] rms,etc.

6.  Use semantic mapping or clustering.

Rεaders can  εasily be  overwhelmed  by a  long  string  of ideas  or εvεnts.  The
strategy of semantic mapping,  or grouping  idεas into meaningful  dustεrs, helps
thε reader to provide some order to thε chaos.  Making such semantic maps can bε

This requires  the application of sophisticated top-down  proc℃ssing skills.  Thε

b.  The policeman held up his hand and stopped the car.

her birthday money and rushed into the house ... (Rummelhart, 1977,

PRINCIPLES FOR TEACHING READING SKILLS

1.  In an integrated course, don’t  overlook a  specific focus on reading skills.
ESL  students who ar℃ litεI‘ tε in thεir own language som℃timεs ar'℃ left to thεir
own dεvices when it  comεs to l℃arning rεading skills.  We  oftεn assume that  thεy
will  lεarn  good  rεacting  simply  by  absorption  through  genεrous  offerings  of
εxtεnsiv,ε  rεading opportunitiεs.  In  reality,  thεre is  much  to  bε gained  by  your
focusing  on rεading skills.  This  chaptεr has  provided some guidelines  on how  to
direct that focus.  On the othεr hand, it should be clear from prεvious comments in
this chaptεr that it is important to make sure that your students have ample time for
εxtensive  reading.  Sustained  silent  reading  allows  them  to  develop  a  sεnse  of
fluency.  Also, silent reading then bεcomes an excεllent method for self instruction
on the part of the learner.

2.  Use techniques that are intrinsically motivating.

What do you think mak℃S for  intεrεsting and relevant rεading for your studεnts?
Of the long list of gεnres at the beginning of this chapt야 how many w피 your students
εncountεr in “ real life”?  Use  those texts.  What ar1ε your students' goals in learning to
rεad English?  Focus on thosε goals.  Choosε material that is relevant to those goals.

374

CHAPTER  20  Teaching Reading

One popular and intrinsically motivating approach to reading instruction is the
Language Experience Approach (LEA), referred to in Chapter 17, where students
create  their  own  material  for  reading.  Other  approaches  in  which  learners  are
given  choices  in  sεlecting reading  material  offer a  degree  of intrinsic  motivation.
Carefully sequεnced readings and instructional strategies that are success-oriented
give further intrinsic involvement in the process.  Another way to enhance intrinsic
motives  is  to  offiεr  opportunities  for  learners  to  gauge  their  progress  through
P℃riodic instructor-initiated assessments and self assessments.

3.  Balance authenticity and readability in choosing te었s.

By now, the importance of authentic language should be more than clear.  But
in teaching reading, one issue that has invited some controversy is the advisability of
what are called “simplified texts,” in which an otherwise authentic text is  edited to
keep language within the proficiency level of a  set of students.  In order for you to
make a decision on this issue, it is important to distinguish between (a) simple texts
and (b) simplified texts and to understand sources of complexity in reading material.
Authentic simple texts can either be devised or located in the real world.  From
ads  to  labεls to  reports  to  essays,  texts  ar'ε  available  that  are  grammatically  and
lexically  simple.  Simplifying  an  existing  potential  reading  selection  may  not  be
necessary.  Yet if simplification must be done, it is important to prεsεrve the natural
redundancy, humor, wit, and other captivating fεatures of the ori횡nal matεrial.

Second, you might ask yourself what “ simplicity” is and then determine if a  S[ }
called simplified text is really simpler than its original.  Sometimes simplified texts
remove so much natural redundancy that they actually become difficult.  And what
you perceive as textual complexity may be more a product of background schemata
than  of linguistic  complexity.  Take  another  look  at  the  list  of characteristics  of
written  language earlier in this  chapter and you will  no  doubt  see what it  is  that
makes a  text difficult.  In light of those criteria, is  a  simplified text really simpler?
The answer may be no. Richard  Day and Julian  Bamford (1998, p. 53), in warning
against “the cult of authenticity and the myth of simplification,” contended that our
CLT approach has overemphasized the need for so-called authenticity, and that ther1ε
is indeed a  place for simplified texts in reading instruction.

Christine  Nuttall  (1996)  offered  three  criteria  for  choosing  reading  texts  for
students:  (1)  suitability  of  content:  material  that  students  will  find  interesting,
enjoyable,  challen~잊ng, and  appropriate  for  thεir  goals  in  learning  English;  (2)
exploitability: a text that facilitates the achievement of certain language and content
go와s, that is exploitable for instructional tasks and techniques, and that is integratable
with other skills (listening, spea퍼ng, writing); (3) readability: a text with lεxical and
structural 띠fficulty that will challenge students without overwhelming thεm.

4.  Encourage the development of reading strategies.

Already in this chapter, 10 different reading strategies have been discussed.  To

what extent are you getting your students to use all these sσategies?

[ HAPTER 20  Teaching  Reading

375

5.  Include both bottom-up and top-down techniques.

In ourcrazε for communicative, authentic languagε activity in thε classroom, we
somεtimεs forget  that lεarners can  indεεd bεnεfit from  studying  the  fundamεntals.
Make sure that you give enough classroom  time  to focusing  on thε building blocks
of writt℃n language, gearεd appropriately for each lεvel.

6.  Follow the SQ3R sequence.

Onε ℃ffectivε series of procεdures for approaching a rεa ding tεxt has com℃ to

be labεlεd thε SQ3R techniquε, a  process consisting of the following five  stεps:

1.  Surve;’; Skim  thε text for an overview of main ideas.
2.  Question:The rεader asks questions about what hε or she wishεs to g℃t out

of the text.

3.  Reαd: Read  thε tεxt whil℃ looking for answers to the previously formulatεd

questions ’

4.  Recite: Reprocεss the salient points of th℃ text through oral or written

language.

5.  Review: AssεSS thε importance of what onε has just rεad and incorporatε it

into  long-tεrm associations.

This  S℃ries of techniques of coursε may  not fit  all  classes and  contεxts, but it

serves as a gεnεral guide for a reading class.

7.  Plan on prereading, during-reading, and after-reading phases.

It’s  tεmpting, espεcially at  intermεdiate and  advanced  lev℃ls, to  tell  students,
“ Okay  now, class,  rεad the  next  two  pagεs silεntly.” No  introduction, no  hints  on
anything special to do whilε reading, and nary a thought about something to follow
the silεnt rεading period.  A good rubric to kεεp in mind for tεaching reading is thε
following three-part framεwork:

1.  Before you read: Spend somε timε introducing a topic, encouraging

skimming, scanning, predicting, and activating schemata.  Students can
bring the best of thεir knowledgε and skills  to a text when they hav1ε beεn
given a chance to “ ease into” thε passagε.

2.  W꺼ile you read: Not all  rεading is simply εxtεnsive or global reading.

Th erε maybe cεrtain facts or rhetorical devicεs that students should takε
note ofwhilε thεy read.  Give  studεnts a  sens℃ of purpose for reading
rather than just reading becausε you ordered it.

3.  After you read: Comprehension questions arε just onε form of activity

appropriate for postreading.  Also consider vocabulary study, identifying

the author's purposε, discussing thε author's line of reason“ig, examining

grammatical structurεs, or steεring students toward a follow-up  writing

εx℃rcisε.

376

CHAPTER  20  Teaching Reading

8.  Build an assessment aspect into your techniques.

Bεcause reading, likε listεning comprehεnsion, is totally unobsεrvable (we have
to infer comprehεnsion from othεr behavior), it is as important in reading as  it is  in
listening to be ablε to accurately assess students' comprεhension and developmεnt
of skills.  Consider some of thε following  ovεn rεsponses (model℃d after the list in
Chapter 18 for listening) that indicate comprehension:

1.  Doing-the readεr responds physically to a command.
2.  Choosing-the rεadεr sεlects from alternatives posed orally or in writing.
3.  Transferring-the rεadεr summarizεs orally what is read.
4.  Answεring-the rεadεr answεrs questions about the passage.
5.  Condεnsing-the reader outlines or takεs notes on a passage.
6.  Extending-thε reader provides an ending to a story.
7.  Duplicating-the read℃r trans latεs the mεssage into the native language or

copies it (b℃ginning level, for vεry short passages only).

8.  Modeling-the reader puts togethεr a toy, for example, aftεr reading

directions for assembly.

9.  Conversing-the rεader engagεs in a conversation that indicates appropriate

processing of information.

1WO READING LESSONS

Following are excerpts from two different ESL  textbooks designed to teach reading
skills.  In both cases, of course, the other three skills are implied in thε unfolding of
thε lesson.

The first εxcerpt (Figur'ε 20.3), on rain forests, is designed for beginners (Boonε,
Bεnnett, & Motai, 1988).  It illustrates the use of natural, authentic language and tasks
at  thε beginning  lev'ε1.  Some  attention  is  given  to  bottom-up  skills,  but  not  at  thε
expense of top깅own processing, even at this level.

The second excerpt (Figure  20.4  on pages  379-385), on gεn℃tic  εngineering
and  DNA,  is  for  advanced  students  (Silberstein,  Dobson,  &  Clarkε,  2002).  It
illustratεs the use of an article from a nεwspaper as the main focal point for reading.
Notice that the lesson begins with top-down processing, on the assumption that at
this  lev1ε1, the grεater need is  for activating  schεmata (note  thε “Bζfore You  Bεgin”
and “Skimming  and  Scanning"  sections)  and  understanding  the  organization  and
purpose  of the  article.  Another  genre  is  also  presented:  a  chart  of  thε human
genome.  Students  are  then  led  to  take  a  more  detailed  look  at  their  level  of
comprehension  of  each  genre,  followed  by  “ Critical  Reading,”  which  calls  for
students to articulate their beliefs.  Open discussion or writing is  then encouragεd,
and follow℃d by an exercise in strategic study of “Vocabulary from Context.”

CHAPTER  20  Teaching  Reading

377

Figure 20.3.  Rain  forests  (from  Boone,  Bennett,  &  Motai,  1988,  pp.  14-15)

RAIN  FORESTS
by Scott Adelson

Have you  ever seen  a rain  forest?  Where do rain  forests  grow? What is  unusual  or
unique about rain  forests?  Are  they  important to the  world?

This  text is  about special  forests  in  tropical  areas  of the  world that are being cut down,
and  about a special  group that is  trying to save  them.

믿 Vocabular때 Watch for

debt
organization
conservation
reserve
basin
region
savannah

-money you  owe to another person
-group
-saving the  land and  the  animals
-a safe  place for animals and  nature
-valley where there  is  a river
-area;  large place
-dry, flat  land;  plain

I흡| READ

In  many tropical  countries,  people are  cutting down rain  forests  to  make room  for

farms.They  hope that the farms  will make money for them  so  that they can  pay their
debts.  But a new organization is  trying to  help these  countries save  their forests.  The
name of this organization  is  Conservation  International.  Conservation  International  pays
countries  not to cut down their rain  forests.

Their first agreement was  with  Bolivia,  for a 4,000,000 acre reserve  in  the Amazon

River basin  in  northeast Bolivia. The  region  has  savannahs,  deep woods,  and  rain
forests.  It is  famous  for the different plants and  unusual  wildlife that  live there.  Bolivia
and  Conservation  International will take  care of the  reserve  together.

This  idea  of helping countries make rain  forest  reserves  is  so  unusual  that Brazil
and  Ecuador,  which are both  interested  in  this  program,  are already  having talks with
Conservation  International.

Do you  understand  the  text?

1.  Here are some common English sayings. What do 버ey mean?  Do they

2.  How might recent discoveries  in genetics contribute roan understanding

3.  In discussions of pers。nality, most explanations can be  described as

these explanations?

4.  Would you care  if others had a copy of your medical records and learned

about your genetic makeup?

Recent discoveries  by scientists working on the  Human Genome Project have been reported in
scholarly publications and the popular press. They report success in mapping the human genome.
Gene mapping is  the process of identifying which genes lie where on the DNA strands that make up
biol。?ical inheritance. The following article, which appeared in  newspapers across  the United States,
examines potential dangers of the research.

2.  Who are the following individuals, and whac are cheir scientific qualifications? What role do they

Vocabulary from Context

Both the ideas and the vocabulary in  the following p양sage are  taken from “ Gene Mapping May
Foster Discrimination.” Use the context provided to determine the meanings of the italicized words.
Write a definition, synonym, or description of each of the italicized vocabulary items in the space
provided.

The gene is often called the building block oflife because it is

the smallest unit to carry information from one generation to
another.  Scientists working in the field of genetics are able to
understand how one generation inherits important characteristics
from the previous generation.  But we are able to understand much
more than merely why a particular family has a large number of
red-haired children. We are also able to discover whether an indi
vidual is  likely to live a long life or suffer from particular diseases.
This is call뼈 pre.찌‘ctive genetics,  and the benefits of such research
are many.

This description of the genetic code of the human being has

long been the goal of scientists. Doctors are interested in this
research  because of the possibilities it would provide for treating
long-term illnesses. The treatment of chronic diseases such as
asthma and diabet,야, for example, could be greatly improved by
early diagnosis. There is also the possibility that an understanding
of mutations in genetic sequences could be used  co  cure disease.
Some researchers believe, for example, that genetic chan흉S are
responsible for many common diseases.  Predictive genetics can 꾀so
help in situations where there is a pred때osition for illness.  In some
cases, a family history indicates a high pr。bability of developing a
disease.  Genetic mapping could indicate if that will  happen,
thereby increasing the chances of early diagnosis and treatment.

Whtie n。 one doubts the benefits of gene mapping, it is also

true that there is a dark side to all this knowledge. As with all
scientific adv:잉tees, the p。ssibility exists that knowledge would be
used to violate basic human rights.  For example, you might be
denied a job or a chance to move up in the company because your
genetic map indicates that you have a hi힘1 probability of contract
ing a certain disease. The possibility that this would be used as a
reason  not to hire an employee or promote within 바e company is
what worries people.

The insurance industry is another area where these concerns
arise.  Insurance companies provide financial security for people by
pr'。mising that they will continue to be paid in the event that they
are unable to work. There is widespread fear that employees
needing workers’compensation would be denied because of their
genetic history. This type of discrimination  is  illeg뇌, j따t as it is
illegal to reject you because of your gender or race.  However, the
dangers increased recently because of research reports published in

two  respeαed journals, Nature and Science,  which des띠be the

Many experts believe that the only way co address this risk is co

pass national laws that prohibit discrimination. Such laws would
forbid employers from  failing co promote an employee or from
dismissing an employee from  the company merely because of
genetic makeup.  It is clear char such laws would noc eliminate
generic discrimination entirely, but they would greatly reduce such

ASSESSING READING

The  classic  principles  of classroom  assessment  apply  to  your  attempts  to  assess
reading  comprehension: Be  specific  about  which  micro- or macroskill(s)  you  are
assessing; identi디 the genre of writtεn communication that is being evaluated; and
choose carefully among the range of possibilities from simply perceiving letters or
words  all  the  way  to  extensive  reading.  In  addition,  for  assessing  reading, some
attention should be given to the highly strategic nature of reading comprehension
by accounting  for  which of the  many  strategies  for  reading  ar,ε  being examined.
Finally, reading assessment implies differentiating bottom-up  from  top깅own tasks,
as well as focus on form versus focus on meaning.

In your efforts to design tests at any one or a combination of thesε levels and
categories, consider the  following  taxonomy of tasks.  These  are  not  meant to  be
εxhaustive, but rather to provide an overview of some possibilities.

1.  Perceptive reading (recognition of symbols, letters, words)

•  reading aloud
•  copying (reproduce in writing)
•  multiple-choice recognition (including true-false and fill-in-the-blank)
•  picture-cued identification

2.  Selective reading (focus on m야phology, grammar, lexicon)

•  multiple choice grammar/vocabulary tasks
•  cont않tualized multiple choice (within a short paragraph)
•  sentence-level cloze tasks
•  matching tasks
•  grammar/vocabulary editing tasks (multiple choice)
•  picture-cued tasks (Ss  choose among 양aphic represεntations)
•  gap-fil파ig tasks (e.g., sentence completion)

386

CHAPTER  20  Teaching Reading

3.  Interactive reading

•  discourse-level doze tasks (requiring knowledge of discourse)
•  reading + comprehension questions
•  short-answer responses to reading
•  discourse editing tasks (multiple choice)
•  scanrung
•  re-ordering sequences of sentences
•  responding to charts, maps, graphs, diagrams

4.  Extensive reading

•  s힘mming
•  summanzmg
•  responding to reading through short essays
•  note taking, marginal notes, highli양1ting
•  outlining

OBJECTIVES  Afte「 reading  this chapter,  you  will  be able to:

The  psycholinguist  Eric  Lenneberg  (1967)  once  noted,  in  a  discussion  of
“species-specific” human behavior, that human beings universally learn to walk
and  to  talk,  but  that  swimming  and  writing  are  culturally  specific,  learned
behaviors.  We learn to swim if there is a body of water available and usually only
if someone teaches us.  We  learn to write if we are members of a literate society,
and usually only if someone teaches us.

Just as  there are nonswimmers, poor swimmers, and excellent swimmers, so
it is for writers.  Why isn’t  everyone an excellent writer? What is it about writing
that blocks so many people, even in their own native language? Why don’t  people
learn  to  write “ naturally,”  as  they  learn  to  talk?  How  can  we  best  teach  second
language  learners  of English  how to write? What  should  we  be  tηing to  teach?
Let’s  look at these and many other related questions as  we tackle  the last  of the
“four skills.”

RESEARCH ON SECOND LANGUAGE WRITING

Trends  in  the  teaching  of writing  in  ESL  and  other  foreign  languages  have,  not
surprisingly, coincided with those of the teaching of other skills, especially listening
andspea힘ng.  You will recall from earlier chapters that as communicative language
teaching gathered momentum in the 1980s, teachers learned more and more about
how to teach fluency, not just accuracy, how to use authentic texts and contexts in
the classroom, how to focus on the purposes of linguistic communication, and how
to  capitalize  on  learners' intrinsic  motives  to  learn.  Those  same  trends  and  the
principles that under횡rded them also applied to advances in the teaching of writing
in second language contexts.

390

CHAPTER  21

1능aching Writing

391

Over the past few decades of research on teaching writing to second language
learners, a  numbεr of issues have appeared, some of which remain controversial in
spite of reams of data on second language writing.  Her,ε is  a brief look at some of
thosε issues.

1.  Composing versus writing

A simplistic view of writing would assume that written language is  simply thε
graphic rεpresentation of spoken language, and that written performance is  much
like  oral  performance,  the  only  differεnce  lying  in  graphic  instead  of  auditory
signals.  Fortunately, no one holds this  viεw today.  The process of writing requires
an  ℃ntirely  different  set  of  competencies  and  is  fundamentally  differ,εnt  from
speaking  in  ways  that  have  already  been  reviewed  in  the  last  chapter.  The
permanence  and  distanc℃  of  writing,  coupled  with  its  unique  rhεtori cal
conventions, indeed makε writing as  diff;εrent from speaking as  swimming is  from
walking.

One  major  themε in  pedagogical  research  on  writing  is  the  nature  of  the
composing  process  of  writing  (O'Brien,  2004;  Silva  &  Brice,  2004).  Written
products  are  often  the  result  of thinking,  drafting,  and  re、rising procedures  that
require  specialized skills, skills  that not  eveπ speaker develops  naturally.  Further,
studεnts exhibit a  number of different  styles  and preferences  in their composing
processes  (Chεn, 2005).  The  upshot  of the  compositional  nature  of writing  has
produced writing pedagogy that focuses students on how to gεnerate ideas, how to
organize them coherently, how to use discourse markers and rhetorical conventions
to put them cohesively into a written text, how to revise  text for clearer meaning,
how to edit text for appropriate grammaι and how to produce a final product.

2.  Process versus product

Recognition  of the  compositional  nature  of writing  has  changed  the  face  of
writing classes.  A half a  centuη ago, writing teachers wer,ε mostly concerned with
the final  product of writing: thε essay, the report, the stoη, and what that product
should “look”  like.  Compositions  were  supposed  to (a)  meet certain  standards  of
prescribed English rhetorical stylε, φ) reflect accurate grammar, and (c) be organized
in conformity with what the audience would consider to be conventional.  A good
deal of attention was placed on “modεl” compositions that students would emulate
없id on how well a student’s final product meastπed up against a list of criteria that
included  content,  organization,  vocabulary  use,  grammatical  use,  and  mechanical
considerations such as spelling and punctuation.

There is nothing inherently wrong with attention to any of the above criteria.
They  are  still  the  concern  of writing  teachers.  But  in  due  course  of tinle,  we
became better attuned to the advantage given to learners when they were seen as
creators of language, when they wεre allowed to focus on content 따id message, and
when their own individual intrinsic motives were put at the center of learning.  We
began to develop what is now termed the process approach to writing instruction.
Process approaches do most of the following  (adapted from Shih, 1986):

392

CHAPTER  21  Teaching Writing

•  focus on the process of writing that leads to the final written product;
•  help student writers to understand their own composing process;
•  help  them  to  build  repertoires  of strategies  for  prewriting,  drafting,  and

rewnting;

•  give students time to write and rewrite;
•  place central importance on the process of revision;
•  let students discover what they W없it to say as  they write;
•  give  students feedback  throughout the composing process (not just on the
final  product) as  thεy attempt to bring their expression closer and closer to
mtentton;

•  encourage feedback from both the instructor and peers;
•  include  individual  conferences  between  teacher  and  studεnt during  the

process of composition.

Perhaps  you  can  personally  appreciate  what  it  means  to  be  askεd to  write
something-say, a lεtter to an editor, an article for a nε:wsletter, a paper for a course
you’re  taking-and to  allow  thε veη process of putting ideas  down  on paper to
transform thoughts into words, to sharpen your main ideas, to give them structure
and coherent organization.  As your first draft goes through perhaps several steps of
revision, your thesis and developing ideas more and more resemble something that
you would consider a final product.  If you have done this, you have used your own
process approach to writing.

You may also know firsthand what it is like to tπ to come up with a “perfect”
fmal  product  without  thε above  process.  You  may  have  experiεneed “writer's
cramp” (mental blocks) that severely hampered any progress.  You  may have felt  a
certain level of anxiety building within you as you felt  the pressur1ε to write an in
class  essay  that  would  be  judged  by  the  teacher,  graded, and  returned  with  no
chance  for  your  future  revision.  The  process  approach  is  an  attempt  to  take
advantage of the nature of the written code (unlike conversation, it can be plann℃d
and given an unlimited number of revisions before its “ release”) to give students a
chance to think as  they write.  Another way of putting it is that writing is  indeεd a
tbiηking process.

Over three decades ago, Peter Elbow (1973) expressed the concept of process
writing in w용ing teachers to discard the notion that “first  you figure  out what you
want to say ... don’t  start writing t피 you do” (p. 14).  Elbow and m없iy experts since
then have noted that “this idea of writing is backwards” (Elbow, 1973,p.15).  Instead,
process approaches feature  the following  practices (adapted from Hedgcock, 2005,
pp. 604-605):

•  allowing students to discover their own voice (see #7 below)
•  freewri따ig,journa파ig, and fluency activities
•  tasks that engage learners in meaningful writing
•  giving writers a sense of audience and authentic tasks

」

CHAPTER  21  Teaching  Writing

393

•  encouraging invention, prewriting, and rεvision strategiεs
•  providing formative feedback through conferεncing

The  current  emphasis  on  procεss writing  must  of  coursε bε seεn in  the
perspectiv,ε of history and future  dεvεlo pmεnts (Casanave, 2004; Hedgcock, 2005;
0’ Briεn, 2004;  Silva  &  Lεki, 2004).  Som℃ rεsεarch (Atkinson,  2003)  has  already
claimεd that wε ar'ε  now in  a “ post-process” εra, whilε othεrs (Matsuda, 2003)  arε
morε circumspect by noting  that  thε concεpt of post-procεss, in fact,  only  rεjεcts
“ the dominance of process at  the  expεnse of othεr aspεcts of writing and writing
instruction" (Matsuda, 2003, pp. 78-79).  As  in most  languagε-teaching approaches,
it is quite possible for you to go to an extrεmε in emphasizing procεss to the εxtent
that  the  final  product  diminishεs in  importance.  Try  not  to  lεI this  happen! Thε
product is, aft℃r all, the ultimate goal; it is  thε rεason that we go through thε procεSS
of prεwriting, drafting,  revising,  and  editing.  Without  that  final  product  firmly  in
viεw, we could quite simply drown oursεlv'εs in a sea of revisions ‘  Proc℃ss is not thε
εnd; it is  thε means to thε εnd.

3.  Contrastive rhetoric

Robert Kaplan's (1966) article on contrastiv,ε rhεtoric has been thε subject of
much discussion and debate εver since.  Kaplan’S thesis was that differεntlanguagεs
(and their cultures) have diffiεrεnt patterns of writt℃n discourse.  English discoursε,
according to Kaplan (p. 14), was schematically describεd as proceeding in a straight
line,  Semitic  writing  in  a  zigzag  formation,  Oriental  [sic]  written  discourse  in  a
spiraling line, and so forth (seε Figurε 21.1).

Th℃ point  of Kaplan’s  conclusions  about  how  we  write  was, of coursε, that
lεarnεrs of English bring with them certain predispositions, which comε from thεir
nativ,ε  languagεs,  about  how  to  organize  their  writing.  If  English  writεrs  gεt
“ straight” to thε point, and Chinεsε writ℃rs “spiral” around the point, th℃n a Chinese
speakεr who is learning English will  encountεr somε difficulty in learning to write
English  discoursζ

Teaching  Writing

There were problems with Kaplan’s study (Casanave, 2004; Connor, 2002), some
of  which  Kaplan  has  rεcently  respondεd to  (Kaplan,  2005).  His  diagrams  and
conclusions  werε simplistic  and  overgεneralized.  Simplistic, because  hε based  his
conclusions  about  En밍ish discourse  on style  manuals  rathεr than using  data from
actual  writing  in  En행sh.  Overgeneralized,  because  onε  cannot  concludε  that
English  writεrs  consistently  us℃ a  “straight-line”  attack  on  a  thesis  and  certainly
cannot make any  genεralization that  appliεs, for  ε:xample, to  all  Oriental languages.
Furthεrmore, without a native-speaking En밍ish control group, one cannot dεtεrmine
if th℃ “ difficulty” of his sample data is  simply th℃ difficulty any inexperiεnced writer
might encounter in learning to write.

Nεvertheless, there  was  and  st퍼 is  a  ring  of truth  to  Kaplan’S claims,  as  both
Kaplan (2005) himself and Connor (2002) have noted.  In fact, Connor has donε much
“to move contrastive rhetoric out of the quagmirε in which it had been lodg℃d for so
long" (Casanave, 2004, p. 41).  No  onε can deny thε effect of one’s  nativ,℃ cultur'ε, or
onε’s predispositions  that  arε thε product  of perhaps  years  of schooling,  reading,
writing,  thinking,  asserting,  arguing,  and  defending.  In  our  curriεnt  paradigm  of
attεnding carεfully to schemata and scripts, native  languagε pattεms of thinking and
writing  simply cannot  be  ru1εd out.  A balanced position on this  issuε, then, would
uph이d thε importancε of your  carefully  attending  to  the  rhetorical  first  languagε
interfer'εncε that may bε at play in your studεnts’ writing.  But rather than holding a
dogmatic or predictiv1ε viεw (that certain writers will experience φfficulty becausε of
their native  language), you  would  bε mor'ε prudεnt to adopt a “weak” position (see
PLLT,  Chapter 9) in which you would considεr a  studεnt’S cultural/literary schemata
as only one possiblε source of difficulty.

In  rεcent years  new  1℃Sεarch studies  have  appeared  that  tacklε the  issue  of
contrastive rhεtoric (Casanav1℃, 200솟 Connor, 2002).  According to Connor, a theory
of contrastiv℃ rhetoric  is  influencεd by  more  than  first  languag,ε pattεrns; factors
such as  linguistic  relativity,  thεory of rhetoric, text linguistics, discoursε types and
gεIlf1εs, literacy,  and  translation  all  contributε toward  a  comprehεnsiv1ε thεory of
contrastive  rhetoric.  Onε  important  conclusion  from  this  rεnew℃d  wav,ε  of
I℃search is  the  significance  of valuing  studεnts’  native-language-related  rhetorical
traditions, and of guiding them through a process of understanding those schemata
while  not attempting  to  eradicate  them.  That  self understanding  on  the  part  of
students  may  then  lend  itsεlf to  a  more  effective  appr,εciation and  use  of English
rhetorical conventions.

4.  Differences between Ll and L2 writing

In the  1970s, rεsεarch on second language writing was strongly influεnced by
previous  research  on  native  language  writing.  Assumptions  were  made  that  thε
composing  processes  in  both  instances  were  similar  if  not  identical.  But  it  is
imperativ1ε  for  teachers  to  undεrstand that  thεrε are  in  fact  many  diffiεrences
betwεen the two, as Tony Silva (1993) so clεarly demonstrated in a  comprehensiv'ε
survey of L2  writing.  Silva  found  that  L2  writers  did  lεss planning, and  that  they
wer,ε lεss fluent  (used  fewer  words),  less  accurate  (made  more  errors), and  lεSS

1농aching Writing

395

effective in stating goals and organizing material.  Differences in using appropriate
grammatical and rhεtorical conv,εntions and lεxical variety were also found, among
other features.

π1ε questions that are currently being addressed in this area (Hedgcock, 2005)
cεnter on  dif£εrεncεs  between  L1  and  L2  writing  and  sorting  out  appropriate
approachεs to  L2  writing.  Some  pedagogical  implications  of these  quεstions are
that (a) it is important to determine appropriate approaches to writing instruction
for L2  writers in different contexts, (b) writing teachers need to bε εquipped to deal
ef£εctiv1εly with the sociocultural and linguistic  differences of L2  studεnts, and (c)
the  assessment  of  L2  writing  may  need  to  take  into  account  the  fundamental
differencεs bεtweεn most L 1 and L2  writing.

5.  Authenticity

Another issue in the tεaching of writing surrounds thε quεstion of how much
of our classroom writing is “ real” writing (Casanave, 2004; Hεdgcock, 2005; Silva  &
Brice, 2004).  That is, how authentic are the classroom writing ε:xercises that wε ask
students to perform? One could address this question by asking how much writing
thε average college-educatεd personinWεstern society actually does, and what kind
of writing.  I would venture to  say  veη little, and that little  amounts  to filling  out
forms, writing telephone messages, e-mailing, and occasionally dashing off a letter or
postcard.  In the era of electronic communication (video, phone, computer, etc.) we
are less and less called upon to compose.  I was recently consultεd by a friend who
is studying to be certified as a realtor.  Part of his certification examination involved
a simple one- or two-page written essay.  The prospect frightened him!

So,  why  do  we  want  students  to  write?  In  school,  writing  is  a  way  of life.
Without  some  ability  to  express  yoursεlf in  writing,  you  don’t  pass  the  coursε.
Across the agε levels from elementary school through university graduate coursεS,
we  write  in  order  to  succeed  in  mastering  thε subject  matter.  In  En밍ish for
Academic Purposes (EAP), writing ranges from short phrases (as in fill-in-the-blank
tests), to brief paragraphs (as in essay question exercisεs and tests), to brief reports
of  many  different  kinds,  to  a  h피-length  research  paper.  In  vocational-technical
En밍ish  (where  students  are  studying  En밍ish  in  connection  with  a  trade  or
occupation), students  need to  fill  out forms,  write  simple  messages, write  ceπain
conventional reports (for g없nple, a bid on a contract, an inspection rεport), and at
the  most "creative”  end  of the  continuum, write  a  brief business  lεtter.  In  adult
education and survival  English  classes, filling  out simple forms  and questionnaires
may  be  as  sophisticated  as  students'  needs  get.  This  leaves  EAP  as  the  major
consumer  of  writing  techniques,  especially  writing  techniquεs  that  concern
themselves  with  the  composing  process:  dev1εlopment of ideas,  argument,  lo횡c,
cause and effect, etc., as Paltridge (2004) aptly describes in a survey of teaching EAP.
Another way to look at the authentic따 issue in classroom writing is to 띠앞inguish
between real wri삼ng and display writing.  Real writing, as exp때ned by Ann Raimes
(1991), is  writing  when  the  reader doesn’t  know  the  answer  and  genuinely  wants
information.  In many academic/school contexts, however, if the instructor is the sole

396

[ HAPTER 2 7  Teaching  Writing

readεr, writing is prim따ily for the display of a studεnt’s knowledge.  Writtεn εxercises,
short-없lSWεr essays, 따id other writing in tεst situations are instances of display writing.
Should  we  as  tεachεrs incorporate  morε rεal writing  in  our  classrooms?  In
som℃ ways,  yεs.  If ESL  coursεs strive  to  be  more  content-based,  theme-basεd, or
task-based, studεnts arε mor℃ likεly to bε given thε opportunity to convey gεnuinε
information  on  topics  of  intrinsic  inter℃st.  But  display  writing  is  not  totally
unjustifiεd.  Writing to display onε’s kn owlεdg<ε is a fact of life in the classroom, and
by getting your students to perform well in display writing εxεrcisεs, thεy can learn
skills that will hεIp thεm to succeed in mπh℃r a cad℃mic pursuits.

6_  Responding to student writing

The gradual recognition of writing as a process of thinking and composing was
a  natural  by-product  of  CLT.  With  its  emphasis  on  learnεr-cεntered instruction,
student-stud℃nt  nεgotiation,  and  strategiεs-based  instruction  that  valuεs  the
variability of lεarnεrs’ pathways to success, CLT  is an appropriatε locus for procεSS
writing.  As  students  ar,ε encouraged (in  reading)  to  bring  thεir own  schemata  to
bear on understanding tεxts, and in writing to  dev1εlop their own ideas, offer their
own critical  analysis, and find  their own voice (see #7 below), the  role of teacher
must be one of facilitator and coach, not an authoritativε director and arbiter.

This facilitative  role of thε writing teacher has inspired resεarch on the rolε of
the teacher as a rεsponder to students' writing (Casanave, 2004; Fεrris & Hedgcock,
2005;  H℃dgcock, 2005;  Silva  &  Brice,  2004).  As  a  facilitator,  the  teacher  offers
guidance in hεlping students to engage in the thinking process of composing but,
in a spirit of respect for  studεnt opinion, must not imposε his or her own thoughts
on student writing.  Howεver, as Joy Reid (1994, p. 273) pointed out, our pεnchant
for laissez-faire  approachεs to  commεnting on student writing may have gone too
far.  “ Instead  of entering  the  conversation  of composing  and  drafting,  instead  of
helping  students  nεgotiate  bεtween  thεir  interests  and  purposes  and  thε
experiences and intεntions of their academic readers, many tεachers have rεtr'εatεd
into a hands-off approach to student writing.” Short of “ appropriating” student text,
we can offer usεful feedback that rεspεcts students' valuεs and bεIiεfs.  Dana Fεrris
(1997)  offered  useful  guidelines  for  making  teacher  comrnentaπ more  effective.
For ex없nple, Ferris found that when teachers (a) requεsted spσific information and
(b)  made  summaη comments  on  grammar,  more  substantive  student  rεvisions
ensued than when teachers (a) posed questions and (b) made positive comments.
We are still exploring ways to offer optimal feedback to student writing.

7.  Voice and identity

Weaving in and out of several of the above topics, especially the last one, is thε
issue of how to pr'εserve the cultural and social identities of students but at the samε
time to  tεach English language writing conventions.  This  issuε is  especially acute
in  the  case  of EAP  writing  pr앵rams where  a  major goal  is  for  students  to  writε
acceptable academic prose in their respective subject-matter fields (Paltridge, 2004).
In  othεr writing  courses,  how,εver,  the  problεm is  also  significant  as  course

CHAPTER  21  Teaching  Writing

397

d℃signεrs  and  instructors  must  attεnd  to  “the  socially  and  politically  situated
contexts of writing and how these contexts influence both how writing gets done
and thε εnd products of writing" (Casanavε, 2004, p. 84).  In somε ways thε issuε is
onε of authenticiη, mεntionεd above, and in othεr ways it has ovεrt onεs of critical
pεdagogy (s℃ε Cha ptεr 26).  Recent rεsεarch indicates that some progress is  bεing
madε toward  focusing  students  on  writing  for  meaningful  purposes  within  thεir
own sociopolitical contεxts (Atkinson, 2003; Casanave, 2003), and not just creating
writing assignmεnts that will force  cεrtain rhεtorical competenciεs.

Th℃se Sεvεn categories  comprisε just  a  few  of the  many  intriguing  current
questions  in  teaching  writing.  By  acquainting yourself with  thεse issues, you will

writing teacher.

TYPES OF WRITTEN LANGUAGE

In  Chapter  20,  on  pagεs 362-363, wεre some  30-odd  types  of written  language
“ forms. ” As  you considεr an ESL class that you might be teaching, how many of thεsε
typ℃S of writing will your students be likely to produce thεmselv1εs? Those types that
they  will  indeed  need,  ℃ithεr for  further  study  of  English  or  for  their  ultimatε
academic/vocational goals ‘ should then become thε prime focus  of “ I℃al ” writing in
your classroom.

CHARACTERISTICS OF WRITTEN LANGUAGE: A W띤ITER’S VIEW

In  Chapt℃r 20, some characteristics of written  language  from  the  perspective of a
readεr werε set forth.  Let’s  revisit those from a  writεr’s viewpoint.

1.  Permanence

Oncε somεthing is  writtεn down and dεlivered in its final form to its  intendεd
audience, the  writεr abdicatεs a  certain  powεr: the  power to  emεnd, to  clarify, to
withdraw.  That  prospεct  is  the  single  most  significant  contributor  to  making
writing  a  scary  opεration!  Student  writers  oftεn feel  that  thε act  of releasing  a
written work  to  an instructor is  not unlike  putting  thεmsεlves in front  of a  firing
squad.  Thεrefore, whatever you  can do  as  a  teacher, guide, and facilitator  to  help
your studεnts to revise and refinε their work before final  submission will hεlp givε
th℃m confidencε in their work.

2.  Production time

The good news is  that, given  appropriatε strεtchεs of tim℃, a writer can  indeεd
become  a  “ good”  writer  by  developing  efficient  processes  for  achieving  thε final
product.  The  bad  nεws is  야iat many educational  conte었S demand  student  writing
within timε limits, or“writing for display” as noted in thε prεvious section (exaniination

1겉'aching Writing

wri따ig, for 앓따nple).  So, one of your go외s, especially if you 았C teaching in an EAP
context, would be to train yo따 students to make the best possible u똥 of such time
limitations.  πtis may mean sa띠ficing some process time, but with sufficiεnttraining
in  process  writing,  combined  with  practice  in  display  writing,  you  can  help  your
students dεal with time limitations.

3.  Distance

One of the thorniest problems writers face  is anticipating thεir audience.  That
anticipation  ranges  from  general  audience  characteristics  to  how  specific  words,
phrases, sentencεs, and paragraphs w피 bε intεrprεted.  The distance factor requires
what might be termed “cognitive” empathy, in that good writers can “read” their own
writing from the perspective of the mind of the targeted audience.  Writers need to
be able to predict the audience’s general knowledge, cultural and literary schemata,
specific subject-matter knowledge, and veπ important, how their choice of language
will be interpreted.

4.  Orthography

Eve11πhing from  simple  greetings  to  extremely  complex  ideas  is  captured
through  thε  manipulation  of  a  few  dozen  lettεrs  and  othεr written  symbols.
Sometimes we  takε for granted the mastering of the mεchanics of English writing
by our students.  If students are nonliterate in the native language, you must begin
at  the  veη beginning  with  fundamentals  of  reading  and  writing.  For  literate
students, if their native language system is  not alphabetic, new symbols have to be
produced by hands  that may have  bεcome accustomed to another system.  If the
native  language has a different phoneme-graphεme system (most do!), then some
attention is due here.

5.  Complexity

In  thε previous  chapter, the  complexity of written-as opposed to  spoken
language was illustrated.  Writers must learn how to remove redundancy (which may
not jibe  with their first  language  rhetorical  tradition), how to  combine  sentences,
how to  make  references  to  other elements  in  a  text, how to  create  syntactic  and
lexical variety, and much more.

6.  Vocabulary

As  was  noted  in  Chapter  20, written  language  places  a  heavier  demand  on
vocabul따y use  than  does  speaking.  Good writers will  learn to  take  advantage  of
the richness of English vocab버따y.

7.  Form떼ity

Whether a  student is  filling  out a  questionnaire or writing a  full-blown  essay,
the conventions of each form must be followed.  For ESL students, the most difficult
and complex conventions occur in academic writing where students have to learn
how to describe, expl없n, compare, contrast, illustrate, defend, criticize, and argue.

TYPES OF CLASSROOM WRITING PERFORMANCE

Whilε various genr℃S of written texts abound, classroom writing performancε is, by
comparison,  limitεd.  Consider  thε following  fiv,ε  major  categoriεs of  classroom
writing pεrformancε;

1.  Imitative, or writing down

At the beginning level of learning to write, students will simply “writε down"
En밍ish lεttεrs, words, and  possibly sentencεs in order to learn the conventions of
the  orthographic  code.  Somε forms  of dictation fall  into  this  catεgory, althou~강1
dictations  can  sεfV1ε to  teach and test  higher-ordεr processing as well.  Dictations
typically involve  thε following steps:

[ HAPTER 21  Teaching  Writing

a.  Tεacher rεads a short paragraph once or twice at normal speεd.
b.  Teacher reads thε paragraph in shoπ phrase units of thrεε or four words

εach,and εach unit is  followed  by a pause.

c.  During the pausε, students write εxactly what thεy hεar.
d.  Teacher then rεads thε who!ε paragraph once more at normal speed so

studεnts can chεck their writing.

ε.  Scoring of studεnts’ written work can utilizε a  numbεr of rubrics for
assigning points.  Usually  spεHing and punctuation errors arε not
considεrεd as  sεvere as grammatical εrrors.

2.  Intensive, or controlled

Writing  is  sometimes  usεd as  a  production  mode  for  learning,  rεinforcing, or
testing grammatical concepts.  This intεnsiv,ε writing typically appears in controlled,
writtεn grammar  exεrcises.  This  type  of  writing  doεs not  allow  much,  if any,
crεativity on the part of the writεr.

A common form of controlled writing is  to prεsent a paragraph to studεnts in
which they hav,ε to alter a giv1εn structurε throughout.  So, for εxamplε, thεy may bε
askεd to change all  prεsεnt tense vεrbs to  past tense; in such a  case, students may
n℃℃d to alter other tim℃ rεfer·εncεs in  thε paragraph.

Guided  writing  loosεns  the  tεacher's  control  but  still  offers  a  sεriεs  of
stimulators.  For ℃xample, thε teacher might get studεnts to tell a story just viewed
on  avidεotapε by  asking  thεm a  series  of quεstions: Whεrε does  thε story  takε
placε? Dεscribe the principal character.  What doεs hε say to thε woman in thε car?
Yεt another form  of controllεd writing  is  a  dicto-comp.  Hεrε, a  paragraph  is
rεad at  normal  speed, usually  two  or threε timεs; then the  teachεr asks  students  to
rεwrite the paragraph to the bεst of th℃ir recollεction of the reading.  In on℃ of sεvεral
variations of thε dicto-comp tζchniquε, the tεacher,aftεr reading thε passag,ε, puts kεy
words from  thε paragraph, in sequence, on the chalkboard as  cues for  the  studεnts.

3.  Self-writing

A significant proportion of classroom writing may be devotεd to sεif-writing, or
writing with only the self in mind as an audiencε.  The most salient instance of this
catεgory in classrooms is  notε taking, where students takε notεs during a l℃cture for
the purpose of latεr recall.  Other note taking may be don℃ in the margins of books

Diary  or  journal  writing  also  falls  into  this  catεgory.  Howεver, in  many
circumstancεs a  dialogue journal, in which a  student rεcords thoughts, feelings,
