CHAPTER  21  Teaching  Writing

4.  Display writing

It was  notεd earliεr that writing within thε school curricular context is  a way
of life.  For  all  languag℃ students, short-answer  exercisεs, essay  examinations, and
εven research  reports will  involve  an  elemεnt of display.  For academically bound
ESL students, one of the academic skills that they need to master is a wholε array of
display writing tεchniques.

5.  Real writing

While  virtually  εvery classroom  writing  task  will  have  an  element  of display
writing  in  it,  somε classroom  writing  aims  at  the  gεnuine  communication  of
mεssagεs to an audiencε in need of thosε messages.  The two categoriεs of rεal and
display  writing  are  actually  two  εnds of a  continuum,  and  in  between  the  two
ε:xtremεs li℃S somε combination of display  and  real  writing.  Three  subcatεgories
illustratε how rεality can bε injected:

a.  Academic.  Thε Language  Experiεnce Approach giv1εs groups of students

opportunities to convey genuine information to εach other.  Content-based
instruction encourag℃S the εxchange of usεful information, and somε of
this learning usεs the writtεn word.  Group problem-solving  tasks,
especially thosε that relate to current issuεs and other personally relevant
topics, may hav'ε a writing component in which information is gεnuinely
sought and convεyεd.  Peer-editing work adds to what would otherwise b℃
an audiεncε of one (the instructor) and provides real writing opportunity.
In certain ESP  and EAP  courses, students may exchange new information
with each other and with th℃ instructor.

b.  VocationaVtechnical.  Quite a variety of real writing can take placε in

classes of studεnts studying English for  advancεment in thεir occupation.
Rεal lεtters can bε writtεn; gεnuinε dir'εctions for  somε operation or
assεmbly might be given; and actual forms can bε fillεd out.  These
possibilities are εven greatεr in what has come to be callεd “ English in thε
Workplace;’ whεre ESL  is offered within companies and corporations.

c.  Personal.  In virtually any ESL  class, diaries, letters, postcards, notes,

pεrsonal messages, and other informal writing can take place, εspecially
within thε context of an intεractive classroom.  Whilε cεrtain tasks may be
somεwhat contrived, nεv℃rtheless the genuine ε:xchange of information
can happεn.

PRINCIPLES FOR TEACHING W메ITING SKILI.S

Out  of all  of  these  charactεristics  of  the  written  word,  along  with  micro- and
macroskills and research issues, a number of specific principles for teaching writing
skills εmerge.

CHAPTER  21

7같·aching Writing

403

1.  Incorporate practices of “good”  writers.

This first  guideline is sweeping.  But as you contemplate devising a technique
that has a writing goal in it, consider the various things that efficient writεrs do, and
sεc if your techniquε includes some of these practices.  For example, good writers

•  focus on a go외 or main idea in writing,
•  perceptively gauge their audience,
•  spend somε time (but not too much!) planning to write,
•  easily let their first ideas flow onto thε paper,
•  follow a general organizational plan as they write,
•  solicit and utilize feedback on their writing,
•  are not wedded to certain surface structures,
•  revise their work willingly and efficiently,
•  patiently make as  many revisions as needed.

2.  Balance process and product.

Because  writing  is  a  composing  process  and  usually  requirεs multiple  drafts
before  an  εftiεctive product  is  created, make  sure  that  studεnts are  carefully  led
through  appropriate  stages  in  the  process  of composing.  This  includes  careful
attention to your own role as a guide and as a responder (see #8).  At the same time,
don’t  get  so caught up in  the stages leading  up to the final  product that you lose
sight of the ultimate attainment: a clear, articulate, well-organized, effective piece of
writing.  Make sure students see that everything leading up to this final creation was
worth the effort.

3.  Account for culturaVliterary backgrounds.

Make sure that your techniques do not assume that your students know En밍ish
rhetorical  conventions.  If there  are  some  apparent  contrasts  between  students'
native  traditions  and  those  that  you  are  tηing to  teach,  tη to  help  students  to
understand  what  it  is,  exactly, that  they are  accustomed  to  and  then, by degrees,
bring them to thε use of acceptable En밍ish rhetoric.

4.  Connect reading and writing.

Clearly, students learn to  write in part by carefully observing what is  already
written.  That is, they learn by observing, or reading, the written word.  By reading
and studying a variety of relevant types of text, students can gain important insi항ltS
both about how they should write and about subject matter that may become the
topic of their writing.

5.  Provide as much authentic writing as possible.

Whether writing is real writing or for displaκ it can st피 be authentic in that the
purposes for writing are clear to the students, the audience is specified overtly, 따1d
there  is  at  lεast  some  intent  to  convey  meaning.  Sharing  writing  with  other
students in the class is  one way to add authenticity.  Publishing a class newsletter,

404

CHAPTER  21  Teaching Writing

writing  lettεrs to  peoplε outside  of class, writing  a  script  for  a  skit  or  dramatic
presentation, writing  a  resume, writing  advertisεments-all these  can  bε sεεn as
au thεntic writing.

6.  Frame  your techniques  in  terms  of prewriting,  drafting,  and  revising
stages.

Process writing  approachεs Iεnd to be framed in thrεε stages of writing.  Thε
prewriting  stage  encouragεs  thε  generation  of  ideas,  which  can  happen  in
numerous ways:

•  r℃a ding (εxtensively) a  passagε
•  skimming and/or scanning a  passagε
•  conducting some outside rεsεarch
•  brainstorming (see below)
•  listing (in writing-individually)
•  dustεring  (bεgin  with  a  kεy word,  thεn  add  other  words,  using  frεe

association)

•  discussing a  topic or question
•  instructor-initiated quεstions and probεs
•  fr,εεwriting (see below)

Examples of brainstorming and freewriting, from  Challeηges (Brown, Cohen, &
0 ’Day, 1991), ar'ε shown in Figure 21. 3.

The drafting and revising stages arε thε cor1ε ofprocεss writing.  In traditional
approaches  to  writing  instruction,  stud℃nts  eithεr  are  givεn  timed  in-class
compositions  to  write  from  start  to  finish  within a  class  hour, or they  ar,ε given a
homεwork writing assignmεnt.  The first option giv1℃S no opportuniη for systεma tic
drafting, and thε second assumεs that if students did any drafting at all, they would
simply  have  to  lεarn thε tricks  of the  trade  on  th℃ir own.  In a  procεss approach,
drafting  is  viewed  as  an  important  and  complex  sεt of stratεgiεs, the  masteπ of
which takes time, patience, and trainεd instruction.

Sev1εral stratεgiεs and skills apply to  thε drafting/revising procεss in writing:

•  getting started (adapting the fo℃εwriting t℃chnique)
•  “optimal” monitoring of one’S  writing (without prematur,ε εditing and div'εrted

attention to wording, grammar, etc.)

•  peer-reviewing for content (accεptin!νusing classmatεs’ comments)
•  using the instructor's fεεdback
•  editing for grammatic때 errors
•  “read-aloud” technique (in sm따l groups or pairs, studεnts read their almost- mal

drafts to each other for a fmal check on errors, flow of ideas, etc.)

•  proofoεa ding

CHAPTER  21  Teaching  Writing

405

GENERATING  IDEAS

•  Brainstorming

Let’s think about the  future for a moment.  Let's  focus  our attention on  how it might affect your
present or future job.  Have you  thought about the changes  that might occur in your field? To  help
you  think about this  question,  you  are going to make two lists of ideas  concerning changes  in
your field O「 in  the field  you  plan to enter.

DIRECTIONS:  Use  your  knowledge and  imagination to follow these  steps.

1.  Prepare two sheets  of paper with the following:

a.  What changes  have occurred  in  my field  in  the  last twenty years?

Your  field-today’s date

b.  What changes  do I expect to occur in  my field  in the next twenty years?

Your field-the date twenty years  from  now

2.  As  quickly as  possible,  think of as  many  ideas  as  you  can  to answer the question  on  sheet a.
a.  Take  between  five and  ten  minutes to  list every  idea  that comes  to your mind.
b.  Do not evaluate your  ideas.  That will come later.

3.  When you  have written down everything you can  think of,  go over the list to evaluate what you

have written.  Cross  out the ideas that don’t fit.
4.  Repeat this  process  (steps  2 and  3)  for sheet b.

This  process,  called  brainstorming,  is  a  useful  technique  in  writing  because  it permits  you  to
approach  a topic  with  an  open  mind.  Because  you  do  not judge your  ideas  as  they  emerge,  you
free  yourself to  come  up  with  ideas  that you  might not even  know you  had.  Brainstorming  is  one
of several  different  ways  to  begin  writing.  In  the  following  pages,  we  will  introduce  some  other
methods that will  help you  to explore ideas  that you  might want to write about.

•  Working in a  Group

In the preceding exercise you  worked  individually, using brainstorming to establish your own ideas,
to follow your own train  of thought.  Another effective way  to  generate  ideas  is  to work in  a small
group where you  share your brainstormed  ideas with the rest of the group members.  By doing this,
each  of you  will have an  opportunity to further expand your own ideas.

DIRECTIONS:  Form  a small  group (three to five people).  Use the following guidelines for your group

discussion.

1.  Take  turns  reading your lists of changes  in your field to each  other.
2.  Compare your classmates'  lists to yours,  looking for similarities and  differences.

a.  Mark the  changes  on  your  list that are similar.
b.  Add  to your list new ideas of changes  that apply to your field.

3.  As  a group,  select three  changes  that applied to the fields of each  group member.  If you  have

time,  you  can  discuss  these three  ideas.

(Continued)

4.  Choose a  reporter from  your group to  share your  three changes with  the  rest of the class.

been  eliminated  by  the  student  in  the  last  step  of the  brainstorming exercise  because they  did  not
fit.)  The  changes  that were similar  in  each  list  have  been  labeled.

You  have  just  begun  to  explore  the  question  of  changes  in  your  field.  Some  of  your  ideas  will
interest  you  more  than  others.  Now  you  will  have  an  opportunity to  develop  your  thinking  about
one of these  ideas.

DIRECTIONS:  Follow these steps to generate further  ideas on  this  topic.

1.  From  your lists of changes,  choose one  idea that  interested you.
2.  Write that idea at the top of a  clean sheet of paper.
3.  For ten  minutes, write about this topic without stopping.  This  means that you should be writing

something constantly.

a.  Write down  everything that comes to  your  mind.
b.  Do  not judge your  ideas.
c.  Do  not worry  about your spelling and grammar.
d.

If  you  run  out of things to say,  continue writing whatever comes to your  mind.

This  process is  called freewriting.  It  is  designed to  help you free  ideas that you  might not realize
that you  have.  An  important aspect of freewriting  is  that you  write without being concerned about
spelling, punctuation, or grammar.  Of course, these elements of writing are important,  but students'
concern  about them  can  sometimes  inhibit the free  flow  of their  ideas.  Freewriting  is  a  technique
to generate  ideas;  it  should  be used  as  a  beginning,  as  an  initial  exploration  of the  ideas  that you
have about a topic.

You  can  use your freewriting  to  help  you  get started  with  related  tasks.  In  fact,  you  might  want
to  refer  to this  freewriting  when  you  are  doing other writing tasks  later  in  this  unit.  까ierefore, you
should put this and all  other freewriting  that you  do into a  notebook that you  can  refer to when  you  a「e
generating ideas for  future  assignments.

LESSON 3

COMPOSING

ON YOUR  OWN

In  this  unit  you  have  read  about the  issues  surrounding the predicted  population explosion.  You
have  also worked  with  important writing  techniques  such  as  showing and  using  facts  and  statistics.
Let's  now try  to  apply  what you  have  learned  to  the writing  process.

THE  FIRST  DRAFT

Choosing a  Topic

DIRECTIONS:  Choose one of the following  topics to  write about in  a  paragraph.
A.  Explain  the  information  introduced  in  the following  bar graph.
B.  In  the final  paragraphs of the article “The World’s Urban  Explosion,'’ the author 「aises the question
of what the effects of the population explosion  might be in  the future.  Imagine your city,  town,  or
village  in  the  year  2025.  Imagine  that  the  population  predictions did,  in  fact,  come true.  Place
yourself in  the scene,  and  describe what you  see.

Note:  Notice how different these topics  are from  one another.  The first  topic asks you  to write an
explanation  which  analyzes  a graph.  The  second  topic  asks  for  a  description.  Think  about the pos
sible purposes of each topic.  How  do you  think these purposes will  affect the tone of each  piece?

populations in billions

Generating Ideas

First,  we  need  to  find  ways  to  unlock the  hidden  ideas  we  have  in  our  minds.  In  this  unit
you  have  learned  to  use  brainstorming,  freewriting,  and  looping.  Try  these  techniques  in  any
combination  that  works  for  you.  Reading  also  helps  to  generate  ideas.  As  you  write,  keep  in
mind the  information you  learned  about this topic  in  the  readings.

[ HAPTER 21  Teaching Writing

409

Writing the First  Ora야

After  exploring  your  ideas,  put  them  into  paragraph  form,  keeping  in  mind  how showing and
using facts  and  statistics  makes  writing  powerful  and  convincing.  Our task  here  is  to discover  how
we  can  best  express  our  ideas  in  the  clearest  manne 「 possible  so  that  our  readers  will  receive  the
same  message,  with  the  same  impact,  that we intended.

Peer-editing

What follows  is  an  element of the  writing pro[ ess that  is  especially  important:  sharing  what we
have  written  with  others,  our  readers,  to  see  if we  have  been  successful  in  conveying  our  intended
meaning‘  This  step  can  be  a  fascinating  adventure.  We  step  out of our own  selves,  to  see  what we
have  created  through  the  eyes  of others,  to  discover the  impact  of our  words  on  the  thoughts  of our
「eaders, so  that we can  then  use  the  information  to  improve what we have written.  We call  this peer
editing.  Peer-editing  is  a true  sharing  process.  Not only do  you  get  feedback  from  your classmates,
but you  also give feedback to them.  It is a two-way street.  You  learn  to be a better writer and a better
reader.  In  the  following  exercise  you  will  work  with  several  classmates,  taking  the  roles  of  both
reader and  writer‘

DIRECTIONS:  Work with  four  other classmates  who chose to  write on  the  same  topi [ as  you  did.

1.  Discuss  the  idea-generating techniques  that you  each  used  to W「 ite this  composition.

, 2.  Read  each  other's  papers  silentlν and an‘wer the following questions  for  each  paper:

a.  What do you  like the  most about the W「 iting?
b.  What is  the main  idea?
c.  Who is  the audience,  and  what is  the purpose?
d  What convincing details does  the  writer  use?
e.  Where could the writer add  details to  make the piece more convincing?
f.  What areas  in  the writing seem  unclear?
g.  How could  the  writer make the piece clearer?

3.  Now,  for  each  paper,  compare  your  notes  on  the  questions  to  help  the  writer  think  of ways  to

improve the  piece.

Revising

You  have  gotten  feedback  about your  composition  from  several  classmates.  Now you  can  use
what you  learned  about you 「 writing to  improve it,  to  make  it clearer and  more convincing.  Writers
call  this  step  of the  process  revising.  All  good  writers  go  through  several  steps  of revision  because
they  want  to  make  their  writing  the  best  it  can  be.  At  this  point  they  reconsider  what  they  have
written,  get feedback  from others,  and then  make  changes.

([ontinued)

410

CHAPTER  21  Teaching  Writing

Review  your  notes  from  your  peer-editing  session.  Think  about  the  comments  made  by  your  peer
readers;  in  partic비 ar,  comments  they  agreed  on.  If you  agree  with  them,  you  can  revise the  piece.
Remember,  however,  that you  are the final  judge as  to what you  want to include or eliminate in your
writing.

Make corrections  directly on  your first  draft.  Do not be  afraid to  mark  up this  paper.  You  can
scratch out unnecessary or irrelevant information, squeeze ideas that you want to add into the margin,
and  even  cut  up  and  repaste  your  paper to  change  the  order or make  additions.  You  might be  sur
prised to see  the  revising process of professional  writers.  Their drafts will often be illegible to anyone
but themselves!

THE SECOND  DRAFT

Writing the Second  Dra텀 and  Proofreading

Once you  have made the necessary changes  in your paper,  you can  rewrite it legibly.  As  you are
rewriting,  you  may  think of more changes  that  you  would  like to  make.  Do not hesitate  to  continue
revising  during this  step.  Writing  takes  time  and  a  lot of thought,  so  take  advantage  of this  stage  to
keep  improving what you  have already done.  After you have rewritten your paper,  go over it carefully
to  see  if  the  language  sounds  correct  and  if  your  message  seems  complete  and  understandable.
Finally,  submit your paper to your teacher.

Using Your Teacher's  Feedback

When  your paper  is  returned  to  you,  spend  time examining the  comments  your teacher made.
This  is  a good  time to  compare  your classmates'  responses  to your teacher’s,  taking  into account the
changes  you  made between the original  draft and  the revised  paper.  Did you  improve on the  paπs of
your original  paper that your classmates  encouraged  you  to work on?  Did your teacher comment on
aspects of your paper that your classmates did not comment on?  Share this  information with the class
mates you  did peer-editing with.  For each  paper you  looked at,  compare the comments you  made to
the  teacher's  comments.  Keep  in  mind  the  ideas  you  and  your teacher  had  in  common  about each
pape[ Also,  notice comments that your teacher made that you  missed.  This  is  valuable  information.
You’II  use  it the  next time you  write and  the next time you  do peer-editing.

Keeping a  Journal

In  this  unit we read  about population growth,  about changes  that we expect to take  place  in the
future that will affect our lives.  For  a moment,  reflect back  in  time.  Try to visualize a place from  your
distant past,  any  place that sticks out in  your mind.  Now roll  the clock back  up to the  present.  If the
place  looks  very  different in  the present,  you've found  your journal  topic.  If not,  staπ again  until  you
come up with  a scene that  has  changed  over a period of time.  When you’ve found this place that has
changed,  write about it.  You  can  choose to describe it as  it was  in the past,  in the present,  or you  can
do  both.  You  might want to  write  about  how the  changes  in  the  place  have  affected  you.  Whatever
aspect of the place you choose to write about, make sure that you have a single purpose, a central focus,
and try to include detail  that helps to develop that main point only.  Remember that when  you  choose
to  write about something  that  is  familiar and  important to  you,  the  task  of writing  is  easier  and  more
pleasurable.

CHAPTER  21

Teaching ”lriting

411

7.  Strive to offer techniques that are as interactive as possible.

It  is  no  doubt  already  apparent  that  a  process-oriented  approach  to  writing
instrnction  is,  by  definition,  interactive  (as  students  work  in  pairs  and  groups  to
gεnerate  idεas  and  to  peer-edit),  as  well  as  Iεarner-cεnterεd  (with  ample
opportunitiεs  for  studεnts  to  initiatε  activity  and  exchange  ideas).  Writing
techniquεs that focus  on  purposεs other than compositions (such as  lettεrs ‘ forms,
memos,  dir1εctions, short  rεports) are  also  subject  to  thε principlεs of  intεractive
classrooms.  Group  collaboration,  brainstorming,  and  critiquing  ar1ε  as  εasily  and
succεssfully a part of many writing-focusεd tεchniquεs.  Don’t buy into thε myth that
writing is  a solitary activity!  Somε of it is, to be surε, but a good deal of what makes
a good writer can be most eft4εct iv'εly lεarnεd within a  communiη of lεarnεrs.

8.  Sensitively apply methods of responding to and coπecting your students’
wiiting.

In  Chaptεr 19, some principles of error correction werε suggεsted for  dealing
with learnεrs ’ speech errors.  Error corr'εction in writing must  bε approached in a
different  mannεr Because  writing,  unlike  speaking,  oftεn  includεs an  extensive
planning stag,ε, εrror treatment can begin in the drafting and revising stages, during
which timε it is  more  appropriatε to  considεr errors among sεveral features  of the
whole proc℃ss of responding to student writing.  As a student recεiv'εs responses to
written work, εrrors-just one of sev1εral possible things to rεspond to-arε rarely
changed outright by the instructor; rather, thεy are tr1εatεd through self-‘correction,
pεεr-correction, and instructor-initiated comments.

As  you respond to your students’ writing, rememb℃r that you  ar,ε  thεre as  an
ally, as a guidε, as a facilitator.  After the final work is  turnεd in, you may ind℃ed hav1ε
to  assumε the  position of judge  and  evaluator  (seε bεlow for  some  commεnts on
εvaluation), but until  then, the role  of consultant will  bε the  most  productiv,ε way
to respond.  Ideally, your rεsponses-or at lεast some of thεm-will bε writtεn and
oral as you hold a  conferencε, howεver short, with a student.  Under lεss than idεal
conditions, writtεn comments may have to suffice.

Here arε somε guidelines for responding to the first draft.

a.  Rεsist the temptation to  trεat minor (local) grammatical ℃rrors; major

(global) εrrors within relεvant paragraphs-sεε (e) below-can at this
stage be indicated εithεr dirεctly (say, by underlining) or indirectly (for
example, by a check nε:xt to the line in which an error occurs).
b.  Genεrally rεsist th℃ tεmptation to rewrite a  studεnt ’s sεntences.
c.  Commεnt holistically, in terms of the clarity of thε overall thesis and the

gεneral structural organization.

d.  Comment on thε introductoπ paragraph.
e.  Comment on features that appear to be irrelevant to the topic.
f.  Quεstion clεarly inadequate word choices and awkward εxpr,εssion within

those paragraphs/sεntencεs that arε rεlevant to the topic.

412

CHAPTER  21  Teaching Writing

For the subsequent dr.때s,  your rεsponses can include all of the abov,ε εxcept

that (a) now may chang℃ its charactεr slightly:

g.  Minor (“local”) grammatical and mechanical (spεHing, punctuation) εrrors

should be indicatεd, but not corr℃ctεd for thε student.

h.  Comment on thε specific clarity and strength of all  main ideas, supporting

ideas, and on argument and logic.

i.  Comment on any further word choices and expressions that may not be

j.  Check cohesive devices within and across paragraphs.
k.  In academic papers, comment on documentation, citing sources, evidencε,

and other support.

I.  Comment on thε adequacy and strεngth of the conclusion.

9.  Clearly  instruct  students  on  the  rhetorical,  formal  conventions  of
writing.

Each type of writing has its formal properties.  Don’t  just assume that students
will  pick  these  up  by  absorption.  Make  them  explicit.  A  reading  approach  to
writing  is  very  helpful  here.  For  acadεmic  writing,  for  εxample, some  of  the
fεatures  of  English  rhetorical  discourse  that  writεrs  use  to  explain,  proposε
solutions, debate, and argue are as follows:

•  a clear statement of the thesis or topic or purpose
•  use of main ideas to develop or clariη the thesis
•  use of supporting ideas
•  supporting by “ telling”; describing
•  supporting by “showing”: giving  evidεnce, facts, statistics, etc.
•  supporting by linking causε and εffect
•  supporting by using comparison and/or contrast

ASSESSING WRITING

The  assεssment of writing, especially in a  process-oriented  classroom, is  a  thorny
issue  (see  Ferris  &  Hεdgcock, 2005,  for  an  overview).  If you  are  a  guide  and
facilitator of students' performancε in the ongoing process of developing a piεce of
written work, how can you also be the judge? What do you judge? The answer to the
first question-how can you be a judge and a guide at the same time-is one of thε
primaπ dilεmmas of all  teachers.  Juggling  this  dual  role  requires  wisdom  and
sεnsitivity.  The key to bεing a judge is fairness and explicitness (reliability) in what
you take into account in your evaluation.

Table  21.2.  Categories for  evaluating writing  (adapted from  J.  D.  Brown,  1991,
pp.  42-46)

Content

•  thesis  statement
•  related  ideas

opinions

•  use of description,  cause/effect,  comparison/contrast

Organization

•  effectiveness of introduction
•  logical  sequence of ideas
•  conclusion
•  appropriate  length

Discourse

•  topic sentences
•  paragraph  unity
•  transitions
•  discourse  markers
•cohesion
•  rhetorical  conventions
•  reference
•  fluency
•economy
•  variation

Syntax

Vocabulary

Mechanics

•spelling
•  punctuation
•  citation of references  (if applicable)
•  neatness and appearance

A  glancε through  the  last  cεntury of  languagε-tεaching practices  revεals  mix℃d
opinions about the place of tεaching language forms, dεpending on the mεthod or
εra.  In  th℃  Grammar  Translation  Mεthod and  in  cognitive  code  lεarning  (see
Cha ptεr 2),  formal  aspects  of l없iguagε receiv1εd cεntral attention.  In  thε Direct
Method and the NaturalApproach,ov,εn focus on form was almost forbidden.  Somε
manifestations  of  CLT,  espεcially indirect  approaches,  advocated  only  a  passing
attention  to  form,  while  othεr proponents of CLT  injεctεd healthy  dosεs of form
focusεd tεchniques into a  communicativ1ε curriculum.  A decade ago, perhaps, our
profession  was  inundated  with  a  swarm  of  mixed  mεssages about  thε placε of
grammar  and  vocabulary  in  the  communicativ1ε languagε classroom, with  strong
advocatεs on both sides.

Today, only a handful of languag'ε-tεaching exp℃rts advocate  ηo focus on form
(“zero option”) at all, a  primε proponεnt of which is  Krashεn (1997) with his input
hypo thεsis (sεc PLLT,  Chapter  10).  Currεnt viεws of second  language  classroom
methodology are almost univεrsally in agrεεment on the inlportance of somε form
focused instruction within the  communicativ,ε framework, ranging from  εxplicit
treatmεnt  of  rules  to  noticing  and  consciousness-raising  (Ellis,  2001,  2006;
Williams,  2005)  techniquεs for  structuring  input  to  learners.  This  of coursε still
lεaves open a  wide  range  of options from  which you  must choose, depεnding on
your students, their purposes, and the context.

The forms of languagε include the organizational components of languag1ε and
the  와rstematic  rules  that  govern  thεir  structure.  Phonolo!밍cal,  grammatical,  and
lexical forms  occupy the three  principal formal  catεgories that typically appear in a

THE PIACE OF GRAMMAR

Grammar  is  the  야rstem  of  rules  gov,εming  thε  conventional  arrangemεnt 없ld
relationship of words in a sεntεnee.  In placε of “words;’ I could, for more specificity,
have said “m아phemεs;’  but for the moment just remember that the  compon℃nts of
words  (prefixεs, suffixεs, roots,  vεrb and  noun  ℃ndings, εtc.)  ar1℃ in deεd a  paπ of
grammar.  Technically, grammar  refers  to  sεntencε-lev1εl rulεs 01피y, and  not  to  rules
governing  the  relationship  따nong  sentεncεs,  which  we  commonly  rεfεr  to  as
discourse rules.  But for the sake of simplicity, I will include discours℃ considεrations
in this discussion of grammar-focusεd instruction.

In  thε  widely  acc℃pted  definition  of  communicativ1ε  competencε that  was
re viεW℃d in Chapters  3  and  4  (see  also  PLLT,  Chapter 8), grammatical  competεnce
occupiεs a prominent position as a major component of communicative compεtencζ
Organization떼 competence is  an intricatε, com plεx array of rules, some of which
govern  the  sentencε (grammar),  while  othεrs  govern  how  we  string  sentences
together (discourse).  Without the structure that organizational constraints impose on
our communicative attempts, our language would simply be chaotic.

Organizational compεtence is  ηecessaη for communication to takε place, but
not  sujficieηt to  account  for  all  production  and  reception  in  language.  As  Diane
Larsen-Fr'εeman (2003) pointed out, grammar is one of three dimensions of languagε
that are interconnected.  Grammar gives us the form or the structures of language,
but  those  forms  are  literally  meaningless  without  a  second  dimεnsion, that  of
semantics  (meaning),  and  a  third  dimension,  pragmatics.  In  other  words,
grammar tells us how to construct a sentence (word order, verb and noun systems,
modifiers,  phrases,  clauses,  etc.),  and  discourse  rules  tell  us  how  to  string  those
sentences  togεth er.  Sεman tics  tεUs us  something about  the  meaning(s)  of words
and strings of words.  Then pragmatics tells us about which of several mεanings to
assign given the context of an utterance or writtεn text.  Contεxt takes into account
such things as

•  who the speaker/writer is,
•  who the audience is,
•  wher,ε the communication takes place,
•  what communication takes place before and after a  sentεnce in question,
•  implied versus litεral mεanings,
•  styles and registers,
•  the alternative forms among which a producer can choose.

It’s  important to grasp  thε sign퍼cance of the  intεrconnectεdness of all  three
dimεnsions: No  one dimension is  sufficiεnt.  In such a view, grammar, according to
Larsen-Freeman (2003), is a dynamic process and learners ar1ε called on to engage in
what  she  callεd “grammaring.'’ 까1ε  latter  departs  from  the  usual  traditions  of
teaching grammar as a body of kηoαledge, and instead treats grammar as a skill.

CHAPTER  22  Form-Focused Instruction

421

So, no onε can tell you that grammar is irrelevant, or that grammar is  no long℃r
neεdεd  in  a  CLT  framework.  No  one  s℃riously  quεstions  the  prominence  of
grammar as an organizational framework within which communication opεrates.

TO TEACH OR NOT TO TEACH GRAMMAR

The next quεstion, then, is  whεth℃r to teach grammar in languagε classεs, and if so,
how to Iεach it.  As  noted above, varied  opinions on the question can bε found  in
the  literature  on  language  teaching.  Reason,  balancε,  and  thε  expεriεncε  of
teachεrs in  recent  communicative  methodology tell  us  that  judicious attention  to
grammatical  form  in  thε  adult  classroom  is  not  only  helpful,  if  appropriate
techniques  arε usεd, but  εssential  to  a  speεdy l℃arning procεss (seε  Ellis,  2006;
Nassaji  &  Fotos, 2004; Nunan, 2005).  Appropriate grammar-focusing techniques

•  are embεdded in meaningful, communicative contexts,
•  contribute positively to communicative goals,
•  promote accuracy within fluent, communicativε language,
•  do not overwhεlm students with linguistic terminology,
•  are as  liv1εly and intrinsically motivating as  possible.

For adults, thε question is  not so much whεther to tεach grammar, but rather,
what the optimal conditions for  the  teaching of grammar are, and what dεgree of
overt  attεntion  should  bε included  in  such  form-focused  instruction.  Rod  Ellis
(2006,pp.102-103) offerεd some of his own answεrs to quεstions about wh℃n and
how to teach grammar, which are capsulized here:

•  Both  form  and  mεaning should  be  emphasized;  learnεrs neεd to  havε the

opportunity to practicε forms in communicative tasks.

•  Focus  mor1ε strongly on forms  that are problεmatic for learners.
•  Explicit grammar tεaching is  more effiεctiv1ε at the intermεdiatε to advancεd

lεvels than beginning levεls.

•  Attεnd to both input-basεd (comprεhension) and output-basεd (production)

grammar.

•  Both  deductiv1ε and  inductivε approachεs can be  useful, depending  on thε

contεxt and purposε of instruction.

•  Iηcidental focus on form is  valuable in that it treats errors that occur whilε

lεarn℃rs ar,ε εngaged in meaningful communication.

•  Corrective  f℃℃dback can  facilitate  acquisition  if it  involves  a  mixture  of

implicit and εxplicit feedback.

•  Separatε grammar lessons (“focus  on forms ”) and grammar  intεgratεd into
communicati、T1ε activities (“focus on form ”) are both viable, depending on the
context.

422

CHAPTER  22  Form-Focused Instruction

Another way of viewing the rolε of grammar in language teaching is  to look at
variables  that  Cεlee-Murcia (199la)  first  proposed, but  that  have  been  addr1εssed
repεatedly, in  one  form  or  another,  since  then  (Larsen-Freeman,  2003;  Nassaji  &
Fotos, 2004; Nunan, 2005; Williams, 2005) ‘  We’ll  look at six variables.

1.  Age

It  is  clεar  that  due  to  normal  intellectual  dεvεlopmεntal  variables,  young
children  can  profit  from  a  focus  on  form  if attεntion to  form  is  offerεd through
structurεd input and incidental, indir,εct εrror treatment.  Somewhat oldεr childrεn
may benefit as well from  vεry simplε generalizations (such as “ This  is  the way we
say it when wε’re talking about yesterday”) and concrεte illustrations ‘  Adults, with
their abstract intellεctual capabilities, can usε grammatical pointers to advance their
communicativ1ε abilities.

2.  Proficiency level

If we force  too much grammar focus  on beginning lεvεl learners, wε run the
risk of blocking their acquisition of fluεncy skills.  At this lεvel, grammatical focus is
helpful  as  an  occasional “ zoom  lεns ” with  which  wε zεro in  on  somε aspεct of
language but not helpful if it becomes thε major focus of class work.  Most resεarch
agrεes that at the intεrmediatε to advanced lεvεls, a more explicit focus  on form is
less  likεly to disturb  communicative fluency(cid:157)  and can assist learners in developing
accuracy (Celeε-Murcia, 1991a; Ellis, 2006).

3.  Educational background

Students who  ar,ε nonliteratε or who  havε no formal  educational background
may find it difficult to grasp thε complexity of grammatical terms and εxplanations.
Highly  educated  students,  on  thε other  hand,  are  cognitivεly more  recεptive to
grammar focus and may insist on εrror correction to hεlp refine their already fluent
sk피s.

4.  Language skills

Because  of  the  pεrmanence of  writing  and  th℃ demand  for  perfection  in
grammatical  form  in  written  English,  grammar  focus  is  absolutely  necessaη in
improving  written  English.  In  speaking  skills,  focus  on  form,  especially  through
incidental  feedback,  has  been  shown  in  some  studies  to  be  effective  (Williams,
2005).  For  input-based  skills  (listεning and  reading), there  is  some  evidence  that
treatment of perceived sources of error can aid learners (Larsen-Fr1εeman, 2003).

5.  Style (r앵ister)

Informal  contεxts  often  make  fewer  dεmands on  a  learnεr’S  grammatical
accuracy.  In  casual  conversation  among  peεrs, for  example,  minor  errors  ar1ε
acceptable, while  more formal  contεxts (say,  a  student consulting with a  t℃acher)
usually  require  greater  grammatical  accuracy.  Similarly,  in  writing,  tolerance  for
error is  higher in, say, a quick ε-mailed messag1ε than in a formal εssay.

6.  Needs and goals

If learnεrs are headεd toward prof1εssional goals, thεy may need to strεss formal
accuracy morε than lεarnεrs at  thε survival level.  In  eithεr casε, mεssagε clarity is
a  primε critεrion.

Th esε six catεgoriεs should bε lookεd on as  general guidelinεs for judging thε
nε℃d  for  conscious  grammatical  focus  in  thε  classroom,  but  nonε  of  thesε
suggestions is absolute! For example, you can probably think of numerous situations
where it is important to focus on form with beginners, or to get lεarners away from
too intensε a grammatical focus  in thε context of a formal  rεgist er.

ISSUES ABOUT HOW TO TEACH GRAMMAR

While  thε professional  community  in  genεral agrεεs on  thε importance  of form
focused instruction (Nassaji & Fotos, 2004; Williams, 2005), therε are still dεgrεes of
opinion  on what  kind  of instruction  should be  offered to  learn℃rs.  Four  primary
issu℃S characterizε this ongoing prof;εssional discussion.

1.  Should grammar be presented inductively or deductively?

Do  learners  benefit  from  an  inductive  approach  in  which  various  languagε
forms are practiced hut in which the learners are left to discover or induce rules and
generalizations  on  their  own?  Or  would  thεy  be  better  off  bεing  given  a
rule/generalization by the teacher or textbook and thεn allowed to practice various
instancεs of language  to  which  the  rule  applies? Th℃se two  approaches  are  often
contrastεd with εach other when quεstions about grammar teaching arise.

In  most  contεxts, an  inductive  approach  is  more  appropriate  becausε of thε

following:

•  It  is  morε in  keeping  with  natural  languag℃ acquisition  (where  rulεs arε

absorbed subconsciously with little or no conscious focus).

•  It  conforms  more  easily  to  thε concεpt of interlanguage  dεvεlopment in
which  Iεarners  progress,  on  variable  timetables,  throu양1  stages  of  rulε
acquisition.

•  It allows students to get a communicative “fεel” for some aspect of languagε

bεfor,ε possibly bεing overwhelmed by grammatical ε:xplanations.

•  It  builds  more  intrinsic  motivation  by allowing  students  to  discover  rulεs

rather than being told them.

There may be occasional moments, of course, when a deductive approach-or
a  blend  betv.℃이 the two-is indeed warranted.  In practice, the distinction is  not
always  appar℃nt.  Considεr  the  following  excerpt  from  a  low  intermediate
classroom (the T has asked Ss  to tell  thε rest of the class about a  recεnt journεy):

After Kamal finished  his story, this  time  with a  more accurate  use  of the past
tense, the teachεr put the verbs they used on the board, listed their past tense forms,
and had studεnts practice them.  While you mi항it question the appropriateness of
the  inteπuption here, the  point  is  that  the  lesson’S objective  was  to  use  the  past
tense,  따id the  teacher's  focus  on  the  past  tense  in  this  particular  instance  was
deductive for  the  rest of the students in the  class  who were  listening.  But  it  was
inductive in that the focus on the past actually was triggered by students’ meaningful
performance.

2.  Should we use grammatical explanations and technical terminology in
a  CLT classroom?

Our  historical  roots  (in  Grammar Translation  methodology)  placed  a  strong
emphasis  on  grammatical  explanations  (in  the  mother  tongue)  and  on  the
terminology  necessary  to  carry  out  those  explanations.  M따iy  forεign language
learners in the United States have remarked that thεir first and only εncounter with
grammatical  concepts  was  not in  English  (language  arts  classes)  but  in  a  foreign
language  class, where  that  they learned about  subjects, predicates, direct  objεcts,
and intransitive verbs.

In  CLT  classes, the  use  of grammatical  explanation  and terminology must be
approached with  C았C We  teachers  are  sometimes so  eager  to  display  our hard
earned metalinguistic knowledge that we forget  that our students are so busy just
learning the language itself that the added load of complex rules and terms is too
much  to  bear.  But  clearly,  ad띠ts  can  benefit  from  occasional  explanations.
Following  a  few  simple  φut not  always  easily  interpreted)  rules  of thumb  will
enhance any grammatical explanations you undertake.

•  Keep your expl없iations brief and simple.  Use the mother tongue if students

cannot follow an explanation in En행sh.

•  Use charts and other visuals whenever possible to graphic때ly depict

grammatic외 relationships.

•  Illustrate with clear, unambiguous 앉따nples.
•  Try to account for 째rying cognitive styles 따nong your students (for example,

analytical l얹mers w피 have an easier time picl따ig up on grammatical
explanations than will holistic learners).

CHAPTER  22  Form-Focused Instructi。n

425

•  Do not get yoursεIf (and studεnts!) tied up in knots ovεr so-callεd

εxceptions to rules.

•  If you don’t  know how to εxplain something (for instancε, if a  studεnt asks
you about a point of grammar and you  ar,ε not sure of the rule), do not risk
giving falsε information (that you may have to rεtract latεr, which will cause
even morε εmbarrassment).  Rather, tεll students you w파 rεsearch that point
and bring an answer back thε next day.

3.  Should grammar be taught in separate “ grammar only”  classes?

The  collectiv'ε  expεrience of  the  last  two  dεcadεs or  so  of  CLT  practice,
combined with the research on thε emεctivεnεss of grammatical instruction (Nassaji
&  Fotos, 2004;  Nunan, 2005), indicates  the  advisability  of embεdding grammatical
tεchniqu℃S into  general  languagε coursεs, f' th℃r than  singling  grammar  out  as  a
discrεIε “ skill”  and  treating  it  in  a  sεparatε  course.  Grammatical  information,
whether consciously or subconsciously lεarnεd,is an ℃nabling system, a compon℃nt
of  communicativε  compεtence  likε  phonology,  discourse,  the  lexicon,  εtc.
Thereforε, as  courses  help  students  to  pursue  rele、rant language  goals, grammar is
best brought into th℃ pictur,ε as  a contributor toward those goals.

In  somε curricula, however, cεrtain class hours, workshops, or coursεs are set
aside  for  grammar  instruction.  In  a  l값1guagε-tεaching  paradigm  that  swεsses
communicative,  interactive.  mεaningful learning‘  such  courses  may  appεar to  be
anachronisms.  Under  cεrtain  conditions,  howεvεr,  they  can  providε  a  usεful
function, εspεcially for  high  intermεdiate to  advancεd learners, whεrε a  modicum
of fluency is  alrεady in placε (Ellis, 2006).  Thosε conditions arε as follows:

•  Thε grammar coursε is  εxplicitly integratεd into the total curriculum so that
studεnts can readily relate grammatical pointεrs to thεirothεr work in English.
•  Th℃ r℃st of the curriculum (or th℃ bulk of studεnts ’ us℃ of language  outsidε
of thε grammar class)  controls  thε contεnt of the grammar course, and not
vice versa ‘  That is, the 얄ammar coursε “ servεs” (enhancεs) the curriculum.
Forexamplε, a significant portion of the agenda for the grammar class should
come from students’ work in other coursεs.

•  Grammar is  contextualizεd in meaningful language use.
•  Thε course is  tailorεd as much as possible for specific problεms students arε
experiencing.  For  example, in  grammar “workshops” for  intermεdiate and
advanced  students,  grammatical  topics  come  from  the  studεnts’  own
performance  in  other classes,  rathεr than  bεing preset  by a  curriculum or

t℃xtbook.

•  Somεtimεs grammar modulεs in a standardized test preparation coursε serve
as  helpful  revi℃ws of grammatical  principles that may  be  incorporatεd into
the test.

•  Thε ultimate  test of the success  of such  coursεs is  in  the  improvement of
students' pεrformancε outside  of thε grammar  class,  not  in  their  score  on
discrete-point grammar tεsts.

426

CHAPTER  22  Form-Focused Instruction

Under  these  conditions, then, grammar  assumes  its  lo밍C때 role  as  one  of several
supporting foundation stones for communication.

4.  Should teachers coπect grammatical errors?

Many  student ℃πors in speech and writing  perform따ice are  grammatical.  It  is
interesting that  rεsearch evidence shows that rarely is overt grammatical correction
by teachers in the classroom of any consequence in improving learners' language.  But
we  do  hav'ε  evidεncε that  various  other  forms  of  attention  to  없id  treatmεnt of
grammatical errors have an impact on learners.  (See PUT, Chapter 9, for an overview
of error treatment in SLA and Chapter 19 of this book for a discussion of the treatment
of spoken εrrors.) Therefore, it is prudent for you to en쩔ge in such treatment, as long
as  you  adhere  to  principles  of  maintaining  communicative  flow,  of  maximizing
student self-corrεction, and of sensitively considering the affective and linguistic place
the learner is in.

The  treatment  of grammatical  (and  discourse) errors  in writing  is  a  different
matter.  In  process  writing  approaches, overt  attention  to  local grammatical  and
rhetorical (discourse) errors is normally delayed until learners have completed one
or two  drafts  of a  paper.  Global errors  that impede  meaning  must  of course  be
attended to earlier in the process.  Studiεs have shown (Ferris &  Hedgcock, 2005)
that  certain  attention  to  ℃rrors does  indeed  make  a  differεncε in  final  written
products.

GRAMMAR TECHNIQUES

Following are some sample techniques for teaching grammar, using Sandra McKay’s
(1985) classifications that have withstood the test of time.

1.  Charts

Charts and graphs are useful devices for practicing patterns, clari다ing grammatical
relationships, and  even  for  understanding  sociolinguistic  and  discoursε constraints.
The exercise in Figure 22.1  stimulates students to practice frequency adv'εrbs.

Another grammatical system that lends itself well to charts is the verb system.
Figure 22.2 on pages 428-429 illustrates a commonly used system of di℃picting some
verb tenses.

2.  Objects

Objects brought into the classroom not only liven up the context but provide
a  kinesthetic,  hands-on  dimension  to  your  teaching.  By  enga횡ng studεnts  in
communication with each other, you also stimulate them to practice conversation
rules and other discourse  constraints.  To  teach the possessive  to beginning  level
students, for  ex따nple, bring  in a  few small items such as  a  necklace, a  purse, and
some glasses.  Then ask students to put two  or three of their own things on their
desks.  Then  do  something  like  the  three  exercises  in  Figure  22.3  on page  430.

3.  Maps and drawings

Maps, also  mentioned in Chapter  14  in the  discussion  about group work, are

and  other  intεractive  techniques,  they  can  also  serve  to  illustrate  certain
grammatical structures.  For example, maps can stimulate learners’ usε of

•  preposi1다.onal phrases (up the street, on the left,  over the hill,  etc.),
•  밍Liestion forms (ψhere, how do I get to,  can you tell ηie, is this,  etc.),
•  impera1디1ves (go,  walk, look out for,  etc.),
•  appropriate discourse for getting someone’s attention, asking for dir,εctions,
receiving and clari다ing given information, and terminating the conversation.

Sandra McKay su잃:ested using drawings of circles, squares, and other familiar shapes
to teach locative words (see Figure 22.4 on page 431).

Introducing Tenses

A  visual  representation  can  often  be  clearer than  a verbal  one to  int「oduce a tense. This  is
especially true where students  do not have similar tense  systems  in  their mother tongue.
Time  can  be  shown  by a  line across  the  board.  An arrow pointing down  indicates this
moment now. To  the  left of the arrow  is  past  time,  to  the  right  is  the future.  A  cross
indicates a single event,  a row of dots denotes  an  action that  lasted  or will  last for a
period  of time. The  uses  of most tenses  can  be shown  and  contrasted  pictorially on  such
a time  line,  as  shown  in the following examples.

1.  He  used to  smoke (in  the  past,  not anymore).

(cid:157)

2.  She  works  in  the market (did  in  the past and  will  continue  in the future).

(cid:157)

3.  He  is  having his supper (eating  now,  having started  a short while ago  in  the  past,  but
this  will  not continue for any appreciable  length  of time).

4.  He got up at six o'clock (in  the  past,  a single event).

6.  VVe'I/  travel by plane (in  the future).

SIMPLE  DRAWINGS

With  Prepositional  Phrases
of location
Drawings of simple shapes can  be used to  provide practice in  stating locations and giving
directions.  In  order to  do this,  you  might begin  by  using the following drawing,  modeling
the expressions which  follow.

To  Describe  locations
To  Give  Directions

|

|

$
O []

D

The circle  is  in  the center of the paper.
The diamond  is  directly above the circle.
The square is  to the r땅ht of the circle.
The  rectangle  is  in  the upper r땅ht-hand comeκ
The triangle  is  in  the lower left-hand comeκ

After you  have  introduced these terms tell  the students to take out a piece of paper.
Give them a series of commands and  have them  draw these on  this  paper.  (E.g”  Draw a
square in  the  upper  left-hand corner.  Draw a circle inside the square.)

Later you  might use this same technique to  introduce more technical  vocabulary of
shapes along with  the relative proportion (E.g.,  Draw a triangle  in  the center of the paper.
Draw a circle above the triangle. The diameter of the circle should be the same  len망h as the
base of the triangle.)

GRAMMAR SEQUENCING IN TEXTBOOKS AND CURRICULA

Grammatical sεquencing received a great  deal  of attention in  the  1950s  and  1960s
when curricula 따id textbooks were organized around g없nmatic때 categories.  Some
lanl긴iage  profession와S  were  of  the  opinion  that  띠fficulty  could  be  predicted
(espe따lly if the  native  language  were  taken into consideration) and that therefore
grammar in  a  curriculum  should  be  sequenced  in  a  progr℃ssion of easier to  more
difficult  items.  Yet  no  one  had  been  ablε to  veri다 empirically such hierarchies  of
difficulty by thε time the debate over grammatical sequencing whimpered to a halt
and situational and notional-functional curricula assumed pop버따ity.  At that point the
question shifted more to whether or not there was an optim쇠 funαion쇠 sequεnce.

Preparing the Students

Presentation: Conversation

A.  Introduce future  time expressions and the

future  with  the present continuous tense.  On
the board,  write the following  sentence.
Underline  is and  -ing:

Mark  뜨 driv낀올 to Colorado tomorrow.

Tell  the students that you  want them to  help
you  continue to  write a story  about Mark.
Write another sentence on the board:

He’S  leaving early  in  the morning,  and  he닝
taking a friend  with  him ‘

Now  have the class suggest other  lines for  the
story.  Write them  on the  board.  Finally,  call
on students to underline all  the examples of
the present continuous tense.

B.  Review the word  let's  used  in  making sugges
tions or  invitations.  Have the students per
form  actions which  you  suggest.  For  example,
with  appropriate gestures,  say “ Let’5  stand  up
and  stretch.” (The  students stand  up and
stretch.)  Make several  other suggestions and
have the class carry out the actions.  Be  sure
that you  participate.

A.  Have the students look at the picture.  Establish
the context-」Carlos and Tetsuo are talking
about a school  break.  Read the conversation
or play the cassette.  Have the students listen as
they  read  along silently  in  their books.

B.  Answer any  questions students have about

vocabulary or structures.  Introduce or review
the words  during,  break,  stay,  go away,  beach,
vacation,  and  rest. Then  have the students
close their books. Ask  them  questions about
the conversation.  For example:

Do Carlos and Tetsuo  have a break soon?
Are  they both staying  in  Dallas?
Where are they going?
Why’s Carlos going to  Los  Angeles?
When's he leaving?
Do they think they  need a  rest?
Are they both going to drink a soda?
How  much  does  a soda cost?

C.  In  pairs,  have the students practice the con
versation.  Encourage them to use their own
ideas  by  changing the names of places,  times,
and activities.  Call  on several  pairs to  present
their conversations to the class.

In recent years, we have witnessed a  return to a  more  bal없iced viewpoint in
which grammar is seen as one of several organizational aspects of communicative
competence, all  of which  should  be considered in programming  a  textbook  or a
curriculum.  In  this  perspective,  the  question  of  an  optimal  sequence  of
grammatic때 structures is not irrelevant, but with our current disciplinaηr maturity,
we seem to agree that

•  grammatical categories are onε of several considerations in curricular

sequencing

•  a  curriculum  usually  manifests  a  logical  sequεncε of  basic  grammatical
structures  (such  as  introducing  thε past perfect tεnse after  the  past  tεnsε,
relative clauses after question formation), but such a  sequenc;ε may bε morε
a  factor  of frεquency and  usefulness  than  of clearly  identified  degrεes of
linguistic difficulty

•  beyond those basic structures, a few permutations herε and therε will makε
little  difference  in  the  ℃ventual success  of students, as  long  as  language  is
being learned in thε contεxt of a  communicativε curriculum

“ Scope  and  sequence” charts  found  in  most tε:xtbooks ar'ε good  examples  of
how a  course  sεquences grammatical, functional,  and  situational  elemεnts of thε
course.  The  chart shown  in Chapter 7  (pag℃S 120-123) is  illustrative  of a  typical
sequence of grammatical structurεs in a  basal ESL  series.  In arranging the order of
structur,εs, the principlεs of simplicity and foεquency were followed.  Therefore, the
mor,ε “complεx” tenses and clause formations  come later in  thε sεries.  While  onε
could  quibble  with  cεrtain  εlements  and  suggest  alternative  permutations,
nevertheless learners’ success in a  coursε like this  seεms to be mor,ε a  factor of (a)
clear,  unambiguous  presentation  of material  and  φ) opportuniη for  meanin행Il,
interactive practice, rather than a factor of a grammar point presented a wεek earlier
or latεr.

CURRENT ISSUES IN VOCABULARY TEACHING

CHAPTER  22  Form-Focused lnstrucfi,。n

435

The other “ half" of form-focused instruction is vocabulary-the thousands of lexical
building blocks that are available to th℃ average user of a language.  As we consider
vocabulary teaching, be reminded again that lεxical items ar1ε basic to all of the four
skills, and so vocabulary is not a “ skill” as we normally use the term.  The skill comes
in  the  efficient  storage  (competence)  and  adept  retrieval  (performance)  of those
units.  How vocabulary  should  be  taught  has  stimulated  some  controversies  over
time, which wε’ll first  take a brief look at here.

One of the casualtiεs of the early approaches to CLT was a loss of a concertεd
focus  on  the  lexical  forms  of  language.  While  traditional  language-teaching
methods highlighted vocabulaπ study with lists, definitions, written and oral dr피s,
and  flash  cards,  there  was  a  period  of timε when “ the  teaching  and  learning  of
vocabulary  [were]  undervalued" (Zimmerman,  1997, p. 5).  In  thε zeal  for  natural,
authentic classroom tasks and activities, vocabulaπ focus was swept under the rug.
Further, as  teachers more and more perceived their rolε as  facilitators  and guides,
they became more reluctant to take the directiv1ε and sometimes intrusive steps to
turn students' focus to lexical form.

Toward  the  end  of  the  twentieth  century,  we  saw  a  revival  of  systematic
attention to vocabulary learning across a number of proficiεncy lev,εls and contexts.
Ranging from very explicit focus, such as that found in Michael Lewis’s (1993, 1997‘
2000)  Lexical  Approach,  to  more  indirect  approachεs  in  which  vocabulary  is
incorporated  into  communicative  tasks,  attεntion  to  lεxical  forms  is  now  more
central to the  developmεnt of language  curricula (Nation, 2001, 2003, 2005; Read,
2004).

One  of the “hot  topics” of the  last  decade  in vocabulary  teaching  is  whether
learners ar,ε better seπed in the long run with incidental exposure to lexical items
(that is, as a by-product of communicativ,ε activities), or with intentional, explicit
focus  on  vocabulary.  In  the  earlier  years  of  CLT  approaches, “the  concept  of
incidental  learning  offerεd the seductive prospect that, provided the learners had
access to sufficient comprehensible input, L2  vocabulary acquisition would largely
take care of itself" (Read, 2004, p. 147).  However, as  Read (2004), Hulst뻐 (2001),
and others have shown, intεntional vocabulary focus accounts for  significant gains
in  acquisition,  and  it  may  therefore  be “ necessary  to  re-visit  such  unfashionable
procedures as  regular  rehearsal of words, rote  learning, and training in  automatic
word  recognition  ... particularly  for  beginning  and  intermediate  level  learners”

(Hulst”n, 2001, p. 275).

A further development in vocabulaπ teaching is  the rapid growth of coφus
linguistics  and  the  volumes  of raw  data  that  are  now  available  in  corpora  that
encompass  spoken  and  written  language, genres  of each, as  well  as  data  from  a
number of varieties of world Englishes.  Conrad (2005) and others have described
numerous ways in which corpus linguistics has improved our collective capacity to
expose learners to rεal-world language.  We  have ready access not just to statistics

436

CHAPTER  22  Form-Focused Instruction

such as word frequency counts, but morε important, collocations (words that tend
to appear in thε company of other words).  Concordancing enablεs learnεrs (and
tεxtbook writεrs) to seε words in contεxt (McCarthy, 2004) ‘  And th℃Sε voluminous
copora  providε  data  banks  through  which  we  can  mor,ε  dosεly  εxaminε and
a ppr,εciatε associations  betwεεn grammatical and lexical units (Hunston & Francis,
2000).

Currεnt practicεs in teaching vocabulary, espεcially in view of thε t℃chnology
of corpus linguistics, are clearly not simply a  rεbirth of thε samemεthods of half a
cεntury ago.  Rather  than  viεwing vocabulary  items  as  a  long  and  boring  list  of
words  to  be  definεd and  memorizεd, lexical  forms  now  play  a  central  role  in
contextualized, mεaningful language.  Learn℃rs can  be  guided  in  specific  ways  to
intεrnalize thεse important building blocks of language.

PRINCIPLES FOR TEACHING VOCABULARY

Bεlowar1ε somε guidelines for the communicative treatmεnt of vocabulary instruction.

1.  Allocate specific class time to vocabulary learning.

In  the  hustl℃ and  bustle  of our  intεractiv1ε classrooms, sometimes  wε gεt so
caught up in lively group work and meani앵ful communication that we don’t pause
to  dεvote some  attention  to  words.  After  all,  words  are  basic  building  blocks  of
language;  in  fact,  survival-levεl  communication  can  take  place  quite  intεlligibly
when  peoplε simply  string  words  together-without  applying  any  grammatical
rules at all!  So, if we’re interested in bεing communicative, words ar1ε among thε first
priorities.

2.  Help students to learn vocabulary in context.

The  best

intεrnalization  of  vocabulary  comεs  from  εncounters
(compr1εhεnsion or  production)  with  words  within  thε contεxt of surrounding
discourse.  Data from linguistic corpora can provide real-world actual language that
has  been  printed  or  spoken.  Rather  than  isolating  words  and/or  focusing  on
dictionary definitions, learners  can  benefit  from  attending  to vocabulary within  a
communicative framework in which items appear.  Studεnts will then associate nεw
words with a meaningful context to which they apply.

3.  Play down the role of bilingual dictionaries.

A corollary to the above is  to help students to resist the tεmptation to overusε
their  bilingual  dictionaries.  In  rεcent  yεars,  with  the  common  availability  of
electronic pocket dictionaries, studεnts are ev,εn more εasily tempted to punch in a
word  thεy don’t  know  and  get  an  instant  rεsponse.  It is  unfortunate  that  such
practices rar,εly hεlp students to intεrnalize the word for later recall and use.

[ HAPTER 22  Form-Fa [ used Instruction

437

How℃vεr,  recent  yεars  hav℃  Sεεn  an  increasing  popularity  of  learn℃rs ’
dictionaries-which  are  En밍ish-English  dictionariεs  modified  for  more  lεarner­
friendly  definitions,  metaphors,  idioms,  and  contrasts ‘  When  a  dictionary  is
warrantεd, such publications can be veπ us℃ful.

4.  Encourage students to develop strategies for determining the meaning
of words.

Included in the discussion of learning strategiεs in Chapter 16 arε references to
learning words.  Also, Chapter 20 rεfεrred to such stratεgies as guessing vo앉bulary
in  context.  A  numbεr of “clues” are  available  to  learners  to  dev1εlop “word  attack"
strntegiεs.  Figure  22.8  on  pages  438-439  provides  a  dεtailεd taxonomy  of such
strategies with εX따nples.

5.  Engage in “unplanned”  vocabulary teaching.

In all  likεlihood, most of the attention you givε to vocabulary learning will bε
unplanned: those moments when a  studεnt asks about a word or when a  word has
appeared  that you  feel  deserves  some  attention.  ThεS℃ impromptu  moments  are
vεry imp야tant.  Somεtimεs, they are  simply  briεf little  pointers; for  example, thε
word “ clumsy” once appεared in a paragraph studεnts werε rεading and the teachεr
volunteered:

Sometimes,  such  impromptu  moments  may  be  extendεd: The  teacher  gives

The  principal  (main)

b.  Synonyms and antonyms: Most students

4.  Inference Clues

까iese types of clues require a higher level of
analytical  skill  and  practice  than  previous
types dealt with. They should be approached
slowly,  moving  from  obvious  answers  to
increasingly  vague  exercises.  The  ESL  stu
dent  should  never  be  expected  to  do  the
same  kind  of inferring  that a native  speaker
could do, but should be encouraged to go as
far  as  possible as  long as  the guessing  is  not
allowed to  become wild.  For  all  three  types
of  clues  (example,  summary,  and  experi
ence)  the  same  method  of  practice  in  (i)
recognition  of  clue  elements  and
(ii)
obtaining meaning from the elements can be
followed.

CHAPTER  22  Form-Focused lnstructi。n

Unfortunately, profession때 pend띠urns have a disturbing way of swinging too
far  one  way  or  the  other,  and  sometimes  the  only  way  we  can  get  enou방1
perspective to see these overly long arcs is  throu양1 피ndsi양it.  Hindsi양it has now
taught us  that there was some overreaction to the almost  exclusive  attention that
grammar and vocabulary received in the first  two-thirds of the twentieth centutγ.
So-called “natural”  approaches  in  which  grammar was  considered  damaging were
equally overreactive.  Advocating the “absorption” of grammar and vocabulary with
no  overt attention whatsoever to  languag'ε forms  went too  far.  We  now seem to
have a healthy respect for the place of form-focusεd instruction-attεntion to those
basic “bits  and pieces” of a  language-in an interactive  curriculum.  And  now we
C없1 pursue the  business of finding  better and better techniques for  getting  these
bits and pieces into the communicative repertoires of our learners.

TOPICS FOR DISCUSSION, ACTION, AND RESEARCH

[Note: (I) Individual work; (G) group or pair work; (C) whole-class discussion.]

1.  (I) It might be useful to review the section on form-focused instruction and

error treatment in PLLT, Chapter 9, where background research and
terminology are explained.

2.  (G) Sometimes grammatical knowledge isn’t  sufficient to understand “hidden”
or implied meanings of what people say or write.  Tell pairs to look at the
following:

a.  “Oh!That’s just great! ” [falling intonation]
b.  “Good to see you again, Helen.  You’ve lost some weight, haven’t

you?”

c.  “Bπrr! It’s sure cold in this house! ”

까ie “surface'’ g없nmatical meaning differs from potential “deep” structure
meanings.  Ask the pairs to ident퍼r those meanings, and, if possible, to think
of other examples.  Then have them devise a few techniques that could be
used to teach such pragmatic aspects of English, and share their ideas with
the rest of the class.

3.  (l/C) Observe a class in which the teacher uses some form-focused

instruction.  Evaluate the effectiveness of the class using the five  grammar
focusing techniques on page 421.  Share your observations with the whole
class.

4.  (G) Assign a separate, different grammar “point” to every two pairs and have
them do the following: One pair figures out how to teach that point with a
deductive approach and describes students for which such an approach is
justified; the other pair is directed to do the same with an inductive approach.
Pairs then present their suggestions to the whole class for comparison.

5.  (C) On page 424, an example of a teacher's intervention is given.  Discuss the
following as a whole class: Was the teacher's interruption warranted? What are

ASSESSING  LANGUAGE  SKILLS

‘.

Assessment  is  an  integral  aspect  of  the  pedago~잊cal  process  of
designing  lessons, implementing them, and  evaluating  their success.
Without  따1 assessment  component  in every  lesson, every  unit, 따id
every course, we couldn’t determine the attainment of objectives and
go싫s.

This  section  of  Teaching  l:ry  Principles  addresses  concepts,
issues, and practic외ities of assessment in the classroom.  It is intended
to serve simply as  an introduction to classroom-based  assessment
enou양1 to supply you with some useful tools for creating or adap따lg
yo따 own assessment procedures.  For a comprehensive treatment of
l없iguage assessment, I recommend reading my companion textbook,
/Anguage Assessment: Principles  and Classroom  Practices  (Brown,
2004).

Chapter 23  offers  a  survey of principles and basic  concepts in:
language  assessment,  beginning  with  the  articulation  of five  basic
principles  that  underlie  virtually  every  assessment  procedure, from
formal tests to informal, incidental assessmeηts that are P양t ofvirtu
따ly every  moment of teaching.  These  principles  are  followed  by a
look at historical developments in the field, a look at  lar뿔융cale and
standard응based testing, and  an  때alysis of the  critical  ethical  issues
involved in assessment.

Chapter 24 is focused directly on the classroom, and on what you
as a teacher can do to develop principled, effective, informative 싫sess­
ments  in  your  teaching  process.  After  a  primer  on  writing  formal
tests and test items, you're led throu양I a succession of alternatives in
assessment:  the  feasibility  of using  portfo뼈s, jourr때s, conferences,
observations, and  selε and  peer-assessment.  The .chapter concludes
with a closer look at the dilemmas presented by balancing principles
ofprac디C외ity /reliability with principles of 때thenticity and washback
in the classroom.

TESTING, ASSESSING, AND TEACHING

Before  diving into a  dεscription of the  language  assessment field, it’S  important to
define some frequently misundεrstood terms.  The word assessmeηt has, in recent
years,  becomε  a  popular  word  for  εducators-in  much  the  same  way  that

444

[ HAPTER 23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

445

communicative  or  inter.αctiνe havε gainεd wides prεad acceptanc℃ in  language
teaching circles.  You  are said to be on the cutting εdg<ε if you assess your studεnts
in lieu of testiηg them.  This is a  misundεrstanding.

On  th℃  onε hand,  somεtimes you  find  writεrs  using  test  and  assessment
synonymously, which  is  not  the  case.  A test is  a  method of measuring a  pεrson ’s
ability  or  knowlεdge in  a  givεn domain,  with  an  εmphasis on  the  concepts  of
method and measuring.  T℃sts are instrumεnts that are (usually)  car,εfully designεd
and  that  havε  identifiablε  scoring  rubrics.  Tεsts  ar,ε  prεpared  administrative
procεdurεs that  occupy  identifiable  time  periods  in  a  curriculum  when  learners
muster all  thεir facultiεs to  offer  peak performancε, knowing that  thεir responsεs
ar,ε being measurεd and evaluated.

Assessment, on  th℃ othεr hand, is  an  ongoing  process  that  εncompasses a
much  wid℃r  domain ‘  Whenεvεr a  studεnt  rεsponds  to  a  question,  off1εrs  a
commεnt, or triεs out  an℃W word or structure, thε teachεr subconsciously makεs
an  assεssmεnt of the  student’S  performance.  Written  work-from  a  jotted깅own
phrasε to  a  formal  essay-is  performance  that  ultimatεly  gets  assessed  by  self,
teach℃r, and possibly othεr students.  Reading and listening activiti℃S usually require
some  sort  of productive  p℃rformance that  thε tεachεr implicitly  judges, however
pεriphεral that judgment may be.  A good teacher nev,εr ceasεs to assess  studεnts,
whεthεr thosε assessments are incidental or intentional.

In  this  viεw of thesε two  concεpts, tests  ar'ε  subsεts of assεssment; thεy ar,ε
certainly  not  the  only  form  of assessmεnt that  a  teachεr can  make.  Tests  can  bε
useful  devicεs, but  they  ar,ε  only  onε among  many  procedur,εs  and  tasks  that
teach℃rs can ultimately usε to assεss students.

But now, you might be thinking, if you mak℃ assessments every timε you teach
som℃thing in  thε classroom,  doεs all  teaching  involve  assessment?  Are  tεachεrs
constantly assessing students with no  room for  interaction that is  assεssment-frεe?
The  answer  depεnds on  your  pεrspεctive.  For  optimal  learning  to  take  place,
students  must  hav,ε the  freedom  in  thε classroom  to  experiment, to  try  out their
own  hypothεsεs about  language  without  feεling that  their  ovεrail competεncε is
being  “judgεd”  in  terms  of  thos℃ trials  and  εrrors.  In  thε  samε way  that,  say,

to practice their skills with no implications for their final  placεment on that day of
days, so also must your lεarners have ample opportunities to “play" with language in

practice games of languagε learning, the opportunitiεs for learners to listen, think,

whatεver it is  that thεy are trying to S℃t in place.

At the same time, during these practice activities, teachers (and tennis coaches)

the  same  learning  community? And, in  thε idεal classroom, all  thesε observations

What  you’re  doing  when  you’re  “ coaching”  your  students  and  giving  them

On  the  other  hand,  formal  assεssmεnt  includes  exεrcises  or  procedures
