PRINCIPLES OF IANGUAGE ASSESSMENT

Whεther you are focusing on tεsting or assεssing, a finitε numbεr of principlεs can
bε named that sεrvε as guidelines for the design of a new tεst or assessment and for
evaluating the  εfficacy of an existing procedure.  I  offer five  such  principlεs hεre.
They rεprεsent a  synthesis of what various assessment  spεcialists citε as  prioritiεs
for  the  dεsign of language  assessments.  As  I  εxplain each principle, I  will  use  thε
term test here as a  genεric tεrm for both test and Joηηal assessmeηt sincε all  the
principles apply to both.

Practicality

A  good  test  is  practical.  It  is  within  thε  mεans of  financial  limitations,  timε
constraints,  easε of administration, and  scoring  and  int℃rpretation.  A  test  that  is
prohibitively εxpζnsive is  impractical.  A  test  of languagε proficiency that  takεs a
studεnt 10 hours to comp letε is impractical.  A test that rεquir'εs individual onε-to­
one  proctoring  is  impractical  for  a  group  of 500  people  and  only  a  handful  of
εxaminers‘  A test that takes a few minutes for a  student to takε and several hours
for an examiner to εvaluate is impractical for most classroom situations.  A test that

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Prin [ iples and Issues

447

can  be  scored  only  by  computer is  impractical  if thε test  takεs plac℃ a  thousand
miles  away  from  the  nearest  computer.  Thε  value  and  quality  of  a  tεst  are
dεpendent upon such nitty-gritty, practical considεrations.

The  εxtent to  which a  Iεst is  practical  somεtimεs hinges  on whethεr a  tεst is
dεsign℃d to  be  norm-referenced  or  criterion-referenced.  ln  norm-rdiεrεncεd
tests,  εach test-takεr’S scor,℃ is  intεrpr'εted in  rεlation to  a  mεan, median,  standard
dεviation, and/or  percentilε I‘  nk.  The  purpose  in  such  tests  is  to  placε test-t와εrs
along a  mathematical continuum in  rank order.  Typical of norm-referencεd tεsts arε
standardizεd tests intended to bε administer'εd to large audiεncεs, with I℃suits quickly
diss℃minated to tεst-takers.  Such tεsts must hav,ε fixed, predεterminεd responsεs in a
format  that can bε εlεctronically scannεd.  Practicality is  a primaπ issue‘

Criterion-rεfer,εnced tests,  on  thε other  hand,  are  designed  to  givε test-takers
fεedback on spεcific course or lεsson objectivεs, that is, the “ critεria.” Classroom tests
involving  smallεr numbers, and connected to  a  curriculum, are  typical of criterion
rεferenced tεsting.  Hεrε, more  time  and  effort  on  thε part  of  thε teacher  (test
administrator)  arε usually  required in order to  delivεr thε fεεdback.  One could say
that criterion-rεfεrεnced tests may, in  thε opinion of some, considεr practicality as a
secondary  issuε in  the  design  of the  tεst; teachers  may  sacrific℃ timε and  effort  in
order to offer students appropriate and useful fεεdback-thε instructional value of a
test.  Testing  (assessing)  and  tεaching  ar'ε  intεrrelatεd,  as  notεd  already  in  this
chapter.

Reliability

A reliable tεst is  consistent and dεpεndable.  A number of sourcεs of unreliability
may bε idεntified:

•  thε tεst itself (its construction), known as test reliability
•  thε administration of a test
•  thε test-taker, known as  student-related rεliability
•  the scoring of thε tεst, known as  rater (or scorεr) reliability

I  oncε participatεd in  thε administration of a  test  of aural  comprehension in
which a tape recorder playεd items for  comprehεnsion, but bεcause of street noisε
outside  the testing  room, students in the room who were sitting nex:t  to windows
wεre impaired  from  h℃aring  the  tape  accurately.  That  was  a  clear  casε of  tεst
administration  unr,εliability.  Sometimes  a  test  yields  unr,εliable rεsults because  of
factors bεyond the control of the test writ야 but within the test-takεr, such as illness,
a “ bad day,” or no slεεp the night before (student-relatεd reliability).

Sc or,εr reliability sometimεs refers to th℃ consistency of scoring by two or more
scorers, but for  classroom  teachers  and your own classroom-basεd assεssment, it’s
rare that you w퍼 havε the luxury of a sεcond scor,εr or grader.  So, how does scorer
rεliability εnter into the picturε when you are thε 0띠y scorer? Intεrestingly enough,
rεliability is  an extremεly important issuε that every teachεr has to contend with.

448

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

Bεca usε wε arε 따l human, wε can bε unr,εliable in the consistency we apply to test
evaluation.  Unclεar scoring  critεria, fatiguε, carelessness, or a  bias  toward “ good”
and  “bad”  students  can  all  play  a  part  in  our  own  unreliability.  If subjective
techniquεs are employεd in the scoring or grading of a test, reliab피ty can sufuεr.  A
test of pronunciation in which  thε scorer is  to assign a  number between  on℃ and
five  might bε unreliable if thε scoring specifications  ar,ε not clεar.  Or lεt’s say you
have 40 tests to evaluate and it’s late thε night bεfor℃ you must rεturn the tests:  You
can  bε tεmptεd toward  inconsistεnt application  of your  criteria for  judgment.  If
scoring  directions  are  clear  and  specific  as  to  thε εxact details  that  you  should
attend to, then such scoring can become reasonably consistεnt and dεpεndable.

Validity

By far thε mostcomplεx criterion of a good test is validity, the dεgrεe to which thε
test actually measures what it is intended to measure.  A valid test of reading ab피ty
is  one  that  actually  measures  reading  ability  and  not,  say,  20/20  vision,  pr,εvious
knowledge  of  a  subject,  or  some  othεr variable  of  questionable  relevance.  To
measure writing ability, one might coneεivably ask students to write as many words
as  thεy can in  15  minutes, then simply count the words for  thε final  score.  Such a
test  would  be  εasy to  administer  (practical)  and  thε scoring  quite  dependablε
(r1εliable).  But it would hardly constitutε a valid test of writing ability unless some
consideration wer1ε giv℃n to  thε communication and organization of ideas, among
other factors.  Some  hav1ε fεlt that  standard  languagε proficiency  tests, with  their
context-reduced,  CALP  (cognitive  academic  languagε  proficiency)-oriεntεd
language  and  limited  str,εtches  of discourse,  are  not  valid  mεasures of  languagε
“ proficiency” since they do not appεar to tap into thε communicative compεtεncε
of thε learner.  There  is  good  reasoning  behind such criticism; nevertheless, what
such proficiency tests lack in validity, thεy gain in practicality and rεliability.  We will
return to thε quεstion of largε-scalε proficiεncy latεr in this chaptεr.

How does one establish the validity of a  tεst? Statistical correlation with other
related  measur,εs is  a  standard mεthod.  But  ultimatεly, validity can be  εstablished
only by  observation  and  thεorεtical justification.  There  is  no  final,  absolute, and
objectivε measure  of validity.  We  hav,ε to  ask  questions  that  give  us  convincing
εvidence that  a  test  accurately  and  sufficiεntly  measures  thε test-taker  for  thε
particular objectiv,ε, or critεrion, of thε tεst.  If that εvidence is th℃re, then the test
may be said to have criterion validity.

In  tests  of language, validity  is  supported  most  convincingly  by  subs℃quεnt
personal obsεrvation by teachers and pεers.  Thε validity of a high score on the final
ex따n of a  for1εign language course will  be substantiated by “actu찌” proficiency in
the language.  A classroom tεst designed to assess masteπ of a point of grammar in
communicative use w피 hav'℃ validity if tεst scor,εs correlatε either with obsεrvεd
subsequent behavior or with other communicative measures of the grammar point
m  quest10n.

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

449

How can teachεrs be somewhat assured that a test, whether it is a standardized
test or onε constructed for classroom use, is  indeed valid? Three types of validation
are important in your role as  a classroom teach℃r: content validity, face validity, and
construct validiη.

Content validity

If a test actually samples the subject matter about which conclusions are to be
drawn, if it requires the test-taker to perform the behavior that is  being measured, it
can  claim  content  validity.  You  can  usually  determine  content  validity,
observationally, if you can clearly define the achiεvement that you are measuring.  A
test of tennis competency that asks someone to run a  I 00-yard dash lacks contεnt
validity.  If you are trying to assess a person’s  ab피ty to speak a second language in
a  conversational  setting,  a  test  that  asks  the  learner  to  answer  paper-and-pencil
multiple-choice  quεstions  requiring  grammatical  judgments  does  not  achieve
content validity.  A test that requires the learner actually to speak within some sort
of authentic context does.

In  most  human  situations,  we  are  best  tested  in  something  when  we  are
required to perform a sampling of the criterion behavior.  But ther'ε are a few highly
specialized  and  sophisticated  testing  instruments  that  do  not  have  high  content
validity yet arε nevertheless valid.  Projective personality tests are a prime example.
The  Thematic  Apperception  Test  and  the  Rorschach  “inkblot”  tests  have  little
content validity, yet thεy have been shown to be accurate in assessing ceπain types
of deviant  personality  behavior.  A  test  of field  independence  as  a  prεdiction of
language success in the classroom may have potentially good criterion validity but
poor content  validity  in  that  the  ability  to  detect  an  embedded geometric  figure
bears  little  direct  resemblance  to  the  ability  to  speak  and  hear  a  language.  As
already  noted,  standard  proficiency  tests  often  don’t  get  high  scores  on  content
validity.

Face v싫idity

A concept that is veπ closely related to content validity is face validity, which
asks  the  quεstion “ Does  the  test,  on  thε ‘face’  of  it,  appear  from  the  learner's
perspective to test what it is designed to test?”  To achieve “ peak” performance on
a  test, a learner needs to be convinced that the test is indeed testing what it claims
to  test.  Once  I  administered  a  dictation  test  and  a  doze  test  (see  below  for  a
discussion of doze tests) as a placement test for an experimental group of learners
of English as  a  second language.  Some learners were upset because such tests, on
the face  of it, did  not  appear to  them  to  test  their true  abilities  in  English.  Face
validity is almost always perceived in terms of content:  If the test samples the actual
content of what the learner has achieved or expects to achieve, then face  validity
will be perceived.

Construct v쩌idity

A  t피띠 categoπ of  validi마 that  teachers  must  be  aware  of 헝 cons띠ering
language tests is ~onstfl1얀땐U벌ty.  One way to look at construct validity is to ask the

example,  does  not  sample  oral  production,  yet  oral  produc디on is  obviously  an
important  part  of succeeding  academically  in  a  university  course  of study.  The
TOEFL'’s absence of oral production contεnt is  justified by research that has shown

the crucial  nεed to offer a  financially affordable proficiency test and the high cost
of administering  and  scoring  야al  production  tests,  the  omission  of 야al  content
from the TOEFL  has been accepted as a  necessity in the professional community.

There are othεr forms of validity that you might want to consider (see Brown,
2004,  pp.  22-2η, but  these  three  are  the  most  relevant  to  classroom-based
assessment, 따id are at the same time indispensable to your understanding of what
makes a “good” test.

Authenticity

A fourth major principle of language testing is authenticity, a concεpt that is a little
slippery to define, especially within the art and science of evaluating and designing
tests.  Bachman  &  Palmer  (1996,  p.  23)  define  authεnticity  as  “the  degree  of
correspondence of the characteristics of a  given language  test task to the features
of a  target language  task," and then suggest an agenda for  identifying  those target
language tasks and for transforming thεm into valid test items.

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

451

Essεntially, when you make a claim for authenticity in a test task, you are saying
this is a task that is  likεly to be enacted in thε “ real world.”  Many test item types fail
to simulate real-world tasks.  They may be contrived or artificial in their attempt to
target a  grammatical form  or a  lexical item.  The sequencing of items that bear no
relationship to onε another lacks authεnticity.  One does not have to look vεπ long
to  find  rεading comprehension  passages  in  proficiency  tests  that  hardly  reflect  a
real-world passage.

In a test, authenticity may be present in the following ways:

•  Thε languag,ε in the test is as natural as possible.
•  It℃ms are contextualized rather than isolated.
•  Topics and situations are interesting, enjoyable, and humorous.
•  Some thematic organization to items is provided, such as through a stoη line

or episode.

•  Tasks represent, or closely approximate, real-world tasks.

Thε authenticity of test tasks in recent years has increased noticeably.  Two or
three  decades  ago  unconnected,  boring,  contrived  items  were  accεpted  as  a
necessary by-product of testing.  Things  have  changed.  It was once assumed that
large-scale  testing  could  not  stay  within  budgetary  constraints  and  include
performance of the  productive  skills, but now many such tests offer speaking and
W디ting components.  Reading  passages  are  selected from  real-world  sourcεs that
test-takers  are  likely  to  have  encountered  or will  encounter  some  day.  Listening
comprehension sεctions feature natural language with hesitations, white noise, and
interruptions.  More  and  more tests offer items that are “episodic,” in that they are
sequenced to form meaningful units, paragraphs, or stories.

Washback

When  students  take  a  test, ideally  they will  receive  information (feedback)  about
their compεtence, based on their performance.  That feedback should “wash back"
to them in the form  of useful  diagnoses of strengths and weaknesses.  Washback
also  includes  the  effects  of an  잃sessment on  teaching  and  learning  prior  to  the
assessment itself, that is, on preparation for the assessment.  Informal assessment is
by  nature  more  likely  to  have  built-in  washback  effects,  because  the  teacher  is
usually  providing  interactive  feedback.  Formal  tests  can  also  have  positive
washback,  but  they  are  also  subject  to  an  inadvertent  absence  of  washback  if
students simply receive a letter grade or a single overall numerical score.

The  challenge  to  teachers  is  to  create  classroom  tests  that  serve  as  learning
devices  through  which  washback  is  achieved.  Students’ incorrect  responses  can
become windows of insight into further work.  Their correct responses may need
to  be  praised,  especially  when  they  represent  accomplishments  in  a  student’S
interlanguage.  Tεachers  can  suggest  strategies  for  success  as  part  of  their
“ coaching”  role.  Washback  enhances  a  number  of  basic  principles  of language

452

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

acquisition:  intrinsic  motivation,  autonomy,  self-confidence,  languagε  ego,
interlanguage, and strategic investment, among others.

One way to  enhance washback is  to comment generously and  specifically on
test performance.  Many  teachers, in our overworked (and underpaid!) lives, return
tests to students with a sin횡c letter grade or numerical score on thεm, and consider
their job done.  In  re때ity, letter grades and a score showing the numbεr of right or
wrong give absolutely no information of intrinsic interest to the student.  Grades and
scores reduce a mountain of linguistic and cognitive perform따ice data to an absurd
molehill.  At  best,  they  give  a  rεlative  indication  of  a  formulaic  judgment  of
performance  as  compared  to  others  in  the  class-which  fosters  competitive, not
cooperative, learning.

With this in mind, when you return a written test or a data sheet from an oral
production  test,  consider  giving  more  than  a  number,  grade,  or  phrasε as  your
feedback.  Even if your evaluation is not a neat little paragraph appended to the test,
at least you can respond to as many details throughout the test as time will permit.
Give  praise  for  str1εngths-the “good  stuff”-as well  as  constructive  criticism  of

weaknesses.  Give strategic hints on how a student might “nprove certain elements

of performance.  In other words, take some time to make the test performance an
intrinsically  motivating  experience  through  which  a  student  will  feel  a  sensε of
accomplishment and challenge.

A little bit of washback may also accrue to students through a specification to
the student of the numerical scores on the various subsections of the test.  A sεction
on  verb  tenses,  for  example,  that  yields  a  relatively  low  score  may  seπε the
diagnostic purpose of showing the student an area of challenge.

Washback  also  implies  that  students  have  ready  access  to  you  to  discuss  the
feedback and evaluation you have given.  I’m  sure you have known teachεrs in your
life  with  whom  you  wouldn’t  dare  “argue”  about  a  grade.  In  an  interactiv1ε,
cooperative, collaborative classroom, one could hardly promote such an atmosphere.
For  learning  to  continue, students  need  to  have  a  chance  to “feεd back" on your
feedback, to seek clarification of any issues  that are fuzzy,  and to appropriately set
new goals for themselves for the days and weeks ahead.

Washback may also imply the benefit learners expe디ence in their preparation for
a  test,  befor,ε  the  fact.  (I  like  to  facetiously  call  this  “wash  forward.'’)  By  using
appropriate strate!칭es for reviewing, synthesizing, and consolidating of material before
taking  a  test,  students  may  find  that  the  preparation  time  is  as  beneficial  as  the
feedback received after the fact.  Sometimes, preparation 따id reviewing before a test
is even more instructive than the test itselfl You C따1 be a facilitator in this process by
helping to direct yo따 students toward productive 따id relevant reviewing techniques.
You  now  have  five  basic  principles  for  designing  effective  tests  and

assessments in your classroom:

1.  Practicality
2.  Reliability
3.  Validity (content, face, and construct)

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

453

4.  Authεnticity
5.  Washback

If in  your  languagε teaching  you  can  attend  to  thεse principlεs in  evaluating  or
adapting existing  procedurεs, or in dεsigning nεW on℃S on your own, then you are
wε11 on  the  way  to  making  accuratε  judgments  about  thε  compεtencε of  the
learners with whom you are working.

KINDS OF TESTS

There are many kinds of tests, εach with aspεcific purposε and a particular critεrion
to  be  measured.  Bεlow you  will  find  d℃scriptions of five  test  types  that  are  in
common use  in language curricula.  Explanations  h℃re are only for  the purpose of
hεlping you to identify and differentiate  among  typεs, not to  sεrve as  a  manual for

Proficiency Tests

If your  aim  in  a  tεst is  to  tap  global  competence  in  a  languagε, then you  are,  in
conventional  terminology, testing proficiency.  A proficiency test is  not  int℃nded
to  be  limited  to  any  one  coursε, curriculum,  or  singlε  skill  in  thε  language.
Proficiency tests  havε tr‘aditionally  consistεd of standardized multiplε-choicε items
on  grammar,  vocabulary,  reading  comprehεnsion,  aural  comprehεnsion,  and
somεtimes a  samplε of writing.  Such tests oft℃n have content validity wεaknεsses,
as  alr1εady notεd above, but after  sev,εral dεcadεs of construct validation  research,
some grεat strid℃s havε bεεn made toward constructing communicativε proficiency

tεsts.

Typical examples of standardized proficiεncy tεsts arε thε Test  of English as a
Foreign  Language  (fOEFL ®)  and  the  Internationαl English  Language  Testiηg
System (IELTS).  Togεther, thεy ar1ε usεd by thousands of educational institutions as
an indicator of a prospective studεnt’S ability to und℃rtakε a cadεmic or prof;εssional
work in an English  medium.  Both TOEFL  and IELTS  consist of performance on all
four  skills  and  both  are  now  computer-based.  Thε  comprehεnsion  sections
(listεning and  reading)  of virtually  all  large-scale  proficiency  tests  are  computer

(speaking and writing) usually demand human scorers.

Diagnostic Tests

A  diagnostic  test  is  designεd to  diagnose  a  particular  aspect  of a  language.  A
diagnostic  test  in  pronunciation  might  hav1ε  the  purpose  of dεtεrmining which
phonological  fεatur1εs  of English  arε difficult  for  a  learner  and  should  therefore

454

[ HAPTER 23  Language Assessment:  Principles  and Issues

become a  part of a curriculum.  Usually,  such tests offer a  chεcklist of features  for

diagnostic  would  first  elicit  a  writing  sample  from  students.  Thεn the  teacher

which studεnts have acquired language featur1℃S that have already beεn taught.

Placement Tests

Cεrtain proficiency  tεsts and  diagnostic  tests  can  act  in  thε role  of placement

thereby provides an indication of thε point at which thε student will find a lεvεl or

An achievement test is rεlated directly to classroom lessons, units, or even a total
curriculum.  Achievemεnt tests  are  limited  to  particular  matεrial  covered  in  a

Aptitude Tests

Finally, wε nεεd to consider the type of tεst that is  given to a  pεrson prior to  any

languagε aptitude test is designed to measure a person’S capacity or gεneral ability

(Ml.AD  (Carroll  &  Sapon,  1958)  and  the  Pimsleur  Laηguage Aptitude  Batteη
(PLAB)  (Pimsleur,  1966).  Both  are  English  language  tests  and  require  students  to

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

455

This genre of aptitude test is seldom used today.  Instead, the mεasur,εment of
language  aptitude  has  taken the  direction  of examining the  relationship  between
more  pragmatic  and  strategic  abilities  of  would-be  language  learners  and  their
eventual  success  in  natural,  real-world  input  generation,  interaction,  and  output
performancε (Dorny1εi &  Skehan, 2003; Robinson,  2005; Skehan,  1998a).  Any  test
that claims to predict success in learning a language is undoubtedly flawed, because
we now know that with appropriate self knowledge, active strategic involvement in
learning,  and/or  strategies-basεd  instruction,  virtually  everyone  can  succeed
eventually.  (A full  discussion of language aptitude and aptitude tests can bε found
in PLLT,  Chapter 4.)

Within each of the five  categories of tests above, there are a variεty of different

possiblε techniques and procedures.  These rang,ε from

•  objective to subjective scoring procedures,
•  opεn-ended to structured response options,
•  multiple-choice to fill-in-the-blank item design formats,
•  written to oral performance modes.

Tests of each of the modes of performance can bε focused on a continuum of
linguistic  units,  from  smaller  to  larger:  phonology  and  orthography,  words,
sεntences, and  discourse.  In  interpreting  a  test  it  is  important  to  note  which
linguistic  units  are  being  tested.  Oral  production  tests  can  be  tests  of  overall
conversational  fluency  or pronunciation of a  particular subset of phonology, and
can take  the  form  of imitation, structured responses, or free  responsεs.  Similarly,
listening comprehension tests can concεntratε on a  paπicular feature  of language
or on overall listening for general meaning.  Tεsts of reading can cov1εr the range of
language units and can aim to test comprehension of long or short passages, single
sentences, or εven phrases  and  words.  Writing  tests  can  take  on  an  opεn-ended
form with freε composition, or be structured to elicit anything from correct spelling
to discourse-level competence.

IDSTORICAL DEVELOPMENTS IN IANGUAGE ASSESSMENT

Historically, language testing trends and practices have followed the changing winds
and shifting sands of methodology described earlier in this book (Chapter 2).  For
example, in  the  1950s, an  era of behaviorism and special  attention  to contrastive
analysis, testing  focused  on specific  language  elements such as  the  phonological,
grammatical, and lexical contrasts between two languages.  In the 1970s 따id 1980s,
communicative  thεories of language  brought  on  more  of an  integrativ1ε view  of
testing  in which testing  speci와ists claimed that “ the whole of the commurucattve
event was considerably greater than the sum of its linguistic elemεnts” (Clark, 1었'~!.:.>~
p. 432).  Today, test designers are st피 challenged in their quest for more authi  ·' · ·

456

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment: Principles and Issues

content-valid  instruments  that  simulate  real-world  interaction  while  st피 meeting
reliability and practicality criteria (Leung &  Lewkowicz, 2006).

깐tis historical perspective underscores two major approaches to language testing
that st피 pre때il,even 표 in mutatεd form, today: the choice between discrete-point and
integrative testing methods.  Discrete-point tests were constructed on the assumption
that language can be broken down into its component parts and those p없 adequately
tested.  Those  components  are  basically  the  s피lls  of  listening,  speaking,  reading,
writing, the V았ious hierarchical units of l때guage (phonology /graphology, morphology,
lexicon, 윈rntax, discourse) within each skill, and subcategories within those units.  So,
for g따nple, it  was  claimed  that  a  typic때 proficiency  test  with  its  sets  of m버tipl·ε­
choice  questions  divided  into grammar, vocabulary, reading, and the  likε, with some
items attending to smaller units and others to largεr units, can measure these discrete
points of language and, by adequate sam1뻐ig of these units, can achiεve v외idity.  Such
a rationale is not unreasonable if one considers types of testing theory in which certain
constructs are measured by breaking down their component parts.

The discrete-point approach met with some criticism as  we emerged into an
era of emphasizing communication, authenticity, and context.  The earliest criticism
(Oller, 1979) argued that language competence is a unified set of interacting abilities
that  cannot  be  tested  sεparately.  The  claim  was,  in  short,  that  communicative
competence is so global and requires such integration (hence the term “ integrative”
testing)  that  it  cannot be  captured in  additive  tests  of grammar and  reading  and
vocabulary and other discrete  points of language.  Others (Cziko,  1982; Savignon,
1982) soon followed in their support for integrative testing.

Just what does an integrative test look like?  Two types of test have been held
up  as  examples  of integrative  tests:  doze  tests  and  dictations.  A  doze  test  is  a
reading passage (of, say, 150 to 300 words) that has been “mutilated” by the deletion
of roughly  eveπ sixth or seventh word; thε test-taker is  required to supply words
that fit into those blanks.  John Oller (1979) daimed that doze test results are good
measures of overall proficiency.  According to theoretical constructs underlying this
daim,  the  ability  to  supply  appropriate  words  in  blanks  requires  a  number  of
abilities  that  lie  at  the  veπ heart  of  competence  in  a  l따iguage: knowledge  of
vocabulary, grammatical structure, discourse structure, reading skills and strategies,
따id an internalized “ expectancy” g없nmar (that enables one to predict an item that
will  come  next  in  a  sequence).  It  is  argued  that  successful  completion  of doze
items  taps  into  all  of those  abilities,  which  are  the  εssence of  global  language
proficiency.

The dictation is familiar to virtually all dassroom language learners.  (fhe steps
for administering a dictation are outlined in Chapter 21.)The argument for daiming
dictation  as  an  integrative  test  is  that  it  taps  into  grammatical  and  discourse
competencies  required  for  other  modes  of performance  in  a  language.  Further,
dictation  test  results  tend  to  correlate  strongly  with  other  tests  of proficien다·
Success on a dictation requires careful listening, reproduction in writing of what is
heard, efficient short-term memorγ, and, to 없1 extent, some expectancy rules to 따d

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

457

thε  short-term  memory.  Dictation  testing  remains  more  classroom-centered
because large-scale administration of dictations is  quite impractical from  a  scoring
standpoint.  Reliability  of scoring  criteria  is  also  a  problem that  is  not  present  in
multi plε-choice, εxact-word doze test scoring.

Proponεnts of integrative  test  methods  (Lowe  &  Stansfield,  1988; Oller,  1979)
soon centered their argument on what became known as the unitary trait hypothesis,
which S앵gested an “ indivisible'’view of language proficien다, namely, that vocab버aπ,
grammar, phonology, the “four skills,” and other discrete points of language cannot, in
fact,  be distinguished  from  ℃ach other.  Thε unitary trait hypothesis  contendεd that
there is a gεnεral factor of language proficien다7 such that all the discrete points do not
add up to that whole.

Others argued strongly against the unitary trait position.  For example, Hossεin
Farhady (1982) found significant and widely vaηing differences in pεrformance on
six different components of an  ESL  proficiency test, depending on subjects’ native
countη, major  field  of  study,  and  graduate  versus  under'양aduate status.  So,  for
example, Brazilians scored veη low in listening comprehension and relativ,εly high
in  reading  compr'εhεnsion.  Filipinos, whose scores on five  of the six components
of the test were considerably higher than Brazilians’ scores, were actually lower than
Brazilians in reading comprehension scores.  Farhady’s contentions wer,ε supported
in other research that  seriously  questioned the  unitaπ trait  hypothesis.  Finally, in
the face  of thε evidence, OUεr (1983b, p. 352) backed down and admitted that “ the
unitary trait hypothesis was wrong.”

CURRENT ISSUES IN LANGUAGE ASSESSMENT

In some ways, rεcent work in language  assessmεnt makes the above account seem
like  ancient history.  So  many steps have been taken to improve the construction,
delivery, and scoring of assessments that it’s difficult to capsulize them here.  From
an era not too many decades ago when virtually all tests were thought to n℃cessitate
decontextualized linguistic stimuli of dubious authenticity, we have emerged into a
new  world  of  more  communicative,  learner-centered,  performance-based
assessment.  Nevertheless,  many  challenges  remain-in  the  world  of commercial
production of language tests and in the language classroom.  Let’s  look at a few of
those highlights.

Large-Scale Language Proficiency Testing

The first  issue  that springs into the mind of most of us when “ language  testing” is
mentioned  is  our  global  obsession  over  standardized  tests,  mass  produced  by
corporations and gov1εrnment agencies, hailed as empiric따ly validated, and thought
to  provide  accurate  measures  of ability.  Are  such  tests valid? Are  they authentic?
What are the consequences for test-takers of such testing? To what use are they put?

헬

458

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

Thε accumulated  rεsearch on  languagε Iεsting  in  th℃ last  half  of  thε twentieth
cεntury producεd somε bad  news  and  some good  n℃ws for  thε daunting  task  of
largε-scale, standardiz℃d proficiency assessment.

In order to test hundreds, if not tεns of thousands, in thε casε of tests like  th℃
TOEFL®, TOEIC,  and  IELTS,  thε principlε of practicality  is  always  foremost.  In  a
world  of  commercial  competition,  costs  must  be  within  rεach  of  customεrs.
Assessing multitudes of test-takεrs (εithεr manually, in  paper-and-pεncil formats, or
in  computer갑eliv,εred  tεchnology)  requirεs intricate  procedurεs to  protect  thε
security of items and forms ‘  Tasks need to be designed to mirror language tasks of
the real world, yet allow for rapid scoring at a markεtable cost.  To  be sure, virtually
no one is  looking for the magic of a  unitary trait  in  order to  accomplish  this end.
Furthermore,  language-tεsting  spεcialists  arε not  banking  εntirεly on  a  discrete
point approach for solutions, either.  Thε crux of thε issue lies in finding ways to tap
into the communicatiνe abilities of languagε users.

Thε good  news  is  that  rεsearchers continue  to  focus  on  the  componεnts of
communicativ,ε competence in  thεir εfforts to specify the multiple  languagε traits
that must be mεasur,εd in a valid test.  Listening, speaking, rεading, and writing  ar℃
but onε dimension of a multi-trait approach to testing.  Bachman’s (1990) modεl of
communicative  language  proficiency  is  still  a  viable  template  for  designing  large
scale  tests.  Along  with  thε components  of organizational  (phonology,  grammar,
discourse)  compεtεnce, languagε tεsts of the new millennium  arε focusing  on the
pragmatic  (sociolinguistic,  functional),  stratεgic,  and  intεrpεrsonal/affiεctive
components of languagε ability (Kunnan, 2005; Leung & Lεwkowicz, 2006).

According  to  criteria  set  forth  by  Bachman  (1991),  a  communicative  tεst
needed  to  meεt some  rather  string'εnt  critεria.  It  had  to  test  for  grammatical,
discourse,  sociolinguistic,  and  illocutionary  competεnce  as  well  as  stratεgic
compεtεnce.  It had to be pragmatic in that it rεquires the  learnεr to use language
naturally for genuine communication and to relate to thoughts and fεεlings, in short,
to  put  authentic  language  to  usε within  a  context.  It  needed  to  be  direct  (as
opposεd to  indirεct tests  that  may  lose  content  validity).  And  it  should  test  thε
learnεr in  a  variεty of language  functions.  Thesε criteria  rεprεsented a  departurε
from  earlier  languag,ε  proficiεncy  tεsts  by  rεquiring  multiple  sources  of  input
(skills), task  dependency  (tasks  building  on  previous  tasks  in  thε test), and  more
emphasis on pragmatic and sociolinguistic factors than was found in previous tεsts.
At the pres℃nt time, designεrs of largε-sealε language tests are still struggling
with somε of Bachman’s criteria, but somε progrεss has bεenmadε. Tεsts such as
the TOEFL®,  in a  new incarnation in  2005, includε performancε on all  four skills,
interactive  response  techniquεs,  and  improvεd  tasks  rεquiring  pragmatic,
discourse,  and  sociolinguistic  competence  in  order  to  accurately  respond.
Computer dεliveη, scoring, and reporting technology has helpεd tεst dεsigners to
vεnture into morε complex tasks without incurring unduε costs (Jamieson, 2005).
Further, corpus linguistics has contributed to designers’ ability to  crεatε authentic

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment:  Principles and Issues

459

Further  challenges  of course  remain.  A  perple강ng and  extremely  complex
issue  in  language  assessment  globally  is  the  incr,εasing recogr피tion of varieties  of
international  English.  A  few  years  ago  one  could  be  smugly  content  to  include
among test stimuli a variety of “ accents” and consider the matter of world Englishεs
to  be  covered.  Today,  we  are  confronted  with  the  challenge  of assessing  multi
modal  skills  across  grammatical,  lexical,  discourse,  pragmatic,  and  sociolinguistic
planes-far beyond thε simple question of accεnt (Leung & Lewkowicz, 2006).  Yεt
another challenge  in  large-scale  proficiency testing  is  the  assessmεnt of language
within the specific purposes to which language will be put.  Dou밍as (2005) raises
interesting questions about how to conceiv,ε of“overall” language ability if there ar1ε
multiple  contexts  in  which  a  second  language  is  used.  Is  a  “general  purpose”
proficiency test really of much use to anyonε? Douglas argues for greater attention
to specific purposes tests.

Authenticity

A  second  and  certainly  not  unrelated  curr,εnt  issue  in  language  assεssment is
au th℃nticity.  Earlier in this chapter you werε introduced to authenticity as one of
five  major  underlying  principlεs of languagε assessment.  The  focus  in  languagε
pedagogy on communication in real-world  contεxts has spurred many attempts to
create  mor1ε  communicative  assessment  procεdures.  Lyle  Bachman’S  (1990)
seminal book was a  call for communicative testing to include all the components
interpersonal
of  communicativε  competence  (including  pragmatic  and
competence).  Current  approaches,  as  described  by  Leung  (2005)  and Alderson
and  Banerjee  (2001,  2002),  reveal  that  assessment  specialists  and  classroom
teachers are answering the call.  Important strides hav1ε been made to enable both
technicians and teachers to design authentic tests, yet even further steps are being
made as wε speak.

One of the keystones of communicative competence theories is thε intεractiv,℃
nature of language.  Usεrs of a language creatively interact with other people as well
as with texts (including test items).  Simply put, this means that tests have to involve
people  in  actually  performing  the  behavior that we want to  measure.  Paper-and
pencil  or  computεr깅리ivered multiple-choice  tests  certainly  do  not  involve  test
takers in speaking, requesting, responding, intεracting, or in combining listening and
spea따ng, or  reading  and  writing.  Interactive  testing  involves  them  in  all  of the
above rather than relying on the assumption that a good paper-and-pencil test-taker
is a  good overall language performer.

More  than  two  dεcadεs ago, Mueller (1987, p.  124)  noted  that  what  we  are
being asked to do is to “take the audacious step of making testing truly interactive:
... a  lively exchange of stimulating ideas, opinions, impressions, reactions, positions
or  attitudes.  Students  can  be  actively  involved  and  interested  paπicipants when
their task is not restricted to providing thε one and only correct answer.”

Creating authentic  tasks  within formal  assessment procedures presents some
dilemmas.  Authentic  tasks  are  often  complεx and  lack  practicality  (Alderson  &

460

CHAIπER 23  Language Assessment: Principles and Issues

Bane디ee, 2002).  They  are  difficult  to  erεate and  even  more  difficult  to  evaluate
because they oftεn involve  rεliab피ty issues.  An  oral interview can go a  long way
toward authenticity, but the possib피ty of unpredictable responses from t℃st-takers
sometimes  means  we  have  to  make  a “ judgment  call” that  could be  inconsistent.
Another problem raised by authentic assessment tasks is how to judg'ε the difficulty
of a  task, an important factor in standardized testing α.eung &  Lewkowicz, 2006).
Authentic  tasks  are  rarely  confined  to  one  simplε  level  of  difficulty  across
phonological, syntactic, discourse, and pragmatic planes.  Finally, authenticity almost
always  mεans the  integration of two  or more skills, and so  how is  an  evaluator to
judge, say, both the listening competence and the speaking competence of a learner
on an interview task? They are interdependent skills, and so  a speaking error may
actually stem from a listεning error, or vice versa.

Performance-Based Assessment

Closely  related  to  the  issue  of  authenticity  is  what  has  come  to  be  called
performance-based  assessment.  An  authentic  task  in  any  assessment  implies
that the test-taker (or classroom student) must engage in actual pεrformance of the
specified  linguistic  objective.  In  educational  settings  around  the  world,  test
designers and classroom teachers are no‘W  tackling this new agenda (Lεung, 2005).
Instead of just offering papεr-and-pencil single-answer tests of possibly hundreds of
discrete items, performance-based testing of typical school subjects involves

•  opεn-endεd problem solving tasks
•  hands-on pr1이ects
•  student portfolios
•  expεnments
•  tasks in various genres of writing
•  group projects

To  be  sure, such testing  is  time-consuming  and  therefore  expensive, but  the
losses in practicality ar'ε made up for in hi양ier validity.  Students are  testεd as they
actually  perform the  behavior itself‘  In technical terms, higher contεnt validity is
achieved as learners are measured in the process of performing the objectives of a
lesson or course.

In  the  ESL  context,  performance-based  testing  means  that  you  may  have  a
difficult time distinguishing between formal and informal testing.  If you do a little
less setting aside of formally structured tεchniques labeled as “tests” and a little more
formative  evaluation  during  students'  performance  of various  tasks,  you  will  be
taking some steps toward meeting some of the goals of performance-based testing.

Challenges from Innovative Theories of Intelligence

Intelligence was once viewed strictly as  the ab피ty to perform (a) linguistic and (b)
logical-mathematical problem solving.  This “IQ” concept of intelligence permeated
the Western world and its way of testing for almost a  centuη.  Since “smartness” in
general  is  mεasured by timed, discrete-point  tests  consisting  of many  little  items,
then  why shouldn’t  every field  of study be  so  measured? Today  we  st피 live  in a
world of standardized, norm-referenced tests that are timed, multiple-choice, tricky,
long, and artificial.

Research on intelligence by psychologists like Gardner (1983, 1999), Sternberg
(1988,  1997),  and  Goleman  (1995)  challenged  the  traditional  psychometric
orthodoxy.  Standard theories of intelligence, on which standardized IQ (and other)
tests  are  based,  were  expanded  to  include  inter- and  intrapersonal,  spatial,
kinesthetic, contextual, and emotional intelligences, among others.  (For a  summaπ
of these theories of intelligence, see PLLT, Chapter 4.)

These new conceptu외izations of intelligence infused the decade of the 1990s
with  a  sense of both freedom  and responsibility  in  our testing  agenda.  We  were
freed from exclusive reliance on timed, discrete-point, analytical tests in measuring
language.  We were liberated from the tyranny of“objectivity” and its accompanying
impersonalness.  But  we  also  assumed  the  responsibility  for  tapping  into  whole
language  skills,  learning  processes,  and  the  ability  to  negotiate  meaning.  Our
challenge was to test interpersonal, creative, communicative, interactive skills, and
in doing so, to place some trust in our subjectivity, our intuition.

Expanding “Alternatives” in Classroom-Based Assessment

Implied in some of the above discussion are two interconnected current challenges.
Performance-basεd  assessment  that  embodies  a  learner-centered,  authentic
approach  to  designing  assessment  tasks  goes  hand  in  hand  with  an  increasing
recognition (and celebration) of classroom teachers as wholly capable professionals
who  can  design  their  own  classroom-based  procedures  with  confidence.  In  the
“old” days teachers labored undεr the impr1εssion that test design must be left to the
experts and  it was  the teacher's  job  to  simply trust those experts and  administer
their tests.  There was something wrong with this picture!

Current  practice  sees  a  great  deal  of  action  by  teachers  more  and  more
involved in the creation of their own instruments and/or the w피ing adaptation of
published  tests  for  their  own  classroom  context.  In  what  has  been  termed
“classroom-based  teacher  assessment”  α.eung, 2005),  or  more  commonly  simply
classroom-based assessment, a  number of current challenges and issues merge:
authentic  assessment,  performance-based  assessment,  formative  assessment
(designed to 삶cilitatε a  student’s  continued Joηηation of language competence),
informal assεssment, and alternatives in 싫sessment.

Some references to alternatives in assessment refer to “alternative assessment,"
a  term  that  conveys  the  wrong  message, as  Brown  and  Hudson  (1998)  note.  To

462

CHAPTER  23  Language Assessment: Principles and Issues

speak of alterηative assessment  implies  something  that  is  outside  of or “exempt
from the requirements ofresponsible test construction"(Brown & Hudson, 1998,p.
65η. Instead,  alternatives  in  assessmεnt recognize  that  tests  are  one  of  many
possible  alternativ,εs within  the  superordinate  concept of assεssment.  Table  23.1

It should be noted here that traditional testing offers significantly higher levels
of  practicality.  Considerably  more  time  and  higher  institutional  budgets  are
rεquired to administer and evaluate  assessments  that presuppose more  subjective
evaluation, more  individualization, and more interaction in the process of offering
feedback.  The payoff for  the latter, however, comes with more useful washback to
studεnts, better possibilities for intrinsic motivation, and 버timately greater validity.
Wε ’ll return to the topic of alternatives in assessment in Chapter 24.

Ethical Issues: Critical Language Assessment

The first  issue described in this section, large-scale proficiεncy testing, brings with
it more than simply linguistic and psychometric challenges.  One of the by-products
of a  rapidly  growing  testing  industry  is  the  danger of an  abuse  of power.  “Tεsts
re prεsent a  social  technology  deeply  embedded  in  education,  government,  and
business; as  such they provide  the  mechanism  for  enforcing  power  and control.
Tests are most powerful as they are often the single indicators for determining the
futurε  of  individuals"  (Shohamy,  1997,  p.  2).  Test  designers,  and  the  corporate
sociop이itical  infrastructure  that  they  represent,  have  an  obligation  to  maintain
certain  standards  as  specified  by  thεir  diεnt  εducational  institutions.  These
standards  bring  with  them  certain  ethical  issues  surrounding  the “gatekeeping”
nature of “high-stakes” standardized tests.

•  Test designεrs have a  responsibility to off.εr multi plε modes of pεrformancε

to account for varying sty!εs and abilities among test-takers.

•  Tests ar,ε deeply embedded in culturε and ideology.
•  Test-takεrs are political subjεcts in a  political context.

Th esε issues  are  not  new.  More  than  a  cεntury ago,  British  educator  E  Y.
Edgeworth (1888)  challengεd the potential inaccuracy of contemporary qualifying
εxaminations for university εntrance.  But in recent years, the debate has heatεd up.
In  1997, an entire issue of the journal Languαge Testiηg was  devotεd to questions
about ethics in  languagε testing.  And  Shohamy’s  (2001)  book, The  Power of Tests,
takes a  Jong critical look at curr,ξnt issues worldwide

One  of thε problems  of critical  language  tεsting surrounds  the  widεspr℃ad
bεliεf that standardized tests designed by reputable test manufacturers are infallible
in  thεir  predictive  validity.  Univεrsities,  for  examplε, will  dεny admission  to  a
studεnt whose TOEFL®  scor,ε falls  onε point below the rεquisitε scor'ε, even though
that  student,  if  offerεd  other  m℃asures  of  languagε  ability,  might  demonstrate

A  further  problεm with our test-oriented  culturε lies  in  thε agεndas of those
who  design  and  those  who  utilize  the  tests.  Tests  arε used  in  some  countries  to
dεny citizenship (Shohamy, 2001).  Tests are by naturε culture-biasεd and therefore
may disenfranchise members of a  nonmainstream value system.  Test producers are
always in  a  position of power over test-takers and  therefor,ε can imposε social and
p이itical ideolo밍es on tεst-takers throu!감1 standards of acceptablε and unaccεptablε
items.  Tεsts  promote  thε  notion  that  answers  to  real-world  problems  hav1ε

latter is  that  tests  pr,εsume to  refl℃ct an  appropriate  core of common knowledge
and  acceptable  bεhavior; therefore  the  tεst-taker must  buy  into  such  a  systεm of
beliefs in order to make the cut.

As a language teachεr, you might be able to εX℃rcise some influεnee in thε ways

Tests have become a way of life in the educational world.  In every le따ning experience
there comes a time to pause and take stock, to put our focal processεs to their best use,
따id to demonstrate accumulated skills or knowledge.  From pop quizzes to fin외 ex없ns
to S때ldar띠zed entrance g따ns, tes잉 are crucial milestones in the journεy to succεSS.
It is unfortunate that learners 외l too often view tests as dark clouds h따1횡ng over their
heads,  upse띠ng them  with  U방ltr따ig bolts  of 따lXiety as  they  anticipate  a  h없l  of
questions they can’t  따iswer 따id, worst of 때I, a flood of disappointment if they don’t
make the grade.  Students tend to feel “ prodded, jostled, or dragged by an establishment
bent  on  spo피ng what  mi양it  otherwise  be  a  pI얹S따it  student  life”  (Muelleε 1987,
p.124).

￦ithin this atmosphere of gloom and doom, can tests be positive εxperiences?
Can they build a person’s  confidence? Can they be part of an ongoing interaction
between teacher 없id learners? Can they bring out the best in students? The answεζ
surp디singly, is 없1 encouraging yes when teachers 없id other educators understand
the benefits of tests and their place within the larger domain of assessment.

In  this  chapter, we  turn from  the  macro-issues  and challenges  of language
assessment  as  a  discipline  to  the  day-to-day  micro-issuεs  that  you  face  as  a
classroom teacher.  The discussion in this chapter is designed to briefly acquaint

NORM-REFERENCED AND CRITERION-REFERENCED TESTS

Beforε proceeding  dirεctly to  the  proc℃ss of designing  and  implementing tests  in
your classroom, a  fεw words  arε nεC℃ssaηr in order to make one more distinction.
Thε dichotomy bεtween norm- and  critεrion-r,εfεrεncεd testing is  not just anothεr
tεchnical construct that you can soon forgεt oncε you’ve read this chaptεr.  Rathεr,
thε two concepts help to rεsolv1ε many a misunderstanding that Iεachers have had.
Sometim℃S  Iεachers think  that  εveπ classroom  tεst, quiz,  or  εX℃rcise should  bε
mathematically scored, chartεd, and assign℃d pεrcεntilεs or quartiles.  Not so!  Nor
should  a  largε-scale proficiεncy tεst bε ε:xpectεd to  b℃ responsiv,ε to  somε of the
criteria demandεd of classroom tests.

In  norm-referenced tεsts, εach test-takεr’S scor1ε is  intεrpr℃ted in rεlation to
a  mean  (av,℃ragε  score),  mεdian  (middlε  scor,ε),  standard  deviation  (εxtent  of
variance  in  scores), and/or pεrc℃ntilε rank‘  Thε purposε in  such  tεsts is  to  placε
tεst-takers along  a  mathematical  continuum  in  rank  order,  and  little  else.  If test
takεrs are differentiated throu밍1 their pεrformance on the instrumεnt, the mission
of  a  norm-r1εfεrεncεd  test  has  virtually  been  accomplished.  Scores  ar1ε  usually
rεportεd back to the tεst-taker in the form of a numerical scor1ε (e.g., 230 out of 300)
and/or a perceηtile rank  (e.g., 84 percent, which  means  that the  test-takεr’S scor,℃
was bεtter than 84 pεrcent of the total number of test-takers, but bεlow 16 pεrcεnt
of  thε  test-tak℃rs  in  that  administration).  Typical  of  norm-rεferencεd  t℃sts  ar℃
standardized tests like the Scholastic Aptitude Test  (SA'η or the Test of En밍ish as a
Foreign  Language  (TOEFL®),  intεndεd to be administered to large  audiencεs, with
rεsuits  efficiently  disseminated  to  tεst-takers.  Such  tests  usually  hav1ε  fixed,
predetermined  rεsponses  in  a  format  that  can  be  quickly  scored  at  minimum
expense.  Money and efficiency  arε important concerns in thεse tests.  Thεrefore,
the principlεs of practicality, reliability, and validity are primaη.

Criterion-referenced tests, on the othεr hand, are designεd to giv1ε tεst-takεrs
fεedback on  specific  course  or  lesson  objectiv'℃s, or  the “critεria” of the  course.
Classroom achievεment tεsts involving just thε studεnts in one class, and connected
to  a  curriculum,  are  typical  of  criterion-rεfer'εncεd  tεsting‘  Her'ε,  thεI℃  is  a
significant  responsib피ty on the  part of the  teacher  (as  thε test  administrator)  to
deliver useful, appropriatε feedback  to students, or what on℃r (1979, p. 52) C외l
“instructional  value.”  In  a  criterion-refer,εncεd tεst,  thε distribution  of stud1
scores  across  a  continuum  may  be  of little  concern,  as  long  as  the  ·
assesses  appropriatε objectives.  In  th℃ following  sections of this  chapteι
εmphasis on  classroom-based  assessment  (as  opposed  to  standardized,

468

CHAPTER  24  Classroom-Based Assessment

testing),  criterion-referenced  testing  emerges  as  a  more  significant  focus.  The
principle  of  practic따ity  st피 remains  important  to  you,  but  authenticity  and
especially washback are of primary concern.

So, the next time you’re perplexed about why a large-scale test doesn’t  providε
washback, or why  students' scores  on  your  bi-weekly  quizzes  don’t  adhere  to  a
normal curve, or why it takes such a long time to evaluate your students' essays, or
why  a  multiple-choice  test  may  not  elicit  actual  language  performance  from
students  in  your  classroom,  you  might  find  answers  in  the  nature  of  criterion
referenced versus norm-referenced testing.  For the remainder of this chapter, we’u
be looking strictly at the former: tests and assessments in your classroom designed
to elicit performance on the specific critεria, or objectives, of your course.

SOME PRACTICAL STEPS TO TEST CONSTRUCTION

If you haven’t 따rεady had an occasion to create and administer a classroom test, your
time is  coming soon!  Now that you have  read about testing issues  in  this  and the
pr'εvious chapter, you may be thinking that you must now go out there and create a
wonderfully innovative instrument that will g따ner the accolades of your colleagues
and the admiration of your students.  Well, don’t  be too hard on yourself! Traditional
testing  techniques  can  be  applied  in  따1  interactive,  communicative  language
curriculum.  Your  best  tack  as  a  new teacher is  to  work within  the  guidεlines of
accepted, known tes디ng techniques, yet you can st피 give  an authεntic, interesting
flavor to your tests.  Slowly, with experience, you can get bolder in your attempts
which we’U talk about in the next section of the chapter.  In that spirit, hεrε are some
practic따 steps to take in constructing classroom tests.

1.  Test toward clear, unambiguous objectives.

In Chapters 9 and 10, you were strongly advisεd to state cleaζ specific objectives
for courses  and lessons.  Doing so enables you to focus  clearly on such objectives
when it comes to either informal or formal assessment.  Likewise, for a test that you
design, make sure that students actually perform the criterion objectives that your
test was designed to assess.  You don't want your students to say, after a test,“I don’t
see a connection between yo따 test and what we studied.”

You  need  to  know  as  specifically  as  possible  what  it  is  you  want  to  test.
Sometimes teachers give tests simply because it’s Friday or it’s the third week of thε
course; after hasty glances at the chapter(s) covered during the period, they dash off
some test items so the students will have something to do during the class period.
πlis is  no way to approach a  test!  Instead, carefully list everyπ피ng that you think
your students should “know” or be able to “do,” based on the material the students
ar,ε responsible for.

Your “objectives” can, for testing p따poses, be as simple as the following list of
grammatical structures and communicative  s따lls in a  unit that, let’s  say, you have
recentη tau양it:

4.  Revise your test.

•  Doεs the difficulty of each itεm seεm to bε appropriatε for your students?

5.  Final-edit, word-process, and print the test.

In an ideal situation, you would try out all  your Iεsts on some students before
actually  administεring them.  In  our daily  classroom  teaching, the  tπout phase  is
virtually impossibl℃, and so you must do what you can to bring to your stud℃nts an
instrument that is,  to the  best of your ability, practical, reliablε, and valid.  So, aftεr
carεful complεtion of the drafting phase, a final  εdit is in order.

In your final  editing of thε t℃st bεfore typing  it for presentation to your class,
imagine that you are  onε of your students.  Go  through each set of dirζctions and
all  items  slowly  and  delibεrately,  timing  yourself  as  you  do  so.  Oftεn  wε
underestimate the time  studεnts will  need to  completε a test.  If the test nεεds to
be shortεned or lengthεned, make the necessary a띠 ustments.  Then make surε your
test  is  neat and  unclutter1εd on the page, reflecting all  thε care  and precision you
have  put into  its  construction.  If your test  has  a  listening  component, makε sure
your script is  clεar and that the audio equipment you will  usε is in working ordεr.

6.  Utilize your feedback after administering the test.

After  you  givε the  tεst, you  W퍼 hav,ε some  information  about  how  e싫y or
difficult it was, about the time limits, and about your studεnts’  affectiv1ε rεaction to

7.  Provide ample washback.

As  you evaluate the test and return it  to your students, your kεdback should
reflect the principles of washback discussed earlier.  Use the information from  thε
test performance as a springboard for review and/or for moving on to the next unit.

TURNING EXISTING TESTS INTO MORE EFFECTIVE PROCEDURES

For many language learners, the mention of the word test evokes images of w외king
into a classroom after a sleepless night, of anxiously sitting hunched over a test page
while  a  clock ticks  ominously, and of a  mind suddenly gone  empty as they vainly
attempt  to  “ multiple  guess”  their  way  through  the  ordeal.  How  can  you,  as  a
classroom teacher and designer of your own tests, change this image?

Now that you have considered standard steps for creating a somewhat traditional
classroom  test,  let’s  go  a  little  furthεE  Consider  the  following  four  guidelines  for
converting  what  might  be  ordinary,  traditional  tests  into  authentic,  interesting,
appropriately  challen횡ng,  washback용iving  learning  opportunities  designed  for
learners’ best performance and for optimal feedback.

1.  Facilitate strategic options for test-takers.

πie first  principle  is  to  offer  your  learnεrs appropriate, useful  strategies  for
ta떠ng the  test.  With  some  prep따ation in  test-taking  strategies, learners  can  allay
some of their fears and put their best foot forward during a test.  Through strate밍es〔
based test-taking, they can avoid miscues due to the format of the test alone.  They
should also  be able  to demonstrate  their competence through an optimal  level  of
performance.  Years  ago, Merrill  Swain (1984)  referred to the principle of “ bias for
best'’ as  an important  go때 for  teachers in designing  tests.  In other words, design,
prepare, adn피피ster, and evaluate  tests in such a  way that the best performance of
your students will bε elicited! One of the ways to bias your test for best performance
is by encouraging before-, during-, and after-test options, as listed in Table 24.1.

2.  Establish face v싫idity.

Sometimes students don’t  know what is being tested when they tackle a  test.
Sometimes they feel, for a  variety of possible reasons, that a  test isn’t  testing what
they feel it was intended to test.  Face validity, as we saw in the previous chapteζ
means that in the students' perception, the test is valid.  You can help to foster that
perception with

•  a carefully constructed, well-thought-out format,
•  a test that is clearly doable within the allotted time 뼈꾀t,

Table 24.1.  Before-,  during-,  and after-test strategies

CHAPTER  24  Classroom-Based Assessment

473

Before the Test

1.  Give students all  the  information  you  can about the test.  Exactly what
will  the test cover? Which topics will  be the most impoπant? What kind
of items will  be included?  How  long will  it  be?

2.  Encourage  students  to  do  a  systematic  review  of  material.  For
example,  skim  the  textbook  and  other  material,  outline  major  points,
write down examples, etc.

3.  Give  them  practice tests or exercises,  if available.
4.  Facilitate formation  of a study group,  if  possible.
5.  Caution students to get a good  night’s  rest  before the test.
6.  Remind  students to get to the classroom early.

During the Test

1 .  As  soon  as  the test  is  distributed,  tell  students to quickly  look over the

whole test in  order to get a good grasp of its  different parts.

2.  Remind  them to mentally figure out how much  time they will  need for

each  part.

3.  Advise  them  to concentrate as  carefully as  possible.
4.  Alert students a few  minutes before the end of the class  period so that
they  can  proofread their answers,  catch  careless errors,  and still  finish
on  time.

After the Test

1.  When  you  return  the test,  include feedback on  specific  things  the stu
dent did well,  what he or she did  not do well,  and if  possible,  the rea
sons for  such  a judgment on  your part.

2.  Advise the student to pay careful attention in  class to whatever you say

about the test results.

3.  Encourage questions from  students.
4.  Advise students to make a  plan to  pay special attention  in  the future to

points that they are weak on.

•  items that are clear and uncomplicated,
•  directions that are crystal clear,
•  tasks that are familiar and relate to their course work, and
•  a difficulty level that is appropriate for your students.

[ lassroom Based Assessment

3.  Design authentic tasks.

Make sure that the languag,ε in your tεst is as natural and authεntic as possible.
Also, try to give languagε some contεxt so that items aren’t just a string of unr'εlated
language  samplεs.  Thematic  organization  of  items  may  help  in  this  regard.  Or
consider a storyline that may run throu힐1 your items.

Also,  the  tasks  themselvεs need  to  be  tasks  in  a  form  that  students  have
practiced and feεl comfortable with.  A classroom t℃st is  not thε timε to introducε
brand-nεw tasks becausε you won’t  know if student difficulty is a factor of thε task
itself or of thε languagε you are t℃sting.

4.  Work for washback.

Wash back, dεscribεd in the prεvious chaptεc is  the benefit that tests omεr to
learning.  ￦hen students  take  a  tεst, they  should  bε able,  within  a  reasonably
short period of timε, to  utilizε the information about thεir competence that test
feedback offers.  Formal tests must therefore be learning dεvices through which
students  can  rεcεive  a  diagnosis  of  arεas  of  strεngth  and  weakness.  Their
in corrεct responses  can become  windows  of insight  about  further  work.  Your
prompt return of written tests with your feedback is therefore vεry important to
intrinsic moti、ration.

One way to  enhancε washback  is  to  providε a  genεrous number of specific
commεnts  on  test  performance.  Many  teachεrs,  in  our  overworkεd  (and
undεrpaid!) lives, are in  thε habit of returning tests to students with a  letter grade
or numbεr score on thεm, and considering their job  done.  In rεality, letter grades
and a score showing the number right or wrong giv,ε absolutely no information of
intrinsic inter,εst to thε student.  Gradεs and scorεs rεduce a mountain of linguistic
and cognitive pεrformance data to an absurd minimum.  At best thεy give a  rεlativ1℃
indication  of a  formulaic  judgm℃nt of performance  as  compared  to  others  in  thε
class-which fosters competitive, not cooperative, learning.

So,  when  you  return  a  written  tεst,  or  even  a  data  sheet  from  an  oral
production tεst, consider giving more  than a  numbεr or grade  or phrase as your
feedback.  Even  if your evaluation  is  not a  neat  little  paragraph, at  least you  can
respond to as many details in thε test as time permits.  Givε praise for strengths
the “good stuff” -as well as  constructive criticism of w℃aknεsses.  Give  strategic
hints on how a student might improvε certain elemεnts of performance.  In othεr
words, take  some  timε to  makε thε tεst performance  an  intrinsically motivating
experience  through  which  a  student  will  feel  a  sensε of accomplishment  and
challenge.

Another way to increase intrinsic motivation among students is to involve thεm
in  th℃ design and  review  process (what I  called  the “wash  forward”  εffect in  the
previous  chaptεr), and  in the evaluation  of responses  afterward.  Depεnding on a
number of contε:xtual variables in your plan to 싫Sεss studεnts, pεer feedback can
be an excellent way for learners to gain washback from the tεst.

475

Finally, washback also implies that students have ready access to)ηu to discuss
the  feedback  and  evaluation  you  have  given.  I’m  sure  you  have  known  teachers
with whom you wouldn’t  dare argue about a grade.  Such a tyrannical atmosphere

themselves for the days and weeks ahεad.

ALTERNATIVES  IN ASSESSMENT

So far in this chapter, the focus has been on the administration of formal tests in thε
classroom.  It  was  noted  earlier  that  assessment  is  a  broad  term  covering  any
conscious effort on the part of a  teacher or student to draw some conclusions on
the  basis  of performance.  Tests  are  a  special  subset of the  range  of possibilities
within  assessment;  of  course  they  constitute  a  veηr salient  subset,  but  not  때l
assessment consists of tests.

In recent years language teachers hav1ε stepped up efforts to develop non-test
assessment options that are nevertheless  car·εfully designed and that adhεrε to the
criteria  for  adequate  assessment.  Sometimes  such  innovations  are  referriεd to  as
alternative  assessment, if only  to  distinguish  them  from  traditional  formal  tests.
However, as you saw in the previous  chapteζ I prefer Brown and Hudson’s  (1998)
phrase, alternatives in assessment, which emphasizes the responsibility to apply
all  assessment  principles  to  such  options,  and  not  to  treat  them  as  strange
aberrations of normal assessment practices.

Sεveral altεrnatives in  assessment  will  be  briefly discussed  here.  For a  more
detailed description and analysis of these options, I recommend that you take a look
at my companion text, Laηguage Assessmeηt (Brown, 2004), Chapter 10.

Portfolios

One  of  the  most  pop띠ar  forms  of  alternative  assessment  now  within  a
communicative  framework  is  the  construction  of  portfolios.  A  portfolio  is  “a
purposeful collection of students' work that demonstrates to students and  others
their efforts, progress, and achievements in given 따eas” (Genesee &  Upsh따, 1996,
p. 99).  Portfolios include essays, compositions, poetry, book reports, 따twork, video
or  audiotapε  re co떠ings  of  a  student’s  oral  production,  journals,  and  에m떠I{" ι
an까피ng else  one  wishes  to  specify.  In  earlier  decades  of our  histotγ, portfol
were thought to be applicable only to younger children, who assembled a poi
of aπ and  written  work  for  presentation  to  a  teacher and/or a  parent.  But'
learners of all ages and in all fields of study are benefiting from the tan횡blCj
on nature of portfolio development.

•  Speci디 to  studεnts what  thε  purposε of  the  portfolio  is  (to  emphasizε

Journals

Usually one thinks of journals simply as opportunities for lεarners to write rεlatively
fr,εely without  unduε concern for  grammaticali다. Journals  can have  a  numbεr of
purposes: language-1εarning logs; grammar discussions; responses  to  rεadings; self
assessment; and  reflections  on  attitudεs and fεεlings about  oneself.  Rεcεntly, the
assessmεnt qualities  of  journal  writing  have  assumεd an  important  role  in  thε
teaching-1εarning process.  Bεcause journal writing is  a  dialoguε between student
and teacher, journals afford a uniquε opportunity for a tεacher to offer various kinds
of feedback to learners.

Using  journals  as  assessment  instruments  requir1εs  a  car'εfully  specified,
systematic approach, using guidεlines similar to those recommendεd for portfolios:

•  Specify to studεnts what the purposε of the journal is (response to reading,

learning log, grammar commεntaη, etc.).

•  Give clear directions to students on how to gεt started (many students W피
never have written a journal before and may be mystified about what to do).
Sometimes an abbreviated model journal entη helps.

•  Give guidelines on length of each εntry and any other format expεctations.
•  Collect journals on pre-announced dates and return thεm promptly.
•  Be clear yourself on the principal purpose of the journal and make sure your

feedback speaks to that purpose.

•  Help students to procεss your kεdback, and show them how to rεspond to

your responses.

Conferences

Fora numbεr of years, confer,εnces havε beεn a routine part of language classrooms,
εspεcially  courses  in  writing.  Conferεncing  can  sεrvε  a  numbεr of  possiblε
functions; among thεm are the following:

•commεnting on drafts of εssays and reports
•  reviewing portfolios
•  responding to journals
•  advising on a student’s  plan for a paper or presεntation
•  εxploring compensatory strategiεs to overcome wεaknesses

•  setting learning goals for the near futurε

Conferεncing has bεcomε a standard part of thε procεss approach to teaching
writing, as  the  teachεr, in a  con'℃rsation about a  draft, facilitates  the  improvement
of the  written  work.  Such  intεraction has  thε advantage  of allowing  one-on-one
interaction betwεen teachεr and  studεnt such that the specific  nεeds of a  studεnt
can recεive direct feedback.  Through confiεrεnces,a tεachεr can assume the role of
a facilitator and guidε, rathεr than a  mastεr controller and dεlivεrεr of final  gradεs.
In this  intrinsically motivating  atmosphεrε, students can f℃εl that the teacher is  an
ally who is  εncouraging self-r,εRεction.  It is  important to  considεr a  conferencε as
a  dialoguε  that  is  not  to  be  gradεd.  Contεrεnces  ar'ε  by  nature  formative,  not
summative;  formative  assessmεnt  points  students  toward  further  development,
rather than offiεring a final  summation of pεrformancζ

Observations

One of the characteristics of an effective  teachεr is  the ability to  observe students
as  they perform.  Tεachεrs arε constantly  engagεd in a  process of taking  studεnts ’
performance  and  intuitively  assessing  it  and  using  those  evaluations  to  offer
feedback.  Without εvεr administering a  test  or a  quiz,  teachers know a  lot  about
their students.  In fact, experienced  teachers are  so good at  this  almost subliminal
process  of assessment  that  their  estimates  of a  student’S competence  are  often
hi양tly correlatεd with actual indεpεndently administered test scores.

On  thε other  hand,  tεachεrs’  intuitions  about  students’ performance  are  not
infalliblε,  and  certainly  both  thε rεliability  and  face  validity  of their  feedback  to
students  can  be  increased  with  the  hεlp of  empirical  mεans of  observing  their
language performance.  Observations can become systematic, planned procεdmεs for
real-timε, almost  surreptitious  rεcording of student  verbal  and  nonv1εrbal behavior.
One of thε objεctives of such observation is  to 싫Sεss students as  much as  possible
without their awareness (and possible consεquεntan없ety) of the observation, so that
the naturalness of thεir linguistic pεrformance will be maximized.  Chεcklists, charts,

[ Jassroom-Based Assessment

rating sealεs, systematic notε taking, and tεachεrs’ journals can all help to support our

What  kinds  of studεnt pεrformancε can be  thε subject of such observations?

Consider a few  possibilitiεs:

•  sent℃ncε-levεl oral production
•  interaction with classmates (coopεration, frεquεncy of oral production)
•  frequεncy of student-initiated responsεs (whol℃ class, group work)
•  quality of tεacher-elicited rεsponsεs
•  evidεncε of listening compr'εhension (questions, clarifications, attention

giving verbal and nonv,εrbal behavior)

•  εvidence of attention span issues, learning style preferences, εtc.
•  usε of stratεgic options in comprehension or production (use of

communication strategies, avoidancε, εtc.)

•  culturally specific linguistic and nonverbal factors (kinesics, proxemics, use

of humor, slang, mεtaphor, etc.)

In ordεr to carry out classroom observation, it is of course important to be clear
about why you arε observing, what you are obsεrving, how you will  obsεrve (what
system you will  usε), and how you will  conv'εy your perceptions to your studεnts.
Checklists and grids ar,ε a common form of rεcording observεd behavior.  In Spada
and Frohlich’s (1995) systεm, grids capture such variablεs as whole-class, group, and
individual performancε; formal versus functional errors; and which onε of thε four
skills  is  involv,℃d.  The  observer  identifies  performance  and  chεcks appropriate
boxes in the grid.  Checklists neεd not be that elaborate.  Simplεr options-noting
occurrences of student errors in cεrtain grammatical categories, or “ action zonεs” in
thε classroom, for example-may bε more realistic.

Rating scalεs have also been suggested for recording observations.  Onε typε of
rating  scale  asks  teachεrs  to  indicatε  the  frεquency  of  occurrence  of  target
pεrformance eithζr on a  sεparate frequencγ scale (always  =  5; never=  1).  Rating
scales  may  bε appropriate  for  recording  obsεrvations after the fact-on  thε same
day but after a class pεriod,for εxample.  Spεcific quantitiεs of occurrences may be
difficult to rεcord while teaching a lesson and managing a classroom, but immediate
subsεquεnt evaluations  might  offiεr some  data  on  observations  that  will  certainly
fade from mεmotγ by the next day or so.

You  will  probably  find  moderate  practicality  and  reliability  in  obsεrvations,
εspecially if the  objectives  are  kept  simple.  Face  validity  and  contεnt validity  ar,ε
likely  to  get  high  marks  sincε observations  are  likely  to  be  intεgrated into  th℃
ongoing process of a  course.  Authenticity is  hi양1 bεca usε, if an observation goes
relatively  unnoticed  by  the  student,  then  ther1℃ is  little  likelihood  of contrived
situations.  Washback can be high if you takε the time and effort to help students to
becomε a warε of your data on their performance.

Acom℃ntional view of language pedagogy might consider sεIf- and peer-assεssment
to  be  an  absurd  rεvεrsal of thε tεaching-learning process.  After  all,  how  could
learners who ar℃ still in thε process of acquisition, espεcially thε early processes, be
capablε of rεndεring an accurate assessmεnt of their own performancε? But a dosεr
look at thε acquisition of any skill rεveals thε importance, if not thε necessity, of self
assessment  and  the  benefit  of pε℃r-assεssment.  What  successful  learner  has  not
developed the ability  to monitor his  or hεr own  performancε and  to  us℃ the data
gatherεd for  adjustmεnts and corrections?  Successful  lεarnεrs extend the  learning
process  well  beyond  the  classroom  and  thε  prεsεncε  of  a  tεachεr  or  tutor,
autonomously mastering  thε art of self-assεssment.  And where p℃℃rs arε availablε
to rεndεr assessmεnts, why not take advantage of such additional input?

Rεsεarch has  shown  (Alderson  &  Bane디εε, 2001;  Brown  &  Hudson,  1998;
0’Mallεy &  Piεrcε, 1996)  a  number  of  advantages  of  sεIf- and  pεεr-assessmεnt:
spεεd,  direct  involvement  of  students,  thε  encouragεment  of  autonomy,  and
incrεased motivation  because  of self involvement  in  thε process  of learning.  Of
coursε,  the  disadvantage  of  subjectivity  looms  large,  and  must  bε  considerεd
whεnever you propose to involve  studεnts in self- and pεεr-assεssmεnt.
