CHAPTER  24  Classroom-Based Assessment

SCRUTINIZING THE ALTERNATIVES

As  you consider using some of the  alternativ1εs that have just been described, two
factors  deserve  your  attention  and  application:  maximizing  practicality  and
reliability  in  your  procedures,  and  focusing  on  your  students'  actual  language

performancε.

Maximizing Practicality and Reliability

The  classroom-basεd tεS잉 described  in  the  first  p따t of t피s chapter, as  well as  the
alternatives in assessment, are contextualized to a specific curriculum, are referenced
to  criteria  ( objec디ves)  of  a  course  or  module,  offer  greater  potential  for  both
authenticity and  washback, and  in the  case  of the “alternatives,”  are  open-ended  in
their response format and timε orientation.  This is a veπ different picture from large
scale standardized tests structured for computer delivery and rapid machine scoring.
One way of loo셈ng at this contrast poses a rather tantalizing challenge to you
as  a  teacher and test designer.  Formal standardized tests are  almost  by definition
highly  practical,  reliable  instruments.  They  are  designed  to  minimize  time  따id
money on the part of test designer and test-taker, and to be painstakingly accurate
in their scoring.  Alternatives such as  portfolios, or conferencing with students on
drafts of written work, or observations of learners over time all require considerable
time and effort on the part of the teacher 없id the student.  Even more time must be
spent if the teacher hopes to offer an evaluation that is  reliable-within students
across time, as well as across students (taking care not to favor one student or group
of students).  But  the  latter techniques  also  offer markedly  greater washback, are
superior formative measures, and, because of their authenticity, usually carry greater
face validity.

Does  this  mean  that  multiplε-choice  formats  are  inevitably  devoid  of
authenticity and washback? And  that  thε more  open-ended, long-term alternatives
are by nature unreliable and will forever cost teachers sleepless ni앙its? I hope not.
The  challenge  that  faces  us  all  as  conscientious  teachers  and  assessors  in  our
profession  is  to  change  this  perception.  With  some  creati껴W and  effort, we  can
transform otherwise inauthentic and negative-washback-producing tests into more
pedagogically fulfilling learning experiences by doing the following:

•  building as much authenticity as possible into multiple-choice task typεs and

items

•  designing classroom tests that have both objective-scoring sections and

open-ended response sections: varying the performance tasks

•  turning multiple-choice test results into diagnostic feedback on areas of

needed improvement

•  maximizing the preparation period before a test to elicit performance

relevant to the ultimate criteria of the test

•  teaching test-taking strategies

CHAIηFR 24  Classroom-Based Assessment

481

•  helping students to see beyond the test: don’t “ teach to the tεst”
•  triangulating  information on a  student before making  a  final  assessment of

com petεnee

The  flip  side  of  this  challenge  is  to  understand  that  the  alternatives  in
assessment are  not by nature destined to flounder in a quagmire of impracticality
and unreliability.  As  we look at alternatives in assessment in this chapter, we must
be mindful of Brown and Hudson’s (1998) admonition to scrutinizε the practicality,
reliab피ty, and validity of those alternatives at the same time that we celebrate their
face validity, washback potential, and authenticity.  It is easy to fly out of the cage of
traditional testing rubrics, but it is tempting in doing so to flap our wings aimlessly
in  accepting  virtually  any  classroom  activity  as  a  viable  alternative.  Assessments
that  are  proposεd to  serve  as  tri따1gulating  measures  of  competence  imply  a
responsibility to be rigorous in determining objectives, response modes, and criteria
for evaluation and interpretation.

Performance-Based Assessment

A second interesting factor at play in the alternatives in assessment is a recognition
of the importance of performance-based assessment, sometimes merely called
performance  assessment  (Leung  &  Lewkowicz,  2006).  η1e push  toward  mor℃
performancε-based assessment  is  part  of  the  same  general  educational  reform
movement that has raised strong objections to using standardized test scores as the
only measures of student competencies σζohn, 2000).  The argument, as  you  can
guess, is  that standardized tests, to a large extent, do not elicit actual performance
on the paπ of test-takers.

Performance-based  assessment  implies  productive,  observable  skills,  such  as
speaking  and  writing,  of content-valid  tasks.  Such  performance  usually,  but  not
always, brings with it an air of authenticity-real-world tasks that students have had
time to develop.  They often imply an integration of language s힘Us, perhaps 쇠l four
skills  in  the  case  of portfolios  따1d conferencing.  Because  the tasks  that  students
perform are consistent with course goals 따1d curriculum, students and teachers are
likely to be more motivated to perform them, as opposed to a set of multiple-choice
questions about grammaticality or reading comprehension.

0’M때ley and Valdez-Pier℃e (1996, p. 5) consider℃d performance-based assessment

to be a subset of authentic assessment, with the following characteristics:

1.  Students make a constructed response.
2.  까1ey engage in higher-on짜·· thinking, with open-ended tasks.
3.  Tasks are meaningful, eη!gagiη!g, and authentic.
4.  Tasks call for the integration of language skills.
5.  Both process aηd product are assessed.
6.  The depth of a student’s  masteπ is emphasized over breadth.

482

CHAPπR 24  Classroom-Based Assessment

Performance-based  assessment  needs  to  bε  approached  with  care.  It  is
tempting  for  teachers  to  잃sume that  if a  student  is  doing  something,  then  the
process has fulfilled its own goal, and the evaluator needs only to make a checkmark
in  the  grade  book  next  to  a  particular  competency.  In  reality,  performances  as
assessment  procedures  need to be treated with the  S없ne rigor as  traditional  tests.
This implies that teachers should:

•  state the overall goal of the performance
•  speciπ the objectives (criteria) of the performance in detail
•  prepare students for performance in stepwise progressions
•  use a reliable evaluation form, checklist, or rating sheet
•  treat  performances  as  opportunities  for  giving  feedback  and  provide  that

fξedback systematic외ly

•  if possible, utilize self- and peer-assessments judiciously

To  sum  up, perform없ice assessmεnt is  not  completely synonymous with the
concept of alternative assessment.  Rather, it is best understood as one of the primary
traits of the many alternatives in assessment that are available to us.

*

It’s  quite  obvious  by  now,  I  hope, that  assessment  is  an  int℃gral part  of the
teaching-learning cycle.  In 따l interactive, communicative curriculum, assessment is
almost  constant.  Tests, as  a  subset  of all  assessment  processes, do  not  nεcessarily
need  to  violate  principles  of  authenticity,  intrinsic  motivation,  and  student
centeredness.  Along  with  some  newer  alternatives  in  assessment,  tests  become
indispensable components of a curriculum.

As  a  reminder  of the  valuε of assessment  in  the  classroom,  remember  that

assessment and teaching are partners in the learning process:

•  Periodic assessments, both formal and informal, can increase motivation as

they seπe as milestones of student pr'앵ress.

•  Assessments encourage retention of information through the feedback they

give on learners' competence.

•  Assessments can provide a sense of periodic closure to various units and

modules of a curriculum.

•  Assessments can encourage students' self-evaluation of their proi갖ess, 없id

spur them to 똥t go외s for themselves.

•  Assessments C따I promote student autonomy as they confirm areas of

strength and areas needing further work.

•  Assessments can aid in evaluating teaching effectiveness.

One of the most invigorating things about teaching is  that you never stop learning.
The  complexity  of the  dynamic  triangular  interplay among  teachers  and  learners
and  subjεct mattεr continually  gives  birth  to  an  εndless number of questions  to
answεc problems to solve, issues to ponder.  Every time you walk into a  classroom
to teach, you face  some of those  issues, and if you’re  a  growing teacher, you  learn
something nεw yourself.  You find out how well a technique works, how a student
procεsses language, how classroom  intεraction can  be  improv,εd, how to assess  a
student’s competence, how emotions entεr into lεarning, or how your teaching style
affects  lεarn℃rs.  The discoveries go on and on-for a  lifetimε.

As  you embark on this journey into the teaching profiεssion, how can you bεst
continue to grow professionally?  How can you most fruitfully  meet the challenges
that lie ahead? Are thεrε some practical goals that you can pursue? So far, as you havε
worked through the material of this book, you have already begun your own teacher
dεvelopment.  Richards  and  Farrell  (2005,  pp.  6-7)  frame  this  dev'εlopmεntal
process in terms of four conceptualizations of teacher learning:

1.  Skill learning.  Teachers develop a range of the basic skills (such as those
addressed in this book) of designing lessons, managing classrooms, assessing
performance, εtc.

2.  Cognitive process.  Teachers' background, experience, knowledg,ε, and
beliefs all contribute to an underlying sεt of cognitiv'ε assumptions about
languag,ε lεarning and teaching.

3.  Personal construction.  Teachers' knowledgε is actively constructed (not

passively received) in an ongoing process of reorganization and
reconstruction as new learning and experiences form a personal framework.

486

CHAPTER  25  Teacher  Development  487

4.  Reflective praαice. Teachers can bεnefit greatly from focused reflection

and critical examination of their own teaching experiences, which then lead
to improvement and further dev,℃lopment.

One of the major themes that continu℃S to be expressed in current research
on  teacher  developmεnt  is  a  constructivist  approach  to  development  that
highlights the active and responsible  role  of teachers to forge  their own personal
frameworks  within  their  respective  situatεd  contεxts (Bailey,  Curtis,  &  Nunan,
2001;  Borg,  2003b;  Freeman,  2002;  Hεdgcock, 2002; Johnson,  2006;  Mann,  2005;
Mullock, 2006; Richards  &  Farrell, 2005).  Teachεrs are trained and continue their
training in what Hedgcock (2002, p. 301) described as “communities of practice”
in  which  teachers  of  varying  degrεes  of  experience  carry  out  thεir  roles  as
practicing  technicians  who  learn  from  εach other.  We  best  fulf피 thε goal  of
professional  development  not  through  a  “transmission"  model  of  education  in
which  knowledge  is  simply  depositεd into  the  brains  of teachers, but through  a
process  model  in  which  teachers  lεarn and  continue  to  develop  their  skill  in
dialogue  with  a  professional  community  Oohnson,  2006).  If you  are  a  novice
teacher reading and studying this book, your dialogue is  just beginning.  You have
perhaps begun to move through the first  two (skill learning, cognitive process) of
the  four  elements  of tεacher development  cited  by Richards  and  Farrell  (2005).
Continuing  to  strengthen  those  two  factors  and  moving  into  stages  of personal
construction and reflective  practice  is  a  process  that will  demand your patience
and perseverance.  Don’t  expect to become a “ master” teacher ov,εmight!

Right  now,  as  you  begin  your  teaching  career,  you  can  set  some  r℃alistic,
practical goals  that you can focus  on without being overwhelmed by ev,εηrt:hing
you have to attεnd to when you teach.  Just as beginning language learners are in
a controlled modε of operation, able to manag'ε only a few bits of information at
a  time  with  capacity-limited  systems,  so  it  is  with  your  teaching.  If you  try  to
focus  on  everything  in  the  classroom  (the  management  issues,  techniques,
delivery,  body  language,  feedback,  individual  attention,  lesson  goals,  mid-lesson
alterations, etc.) all at once, you may end up doing nothing well.  In due course of
time, however, dealing with the abundance of cognitive/emotional phenomena in
the  classroom  will  be  sufficiently  automatic  that  you  will  indeed  manage  to
operate on many planes simultaneously.

As you read on here, you w피 find some ideas that you can immediately put to

work and others that may apply to you after you have gained some experience.

PEAK PERFORMERS

Are you doing the best you can do? Are you “bεing all that you can be,” in a process
of self actualization, in Maslow’s terms? Or are you satisfied with getting by? In the
stressful (but rewarding) world of teaching, it’s easier than you might imagine to slip
into a pattern of just keeping a step ahead of your students as you stru잃le through

488

CHAPTER 25  Teacher Development

long working hours and cope with largε classes.  This pattern is the bεginning of a
downward spiral that you should avoid at all costs.  How do you do that? In part by
practicing the bεhaviors of peak pεrformers, peoplε who arε rεaching their fullest
potεntial  and  therefore  who,  in  turn,  rεap succεss.  Consider  thε following  five
maxims (among many) of peak performers (adapted from Covey, 1990, and othεrs)
that you might apply to yourself, even at this early stage in your carεer:

1.  Believe in yourself.

Teaching  is  no  εasy profession‘  It  rεquires dεεp dedication,  a  willingness  to
work long hours, a genuine dεsire to help other pεople, a commitment somεtimεs to
“ walk  the  second  mile"  in  facilitating  students’  bεst performancε, cognizance  of a
professional  cor,ε of knowledge, an  ab피ty to  be “ on tap” in front  of students many
hours in a day, and more.  Are you up to the (possibly) daunting prospect of bεing a
teacher? Almost every “formula ” for succεSS bε양ns with the importance of bellεving
that you 았ε fully  capable of undertaking the task(s) at hand.  So, at the outset, you
neεd to be convinced that you can indeed be a teacher and bε an excellent one!

2.  Set realistic goals.

P℃ak performers know thεir limitations and  str1εngths and their fεelings and
neεds, and  then  set goals  that  will  bε realistic  within  this framework.  Thεy S℃t
their own goals and don’t  lεt the world around thεm (collεagues, supervisors, or
friends)  dictatε goals  to  them.  If you  have  a  sεnsε of overall  purposε in  your
careεr as  a  mission,  then  this  mission  will  unfold  in  the  form  of daily,  wεekly,
monthly, or annual goals.

It’s always a good idεa to writε down some short-tεrm and long-term go때s.  Be
realistic in terms of what you can accomplish.  Bε specific in your statεmεnts.  Here
are somε exam plεs to gεI thewhεεls turning.

•  Rεadxnumbεr of tεacher resourcε books this yεar.
•  Design  my  n℃xt test  to  be  mor,ε authentic, biased for  best, with maximum

wash back‘

•  Observe five other tεachers this semester.
•  Monitor my error treatments in the classroom.
•  Attend two professional confer,εnces/workshops this year.

3.  Set priorities.

h ’s crucial that you hav1ε a S℃nse of what is most k때아tant, what is least import없lt,
and everything in bεtwεen in your profession외 goals and tasks.  If you don’t, you C떠1
end up  spending  too  much  time  on low-priority  tasks  that  rob you of thε time  you
should be spen이ngon 피항1℃r priorities.  Priority-sεtting rεquires a sense of your wholε
pro tiεssional and pεrsonal life, and how you 앙C going to use your waking hours.

4.  Take risks.

Peak performers don’t  play it safε all  the time.  They are not afraid to tπ nεw
things.  Nor are they put off by limiting circumstancεs: what cannot bε done, or “ the

CHAPπR 25  Teacher Development  489

way” things are done.  They don’t  파iger in the safety of a “comfort zone”; instead,
they reach out for new challenges.

The key to risk taking as a peak performance strategy, howev,℃r, is not simply in
taking the risks.  It is in learning from your“failures.”  When you risk a nεw techniquε
in the classroom, try a new approach to a difficult student, or make a frank comment
toasupeπisor, you must be willing to accept possible “failure ” in your attempt.  Then
you assess all thε facets 。f that failure and turn it into 때 experience that teaches you
something about how to calculate the next risk.

5.  Reduce and man쟁e stress factors.

Contrary to some perceptions from outside our profession, teaching is a career
with  all  the  makings  for  hi화i-stress conditions.  Think  of somε of the  sourcεs of
stress in this business: long hours, large classes, low pay, pressure to “perform” in the
classroom, high student expectations, professional demands outside the classroom,
emotional connections with students' lives, bureaucracies, pressure to keep up with
a  rapidly  changing  field,  information  overload.  Managing  those  potential  stress
factors is an import따it key to keeping yourself fresh, creative, bri양it, and happy.

One of the cardinal rules of stress management is setting priorities, which has
already  been  d℃alt  with  above.  Another  rule  was  also  touched  on:  Know  your
limitations.  Other rules follow-don’t  take on too many extra duties; take  time for
yourself; and balance your personal and  professional time.  Peak performers don’t
spend 18 hours a day working.  They don’t  get so consumed with their profession
that the rest of thεir life is a shambles.  Thεy work hard but stop to play.  They know
how to relax, and do so reg버arly.  And they develop fulfilling personal rεlationships
with family and friends that provide enrichment and renewal.

As  you  begin  a  teaching  career, you  may feel  the  wei방it  of heavy demands.
And  tεaching is  not one of those  careers where you can necessarily leave  all  the
cognitive and εmotional load in the office.  So, you can expect to be thε proverbial
overworked  and  underpaid  laborer.  But  in  the  midst  of  those  demands,  σy to
balance your lmε, and take everything in perspective.

Table 25.1.  Characteristics of a good  language teacher

GOOD LANGUAGE-TEACHING CHARACTERISTICS

Technical  Knowledge

1.  Understands the  linguistic systems  of English  phonology,  grammar,  and  discourse.
2.  Comprehensively grasps  basic principles of language  learning and teaching.
3.  Has fluent competence  in speaking,  writing,  listening to,  and  reading  English.
4.  Knows  through  experience what it is  like to  learn  a foreign  language.
5.  Understands the close connection  between  language and culture.
6.  Keeps  up  with the  field  through  regular reading and  conference/workshop atten

dance.

Pedagogical  Skills

7.  Has  a well-thought-out,  informed approach  to language teaching.
8.  Efficiently designs and  executes  lesson  plans.
9.  Understands and  appropriately uses  a variety of techniques.

10.  Monitors  lessons  as  they  unfold and  makes effective mid-lesson alterations.
11.  Effectively perceives students'  linguistic and  personal  needs,  along with  their various

styles,  preferences,  strengths,  and  weaknesses.

12.  Gives optimal  feedback  to  students.
13.  Stimulates  interaction,  cooperation,  and  teamwork  in  the classroom.
14.  Uses  appropriate principles of classroom  management.
15.  Uses  effective,  clear presentation  skills.
16.  Creatively adapts textbook  material  and other audio,  visual,  and  mechanical  aids.
17.
18.  Uses  interactive,  intrinsically motivating techniques to create effective tests.

Innovatively creates  brand-new materials when  needed.

Is  aware of cross-cultural  differences and  is  sensitive to  students'  cultural  traditions.

Interpersonal Skills
19.
20.  Enjoys  people;  shows  enthusiasm,  warmth,  rapport,  and  appropriate humor.
21.  Values  the opinions and  abilities of students.
Is  patient in  working with students of lesser ability.
22.
23.  Offers challenges to  students  of exceptionally high ability.
24.  Cooperates  harmoniously and  candidly with colleagues,  including seeking opportuni-

ties  to share thoughts,  ideas,  and  techniques.

Personal Qualities
25.
26.
27.  Engages  in  regular reflection  on one's  own teaching practice and strives  to  learn from

Is  well-organized,  conscientious  in meeting commitments,  and dependable.
Is  flexible when  things go awry.

those reflective practices.

28.  Maintains an  inquisitive mind  in trying out new ways of teaching.
29.  Sets  short-term  and  long-term goals for continued professional  growth.
30.  Maintains and exemplifies  high ethical  and moral  standards.

CHAPTER  25  Teacher  Development

Figure  25.2.  Teacher  self-observation form  (adapted  from  Christison  &  Bassano,  1984)

Thoughtfully consider each  statement.  Rate  yourself in  the following way:

3 = Excellent

2 = Good

1 = Needs  Improvement

0 = Not Applicable

Write  your  ratings  in  the  blanks.  When  you've  finished,  give  overall  consideration  to  the
various  areas.

I.  Learning Environment

A.  Relationship to Students
_

1.  I establish  good  eye  contact with  my  class.  I do  not talk over their heads,  to  the

chalkboard,  or to just one person.

2.  If I tend  to  teach  predominantly to  one  area  of the classroom,  I am  aware of this.  I

make a conscious effort at all  times to pay attention to  all  students  equally.

_

3.  I divide my  students  into small  groups  in  an  organized  and  principled  manner.  I
recognize that these groups should differ in size and composition, varying with the
objective of the group activity.

B.  The  Classroom
_
_
_

1.  If possible,  I arrange the  seating  in  my class to suit the  class  activity for the day.
2.  I consider the  physical  comfort of the  room,  such  as  heat and  light.
3.  When  I need  special  materials or equipment,  I have them  set  up before the class

begins.

C.  Presentation
_

1.  My handwriting on  the  chalkboard  and  charts  is  legible from  all  locations  in  the
classroom.  It is  large enough  to accommodate students with vision  impairments.
2.  I  speak  loudly enough  to  be  heard  in  떠| parts  of the  classroom,  and  I enunciate

clearly.

3.  I vary the exercises  in  class,  alternating rapid and  slow-paced activities to keep  up

the  maximum  interest in  the class.

4.  I  am  prepared  to  give  a  variety  of explanations,  models,  or  descriptions  for  all

students.

5.  I help the  students  form  working principles and generalizations.
6.  Students use  new skills or concepts  long enough so that they are retained and thus

future application  is  possible.

7.  I plan  for “thinking time” for  my students  so  they can  organize their thoughts and

plan  what they are  going to say  or do.

_

_

_

_
_

_

D.  Culture and Adjustment
_
_

1.  I am  aware that cultural  differences affect the  learning situation.
2.  I  keep  the  cultural  background(s)  of  my  students  in  mind  when  planning  daily
activities  and  am  aware  of cultural  misunderstandings  that  might  arise  from  the
activities  I choose.

3.  I promote an  atmosphere of understanding and  mutual  respect.

CHAPTER  25  Teacher  Development

497

II.  The  Individuals

A.  Physi [ al Health

1.  I know which students  have  visual  or aural  impairments and  seat them  as  close  to

my usual  teaching positions  as  possible.

2.  I am  aware  that  a  student's  attention  span  varies  from  day  to  day,  depending  on
mental  and  physical  health  and  outside distractions.  I pace  my  class  activities  to
accommodate the strengths.  I don't continue with an  activity that  may exhaust or
bore them.
I begin  my class  with a simple activity to wake students  up  and  get them  working
together.

3.

4.  I  am  sensitive  to  individual  students  who  have  bad  days.  I don't  press  a  student

5.
6.

who  is  incapable of performing at the  usual  level.
I try  to challenge  students  who are  at their best.
If I am  having a bad day and  feel  it might affect my  normal  teaching style,  I let my
students  know it so  there  is  no misunderstanding about my feelings  for them.

_

_

_

B.  Self Concepts

1 .  I treat my students  with  the same  respect that  I expect them  to show me.
2 ‘

I plan “one-centered”  activities that give all  students an  opportunity at some point
to  feel  important and  accepted.

3.  I like to teach  and  have  a good time teaching-on most days.

C.  Aptitude and  Perception

1.  I am  aware that  my students  learn  differently.  Some  students  are  visual-receptive,

some are  motor-receptive,  and others  are  audio-receptive.

2.  My exercises  are  varied;  some  are  visual,  aural,  oral,  and  kinesthetic.  I  provide
models,  examples,  and  experiences to  maximize learning  in  each  of these  areas.
3.  I know basic concepts  in the memory process.  When applicable,  I use association

to  aid students  in  rapid  skills  acquisition.

D.  Reinforcement
_
_

1.  I tell  students when they have done well, but I don't let praise become mechanical.
2.  I  finish  my  class  period  in  a  way  that  will  review  the  new  concepts  presented
during  the  class  period.  My  students  can  immediately  evaluate  their  under
standing of those  concepts.

_
_

3.  My tests  are  well-planned and -produced.
4.  I make  my system  of grading clear  to  my students  so  that  there  are  no  misunder

standings  of expectations.

E.  Development

1.  I  keep  up  to  date  on  new techniques  in  the  ESL  profession  by  attending  confer
ences  and  workshops  and  by reading  pertinent professional  articles  and books.
2.  I realize that there  is  no one  right way  to  present a  lesson.  I try  new ideas  where

_

and  when  they seem  appropriate.

3.  I observe other ESL  teachers  so  that I can  get other ideas  and  compare them to  my

own teaching style.  I want to have  several  ideas  for teaching one concept.

川. The Activity

A.  Interaction
_
_

1.  I minimize  my  role  in  conducting the activities.
2.  I organize the activities so they  are suitable for  real  interactions among students.
3.  The  activities  maximize student involvement.
4.  The  activities  promote spontaneity or experimentation  on  the  part of the  learner.
5.  The  activities  generally  transfer  attention  away  from  “self”  and  outward  toward  a

“task.”

6.  The activities are organized to ensure a high  success rate,  leaving enough room for

amount of correction for  the activity.

_
_

1 .  The activity  is  focused.
2.  The  content of the  skill  presented  will  be  easily  transferrable  for  use  outside  the

class.

_
_

3.  The activity  is  geared to  the  proficiency  level  of  my  class or slightly  beyond.
4.  The  content of the activity  is  not too sophisticated for  my  students.
5.  I make the content of the activity  relevant and  meaningful  to  my  students'  world.

CIASSROOM-BASED “ACTION” RESEARCH

Research is a scary word for many of us.  We  arε happy to leave it in someone else’s
hands because it involves statistics (which we hate), experimental design (which we
don’t  know), and the  interpretation of ambiguous  results  (which wε think is  best
left to the “experts”).  Even so, leaving all thε research in the hands of researchers is
an upside깅own policy, as Anne  Meek (1991, p. 34)  noted: “The  main  thing wrong
with the world of education is that there’s this one group of people who do it-the
teachers-and  then  there’S  another  group  who  think  they  know  about  it-the
researchers.  The group who think they know about teaching tπ to find out more
about it in order to tell thε teachers about teaching-and that is total reversal.” She
goes  on  to  stress  the  importance  of teachers  doing  their  own  research  in  thεir
classrooms.

Actually, research doesn’t have to be a scary prospect at 따l.  You’re researching
ideas all the time, whether you know it or not.  If, as a growing teacher, you have as
a  go때 to  improve  the  quality  of your  teaching,  then  you’U ask  some  relevant
questions, hypothesize  some possible  answers  or solutions, put the solutions to a
practical tryout in the classroom, look for certain results, and weigh those results in
some  manner  to  determine  whether your  hypothesized  answer  held  up.  Such  a
process is q버te formal in following  classic steps for experimental research.  It may
not be a habitual practice for you.

A good deal of classroom research is  an informal, everyday occurrence.  You
divide up small groups in a different way to stimulate a better exchange of ideas; you

[ HAPTER 25  Teacher  Development

499

modify  your  usual  nondirective  approach to  gεtting students  to  study  hardεr and
takε a  bold,  dirεct, no-nonsensε approach; you  try  a  videotapε as  a  convεrsation
stimulus; you  try  a  deductive  approach  to  prεsenting a  grammar point  instead  of
your usual  inductivε approach ‘  0thεr classroom  research  may be  mor,ε of a  long
term process that covεrs a term or mor,ε.  In this mode, still in an informal mannεc

’'OU  may  try  out  some  learner  stratεgy training  techniques  to  sεε if students  do

bεttεr at  con\℃rsation skills; you may do a daily  thr'εe-minute pronunciation drill to
see if studεnts ’  pronunciation improves; you may assign specific εxtra-class rεading
to sεε if rεading compreh℃nsion improves.

This  kind  of  classroom-basεd  rεsearch  has  commonly  bεεn callεd  action
research,  in  which “ paπicipants in a  given  social  situation  [a  language  class]  are
th℃mselves centrally involved  in a  systεmatic process of inquiry arising from  thεir
own practical concerns” (Burns, 2005, p. 241).  Action research is  carri℃d out not so
much to fulfill a thεsis requiremεnt or to publish a journal article as to improv,ε your
own understanding of the teaching-lεarning procεSS in thε classroom.  The payoff
for treating your tεaching-learning questions sεriously is, ultimately, your becoming
a bεttεr teach℃r.  And, yes, you might also find  that what you have learnεd is worth
sharing with othεr teachers, eithεr through informal conversations in the teacher's
lunchroom or through a  confεrencε prεsεntation.

David Nunan (1989b, p. 36) suggestεd the following possible questions, divided

into four  catεgoriεs, that might form the cεntral focus  of some action rεsearch:

Linguistic features of learners' language

1.  In  my  tεaching, I  genεrally provide an application task  to follow  up a  formal
presentation.  What  kind  of  languagε itεms do  learnεrs actually  use  in  thε
application task?

2.  Do  learnεrs more  εasily learn  closed  class  items  (e.g.,  pronouns/demonstra
tives)  when  thεsε are  prεsentεd as  paradigms, or when they are  taught  sepa
rately ovεr a pεriod of time?

Interactive features of classroom language

3.  In what ways do turn-taking and topic management vaηr with variations in the

sizε and composition of learner groups?

4.  Are  learners  more  εffective at  conversational  management  when  techniques
such  as  holding  thε floor,  bringing  in  another  speaker,  etc.,  are  consciously
taught?

Tasks

5.  Which tasks  stimulate more interaction?
6.  ￦hich tasks work best with mixed-ability groups?

Strategies

7.  Is  thεrε  a  conflict  bεtween thε  classroom  activities  I  favor  and  those  my

learners pr,εfεr?

8.  Do  my  best  learners  share  certain strategy  pr,εfεrεnces that  distinguish  thεm

from less  efficiεnt Iεarners?

CHAPTER  25  Teacher  Development

Onε of the  possiblζ benefits of action  resεarch is  its stimulus for  collaboration
among tεachers.  A few years ago I instigated a  collaborative effort at the Amεrican
Languagε Institute  at  San  Francisco  Statε University  to  study  the  eftiεct  of  εrror
treatment  on the  pεrformance of our ESL  studεnts.  Two  matchεd sections  of the
same low-intεrmediatε intensive English course wεrε selεctεd for  invεstigation ovεr
a  seven-week  period.  An  oral  prε-tεst  was  dεsignεd by  thε rεsεarch group  and
administεr℃d to  εach student.  In  one  section,  teachers  dεliberatεly withheld  any
treatmεnt of present  tense,  present  progressive, and  third  person  singular  sp℃εch
errors committed by thε students.  In the  othεr sεction, tεachers attεmpted to  tr'εat
ovεrtly all such errors that thεy noticed.  During the sevεn-week study time, teachers
observεd each other, and other members of the  r℃search group not tεaching those
sections also came in to observe, mostly to check up on thε εxtεnt to which teachers
were carη따ig out their respectiv,ε charge.  At  the εnd of thε sεven-wεεk period, the
pre-test was readministered as a post-test, and gain scores wεre calculatεd.

The  statistical  findings  of this  little  study  were  disappointing:  no  significant
diffiεrence  between  the  two  sεctions!  But  the  pεdagogical gains  accrued  by  thε
collaboration  among  eight  teachers  were  more  than  worth  the  εffort.  In  th℃
procεss of investigating a potentially intεresting instructional variable, teachers did
the  following,  all  collaboratively:  Thεy  formulated  rεsεarch  hypothesεs;  they
designεd the study; they designεd a  tεst; they obsεπed and gave  fεεdback to each
other; they wer'ε sensitizεd to the complexities of error treatmεnt; and they lowεrεd
their fear of pεrforming research!

You  st퍼 may  be  feeling  a  little  queasy  about  labεling some  of your  teachεr
inquisitiveness as  research.  Can I really ask the “ right” quεstions? How do I know if
my  research  methodology  is  sound?  How  will  I  deal  with  numεrical  results
(statistics)? Will my conclusions be valid?  Good questions.  First of all, I recommend
that you consult a teacher resource book on classroom resεarch  (Burns, 1999; Edge,
2001; Freeman, 1998; Wallace, 1998).

Second, considεr the following pointεrs to get yourself startεd on some simple

but potentially effective action research.

1.  Convert your “ideas” into spe따ic questions.

You may have quitε a fεw “ ideas” about things that you could investigatε in thε
classroom.  That’s good; keep thosε creativε juices flowing.  But in ordεr to be able
to draw conclusions, your ideas  hav1ε to be converted into  questions  that you can
answer.  Sometimes  thosε  questions  ar,ε  too  broad:  Is  communicativ'ε language
teaching effective? How useful is reading aloud in class? Doεs process writing work?
So,makε surε that your quεstions arε specific ℃nough that you can look back
after your investigation and really come up with an answer.  The questions do not
have to be long and drawn out, just specific, 파t the eight questions listed εarliεr.
As an example here, let’s  consider the following question:

CHAPTER  25

Teacher Development

501

Given  a  selection  of six commonly  used techniques,  how do  th잉
compare with each other in terms of stimulating interaction?

2.  Operationally det1ne the elements of your question.

Next,  takε  your  question  and  operationally  define  all  the  elements  in  it.
“Operational” means that you have a measurable means for determining something.
So, in the εxamplε question above, let’S  say that for  the purpose of your research
you have selected six small-group techniques (jigsaw, role play, etc.).  You will limit
your investigation to  those  six.  Interaction then has  to be defined.  Suppose you
define  interaction  as  the  total  number of turns  taken  in  each  group.  And, for  a
possible additional interesting statistic, total up the number of minutes of student
talk as  well.

3.  Determine how you will answer your question.

Now  you  ar1ε  ready  to  launch  the  investigation.  How  will  you  answer  the
question? Your rεsearch methodology may call for several weeks of data collεcting
and, in this particular case, some tape rεcorders, since you W피 not be ablε to rεcord
data for several small groups at once and attend to the techniques as well.  For each
of the six designated techniques, you will have a tape recorder placed in each small
group and running during the entire technique.  αes, the tape recorders may inhibit
some studεnts ‘ but that’S  the risk you have to take.) You will (perhaps with the help
of a colleague?) then listen to each tape and tally the number of turns for each and
add up minutes of talk as  well.  Assuming that you have allowed all  the groups an
equal  number  of total  minutes  within  each  technique, you  can  come  up  with  a
grand total of turns and minutes for each technique.  The number of turns for each
technique will determine its rank order amo;맹 the six.

4.  Interpret your results appropriately.

According  to  your  findings  (see  below),  technique  A  stimulates  the  most
interaction, B is  next, and so on.  But your conclusion may not be so simple.  Every
research  study  has  its  necessary  caveats,  so  before  you  make  a  sweeping
generalization about your findings, it will help to state, even if only for yourself, some
of the limitations of your results.  Here are the results you found:

[ HAPTER 25  Teacher Development

First, can you be sure that technique A stimulated significantly more turns than
technique B? And B more  than C, etc.? Ask  a  statistician to help you to determine
how probable it is  that your results stemmed from the technique rather than from
just  random  possibilities.  πlis way  you  will  be  able  to  determine  the  statistical
significance of your findings.

Second, notice that the number of minutes of student talk didn’t  correspond,
meaning that in  some techniques (A, for  example) there was some relatively  rapid
turn-taking  interspersed  with  student  silence,  and  in  other  techniques  (F,  for
example)  certain students  talked  for  longer stretches  of time.  Thls  may  give  you
cause to redefine interaction or at least to interpret your results accordin!혀y.

Finally,  results  need  to  be  seen  in  terms  of other limitations  in  the  study
itself: the  choice and  number of tasks, the  number of students, the  operational
definitions chosen, and your particular group of students.  You may, for example,
be  temptεd to  generalize  results  of classroom  research  to  the  world  at  large.
Bεware.  Your  safest  conclusion  is  one  that  reports  what  you  found  for  your
class,  and  to  invite  others  to  replicate  your study if they wish  to  see  if similar
results are obtained.

Classroom-based  research  is  ideally  suited  to  current  practice  in  language
teaching  where  we  are  not  in  the  business  of buying  into  one  of the “ designer”
methods  with  their  prescriptions  of what  teachers  should  do  in  the  classroom.
Instead,  our  communicative,  interactive  l없iguage-teaching approach  asks  every
teacher to assess his or her own classroom of students and to design instructional
techniquεs  that  work  under  those  particular  conditions,  for  those  particular
learners, who are pursuing particular purposes in learning the English language.

Further, action research is an excellent way to promote teacher development
within a  framework  that values  the personal construction of teacher knowledge
as  opposed to a  transmission model.  It fosters  a spirit of understanding of one's
own beliefs, knowledge, and experience.  It forces  teachers to look at their own
situated context.  And, almost inevitably, action research encourages collaborative
relationships among teachers (Burns, 2005; Freeman, 2002; Mann, 2005).

I핀A다IER COUABORATION: LEARNING FROM EACH O깐IER

The  process of continuing  to develop  your professional  expertise  as  a  teacher is
sometimes  difficult  to  manage  alone.  The  challenges  of  teaching  in  a  rapidly
ch없iging profession almost necessitate collaboration with other teachers in order
to  stay  on  the  cutting  edge  (Murphey  &  Sato,  2005).  Can  you  successfully
collaborate  with  other  teachers  to  fulfill  your  expectations?  Let  me  suggest  six
forms of collaboration-of teachers learning from each other-that have worked for
others and that may work for you.

CHAPTER  25  Tea [ her Development

503

1.  Peer coaching

Already in this chapter you have been given somε guidelinεs for observation of
both  yoursεκ and  other  tεachers.  Peer  coaching  is  a  systematic  proc℃SS  Of
collaboration in which onε teachεr obsεrves and gives fε℃dback to another tεachεr,
usually  with  somε form  of  reciprociα  Katε  KinsεIla  (1994,  p.  35)  dεfines and
elaborates as  follows:

Peer  coaching  is  a  structurεd  process  by  which  trainεd  faculty
membεrs voluntarily  assist  each  other  in  εnhancing thεir teaching
within  an  atmospherε  of  collεgial  trust  and  candor,  through:
(1)  devεlopment of individual  instructional  improvement  goals  and
clεar  observation  critεria;  (2)  reciprocal,  focused,  nonevaluative
classroom  observations;  and  (3)  prompt  constructive  feedback  on
thosε observations.

Obsεrvεrs nεεd not  technically  be “ peεrs ” in  εvεry sεnsε of the word, but as
colleaguεs,  observer  and  tεacher  engage  in  a  coopεrativ,ε  process  of  mutual
communication about the actual teaching-learning procεss as  dir'εctly obsεrved in
the  classroom.  Feedback  is  classifiεd as  formative  r  ther than summative.  It  is
offiεred and r℃Cεived as  information for  the enhancemεnt of one’s  futurε tεaching,
not as  data for summing up one ’s competencies as  a tεacher.

Peεr coaching can bε espεcially helpful if you focus on cεrtain aspεcts of your
teaching.  If you ’vε been concerned, say,  about  thε quantity of tεacher talk  versus
student  talk  in  your  t℃aching, a  peer  observεr may  be  able  to  give  you  some
feedback  that could lεad you  to make  some adjustments.  Among topics that peer
coaching programs havε center'εd on are distribution of student participation across
the  classroom;  teacher  speech  mannerisms,  pattεrns, eye  contact,  and  nonverbal
distractors; group and pair work managemεnt; and transitions  from  on℃ activity to
thε next, to the next.

Peer  coaching  is  ablε to  off)εr a  pεrsonalized opportunity  for  growth.  Both
sides of the team benefit: Thε observer is  call℃d upon to carefully analyze another’s
teaching and therεby sharpεn his or her own metacognitive ability to reflεct on thε
teaching  procεss;  thε  teacher  bεing  observ,εd  is  nudged  out  of  what  might
otherwise  be  some  complacency into  a  heightened  awareness  of his  or her own
areas of strength and weakness.

2.  Team teaching

To  the  extent  that  thε  structur,ε  and  budget  of your  program  permit,  team
teaching  can  b℃ an  εxtraordinarily rewarding  experiεnce.  Several  models  of tεam
teaching  are  common:  (1)  Two  teachers  ar,ε  overtly  prεsent  throughout  a  class
pεriod, but <lividε rεsponsibility b℃tween thεm; (2) two teachers takε different halv1εs
of a class pεriod, with onε tεacher stζpping asidε whilε the other performs; and (3)
two or more tεachers tεach differ'εnt consecutiv1ε periods of onε group of learnεrs,
and must collaboratε closely in carπing out and modifying curricular plans.

504

CHAPTER  25

I겉acher Development

The  first  two  models  are  less  frequently  found  among  English  language
pr앵rams not because of absence of reward for student and teacher, but because of
budgetary  limitations.  The  third  model  is  extremely  common  in  the  English
language-teaching  world,  especially  whenever  a  group  of  learners  compose  an
intact  set  of students  across  two  or  more  class  periods.  Within  this  model,  the
importance  of collaboration  is  sometimes  under'εstimated.  Teachers  may  be  too
ready to assumε that a curriculum spanning a whole term of, say, 10 to 15 wεeks will
simply proceed as planned, only to discover that another teacher has not bεεn able
to follow the time-plan, throwing off the expected sequencing of material.

The  advantages  of team  teaching, especially  in  the  first  two  models, parallel
those  of  peer  coaching.  Teachers  are  encouraged  to  collaborate,  to  consider
respective  strengths,  and  to  engage  in  reflective  practice.  In  the  third  model,
teachers  must  develop  a  pattern  of frequent  communication  and  exchange,  the
fruits of which often are greater professional growth.

3.  Action research

Classroom-based, or “action;’ research has already been described in a previous
section  of  this  chapter.  Research  in  the  language  classroom  offers  another
opportunity for  you  to  collaborate  with other teachers  in creative  and ultimately
rewarding ways.

4.  Collaborative curriculum development and revision

The  process  of  curriculum  development  and  revision  warrants  a  similar
collaborative effort.  In the same way that teachers are sometimes all  too happy to
turn over research to the experts, so we arε tempted to get curriculum specialists
to do course and pro~얄am development.  Growing, dynamic language programs are
a  product of an ongoing  creative  dialogue  between teachers and among  teachers
and  those  that  are  assigned  to  compile  curricula.  Not  to  involve  teachers  in  the
process is to run the risk of programs that are generated in a vacuum of sorts, devoid
of a dynamic interaction 없nong student, teacher, and administrator.

At  the American  Language  Institute, our curriculum supervisors are  in daily
communication with teachers.  As  teachers consult with them on lesson design,
textbook adaptation, and pedagogical innovations, new curricula are  born every
day.  This kind of collaboration results in solicited teacher contributions to course
syllabuses,  which  are  then  adapted  and  incorporated  into  established,  revised
curricula.  Thus  the  curricula  for  courses  are  in  a  slow  but  constant  state  of
creative change.

5.  Presenting at a  professional conference

At San  Francisco State University, the graduating class in the M.A.  program in
TESOL plans and administers a semi-annual professional conference at which each
graduate makes a  profession찌 presentation.  Graduates find that this  is  not just an
excellent training ground for conference planning, but that it forces them to prepare
and deliver a high껴uality presentation on a topic of professional interest.  A number

CHAPTER  25  Teacher  Development

505

of thosε presentations  are  co-presented,  giving  thε prεsenters an  opportunity  to
collaboratε and p0<] l their efforts‘

I include the suggεstion of conferencε presentations hεrε because it's difficult
η at to collaboratε in some way on such events.  Even if you do a solo presεntation,
somε collaboration  is  often  involved  in  prεparing, in  critiquing, and, of coursε, in
intεracting with one’s audiencε.  A confer,εncε prεsεntation is a superb opportunity
to  develop  some  of  your  own  idεas  and  to  sharε  thεm with  a  prof1εssional
community.  It  givεs you  an  impetus  to  focus  on  somε background  reading,  to
systεmatically  pull  togethεr  an  idea  or  topic,  to  prεsεnt  it  clεarly  and
enthusiastically,  and  ultimately  to  ℃stablish nεw professional  connεctions beyond
your own institution.  It’s also an εxcellent addition to your resume!  Somε ideas for
prεsεntations:

•  a task or activity that demonstrates a principlε Orthεme (ε .g., environmεntal

action)

•  a  sεt of tasks or activitiεs (e.g., a project involving all four skills) focused on

a  thεmε

•  a  study of students'  languagε dεvelopment in  a  spεcific arεa (℃ .g.,  relative

clausεs, verb tensεs)

•  a curriculum or modulε that you created for a course
•  a focus on a learning principlε (ε.g., anxiety) with background resεarch and

practical examplεs for facilitating learning

4.  Writing for publication

One of the most rεwarding εnd℃avors in a teacher’s life is professional writing
for  publication.  My  first  journal  article  was  an  outgrowth  of a  little  classroom
basεd expεriment that  I  did  on the  English  spelling  systεm (Brown,  1970).  I  did

was accepted!’

projects,  among  many  othεr  possibilitiεs,  can  become  a  potentially  usεful,
interεsting,  and  innovative  addition  to  professional  litεraturε  in  thε  field  of
language  teaching.  With  a  huge  variεty of  journals  in  our  fiεld,  in  addition  to
newslεttεrs, local  profiεssional  booklεts, and  a  growing  numbεr of Wεb-basεd
opportunities for writing (Murphey εt al., 2005), you should by all  mεans consider
this creative outlet for your εnεrgiεs

Consider thesε possible gεnres of writing that you can do:

•  writing a profiεssional journal article on somε action research you have donε

•  writing up a “teaching tip” on a task you dev,εloped in your class

•  publishing a student textbook
•  compiling a  teachεr’s manual for a  studεnt textbook
•  εditing a textbook for a publisher
•  reviεwing a manuscript for a  publishεr
•  creating Wεb-based biogs or other εlectronic rεsourc℃S
•  compiling an annotated bibliography of references on a  spεcific topic

It’s  tεmpting  for  tεachεrs  to  assume  that  professional  writing  must  bε
undertakεn by “εxpεrts” (Ph.D.'s  and profiεssors and  thε like), and  cεrtainly not by
“ ordinary” tεachεrs.  Nonsense!  In the same way that rεsεarch is  not th℃ εxclusive
province of fancy psychometricians, as we  notεd above, professional writing is  (or
should  bε)  equally  sharεd  by  classroom  teachers.  Many  teachers  find  that
professional  writing  is  not  only  an  excellent  motivating  ε:xpεrience, but  also  a
r℃warding onε that opens up nεw pεrsonal and professional vistas.

OBJECTIVES  After  reading  this  chapter,  you  will  be able to:

You must be the change you want to see in the world.
-Gandhi

In this final chapter I want to address another aspεct of language teaching, beyond that
of teacher dev1εlopment, that penetrates well below the surface of the  cogr피디ve 따id
technical skills implied in effective teaching.  The αiticαl nature of language learning
and teaching can in  many ways  be  peπasive in your professional  life.  Ask  yourself
what, in  thε deepest part of your soul, drives  you  in  your chosen profession.  What
convictions  and  beliefs  directed  you  into  the  profession?  What  sense  of  purpose
continues to propel you throu행 hours and days and weeks of teaching (possibly at
a  fairly  low  wag1ε)? What  is  it  that  somehow prods you  to wake  up  every  Monday
morning,  a  bit  blurry-eyed  perhaps,  but  ready  to  face  new  challenges  and
opportunities?

Somewhere in those deep recesses of your mind and emotion you are guided
by a  sense of mission, of purpose, and of dedication to a  profession in which you
believe you can makε a  difference.  Your sense of social responsibility directs you
to  be  an  agent  for  change.  You’re  driven  by convictions  about  what this  world
should look like, how its people should behave, how its gov1εrnments should control
that behavior, and how its inhabitants should be paπners in the stewardship of the
planet.  In the words of Giroux and McLaren (1989, p. xiii), you strive to “embody in
[your]  tεaching a vision of a better and more humanε life.”

CRITICAL PEDAGOGY

These fundamental moral principles 뼈t피n us have been examined in recent years in
what educational  professionals  have  called  critical @없g앵y.  Suresh  Canagarajah
(2005) shied away from  a  dirεct, εmpirical definition of critical pedagogy, but noted
that it implies a way of “ doing” learning 따id teaching, motivated by our beliefs about
εducation and  its  place  in  society: “Critical  students  and  teachers  are  prepared  to
situate learning in the relevant social contexts, unravel  the implications of power in
pedagO!킥cal activity, and commit themsεlves to transforming the means 따id ends of
learr피ig, in order to construct more egalitari따i, equitable, and ethical educational and
social en찌ronments” (p. 932).

Critical  pεdagogy  has  been  a  focus  of  inter'εst  in  the  language-teaching
profiεssion  for  some  time.  In  1999,  the  TESOL  Quarteψ/ devoted  its  entire
(Autumn) issue  to critical pedagogy, with contributions from  a  number of authors
rεpr1℃senting various  perspectiv'εs and subfields (Pennycook, 1999).  Now, it would
be remiss of any language teacher to skirt around issues of power and p이itics and
religion in the classroom, simply because they evoke strong emotions‘

Teaching As A  Subversive Activity

The call  for teachers to act critically, as  agents for  change, is  not a  new one.  Four
d℃cades ago, Postman and Weingartnεr (1969) shook some educational foundations
with their best seller, 1강a chiηg as a  Subversiνe Activity.  In their stinging  critiquξ
of the American educational establishment, they challenged teachers to enable their
students to become “ crap” detectors: (a) crap detectors in creating major chaη:ges in
our social, εconomic, and political systems; φ) crap detectors who can cut through
burgeoning  bureaucracies  (which,  they  note,  따e  repositories  of  conventional
assumptions  and  st때dard practices); and  (c)  crap  detectors  who  can  release  us
from  the stranglehold of the media, which creates its own version of censorship.

The call for subversiv;ε teaching is not unlike the challenge to En헝ish language
teachers  today  to  engage  in  critical  pedagogy.  Those  of us  who  teach  languag'εs
may  indeed  have  a  special  responsibility  to  “subvert”  attitudes  and  beliefs  and
assumptions that ultimately impede the attainment of such goals as equality, justice,
freedom, and opportunity.

In  recent  years  some  educators,  notably  Henry  Giroux  (2006)  and  Peter
McLaren  (2005), along with Julian Edge (2003) in our own proJ뚫ssion of language
educators,  have  taken  a  strong  interpretation  of  the  implications  of  critical
pedagogy.  The title alone of McLaren’s (2005) book, Capitalists and Conquerors:A
Critical Pedag1앵y Against the Empire,  pro찌des a clear picture of its contents: a call
to teachers for classroom practices that assertively direct students toward becoming
critical  thinkers  (and  doers)  in  the face  of war, violence, corruption, imperialism,
greed, and waste of natural resources.

Some Cautionary Observations

De batεs OV<εr the  means  and  εnds of carrying  out  critical  pedagogi℃S bring  with
thεm somε warnings.  We  are  rεmindεd that  our  learnεrs  must  be  freε  to  be
thεmsεIves, to think for  thεmsεIves, to bεhavε in tellεctually without coercion from
a  powerful  elitε  (Clarke,  1990,  2003),  to  cherish  thεir bεliefs and  traditions  and
cultur'εs without the threat of forcεd change (Bεnεsch, 2001;  Edgε, 1996; Norton &
Toohεy, 2004).  In our classrooms, wherε “ the  dynamics  of power and domination
permεat℃ thε fabric  of classroom  Imε ” (Auerbach,  1995, p.  9), we  ar,ε  alεrted to  a
possibl℃ “ covεn political agenda  [benεath our]  overt tεchnical agenda” (Phillipson,
1992, p.  27).  ]띠ian Edgε (2003, p. 705)  cautions  against  those  who  might  tεach
English  out of a  rεligious “ conv'εrsion-motivatεd” zεal, lεst thε very act of tεaching
English creatε its own hegemony, a  thεme εchoεd by Scollon (2004).

What has come to bε known as liberation education, among othεr tεrms (sεε
Clark,ε, 1990, 2003; Freirε, 1970), must no doubt be tempered with  somε cautionary
obsεrvations.  Somε hav,ε  rεcently arguεd that  our  ostεnsibly bεnign 싫sumptions
about teaching methodology (sεe Canagarajah, 2005; Holliday, 1994; Pennycook, 2001)
hav,ε  an  element  of  controversy  in  thεm.  Why,  thεrε’s  hardly  a  pεrson  in  this
profession  who  would  not  stand  up  with  your  hand  ov1εr  your  heart  to  salutε
“ communicativ1ε languagε teaching” or “ coopεr.itiv1ε lεarning 꺼 But are all these warm
and  fuz강, soft  따id tεnder approaches  to  thε classroom  univ,εrsally acc℃pted by  all
cultures and all educational traditions? Probably not.  In an article titled “Towards Less
Humanistic English Teaching,'’ Gadd (1998)  cautionεd against  viεwing oursεIves as  a
“ nurturer of souls  . 시’ bεcause this “ inappropriate  and  oppressiv1ε role  ...  doεs not
encourage or permit thε students’ int℃llectual and cognitive  dev1εlo pmεnt.'’  (Arnold’s
reply to Gadd in the samε issue is worth car,εful reading.)

Thε counterpoint  to  this  rallying  of  tεachεrs  to  change  a  world  mir1εd  in
bureaucracies  is  epitomized, as  I  see  it,  in  what  Skutnabb-Kangas  and  Phillipson
(1994)  called  Iiηguicism.  Phillipson  (1992)  argued  that,  historically  at  least,
worldwide  English  language  tεaching has  sεπed to  “lεgitimatε  ·  ‘
.  an  unequal
division of powεr and  rεsources," as “ thε dominant language  [English]  is  glorified,
[and]  dominatεd languagεs are  stigmatized”  (p.  27).  Now,  while  Holliday  (1994)
rightfully argues, I  think, that Phillipson’s  stance “- . .  implies a  conspiracy viεw of
English language  teaching which is  ovεr-simplistic and naivε” (p. 99), nεverthelεSS
I think all of us, if we havεn’t donε so already, nεed to tak℃ heed lεst wε becomε thε
inadvertεnt perpetuators of a  widening  of the  gap  between haves  and  have-nots.
Languagε is  powεc and thε unequal  distribution  of language  pr앵rams across  thε
world  surely  could  contributε  to  the  ultimate  unequal  distribution  of  power
(Macedo, Dendrinos, &  Gounari, 2003; Norton &  Toohεy, 2004).

Is  there a  middle ground? A numbεr of rεcent articles and books  suggεst that
there is (Canagarajah, 2005; Clarkε, 2003; Edg1℃, 2003;Johnston, 2003; Snow, 2001).
In  the  face  of  currεnt  mounting  threats  from  tεrrorist  organizations,  warring

we are in a period of dir℃ neεd of information, understanding, and communication.

HOT TOPICS IN THE LANGUAGE CIASSROOM

A number of thε so-called “ hot topics” that wε somεtimes addr1εss in our classrooms,
such  as  nonviolεnee, human  rights,  gender  εquality, racial/℃thnic discrimination,
hεalth issues, εnvironmental action, and political activism, arε controversial.  Thεy
dεmand critical  thinking  and  the  neεd to  bε sensitiv1ε to  studεnts ’  valuε systems.
They  cannot, in  any  context, be simply  thrown  into  a  classroom  routine  without
risking aliεnation, anger, or resεntmεnt on the paπ of studεnts.

Guidelines for Dealing with Controversial Issues
in the Classroom

I would likε to suggest thrεe guiding principles for engaging in teaching with social
responsibility, that is, critical pεdagogy that fully respects the values and beliefs of
your students.

1.  Teachers are responsible for giving students opportunities to learn

about important social I  moral I  ethical issues and to analyze all sides
of an issue.  A language class is  an id℃al locus for offering information on
topics of significanc℃ to students.  Thε objectiv'εs of a curriculum ar℃ not
limited to linguistic factors alone, but also include dεveloping the art of
critical thinking.  Complex issues (say, rεligious fundamentalism or
homosexuality, for example) can become the focus of intrinsically motivating

content-basεd languag℃ lεarning.

2.  Teacher「S are responsible for creating an atmosphere of respect for
each other’s  opinions, beliefs, and ethnic/cultural diversity.  The
classroom becomes a  modεl of the world as a context for tolerance and for
thε a ppr℃ciation of divεrsity.  Discourse structures such as “ I see your point,
but .. j’ are explicitly taught and used in classroom discussions and debates.
Students learn how to disagree without imposing their own belief or opinions

on others.  In all  this, it is  imp야tant that thε tεa ch er’s personal opinions or
bεIiεfs rεmain sensitiv1εly covεrt, lest a student fεεl coerced into thinking

3.  Teachers are responsible for maintaining a  threshold of morality and
ethics in the classroom climate.  Occasionally a tεacher nεeds to ℃Xεrcise
some discipline when students show disrespect or hatred basεd on, say, racε,
religion, εthnicity, or gεnder.  Tεachεrs should εnsurε that “ universal” moral

This guideline is, in εffect, a  paradox bεca usε it  prεsupposεs certain valuεs to
be beyond rεproach.  Such a presupposition violatεs thε vεry principlε of
respect captured in the guideline #2 above.  Nεvertheless, this is  whεrε one’s
pedagogy becomes “critical” in that the teachεr’S vision of“a  bεtter and mor1ε
humanε lifiε” is  usually prεdi catεd on such basic values.

Examples of Socially Responsible Teaching from Around
the World

Consider the following examplεs of classroom activities from around thε world.  Do
thεy abidε by the abov1ε guidεlines? Can your classroom rε-create any of them?

In  Brazil, a  curriculum for  childrεn takes  thεm on an  adventure  trip

see  thε world  as  it  could be if everyonε respected  it.  Thε pr앵ram
Iεaches appr,εciation for Native Indians of Brazil, thεir culture, storiεs,
and  music;  it  tεachεs  gεndεr-role  equity,  animal  rights,  and
environmental stεwardship.  (Maria Rita Vieira)

In  Japan,  a  classroom  r℃Sεarch project  callεd “ Dreams  and  Dream
Makers"  had  students  choose  a  person  who “worked  to  mak℃ the
world a more pεaceful placε.” (Donna Mclnnis)

In Singapor,ε, an activity called “stamping out insults" focusεd on why
people  insult  othεrs  and  helped  students  to  learn  and  us℃ kind,
affirming words as they disagreed with onε another.  (Georgε Jacobs)

In China, a teacher had students study oppr1εssion and supprεssion of
free  speεch in thε formεr So viεt Union, calling for critical analysis of
the  roots  and  remedies  of  such  dεnial  of  frεedom.  Without
espousmg any particular point of view himself, and under the guise

to comprεhend alternativ,ε points of view.  (Anonymous by request)

517

In  Armenia,  a  teacher  had  students  share  their  grandparents’
experiencεs during the 1915Armenian genocide when more than 1.5
million Armenians  were  killed  in Turkey.  Nearly  eveπ student  had
family  members  who  had been killed.  Discussions focused  on how
ethnic  groups  could  overcome  such  catastrophes  and  learn  to  live
together as cooperative, peaceful neighbors.  (Nick Dimmitt)

A tεacher in Israel told of a  unit  in which students had to create an
ethical  marketing and advertising  campaign for  a  product.  Cases  of
Colgate’S  widening  the  mouth  of toothpaste  tubes  and  of Revlon’s
making thε glass on nail p이ish bottles a little thicker led students to
debate εthical business issues.  (Stuart Carroll)

In  Egypt, a  culture where equal  opportunities and rights  of women
are abridged, a teacher used an activity in a class with both men and
women in it  that culminated in  the students’ collaboratively writing
upa “ bill of rights” for women in Egypt.  (Mona Grant Nashed)

In  the  United  States,  following  the  September  11,  2001  terrorist
attacks, a  student asked the teacher what “Middle  East” meant; when
the  teach℃r defined  the  term,  thε student  responded with, “Oh, you
mean ‘tεrrorists’.”  The teacher used the next 10 minutes to sensitively

misinformation that they often convey.  (Anonymous)

A  number  of recent  presentations  and  publications  reveal  that  teachers  are
increasingly  taking  bold  steps  to  incorporate  global  concerns  and  social
responsibility in their classrooms (Garfield, 2003; Givner, 2004; Graybill & Shehane,
2006;  Sampedro  &  Hillyard,  2004).  At  TESOL  conferences  one  can  always  find  a
substantial  number  of  presentations  on  controversial  issues.  In  the  TESOL
organization, one  of the  strongest  caucuses  is  Teachers  for  Social  Responsibiliπ.
Similar interest groups in other organizations are strengthening teachers' abilities to
engage  in  critical  pedagogy.  In  Japan,  a  quaπerly publication,  Global Issues  in
Laη1guage Education Newsletter, has a national readership.

Can you, in turn, engage in sensitive critical pedagogy in your classroom?  What
are some activities you can do that would respect students' points of view yet stir
them  to  a  higher consciousness  of their own role  as  ag'εnts of change? The little
differences hεre and there that you make can add up to fulfilling visions of a better
and more humane world.

MORAL DILEMMAS AND MORAL IMPERATIVES

The process of engaging in a  socially  rεsponsible approach in which wε teachers
takε  up  the  challengε  of  being  agεnts  for  change  brings  with  it  somε moral
dilεmmas.  How far  should we  push our own pεrsonal beliεfs and  ag℃ndas in  our
zeal for rεalizing visions of a  bettεr world and for creating critically thinking futur,℃
leadεrs among our students? Do you have the right to subvεrs iv,εly push your vision
of th℃ ideal world out  thεre? At  l℃ast five  moral  dilεmmas present themsεIves, but
εach dilεmma carries  with  it  what  I  claim  is  a  moral  impεrative.  Consider  thε
following  dilεmmas, and their corollariεs in  thε form of imperativ,εs, that call us  to
action as  socially rεsponsiblε teachεrs:

1.  Risking the cultural biases of communicative approaches

Our  widεly acceptεd communicative  approach  to  language  tεaching (CLl],
which aims to empower and valuε studεnts, may itsεIf rεflect a  cultural bias that is
not universally embracεd.  Not all educational traditions value the learner-centεrεd,
interactive  approachεs that could-in the mind of a  tεachεr-usurp the  teachεr’s
authority (and power) in the classroom.  I believe such a  dilεmma can be r℃solved
in the following  moral imperative:

Respect th℃ diversity of cultural pattεrns and expectations among our
students, whilε utilizing thε b℃st methodological approachεs available
to accomplish coursε goals and objεctivεs.

2.  Avoiding contributing to disempowerment

Our  altruistic  “ agendas”  for  bringing  English  to  the  world  at  large  have  thε
potential  of  lεgitimizing an  unequal  division  of power  and  resources.  As  notεd
abovε, thε V℃ry act of tεaching English may havε thε residual εff;εct of widening thε
gap  betwεen “ hav1εs” and “ havε-nots ” by enabling an εlite class  to  distinguish itself
from  a  less  powεrful group  by the  ability to  use  English.  Herε’s a  possible  moral
imperativ,ε that may resolve this:

Help  studεnts to claim their own power and rεsourcεs and to bridge
the  gaps  that  separate  countries,  p이itical  structurεs, religions,  and
values  through  a  unifying  language, but  do  all  wε can to  cεlebrate
indigenous heritage languages and cultures.

3.  Creating inoffensive yet interesting materials

In our curricular materials, our  choicεs of topics  and  issues  present us  with
opportunities to stimulate critical thinking but also to offend and polarize studεnts‘
But thosε matεrials risk being vεry bland and uninteresting if evεηrthing that has a

519

rεmotε potential of bεing offensiv'ε is  dεlεted.  Pεrhaps some middle ground can bε
a chiεved in this moral imperative:

Sensitivεly, with  duε att℃ntion  to  the  potential  for  students  to  bε
offend℃d and  polarizεd, approach  critical,  rεlevant, and  informative
issuεs  in  appropriate  pedagogical  contexts  with  as  balanced  a
pεrspective as  possible, and with  thε particular cultural  sensitivities
of your studεnts wε11 in mind.

4.  Remaining as neutral as possible

Our  discussions,  dεbates, group  work  activities,  essays,  and  other  classroom
tasks  offer  opportunities  for  us  to  bε agents  for  change,  but  does  our  zeal  for
realizing  our  own  vision  of a  better world  stand  in  the  way  of genuinely  εqual,
balanced  trεatmεnt of all  sidεs of controversial  issuεs? Is  it  realistic  to think that a
teacher can complεtely hide his  or hεr own bεliefs on such fundamεntal issues  as
racial  hatr<εd,  nonviolεnce, gender  εquality, trεatmεnt of those  with  homosεxual
orientations, environmental stewardship, or freεdom of expression, to name a few?
Studεnts have  an  uncanny ability to “ psych out”  thεir tεachεrs, and it’s  difficult for

Perhaps this dilemma can be at least partially resolv'εd in the following  imperative:

critical  thinking  on complex  issuεs, rεmain as  neutral as  possible  in

covertly or “ subv'εrsively.”

5.  Dealing with assessment standards

Large-scale  standardized  tests  ar,ε  widεly  embraced  by  a  budget-conscious
establishment, but ar'ε thεy all free of cultural and socioεconomic bias? It was notεd
in  Chapter  24  that  critical  language  testing  is  another  prominεnt issue  in  our
classrooms today.  Pεrhaps a  rout℃ to some solutions can be found  in thε following
moral imperative:

Carπ out resεarch to improve the authenticity and predictiv,ε validity
of standardizεd testing, lobby for funding for more performance-based

AGENTS FOR CHANGE

I’m  sure  that  all  five  of  thε abOV<ε dilemmas  arε commonly  experiεncεd among
